VOLUME ONE



 Chapter 1. MarseillesГўВҖВ”The Arrival

On the 24th of February, 1815, the look-out at Notre-Dame de la Garde
signalled the three-master, the _Pharaon_ from Smyrna, Trieste, and
Naples.

As usual, a pilot put off immediately, and rounding the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf,
got on board the vessel between Cape Morgiou and Rion island.

Immediately, and according to custom, the ramparts of Fort Saint-Jean
were covered with spectators; it is always an event at Marseilles for a
ship to come into port, especially when this ship, like the _Pharaon_,
has been built, rigged, and laden at the old Phocee docks, and belongs
to an owner of the city.

The ship drew on and had safely passed the strait, which some volcanic
shock has made between the Calasareigne and Jaros islands; had doubled
PomГғВЁgue, and approached the harbor under topsails, jib, and spanker,
but so slowly and sedately that the idlers, with that instinct which is
the forerunner of evil, asked one another what misfortune could have
happened on board. However, those experienced in navigation saw plainly
that if any accident had occurred, it was not to the vessel herself,
for she bore down with all the evidence of being skilfully handled, the
anchor a-cockbill, the jib-boom guys already eased off, and standing by
the side of the pilot, who was steering the _Pharaon_ towards the
narrow entrance of the inner port, was a young man, who, with activity
and vigilant eye, watched every motion of the ship, and repeated each
direction of the pilot.

The vague disquietude which prevailed among the spectators had so much
affected one of the crowd that he did not await the arrival of the
vessel in harbor, but jumping into a small skiff, desired to be pulled
alongside the _Pharaon_, which he reached as she rounded into La
RГғВ©serve basin.

When the young man on board saw this person approach, he left his
station by the pilot, and, hat in hand, leaned over the shipГўВҖВҷs
bulwarks.

He was a fine, tall, slim young fellow of eighteen or twenty, with
black eyes, and hair as dark as a ravenГўВҖВҷs wing; and his whole
appearance bespoke that calmness and resolution peculiar to men
accustomed from their cradle to contend with danger.

ГўВҖВңAh, is it you, DantГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ cried the man in the skiff. ГўВҖВңWhatГўВҖВҷs the
matter? and why have you such an air of sadness aboard?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA great misfortune, M. Morrel,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man, ГўВҖВңa great
misfortune, for me especially! Off Civita Vecchia we lost our brave
Captain Leclere.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd the cargo?ГўВҖВқ inquired the owner, eagerly.

ГўВҖВңIs all safe, M. Morrel; and I think you will be satisfied on that
head. But poor Captain LeclereГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat happened to him?ГўВҖВқ asked the owner, with an air of considerable
resignation. ГўВҖВңWhat happened to the worthy captain?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe died.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFell into the sea?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, sir, he died of brain-fever in dreadful agony.ГўВҖВқ Then turning to
the crew, he said, ГўВҖВңBear a hand there, to take in sail!ГўВҖВқ

All hands obeyed, and at once the eight or ten seamen who composed the
crew, sprang to their respective stations at the spanker brails and
outhaul, topsail sheets and halyards, the jib downhaul, and the topsail
clewlines and buntlines. The young sailor gave a look to see that his
orders were promptly and accurately obeyed, and then turned again to
the owner.

ГўВҖВңAnd how did this misfortune occur?ГўВҖВқ inquired the latter, resuming the
interrupted conversation.

0023m



ГўВҖВңAlas, sir, in the most unexpected manner. After a long talk with the
harbor-master, Captain Leclere left Naples greatly disturbed in mind.
In twenty-four hours he was attacked by a fever, and died three days
afterwards. We performed the usual burial service, and he is at his
rest, sewn up in his hammock with a thirty-six-pound shot at his head
and his heels, off El Giglio island. We bring to his widow his sword
and cross of honor. It was worth while, truly,ГўВҖВқ added the young man
with a melancholy smile, ГўВҖВңto make war against the English for ten
years, and to die in his bed at last, like everybody else.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, you see, Edmond,ГўВҖВқ replied the owner, who appeared more comforted
at every moment, ГўВҖВңwe are all mortal, and the old must make way for the
young. If not, why, there would be no promotion; and since you assure
me that the cargoГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs all safe and sound, M. Morrel, take my word for it; and I advise
you not to take 25,000 francs for the profits of the voyage.ГўВҖВқ

Then, as they were just passing the Round Tower, the young man shouted:
ГўВҖВңStand by there to lower the topsails and jib; brail up the spanker!ГўВҖВқ

The order was executed as promptly as it would have been on board a
man-of-war.

ГўВҖВңLet goГўВҖВ”and clue up!ГўВҖВқ At this last command all the sails were lowered,
and the vessel moved almost imperceptibly onwards.

ГўВҖВңNow, if you will come on board, M. Morrel,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, observing the
ownerГўВҖВҷs impatience, ГўВҖВңhere is your supercargo, M. Danglars, coming out
of his cabin, who will furnish you with every particular. As for me, I
must look after the anchoring, and dress the ship in mourning.ГўВҖВқ

The owner did not wait for a second invitation. He seized a rope which
DantГғВЁs flung to him, and with an activity that would have done credit
to a sailor, climbed up the side of the ship, while the young man,
going to his task, left the conversation to Danglars, who now came
towards the owner. He was a man of twenty-five or twenty-six years of
age, of unprepossessing countenance, obsequious to his superiors,
insolent to his subordinates; and this, in addition to his position as
responsible agent on board, which is always obnoxious to the sailors,
made him as much disliked by the crew as Edmond DantГғВЁs was beloved by
them.

ГўВҖВңWell, M. Morrel,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңyou have heard of the misfortune
that has befallen us?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYesГўВҖВ”yes: poor Captain Leclere! He was a brave and an honest man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd a first-rate seaman, one who had seen long and honorable service,
as became a man charged with the interests of a house so important as
that of Morrel & Son,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars.

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ replied the owner, glancing after DantГғВЁs, who was watching the
anchoring of his vessel, ГўВҖВңit seems to me that a sailor needs not be so
old as you say, Danglars, to understand his business, for our friend
Edmond seems to understand it thoroughly, and not to require
instruction from anyone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, darting at Edmond a look gleaming with hate.
ГўВҖВңYes, he is young, and youth is invariably self-confident. Scarcely was
the captainГўВҖВҷs breath out of his body when he assumed the command
without consulting anyone, and he caused us to lose a day and a half at
the Island of Elba, instead of making for Marseilles direct.ГўВҖВқ

0025m



ГўВҖВңAs to taking command of the vessel,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel, ГўВҖВңthat was his
duty as captainГўВҖВҷs mate; as to losing a day and a half off the Island of
Elba, he was wrong, unless the vessel needed repairs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe vessel was in as good condition as I am, and as, I hope you are,
M. Morrel, and this day and a half was lost from pure whim, for the
pleasure of going ashore, and nothing else.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ said the shipowner, turning towards the young man, ГўВҖВңcome this
way!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn a moment, sir,ГўВҖВқ answered DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңand IГўВҖВҷm with you.ГўВҖВқ Then calling
to the crew, he said, ГўВҖВңLet go!ГўВҖВқ

The anchor was instantly dropped, and the chain ran rattling through
the port-hole. DantГғВЁs continued at his post in spite of the presence of
the pilot, until this manГ…В“uvre was completed, and then he added,
ГўВҖВңHalf-mast the colors, and square the yards!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou see,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңhe fancies himself captain already, upon my
word.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd so, in fact, he is,ГўВҖВқ said the owner.

ГўВҖВңExcept your signature and your partnerГўВҖВҷs, M. Morrel.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd why should he not have this?ГўВҖВқ asked the owner; ГўВҖВңhe is young, it is
true, but he seems to me a thorough seaman, and of full experience.ГўВҖВқ

A cloud passed over DanglarsГўВҖВҷ brow.

ГўВҖВңYour pardon, M. Morrel,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, approaching, ГўВҖВңthe vessel now
rides at anchor, and I am at your service. You hailed me, I think?ГўВҖВқ

Danglars retreated a step or two. ГўВҖВңI wished to inquire why you stopped
at the Island of Elba?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not know, sir; it was to fulfil the last instructions of Captain
Leclere, who, when dying, gave me a packet for Marshal Bertrand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen did you see him, Edmond?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe marshal.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel looked around him, and then, drawing DantГғВЁs on one side, he said
suddenlyГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңAnd how is the emperor?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well, as far as I could judge from the sight of him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou saw the emperor, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe entered the marshalГўВҖВҷs apartment while I was there.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you spoke to him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, it was he who spoke to me, sir,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, with a smile.

ГўВҖВңAnd what did he say to you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAsked me questions about the vessel, the time she left Marseilles, the
course she had taken, and what was her cargo. I believe, if she had not
been laden, and I had been her master, he would have bought her. But I
told him I was only mate, and that she belonged to the firm of Morrel &
Son. ГўВҖВҳAh, yes,ГўВҖВҷ he said, ГўВҖВҳI know them. The Morrels have been shipowners
from father to son; and there was a Morrel who served in the same
regiment with me when I was in garrison at Valence.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Pardieu!_ and that is true!ГўВҖВқ cried the owner, greatly delighted. ГўВҖВңAnd
that was Policar Morrel, my uncle, who was afterwards a captain.
DantГғВЁs, you must tell my uncle that the emperor remembered him, and you
will see it will bring tears into the old soldierГўВҖВҷs eyes. Come, come,ГўВҖВқ
continued he, patting EdmondГўВҖВҷs shoulder kindly, ГўВҖВңyou did very right,
DantГғВЁs, to follow Captain LeclereГўВҖВҷs instructions, and touch at Elba,
although if it were known that you had conveyed a packet to the
marshal, and had conversed with the emperor, it might bring you into
trouble.ГўВҖВқ

0027m



ГўВҖВңHow could that bring me into trouble, sir?ГўВҖВқ asked DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңfor I did
not even know of what I was the bearer; and the emperor merely made
such inquiries as he would of the first comer. But, pardon me, here are
the health officers and the customs inspectors coming alongside.ГўВҖВқ And
the young man went to the gangway. As he departed, Danglars approached,
and said,ГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңWell, it appears that he has given you satisfactory reasons for his
landing at Porto-Ferrajo?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, most satisfactory, my dear Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, so much the better,ГўВҖВқ said the supercargo; ГўВҖВңfor it is not
pleasant to think that a comrade has not done his duty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDantГғВЁs has done his,ГўВҖВқ replied the owner, ГўВҖВңand that is not saying much.
It was Captain Leclere who gave orders for this delay.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTalking of Captain Leclere, has not DantГғВЁs given you a letter from
him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo me?ГўВҖВ”noГўВҖВ”was there one?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI believe that, besides the packet, Captain Leclere confided a letter
to his care.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf what packet are you speaking, Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, that which DantГғВЁs left at Porto-Ferrajo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow do you know he had a packet to leave at Porto-Ferrajo?ГўВҖВқ

Danglars turned very red.

ГўВҖВңI was passing close to the door of the captainГўВҖВҷs cabin, which was half
open, and I saw him give the packet and letter to DantГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe did not speak to me of it,ГўВҖВқ replied the shipowner; ГўВҖВңbut if there be
any letter he will give it to me.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars reflected for a moment. ГўВҖВңThen, M. Morrel, I beg of you,ГўВҖВқ said
he, ГўВҖВңnot to say a word to DantГғВЁs on the subject. I may have been
mistaken.ГўВҖВқ

At this moment the young man returned; Danglars withdrew.

ГўВҖВңWell, my dear DantГғВЁs, are you now free?ГўВҖВқ inquired the owner.

ГўВҖВңYes, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have not been long detained.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo. I gave the custom-house officers a copy of our bill of lading; and
as to the other papers, they sent a man off with the pilot, to whom I
gave them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you have nothing more to do here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNoГўВҖВ”everything is all right now.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you can come and dine with me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI really must ask you to excuse me, M. Morrel. My first visit is due
to my father, though I am not the less grateful for the honor you have
done me.ГўВҖВқ

0029m



ГўВҖВңRight, DantГғВЁs, quite right. I always knew you were a good son.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd,ГўВҖВқ inquired DantГғВЁs, with some hesitation, ГўВҖВңdo you know how my
father is?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I believe, my dear Edmond, though I have not seen him lately.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, he likes to keep himself shut up in his little room.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat proves, at least, that he has wanted for nothing during your
absence.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs smiled. ГўВҖВңMy father is proud, sir, and if he had not a meal left,
I doubt if he would have asked anything from anyone, except from
Heaven.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, after this first visit has been made we shall count on
you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI must again excuse myself, M. Morrel, for after this first visit has
been paid I have another which I am most anxious to pay.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue, DantГғВЁs, I forgot that there was at the Catalans someone who
expects you no less impatiently than your fatherГўВҖВ”the lovely MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs blushed.

ГўВҖВңAh, ha,ГўВҖВқ said the shipowner, ГўВҖВңI am not in the least surprised, for she
has been to me three times, inquiring if there were any news of the
_Pharaon_. _Peste!_ Edmond, you have a very handsome mistress!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShe is not my mistress,ГўВҖВқ replied the young sailor, gravely; ГўВҖВңshe is my
betrothed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSometimes one and the same thing,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, with a smile.

ГўВҖВңNot with us, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңWell, well, my dear Edmond,ГўВҖВқ continued the owner, ГўВҖВңdonГўВҖВҷt let me detain
you. You have managed my affairs so well that I ought to allow you all
the time you require for your own. Do you want any money?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, sir; I have all my pay to takeГўВҖВ”nearly three monthsГўВҖВҷ wages.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are a careful fellow, Edmond.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSay I have a poor father, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, I know how good a son you are, so now hasten away to see
your father. I have a son too, and I should be very wroth with those
who detained him from me after a three monthsГўВҖВҷ voyage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I have your leave, sir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, if you have nothing more to say to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNothing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCaptain Leclere did not, before he died, give you a letter for me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe was unable to write, sir. But that reminds me that I must ask your
leave of absence for some days.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo get married?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, first, and then to go to Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery good; have what time you require, DantГғВЁs. It will take quite six
weeks to unload the cargo, and we cannot get you ready for sea until
three months after that; only be back again in three months, for the
_Pharaon_,ГўВҖВқ added the owner, patting the young sailor on the back,
ГўВҖВңcannot sail without her captain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWithout her captain!ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs, his eyes sparkling with animation;
ГўВҖВңpray mind what you say, for you are touching on the most secret wishes
of my heart. Is it really your intention to make me captain of the
_Pharaon_?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf I were sole owner weГўВҖВҷd shake hands on it now, my dear DantГғВЁs, and
call it settled; but I have a partner, and you know the Italian
proverbГўВҖВ”_Chi ha compagno ha padrone_ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳHe who has a partner has a
master.ГўВҖВҷ But the thing is at least half done, as you have one out of
two votes. Rely on me to procure you the other; I will do my best.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, M. Morrel,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the young seaman, with tears in his eyes,
and grasping the ownerГўВҖВҷs hand, ГўВҖВңM. Morrel, I thank you in the name of
my father and of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs all right, Edmond. ThereГўВҖВҷs a providence that watches over the
deserving. Go to your father; go and see MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, and afterwards come
to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall I row you ashore?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, thank you; I shall remain and look over the accounts with
Danglars. Have you been satisfied with him this voyage?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is according to the sense you attach to the question, sir. Do you
mean is he a good comrade? No, for I think he never liked me since the
day when I was silly enough, after a little quarrel we had, to propose
to him to stop for ten minutes at the island of Monte Cristo to settle
the disputeГўВҖВ”a proposition which I was wrong to suggest, and he quite
right to refuse. If you mean as responsible agent when you ask me the
question, I believe there is nothing to say against him, and that you
will be content with the way in which he has performed his duty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut tell me, DantГғВЁs, if you had command of the _Pharaon_ should you be
glad to see Danglars remain?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCaptain or mate, M. Morrel, I shall always have the greatest respect
for those who possess the ownersГўВҖВҷ confidence.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs right, thatГўВҖВҷs right, DantГғВЁs! I see you are a thoroughly good
fellow, and will detain you no longer. Go, for I see how impatient you
are.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I have leave?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo, I tell you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMay I have the use of your skiff?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, for the present, M. Morrel, farewell, and a thousand thanks!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hope soon to see you again, my dear Edmond. Good luck to you.ГўВҖВқ

The young sailor jumped into the skiff, and sat down in the stern
sheets, with the order that he be put ashore at La CanebiГғВЁre. The two
oarsmen bent to their work, and the little boat glided away as rapidly
as possible in the midst of the thousand vessels which choke up the
narrow way which leads between the two rows of ships from the mouth of
the harbor to the Quai dГўВҖВҷOrlГғВ©ans.

The shipowner, smiling, followed him with his eyes until he saw him
spring out on the quay and disappear in the midst of the throng, which
from five oГўВҖВҷclock in the morning until nine oГўВҖВҷclock at night, swarms in
the famous street of La CanebiГғВЁre,ГўВҖВ”a street of which the modern
PhocГғВ©ens are so proud that they say with all the gravity in the world,
and with that accent which gives so much character to what is said, ГўВҖВңIf
Paris had La CanebiГғВЁre, Paris would be a second Marseilles.ГўВҖВқ On turning
round the owner saw Danglars behind him, apparently awaiting orders,
but in reality also watching the young sailor,ГўВҖВ”but there was a great
difference in the expression of the two men who thus followed the
movements of Edmond DantГғВЁs.



 Chapter 2. Father and Son

We will leave Danglars struggling with the demon of hatred, and
endeavoring to insinuate in the ear of the shipowner some evil
suspicions against his comrade, and follow DantГғВЁs, who, after having
traversed La CanebiГғВЁre, took the Rue de Noailles, and entering a small
house, on the left of the AllГғВ©es de Meilhan, rapidly ascended four
flights of a dark staircase, holding the baluster with one hand, while
with the other he repressed the beatings of his heart, and paused
before a half-open door, from which he could see the whole of a small
room.

This room was occupied by DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ father. The news of the arrival of
the _Pharaon_ had not yet reached the old man, who, mounted on a chair,
was amusing himself by training with trembling hand the nasturtiums and
sprays of clematis that clambered over the trellis at his window.
Suddenly, he felt an arm thrown around his body, and a well-known voice
behind him exclaimed, ГўВҖВңFatherГўВҖВ”dear father!ГўВҖВқ

The old man uttered a cry, and turned round; then, seeing his son, he
fell into his arms, pale and trembling.

ГўВҖВңWhat ails you, my dearest father? Are you ill?ГўВҖВқ inquired the young
man, much alarmed.

ГўВҖВңNo, no, my dear EdmondГўВҖВ”my boyГўВҖВ”my son!ГўВҖВ”no; but I did not expect you;
and joy, the surprise of seeing you so suddenlyГўВҖВ”Ah, I feel as if I were
going to die.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, come, cheer up, my dear father! ГўВҖВҷTis IГўВҖВ”really I! They say joy
never hurts, and so I came to you without any warning. Come now, do
smile, instead of looking at me so solemnly. Here I am back again, and
we are going to be happy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, my boy, so we willГўВҖВ”so we will,ГўВҖВқ replied the old man; ГўВҖВңbut
how shall we be happy? Shall you never leave me again? Come, tell me
all the good fortune that has befallen you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGod forgive me,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңfor rejoicing at happiness
derived from the misery of others, but, Heaven knows, I did not seek
this good fortune; it has happened, and I really cannot pretend to
lament it. The good Captain Leclere is dead, father, and it is probable
that, with the aid of M. Morrel, I shall have his place. Do you
understand, father? Only imagine me a captain at twenty, with a hundred
louis pay, and a share in the profits! Is this not more than a poor
sailor like me could have hoped for?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, my dear boy,ГўВҖВқ replied the old man, ГўВҖВңit is very fortunate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, with the first money I touch, I mean you to have a small
house, with a garden in which to plant clematis, nasturtiums, and
honeysuckle. But what ails you, father? Are you not well?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҷTis nothing, nothing; it will soon pass awayГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”and as he said so the
old manГўВҖВҷs strength failed him, and he fell backwards.

ГўВҖВңCome, come,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңa glass of wine, father, will revive
you. Where do you keep your wine?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no; thanks. You need not look for it; I do not want it,ГўВҖВқ said the
old man.

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, father, tell me where it is,ГўВҖВқ and he opened two or three
cupboards.

ГўВҖВңIt is no use,ГўВҖВқ said the old man, ГўВҖВңthere is no wine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat, no wine?ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, turning pale, and looking alternately at
the hollow cheeks of the old man and the empty cupboards. ГўВҖВңWhat, no
wine? Have you wanted money, father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI want nothing now that I have you,ГўВҖВқ said the old man.

ГўВҖВңYet,ГўВҖВқ stammered DantГғВЁs, wiping the perspiration from his brow,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңyet I
gave you two hundred francs when I left, three months ago.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, Edmond, that is true, but you forgot at that time a little
debt to our neighbor, Caderousse. He reminded me of it, telling me if I
did not pay for you, he would be paid by M. Morrel; and so, you see,
lest he might do you an injuryГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, I paid him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңit was a hundred and forty francs I owed
Caderousse.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ stammered the old man.

ГўВҖВңAnd you paid him out of the two hundred francs I left you?ГўВҖВқ

The old man nodded.

ГўВҖВңSo that you have lived for three months on sixty francs,ГўВҖВқ muttered
Edmond.

ГўВҖВңYou know how little I require,ГўВҖВқ said the old man.

ГўВҖВңHeaven pardon me,ГўВҖВқ cried Edmond, falling on his knees before his
father.

ГўВҖВңWhat are you doing?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have wounded me to the heart.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mind it, for I see you once more,ГўВҖВқ said the old man; ГўВҖВңand now
itГўВҖВҷs all overГўВҖВ”everything is all right again.ГўВҖВқ

0035m



ГўВҖВңYes, here I am,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңwith a promising future and a
little money. Here, father, here!ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңtake thisГўВҖВ”take it, and
send for something immediately.ГўВҖВқ And he emptied his pockets on the
table, the contents consisting of a dozen gold pieces, five or six
five-franc pieces, and some smaller coin. The countenance of old DantГғВЁs
brightened.

ГўВҖВңWhom does this belong to?ГўВҖВқ he inquired.

ГўВҖВңTo me, to you, to us! Take it; buy some provisions; be happy, and
tomorrow we shall have more.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGently, gently,ГўВҖВқ said the old man, with a smile; ГўВҖВңand by your leave I
will use your purse moderately, for they would say, if they saw me buy
too many things at a time, that I had been obliged to await your
return, in order to be able to purchase them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo as you please; but, first of all, pray have a servant, father. I
will not have you left alone so long. I have some smuggled coffee and
most capital tobacco, in a small chest in the hold, which you shall
have tomorrow. But, hush, here comes somebody.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҷTis Caderousse, who has heard of your arrival, and no doubt comes to
congratulate you on your fortunate return.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, lips that say one thing, while the heart thinks another,ГўВҖВқ murmured
Edmond. ГўВҖВңBut, never mind, he is a neighbor who has done us a service on
a time, so heГўВҖВҷs welcome.ГўВҖВқ

As Edmond paused, the black and bearded head of Caderousse appeared at
the door. He was a man of twenty-five or six, and held a piece of
cloth, which, being a tailor, he was about to make into a coat-lining.

ГўВҖВңWhat, is it you, Edmond, back again?ГўВҖВқ said he, with a broad
Marseillaise accent, and a grin that displayed his ivory-white teeth.

ГўВҖВңYes, as you see, neighbor Caderousse; and ready to be agreeable to you
in any and every way,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs, but ill-concealing his coldness
under this cloak of civility.

ГўВҖВңThanksГўВҖВ”thanks; but, fortunately, I do not want for anything; and it
chances that at times there are others who have need of me.ГўВҖВқ DantГғВЁs
made a gesture. ГўВҖВңI do not allude to you, my boy. No!ГўВҖВ”no! I lent you
money, and you returned it; thatГўВҖВҷs like good neighbors, and we are
quits.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe are never quits with those who oblige us,ГўВҖВқ was DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ reply; ГўВҖВңfor
when we do not owe them money, we owe them gratitude.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhatГўВҖВҷs the use of mentioning that? What is done is done. Let us talk
of your happy return, my boy. I had gone on the quay to match a piece
of mulberry cloth, when I met friend Danglars. ГўВҖВҳYou at
Marseilles?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳYes,ГўВҖВҷ says he.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI thought you were at Smyrna.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳI was; but am now back again.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳAnd where is the dear boy, our little Edmond?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWhy, with his father, no doubt,ГўВҖВҷ replied Danglars. And so I came,ГўВҖВқ
added Caderousse, ГўВҖВңas fast as I could to have the pleasure of shaking
hands with a friend.ГўВҖВқ

0037m



ГўВҖВңWorthy Caderousse!ГўВҖВқ said the old man, ГўВҖВңhe is so much attached to us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, to be sure I am. I love and esteem you, because honest folks are
so rare. But it seems you have come back rich, my boy,ГўВҖВқ continued the
tailor, looking askance at the handful of gold and silver which DantГғВЁs
had thrown on the table.

The young man remarked the greedy glance which shone in the dark eyes
of his neighbor. ГўВҖВңEh,ГўВҖВқ he said, negligently, ГўВҖВңthis money is not mine. I
was expressing to my father my fears that he had wanted many things in
my absence, and to convince me he emptied his purse on the table. Come,
fatherГўВҖВқ added DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңput this money back in your boxГўВҖВ”unless neighbor
Caderousse wants anything, and in that case it is at his service.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, my boy, no,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВңI am not in any want, thank God, my
living is suited to my means. Keep your moneyГўВҖВ”keep it, I say;ГўВҖВ”one never
has too much;ГўВҖВ”but, at the same time, my boy, I am as much obliged by
your offer as if I took advantage of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was offered with good will,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңNo doubt, my boy; no doubt. Well, you stand well with M. Morrel I
hear,ГўВҖВ”you insinuating dog, you!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. Morrel has always been exceedingly kind to me,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңThen you were wrong to refuse to dine with him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat, did you refuse to dine with him?ГўВҖВқ said old DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңand did he
invite you to dine?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, my dear father,ГўВҖВқ replied Edmond, smiling at his fatherГўВҖВҷs
astonishment at the excessive honor paid to his son.

ГўВҖВңAnd why did you refuse, my son?ГўВҖВқ inquired the old man.

ГўВҖВңThat I might the sooner see you again, my dear father,ГўВҖВқ replied the
young man. ГўВҖВңI was most anxious to see you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut it must have vexed M. Morrel, good, worthy man,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse.
ГўВҖВңAnd when you are looking forward to be captain, it was wrong to annoy
the owner.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut I explained to him the cause of my refusal,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңand
I hope he fully understood it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, but to be captain one must do a little flattery to oneГўВҖВҷs
patrons.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hope to be captain without that,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңSo much the betterГўВҖВ”so much the better! Nothing will give greater
pleasure to all your old friends; and I know one down there behind the
Saint Nicolas citadel who will not be sorry to hear it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ said the old man.

ГўВҖВңYes, my dear father, and with your permission, now I have seen you,
and know you are well and have all you require, I will ask your consent
to go and pay a visit to the Catalans.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo, my dear boy,ГўВҖВқ said old DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңand Heaven bless you in your wife,
as it has blessed me in my son!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHis wife!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңwhy, how fast you go on, father DantГғВЁs;
she is not his wife yet, as it seems to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, but according to all probability she soon will be,ГўВҖВқ replied
Edmond.

ГўВҖВңYesГўВҖВ”yes,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңbut you were right to return as soon as
possible, my boy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd why?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause MercГғВ©dГғВЁs is a very fine girl, and fine girls never lack
followers; she particularly has them by dozens.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally?ГўВҖВқ answered Edmond, with a smile which had in it traces of
slight uneasiness.

0039m



ГўВҖВңAh, yes,ГўВҖВқ continued Caderousse, ГўВҖВңand capital offers, too; but you
know, you will be captain, and who could refuse you then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMeaning to say,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs, with a smile which but ill-concealed
his trouble, ГўВҖВңthat if I were not a captainГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEhГўВҖВ”eh!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, shaking his head.

ГўВҖВңCome, come,ГўВҖВқ said the sailor, ГўВҖВңI have a better opinion than you of
women in general, and of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs in particular; and I am certain that,
captain or not, she will remain ever faithful to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo much the betterГўВҖВ”so much the better,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВңWhen one is
going to be married, there is nothing like implicit confidence; but
never mind that, my boy,ГўВҖВ”go and announce your arrival, and let her know
all your hopes and prospects.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will go directly,ГўВҖВқ was EdmondГўВҖВҷs reply; and, embracing his father,
and nodding to Caderousse, he left the apartment.

Caderousse lingered for a moment, then taking leave of old DantГғВЁs, he
went downstairs to rejoin Danglars, who awaited him at the corner of
the Rue Senac.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңdid you see him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have just left him,ГўВҖВқ answered Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңDid he allude to his hope of being captain?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe spoke of it as a thing already decided.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed!ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңhe is in too much hurry, it appears to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, it seems M. Morrel has promised him the thing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that he is quite elated about it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, yes, he is actually insolent over the matterГўВҖВ”has already offered
me his patronage, as if he were a grand personage, and proffered me a
loan of money, as though he were a banker.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich you refused?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMost assuredly; although I might easily have accepted it, for it was I
who put into his hands the first silver he ever earned; but now M.
DantГғВЁs has no longer any occasion for assistanceГўВҖВ”he is about to become
a captain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPooh!ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңhe is not one yet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi!_ it will be as well if he is not,ГўВҖВқ answered Caderousse; ГўВҖВңfor
if he should be, there will be really no speaking to him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf we choose,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars, ГўВҖВңhe will remain what he is; and
perhaps become even less than he is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNothingГўВҖВ”I was speaking to myself. And is he still in love with the
Catalane?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOver head and ears; but, unless I am much mistaken, there will be a
storm in that quarter.ГўВҖВқ

0041m



ГўВҖВңExplain yourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy should I?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is more important than you think, perhaps. You do not like DantГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI never like upstarts.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen tell me all you know about the Catalane.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know nothing for certain; only I have seen things which induce me to
believe, as I told you, that the future captain will find some
annoyance in the vicinity of the Vieilles Infirmeries.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat have you seen?ГўВҖВ”come, tell me!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, every time I have seen MercГғВ©dГғВЁs come into the city she has been
accompanied by a tall, strapping, black-eyed Catalan, with a red
complexion, brown skin, and fierce air, whom she calls cousin.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally; and you think this cousin pays her attentions?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI only suppose so. What else can a strapping chap of twenty-one mean
with a fine wench of seventeen?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you say that DantГғВЁs has gone to the Catalans?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe went before I came down.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLet us go the same way; we will stop at La RГғВ©serve, and we can drink a
glass of La Malgue, whilst we wait for news.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome along,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңbut you pay the score.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf course,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars; and going quickly to the designated
place, they called for a bottle of wine, and two glasses.

PГғВЁre Pamphile had seen DantГғВЁs pass not ten minutes before; and assured
that he was at the Catalans, they sat down under the budding foliage of
the planes and sycamores, in the branches of which the birds were
singing their welcome to one of the first days of spring.



 Chapter 3. The Catalans

Beyond a bare, weather-worn wall, about a hundred paces from the spot
where the two friends sat looking and listening as they drank their
wine, was the village of the Catalans. Long ago this mysterious colony
quitted Spain, and settled on the tongue of land on which it is to this
day. Whence it came no one knew, and it spoke an unknown tongue. One of
its chiefs, who understood ProvenГғВ§al, begged the commune of Marseilles
to give them this bare and barren promontory, where, like the sailors
of old, they had run their boats ashore. The request was granted; and
three months afterwards, around the twelve or fifteen small vessels
which had brought these gypsies of the sea, a small village sprang up.
This village, constructed in a singular and picturesque manner, half
Moorish, half Spanish, still remains, and is inhabited by descendants
of the first comers, who speak the language of their fathers. For three
or four centuries they have remained upon this small promontory, on
which they had settled like a flight of seabirds, without mixing with
the Marseillaise population, intermarrying, and preserving their
original customs and the costume of their mother-country as they have
preserved its language.

Our readers will follow us along the only street of this little
village, and enter with us one of the houses, which is sunburned to the
beautiful dead-leaf color peculiar to the buildings of the country, and
within coated with whitewash, like a Spanish posada. A young and
beautiful girl, with hair as black as jet, her eyes as velvety as the
gazelleГўВҖВҷs, was leaning with her back against the wainscot, rubbing in
her slender delicately moulded fingers a bunch of heath blossoms, the
flowers of which she was picking off and strewing on the floor; her
arms, bare to the elbow, brown, and modelled after those of the
Arlesian Venus, moved with a kind of restless impatience, and she
tapped the earth with her arched and supple foot, so as to display the
pure and full shape of her well-turned leg, in its red cotton, gray and
blue clocked, stocking. At three paces from her, seated in a chair
which he balanced on two legs, leaning his elbow on an old worm-eaten
table, was a tall young man of twenty, or two-and-twenty, who was
looking at her with an air in which vexation and uneasiness were
mingled. He questioned her with his eyes, but the firm and steady gaze
of the young girl controlled his look.

ГўВҖВңYou see, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңhere is Easter come round
again; tell me, is this the moment for a wedding?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have answered you a hundred times, Fernand, and really you must be
very stupid to ask me again.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, repeat it,ГўВҖВ”repeat it, I beg of you, that I may at last believe
it! Tell me for the hundredth time that you refuse my love, which had
your motherГўВҖВҷs sanction. Make me understand once for all that you are
trifling with my happiness, that my life or death are nothing to you.
Ah, to have dreamed for ten years of being your husband, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, and
to lose that hope, which was the only stay of my existence!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt least it was not I who ever encouraged you in that hope, Fernand,ГўВҖВқ
replied MercГғВ©dГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңyou cannot reproach me with the slightest coquetry.
I have always said to you, ГўВҖВҳI love you as a brother; but do not ask
from me more than sisterly affection, for my heart is anotherГўВҖВҷs.ГўВҖВҷ Is
not this true, Fernand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, that is very true, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man, ГўВҖВңYes, you
have been cruelly frank with me; but do you forget that it is among the
Catalans a sacred law to intermarry?ГўВҖВқ

0045m



ГўВҖВңYou mistake, Fernand; it is not a law, but merely a custom, and, I
pray of you, do not cite this custom in your favor. You are included in
the conscription, Fernand, and are only at liberty on sufferance,
liable at any moment to be called upon to take up arms. Once a soldier,
what would you do with me, a poor orphan, forlorn, without fortune,
with nothing but a half-ruined hut and a few ragged nets, the miserable
inheritance left by my father to my mother, and by my mother to me? She
has been dead a year, and you know, Fernand, I have subsisted almost
entirely on public charity. Sometimes you pretend I am useful to you,
and that is an excuse to share with me the produce of your fishing, and
I accept it, Fernand, because you are the son of my fatherГўВҖВҷs brother,
because we were brought up together, and still more because it would
give you so much pain if I refuse. But I feel very deeply that this
fish which I go and sell, and with the produce of which I buy the flax
I spin,ГўВҖВ”I feel very keenly, Fernand, that this is charity.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd if it were, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, poor and lone as you are, you suit me as
well as the daughter of the first shipowner or the richest banker of
Marseilles! What do such as we desire but a good wife and careful
housekeeper, and where can I look for these better than in you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFernand,ГўВҖВқ answered MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, shaking her head, ГўВҖВңa woman becomes a bad
manager, and who shall say she will remain an honest woman, when she
loves another man better than her husband? Rest content with my
friendship, for I say once more that is all I can promise, and I will
promise no more than I can bestow.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI understand,ГўВҖВқ replied Fernand, ГўВҖВңyou can endure your own wretchedness
patiently, but you are afraid to share mine. Well, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, beloved by
you, I would tempt fortune; you would bring me good luck, and I should
become rich. I could extend my occupation as a fisherman, might get a
place as clerk in a warehouse, and become in time a dealer myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou could do no such thing, Fernand; you are a soldier, and if you
remain at the Catalans it is because there is no war; so remain a
fisherman, and contented with my friendship, as I cannot give you
more.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I will do better, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs. I will be a sailor; instead of the
costume of our fathers, which you despise, I will wear a varnished hat,
a striped shirt, and a blue jacket, with an anchor on the buttons.
Would not that dress please you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean?ГўВҖВқ asked MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, with an angry glance,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңwhat do you
mean? I do not understand you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI mean, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, that you are thus harsh and cruel with me, because
you are expecting someone who is thus attired; but perhaps he whom you
await is inconstant, or if he is not, the sea is so to him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFernand,ГўВҖВқ cried MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңI believed you were good-hearted, and I was
mistaken! Fernand, you are wicked to call to your aid jealousy and the
anger of God! Yes, I will not deny it, I do await, and I do love him of
whom you speak; and, if he does not return, instead of accusing him of
the inconstancy which you insinuate, I will tell you that he died
loving me and me only.ГўВҖВқ The young girl made a gesture of rage. ГўВҖВңI
understand you, Fernand; you would be revenged on him because I do not
love you; you would cross your Catalan knife with his dirk. What end
would that answer? To lose you my friendship if he were conquered, and
see that friendship changed into hate if you were victor. Believe me,
to seek a quarrel with a man is a bad method of pleasing the woman who
loves that man. No, Fernand, you will not thus give way to evil
thoughts. Unable to have me for your wife, you will content yourself
with having me for your friend and sister; and besides,ГўВҖВқ she added, her
eyes troubled and moistened with tears, ГўВҖВңwait, wait, Fernand; you said
just now that the sea was treacherous, and he has been gone four
months, and during these four months there have been some terrible
storms.ГўВҖВқ

Fernand made no reply, nor did he attempt to check the tears which
flowed down the cheeks of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, although for each of these tears he
would have shed his heartГўВҖВҷs blood; but these tears flowed for another.
He arose, paced a while up and down the hut, and then, suddenly
stopping before MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, with his eyes glowing and his hands
clenched,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңSay, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңonce for all, is this your final
determination?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI love Edmond DantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ the young girl calmly replied, ГўВҖВңand none but
Edmond shall ever be my husband.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you will always love him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs long as I live.ГўВҖВқ

Fernand let fall his head like a defeated man, heaved a sigh that was
like a groan, and then suddenly looking her full in the face, with
clenched teeth and expanded nostrils, said,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңBut if he is deadГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf he is dead, I shall die too.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf he has forgotten youГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs!ГўВҖВқ called a joyous voice from without,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the young girl, blushing with delight, and fairly
leaping in excess of love, ГўВҖВңyou see he has not forgotten me, for here
he is!ГўВҖВқ And rushing towards the door, she opened it, saying, ГўВҖВңHere,
Edmond, here I am!ГўВҖВқ

Fernand, pale and trembling, drew back, like a traveller at the sight
of a serpent, and fell into a chair beside him. Edmond and MercГғВ©dГғВЁs
were clasped in each otherГўВҖВҷs arms. The burning Marseilles sun, which
shot into the room through the open door, covered them with a flood of
light. At first they saw nothing around them. Their intense happiness
isolated them from all the rest of the world, and they only spoke in
broken words, which are the tokens of a joy so extreme that they seem
rather the expression of sorrow. Suddenly Edmond saw the gloomy, pale,
and threatening countenance of Fernand, as it was defined in the
shadow. By a movement for which he could scarcely account to himself,
the young Catalan placed his hand on the knife at his belt.

ГўВҖВңAh, your pardon,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, frowning in his turn; ГўВҖВңI did not
perceive that there were three of us.ГўВҖВқ Then, turning to MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, he
inquired, ГўВҖВңWho is this gentleman?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne who will be your best friend, DantГғВЁs, for he is my friend, my
cousin, my brother; it is FernandГўВҖВ”the man whom, after you, Edmond, I
love the best in the world. Do you not remember him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes!ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, and without relinquishing MercГғВ©dГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ hand clasped in
one of his own, he extended the other to the Catalan with a cordial
air. But Fernand, instead of responding to this amiable gesture,
remained mute and trembling. Edmond then cast his eyes scrutinizingly
at the agitated and embarrassed MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, and then again on the gloomy
and menacing Fernand. This look told him all, and his anger waxed hot.

ГўВҖВңI did not know, when I came with such haste to you, that I was to meet
an enemy here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn enemy!ГўВҖВқ cried MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, with an angry look at her cousin. ГўВҖВңAn enemy
in my house, do you say, Edmond! If I believed that, I would place my
arm under yours and go with you to Marseilles, leaving the house to
return to it no more.ГўВҖВқ

FernandГўВҖВҷs eye darted lightning. ГўВҖВңAnd should any misfortune occur to
you, dear Edmond,ГўВҖВқ she continued with the same calmness which proved to
Fernand that the young girl had read the very innermost depths of his
sinister thought, ГўВҖВңif misfortune should occur to you, I would ascend
the highest point of the Cape de Morgiou and cast myself headlong from
it.ГўВҖВқ

Fernand became deadly pale. ГўВҖВңBut you are deceived, Edmond,ГўВҖВқ she
continued. ГўВҖВңYou have no enemy hereГўВҖВ”there is no one but Fernand, my
brother, who will grasp your hand as a devoted friend.ГўВҖВқ

And at these words the young girl fixed her imperious look on the
Catalan, who, as if fascinated by it, came slowly towards Edmond, and
offered him his hand. His hatred, like a powerless though furious wave,
was broken against the strong ascendancy which MercГғВ©dГғВЁs exercised over
him. Scarcely, however, had he touched EdmondГўВҖВҷs hand when he felt he
had done all he could do, and rushed hastily out of the house.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed, running furiously and tearing his hairГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңOh, who
will deliver me from this man? WretchedГўВҖВ”wretched that I am!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHallo, Catalan! Hallo, Fernand! where are you running to?ГўВҖВқ exclaimed a
voice.

The young man stopped suddenly, looked around him, and perceived
Caderousse sitting at table with Danglars, under an arbor.

ГўВҖВңWellГўВҖВқ, said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңwhy donГўВҖВҷt you come? Are you really in such a
hurry that you have no time to pass the time of day with your friends?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңParticularly when they have still a full bottle before them,ГўВҖВқ added
Danglars. Fernand looked at them both with a stupefied air, but did not
say a word.

ГўВҖВңHe seems besotted,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, pushing Caderousse with his knee.
ГўВҖВңAre we mistaken, and is DantГғВЁs triumphant in spite of all we have
believed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, we must inquire into that,ГўВҖВқ was CaderousseГўВҖВҷs reply; and turning
towards the young man, said, ГўВҖВңWell, Catalan, canГўВҖВҷt you make up your
mind?ГўВҖВқ

Fernand wiped away the perspiration steaming from his brow, and slowly
entered the arbor, whose shade seemed to restore somewhat of calmness
to his senses, and whose coolness somewhat of refreshment to his
exhausted body.

ГўВҖВңGood-day,ГўВҖВқ said he. ГўВҖВңYou called me, didnГўВҖВҷt you?ГўВҖВқ And he fell, rather
than sat down, on one of the seats which surrounded the table.

ГўВҖВңI called you because you were running like a madman, and I was afraid
you would throw yourself into the sea,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, laughing.
ГўВҖВңWhy, when a man has friends, they are not only to offer him a glass of
wine, but, moreover, to prevent his swallowing three or four pints of
water unnecessarily!ГўВҖВқ

Fernand gave a groan, which resembled a sob, and dropped his head into
his hands, his elbows leaning on the table.

ГўВҖВңWell, Fernand, I must say,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, beginning the
conversation, with that brutality of the common people in which
curiosity destroys all diplomacy, ГўВҖВңyou look uncommonly like a rejected
lover;ГўВҖВқ and he burst into a hoarse laugh.

ГўВҖВңBah!ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңa lad of his make was not born to be unhappy in
love. You are laughing at him, Caderousse.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ he replied, ГўВҖВңonly hark how he sighs! Come, come, Fernand,ГўВҖВқ said
Caderousse, ГўВҖВңhold up your head, and answer us. ItГўВҖВҷs not polite not to
reply to friends who ask news of your health.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy health is well enough,ГўВҖВқ said Fernand, clenching his hands without
raising his head.

ГўВҖВңAh, you see, Danglars,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, winking at his friend, ГўВҖВңthis
is how it is; Fernand, whom you see here, is a good and brave Catalan,
one of the best fishermen in Marseilles, and he is in love with a very
fine girl, named MercГғВ©dГғВЁs; but it appears, unfortunately, that the fine
girl is in love with the mate of the _Pharaon_; and as the _Pharaon_
arrived todayГўВҖВ”why, you understand!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; I do not understand,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВңPoor Fernand has been dismissed,ГўВҖВқ continued Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңWell, and what then?ГўВҖВқ said Fernand, lifting up his head, and looking
at Caderousse like a man who looks for someone on whom to vent his
anger; ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs is not accountable to any person, is she? Is she not
free to love whomsoever she will?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, if you take it in that sense,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңit is another
thing. But I thought you were a Catalan, and they told me the Catalans
were not men to allow themselves to be supplanted by a rival. It was
even told me that Fernand, especially, was terrible in his vengeance.ГўВҖВқ

Fernand smiled piteously. ГўВҖВңA lover is never terrible,ГўВҖВқ he said.

ГўВҖВңPoor fellow!ГўВҖВқ remarked Danglars, affecting to pity the young man from
the bottom of his heart. ГўВҖВңWhy, you see, he did not expect to see DantГғВЁs
return so suddenlyГўВҖВ”he thought he was dead, perhaps; or perchance
faithless! These things always come on us more severely when they come
suddenly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, _ma foi_, under any circumstances!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, who drank as
he spoke, and on whom the fumes of the wine began to take
effect,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңunder any circumstances Fernand is not the only person put out
by the fortunate arrival of DantГғВЁs; is he, Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, you are rightГўВҖВ”and I should say that would bring him ill-luck.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, never mind,ГўВҖВқ answered Caderousse, pouring out a glass of wine
for Fernand, and filling his own for the eighth or ninth time, while
Danglars had merely sipped his. ГўВҖВңNever mindГўВҖВ”in the meantime he marries
MercГғВ©dГғВЁsГўВҖВ”the lovely MercГғВ©dГғВЁsГўВҖВ”at least he returns to do that.ГўВҖВқ

During this time Danglars fixed his piercing glance on the young man,
on whose heart CaderousseГўВҖВҷs words fell like molten lead.

ГўВҖВңAnd when is the wedding to be?ГўВҖВқ he asked.

ГўВҖВңOh, it is not yet fixed!ГўВҖВқ murmured Fernand.

ГўВҖВңNo, but it will be,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңas surely as DantГғВЁs will be
captain of the _Pharaon_ГўВҖВ”eh, Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

Danglars shuddered at this unexpected attack, and turned to Caderousse,
whose countenance he scrutinized, to try and detect whether the blow
was premeditated; but he read nothing but envy in a countenance already
rendered brutal and stupid by drunkenness.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said he, filling the glasses, ГўВҖВңlet us drink to Captain Edmond
DantГғВЁs, husband of the beautiful Catalane!ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse raised his glass to his mouth with unsteady hand, and
swallowed the contents at a gulp. Fernand dashed his on the ground.

ГўВҖВңEh, eh, eh!ГўВҖВқ stammered Caderousse. ГўВҖВңWhat do I see down there by the
wall, in the direction of the Catalans? Look, Fernand, your eyes are
better than mine. I believe I see double. You know wine is a deceiver;
but I should say it was two lovers walking side by side, and hand in
hand. Heaven forgive me, they do not know that we can see them, and
they are actually embracing!ГўВҖВқ

Danglars did not lose one pang that Fernand endured.

ГўВҖВңDo you know them, Fernand?ГўВҖВқ he said.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ was the reply, in a low voice. ГўВҖВңIt is Edmond and MercГғВ©dГғВЁs!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, see there, now!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңand I did not recognize them!
Hallo, DantГғВЁs! hello, lovely damsel! Come this way, and let us know
when the wedding is to be, for Fernand here is so obstinate he will not
tell us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHold your tongue, will you?ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, pretending to restrain
Caderousse, who, with the tenacity of drunkards, leaned out of the
arbor. ГўВҖВңTry to stand upright, and let the lovers make love without
interruption. See, look at Fernand, and follow his example; he is
well-behaved!ГўВҖВқ

0051m



Fernand, probably excited beyond bearing, pricked by Danglars, as the
bull is by the bandilleros, was about to rush out; for he had risen
from his seat, and seemed to be collecting himself to dash headlong
upon his rival, when MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, smiling and graceful, lifted up her
lovely head, and looked at them with her clear and bright eyes. At this
Fernand recollected her threat of dying if Edmond died, and dropped
again heavily on his seat. Danglars looked at the two men, one after
the other, the one brutalized by liquor, the other overwhelmed with
love.

ГўВҖВңI shall get nothing from these fools,ГўВҖВқ he muttered; ГўВҖВңand I am very
much afraid of being here between a drunkard and a coward. HereГўВҖВҷs an
envious fellow making himself boozy on wine when he ought to be nursing
his wrath, and here is a fool who sees the woman he loves stolen from
under his nose and takes on like a big baby. Yet this Catalan has eyes
that glisten like those of the vengeful Spaniards, Sicilians, and
Calabrians, and the other has fists big enough to crush an ox at one
blow. Unquestionably, EdmondГўВҖВҷs star is in the ascendant, and he will
marry the splendid girlГўВҖВ”he will be captain, too, and laugh at us all,
unlessГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”a sinister smile passed over DanglarsГўВҖВҷ lipsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңunless I take a
hand in the affair,ГўВҖВқ he added.

ГўВҖВңHallo!ГўВҖВқ continued Caderousse, half-rising, and with his fist on the
table, ГўВҖВңhallo, Edmond! do you not see your friends, or are you too
proud to speak to them?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, my dear fellow!ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңI am not proud, but I am happy,
and happiness blinds, I think, more than pride.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, very well, thatГўВҖВҷs an explanation!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВңHow do you
do, Madame DantГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs courtesied gravely, and saidГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңThat is not my name, and in my
country it bodes ill fortune, they say, to call a young girl by the
name of her betrothed before he becomes her husband. So call me
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, if you please.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe must excuse our worthy neighbor, Caderousse,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңhe is
so easily mistaken.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo, then, the wedding is to take place immediately, M. DantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ said
Danglars, bowing to the young couple.

ГўВҖВңAs soon as possible, M. Danglars; today all preliminaries will be
arranged at my fatherГўВҖВҷs, and tomorrow, or next day at latest, the
wedding festival here at La RГғВ©serve. My friends will be there, I hope;
that is to say, you are invited, M. Danglars, and you, Caderousse.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd Fernand,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse with a chuckle; ГўВҖВңFernand, too, is
invited!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy wifeГўВҖВҷs brother is my brother,ГўВҖВқ said Edmond; ГўВҖВңand we, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs and
I, should be very sorry if he were absent at such a time.ГўВҖВқ

Fernand opened his mouth to reply, but his voice died on his lips, and
he could not utter a word.

ГўВҖВңToday the preliminaries, tomorrow or next day the ceremony! You are in
a hurry, captain!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDanglars,ГўВҖВқ said Edmond, smiling, ГўВҖВңI will say to you as MercГғВ©dГғВЁs said
just now to Caderousse, ГўВҖВҳDo not give me a title which does not belong
to meГўВҖВҷ; that may bring me bad luck.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour pardon,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars, ГўВҖВңI merely said you seemed in a hurry,
and we have lots of time; the _Pharaon_ cannot be under weigh again in
less than three months.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe are always in a hurry to be happy, M. Danglars; for when we have
suffered a long time, we have great difficulty in believing in good
fortune. But it is not selfishness alone that makes me thus in haste; I
must go to Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, really?ГўВҖВ”to Paris! and will it be the first time you have ever been
there, DantГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you business there?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot of my own; the last commission of poor Captain Leclere; you know
to what I allude, DanglarsГўВҖВ”it is sacred. Besides, I shall only take the
time to go and return.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, I understand,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, and then in a low tone, he
added, ГўВҖВңTo Paris, no doubt to deliver the letter which the grand
marshal gave him. Ah, this letter gives me an ideaГўВҖВ”a capital idea! Ah;
DantГғВЁs, my friend, you are not yet registered number one on board the
good ship _Pharaon_;ГўВҖВқ then turning towards Edmond, who was walking
away, ГўВҖВңA pleasant journey,ГўВҖВқ he cried.

ГўВҖВңThank you,ГўВҖВқ said Edmond with a friendly nod, and the two lovers
continued on their way, as calm and joyous as if they were the very
elect of heaven.



 Chapter 4. Conspiracy

Danglars followed Edmond and MercГғВ©dГғВЁs with his eyes until the two
lovers disappeared behind one of the angles of Fort Saint Nicolas;
then, turning round, he perceived Fernand, who had fallen, pale and
trembling, into his chair, while Caderousse stammered out the words of
a drinking-song.

ГўВҖВңWell, my dear sir,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars to Fernand, ГўВҖВңhere is a marriage
which does not appear to make everybody happy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt drives me to despair,ГўВҖВқ said Fernand.

ГўВҖВңDo you, then, love MercГғВ©dГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI adore her!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor long?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs long as I have known herГўВҖВ”always.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you sit there, tearing your hair, instead of seeking to remedy
your condition; I did not think that was the way of your people.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat would you have me do?ГўВҖВқ said Fernand.

ГўВҖВңHow do I know? Is it my affair? I am not in love with Mademoiselle
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs; but for youГўВҖВ”in the words of the gospel, seek, and you shall
find.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have found already.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI would stab the man, but the woman told me that if any misfortune
happened to her betrothed, she would kill herself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPooh! Women say those things, but never do them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do not know MercГғВ©dГғВЁs; what she threatens she will do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIdiot!ГўВҖВқ muttered Danglars; ГўВҖВңwhether she kill herself or not, what
matter, provided DantГғВЁs is not captain?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBefore MercГғВ©dГғВЁs should die,ГўВҖВқ replied Fernand, with the accents of
unshaken resolution, ГўВҖВңI would die myself!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs what I call love!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse with a voice more tipsy than
ever. ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs love, or I donГўВҖВҷt know what love is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңyou appear to me a good sort of fellow, and
hang me, I should like to help you, butГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңbut how?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear fellow,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars, ГўВҖВңyou are three parts drunk; finish
the bottle, and you will be completely so. Drink then, and do not
meddle with what we are discussing, for that requires all oneГўВҖВҷs wit and
cool judgment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВ”drunk!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңwell thatГўВҖВҷs a good one! I could drink four
more such bottles; they are no bigger than cologne flasks. PГғВЁre
Pamphile, more wine!ГўВҖВқ

And Caderousse rattled his glass upon the table.

ГўВҖВңYou were saying, sirГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ said Fernand, awaiting with great anxiety the
end of this interrupted remark.

ГўВҖВңWhat was I saying? I forget. This drunken Caderousse has made me lose
the thread of my sentence.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDrunk, if you like; so much the worse for those who fear wine, for it
is because they have bad thoughts which they are afraid the liquor will
extract from their hearts;ГўВҖВқ and Caderousse began to sing the two last
lines of a song very popular at the time:

ГўВҖВҳTous les mГғВ©chants sont buveurs dГўВҖВҷeau;

CГўВҖВҷest bien prouvГғВ© par le dГғВ©luge.ГўВҖВҷ1


ГўВҖВңYou said, sir, you would like to help me, butГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but I added, to help you it would be sufficient that DantГғВЁs did
not marry her you love; and the marriage may easily be thwarted,
methinks, and yet DantГғВЁs need not die.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDeath alone can separate them,ГўВҖВқ remarked Fernand.

ГўВҖВңYou talk like a noodle, my friend,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңand here is
Danglars, who is a wide-awake, clever, deep fellow, who will prove to
you that you are wrong. Prove it, Danglars. I have answered for you.
Say there is no need why DantГғВЁs should die; it would, indeed, be a pity
he should. DantГғВЁs is a good fellow; I like DantГғВЁs. DantГғВЁs, your
health.ГўВҖВқ

Fernand rose impatiently. ГўВҖВңLet him run on,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, restraining
the young man; ГўВҖВңdrunk as he is, he is not much out in what he says.
Absence severs as well as death, and if the walls of a prison were
between Edmond and MercГғВ©dГғВЁs they would be as effectually separated as
if he lay under a tombstone.ГўВҖВқ

0056m



ГўВҖВңYes; but one gets out of prison,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, who, with what
sense was left him, listened eagerly to the conversation, ГўВҖВңand when one
gets out and oneГўВҖВҷs name is Edmond DantГғВЁs, one seeks revengeГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat matters that?ГўВҖВқ muttered Fernand.

ГўВҖВңAnd why, I should like to know,ГўВҖВқ persisted Caderousse, ГўВҖВңshould they
put DantГғВЁs in prison? he has neither robbed, nor killed, nor murdered.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHold your tongue!ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВңI wonГўВҖВҷt hold my tongue!ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse; ГўВҖВңI say I want to know why
they should put DantГғВЁs in prison; I like DantГғВЁs; DantГғВЁs, your health!ГўВҖВқ
and he swallowed another glass of wine.

0057m



Danglars saw in the muddled look of the tailor the progress of his
intoxication, and turning towards Fernand, said, ГўВҖВңWell, you understand
there is no need to kill him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly not, if, as you said just now, you have the means of having
DantГғВЁs arrested. Have you that means?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is to be found for the searching. But why should I meddle in the
matter? it is no affair of mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know not why you meddle,ГўВҖВқ said Fernand, seizing his arm; ГўВҖВңbut this I
know, you have some motive of personal hatred against DantГғВЁs, for he
who himself hates is never mistaken in the sentiments of others.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI! motives of hatred against DantГғВЁs? None, on my word! I saw you were
unhappy, and your unhappiness interested me; thatГўВҖВҷs all; but since you
believe I act for my own account, adieu, my dear friend, get out of the
affair as best you may;ГўВҖВқ and Danglars rose as if he meant to depart.

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ said Fernand, restraining him, ГўВҖВңstay! It is of very little
consequence to me at the end of the matter whether you have any angry
feeling or not against DantГғВЁs. I hate him! I confess it openly. Do you
find the means, I will execute it, provided it is not to kill the man,
for MercГғВ©dГғВЁs has declared she will kill herself if DantГғВЁs is killed.ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse, who had let his head drop on the table, now raised it, and
looking at Fernand with his dull and fishy eyes, he said, ГўВҖВңKill DantГғВЁs!
who talks of killing DantГғВЁs? I wonГўВҖВҷt have him killedГўВҖВ”I wonГўВҖВҷt! HeГўВҖВҷs my
friend, and this morning offered to share his money with me, as I
shared mine with him. I wonГўВҖВҷt have DantГғВЁs killedГўВҖВ”I wonГўВҖВҷt!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd who has said a word about killing him, muddlehead?ГўВҖВқ replied
Danglars. ГўВҖВңWe were merely joking; drink to his health,ГўВҖВқ he added,
filling CaderousseГўВҖВҷs glass, ГўВҖВңand do not interfere with us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ good health!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, emptying his glass,
ГўВҖВңhereГўВҖВҷs to his health! his healthГўВҖВ”hurrah!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut the meansГўВҖВ”the means?ГўВҖВқ said Fernand.

ГўВҖВңHave you not hit upon any?ГўВҖВқ asked Danglars.

ГўВҖВңNo!ГўВҖВ”you undertook to do so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars; ГўВҖВңthe French have the superiority over the
Spaniards, that the Spaniards ruminate, while the French invent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you invent, then,ГўВҖВқ said Fernand impatiently.

ГўВҖВңWaiter,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңpen, ink, and paper.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPen, ink, and paper,ГўВҖВқ muttered Fernand.

ГўВҖВңYes; I am a supercargo; pen, ink, and paper are my tools, and without
my tools I am fit for nothing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPen, ink, and paper, then,ГўВҖВқ called Fernand loudly.

ГўВҖВңThereГўВҖВҷs what you want on that table,ГўВҖВқ said the waiter.

ГўВҖВңBring them here.ГўВҖВқ The waiter did as he was desired.

0059m



ГўВҖВңWhen one thinks,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, letting his hand drop on the paper,
ГўВҖВңthere is here wherewithal to kill a man more sure than if we waited at
the corner of a wood to assassinate him! I have always had more dread
of a pen, a bottle of ink, and a sheet of paper, than of a sword or
pistol.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe fellow is not so drunk as he appears to be,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars. ГўВҖВңGive
him some more wine, Fernand.ГўВҖВқ Fernand filled CaderousseГўВҖВҷs glass, who,
like the confirmed toper he was, lifted his hand from the paper and
seized the glass.

The Catalan watched him until Caderousse, almost overcome by this fresh
assault on his senses, rested, or rather dropped, his glass upon the
table.

ГўВҖВңWell!ГўВҖВқ resumed the Catalan, as he saw the final glimmer of
CaderousseГўВҖВҷs reason vanishing before the last glass of wine.

ГўВҖВңWell, then, I should say, for instance,ГўВҖВқ resumed Danglars, ГўВҖВңthat if
after a voyage such as DantГғВЁs has just made, in which he touched at the
Island of Elba, someone were to denounce him to the kingГўВҖВҷs procureur as
a Bonapartist agentГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will denounce him!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the young man hastily.

ГўВҖВңYes, but they will make you then sign your declaration, and confront
you with him you have denounced; I will supply you with the means of
supporting your accusation, for I know the fact well. But DantГғВЁs cannot
remain forever in prison, and one day or other he will leave it, and
the day when he comes out, woe betide him who was the cause of his
incarceration!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I should wish nothing better than that he would come and seek a
quarrel with me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, and MercГғВ©dГғВЁs! MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, who will detest you if you have only the
misfortune to scratch the skin of her dearly beloved Edmond!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue!ГўВҖВқ said Fernand.

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ continued Danglars; ГўВҖВңif we resolve on such a step, it would
be much better to take, as I now do, this pen, dip it into this ink,
and write with the left hand (that the writing may not be recognized)
the denunciation we propose.ГўВҖВқ And Danglars, uniting practice with
theory, wrote with his left hand, and in a writing reversed from his
usual style, and totally unlike it, the following lines, which he
handed to Fernand, and which Fernand read in an undertone:

ГўВҖВңThe honorable, the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney, is informed by a friend of the
throne and religion, that one Edmond DantГғВЁs, mate of the ship
_Pharaon_, arrived this morning from Smyrna, after having touched at
Naples and Porto-Ferrajo, has been intrusted by Murat with a letter for
the usurper, and by the usurper with a letter for the Bonapartist
committee in Paris. Proof of this crime will be found on arresting him,
for the letter will be found upon him, or at his fatherГўВҖВҷs, or in his
cabin on board the _Pharaon_.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery good,ГўВҖВқ resumed Danglars; ГўВҖВңnow your revenge looks like common
sense, for in no way can it revert to yourself, and the matter will
thus work its own way; there is nothing to do now but fold the letter
as I am doing, and write upon it, ГўВҖВҳTo the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney,ГўВҖВҷ and thatГўВҖВҷs
all settled.ГўВҖВқ And Danglars wrote the address as he spoke.

ГўВҖВңYes, and thatГўВҖВҷs all settled!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Caderousse, who, by a last
effort of intellect, had followed the reading of the letter, and
instinctively comprehended all the misery which such a denunciation
must entail. ГўВҖВңYes, and thatГўВҖВҷs all settled; only it will be an infamous
shame;ГўВҖВқ and he stretched out his hand to reach the letter.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, taking it from beyond his reach; ГўВҖВңand as what I
say and do is merely in jest, and I, amongst the first and foremost,
should be sorry if anything happened to DantГғВЁsГўВҖВ”the worthy DantГғВЁsГўВҖВ”look
here!ГўВҖВқ And taking the letter, he squeezed it up in his hands and threw
it into a corner of the arbor.

ГўВҖВңAll right!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВңDantГғВЁs is my friend, and I wonГўВҖВҷt have
him ill-used.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd who thinks of using him ill? Certainly neither I nor Fernand,ГўВҖВқ
said Danglars, rising and looking at the young man, who still remained
seated, but whose eye was fixed on the denunciatory sheet of paper
flung into the corner.

ГўВҖВңIn this case,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, ГўВҖВңletГўВҖВҷs have some more wine. I wish
to drink to the health of Edmond and the lovely MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have had too much already, drunkard,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңand if you
continue, you will be compelled to sleep here, because unable to stand
on your legs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, rising with all the offended dignity of a drunken
man, ГўВҖВңI canГўВҖВҷt keep on my legs? Why, IГўВҖВҷll wager I can go up into the
belfry of the Accoules, and without staggering, too!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDone!ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВҷll take your bet; but tomorrowГўВҖВ”today it is
time to return. Give me your arm, and let us go.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well, let us go,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңbut I donГўВҖВҷt want your arm at
all. Come, Fernand, wonГўВҖВҷt you return to Marseilles with us?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Fernand; ГўВҖВңI shall return to the Catalans.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYouГўВҖВҷre wrong. Come with us to MarseillesГўВҖВ”come along.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will not.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean? you will not? Well, just as you like, my prince;
thereГўВҖВҷs liberty for all the world. Come along, Danglars, and let the
young gentleman return to the Catalans if he chooses.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars took advantage of CaderousseГўВҖВҷs temper at the moment, to take
him off towards Marseilles by the Porte Saint-Victor, staggering as he
went.

When they had advanced about twenty yards, Danglars looked back and saw
Fernand stoop, pick up the crumpled paper, and putting it into his
pocket then rush out of the arbor towards Pillon.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңwhy, what a lie he told! He said he was going
to the Catalans, and he is going to the city. Hallo, Fernand! You are
coming, my boy!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, you donГўВҖВҷt see straight,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңheГўВҖВҷs gone right by the
road to the Vieilles Infirmeries.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңI should have sworn that he turned to the
rightГўВҖВ”how treacherous wine is!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, come,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars to himself, ГўВҖВңnow the thing is at work and
it will effect its purpose unassisted.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 5. The Marriage Feast

The morningГўВҖВҷs sun rose clear and resplendent, touching the foamy waves
into a network of ruby-tinted light.

The feast had been made ready on the second floor at La RГғВ©serve, with
whose arbor the reader is already familiar. The apartment destined for
the purpose was spacious and lighted by a number of windows, over each
of which was written in golden letters for some inexplicable reason the
name of one of the principal cities of France; beneath these windows a
wooden balcony extended the entire length of the house. And although
the entertainment was fixed for twelve oГўВҖВҷclock, an hour previous to
that time the balcony was filled with impatient and expectant guests,
consisting of the favored part of the crew of the _Pharaon_, and other
personal friends of the bridegroom, the whole of whom had arrayed
themselves in their choicest costumes, in order to do greater honor to
the occasion.

Various rumors were afloat to the effect that the owners of the
_Pharaon_ had promised to attend the nuptial feast; but all seemed
unanimous in doubting that an act of such rare and exceeding
condescension could possibly be intended.

Danglars, however, who now made his appearance, accompanied by
Caderousse, effectually confirmed the report, stating that he had
recently conversed with M. Morrel, who had himself assured him of his
intention to dine at La RГғВ©serve.

In fact, a moment later M. Morrel appeared and was saluted with an
enthusiastic burst of applause from the crew of the _Pharaon_, who
hailed the visit of the shipowner as a sure indication that the man
whose wedding feast he thus delighted to honor would ere long be first
in command of the ship; and as DantГғВЁs was universally beloved on board
his vessel, the sailors put no restraint on their tumultuous joy at
finding that the opinion and choice of their superiors so exactly
coincided with their own.

With the entrance of M. Morrel, Danglars and Caderousse were despatched
in search of the bridegroom to convey to him the intelligence of the
arrival of the important personage whose coming had created such a
lively sensation, and to beseech him to make haste.

Danglars and Caderousse set off upon their errand at full speed; but
ere they had gone many steps they perceived a group advancing towards
them, composed of the betrothed pair, a party of young girls in
attendance on the bride, by whose side walked DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ father; the whole
brought up by Fernand, whose lips wore their usual sinister smile.

Neither MercГғВ©dГғВЁs nor Edmond observed the strange expression of his
countenance; they were so happy that they were conscious only of the
sunshine and the presence of each other.

Having acquitted themselves of their errand, and exchanged a hearty
shake of the hand with Edmond, Danglars and Caderousse took their
places beside Fernand and old DantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВ”the latter of whom attracted
universal notice.

The old man was attired in a suit of glistening watered silk, trimmed
with steel buttons, beautifully cut and polished. His thin but wiry
legs were arrayed in a pair of richly embroidered clocked stockings,
evidently of English manufacture, while from his three-cornered hat
depended a long streaming knot of white and blue ribbons. Thus he came
along, supporting himself on a curiously carved stick, his aged
countenance lit up with happiness, looking for all the world like one
of the aged dandies of 1796, parading the newly opened gardens of the
Luxembourg and Tuileries.

Beside him glided Caderousse, whose desire to partake of the good
things provided for the wedding party had induced him to become
reconciled to the DantГғВЁs, father and son, although there still lingered
in his mind a faint and unperfect recollection of the events of the
preceding night; just as the brain retains on waking in the morning the
dim and misty outline of a dream.

0065m
