As Danglars approached the disappointed lover, he cast on him a look of
deep meaning, while Fernand, as he slowly paced behind the happy pair,
who seemed, in their own unmixed content, to have entirely forgotten
that such a being as himself existed, was pale and abstracted;
occasionally, however, a deep flush would overspread his countenance,
and a nervous contraction distort his features, while, with an agitated
and restless gaze, he would glance in the direction of Marseilles, like
one who either anticipated or foresaw some great and important event.

DantГғВЁs himself was simply, but becomingly, clad in the dress peculiar
to the merchant serviceГўВҖВ”a costume somewhat between a military and a
civil garb; and with his fine countenance, radiant with joy and
happiness, a more perfect specimen of manly beauty could scarcely be
imagined.

Lovely as the Greek girls of Cyprus or Chios, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs boasted the same
bright flashing eyes of jet, and ripe, round, coral lips. She moved
with the light, free step of an Arlesienne or an Andalusian. One more
practiced in the arts of great cities would have hid her blushes
beneath a veil, or, at least, have cast down her thickly fringed
lashes, so as to have concealed the liquid lustre of her animated eyes;
but, on the contrary, the delighted girl looked around her with a smile
that seemed to say: ГўВҖВңIf you are my friends, rejoice with me, for I am
very happy.ГўВҖВқ

As soon as the bridal party came in sight of La RГғВ©serve, M. Morrel
descended and came forth to meet it, followed by the soldiers and
sailors there assembled, to whom he had repeated the promise already
given, that DantГғВЁs should be the successor to the late Captain Leclere.
Edmond, at the approach of his patron, respectfully placed the arm of
his affianced bride within that of M. Morrel, who, forthwith conducting
her up the flight of wooden steps leading to the chamber in which the
feast was prepared, was gayly followed by the guests, beneath whose
heavy tread the slight structure creaked and groaned for the space of
several minutes.

ГўВҖВңFather,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, stopping when she had reached the centre of
the table, ГўВҖВңsit, I pray you, on my right hand; on my left I will place
him who has ever been as a brother to me,ГўВҖВқ pointing with a soft and
gentle smile to Fernand; but her words and look seemed to inflict the
direst torture on him, for his lips became ghastly pale, and even
beneath the dark hue of his complexion the blood might be seen
retreating as though some sudden pang drove it back to the heart.

During this time, DantГғВЁs, at the opposite side of the table, had been
occupied in similarly placing his most honored guests. M. Morrel was
seated at his right hand, Danglars at his left; while, at a sign from
Edmond, the rest of the company ranged themselves as they found it most
agreeable.

Then they began to pass around the dusky, piquant, Arlesian sausages,
and lobsters in their dazzling red cuirasses, prawns of large size and
brilliant color, the echinus with its prickly outside and dainty morsel
within, the clovis, esteemed by the epicures of the South as more than
rivalling the exquisite flavor of the oyster, North. All the
delicacies, in fact, that are cast up by the wash of waters on the
sandy beach, and styled by the grateful fishermen ГўВҖВңfruits of the sea.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA pretty silence truly!ГўВҖВқ said the old father of the bridegroom, as he
carried to his lips a glass of wine of the hue and brightness of the
topaz, and which had just been placed before MercГғВ©dГғВЁs herself. ГўВҖВңNow,
would anybody think that this room contained a happy, merry party, who
desire nothing better than to laugh and dance the hours away?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ sighed Caderousse, ГўВҖВңa man cannot always feel happy because he is
about to be married.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe truth is,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңthat I am too happy for noisy mirth;
if that is what you meant by your observation, my worthy friend, you
are right; joy takes a strange effect at times, it seems to oppress us
almost the same as sorrow.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars looked towards Fernand, whose excitable nature received and
betrayed each fresh impression.

ГўВҖВңWhy, what ails you?ГўВҖВқ asked he of Edmond. ГўВҖВңDo you fear any approaching
evil? I should say that you were the happiest man alive at this
instant.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd that is the very thing that alarms me,ГўВҖВқ returned DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңMan does
not appear to me to be intended to enjoy felicity so unmixed; happiness
is like the enchanted palaces we read of in our childhood, where
fierce, fiery dragons defend the entrance and approach; and monsters of
all shapes and kinds, requiring to be overcome ere victory is ours. I
own that I am lost in wonder to find myself promoted to an honor of
which I feel myself unworthyГўВҖВ”that of being the husband of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNay, nay!ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse, smiling, ГўВҖВңyou have not attained that
honor yet. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs is not yet your wife. Just assume the tone and
manner of a husband, and see how she will remind you that your hour is
not yet come!ГўВҖВқ

The bride blushed, while Fernand, restless and uneasy, seemed to start
at every fresh sound, and from time to time wiped away the large drops
of perspiration that gathered on his brow.

ГўВҖВңWell, never mind that, neighbor Caderousse; it is not worthwhile to
contradict me for such a trifle as that. ГўВҖВҷTis true that MercГғВ©dГғВЁs is not
actually my wife; but,ГўВҖВқ added he, drawing out his watch, ГўВҖВңin an hour
and a half she will be.ГўВҖВқ

A general exclamation of surprise ran round the table, with the
exception of the elder DantГғВЁs, whose laugh displayed the still perfect
beauty of his large white teeth. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs looked pleased and gratified,
while Fernand grasped the handle of his knife with a convulsive clutch.

ГўВҖВңIn an hour?ГўВҖВқ inquired Danglars, turning pale. ГўВҖВңHow is that, my
friend?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, thus it is,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңThanks to the influence of M.
Morrel, to whom, next to my father, I owe every blessing I enjoy, every
difficulty has been removed. We have purchased permission to waive the
usual delay; and at half-past two oГўВҖВҷclock the Mayor of Marseilles will
be waiting for us at the city hall. Now, as a quarter-past one has
already struck, I do not consider I have asserted too much in saying,
that, in another hour and thirty minutes MercГғВ©dГғВЁs will have become
Madame DantГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

0069m



Fernand closed his eyes, a burning sensation passed across his brow,
and he was compelled to support himself by the table to prevent his
falling from his chair; but in spite of all his efforts, he could not
refrain from uttering a deep groan, which, however, was lost amid the
noisy felicitations of the company.

ГўВҖВңUpon my word,ГўВҖВқ cried the old man, ГўВҖВңyou make short work of this kind of
affair. Arrived here only yesterday morning, and married today at three
oГўВҖВҷclock! Commend me to a sailor for going the quick way to work!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ asked Danglars, in a timid tone, ГўВҖВңhow did you manage about the
other formalitiesГўВҖВ”the contractГўВҖВ”the settlement?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe contract,ГўВҖВқ answered DantГғВЁs, laughingly, ГўВҖВңit didnГўВҖВҷt take long to
fix that. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs has no fortune; I have none to settle on her. So,
you see, our papers were quickly written out, and certainly do not come
very expensive.ГўВҖВқ This joke elicited a fresh burst of applause.

ГўВҖВңSo that what we presumed to be merely the betrothal feast turns out to
be the actual wedding dinner!ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ answered DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңdonГўВҖВҷt imagine I am going to put you off in
that shabby manner. Tomorrow morning I start for Paris; four days to
go, and the same to return, with one day to discharge the commission
entrusted to me, is all the time I shall be absent. I shall be back
here by the first of March, and on the second I give my real marriage
feast.ГўВҖВқ

This prospect of fresh festivity redoubled the hilarity of the guests
to such a degree, that the elder DantГғВЁs, who, at the commencement of
the repast, had commented upon the silence that prevailed, now found it
difficult, amid the general din of voices, to obtain a momentГўВҖВҷs
tranquillity in which to drink to the health and prosperity of the
bride and bridegroom.

DantГғВЁs, perceiving the affectionate eagerness of his father, responded
by a look of grateful pleasure; while MercГғВ©dГғВЁs glanced at the clock and
made an expressive gesture to Edmond.

Around the table reigned that noisy hilarity which usually prevails at
such a time among people sufficiently free from the demands of social
position not to feel the trammels of etiquette. Such as at the
commencement of the repast had not been able to seat themselves
according to their inclination rose unceremoniously, and sought out
more agreeable companions. Everybody talked at once, without waiting
for a reply and each one seemed to be contented with expressing his or
her own thoughts.

FernandГўВҖВҷs paleness appeared to have communicated itself to Danglars. As
for Fernand himself, he seemed to be enduring the tortures of the
damned; unable to rest, he was among the first to quit the table, and,
as though seeking to avoid the hilarious mirth that rose in such
deafening sounds, he continued, in utter silence, to pace the farther
end of the salon.

Caderousse approached him just as Danglars, whom Fernand seemed most
anxious to avoid, had joined him in a corner of the room.

ГўВҖВңUpon my word,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, from whose mind the friendly treatment
of DantГғВЁs, united with the effect of the excellent wine he had partaken

fortune,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңupon my word, DantГғВЁs is a downright good fellow, and when I
see him sitting there beside his pretty wife that is so soon to be. I

ГўВҖВңOh, there was no harm meant,ГўВҖВқ answered Danglars; ГўВҖВңat first I certainly
did feel somewhat uneasy as to what Fernand might be tempted to do; but

cause for apprehension.ГўВҖВқ Caderousse looked full at FernandГўВҖВ”he was
ghastly pale.

ГўВҖВңCertainly,ГўВҖВқ continued Danglars, ГўВҖВңthe sacrifice was no trifling one,
when the beauty of the bride is concerned. Upon my soul, that future
captain of mine is a lucky dog! Gad! I only wish he would let me take

ГўВҖВңShall we not set forth?ГўВҖВқ asked the sweet, silvery voice of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs;
ГўВҖВңtwo oГўВҖВҷclock has just struck, and you know we are expected in a quarter

0071m



ГўВҖВңTo be sure!ГўВҖВ”to be sure!ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs, eagerly quitting the table;
ГўВҖВңlet us go directly!ГўВҖВқ

His words were re-echoed by the whole party, with vociferous cheers.

At this moment Danglars, who had been incessantly observing every

with an almost convulsive spasm, against a seat placed near one of the

buzz as of many voices, so as to deaden even the noisy mirth of the

quelled every disposition to talk, and almost instantaneously the most
deathlike stillness prevailed.

The sounds drew nearer. Three blows were struck upon the panel of the

ГўВҖВңI demand admittance,ГўВҖВқ said a loud voice outside the room, ГўВҖВңin the name
of the law!ГўВҖВқ As no attempt was made to prevent it, the door was opened,

ГўВҖВңMay I venture to inquire the reason of this unexpected visit?ГўВҖВқ said M.
Morrel, addressing the magistrate, whom he evidently knew; ГўВҖВңthere is
doubtless some mistake easily explained.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf it be so,ГўВҖВқ replied the magistrate, ГўВҖВңrely upon every reparation
being made; meanwhile, I am the bearer of an order of arrest, and
although I most reluctantly perform the task assigned me, it must,
nevertheless, be fulfilled. Who among the persons here assembled
answers to the name of Edmond DantГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ

Every eye was turned towards the young man who, spite of the agitation
he could not but feel, advanced with dignity, and said, in a firm

ГўВҖВңI am he; what is your pleasure with me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEdmond DantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ replied the magistrate, ГўВҖВңI arrest you in the name of

ГўВҖВңMe!ГўВҖВқ repeated Edmond, slightly changing color, ГўВҖВңand wherefore, I

ГўВҖВңI cannot inform you, but you will be duly acquainted with the reasons

M. Morrel felt that further resistance or remonstrance was useless. He
saw before him an officer delegated to enforce the law, and perfectly

situations which the heart of a father or a mother cannot be made to
understand. He prayed and supplicated in terms so moving, that even the

ГўВҖВңMy worthy friend, let me beg of you to calm your apprehensions. Your

ГўВҖВңWhat is the meaning of all this?ГўВҖВқ inquired Caderousse, frowningly, of
Danglars, who had assumed an air of utter surprise.

0073m



ГўВҖВңHow can I tell you?ГўВҖВқ replied he; ГўВҖВңI am, like yourself, utterly

The scene of the previous night now came back to his mind with

ГўВҖВңSo, so,ГўВҖВқ said he, in a hoarse and choking voice, to Danglars, ГўВҖВңthis,

to bring double evil on those who have projected it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNonsense,ГўВҖВқ returned Danglars, ГўВҖВңI tell you again I have nothing

ГўВҖВңNo, you did not!ГўВҖВқ answered Caderousse, ГўВҖВңyou merely threw it byГўВҖВ”I saw

ГўВҖВңHold your tongue, you fool!ГўВҖВ”what should you know about it?ГўВҖВ”why, you

ГўВҖВңWhere is Fernand?ГўВҖВқ inquired Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңHow do I know?ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars; ГўВҖВңgone, as every prudent man ought to

During this conversation, DantГғВЁs, after having exchanged a cheerful

0075m



ГўВҖВңOh, to be sure!ГўВҖВқ responded Danglars, who had now approached the group,
ГўВҖВңnothing more than a mistake, I feel quite certain.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs descended the staircase, preceded by the magistrate, and

ГўВҖВңAdieu, adieu, dearest Edmond!ГўВҖВқ cried MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, stretching out her arms

ГўВҖВңYou donГўВҖВҷt mention those who aided and abetted the deed,ГўВҖВқ said

ГўВҖВңYou can, indeed, when the arrow lights point downward on somebodyГўВҖВҷs

ГўВҖВңWhat think you, Danglars,ГўВҖВқ said one of the party, turning towards him,
ГўВҖВңof this event?ГўВҖВқ

With the rapid instinct of selfishness, Caderousse readily perceived

ГўВҖВңSuppose we wait a while, and see what comes of it,ГўВҖВқ said he, casting a

ГўВҖВңTo be sure!ГўВҖВқ answered Danglars. ГўВҖВңLet us wait, by all means. If he be

ГўВҖВңLet us go, then. I cannot stay here any longer.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith all my heart!ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars, pleased to find the other so

The rumor of EdmondГўВҖВҷs arrest as a Bonapartist agent was not slow in

ГўВҖВңCould you ever have credited such a thing, my dear Danglars?ГўВҖВқ asked M.

0079m



ГўВҖВңCertainly not!ГўВҖВқ returned Danglars. Then added in a low whisper, ГўВҖВңYou

ГўВҖВңThanks, DanglarsГўВҖВ”that will smooth over all difficulties. I fully

ГўВҖВңWell, well,ГўВҖВқ returned M. Morrel, ГўВҖВңwe shall see. But now hasten on

So saying, the worthy shipowner quitted the two allies, and proceeded

0081m



ГўВҖВңYou see,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, addressing Caderousse, ГўВҖВңthe turn things have

ГўВҖВңNot the slightest, but yet it seems to me a shocking thing that a mere

ГўВҖВңOh, no,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, ГўВҖВңthat I can answer for, you did not. I
only wish I could see it now as plainly as I saw it lying all crushed
and crumpled in a corner of the arbor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, if you did, depend upon it, Fernand picked it up, and
either copied it or caused it to be copied; perhaps, even, he did not
take the trouble of recopying it. And now I think of it, by Heavens, he
may have sent the letter itself! Fortunately, for me, the handwriting
was disguised.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you were aware of DantГғВЁs being engaged in a conspiracy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot I. As I before said, I thought the whole thing was a joke, nothing
more. It seems, however, that I have unconsciously stumbled upon the
truth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill,ГўВҖВқ argued Caderousse, ГўВҖВңI would give a great deal if nothing of
the kind had happened; or, at least, that I had had no hand in it. You
will see, Danglars, that it will turn out an unlucky job for both of
us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNonsense! If any harm come of it, it should fall on the guilty person;
and that, you know, is Fernand. How can we be implicated in any way?
All we have got to do is, to keep our own counsel, and remain perfectly
quiet, not breathing a word to any living soul; and you will see that
the storm will pass away without in the least affecting us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAmen!ГўВҖВқ responded Caderousse, waving his hand in token of adieu to
Danglars, and bending his steps towards the AllГғВ©es de Meilhan, moving
his head to and fro, and muttering as he went, after the manner of one
whose mind was overcharged with one absorbing idea.

ГўВҖВңSo far, then,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, mentally, ГўВҖВңall has gone as I would have
it. I am, temporarily, commander of the _Pharaon_, with the certainty
of being permanently so, if that fool of a Caderousse can be persuaded
to hold his tongue. My only fear is the chance of DantГғВЁs being
released. But, there, he is in the hands of Justice; and,ГўВҖВқ added he
with a smile, ГўВҖВңshe will take her own.ГўВҖВқ So saying, he leaped into a
boat, desiring to be rowed on board the _Pharaon_, where M. Morrel had
agreed to meet him.



 Chapter 6. The Deputy Procureur du Roi

In one of the aristocratic mansions built by Puget in the Rue du Grand
Cours opposite the Medusa fountain, a second marriage feast was being
celebrated, almost at the same hour with the nuptial repast given by
DantГғВЁs. In this case, however, although the occasion of the
entertainment was similar, the company was strikingly dissimilar.
Instead of a rude mixture of sailors, soldiers, and those belonging to
the humblest grade of life, the present assembly was composed of the
very flower of Marseilles society,ГўВҖВ”magistrates who had resigned their
office during the usurperГўВҖВҷs reign; officers who had deserted from the
imperial army and joined forces with CondГғВ©; and younger members of
families, brought up to hate and execrate the man whom five years of
exile would convert into a martyr, and fifteen of restoration elevate
to the rank of a god.

The guests were still at table, and the heated and energetic
conversation that prevailed betrayed the violent and vindictive
passions that then agitated each dweller of the South, where unhappily,
for five centuries religious strife had long given increased bitterness
to the violence of party feeling.

The emperor, now king of the petty Island of Elba, after having held
sovereign sway over one-half of the world, counting as his subjects a
small population of five or six thousand souls,ГўВҖВ”after having been
accustomed to hear the ГўВҖВң_Vive NapolГғВ©ons_ГўВҖВқ of a hundred and twenty
millions of human beings, uttered in ten different languages,ГўВҖВ”was
looked upon here as a ruined man, separated forever from any fresh
connection with France or claim to her throne.

The magistrates freely discussed their political views; the military
part of the company talked unreservedly of Moscow and Leipsic, while
the women commented on the divorce of Josephine. It was not over the
downfall of the man, but over the defeat of the Napoleonic idea, that
they rejoiced, and in this they foresaw for themselves the bright and
cheering prospect of a revivified political existence.

An old man, decorated with the cross of Saint Louis, now rose and
proposed the health of King Louis XVIII. It was the Marquis de
Saint-MГғВ©ran. This toast, recalling at once the patient exile of
Hartwell and the peace-loving King of France, excited universal
enthusiasm; glasses were elevated in the air _ГғВ  lГўВҖВҷAnglaise_, and the
ladies, snatching their bouquets from their fair bosoms, strewed the
table with their floral treasures. In a word, an almost poetical fervor
prevailed.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said the Marquise de Saint-MГғВ©ran, a woman with a stern,
forbidding eye, though still noble and distinguished in appearance,
despite her fifty yearsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңah, these revolutionists, who have driven us
from those very possessions they afterwards purchased for a mere trifle
during the Reign of Terror, would be compelled to own, were they here,
that all true devotion was on our side, since we were content to follow
the fortunes of a falling monarch, while they, on the contrary, made
their fortune by worshipping the rising sun; yes, yes, they could not
help admitting that the king, for whom we sacrificed rank, wealth, and
station was truly our ГўВҖВҳLouis the well-beloved,ГўВҖВҷ while their wretched
usurper has been, and ever will be, to them their evil genius, their
ГўВҖВҳNapoleon the accursed.ГўВҖВҷ Am I not right, Villefort?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI beg your pardon, madame. I really must pray you to excuse me, butГўВҖВ”in
truthГўВҖВ”I was not attending to the conversation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMarquise, marquise!ГўВҖВқ interposed the old nobleman who had proposed the
toast, ГўВҖВңlet the young people alone; let me tell you, on oneГўВҖВҷs wedding
day there are more agreeable subjects of conversation than dry
politics.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mind, dearest mother,ГўВҖВқ said a young and lovely girl, with a
profusion of light brown hair, and eyes that seemed to float in liquid
crystal, ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҷtis all my fault for seizing upon M. de Villefort, so as to
prevent his listening to what you said. But thereГўВҖВ”now take himГўВҖВ”he is
your own for as long as you like. M. Villefort, I beg to remind you my
mother speaks to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf the marquise will deign to repeat the words I but imperfectly
caught, I shall be delighted to answer,ГўВҖВқ said M. de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңNever mind, RenГғВ©e,ГўВҖВқ replied the marquise, with a look of tenderness
that seemed out of keeping with her harsh dry features; but, however
all other feelings may be withered in a womanГўВҖВҷs nature, there is always
one bright smiling spot in the desert of her heart, and that is the
shrine of maternal love. ГўВҖВңI forgive you. What I was saying, Villefort,
was, that the Bonapartists had not our sincerity, enthusiasm, or
devotion.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey had, however, what supplied the place of those fine qualities,ГўВҖВқ
replied the young man, ГўВҖВңand that was fanaticism. Napoleon is the
Mahomet of the West, and is worshipped by his commonplace but ambitious
followers, not only as a leader and lawgiver, but also as the
personification of equality.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe!ГўВҖВқ cried the marquise: ГўВҖВңNapoleon the type of equality! For mercyГўВҖВҷs
sake, then, what would you call Robespierre? Come, come, do not strip
the latter of his just rights to bestow them on the Corsican, who, to
my mind, has usurped quite enough.ГўВҖВқ

0085m



ГўВҖВңNay, madame; I would place each of these heroes on his right
pedestalГўВҖВ”that of Robespierre on his scaffold in the Place Louis Quinze;
that of Napoleon on the column of the Place VendГғВҙme. The only
difference consists in the opposite character of the equality advocated
by these two men; one is the equality that elevates, the other is the
equality that degrades; one brings a king within reach of the
guillotine, the other elevates the people to a level with the throne.
Observe,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, smiling, ГўВҖВңI do not mean to deny that both
these men were revolutionary scoundrels, and that the 9th Thermidor and
the 4th of April, in the year 1814, were lucky days for France, worthy
of being gratefully remembered by every friend to monarchy and civil
order; and that explains how it comes to pass that, fallen, as I trust
he is forever, Napoleon has still retained a train of parasitical
satellites. Still, marquise, it has been so with other
usurpersГўВҖВ”Cromwell, for instance, who was not half so bad as Napoleon,
had his partisans and advocates.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you know, Villefort, that you are talking in a most dreadfully
revolutionary strain? But I excuse it, it is impossible to expect the
son of a Girondin to be free from a small spice of the old leaven.ГўВҖВқ A
deep crimson suffused the countenance of Villefort.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҷTis true, madame,ГўВҖВқ answered he, ГўВҖВңthat my father was a Girondin, but
he was not among the number of those who voted for the kingГўВҖВҷs death; he
was an equal sufferer with yourself during the Reign of Terror, and had
well-nigh lost his head on the same scaffold on which your father
perished.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ replied the marquise, without wincing in the slightest degree
at the tragic remembrance thus called up; ГўВҖВңbut bear in mind, if you
please, that our respective parents underwent persecution and
proscription from diametrically opposite principles; in proof of which
I may remark, that while my family remained among the staunchest
adherents of the exiled princes, your father lost no time in joining
the new government; and that while the Citizen Noirtier was a Girondin,
the Count Noirtier became a senator.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDear mother,ГўВҖВқ interposed RenГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңyou know very well it was agreed that
all these disagreeable reminiscences should forever be laid aside.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSuffer me, also, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, ГўВҖВңto add my earnest
request to Mademoiselle de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs, that you will kindly allow
the veil of oblivion to cover and conceal the past. What avails
recrimination over matters wholly past recall? For my own part, I have
laid aside even the name of my father, and altogether disown his
political principles. He wasГўВҖВ”nay, probably may still beГўВҖВ”a Bonapartist,
and is called Noirtier; I, on the contrary, am a staunch royalist, and
style myself de Villefort. Let what may remain of revolutionary sap
exhaust itself and die away with the old trunk, and condescend only to
regard the young shoot which has started up at a distance from the
parent tree, without having the power, any more than the wish, to
separate entirely from the stock from which it sprung.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBravo, Villefort!ГўВҖВқ cried the marquis; ГўВҖВңexcellently well said! Come,
now, I have hopes of obtaining what I have been for years endeavoring
to persuade the marquise to promise; namely, a perfect amnesty and
forgetfulness of the past.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith all my heart,ГўВҖВқ replied the marquise; ГўВҖВңlet the past be forever
forgotten. I promise you it affords _me_ as little pleasure to revive
it as it does you. All I ask is, that Villefort will be firm and
inflexible for the future in his political principles. Remember, also,
Villefort, that we have pledged ourselves to his majesty for your
fealty and strict loyalty, and that at our recommendation the king
consented to forget the past, as I doГўВҖВқ (and here she extended to him
her hand)ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңas I now do at your entreaty. But bear in mind, that should
there fall in your way anyone guilty of conspiring against the
government, you will be so much the more bound to visit the offence
with rigorous punishment, as it is known you belong to a suspected
family.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas, madame,ГўВҖВқ returned Villefort, ГўВҖВңmy profession, as well as the
times in which we live, compels me to be severe. I have already
successfully conducted several public prosecutions, and brought the
offenders to merited punishment. But we have not done with the thing
yet.ГўВҖВқ

0087m



ГўВҖВңDo you, indeed, think so?ГўВҖВқ inquired the marquise.

ГўВҖВңI am, at least, fearful of it. Napoleon, in the Island of Elba, is too
near France, and his proximity keeps up the hopes of his partisans.
Marseilles is filled with half-pay officers, who are daily, under one
frivolous pretext or other, getting up quarrels with the royalists;
from hence arise continual and fatal duels among the higher classes of
persons, and assassinations in the lower.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have heard, perhaps,ГўВҖВқ said the Comte de Salvieux, one of M. de
Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs oldest friends, and chamberlain to the Comte dГўВҖВҷArtois,
ГўВҖВңthat the Holy Alliance purpose removing him from thence?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; they were talking about it when we left Paris,ГўВҖВқ said M. de
Saint-MГғВ©ran; ГўВҖВңand where is it decided to transfer him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo Saint Helena.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor heavenГўВҖВҷs sake, where is that?ГўВҖВқ asked the marquise.

ГўВҖВңAn island situated on the other side of the equator, at least two
thousand leagues from here,ГўВҖВқ replied the count.

ГўВҖВңSo much the better. As Villefort observes, it is a great act of folly
to have left such a man between Corsica, where he was born, and Naples,
of which his brother-in-law is king, and face to face with Italy, the
sovereignty of which he coveted for his son.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUnfortunately,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңthere are the treaties of 1814, and
we cannot molest Napoleon without breaking those compacts.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, well, we shall find some way out of it,ГўВҖВқ responded M. de Salvieux.
ГўВҖВңThere wasnГўВҖВҷt any trouble over treaties when it was a question of
shooting the poor Duc dГўВҖВҷEnghien.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said the marquise, ГўВҖВңit seems probable that, by the aid of the
Holy Alliance, we shall be rid of Napoleon; and we must trust to the
vigilance of M. de Villefort to purify Marseilles of his partisans. The
king is either a king or no king; if he be acknowledged as sovereign of
France, he should be upheld in peace and tranquillity; and this can
best be effected by employing the most inflexible agents to put down
every attempt at conspiracyГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҷtis the best and surest means of
preventing mischief.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUnfortunately, madame,ГўВҖВқ answered Villefort, ГўВҖВңthe strong arm of the law
is not called upon to interfere until the evil has taken place.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen all he has got to do is to endeavor to repair it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNay, madame, the law is frequently powerless to effect this; all it
can do is to avenge the wrong done.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, M. de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ cried a beautiful young creature, daughter to
the Comte de Salvieux, and the cherished friend of Mademoiselle de
Saint-MГғВ©ran, ГўВҖВңdo try and get up some famous trial while we are at
Marseilles. I never was in a law-court; I am told it is so very
amusing!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAmusing, certainly,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man, ГўВҖВңinasmuch as, instead of
shedding tears as at the fictitious tale of woe produced at a theatre,
you behold in a law-court a case of real and genuine distressГўВҖВ”a drama
of life. The prisoner whom you there see pale, agitated, and alarmed,
instead ofГўВҖВ”as is the case when a curtain falls on a tragedyГўВҖВ”going home
to sup peacefully with his family, and then retiring to rest, that he
may recommence his mimic woes on the morrow,ГўВҖВ”is removed from your sight
merely to be reconducted to his prison and delivered up to the
executioner. I leave you to judge how far your nerves are calculated to
bear you through such a scene. Of this, however, be assured, that
should any favorable opportunity present itself, I will not fail to
offer you the choice of being present.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor shame, M. de Villefort!ГўВҖВқ said RenГғВ©e, becoming quite pale; ГўВҖВңdonГўВҖВҷt
you see how you are frightening us?ГўВҖВ”and yet you laugh.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat would you have? ГўВҖВҷTis like a duel. I have already recorded
sentence of death, five or six times, against the movers of political
conspiracies, and who can say how many daggers may be ready sharpened,
and only waiting a favorable opportunity to be buried in my heart?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGracious heavens, M. de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ said RenГғВ©e, becoming more and more
terrified; ГўВҖВңyou surely are not in earnest.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed I am,ГўВҖВқ replied the young magistrate with a smile; ГўВҖВңand in the
interesting trial that young lady is anxious to witness, the case would
only be still more aggravated. Suppose, for instance, the prisoner, as
is more than probable, to have served under NapoleonГўВҖВ”well, can you
expect for an instant, that one accustomed, at the word of his
commander, to rush fearlessly on the very bayonets of his foe, will
scruple more to drive a stiletto into the heart of one he knows to be
his personal enemy, than to slaughter his fellow-creatures, merely
because bidden to do so by one he is bound to obey? Besides, one
requires the excitement of being hateful in the eyes of the accused, in
order to lash oneГўВҖВҷs self into a state of sufficient vehemence and
power. I would not choose to see the man against whom I pleaded smile,
as though in mockery of my words. No; my pride is to see the accused
pale, agitated, and as though beaten out of all composure by the fire
of my eloquence.ГўВҖВқ RenГғВ©e uttered a smothered exclamation.

ГўВҖВңBravo!ГўВҖВқ cried one of the guests; ГўВҖВңthat is what I call talking to some
purpose.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJust the person we require at a time like the present,ГўВҖВқ said a second.

ГўВҖВңWhat a splendid business that last case of yours was, my dear
Villefort!ГўВҖВқ remarked a third; ГўВҖВңI mean the trial of the man for
murdering his father. Upon my word, you killed him ere the executioner
had laid his hand upon him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, as for parricides, and such dreadful people as that,ГўВҖВқ interposed
RenГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңit matters very little what is done to them; but as regards
poor unfortunate creatures whose only crime consists in having mixed
themselves up in political intriguesГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, that is the very worst offence they could possibly commit; for,
donГўВҖВҷt you see, RenГғВ©e, the king is the father of his people, and he who
shall plot or contrive aught against the life and safety of the parent
of thirty-two millions of souls, is a parricide upon a fearfully great
scale?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt know anything about that,ГўВҖВқ replied RenГғВ©e; ГўВҖВңbut, M. de
Villefort, you have promised meГўВҖВ”have you not?ГўВҖВ”always to show mercy to
those I plead for.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMake yourself quite easy on that point,ГўВҖВқ answered Villefort, with one
of his sweetest smiles; ГўВҖВңyou and I will always consult upon our
verdicts.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy love,ГўВҖВқ said the marquise, ГўВҖВңattend to your doves, your lap-dogs, and
embroidery, but do not meddle with what you do not understand. Nowadays
the military profession is in abeyance and the magisterial robe is the
badge of honor. There is a wise Latin proverb that is very much in
point.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Cedant arma togГғВҰ_,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort with a bow.

ГўВҖВңI cannot speak Latin,ГўВҖВқ responded the marquise.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said RenГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңI cannot help regretting you had not chosen some
other profession than your ownГўВҖВ”a physician, for instance. Do you know I
always felt a shudder at the idea of even a _destroying_ angel?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDear, good RenГғВ©e,ГўВҖВқ whispered Villefort, as he gazed with unutterable
tenderness on the lovely speaker.

ГўВҖВңLet us hope, my child,ГўВҖВқ cried the marquis, ГўВҖВңthat M. de Villefort may
prove the moral and political physician of this province; if so, he
will have achieved a noble work.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd one which will go far to efface the recollection of his fatherГўВҖВҷs
conduct,ГўВҖВқ added the incorrigible marquise.

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, with a mournful smile, ГўВҖВңI have already had
the honor to observe that my father hasГўВҖВ”at least, I hope soГўВҖВ”abjured his
past errors, and that he is, at the present moment, a firm and zealous
friend to religion and orderГўВҖВ”a better royalist, possibly, than his son;
for he has to atone for past dereliction, while I have no other impulse
than warm, decided preference and conviction.ГўВҖВқ Having made this
well-turned speech, Villefort looked carefully around to mark the
effect of his oratory, much as he would have done had he been
addressing the bench in open court.

ГўВҖВңDo you know, my dear Villefort,ГўВҖВқ cried the Comte de Salvieux, ГўВҖВңthat is
exactly what I myself said the other day at the Tuileries, when
questioned by his majestyГўВҖВҷs principal chamberlain touching the
singularity of an alliance between the son of a Girondin and the
daughter of an officer of the Duc de CondГғВ©; and I assure you he seemed
fully to comprehend that this mode of reconciling political differences
was based upon sound and excellent principles. Then the king, who,
without our suspecting it, had overheard our conversation, interrupted
us by saying, ГўВҖВҳVillefortГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”observe that the king did not pronounce the
word Noirtier, but, on the contrary, placed considerable emphasis on
that of VillefortГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳVillefort,ГўВҖВҷ said his majesty, ГўВҖВҳis a young man of
great judgment and discretion, who will be sure to make a figure in his
profession; I like him much, and it gave me great pleasure to hear that
he was about to become the son-in-law of the Marquis and Marquise de
Saint-MГғВ©ran. I should myself have recommended the match, had not the
noble marquis anticipated my wishes by requesting my consent to it.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs it possible the king could have condescended so far as to express
himself so favorably of me?ГўВҖВқ asked the enraptured Villefort.

ГўВҖВңI give you his very words; and if the marquis chooses to be candid, he
will confess that they perfectly agree with what his majesty said to
him, when he went six months ago to consult him upon the subject of
your espousing his daughter.ГўВҖВқ

0091m



ГўВҖВңThat is true,ГўВҖВқ answered the marquis.

ГўВҖВңHow much do I owe this gracious prince! What is there I would not do
to evince my earnest gratitude!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is right,ГўВҖВқ cried the marquise. ГўВҖВңI love to see you thus. Now,
then, were a conspirator to fall into your hands, he would be most
welcome.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor my part, dear mother,ГўВҖВқ interposed RenГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңI trust your wishes will
not prosper, and that Providence will only permit petty offenders, poor
debtors, and miserable cheats to fall into M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs
hands,ГўВҖВ”then I shall be contented.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJust the same as though you prayed that a physician might only be
called upon to prescribe for headaches, measles, and the stings of
wasps, or any other slight affection of the epidermis. If you wish to
see me the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney, you must desire for me some of those
violent and dangerous diseases from the cure of which so much honor
redounds to the physician.ГўВҖВқ

At this moment, and as though the utterance of VillefortГўВҖВҷs wish had
sufficed to effect its accomplishment, a servant entered the room, and
whispered a few words in his ear. Villefort immediately rose from table
and quitted the room upon the plea of urgent business; he soon,
however, returned, his whole face beaming with delight. RenГғВ©e regarded
him with fond affection; and certainly his handsome features, lit up as
they then were with more than usual fire and animation, seemed formed
to excite the innocent admiration with which she gazed on her graceful
and intelligent lover.

ГўВҖВңYou were wishing just now,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, addressing her, ГўВҖВңthat I
were a doctor instead of a lawyer. Well, I at least resemble the
disciples of Esculapius in one thing [people spoke in this style in
1815], that of not being able to call a day my own, not even that of my
betrothal.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd wherefore were you called away just now?ГўВҖВқ asked Mademoiselle de
Saint-MГғВ©ran, with an air of deep interest.

ГўВҖВңFor a very serious matter, which bids fair to make work for the
executioner.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow dreadful!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed RenГғВ©e, turning pale.

ГўВҖВңIs it possible?ГўВҖВқ burst simultaneously from all who were near enough to
the magistrate to hear his words.

ГўВҖВңWhy, if my information prove correct, a sort of Bonapartist conspiracy
has just been discovered.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCan I believe my ears?ГўВҖВқ cried the marquise.

ГўВҖВңI will read you the letter containing the accusation, at least,ГўВҖВқ said
Villefort:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳThe kingГўВҖВҷs attorney is informed by a friend to the throne and the
religious institutions of his country, that one named Edmond DantГғВЁs,
mate of the ship _Pharaon_, this day arrived from Smyrna, after having
touched at Naples and Porto-Ferrajo, has been the bearer of a letter
from Murat to the usurper, and again taken charge of another letter
from the usurper to the Bonapartist club in Paris. Ample corroboration
of this statement may be obtained by arresting the above-mentioned
Edmond DantГғВЁs, who either carries the letter for Paris about with him,
or has it at his fatherГўВҖВҷs abode. Should it not be found in the
possession of father or son, then it will assuredly be discovered in
the cabin belonging to the said DantГғВЁs on board the _Pharaon_.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said RenГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңthis letter, which, after all, is but an anonymous
scrawl, is not even addressed to you, but to the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney.ГўВҖВқ

0093m



ГўВҖВңTrue; but that gentleman being absent, his secretary, by his orders,
opened his letters; thinking this one of importance, he sent for me,
but not finding me, took upon himself to give the necessary orders for
arresting the accused party.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen the guilty person is absolutely in custody?ГўВҖВқ said the marquise.

ГўВҖВңNay, dear mother, say the accused person. You know we cannot yet
pronounce him guilty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is in safe custody,ГўВҖВқ answered Villefort; ГўВҖВңand rely upon it, if the
letter is found, he will not be likely to be trusted abroad again,
unless he goes forth under the especial protection of the headsman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd where is the unfortunate being?ГўВҖВқ asked RenГғВ©e.

ГўВҖВңHe is at my house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, come, my friend,ГўВҖВқ interrupted the marquise, ГўВҖВңdo not neglect your
duty to linger with us. You are the kingГўВҖВҷs servant, and must go
wherever that service calls you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, Villefort!ГўВҖВқ cried RenГғВ©e, clasping her hands, and looking towards
her lover with piteous earnestness, ГўВҖВңbe merciful on this the day of our
betrothal.ГўВҖВқ

The young man passed round to the side of the table where the fair
pleader sat, and leaning over her chair said tenderly:

ГўВҖВңTo give you pleasure, my sweet RenГғВ©e, I promise to show all the lenity
in my power; but if the charges brought against this Bonapartist hero
prove correct, why, then, you really must give me leave to order his
head to be cut off.ГўВҖВқ

RenГғВ©e shuddered at the word _cut_, for the growth in question had a
head.

ГўВҖВңNever mind that foolish girl, Villefort,ГўВҖВқ said the marquise. ГўВҖВңShe will
soon get over these things.ГўВҖВқ So saying, Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran extended
her dry bony hand to Villefort, who, while imprinting a son-in-lawГўВҖВҷs
respectful salute on it, looked at RenГғВ©e, as much as to say, ГўВҖВңI must
try and fancy ГўВҖВҷtis your dear hand I kiss, as it should have been.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThese are mournful auspices to accompany a betrothal,ГўВҖВқ sighed poor
RenГғВ©e.

ГўВҖВңUpon my word, child!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the angry marquise, ГўВҖВңyour folly
exceeds all bounds. I should be glad to know what connection there can
possibly be between your sickly sentimentality and the affairs of the
state!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, mother!ГўВҖВқ murmured RenГғВ©e.

ГўВҖВңNay, madame, I pray you pardon this little traitor. I promise you that
to make up for her want of loyalty, I will be most inflexibly severe;ГўВҖВқ
then casting an expressive glance at his betrothed, which seemed to
say, ГўВҖВңFear not, for your dear sake my justice shall be tempered with
mercy,ГўВҖВқ and receiving a sweet and approving smile in return, Villefort
departed with paradise in his heart.



 Chapter 7. The Examination

No sooner had Villefort left the salon, than he assumed the grave air
of a man who holds the balance of life and death in his hands. Now, in
spite of the nobility of his countenance, the command of which, like a
finished actor, he had carefully studied before the glass, it was by no
means easy for him to assume an air of judicial severity. Except the
recollection of the line of politics his father had adopted, and which
might interfere, unless he acted with the greatest prudence, with his
own career, GГғВ©rard de Villefort was as happy as a man could be. Already
rich, he held a high official situation, though only twenty-seven. He
was about to marry a young and charming woman, whom he loved, not
passionately, but reasonably, as became a deputy attorney of the king;
and besides her personal attractions, which were very great,
Mademoiselle de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs family possessed considerable political
influence, which they would, of course, exert in his favor. The dowry
of his wife amounted to fifty thousand crowns, and he had, besides, the
prospect of seeing her fortune increased to half a million at her
fatherГўВҖВҷs death. These considerations naturally gave Villefort a feeling
of such complete felicity that his mind was fairly dazzled in its
contemplation.

At the door he met the commissary of police, who was waiting for him.
The sight of this officer recalled Villefort from the third heaven to
earth; he composed his face, as we have before described, and said, ГўВҖВңI
have read the letter, sir, and you have acted rightly in arresting this
man; now inform me what you have discovered concerning him and the
conspiracy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe know nothing as yet of the conspiracy, monsieur; all the papers
found have been sealed up and placed on your desk. The prisoner himself
is named Edmond DantГғВЁs, mate on board the three-master the _Pharaon_,
trading in cotton with Alexandria and Smyrna, and belonging to Morrel &
Son, of Marseilles.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBefore he entered the merchant service, had he ever served in the
marines?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no, monsieur, he is very young.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow old?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNineteen or twenty at the most.ГўВҖВқ

At this moment, and as Villefort had arrived at the corner of the Rue
des Conseils, a man, who seemed to have been waiting for him,
approached; it was M. Morrel.

ГўВҖВңAh, M. de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ cried he, ГўВҖВңI am delighted to see you. Some of
your people have committed the strangest mistakeГўВҖВ”they have just
arrested Edmond DantГғВЁs, mate of my vessel.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, ГўВҖВңand I am now going to
examine him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, carried away by his friendship, ГўВҖВңyou do not know
him, and I do. He is the most estimable, the most trustworthy creature
in the world, and I will venture to say, there is not a better seaman
in all the merchant service. Oh, M. de Villefort, I beseech your
indulgence for him.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort, as we have seen, belonged to the aristocratic party at
Marseilles, Morrel to the plebeian; the first was a royalist, the other
suspected of Bonapartism. Villefort looked disdainfully at Morrel, and
replied coldly:

ГўВҖВңYou are aware, monsieur, that a man may be estimable and trustworthy
in private life, and the best seaman in the merchant service, and yet
be, politically speaking, a great criminal. Is it not true?ГўВҖВқ

The magistrate laid emphasis on these words, as if he wished to apply
them to the owner himself, while his eyes seemed to plunge into the
heart of one who, interceding for another, had himself need of
indulgence. Morrel reddened, for his own conscience was not quite clear
on politics; besides, what DantГғВЁs had told him of his interview with
the grand-marshal, and what the emperor had said to him, embarrassed
him. He replied, however, in a tone of deep interest:

ГўВҖВңI entreat you, M. de Villefort, be, as you always are, kind and
equitable, and give him back to us soon.ГўВҖВқ This _give us_ sounded
revolutionary in the deputyГўВҖВҷs ears.

ГўВҖВңAh, ah,ГўВҖВқ murmured he, ГўВҖВңis DantГғВЁs then a member of some Carbonari
society, that his protector thus employs the collective form? He was,
if I recollect, arrested in a tavern, in company with a great many
others.ГўВҖВқ Then he added, ГўВҖВңMonsieur, you may rest assured I shall perform
my duty impartially, and that if he be innocent you shall not have
appealed to me in vain; should he, however, be guilty, in this present
epoch, impunity would furnish a dangerous example, and I must do my
duty.ГўВҖВқ

0097m



As he had now arrived at the door of his own house, which adjoined the
Palais de Justice, he entered, after having, coldly saluted the
shipowner, who stood, as if petrified, on the spot where Villefort had
left him. The antechamber was full of police agents and gendarmes, in
the midst of whom, carefully watched, but calm and smiling, stood the
prisoner. Villefort traversed the antechamber, cast a side glance at
DantГғВЁs, and taking a packet which a gendarme offered him, disappeared,
saying, ГўВҖВңBring in the prisoner.ГўВҖВқ

Rapid as had been VillefortГўВҖВҷs glance, it had served to give him an idea
of the man he was about to interrogate. He had recognized intelligence
in the high forehead, courage in the dark eye and bent brow, and
frankness in the thick lips that showed a set of pearly teeth.
VillefortГўВҖВҷs first impression was favorable; but he had been so often
warned to mistrust first impulses, that he applied the maxim to the
impression, forgetting the difference between the two words. He
stifled, therefore, the feelings of compassion that were rising,
composed his features, and sat down, grim and sombre, at his desk. An
instant after DantГғВЁs entered. He was pale, but calm and collected, and
saluting his judge with easy politeness, looked round for a seat, as if
he had been in M. MorrelГўВҖВҷs salon. It was then that he encountered for
the first time VillefortГўВҖВҷs look,ГўВҖВ”that look peculiar to the magistrate,
who, while seeming to read the thoughts of others, betrays nothing of
his own.

ГўВҖВңWho and what are you?ГўВҖВқ demanded Villefort, turning over a pile of
papers, containing information relative to the prisoner, that a police
agent had given to him on his entry, and that, already, in an hourГўВҖВҷs
time, had swelled to voluminous proportions, thanks to the corrupt
espionage of which ГўВҖВңthe accusedГўВҖВқ is always made the victim.

ГўВҖВңMy name is Edmond DantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man calmly; ГўВҖВңI am mate of
the _Pharaon_, belonging to Messrs. Morrel & Son.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour age?ГўВҖВқ continued Villefort.

ГўВҖВңNineteen,ГўВҖВқ returned DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңWhat were you doing at the moment you were arrested?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was at the festival of my marriage, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said the young man,
his voice slightly tremulous, so great was the contrast between that
happy moment and the painful ceremony he was now undergoing; so great
was the contrast between the sombre aspect of M. de Villefort and the
radiant face of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңYou were at the festival of your marriage?ГўВҖВқ said the deputy,
shuddering in spite of himself.

ГўВҖВңYes, monsieur; I am on the point of marrying a young girl I have been
attached to for three years.ГўВҖВқ Villefort, impassive as he was, was
struck with this coincidence; and the tremulous voice of DantГғВЁs,
surprised in the midst of his happiness, struck a sympathetic chord in
his own bosomГўВҖВ”he also was on the point of being married, and he was
summoned from his own happiness to destroy that of another. ГўВҖВңThis
philosophic reflection,ГўВҖВқ thought he, ГўВҖВңwill make a great sensation at M.
de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs;ГўВҖВқ and he arranged mentally, while DantГғВЁs awaited
further questions, the antithesis by which orators often create a
reputation for eloquence. When this speech was arranged, Villefort
turned to DantГғВЁs.

0099m



ГўВҖВңGo on, sir,ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңWhat would you have me say?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGive all the information in your power.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell me on which point you desire information, and I will tell all I
know; only,ГўВҖВқ added he, with a smile, ГўВҖВңI warn you I know very little.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you served under the usurper?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was about to be mustered into the Royal Marines when he fell.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is reported your political opinions are extreme,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort,
who had never heard anything of the kind, but was not sorry to make
this inquiry, as if it were an accusation.

ГўВҖВңMy political opinions!ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңAlas, sir, I never had any
opinions. I am hardly nineteen; I know nothing; I have no part to play.
If I obtain the situation I desire, I shall owe it to M. Morrel. Thus
all my opinionsГўВҖВ”I will not say public, but privateГўВҖВ”are confined to
these three sentiments,ГўВҖВ”I love my father, I respect M. Morrel, and I
adore MercГғВ©dГғВЁs. This, sir, is all I can tell you, and you see how
uninteresting it is.ГўВҖВқ As DantГғВЁs spoke, Villefort gazed at his ingenuous
and open countenance, and recollected the words of RenГғВ©e, who, without
knowing who the culprit was, had besought his indulgence for him. With
the deputyГўВҖВҷs knowledge of crime and criminals, every word the young man
uttered convinced him more and more of his innocence. This lad, for he
was scarcely a man,ГўВҖВ”simple, natural, eloquent with that eloquence of
the heart never found when sought for; full of affection for everybody,
because he was happy, and because happiness renders even the wicked
goodГўВҖВ”extended his affection even to his judge, spite of VillefortГўВҖВҷs
severe look and stern accent. DantГғВЁs seemed full of kindness.

_ГўВҖВңPardieu!ГўВҖВқ_ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңhe is a noble fellow. I hope I shall gain
RenГғВ©eГўВҖВҷs favor easily by obeying the first command she ever imposed on
me. I shall have at least a pressure of the hand in public, and a sweet
kiss in private.ГўВҖВқ Full of this idea, VillefortГўВҖВҷs face became so joyous,
that when he turned to DantГғВЁs, the latter, who had watched the change
on his physiognomy, was smiling also.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңhave you any enemies, at least, that you know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have enemies?ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңmy position is not sufficiently
elevated for that. As for my disposition, that is, perhaps, somewhat
too hasty; but I have striven to repress it. I have had ten or twelve
sailors under me, and if you question them, they will tell you that
they love and respect me, not as a father, for I am too young, but as
an elder brother.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you may have excited jealousy. You are about to become captain at
nineteenГўВҖВ”an elevated post; you are about to marry a pretty girl, who
loves you; and these two pieces of good fortune may have excited the
envy of someone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right; you know men better than I do, and what you say may
possibly be the case, I confess; but if such persons are among my
acquaintances I prefer not to know it, because then I should be forced
to hate them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are wrong; you should always strive to see clearly around you. You
seem a worthy young man; I will depart from the strict line of my duty
to aid you in discovering the author of this accusation. Here is the
paper; do you know the writing?ГўВҖВқ As he spoke, Villefort drew the letter
from his pocket, and presented it to DantГғВЁs. DantГғВЁs read it. A cloud
passed over his brow as he said:

ГўВҖВңNo, monsieur, I do not know the writing, and yet it is tolerably
plain. Whoever did it writes well. I am very fortunate,ГўВҖВқ added he,
looking gratefully at Villefort, ГўВҖВңto be examined by such a man as you;
for this envious person is a real enemy.ГўВҖВқ And by the rapid glance that
the young manГўВҖВҷs eyes shot forth, Villefort saw how much energy lay hid
beneath this mildness.

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ said the deputy, ГўВҖВңanswer me frankly, not as a prisoner to a
judge, but as one man to another who takes an interest in him, what
truth is there in the accusation contained in this anonymous letter?ГўВҖВқ
And Villefort threw disdainfully on his desk the letter DantГғВЁs had just
given back to him.

ГўВҖВңNone at all. I will tell you the real facts. I swear by my honor as a
sailor, by my love for MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, by the life of my fatherГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSpeak, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort. Then, internally, ГўВҖВңIf RenГғВ©e could
see me, I hope she would be satisfied, and would no longer call me a
decapitator.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, when we quitted Naples, Captain Leclere was attacked with a
brain fever. As we had no doctor on board, and he was so anxious to
arrive at Elba, that he would not touch at any other port, his disorder
rose to such a height, that at the end of the third day, feeling he was
dying, he called me to him. ГўВҖВҳMy dear DantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВҷ said he, ГўВҖВҳswear to
perform what I am going to tell you, for it is a matter of the deepest
importance.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI swear, captain,ГўВҖВҷ replied I.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWell, as after my death the command devolves on you as mate, assume
the command, and bear up for the Island of Elba, disembark at
Porto-Ferrajo, ask for the grand-marshal, give him this letterГўВҖВ”perhaps
they will give you another letter, and charge you with a commission.
You will accomplish what I was to have done, and derive all the honor
and profit from it.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI will do it, captain; but perhaps I shall not be admitted to the
grand-marshalГўВҖВҷs presence as easily as you expect?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳHere is a ring that will obtain audience of him, and remove every
difficulty,ГўВҖВҷ said the captain. At these words he gave me a ring. It was
timeГўВҖВ”two hours after he was delirious; the next day he died.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what did you do then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat I ought to have done, and what everyone would have done in my
place. Everywhere the last requests of a dying man are sacred; but with
a sailor the last requests of his superior are commands. I sailed for
the Island of Elba, where I arrived the next day; I ordered everybody
to remain on board, and went on shore alone. As I had expected, I found
some difficulty in obtaining access to the grand-marshal; but I sent
the ring I had received from the captain to him, and was instantly
admitted. He questioned me concerning Captain LeclereГўВҖВҷs death; and, as
the latter had told me, gave me a letter to carry on to a person in
Paris. I undertook it because it was what my captain had bade me do. I
landed here, regulated the affairs of the vessel, and hastened to visit
my affianced bride, whom I found more lovely than ever. Thanks to M.
Morrel, all the forms were got over; in a word I was, as I told you, at
my marriage feast; and I should have been married in an hour, and
tomorrow I intended to start for Paris, had I not been arrested on this
charge which you as well as I now see to be unjust.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңthis seems to me the truth. If you have been
culpable, it was imprudence, and this imprudence was in obedience to
the orders of your captain. Give up this letter you have brought from
Elba, and pass your word you will appear should you be required, and go
and rejoin your friends.

ГўВҖВңI am free, then, sir?ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs joyfully.

ГўВҖВңYes; but first give me this letter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have it already, for it was taken from me with some others which I
see in that packet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStop a moment,ГўВҖВқ said the deputy, as DantГғВЁs took his hat and gloves.
ГўВҖВңTo whom is it addressed?ГўВҖВқ

_ГўВҖВңTo Monsieur Noirtier, Rue Coq-HГғВ©ron, Paris.ГўВҖВқ_ Had a thunderbolt
fallen into the room, Villefort could not have been more stupefied. He
sank into his seat, and hastily turning over the packet, drew forth the
fatal letter, at which he glanced with an expression of terror.

ГўВҖВңM. Noirtier, Rue Coq-HГғВ©ron, No. 13,ГўВҖВқ murmured he, growing still paler.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңdo you know him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort; ГўВҖВңa faithful servant of the king does not know
conspirators.ГўВҖВқ

0103m



ГўВҖВңIt is a conspiracy, then?ГўВҖВқ asked DantГғВЁs, who after believing himself
free, now began to feel a tenfold alarm. ГўВҖВңI have, however, already told
you, sir, I was entirely ignorant of the contents of the letter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but you knew the name of the person to whom it was addressed,ГўВҖВқ
said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңI was forced to read the address to know to whom to give it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you shown this letter to anyone?ГўВҖВқ asked Villefort, becoming still
more pale.

ГўВҖВңTo no one, on my honor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEverybody is ignorant that you are the bearer of a letter from the
Island of Elba, and addressed to M. Noirtier?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEverybody, except the person who gave it to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd that was too much, far too much,ГўВҖВқ murmured Villefort. VillefortГўВҖВҷs
brow darkened more and more, his white lips and clenched teeth filled
