his face with his hands.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs timidly, ГўВҖВңwhat is the matter?ГўВҖВқ Villefort made no
answer, but raised his head at the expiration of a few seconds, and
again perused the letter.

ГўВҖВңAnd you say that you are ignorant of the contents of this letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI give you my word of honor, sir,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңbut what is the
matter? You are illГўВҖВ”shall I ring for assistance?ГўВҖВ”shall I call?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, rising hastily; ГўВҖВңstay where you are. It is for me
to give orders here, and not you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs proudly, ГўВҖВңit was only to summon assistance
for you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI want none; it was a temporary indisposition. Attend to yourself;
answer me.ГўВҖВқ DantГғВЁs waited, expecting a question, but in vain. Villefort
fell back on his chair, passed his hand over his brow, moist with
perspiration, and, for the third time, read the letter.

ГўВҖВңOh, if he knows the contents of this!ГўВҖВқ murmured he, ГўВҖВңand that Noirtier
is the father of Villefort, I am lost!ГўВҖВқ And he fixed his eyes upon
Edmond as if he would have penetrated his thoughts.

ГўВҖВңOh, it is impossible to doubt it,ГўВҖВқ cried he, suddenly.

ГўВҖВңIn heavenГўВҖВҷs name!ГўВҖВқ cried the unhappy young man, ГўВҖВңif you doubt me,
question me; I will answer you.ГўВҖВқ Villefort made a violent effort, and
in a tone he strove to render firm:

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI am no longer able, as I had hoped, to restore you
immediately to liberty; before doing so, I must consult the trial
justice; what my own feeling is you already know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңyou have been rather a friend than a
judge.ГўВҖВқ

0105m



ГўВҖВңWell, I must detain you some time longer, but I will strive to make it
as short as possible. The principal charge against you is this letter,
and you seeГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ Villefort approached the fire, cast it in, and waited
until it was entirely consumed.

ГўВҖВңYou see, I destroy it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңyou are goodness itself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ continued Villefort; ГўВҖВңyou can now have confidence in me after
what I have done.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, command, and I will obey.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen; this is not a command, but advice I give you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSpeak, and I will follow your advice.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI shall detain you until this evening in the Palais de Justice. Should
anyone else interrogate you, say to him what you have said to me, but
do not breathe a word of this letter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI promise.ГўВҖВқ It was Villefort who seemed to entreat, and the prisoner
who reassured him.

ГўВҖВңYou see,ГўВҖВқ continued he, glancing toward the grate, where fragments of
burnt paper fluttered in the flames, ГўВҖВңthe letter is destroyed; you and
I alone know of its existence; should you, therefore, be questioned,
deny all knowledge of itГўВҖВ”deny it boldly, and you are saved.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBe satisfied; I will deny it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was the only letter you had?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSwear it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI swear it.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort rang. A police agent entered. Villefort whispered some words
in his ear, to which the officer replied by a motion of his head.

ГўВҖВңFollow him,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort to DantГғВЁs. DantГғВЁs saluted Villefort and
retired. Hardly had the door closed when Villefort threw himself
half-fainting into a chair.

ГўВҖВңAlas, alas,ГўВҖВқ murmured he, ГўВҖВңif the procureur himself had been at
Marseilles I should have been ruined. This accursed letter would have
destroyed all my hopes. Oh, my father, must your past career always
interfere with my successes?ГўВҖВқ Suddenly a light passed over his face, a
smile played round his set mouth, and his haggard eyes were fixed in
thought.

ГўВҖВңThis will do,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңand from this letter, which might have ruined
me, I will make my fortune. Now to the work I have in hand.ГўВҖВқ And after
having assured himself that the prisoner was gone, the deputy procureur
hastened to the house of his betrothed.

0107m




 Chapter 8. The ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf

The commissary of police, as he traversed the antechamber, made a sign
to two gendarmes, who placed themselves one on DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ right and the
other on his left. A door that communicated with the Palais de Justice
was opened, and they went through a long range of gloomy corridors,
whose appearance might have made even the boldest shudder. The Palais
de Justice communicated with the prison,ГўВҖВ”a sombre edifice, that from
its grated windows looks on the clock-tower of the Accoules. After
numberless windings, DantГғВЁs saw a door with an iron wicket. The
commissary took up an iron mallet and knocked thrice, every blow
seeming to DantГғВЁs as if struck on his heart. The door opened, the two
gendarmes gently pushed him forward, and the door closed with a loud
sound behind him. The air he inhaled was no longer pure, but thick and
mephitic,ГўВҖВ”he was in prison.

He was conducted to a tolerably neat chamber, but grated and barred,
and its appearance, therefore, did not greatly alarm him; besides, the
words of Villefort, who seemed to interest himself so much, resounded
still in his ears like a promise of freedom. It was four oГўВҖВҷclock when
DantГғВЁs was placed in this chamber. It was, as we have said, the 1st of
March, and the prisoner was soon buried in darkness. The obscurity
augmented the acuteness of his hearing; at the slightest sound he rose
and hastened to the door, convinced they were about to liberate him,
but the sound died away, and DantГғВЁs sank again into his seat. At last,
about ten oГўВҖВҷclock, and just as DantГғВЁs began to despair, steps were
heard in the corridor, a key turned in the lock, the bolts creaked, the
massy oaken door flew open, and a flood of light from two torches
pervaded the apartment.

By the torchlight DantГғВЁs saw the glittering sabres and carbines of four
gendarmes. He had advanced at first, but stopped at the sight of this
display of force.

ГўВҖВңAre you come to fetch me?ГўВҖВқ asked he.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied a gendarme.

ГўВҖВңBy the orders of the deputy procureur?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI believe so.ГўВҖВқ The conviction that they came from M. de Villefort
relieved all DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ apprehensions; he advanced calmly, and placed
himself in the centre of the escort. A carriage waited at the door, the
coachman was on the box, and a police officer sat beside him.

ГўВҖВңIs this carriage for me?ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңIt is for you,ГўВҖВқ replied a gendarme.

DantГғВЁs was about to speak; but feeling himself urged forward, and
having neither the power nor the intention to resist, he mounted the
steps, and was in an instant seated inside between two gendarmes; the
two others took their places opposite, and the carriage rolled heavily
over the stones.

The prisoner glanced at the windowsГўВҖВ”they were grated; he had changed
his prison for another that was conveying him he knew not whither.
Through the grating, however, DantГғВЁs saw they were passing through the
Rue Caisserie, and by the Rue Saint-Laurent and the Rue Taramis, to the
quay. Soon he saw the lights of La Consigne.

The carriage stopped, the officer descended, approached the guardhouse,
a dozen soldiers came out and formed themselves in order; DantГғВЁs saw
the reflection of their muskets by the light of the lamps on the quay.

ГўВҖВңCan all this force be summoned on my account?ГўВҖВқ thought he.

The officer opened the door, which was locked, and, without speaking a
word, answered DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ question; for he saw between the ranks of the
soldiers a passage formed from the carriage to the port. The two
gendarmes who were opposite to him descended first, then he was ordered
to alight and the gendarmes on each side of him followed his example.
They advanced towards a boat, which a custom-house officer held by a
chain, near the quay.

The soldiers looked at DantГғВЁs with an air of stupid curiosity. In an
instant he was placed in the stern-sheets of the boat, between the
gendarmes, while the officer stationed himself at the bow; a shove sent
the boat adrift, and four sturdy oarsmen impelled it rapidly towards
the Pilon. At a shout from the boat, the chain that closes the mouth of
the port was lowered and in a second they were, as DantГғВЁs knew, in the
Frioul and outside the inner harbor.

The prisonerГўВҖВҷs first feeling was of joy at again breathing the pure
airГўВҖВ”for air is freedom; but he soon sighed, for he passed before La
RГғВ©serve, where he had that morning been so happy, and now through the
open windows came the laughter and revelry of a ball. DantГғВЁs folded his
hands, raised his eyes to heaven, and prayed fervently.

0111m



The boat continued her voyage. They had passed the TГғВӘte de Mort, were
now off the Anse du Pharo, and about to double the battery. This
manГ…В“uvre was incomprehensible to DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңWhither are you taking me?ГўВҖВқ asked he.

ГўВҖВңYou will soon know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut stillГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe are forbidden to give you any explanation.ГўВҖВқ DantГғВЁs, trained in
discipline, knew that nothing would be more absurd than to question
subordinates, who were forbidden to reply; and so he remained silent.

The most vague and wild thoughts passed through his mind. The boat they
were in could not make a long voyage; there was no vessel at anchor
outside the harbor; he thought, perhaps, they were going to leave him
on some distant point. He was not bound, nor had they made any attempt
to handcuff him; this seemed a good augury. Besides, had not the
deputy, who had been so kind to him, told him that provided he did not
pronounce the dreaded name of Noirtier, he had nothing to apprehend?
Had not Villefort in his presence destroyed the fatal letter, the only
proof against him?

He waited silently, striving to pierce through the darkness.

They had left the Ile Ratonneau, where the lighthouse stood, on the
right, and were now opposite the Point des Catalans. It seemed to the
prisoner that he could distinguish a feminine form on the beach, for it
was there MercГғВ©dГғВЁs dwelt. How was it that a presentiment did not warn
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs that her lover was within three hundred yards of her?

One light alone was visible; and DantГғВЁs saw that it came from MercГғВ©dГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ
chamber. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs was the only one awake in the whole settlement. A
loud cry could be heard by her. But pride restrained him and he did not
utter it. What would his guards think if they heard him shout like a
madman?

He remained silent, his eyes fixed upon the light; the boat went on,
but the prisoner thought only of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs. An intervening elevation of
land hid the light. DantГғВЁs turned and perceived that they had got out
to sea. While he had been absorbed in thought, they had shipped their
oars and hoisted sail; the boat was now moving with the wind.

In spite of his repugnance to address the guards, DantГғВЁs turned to the
nearest gendarme, and taking his hand,

ГўВҖВңComrade,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI adjure you, as a Christian and a soldier, to
tell me where we are going. I am Captain DantГғВЁs, a loyal Frenchman,
thought accused of treason; tell me where you are conducting me, and I
promise you on my honor I will submit to my fate.ГўВҖВқ

The gendarme looked irresolutely at his companion, who returned for
answer a sign that said, ГўВҖВңI see no great harm in telling him now,ГўВҖВқ and
the gendarme replied:

ГўВҖВңYou are a native of Marseilles, and a sailor, and yet you do not know
where you are going?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn my honor, I have no idea.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you no idea whatever?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNone at all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is impossible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI swear to you it is true. Tell me, I entreat.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut my orders.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour orders do not forbid your telling me what I must know in ten
minutes, in half an hour, or an hour. You see I cannot escape, even if
I intended.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUnless you are blind, or have never been outside the harbor, you must
know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLook round you then.ГўВҖВқ DantГғВЁs rose and looked forward, when he saw rise
within a hundred yards of him the black and frowning rock on which
stands the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf. This gloomy fortress, which has for more than
three hundred years furnished food for so many wild legends, seemed to
DantГғВЁs like a scaffold to a malefactor.

ГўВҖВңThe ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf?ГўВҖВқ cried he, ГўВҖВңwhat are we going there for?ГўВҖВқ

The gendarme smiled.

ГўВҖВңI am not going there to be imprisoned,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңit is only used
for political prisoners. I have committed no crime. Are there any
magistrates or judges at the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere are only,ГўВҖВқ said the gendarme, ГўВҖВңa governor, a garrison, turnkeys,
and good thick walls. Come, come, do not look so astonished, or you
will make me think you are laughing at me in return for my good
nature.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs pressed the gendarmeГўВҖВҷs hand as though he would crush it.

ГўВҖВңYou think, then,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңthat I am taken to the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf to be
imprisoned there?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is probable; but there is no occasion to squeeze so hard.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWithout any inquiry, without any formality?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll the formalities have been gone through; the inquiry is already
made.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd so, in spite of M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs promises?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not know what M. de Villefort promised you,ГўВҖВқ said the gendarme,
ГўВҖВңbut I know we are taking you to the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf. But what are you
doing? Help, comrades, help!ГўВҖВқ

By a rapid movement, which the gendarmeГўВҖВҷs practiced eye had perceived,
DantГғВЁs sprang forward to precipitate himself into the sea; but four
vigorous arms seized him as his feet quitted the bottom of the boat. He
fell back cursing with rage.

ГўВҖВңGood!ГўВҖВқ said the gendarme, placing his knee on his chest; ГўВҖВңthis is the
way you keep your word as a sailor! Believe soft-spoken gentlemen
again! Hark ye, my friend, I have disobeyed my first order, but I will
not disobey the second; and if you move, I will blow your brains out.ГўВҖВқ
And he levelled his carbine at DantГғВЁs, who felt the muzzle against his
temple.

For a moment the idea of struggling crossed his mind, and of so ending
the unexpected evil that had overtaken him. But he bethought him of M.
de VillefortГўВҖВҷs promise; and, besides, death in a boat from the hand of
a gendarme seemed too terrible. He remained motionless, but gnashing
his teeth and wringing his hands with fury.

At this moment the boat came to a landing with a violent shock. One of
the sailors leaped on shore, a cord creaked as it ran through a pulley,
and DantГғВЁs guessed they were at the end of the voyage, and that they
were mooring the boat.

His guards, taking him by the arms and coat-collar, forced him to rise,
and dragged him towards the steps that lead to the gate of the
fortress, while the police officer carrying a musket with fixed bayonet
followed behind.

DantГғВЁs made no resistance; he was like a man in a dream; he saw
soldiers drawn up on the embankment; he knew vaguely that he was
ascending a flight of steps; he was conscious that he passed through a
door, and that the door closed behind him; but all this indistinctly as
through a mist. He did not even see the ocean, that terrible barrier
against freedom, which the prisoners look upon with utter despair.

They halted for a minute, during which he strove to collect his
thoughts. He looked around; he was in a court surrounded by high walls;
he heard the measured tread of sentinels, and as they passed before the
light he saw the barrels of their muskets shine.

They waited upwards of ten minutes. Certain DantГғВЁs could not escape,
the gendarmes released him. They seemed awaiting orders. The orders
came.

ГўВҖВңWhere is the prisoner?ГўВҖВқ said a voice.

ГўВҖВңHere,ГўВҖВқ replied the gendarmes.

ГўВҖВңLet him follow me; I will take him to his cell.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo!ГўВҖВқ said the gendarmes, thrusting DantГғВЁs forward.

The prisoner followed his guide, who led him into a room almost under
ground, whose bare and reeking walls seemed as though impregnated with
tears; a lamp placed on a stool illumined the apartment faintly, and
showed DantГғВЁs the features of his conductor, an under-jailer,
ill-clothed, and of sullen appearance.

0113m



ГўВҖВңHere is your chamber for tonight,ГўВҖВқ said he. ГўВҖВңIt is late, and the
governor is asleep. Tomorrow, perhaps, he may change you. In the
meantime there is bread, water, and fresh straw; and that is all a
prisoner can wish for. Goodnight.ГўВҖВқ And before DantГғВЁs could open his
mouthГўВҖВ”before he had noticed where the jailer placed his bread or the
waterГўВҖВ”before he had glanced towards the corner where the straw was, the
jailer disappeared, taking with him the lamp and closing the door,
leaving stamped upon the prisonerГўВҖВҷs mind the dim reflection of the
dripping walls of his dungeon.

DantГғВЁs was alone in darkness and in silenceГўВҖВ”cold as the shadows that he
felt breathe on his burning forehead. With the first dawn of day the
jailer returned, with orders to leave DantГғВЁs where he was. He found the
prisoner in the same position, as if fixed there, his eyes swollen with
weeping. He had passed the night standing, and without sleep. The
jailer advanced; DantГғВЁs appeared not to perceive him. He touched him on
the shoulder. Edmond started.

ГўВҖВңHave you not slept?ГўВҖВқ said the jailer.

ГўВҖВңI do not know,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs. The jailer stared.

ГўВҖВңAre you hungry?ГўВҖВқ continued he.

ГўВҖВңI do not know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you wish for anything?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI wish to see the governor.ГўВҖВқ

The jailer shrugged his shoulders and left the chamber.

DantГғВЁs followed him with his eyes, and stretched forth his hands
towards the open door; but the door closed. All his emotion then burst
forth; he cast himself on the ground, weeping bitterly, and asking
himself what crime he had committed that he was thus punished.

The day passed thus; he scarcely tasted food, but walked round and
round the cell like a wild beast in its cage. One thought in particular
tormented him: namely, that during his journey hither he had sat so
still, whereas he might, a dozen times, have plunged into the sea, and,
thanks to his powers of swimming, for which he was famous, have gained
the shore, concealed himself until the arrival of a Genoese or Spanish
vessel, escaped to Spain or Italy, where MercГғВ©dГғВЁs and his father could
have joined him. He had no fears as to how he should liveГўВҖВ”good seamen
are welcome everywhere. He spoke Italian like a Tuscan, and Spanish
like a Castilian; he would have been free, and happy with MercГғВ©dГғВЁs and
his father, whereas he was now confined in the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf, that
impregnable fortress, ignorant of the future destiny of his father and
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs; and all this because he had trusted to VillefortГўВҖВҷs promise.
The thought was maddening, and DantГғВЁs threw himself furiously down on
his straw. The next morning at the same hour, the jailer came again.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said the jailer, ГўВҖВңare you more reasonable today?ГўВҖВқ DantГғВЁs made
no reply.

ГўВҖВңCome, cheer up; is there anything that I can do for you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI wish to see the governor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have already told you it was impossible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause it is against prison rules, and prisoners must not even ask
for it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is allowed, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBetter fare, if you pay for it, books, and leave to walk about.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not want books, I am satisfied with my food, and do not care to
walk about; but I wish to see the governor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf you worry me by repeating the same thing, I will not bring you any
more to eat.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then,ГўВҖВқ said Edmond, ГўВҖВңif you do not, I shall die of hungerГўВҖВ”that
is all.ГўВҖВқ

The jailer saw by his tone he would be happy to die; and as every
prisoner is worth ten sous a day to his jailer, he replied in a more
subdued tone.

ГўВҖВңWhat you ask is impossible; but if you are very well behaved you will
be allowed to walk about, and some day you will meet the governor, and
if he chooses to reply, that is his affair.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ asked DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңhow long shall I have to wait?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, a monthГўВҖВ”six monthsГўВҖВ”a year.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is too long a time. I wish to see him at once.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said the jailer, ГўВҖВңdo not always brood over what is impossible, or
you will be mad in a fortnight.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou think so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; we have an instance here; it was by always offering a million of
francs to the governor for his liberty that an abbГғВ© became mad, who was
in this chamber before you.ГўВҖВқ

0119m



ГўВҖВңHow long has he left it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTwo years.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas he liberated, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; he was put in a dungeon.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen!ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңI am not an abbГғВ©, I am not mad; perhaps I shall
be, but at present, unfortunately, I am not. I will make you another
offer.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not offer you a million, because I have it not; but I will give
you a hundred crowns if, the first time you go to Marseilles, you will
seek out a young girl named MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, at the Catalans, and give her two
lines from me.ГўВҖВқ

0120m



ГўВҖВңIf I took them, and were detected, I should lose my place, which is
worth two thousand francs a year; so that I should be a great fool to
run such a risk for three hundred.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңmark this; if you refuse at least to tell
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs I am here, I will some day hide myself behind the door, and
when you enter I will dash out your brains with this stool.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThreats!ГўВҖВқ cried the jailer, retreating and putting himself on the
defensive; ГўВҖВңyou are certainly going mad. The abbГғВ© began like you, and
in three days you will be like him, mad enough to tie up; but,
fortunately, there are dungeons here.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs whirled the stool round his head.

ГўВҖВңAll right, all right,ГўВҖВқ said the jailer; ГўВҖВңall right, since you will
have it so. I will send word to the governor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well,ГўВҖВқ returned DantГғВЁs, dropping the stool and sitting on it as
if he were in reality mad. The jailer went out, and returned in an
instant with a corporal and four soldiers.

ГўВҖВңBy the governorГўВҖВҷs orders,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңconduct the prisoner to the tier
beneath.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo the dungeon, then,ГўВҖВқ said the corporal.

ГўВҖВңYes; we must put the madman with the madmen.ГўВҖВқ The soldiers seized
DantГғВЁs, who followed passively.

He descended fifteen steps, and the door of a dungeon was opened, and
he was thrust in. The door closed, and DantГғВЁs advanced with
outstretched hands until he touched the wall; he then sat down in the
corner until his eyes became accustomed to the darkness. The jailer was
right; DantГғВЁs wanted but little of being utterly mad.



 Chapter 9. The Evening of the Betrothal

Villefort had, as we have said, hastened back to Madame de
Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs in the Place du Grand Cours, and on entering the house
found that the guests whom he had left at table were taking coffee in
the salon. RenГғВ©e was, with all the rest of the company, anxiously
awaiting him, and his entrance was followed by a general exclamation.

ГўВҖВңWell, Decapitator, Guardian of the State, Royalist, Brutus, what is
the matter?ГўВҖВқ said one. ГўВҖВңSpeak out.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre we threatened with a fresh Reign of Terror?ГўВҖВқ asked another.

ГўВҖВңHas the Corsican ogre broken loose?ГўВҖВқ cried a third.

ГўВҖВңMarquise,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, approaching his future mother-in-law, ГўВҖВңI
request your pardon for thus leaving you. Will the marquis honor me by
a few momentsГўВҖВҷ private conversation?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, it is really a serious matter, then?ГўВҖВқ asked the marquis, remarking
the cloud on VillefortГўВҖВҷs brow.

ГўВҖВңSo serious that I must take leave of you for a few days; so,ГўВҖВқ added
he, turning to RenГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңjudge for yourself if it be not important.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are going to leave us?ГўВҖВқ cried RenГғВ©e, unable to hide her emotion at
this unexpected announcement.

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ returned Villefort, ГўВҖВңI must!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere, then, are you going?ГўВҖВқ asked the marquise.

ГўВҖВңThat, madame, is an official secret; but if you have any commissions
for Paris, a friend of mine is going there tonight, and will with
pleasure undertake them.ГўВҖВқ The guests looked at each other.

ГўВҖВңYou wish to speak to me alone?ГўВҖВқ said the marquis.

ГўВҖВңYes, let us go to the library, please.ГўВҖВқ The marquis took his arm, and
they left the salon.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ asked he, as soon as they were by themselves, ГўВҖВңtell me what it
is?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn affair of the greatest importance, that demands my immediate
presence in Paris. Now, excuse the indiscretion, marquis, but have you
any landed property?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll my fortune is in the funds; seven or eight hundred thousand
francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen sell outГўВҖВ”sell out, marquis, or you will lose it all.ГўВҖВқ

0123m



ГўВҖВңBut how can I sell out here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have a broker, have you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen give me a letter to him, and tell him to sell out without an
instantГўВҖВҷs delay, perhaps even now I shall arrive too late.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe deuce you say!ГўВҖВқ replied the marquis, ГўВҖВңlet us lose no time, then!ГўВҖВқ

And, sitting down, he wrote a letter to his broker, ordering him to
sell out at the market price.

ГўВҖВңNow, then,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, placing the letter in his pocketbook, ГўВҖВңI
must have another!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo the king.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo the king?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI dare not write to his majesty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not ask you to write to his majesty, but ask M. de Salvieux to do
so. I want a letter that will enable me to reach the kingГўВҖВҷs presence
without all the formalities of demanding an audience; that would
occasion a loss of precious time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut address yourself to the keeper of the seals; he has the right of
entry at the Tuileries, and can procure you audience at any hour of the
day or night.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless; but there is no occasion to divide the honors of my
discovery with him. The keeper would leave me in the background, and
take all the glory to himself. I tell you, marquis, my fortune is made
if I only reach the Tuileries the first, for the king will not forget
the service I do him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn that case go and get ready. I will call Salvieux and make him write
the letter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBe as quick as possible, I must be on the road in a quarter of an
hour.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell your coachman to stop at the door.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will present my excuses to the marquise and Mademoiselle RenГғВ©e,
whom I leave on such a day with great regret.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will find them both here, and can make your farewells in person.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA thousand thanksГўВҖВ”and now for the letter.ГўВҖВқ

The marquis rang, a servant entered.

ГўВҖВңSay to the Comte de Salvieux that I would like to see him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow, then, go,ГўВҖВқ said the marquis.

ГўВҖВңI shall be gone only a few moments.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort hastily quitted the apartment, but reflecting that the sight
of the deputy procureur running through the streets would be enough to
throw the whole city into confusion, he resumed his ordinary pace. At
his door he perceived a figure in the shadow that seemed to wait for
him. It was MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, who, hearing no news of her lover, had come
unobserved to inquire after him.

As Villefort drew near, she advanced and stood before him. DantГғВЁs had
spoken of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, and Villefort instantly recognized her. Her beauty
and high bearing surprised him, and when she inquired what had become
of her lover, it seemed to him that she was the judge, and he the
accused.

ГўВҖВңThe young man you speak of,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort abruptly, ГўВҖВңis a great
criminal, and I can do nothing for him, mademoiselle.ГўВҖВқ MercГғВ©dГғВЁs burst
into tears, and, as Villefort strove to pass her, again addressed him.

ГўВҖВңBut, at least, tell me where he is, that I may know whether he is
alive or dead,ГўВҖВқ said she.

0125m



ГўВҖВңI do not know; he is no longer in my hands,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort.

And desirous of putting an end to the interview, he pushed by her, and
closed the door, as if to exclude the pain he felt. But remorse is not
thus banished; like VirgilГўВҖВҷs wounded hero, he carried the arrow in his
wound, and, arrived at the salon, Villefort uttered a sigh that was
almost a sob, and sank into a chair.

Then the first pangs of an unending torture seized upon his heart. The
man he sacrificed to his ambition, that innocent victim immolated on
the altar of his fatherГўВҖВҷs faults, appeared to him pale and threatening,
leading his affianced bride by the hand, and bringing with him remorse,
not such as the ancients figured, furious and terrible, but that slow
and consuming agony whose pangs are intensified from hour to hour up to
the very moment of death. Then he had a momentГўВҖВҷs hesitation. He had
frequently called for capital punishment on criminals, and owing to his
irresistible eloquence they had been condemned, and yet the slightest
shadow of remorse had never clouded VillefortГўВҖВҷs brow, because they were
guilty; at least, he believed so; but here was an innocent man whose
happiness he had destroyed. In this case he was not the judge, but the
executioner.

As he thus reflected, he felt the sensation we have described, and
which had hitherto been unknown to him, arise in his bosom, and fill
him with vague apprehensions. It is thus that a wounded man trembles
instinctively at the approach of the finger to his wound until it be
healed, but VillefortГўВҖВҷs was one of those that never close, or if they
do, only close to reopen more agonizing than ever. If at this moment
the sweet voice of RenГғВ©e had sounded in his ears pleading for mercy, or
the fair MercГғВ©dГғВЁs had entered and said, ГўВҖВңIn the name of God, I conjure
you to restore me my affianced husband,ГўВҖВқ his cold and trembling hands
would have signed his release; but no voice broke the stillness of the
chamber, and the door was opened only by VillefortГўВҖВҷs valet, who came to
tell him that the travelling carriage was in readiness.

Villefort rose, or rather sprang, from his chair, hastily opened one of
the drawers of his desk, emptied all the gold it contained into his
pocket, stood motionless an instant, his hand pressed to his head,
muttered a few inarticulate sounds, and then, perceiving that his
servant had placed his cloak on his shoulders, he sprang into the
carriage, ordering the postilions to drive to M. de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs. The
hapless DantГғВЁs was doomed.

As the marquis had promised, Villefort found the marquise and RenГғВ©e in
waiting. He started when he saw RenГғВ©e, for he fancied she was again
about to plead for DantГғВЁs. Alas, her emotions were wholly personal: she
was thinking only of VillefortГўВҖВҷs departure.

She loved Villefort, and he left her at the moment he was about to
become her husband. Villefort knew not when he should return, and
RenГғВ©e, far from pleading for DantГғВЁs, hated the man whose crime
separated her from her lover.

0127m



Meanwhile what of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs? She had met Fernand at the corner of the
Rue de la Loge; she had returned to the Catalans, and had despairingly
cast herself on her couch. Fernand, kneeling by her side, took her
hand, and covered it with kisses that MercГғВ©dГғВЁs did not even feel. She
passed the night thus. The lamp went out for want of oil, but she paid
no heed to the darkness, and dawn came, but she knew not that it was
day. Grief had made her blind to all but one objectГўВҖВ”that was Edmond.

ГўВҖВңAh, you are there,ГўВҖВқ said she, at length, turning towards Fernand.

ГўВҖВңI have not quitted you since yesterday,ГўВҖВқ returned Fernand sorrowfully.

M. Morrel had not readily given up the fight. He had learned that
DantГғВЁs had been taken to prison, and he had gone to all his friends,
and the influential persons of the city; but the report was already in
circulation that DantГғВЁs was arrested as a Bonapartist agent; and as the
most sanguine looked upon any attempt of Napoleon to remount the throne
as impossible, he met with nothing but refusal, and had returned home
in despair, declaring that the matter was serious and that nothing more
could be done.

Caderousse was equally restless and uneasy, but instead of seeking,
like M. Morrel, to aid DantГғВЁs, he had shut himself up with two bottles
of black currant brandy, in the hope of drowning reflection. But he did
not succeed, and became too intoxicated to fetch any more drink, and
yet not so intoxicated as to forget what had happened. With his elbows
on the table he sat between the two empty bottles, while spectres
danced in the light of the unsnuffed candleГўВҖВ”spectres such as Hoffmann
strews over his punch-drenched pages, like black, fantastic dust.

Danglars alone was content and joyousГўВҖВ”he had got rid of an enemy and
made his own situation on the _Pharaon_ secure. Danglars was one of
those men born with a pen behind the ear, and an inkstand in place of a
heart. Everything with him was multiplication or subtraction. The life
of a man was to him of far less value than a numeral, especially when,
by taking it away, he could increase the sum total of his own desires.
He went to bed at his usual hour, and slept in peace.

Villefort, after having received M. de SalvieuxГўВҖВҷs letter, embraced
RenГғВ©e, kissed the marquiseГўВҖВҷs hand, and shaken that of the marquis,
started for Paris along the Aix road.

Old DantГғВЁs was dying with anxiety to know what had become of Edmond.
But we know very well what had become of Edmond.



 Chapter 10. The KingГўВҖВҷs Closet at the Tuileries

We will leave Villefort on the road to Paris, travellingГўВҖВ”thanks to
trebled feesГўВҖВ”with all speed, and passing through two or three
apartments, enter at the Tuileries the little room with the arched
window, so well known as having been the favorite closet of Napoleon
and Louis XVIII., and now of Louis Philippe.

There, seated before a walnut table he had brought with him from
Hartwell, and to which, from one of those fancies not uncommon to great
people, he was particularly attached, the king, Louis XVIII., was
carelessly listening to a man of fifty or fifty-two years of age, with
gray hair, aristocratic bearing, and exceedingly gentlemanly attire,
and meanwhile making a marginal note in a volume of GryphiusГўВҖВҷs rather
inaccurate, but much sought-after, edition of HoraceГўВҖВ”a work which was
much indebted to the sagacious observations of the philosophical
monarch.

ГўВҖВңYou say, sirГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ said the king.

ГўВҖВңThat I am exceedingly disquieted, sire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally, have you had a vision of the seven fat kine and the seven lean
kine?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, sire, for that would only betoken for us seven years of plenty and
seven years of scarcity; and with a king as full of foresight as your
majesty, scarcity is not a thing to be feared.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen of what other scourge are you afraid, my dear Blacas?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, I have every reason to believe that a storm is brewing in the
south.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, my dear duke,ГўВҖВқ replied Louis XVIII., ГўВҖВңI think you are wrongly
informed, and know positively that, on the contrary, it is very fine
weather in that direction.ГўВҖВқ Man of ability as he was, Louis XVIII.
liked a pleasant jest.

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ continued M. de Blacas, ГўВҖВңif it only be to reassure a faithful
servant, will your majesty send into Languedoc, Provence, and DauphinГғВ©,
trusty men, who will bring you back a faithful report as to the feeling
in these three provinces?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Canimus surdis_,ГўВҖВқ replied the king, continuing the annotations in his
Horace.

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ replied the courtier, laughing, in order that he might seem to
comprehend the quotation, ГўВҖВңyour majesty may be perfectly right in
relying on the good feeling of France, but I fear I am not altogether
wrong in dreading some desperate attempt.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy Bonaparte, or, at least, by his adherents.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear Blacas,ГўВҖВқ said the king, ГўВҖВңyou with your alarms prevent me from
working.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you, sire, prevent me from sleeping with your security.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWait, my dear sir, wait a moment; for I have such a delightful note on
the _Pastor quum traheret_ГўВҖВ”wait, and I will listen to you afterwards.ГўВҖВқ

There was a brief pause, during which Louis XVIII. wrote, in a hand as
small as possible, another note on the margin of his Horace, and then
looking at the duke with the air of a man who thinks he has an idea of
his own, while he is only commenting upon the idea of another, said:

ГўВҖВңGo on, my dear duke, go onГўВҖВ”I listen.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ said Blacas, who had for a moment the hope of sacrificing
Villefort to his own profit, ГўВҖВңI am compelled to tell you that these are
not mere rumors destitute of foundation which thus disquiet me; but a
serious-minded man, deserving all my confidence, and charged by me to
watch over the southГўВҖВқ (the duke hesitated as he pronounced these
words), ГўВҖВңhas arrived by post to tell me that a great peril threatens
the king, and so I hastened to you, sire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Mala ducis avi domum_,ГўВҖВқ continued Louis XVIII., still annotating.

ГўВҖВңDoes your majesty wish me to drop the subject?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy no means, my dear duke; but just stretch out your hand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhichever you pleaseГўВҖВ”there to the left.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHere, sire?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI tell you to the left, and you are looking to the right; I mean on my
leftГўВҖВ”yes, there. You will find yesterdayГўВҖВҷs report of the minister of
police. But here is M. DandrГғВ© himself;ГўВҖВқ and M. DandrГғВ©, announced by the
chamberlain-in-waiting, entered.

ГўВҖВңCome in,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., with repressed smile, ГўВҖВңcome in, Baron,
and tell the duke all you knowГўВҖВ”the latest news of M. de Bonaparte; do
not conceal anything, however serious,ГўВҖВ”let us see, the Island of Elba
is a volcano, and we may expect to have issuing thence flaming and
bristling warГўВҖВ”_bella, horrida bella_.ГўВҖВқ

M. DandrГғВ© leaned very respectfully on the back of a chair with his two
hands, and said:

ГўВҖВңHas your majesty perused yesterdayГўВҖВҷs report?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes; but tell the duke himself, who cannot find anything, what
the report containsГўВҖВ”give him the particulars of what the usurper is
doing in his islet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ said the baron to the duke, ГўВҖВңall the servants of his
majesty must approve of the latest intelligence which we have from the
Island of Elba. BonaparteГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

M. DandrГғВ© looked at Louis XVIII., who, employed in writing a note, did
not even raise his head. ГўВҖВңBonaparte,ГўВҖВқ continued the baron, ГўВҖВңis mortally
wearied, and passes whole days in watching his miners at work at
Porto-Longone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd scratches himself for amusement,ГўВҖВқ added the king.

ГўВҖВңScratches himself?ГўВҖВқ inquired the duke, ГўВҖВңwhat does your majesty mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, indeed, my dear duke. Did you forget that this great man, this
hero, this demigod, is attacked with a malady of the skin which worries
him to death, _prurigo_?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd, moreover, my dear duke,ГўВҖВқ continued the minister of police, ГўВҖВңwe
are almost assured that, in a very short time, the usurper will be
insane.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңInsane?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRaving mad; his head becomes weaker. Sometimes he weeps bitterly,
sometimes laughs boisterously, at other time he passes hours on the
seashore, flinging stones in the water and when the flint makes
ГўВҖВҳduck-and-drakeГўВҖВҷ five or six times, he appears as delighted as if he
had gained another Marengo or Austerlitz. Now, you must agree that
these are indubitable symptoms of insanity.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOr of wisdom, my dear baronГўВҖВ”or of wisdom,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII.,
laughing; ГўВҖВңthe greatest captains of antiquity amused themselves by
casting pebbles into the oceanГўВҖВ”see PlutarchГўВҖВҷs life of Scipio
Africanus.ГўВҖВқ

M. de Blacas pondered deeply between the confident monarch and the
truthful minister. Villefort, who did not choose to reveal the whole
secret, lest another should reap all the benefit of the disclosure, had
yet communicated enough to cause him the greatest uneasiness.

ГўВҖВңWell, well, DandrГғВ©,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., ГўВҖВңBlacas is not yet convinced;
let us proceed, therefore, to the usurperГўВҖВҷs conversion.ГўВҖВқ The minister
of police bowed.

ГўВҖВңThe usurperГўВҖВҷs conversion!ГўВҖВқ murmured the duke, looking at the king and
DandrГғВ©, who spoke alternately, like VirgilГўВҖВҷs shepherds. ГўВҖВңThe usurper
converted!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDecidedly, my dear duke.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn what way converted?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo good principles. Tell him all about it, baron.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, this is the way of it,ГўВҖВқ said the minister, with the gravest air
in the world: ГўВҖВңNapoleon lately had a review, and as two or three of his
old veterans expressed a desire to return to France, he gave them their
dismissal, and exhorted them to ГўВҖВҳserve the good king.ГўВҖВҷ These were his
own words, of that I am certain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, Blacas, what think you of this?ГўВҖВқ inquired the king triumphantly,
and pausing for a moment from the voluminous scholiast before him.

ГўВҖВңI say, sire, that the minister of police is greatly deceived or I am;
and as it is impossible it can be the minister of police as he has the
guardianship of the safety and honor of your majesty, it is probable
that I am in error. However, sire, if I might advise, your majesty will
interrogate the person of whom I spoke to you, and I will urge your
majesty to do him this honor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMost willingly, duke; under your auspices I will receive any person
you please, but you must not expect me to be too confiding. Baron, have
you any report more recent than this, dated the 20th February, and this
is the 3rd of March?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, sire, but I am hourly expecting one; it may have arrived since I
left my office.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo thither, and if there be noneГўВҖВ”well, well,ГўВҖВқ continued Louis XVIII.,
ГўВҖВңmake one; that is the usual way, is it not?ГўВҖВқ and the king laughed
facetiously.

ГўВҖВңOh, sire,ГўВҖВқ replied the minister, ГўВҖВңwe have no occasion to invent any;
every day our desks are loaded with most circumstantial denunciations,
coming from hosts of people who hope for some return for services which
they seek to render, but cannot; they trust to fortune, and rely upon
some unexpected event in some way to justify their predictions.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sir, go,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., ГўВҖВңand remember that I am waiting for
you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will but go and return, sire; I shall be back in ten minutes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I, sire,ГўВҖВқ said M. de Blacas, ГўВҖВңwill go and find my messenger.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWait, sir, wait,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII. ГўВҖВңReally, M. de Blacas, I must
change your armorial bearings; I will give you an eagle with
outstretched wings, holding in its claws a prey which tries in vain to
escape, and bearing this deviceГўВҖВ”_Tenax_.ГўВҖВқ

0133m



ГўВҖВңSire, I listen,ГўВҖВқ said De Blacas, biting his nails with impatience.

ГўВҖВңI wish to consult you on this passage, ГўВҖВҳ_Molli fugiens anhelitu_,ГўВҖВҷ you
know it refers to a stag flying from a wolf. Are you not a sportsman
and a great wolf-hunter? Well, then, what do you think of the _molli
anhelitu_?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAdmirable, sire; but my messenger is like the stag you refer to, for
he has posted two hundred and twenty leagues in scarcely three days.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich is undergoing great fatigue and anxiety, my dear duke, when we
have a telegraph which transmits messages in three or four hours, and
that without getting in the least out of breath.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, sire, you recompense but badly this poor young man, who has come
so far, and with so much ardor, to give your majesty useful
information. If only for the sake of M. de Salvieux, who recommends him
to me, I entreat your majesty to receive him graciously.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Salvieux, my brotherГўВҖВҷs chamberlain?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is at Marseilles.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd writes me thence.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoes he speak to you of this conspiracy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; but strongly recommends M. de Villefort, and begs me to present
him to your majesty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Villefort!ГўВҖВқ cried the king, ГўВҖВңis the messengerГўВҖВҷs name M. de
Villefort?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd he comes from Marseilles?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn person.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy did you not mention his name at once?ГўВҖВқ replied the king, betraying
some uneasiness.

ГўВҖВңSire, I thought his name was unknown to your majesty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no, Blacas; he is a man of strong and elevated understanding,
ambitious, too, and, _pardieu!_ you know his fatherГўВҖВҷs name!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHis father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, Noirtier.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNoirtier the Girondin?ГўВҖВ”Noirtier the senator?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe himself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd your majesty has employed the son of such a man?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBlacas, my friend, you have but limited comprehension. I told you
Villefort was ambitious, and to attain this ambition Villefort would
sacrifice everything, even his father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, sire, may I present him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis instant, duke! Where is he?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWaiting below, in my carriage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSeek him at once.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hasten to do so.ГўВҖВқ

The duke left the royal presence with the speed of a young man; his
really sincere royalism made him youthful again. Louis XVIII. remained
alone, and turning his eyes on his half-opened Horace, muttered:

ГўВҖВң_Justum et tenacem propositi virum_.ГўВҖВқ

M. de Blacas returned as speedily as he had departed, but in the
antechamber he was forced to appeal to the kingГўВҖВҷs authority.
VillefortГўВҖВҷs dusty garb, his costume, which was not of courtly cut,
excited the susceptibility of M. de BrezГғВ©, who was all astonishment at
finding that this young man had the audacity to enter before the king
in such attire. The duke, however, overcame all difficulties with a
wordГўВҖВ”his majestyГўВҖВҷs order; and, in spite of the protestations which the
master of ceremonies made for the honor of his office and principles,
Villefort was introduced.

The king was seated in the same place where the duke had left him. On
opening the door, Villefort found himself facing him, and the young
magistrateГўВҖВҷs first impulse was to pause.

ГўВҖВңCome in, M. de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ said the king, ГўВҖВңcome in.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort bowed, and advancing a few steps, waited until the king
should interrogate him.

ГўВҖВңM. de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., ГўВҖВңthe Duc de Blacas assures me you
have some interesting information to communicate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, the duke is right, and I believe your majesty will think it
equally important.ГўВҖВқ

0137m



ГўВҖВңIn the first place, and before everything else, sir, is the news as
bad in your opinion as I am asked to believe?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, I believe it to be most urgent, but I hope, by the speed I have
used, that it is not irreparable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSpeak as fully as you please, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the king, who began to give
way to the emotion which had showed itself in BlacasГўВҖВҷs face and
affected VillefortГўВҖВҷs voice. ГўВҖВңSpeak, sir, and pray begin at the
beginning; I like order in everything.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңI will render a faithful report to your
majesty, but I must entreat your forgiveness if my anxiety leads to
some obscurity in my language.ГўВҖВқ A glance at the king after this
discreet and subtle exordium, assured Villefort of the benignity of his
august auditor, and he went on:

ГўВҖВңSire, I have come as rapidly to Paris as possible, to inform your
majesty that I have discovered, in the exercise of my duties, not a
commonplace and insignificant plot, such as is every day got up in the
lower ranks of the people and in the army, but an actual conspiracyГўВҖВ”a
storm which menaces no less than your majestyГўВҖВҷs throne. Sire, the
usurper is arming three ships, he meditates some project, which,
however mad, is yet, perhaps, terrible. At this moment he will have
left Elba, to go whither I know not, but assuredly to attempt a landing
either at Naples, or on the coast of Tuscany, or perhaps on the shores
of France. Your majesty is well aware that the sovereign of the Island
of Elba has maintained his relations with Italy and France?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the king, much agitated; ГўВҖВңand recently we have had
information that the Bonapartist clubs have had meetings in the Rue
Saint-Jacques. But proceed, I beg of you. How did you obtain these
details?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, they are the results of an examination which I have made of a
man of Marseilles, whom I have watched for some time, and arrested on
the day of my departure. This person, a sailor, of turbulent character,
and whom I suspected of Bonapartism, has been secretly to the Island of
Elba. There he saw the grand-marshal, who charged him with an oral
message to a Bonapartist in Paris, whose name I could not extract from
him; but this mission was to prepare menГўВҖВҷs minds for a return (it is
the man who says this, sire)ГўВҖВ”a return which will soon occur.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd where is this man?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn prison, sire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd the matter seems serious to you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo serious, sire, that when the circumstance surprised me in the midst
of a family festival, on the very day of my betrothal, I left my bride
and friends, postponing everything, that I might hasten to lay at your
majestyГўВҖВҷs feet the fears which impressed me, and the assurance of my
devotion.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., ГўВҖВңwas there not a marriage engagement between
you and Mademoiselle de Saint-MГғВ©ran?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDaughter of one of your majestyГўВҖВҷs most faithful servants.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes; but let us talk of this plot, M. de Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, I fear it is more than a plot; I fear it is a conspiracy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA conspiracy in these times,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., smiling, ГўВҖВңis a thing
very easy to meditate, but more difficult to conduct to an end,
inasmuch as, re-established so recently on the throne of our ancestors,
we have our eyes open at once upon the past, the present, and the
future. For the last ten months my ministers have redoubled their
vigilance, in order to watch the shore of the Mediterranean. If
Bonaparte landed at Naples, the whole coalition would be on foot before
he could even reach Piombino; if he land in Tuscany, he will be in an
unfriendly territory; if he land in France, it must be with a handful
of men, and the result of that is easily foretold, execrated as he is
by the population. Take courage, sir; but at the same time rely on our
royal gratitude.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, here is M. DandrГғВ©!ГўВҖВқ cried de Blacas. At this instant the minister
of police appeared at the door, pale, trembling, and as if ready to
faint. Villefort was about to retire, but M. de Blacas, taking his
hand, restrained him.



 Chapter 11. The Corsican Ogre

At the sight of this agitation Louis XVIII. pushed from him violently
the table at which he was sitting.

ГўВҖВңWhat ails you, baron?ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed. ГўВҖВңYou appear quite aghast. Has
your uneasiness anything to do with what M. de Blacas has told me, and
M. de Villefort has just confirmed?ГўВҖВқ M. de Blacas moved suddenly
towards the baron, but the fright of the courtier pleaded for the
forbearance of the statesman; and besides, as matters were, it was much
more to his advantage that the prefect of police should triumph over
him than that he should humiliate the prefect.

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ stammered the baron.

ГўВҖВңWell, what is it?ГўВҖВқ asked Louis XVIII. The minister of police, giving
way to an impulse of despair, was about to throw himself at the feet of
Louis XVIII., who retreated a step and frowned.

ГўВҖВңWill you speak?ГўВҖВқ he said.

ГўВҖВңOh, sire, what a dreadful misfortune! I am, indeed, to be pitied. I
can never forgive myself!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., ГўВҖВңI command you to speak.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sire, the usurper left Elba on the 26th February, and landed on
the 1st of March.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd where? In Italy?ГўВҖВқ asked the king eagerly.

ГўВҖВңIn France, sire,ГўВҖВ”at a small port, near Antibes, in the Gulf of Juan.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe usurper landed in France, near Antibes, in the Gulf of Juan, two
hundred and fifty leagues from Paris, on the 1st of March, and you only
acquired this information today, the 3rd of March! Well, sir, what you
tell me is impossible. You must have received a false report, or you
have gone mad.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas, sire, it is but too true!ГўВҖВқ Louis made a gesture of indescribable
anger and alarm, and then drew himself up as if this sudden blow had
struck him at the same moment in heart and countenance.

ГўВҖВңIn France!ГўВҖВқ he cried, ГўВҖВңthe usurper in France! Then they did not watch
over this man. Who knows? they were, perhaps, in league with him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, sire,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the Duc de Blacas, ГўВҖВңM. DandrГғВ© is not a man to be
accused of treason! Sire, we have all been blind, and the minister of
police has shared the general blindness, that is all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңButГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, and then suddenly checking himself, he was
silent; then he continued, ГўВҖВңYour pardon, sire,ГўВҖВқ he said, bowing, ГўВҖВңmy
zeal carried me away. Will your majesty deign to excuse me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSpeak, sir, speak boldly,ГўВҖВқ replied Louis. ГўВҖВңYou alone forewarned us of
the evil; now try and aid us with the remedy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңthe usurper is detested in the south; and it
seems to me that if he ventured into the south, it would be easy to
raise Languedoc and Provence against him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, assuredly,ГўВҖВқ replied the minister; ГўВҖВңbut he is advancing by Gap and
Sisteron.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAdvancingГўВҖВ”he is advancing!ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII. ГўВҖВңIs he then advancing on
Paris?ГўВҖВқ The minister of police maintained a silence which was
equivalent to a complete avowal.

ГўВҖВңAnd DauphinГғВ©, sir?ГўВҖВқ inquired the king, of Villefort. ГўВҖВңDo you think it
possible to rouse that as well as Provence?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, I am sorry to tell your majesty a cruel fact; but the feeling in
DauphinГғВ© is quite the reverse of that in Provence or Languedoc. The
mountaineers are Bonapartists, sire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ murmured Louis, ГўВҖВңhe was well informed. And how many men had he
with him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not know, sire,ГўВҖВқ answered the minister of police.

ГўВҖВңWhat, you do not know! Have you neglected to obtain information on
that point? Of course it is of no consequence,ГўВҖВқ he added, with a
withering smile.

ГўВҖВңSire, it was impossible to learn; the despatch simply stated the fact
of the landing and the route taken by the usurper.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd how did this despatch reach you?ГўВҖВқ inquired the king. The minister
bowed his head, and while a deep color overspread his cheeks, he
stammered out:

ГўВҖВңBy the telegraph, sire.ГўВҖВқ Louis XVIII. advanced a step, and folded his
arms over his chest as Napoleon would have done.

0141m



ГўВҖВңSo then,ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed, turning pale with anger, ГўВҖВңseven conjoined and
allied armies overthrew that man. A miracle of heaven replaced me on
the throne of my fathers after five-and-twenty years of exile. I have,
during those five-and-twenty years, spared no pains to understand the
people of France and the interests which were confided to me; and now,
when I see the fruition of my wishes almost within reach, the power I
hold in my hands bursts and shatters me to atoms!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, it is fatality!ГўВҖВқ murmured the minister, feeling that the
pressure of circumstances, however light a thing to destiny, was too
much for any human strength to endure.

ГўВҖВңWhat our enemies say of us is then true. We have learnt nothing,
forgotten nothing! If I were betrayed as he was, I would console
myself; but to be in the midst of persons elevated by myself to places
of honor, who ought to watch over me more carefully than over
themselves,ГўВҖВ”for my fortune is theirsГўВҖВ”before me they were nothingГўВҖВ”after
me they will be nothing, and perish miserably from
incapacityГўВҖВ”ineptitude! Oh, yes, sir, you are rightГўВҖВ”it is fatality!ГўВҖВқ

The minister quailed before this outburst of sarcasm. M. de Blacas
wiped the moisture from his brow. Villefort smiled within himself, for
he felt his increased importance.

ГўВҖВңTo fall,ГўВҖВқ continued King Louis, who at the first glance had sounded
the abyss on which the monarchy hung suspended,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңto fall, and learn of
that fall by telegraph! Oh, I would rather mount the scaffold of my
brother, Louis XVI., than thus descend the staircase at the Tuileries
driven away by ridicule. Ridicule, sirГўВҖВ”why, you know not its power in
France, and yet you ought to know it!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, sire,ГўВҖВқ murmured the minister, ГўВҖВңfor pityГўВҖВҷsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңApproach, M. de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ resumed the king, addressing the young
man, who, motionless and breathless, was listening to a conversation on
which depended the destiny of a kingdom. ГўВҖВңApproach, and tell monsieur
that it is possible to know beforehand all that he has not known.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, it was really impossible to learn secrets which that man
concealed from all the world.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally impossible! YesГўВҖВ”that is a great word, sir. Unfortunately, there
are great words, as there are great men; I have measured them. Really
impossible for a minister who has an office, agents, spies, and fifteen
hundred thousand francs for secret service money, to know what is going
on at sixty leagues from the coast of France! Well, then, see, here is
a gentleman who had none of these resources at his disposalГўВҖВ”a
gentleman, only a simple magistrate, who learned more than you with all
your police, and who would have saved my crown, if, like you, he had
the power of directing a telegraph.ГўВҖВқ The look of the minister of police
was turned with concentrated spite on Villefort, who bent his head in
modest triumph.

ГўВҖВңI do not mean that for you, Blacas,ГўВҖВқ continued Louis XVIII.; ГўВҖВңfor if
you have discovered nothing, at least you have had the good sense to
persevere in your suspicions. Any other than yourself would have
considered the disclosure of M. de Villefort insignificant, or else
dictated by venal ambition.ГўВҖВқ These words were an allusion to the
sentiments which the minister of police had uttered with so much
confidence an hour before.

Villefort understood the kingГўВҖВҷs intent. Any other person would,
perhaps, have been overcome by such an intoxicating draught of praise;
but he feared to make for himself a mortal enemy of the police
minister, although he saw that DandrГғВ© was irrevocably lost. In fact,
the minister, who, in the plenitude of his power, had been unable to
unearth NapoleonГўВҖВҷs secret, might in despair at his own downfall
interrogate DantГғВЁs and so lay bare the motives of VillefortГўВҖВҷs plot.
Realizing this, Villefort came to the rescue of the crest-fallen
minister, instead of aiding to crush him.

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңthe suddenness of this event must prove to
your majesty that the issue is in the hands of Providence; what your
majesty is pleased to attribute to me as profound perspicacity is
simply owing to chance, and I have profited by that chance, like a good
and devoted servantГўВҖВ”thatГўВҖВҷs all. Do not attribute to me more than I
deserve, sire, that your majesty may never have occasion to recall the
first opinion you have been pleased to form of me.ГўВҖВқ The minister of
police thanked the young man by an eloquent look, and Villefort
understood that he had succeeded in his design; that is to say, that
without forfeiting the gratitude of the king, he had made a friend of
one on whom, in case of necessity, he might rely.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҷTis well,ГўВҖВқ resumed the king. ГўВҖВңAnd now, gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ he continued,
turning towards M. de Blacas and the minister of police, ГўВҖВңI have no
further occasion for you, and you may retire; what now remains to do is
in the department of the minister of war.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFortunately, sire,ГўВҖВқ said M. de Blacas, ГўВҖВңwe can rely on the army; your
majesty knows how every report confirms their loyalty and attachment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not mention reports, duke, to me, for I know now what confidence to
place in them. Yet, speaking of reports, baron, what have you learned
with regard to the affair in the Rue Saint-Jacques?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe affair in the Rue Saint-Jacques!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Villefort, unable to
repress an exclamation. Then, suddenly pausing, he added, ГўВҖВңYour pardon,
sire, but my devotion to your majesty has made me forget, not the
respect I have, for that is too deeply engraved in my heart, but the
rules of etiquette.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo on, go on, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the king; ГўВҖВңyou have today earned the right
to make inquiries here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ interposed the minister of police, ГўВҖВңI came a moment ago to give
your majesty fresh information which I had obtained on this head, when
your majestyГўВҖВҷs attention was attracted by the terrible event that has
occurred in the gulf, and now these facts will cease to interest your
majesty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary, sir,ГўВҖВ”on the contrary,ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., ГўВҖВңthis
affair seems to me to have a decided connection with that which
occupies our attention, and the death of General Quesnel will, perhaps,
put us on the direct track of a great internal conspiracy.ГўВҖВқ At the name
of General Quesnel, Villefort trembled.

ГўВҖВңEverything points to the conclusion, sire,ГўВҖВқ said the minister of
police, ГўВҖВңthat death was not the result of suicide, as we first
believed, but of assassination. General Quesnel, it appears, had just
left a Bonapartist club when he disappeared. An unknown person had been
with him that morning, and made an appointment with him in the Rue
Saint-Jacques; unfortunately, the generalГўВҖВҷs valet, who was dressing his
hair at the moment when the stranger entered, heard the street
mentioned, but did not catch the number.ГўВҖВқ As the police minister
related this to the king, Villefort, who looked as if his very life
hung on the speakerГўВҖВҷs lips, turned alternately red and pale. The king
looked towards him.

ГўВҖВңDo you not think with me, M. de Villefort, that General Quesnel, whom
they believed attached to the usurper, but who was really entirely
devoted to me, has perished the victim of a Bonapartist ambush?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is probable, sire,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort. ГўВҖВңBut is this all that is
known?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey are on the track of the man who appointed the meeting with him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn his track?ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңYes, the servant has given his description. He is a man of from fifty
to fifty-two years of age, dark, with black eyes covered with shaggy
eyebrows, and a thick moustache. He was dressed in a blue frock-coat,
buttoned up to the chin, and wore at his button-hole the rosette of an
officer of the Legion of Honor. Yesterday a person exactly
corresponding with this description was followed, but he was lost sight
of at the corner of the Rue de la Jussienne and the Rue Coq-HГғВ©ron.ГўВҖВқ
Villefort leaned on the back of an armchair, for as the minister of
police went on speaking he felt his legs bend under him; but when he
learned that the unknown had escaped the vigilance of the agent who
followed him, he breathed again.

ГўВҖВңContinue to seek for this man, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the king to the minister of
police; ГўВҖВңfor if, as I am all but convinced, General Quesnel, who would
have been so useful to us at this moment, has been murdered, his
assassins, Bonapartists or not, shall be cruelly punished.ГўВҖВқ It required
all VillefortГўВҖВҷs coolness not to betray the terror with which this
declaration of the king inspired him.

ГўВҖВңHow strange,ГўВҖВқ continued the king, with some asperity; ГўВҖВңthe police
think that they have disposed of the whole matter when they say, ГўВҖВҳA
murder has been committed,ГўВҖВҷ and especially so when they can add, ГўВҖВҳAnd
we are on the track of the guilty persons.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, your majesty will, I trust, be amply satisfied on this point at
least.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe shall see. I will no longer detain you, M. de Villefort, for you
must be fatigued after so long a journey; go and rest. Of course you
stopped at your fatherГўВҖВҷs?ГўВҖВқ A feeling of faintness came over Villefort.

0145m



ГўВҖВңNo, sire,ГўВҖВқ he replied, ГўВҖВңI alighted at the Hotel de Madrid, in the Rue
de Tournon.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you have seen him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSire, I went straight to the Duc de Blacas.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you will see him, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think not, sire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, I forgot,ГўВҖВқ said Louis, smiling in a manner which proved that all

Noirtier are not on the best terms possible, and that is another
sacrifice made to the royal cause, and for which you should be

ГўВҖВңSire, the kindness your majesty deigns to evince towards me is a

nothing more to ask for.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mind, sir, we will not forget you; make your mind easy. In the

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңyour majesty mistakes; this is an officerГўВҖВҷs

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi!_ГўВҖВқ said Louis XVIII., ГўВҖВңtake it, such as it is, for I have not
the time to procure you another. Blacas, let it be your care to see

ГўВҖВңAnd now,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңmay I inquire what are the orders with which your
majesty deigns to honor me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTake what rest you require, and remember that if you are not able to
serve me here in Paris, you may be of the greatest service to me at

ГўВҖВңSire,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, bowing, ГўВҖВңin an hour I shall have quitted

ГўВҖВңGo, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the king; ГўВҖВңand should I forget you (kingsГўВҖВҷ memories are

ГўВҖВңAh, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the minister of police to Villefort, as they left the

ГўВҖВңWill it be long first?ГўВҖВқ muttered Villefort, saluting the minister,
whose career was ended, and looking about him for a hackney-coach. One
passed at the moment, which he hailed; he gave his address to the
driver, and springing in, threw himself on the seat, and gave loose to
dreams of ambition.

Ten minutes afterwards Villefort reached his hotel, ordered horses to
be ready in two hours, and asked to have his breakfast brought to him.
He was about to begin his repast when the sound of the bell rang sharp
and loud. The valet opened the door, and Villefort heard someone speak
his name.

0147m



ГўВҖВңWho could know that I was here already?ГўВҖВқ said the young man. The valet
entered.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңwhat is it?ГўВҖВ”Who rang?ГўВҖВ”Who asked for me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA stranger who will not send in his name.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA stranger who will not send in his name! What can he want with me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe wishes to speak to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid he mention my name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat sort of person is he?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, sir, a man of about fifty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShort or tall?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAbout your own height, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDark or fair?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDark,ГўВҖВ”very dark; with black eyes, black hair, black eyebrows.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd how dressed?ГўВҖВқ asked Villefort quickly.

ГўВҖВңIn a blue frock-coat, buttoned up close, decorated with the Legion of
Honor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is he!ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, turning pale.

0148m



ГўВҖВңEh, _pardieu!_ГўВҖВқ said the individual whose description we have twice
given, entering the door, ГўВҖВңwhat a great deal of ceremony! Is it the

ГўВҖВңFather!ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort, ГўВҖВңthen I was not deceived; I felt sure it

ГўВҖВңWell, then, if you felt so sure,ГўВҖВқ replied the new-comer, putting his

ГўВҖВңLeave us, Germain,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort. The servant quitted the apartment
with evident signs of astonishment.



 Chapter 12. Father and Son

M. NoirtierГўВҖВ”for it was, indeed, he who enteredГўВҖВ”looked after the servant

proved that he was not exempt from the sin which ruined our first
parents. M. Noirtier then took the trouble to close and bolt the

ГўВҖВңWell, now, my dear GГғВ©rard,ГўВҖВқ said he to the young man, with a very

ГўВҖВңMy dear father,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңI am, on the contrary, delighted;

ГўВҖВңBut, my dear fellow,ГўВҖВқ replied M. Noirtier, seating himself, ГўВҖВңI might

ГўВҖВңAnd if I have come, my dear father,ГўВҖВқ said GГғВ©rard, drawing closer to M.

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed!ГўВҖВқ said M. Noirtier, stretching himself out at his ease in

ГўВҖВңFather, you have heard speak of a certain Bonapartist club in the Rue

ГўВҖВңNo. 53; yes, I am vice-president.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFather, your coolness makes me shudder.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, they induced General Quesnel to go there, and General Quesnel,

0151m



ГўВҖВңAnd who told you this fine story?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear father, I think I already know what you are about to tell me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot so loud, father, I entreat of youГўВҖВ”for your own sake as well as

ГўВҖВңThree days ago? You are crazy. Why, three days ago the emperor had not

ГўВҖВңNo matter, I was aware of his intention.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow did you know about it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy a letter addressed to you from the Island of Elba.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo you; and which I discovered in the pocket-book of the messenger.

ГўВҖВңCome, come,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңwill the Restoration adopt imperial methods so

ГўВҖВңI burnt it, for fear that even a fragment should remain; for that

ГўВҖВңI do better than that, sirГўВҖВ”I save you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do? Why, really, the thing becomes more and more dramaticГўВҖВ”explain

ГўВҖВңThey have not found; but they are on the track.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, that the usual phrase; I am quite familiar with it. When the
police is at fault, it declares that it is on the track; and the

ГўВҖВңYes, but they have found a corpse; the general has been killed, and in

ГўВҖВңA murder do you call it? why, there is nothing to prove that the

ГўВҖВңFather, you know very well that the general was not a man to drown

ГўВҖВңAnd who thus designated it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe king! I thought he was philosopher enough to allow that there was

ill grace that it was really tempting Providence to swear thus, and

ГўВҖВҳVery well, sir, you have gained the victory; tomorrow, perchance, it
will be our turn.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, father, take care; when our turn comes, our revenge will be

ГўВҖВңI do not understand you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou rely on the usurperГўВҖВҷs return?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are mistaken; he will not advance two leagues into the interior of

ГўВҖВңMy dear fellow, the emperor is at this moment on the way to Grenoble;

ГўВҖВңThe people will rise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, to go and meet him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe has but a handful of men with him, and armies will be despatched

ГўВҖВңYes, to escort him into the capital. Really, my dear GГғВ©rard, you are
but a child; you think yourself well informed because the telegraph has
told you, three days after the landing, ГўВҖВҳThe usurper has landed at

ГўВҖВңGrenoble and Lyons are faithful cities, and will oppose to him an

ГўВҖВңGrenoble will open her gates to him with enthusiasmГўВҖВ”all Lyons will

ГўВҖВңIndeed!ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, looking at his father with astonishment,
ГўВҖВңyou really do seem very well informed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEh? the thing is simple enough. You who are in power have only the

ГўВҖВңDevotion!ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, with a sneer.

ГўВҖВңYes, devotion; for that is, I believe, the phrase for hopeful
ambition.ГўВҖВқ

And VillefortГўВҖВҷs father extended his hand to the bell-rope, to summon
the servant whom his son had not called. Villefort caught his arm.

ГўВҖВңWait, my dear father,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңone word more.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSay on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHowever stupid the royalist police may be, they do know one terrible
thing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe description of the man who, on the morning of the day when General
Quesnel disappeared, presented himself at his house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, the admirable police have found that out, have they? And what may

ГўВҖВңDark complexion; hair, eyebrows, and whiskers black; blue frock-coat,

ГўВҖВңAh, ha, thatГўВҖВҷs it, is it?ГўВҖВқ said Noirtier; ГўВҖВңand why, then, have they
not laid hands on him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause yesterday, or the day before, they lost sight of him at the
corner of the Rue Coq-HГғВ©ron.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDidnГўВҖВҷt I say that your police were good for nothing?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but they may catch him yet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said Noirtier, looking carelessly around him, ГўВҖВңtrue, if this
person were not on his guard, as he is;ГўВҖВқ and he added with a smile, ГўВҖВңHe
will consequently make a few changes in his personal appearance.ГўВҖВқ At
these words he rose, and put off his frock-coat and cravat, went
towards a table on which lay his sonГўВҖВҷs toilet articles, lathered his
face, took a razor, and, with a firm hand, cut off the compromising
whiskers. Villefort watched him with alarm not devoid of admiration.

His whiskers cut off, Noirtier gave another turn to his hair; took,
instead of his black cravat, a colored neckerchief which lay at the top
of an open portmanteau; put on, in lieu of his blue and high-buttoned
frock-coat, a coat of VillefortГўВҖВҷs of dark brown, and cut away in front;
tried on before the glass a narrow-brimmed hat of his sonГўВҖВҷs, which
appeared to fit him perfectly, and, leaving his cane in the corner
where he had deposited it, he took up a small bamboo switch, cut the
air with it once or twice, and walked about with that easy swagger
which was one of his principal characteristics.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ he said, turning towards his wondering son, when this disguise
was completed, ГўВҖВңwell, do you think your police will recognize me now.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, father,ГўВҖВқ stammered Villefort; ГўВҖВңat least, I hope not.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd now, my dear boy,ГўВҖВқ continued Noirtier, ГўВҖВңI rely on your prudence to
remove all the things which I leave in your care.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, rely on me,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңYes, yes; and now I believe you are right, and that you have really
saved my life; be assured I will return the favor hereafter.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort shook his head.

ГўВҖВңYou are not convinced yet?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hope at least, that you may be mistaken.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall you see the king again?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerhaps.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWould you pass in his eyes for a prophet?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProphets of evil are not in favor at the court, father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue, but some day they do them justice; and supposing a second
restoration, you would then pass for a great man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, what should I say to the king?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSay this to him: ГўВҖВҳSire, you are deceived as to the feeling in France,
as to the opinions of the towns, and the prejudices of the army; he
whom in Paris you call the Corsican ogre, who at Nevers is styled the
usurper, is already saluted as Bonaparte at Lyons, and emperor at
Grenoble. You think he is tracked, pursued, captured; he is advancing
as rapidly as his own eagles. The soldiers you believe to be dying with
hunger, worn out with fatigue, ready to desert, gather like atoms of
snow about the rolling ball as it hastens onward. Sire, go, leave
France to its real master, to him who acquired it, not by purchase, but
by right of conquest; go, sire, not that you incur any risk, for your
