stadtholder who becomes a king. Then new concessions to the people,
then a constitution, then liberty. Ah, my friend!ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©,
turning towards DantГғВЁs, and surveying him with the kindling gaze of a
prophet, ГўВҖВңyou are young, you will see all this come to pass.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProbably, if ever I get out of prison!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ replied Faria, ГўВҖВңwe are prisoners; but I forget this sometimes,
and there are even moments when my mental vision transports me beyond
these walls, and I fancy myself at liberty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut wherefore are you here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause in 1807 I dreamed of the very plan Napoleon tried to realize
in 1811; because, like Machiavelli, I desired to alter the political
face of Italy, and instead of allowing it to be split up into a
quantity of petty principalities, each held by some weak or tyrannical
ruler, I sought to form one large, compact, and powerful empire; and,
lastly, because I fancied I had found my CГғВҰsar Borgia in a crowned
simpleton, who feigned to enter into my views only to betray me. It was
the plan of Alexander VI. and Clement VII., but it will never succeed
now, for they attempted it fruitlessly, and Napoleon was unable to
complete his work. Italy seems fated to misfortune.ГўВҖВқ And the old man
bowed his head.

DantГғВЁs could not understand a man risking his life for such matters.
Napoleon certainly he knew something of, inasmuch as he had seen and
spoken with him; but of Clement VII. and Alexander VI. he knew nothing.

ГўВҖВңAre you not,ГўВҖВқ he asked, ГўВҖВңthe priest who here in the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf is
generally thought to beГўВҖВ”ill?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMad, you mean, donГўВҖВҷt you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI did not like to say so,ГўВҖВқ answered DantГғВЁs, smiling.

ГўВҖВңWell, then,ГўВҖВқ resumed Faria with a bitter smile, ГўВҖВңlet me answer your
question in full, by acknowledging that I am the poor mad prisoner of
the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf, for many years permitted to amuse the different
visitors with what is said to be my insanity; and, in all probability,
I should be promoted to the honor of making sport for the children, if
such innocent beings could be found in an abode devoted like this to
suffering and despair.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs remained for a short time mute and motionless; at length he
said:

ГўВҖВңThen you abandon all hope of escape?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI perceive its utter impossibility; and I consider it impious to
attempt that which the Almighty evidently does not approve.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNay, be not discouraged. Would it not be expecting too much to hope to
succeed at your first attempt? Why not try to find an opening in
another direction from that which has so unfortunately failed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas, it shows how little notion you can have of all it has cost me to
effect a purpose so unexpectedly frustrated, that you talk of beginning
over again. In the first place, I was four years making the tools I
possess, and have been two years scraping and digging out earth, hard
as granite itself; then what toil and fatigue has it not been to remove
huge stones I should once have deemed impossible to loosen. Whole days
have I passed in these Titanic efforts, considering my labor well
repaid if, by night-time I had contrived to carry away a square inch of
this hard-bound cement, changed by ages into a substance unyielding as
the stones themselves; then to conceal the mass of earth and rubbish I
dug up, I was compelled to break through a staircase, and throw the
fruits of my labor into the hollow part of it; but the well is now so
completely choked up, that I scarcely think it would be possible to add
another handful of dust without leading to discovery. Consider also
that I fully believed I had accomplished the end and aim of my
undertaking, for which I had so exactly husbanded my strength as to
make it just hold out to the termination of my enterprise; and now, at
the moment when I reckoned upon success, my hopes are forever dashed
from me. No, I repeat again, that nothing shall induce me to renew
attempts evidently at variance with the AlmightyГўВҖВҷs pleasure.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs held down his head, that the other might not see how joy at the
thought of having a companion outweighed the sympathy he felt for the
failure of the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs plans.

The abbГғВ© sank upon EdmondГўВҖВҷs bed, while Edmond himself remained
standing. Escape had never once occurred to him. There are, indeed,
some things which appear so impossible that the mind does not dwell on
them for an instant. To undermine the ground for fifty feetГўВҖВ”to devote
three years to a labor which, if successful, would conduct you to a
precipice overhanging the seaГўВҖВ”to plunge into the waves from the height
of fifty, sixty, perhaps a hundred feet, at the risk of being dashed to
pieces against the rocks, should you have been fortunate enough to have
escaped the fire of the sentinels; and even, supposing all these perils
past, then to have to swim for your life a distance of at least three
miles ere you could reach the shoreГўВҖВ”were difficulties so startling and
formidable that DantГғВЁs had never even dreamed of such a scheme,
resigning himself rather to death.

But the sight of an old man clinging to life with so desperate a
courage, gave a fresh turn to his ideas, and inspired him with new
courage. Another, older and less strong than he, had attempted what he
had not had sufficient resolution to undertake, and had failed only
because of an error in calculation. This same person, with almost
incredible patience and perseverance, had contrived to provide himself
with tools requisite for so unparalleled an attempt. Another had done
all this; why, then, was it impossible to DantГғВЁs? Faria had dug his way
through fifty feet, DantГғВЁs would dig a hundred; Faria, at the age of
fifty, had devoted three years to the task; he, who was but half as
old, would sacrifice six; Faria, a priest and savant, had not shrunk
from the idea of risking his life by trying to swim a distance of three
miles to one of the islandsГўВҖВ”Daume, Rattonneau, or Lemaire; should a
hardy sailor, an experienced diver, like himself, shrink from a similar
task; should he, who had so often for mere amusementГўВҖВҷs sake plunged to
the bottom of the sea to fetch up the bright coral branch, hesitate to
entertain the same project? He could do it in an hour, and how many
times had he, for pure pastime, continued in the water for more than
twice as long! At once DantГғВЁs resolved to follow the brave example of
his energetic companion, and to remember that what has once been done
may be done again.

After continuing some time in profound meditation, the young man
suddenly exclaimed, ГўВҖВңI have found what you were in search of!ГўВҖВқ

Faria started: ГўВҖВңHave you, indeed?ГўВҖВқ cried he, raising his head with
quick anxiety; ГўВҖВңpray, let me know what it is you have discovered?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe corridor through which you have bored your way from the cell you
occupy here, extends in the same direction as the outer gallery, does
it not?ГўВҖВқ

0207m



ГўВҖВңIt does.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd is not above fifteen feet from it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAbout that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, I will tell you what we must do. We must pierce through
the corridor by forming a side opening about the middle, as it were the
top part of a cross. This time you will lay your plans more accurately;
we shall get out into the gallery you have described; kill the sentinel
who guards it, and make our escape. All we require to insure success is
courage, and that you possess, and strength, which I am not deficient
in; as for patience, you have abundantly proved yoursГўВҖВ”you shall now see
me prove mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne instant, my dear friend,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңit is clear you do
not understand the nature of the courage with which I am endowed, and
what use I intend making of my strength. As for patience, I consider
that I have abundantly exercised that in beginning every morning the
task of the night before, and every night renewing the task of the day.
But then, young man (and I pray of you to give me your full attention),

committed no offence, and merited not condemnation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd have your notions changed?ГўВҖВқ asked DantГғВЁs with much surprise; ГўВҖВңdo

ГўВҖВңNo; neither do I wish to incur guilt. Hitherto I have fancied myself
merely waging war against circumstances, not men. I have thought it no
sin to bore through a wall, or destroy a staircase; but I cannot so
easily persuade myself to pierce a heart or take away a life.ГўВҖВқ

A slight movement of surprise escaped DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңIs it possible,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңthat where your liberty is at stake you can
allow any such scruple to deter you from obtaining it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell me,ГўВҖВқ replied Faria, ГўВҖВңwhat has hindered you from knocking down
your jailer with a piece of wood torn from your bedstead, dressing
yourself in his clothes, and endeavoring to escape?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSimply the fact that the idea never occurred to me,ГўВҖВқ answered DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңBecause,ГўВҖВқ said the old man, ГўВҖВңthe natural repugnance to the commission
of such a crime prevented you from thinking of it; and so it ever is
because in simple and allowable things our natural instincts keep us
from deviating from the strict line of duty. The tiger, whose nature
teaches him to delight in shedding blood, needs but the sense of smell
to show him when his prey is within his reach, and by following this
instinct he is enabled to measure the leap necessary to permit him to
spring on his victim; but man, on the contrary, loathes the idea of
bloodГўВҖВ”it is not alone that the laws of social life inspire him with a

physiological formationГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs was confused and silent at this explanation of the thoughts
which had unconsciously been working in his mind, or rather soul; for
there are two distinct sorts of ideas, those that proceed from the head
and those that emanate from the heart.

0209m



ГўВҖВңSince my imprisonment,ГўВҖВқ said Faria, ГўВҖВңI have thought over all the most
celebrated cases of escape on record. They have rarely been successful.
Those that have been crowned with full success have been long meditated
upon, and carefully arranged; such, for instance, as the escape of the
Duc de Beaufort from the ChГғВўteau de Vincennes, that of the AbbГғВ©
Dubuquoi from For lГўВҖВҷEvГғВӘque; of Latude from the Bastille. Then there are
those for which chance sometimes affords opportunity, and those are the
best of all. Let us, therefore, wait patiently for some favorable
moment, and when it presents itself, profit by it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңyou might well endure the tedious delay; you were
constantly employed in the task you set yourself, and when weary with
toil, you had your hopes to refresh and encourage you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI assure you,ГўВҖВқ replied the old man, ГўВҖВңI did not turn to that source for
recreation or support.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat did you do then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI wrote or studied.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWere you then permitted the use of pens, ink, and paper?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no,ГўВҖВқ answered the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңI had none but what I made for myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou made paper, pens and ink?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs gazed with admiration, but he had some difficulty in believing.
Faria saw this.

ГўВҖВңWhen you pay me a visit in my cell, my young friend,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI will
show you an entire work, the fruits of the thoughts and reflections of

Colosseum at Rome, at the foot of St. MarkГўВҖВҷs column at Venice, and on

The work I speak of is called _A Treatise on the Possibility of a
General Monarchy in Italy_, and will make one large quarto volume.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd on what have you written all this?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn two of my shirts. I invented a preparation that makes linen as
smooth and as easy to write on as parchment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are, then, a chemist?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSomewhat; I know Lavoisier, and was the intimate friend of Cabanis.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut for such a work you must have needed booksГўВҖВ”had you any?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI had nearly five thousand volumes in my library at Rome; but after
reading them over many times, I found out that with one hundred and
fifty well-chosen books a man possesses, if not a complete summary of
all human knowledge, at least all that a man need really know. I
devoted three years of my life to reading and studying these one
hundred and fifty volumes, till I knew them nearly by heart; so that
since I have been in prison, a very slight effort of memory has enabled
me to recall their contents as readily as though the pages were open
before me. I could recite you the whole of Thucydides, Xenophon,
Plutarch, Titus Livius, Tacitus, Strada, Jornandes, Dante, Montaigne,
Shakespeare, Spinoza, Machiavelli, and Bossuet. I name only the most
important.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are, doubtless, acquainted with a variety of languages, so as to
have been able to read all these?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I speak five of the modern tonguesГўВҖВ”that is to say, German,
French, Italian, English, and Spanish; by the aid of ancient Greek I
learned modern GreekГўВҖВ”I donГўВҖВҷt speak it so well as I could wish, but I am
still trying to improve myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңImprove yourself!ГўВҖВқ repeated DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңwhy, how can you manage to do
so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, I made a vocabulary of the words I knew; turned, returned, and
arranged them, so as to enable me to express my thoughts through their
medium. I know nearly one thousand words, which is all that is
absolutely necessary, although I believe there are nearly one hundred
thousand in the dictionaries. I cannot hope to be very fluent, but I
certainly should have no difficulty in explaining my wants and wishes;
and that would be quite as much as I should ever require.ГўВҖВқ

Stronger grew the wonder of DantГғВЁs, who almost fancied he had to do
with one gifted with supernatural powers; still hoping to find some
imperfection which might bring him down to a level with human beings,
he added, ГўВҖВңThen if you were not furnished with pens, how did you manage
to write the work you speak of?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI made myself some excellent ones, which would be universally
preferred to all others if once known. You are aware what huge whitings
are served to us on _maigre_ days. Well, I selected the cartilages of
the heads of these fishes, and you can scarcely imagine the delight
with which I welcomed the arrival of each Wednesday, Friday, and
Saturday, as affording me the means of increasing my stock of pens; for
I will freely confess that my historical labors have been my greatest
solace and relief. While retracing the past, I forget the present; and
traversing at will the path of history I cease to remember that I am
myself a prisoner.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut the ink,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңof what did you make your ink?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere was formerly a fireplace in my dungeon,ГўВҖВқ replied Faria, ГўВҖВңbut it
was closed up long ere I became an occupant of this prison. Still, it
must have been many years in use, for it was thickly covered with a
coating of soot; this soot I dissolved in a portion of the wine brought
to me every Sunday, and I assure you a better ink cannot be desired.
For very important notes, for which closer attention is required, I
pricked one of my fingers, and wrote with my own blood.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd when,ГўВҖВқ asked DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңmay I see all this?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhenever you please,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңOh, then let it be directly!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the young man.

ГўВҖВңFollow me, then,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, as he re-entered the subterranean
passage, in which he soon disappeared, followed by DantГғВЁs.



 Chapter 17. The AbbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs Chamber

After having passed with tolerable ease through the subterranean
passage, which, however, did not admit of their holding themselves
erect, the two friends reached the further end of the corridor, into
which the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs cell opened; from that point the passage became much
narrower, and barely permitted one to creep through on hands and knees.
The floor of the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs cell was paved, and it had been by raising one
of the stones in the most obscure corner that Faria had been able to
commence the laborious task of which DantГғВЁs had witnessed the
completion.

As he entered the chamber of his friend, DantГғВЁs cast around one eager
and searching glance in quest of the expected marvels, but nothing more
than common met his view.

ГўВҖВңIt is well,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңwe have some hours before usГўВҖВ”it is now
just a quarter past twelve oГўВҖВҷclock.ГўВҖВқ Instinctively DantГғВЁs turned round
to observe by what watch or clock the abbГғВ© had been able so accurately
to specify the hour.

ГўВҖВңLook at this ray of light which enters by my window,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©,
ГўВҖВңand then observe the lines traced on the wall. Well, by means of these
lines, which are in accordance with the double motion of the earth, and
the ellipse it describes round the sun, I am enabled to ascertain the
precise hour with more minuteness than if I possessed a watch; for that
might be broken or deranged in its movements, while the sun and earth
never vary in their appointed paths.ГўВҖВқ

This last explanation was wholly lost upon DantГғВЁs, who had always
imagined, from seeing the sun rise from behind the mountains and set in
the Mediterranean, that it moved, and not the earth. A double movement
of the globe he inhabited, and of which he could feel nothing, appeared
to him perfectly impossible. Each word that fell from his companionГўВҖВҷs
lips seemed fraught with the mysteries of science, as worthy of digging
out as the gold and diamonds in the mines of Guzerat and Golconda,
which he could just recollect having visited during a voyage made in
his earliest youth.

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said he to the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңI am anxious to see your treasures.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© smiled, and, proceeding to the disused fireplace, raised, by
the help of his chisel, a long stone, which had doubtless been the
hearth, beneath which was a cavity of considerable depth, serving as a
safe depository of the articles mentioned to DantГғВЁs.

0213m



ГўВҖВңWhat do you wish to see first?ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңOh, your great work on the monarchy of Italy!ГўВҖВқ

Faria then drew forth from his hiding-place three or four rolls of
linen, laid one over the other, like folds of papyrus. These rolls
consisted of slips of cloth about four inches wide and eighteen long;
they were all carefully numbered and closely covered with writing, so
legible that DantГғВЁs could easily read it, as well as make out the
senseГўВҖВ”it being in Italian, a language he, as a ProvenГғВ§al, perfectly
understood.

ГўВҖВңThere,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңthere is the work complete. I wrote the word _finis_
at the end of the sixty-eighth strip about a week ago. I have torn up
two of my shirts, and as many handkerchiefs as I was master of, to
complete the precious pages. Should I ever get out of prison and find
in all Italy a printer courageous enough to publish what I have
composed, my literary reputation is forever secured.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI see,ГўВҖВқ answered DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңNow let me behold the curious pens with
which you have written your work.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLook!ГўВҖВқ said Faria, showing to the young man a slender stick about six
inches long, and much resembling the size of the handle of a fine
painting-brush, to the end of which was tied, by a piece of thread, one
of those cartilages of which the abbГғВ© had before spoken to DantГғВЁs; it
was pointed, and divided at the nib like an ordinary pen. DantГғВЁs
examined it with intense admiration, then looked around to see the
instrument with which it had been shaped so correctly into form.

ГўВҖВңAh, yes,ГўВҖВқ said Faria; ГўВҖВңthe penknife. ThatГўВҖВҷs my masterpiece. I made it,
as well as this larger knife, out of an old iron candlestick.ГўВҖВқ The
penknife was sharp and keen as a razor; as for the other knife, it
would serve a double purpose, and with it one could cut and thrust.

DantГғВЁs examined the various articles shown to him with the same
attention that he had bestowed on the curiosities and strange tools
exhibited in the shops at Marseilles as the works of the savages in the
South Seas from whence they had been brought by the different trading
vessels.

ГўВҖВңAs for the ink,ГўВҖВқ said Faria, ГўВҖВңI told you how I managed to obtain
thatГўВҖВ”and I only just make it from time to time, as I require it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne thing still puzzles me,ГўВҖВқ observed DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңand that is how you
managed to do all this by daylight?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI worked at night also,ГўВҖВқ replied Faria.

ГўВҖВңNight!ГўВҖВ”why, for HeavenГўВҖВҷs sake, are your eyes like catsГўВҖВҷ, that you can
see to work in the dark?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed they are not; but God has supplied man with the intelligence
that enables him to overcome the limitations of natural conditions. I
furnished myself with a light.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou did? Pray tell me how.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI separated the fat from the meat served to me, melted it, and so made
oilГўВҖВ”here is my lamp.ГўВҖВқ So saying, the abbГғВ© exhibited a sort of torch
very similar to those used in public illuminations.

ГўВҖВңBut how do you procure a light?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, here are two flints and a piece of burnt linen.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd matches?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI pretended that I had a disorder of the skin, and asked for a little
sulphur, which was readily supplied.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs laid the different things he had been looking at on the table,
and stood with his head drooping on his breast, as though overwhelmed
by the perseverance and strength of FariaГўВҖВҷs mind.

0215m



ГўВҖВңYou have not seen all yet,ГўВҖВқ continued Faria, ГўВҖВңfor I did not think it
wise to trust all my treasures in the same hiding-place. Let us shut
this one up.ГўВҖВқ They put the stone back in its place; the abbГғВ© sprinkled
a little dust over it to conceal the traces of its having been removed,
rubbed his foot well on it to make it assume the same appearance as the
other, and then, going towards his bed, he removed it from the spot it
stood in. Behind the head of the bed, and concealed by a stone fitting
in so closely as to defy all suspicion, was a hollow space, and in this
space a ladder of cords between twenty-five and thirty feet in length.
DantГғВЁs closely and eagerly examined it; he found it firm, solid, and
compact enough to bear any weight.

ГўВҖВңWho supplied you with the materials for making this wonderful work?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI tore up several of my shirts, and ripped out the seams in the sheets
of my bed, during my three yearsГўВҖВҷ imprisonment at Fenestrelle; and when
I was removed to the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf, I managed to bring the ravellings
with me, so that I have been able to finish my work here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd was it not discovered that your sheets were unhemmed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no, for when I had taken out the thread I required, I hemmed the
edges over again.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith what?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith this needle,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, as, opening his ragged vestments, he
showed DantГғВЁs a long, sharp fish-bone, with a small perforated eye for
the thread, a small portion of which still remained in it.

ГўВҖВңI once thought,ГўВҖВқ continued Faria, ГўВҖВңof removing these iron bars, and
letting myself down from the window, which, as you see, is somewhat
wider than yours, although I should have enlarged it still more
preparatory to my flight; however, I discovered that I should merely
have dropped into a sort of inner court, and I therefore renounced the
project altogether as too full of risk and danger. Nevertheless, I
carefully preserved my ladder against one of those unforeseen
opportunities of which I spoke just now, and which sudden chance
frequently brings about.ГўВҖВқ

While affecting to be deeply engaged in examining the ladder, the mind
of DantГғВЁs was, in fact, busily occupied by the idea that a person so
intelligent, ingenious, and clear-sighted as the abbГғВ© might probably be
able to solve the dark mystery of his own misfortunes, where he himself
could see nothing.

ГўВҖВңWhat are you thinking of?ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ© smilingly, imputing the deep
abstraction in which his visitor was plunged to the excess of his awe
and wonder.

ГўВҖВңI was reflecting, in the first place,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңupon the
enormous degree of intelligence and ability you must have employed to
reach the high perfection to which you have attained. What would you
not have accomplished if you had been free?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPossibly nothing at all; the overflow of my brain would probably, in a
state of freedom, have evaporated in a thousand follies; misfortune is
needed to bring to light the treasures of the human intellect.
Compression is needed to explode gunpowder. Captivity has brought my
mental faculties to a focus; and you are well aware that from the
collision of clouds electricity is producedГўВҖВ”from electricity,
lightning, from lightning, illumination.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңI know nothing. Some of your words are to me
quite empty of meaning. You must be blessed indeed to possess the
knowledge you have.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© smiled. ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңbut you had another subject for your
thoughts; did you not say so just now?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI did!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have told me as yet but one of themГўВҖВ”let me hear the other.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was this,ГўВҖВ”that while you had related to me all the particulars of
your past life, you were perfectly unacquainted with mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour life, my young friend, has not been of sufficient length to admit
of your having passed through any very important events.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt has been long enough to inflict on me a great and undeserved
misfortune. I would fain fix the source of it on man that I may no
longer vent reproaches upon Heaven.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you profess ignorance of the crime with which you are charged?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do, indeed; and this I swear by the two beings most dear to me upon
earth,ГўВҖВ”my father and MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, closing his hiding-place, and pushing the bed
back to its original situation, ГўВҖВңlet me hear your story.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs obeyed, and commenced what he called his history, but which
consisted only of the account of a voyage to India, and two or three
voyages to the Levant, until he arrived at the recital of his last
cruise, with the death of Captain Leclere, and the receipt of a packet
to be delivered by himself to the grand marshal; his interview with
that personage, and his receiving, in place of the packet brought, a
letter addressed to a Monsieur NoirtierГўВҖВ”his arrival at Marseilles, and
interview with his fatherГўВҖВ”his affection for MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, and their nuptial
feastГўВҖВ”his arrest and subsequent examination, his temporary detention at
the Palais de Justice, and his final imprisonment in the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf.
From this point everything was a blank to DantГғВЁsГўВҖВ”he knew nothing more,
not even the length of time he had been imprisoned. His recital
finished, the abbГғВ© reflected long and earnestly.

ГўВҖВңThere is,ГўВҖВқ said he, at the end of his meditations, ГўВҖВңa clever maxim,
which bears upon what I was saying to you some little while ago, and
that is, that unless wicked ideas take root in a naturally depraved
mind, human nature, in a right and wholesome state, revolts at crime.
Still, from an artificial civilization have originated wants, vices,
and false tastes, which occasionally become so powerful as to stifle
within us all good feelings, and ultimately to lead us into guilt and
wickedness. From this view of things, then, comes the axiom that if you
visit to discover the author of any bad action, seek first to discover
the person to whom the perpetration of that bad action could be in any
way advantageous. Now, to apply it in your case,ГўВҖВ”to whom could your
disappearance have been serviceable?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo no one, by Heaven! I was a very insignificant person.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not speak thus, for your reply evinces neither logic nor
philosophy; everything is relative, my dear young friend, from the king
who stands in the way of his successor, to the employee who keeps his
rival out of a place. Now, in the event of the kingГўВҖВҷs death, his
successor inherits a crown,ГўВҖВ”when the employee dies, the supernumerary
steps into his shoes, and receives his salary of twelve thousand
livres. Well, these twelve thousand livres are his civil list, and are
as essential to him as the twelve millions of a king. Everyone, from
the highest to the lowest degree, has his place on the social ladder,
and is beset by stormy passions and conflicting interests, as in
DescartesГўВҖВҷ theory of pressure and impulsion. But these forces increase
as we go higher, so that we have a spiral which in defiance of reason
rests upon the apex and not on the base. Now let us return to your
particular world. You say you were on the point of being made captain
of the _Pharaon_?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd about to become the husband of a young and lovely girl?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow, could anyone have had any interest in preventing the
accomplishment of these two things? But let us first settle the
question as to its being the interest of anyone to hinder you from
being captain of the _Pharaon_. What say you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI cannot believe such was the case. I was generally liked on board,
and had the sailors possessed the right of selecting a captain
themselves, I feel convinced their choice would have fallen on me.
There was only one person among the crew who had any feeling of
ill-will towards me. I had quarelled with him some time previously, and
had even challenged him to fight me; but he refused.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow we are getting on. And what was this manГўВҖВҷs name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDanglars.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat rank did he hold on board?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe was supercargo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd had you been captain, should you have retained him in his
employment?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot if the choice had remained with me, for I had frequently observed
inaccuracies in his accounts.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGood again! Now then, tell me, was any person present during your last
conversation with Captain Leclere?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; we were quite alone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCould your conversation have been overheard by anyone?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt might, for the cabin door was openГўВҖВ”andГўВҖВ”stay; now I
recollect,ГўВҖВ”Danglars himself passed by just as Captain Leclere was
giving me the packet for the grand marshal.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs better,ГўВҖВқ cried the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңnow we are on the right scent. Did
you take anybody with you when you put into the port of Elba?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNobody.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSomebody there received your packet, and gave you a letter in place of
it, I think?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; the grand marshal did.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what did you do with that letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPut it into my portfolio.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou had your portfolio with you, then? Now, how could a sailor find
room in his pocket for a portfolio large enough to contain an official
letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right; it was left on board.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen it was not till your return to the ship that you put the letter
in the portfolio?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what did you do with this same letter while returning from
Porto-Ferrajo to the vessel?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI carried it in my hand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that when you went on board the _Pharaon_, everybody could see that
you held a letter in your hand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDanglars, as well as the rest?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDanglars, as well as others.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow, listen to me, and try to recall every circumstance attending your
arrest. Do you recollect the words in which the information against you
was formulated?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh yes, I read it over three times, and the words sank deeply into my
memory.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRepeat it to me.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs paused a moment, then said, ГўВҖВңThis is it, word for word: ГўВҖВҳThe
kingГўВҖВҷs attorney is informed by a friend to the throne and religion,
that one Edmond DantГғВЁs, mate on board the _Pharaon_, this day arrived
from Smyrna, after having touched at Naples and Porto-Ferrajo, has been
intrusted by Murat with a packet for the usurper; again, by the
usurper, with a letter for the Bonapartist Club in Paris. This proof of
his guilt may be procured by his immediate arrest, as the letter will
be found either about his person, at his fatherГўВҖВҷs residence, or in his
cabin on board the _Pharaon_.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© shrugged his shoulders. ГўВҖВңThe thing is clear as day,ГўВҖВқ said he;
ГўВҖВңand you must have had a very confiding nature, as well as a good
heart, not to have suspected the origin of the whole affair.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you really think so? Ah, that would indeed be infamous.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow did Danglars usually write?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn a handsome, running hand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd how was the anonymous letter written?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBackhanded.ГўВҖВқ

Again the abbГғВ© smiled. ГўВҖВңDisguised.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was very boldly written, if disguised.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStop a bit,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, taking up what he called his pen, and,
after dipping it into the ink, he wrote on a piece of prepared linen,
with his left hand, the first two or three words of the accusation.
DantГғВЁs drew back, and gazed on the abbГғВ© with a sensation almost
amounting to terror.

ГўВҖВңHow very astonishing!ГўВҖВқ cried he at length. ГўВҖВңWhy your writing exactly
resembles that of the accusation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSimply because that accusation had been written with the left hand;
and I have noticed thatГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat while the writing of different persons done with the right hand
varies, that performed with the left hand is invariably uniform.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have evidently seen and observed everything.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLet us proceed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes, yes!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow as regards the second question.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am listening.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas there any person whose interest it was to prevent your marriage
with MercГғВ©dГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; a young man who loved her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd his name wasГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFernand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is a Spanish name, I think?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe was a Catalan.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou imagine him capable of writing the letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no; he would more likely have got rid of me by sticking a knife
into me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is in strict accordance with the Spanish character; an
assassination they will unhesitatingly commit, but an act of cowardice,
never.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBesides,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңthe various circumstances mentioned in the
letter were wholly unknown to him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou had never spoken of them yourself to anyone?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo no one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot even to your mistress?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, not even to my betrothed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen it is Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI feel quite sure of it now.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWait a little. Pray, was Danglars acquainted with Fernand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNoГўВҖВ”yes, he was. Now I recollectГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo have seen them both sitting at table together under an arbor at
PГғВЁre PamphileГўВҖВҷs the evening before the day fixed for my wedding. They
were in earnest conversation. Danglars was joking in a friendly way,
but Fernand looked pale and agitated.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWere they alone?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere was a third person with them whom I knew perfectly well, and who
had, in all probability made their acquaintance; he was a tailor named
Caderousse, but he was very drunk. Stay!ГўВҖВ”stay!ГўВҖВ”How strange that it
should not have occurred to me before! Now I remember quite well, that
on the table round which they were sitting were pens, ink, and paper.
Oh, the heartless, treacherous scoundrels!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed DantГғВЁs, pressing
his hand to his throbbing brows.

ГўВҖВңIs there anything else I can assist you in discovering, besides the
villany of your friends?ГўВҖВқ inquired the abbГғВ© with a laugh.

ГўВҖВңYes, yes,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs eagerly; ГўВҖВңI would beg of you, who see so
completely to the depths of things, and to whom the greatest mystery
seems but an easy riddle, to explain to me how it was that I underwent
no second examination, was never brought to trial, and, above all, was
condemned without ever having had sentence passed on me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is altogether a different and more serious matter,ГўВҖВқ responded the
abbГғВ©. ГўВҖВңThe ways of justice are frequently too dark and mysterious to be
easily penetrated. All we have hitherto done in the matter has been
childГўВҖВҷs play. If you wish me to enter upon the more difficult part of
the business, you must assist me by the most minute information on
every point.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPray ask me whatever questions you please; for, in good truth, you see
more clearly into my life than I do myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn the first place, then, who examined you,ГўВҖВ”the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney, his
deputy, or a magistrate?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe deputy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas he young or old?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAbout six or seven-and-twenty years of age, I should say.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo,ГўВҖВқ answered the abbГғВ©. ГўВҖВңOld enough to be ambitious, but too young to
be corrupt. And how did he treat you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith more of mildness than severity.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you tell him your whole story?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI did.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd did his conduct change at all in the course of your examination?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe did appear much disturbed when he read the letter that had brought
me into this scrape. He seemed quite overcome by my misfortune.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy your misfortune?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you feel quite sure that it was your misfortune he deplored?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe gave me one great proof of his sympathy, at any rate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe burnt the sole evidence that could at all have criminated me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? the accusation?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; the letter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you sure?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI saw it done.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat alters the case. This man might, after all, be a greater
scoundrel than you have thought possible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUpon my word,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңyou make me shudder. Is the world filled
with tigers and crocodiles?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; and remember that two-legged tigers and crocodiles are more
dangerous than the others.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mind; let us go on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith all my heart! You tell me he burned the letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe did; saying at the same time, ГўВҖВҳYou see I thus destroy the only
proof existing against you.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis action is somewhat too sublime to be natural.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou think so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am sure of it. To whom was this letter addressed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo M. Noirtier, Rue Coq-HГғВ©ron, No. 13, Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow can you conceive of any interest that your heroic deputy could
possibly have had in the destruction of that letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, it is not altogether impossible he might have had, for he made me
promise several times never to speak of that letter to anyone, assuring
me he so advised me for my own interest; and, more than this, he
insisted on my taking a solemn oath never to utter the name mentioned
in the address.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNoirtier!ГўВҖВқ repeated the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңNoirtier!ГўВҖВ”I knew a person of that name
at the court of the Queen of Etruria,ГўВҖВ”a Noirtier, who had been a
Girondin during the Revolution! What was your deputy called?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDe Villefort!ГўВҖВқ The abbГғВ© burst into a fit of laughter, while DantГғВЁs
gazed on him in utter astonishment.

ГўВҖВңWhat ails you?ГўВҖВқ said he at length.

ГўВҖВңDo you see that ray of sunlight?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, the whole thing is more clear to me than that sunbeam is to you.
Poor fellow! poor young man! And you tell me this magistrate expressed
great sympathy and commiseration for you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe did.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd the worthy man destroyed your compromising letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd then made you swear never to utter the name of Noirtier?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, you poor short-sighted simpleton, can you not guess who this
Noirtier was, whose very name he was so careful to keep concealed? This
Noirtier was his father!ГўВҖВқ

Had a thunderbolt fallen at the feet of DantГғВЁs, or hell opened its
yawning gulf before him, he could not have been more completely
transfixed with horror than he was at the sound of these unexpected
words. Starting up, he clasped his hands around his head as though to
prevent his very brain from bursting, and exclaimed, ГўВҖВңHis father! his
father!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, his father,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңhis right name was Noirtier de
Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

At this instant a bright light shot through the mind of DantГғВЁs, and
cleared up all that had been dark and obscure before. The change that
had come over Villefort during the examination, the destruction of the
letter, the exacted promise, the almost supplicating tones of the
magistrate, who seemed rather to implore mercy than to pronounce
punishment,ГўВҖВ”all returned with a stunning force to his memory. He cried
out, and staggered against the wall like a drunken man, then he hurried
to the opening that led from the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs cell to his own, and said, ГўВҖВңI
must be alone, to think over all this.ГўВҖВқ

When he regained his dungeon, he threw himself on his bed, where the
turnkey found him in the evening visit, sitting with fixed gaze and
contracted features, dumb and motionless as a statue. During these
hours of profound meditation, which to him had seemed only minutes, he
had formed a fearful resolution, and bound himself to its fulfilment by
a solemn oath.

DantГғВЁs was at length roused from his reverie by the voice of Faria,
who, having also been visited by his jailer, had come to invite his
fellow-sufferer to share his supper. The reputation of being out of his
mind, though harmlessly and even amusingly so, had procured for the
abbГғВ© unusual privileges. He was supplied with bread of a finer, whiter
quality than the usual prison fare, and even regaled each Sunday with a
small quantity of wine. Now this was a Sunday, and the abbГғВ© had come to
ask his young companion to share the luxuries with him.

DantГғВЁs followed him; his features were no longer contracted, and now
wore their usual expression, but there was that in his whole appearance
that bespoke one who had come to a fixed and desperate resolve. Faria
bent on him his penetrating eye.

ГўВҖВңI regret now,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңhaving helped you in your late inquiries, or
having given you the information I did.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so?ГўВҖВқ inquired DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңBecause it has instilled a new passion in your heartГўВҖВ”that of
vengeance.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs smiled. ГўВҖВңLet us talk of something else,ГўВҖВқ said he.

Again the abbГғВ© looked at him, then mournfully shook his head; but in
accordance with DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ request, he began to speak of other matters.
The elder prisoner was one of those persons whose conversation, like
that of all who have experienced many trials, contained many useful and
important hints as well as sound information; but it was never
egotistical, for the unfortunate man never alluded to his own sorrows.
DantГғВЁs listened with admiring attention to all he said; some of his
remarks corresponded with what he already knew, or applied to the sort
of knowledge his nautical life had enabled him to acquire. A part of
the good abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs words, however, were wholly incomprehensible to him;
but, like the aurora which guides the navigator in northern latitudes,
opened new vistas to the inquiring mind of the listener, and gave
fantastic glimpses of new horizons, enabling him justly to estimate the
delight an intellectual mind would have in following one so richly
gifted as Faria along the heights of truth, where he was so much at
home.

ГўВҖВңYou must teach me a small part of what you know,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңif
only to prevent your growing weary of me. I can well believe that so
learned a person as yourself would prefer absolute solitude to being
tormented with the company of one as ignorant and uninformed as myself.
If you will only agree to my request, I promise you never to mention
another word about escaping.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© smiled.

ГўВҖВңAlas, my boy,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңhuman knowledge is confined within very
narrow limits; and when I have taught you mathematics, physics,
history, and the three or four modern languages with which I am
acquainted, you will know as much as I do myself. Now, it will scarcely
require two years for me to communicate to you the stock of learning I
possess.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTwo years!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңdo you really believe I can acquire all
these things in so short a time?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot their application, certainly, but their principles you may; to
learn is not to know; there are the learners and the learned. Memory
makes the one, philosophy the other.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut cannot one learn philosophy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPhilosophy cannot be taught; it is the application of the sciences to
truth; it is like the golden cloud in which the Messiah went up into
heaven.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңWhat shall you teach me first? I am in a
hurry to begin. I want to learn.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEverything,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©. And that very evening the prisoners
sketched a plan of education, to be entered upon the following day.
DantГғВЁs possessed a prodigious memory, combined with an astonishing
quickness and readiness of conception; the mathematical turn of his
mind rendered him apt at all kinds of calculation, while his naturally
poetical feelings threw a light and pleasing veil over the dry reality
of arithmetical computation, or the rigid severity of geometry. He
already knew Italian, and had also picked up a little of the Romaic
dialect during voyages to the East; and by the aid of these two
languages he easily comprehended the construction of all the others, so
that at the end of six months he began to speak Spanish, English, and
German.

In strict accordance with the promise made to the abbГғВ©, DantГғВЁs spoke no
more of escape. Perhaps the delight his studies afforded him left no
room for such thoughts; perhaps the recollection that he had pledged
his word (on which his sense of honor was keen) kept him from referring
in any way to the possibilities of flight. Days, even months, passed by
unheeded in one rapid and instructive course. At the end of a year
DantГғВЁs was a new man. DantГғВЁs observed, however, that Faria, in spite of
the relief his society afforded, daily grew sadder; one thought seemed
incessantly to harass and distract his mind. Sometimes he would fall
into long reveries, sigh heavily and involuntarily, then suddenly rise,
and, with folded arms, begin pacing the confined space of his dungeon.
One day he stopped all at once, and exclaimed:

ГўВҖВңAh, if there were no sentinel!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere shall not be one a minute longer than you please,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs,
who had followed the working of his thoughts as accurately as though
his brain were enclosed in crystal so clear as to display its minutest
operations.

ГўВҖВңI have already told you,ГўВҖВқ answered the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңthat I loathe the idea
of shedding blood.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd yet the murder, if you choose to call it so, would be simply a
measure of self-preservation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo matter! I could never agree to it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill, you have thought of it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIncessantly, alas!ГўВҖВқ cried the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңAnd you have discovered a means of regaining our freedom, have you
not?ГўВҖВқ asked DantГғВЁs eagerly.

ГўВҖВңI have; if it were only possible to place a deaf and blind sentinel in
the gallery beyond us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe shall be both blind and deaf,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man, with an air
of determination that made his companion shudder.

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ cried the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңimpossible!ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs endeavored to renew the subject; the abbГғВ© shook his head in
token of disapproval, and refused to make any further response. Three
months passed away.

ГўВҖВңAre you strong?ГўВҖВқ the abbГғВ© asked one day of DantГғВЁs. The young man, in
reply, took up the chisel, bent it into the form of a horseshoe, and
then as readily straightened it.

ГўВҖВңAnd will you engage not to do any harm to the sentry, except as a last
resort?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI promise on my honor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңwe may hope to put our design into execution.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd how long shall we be in accomplishing the necessary work?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt least a year.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd shall we begin at once?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt once.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe have lost a year to no purpose!ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңDo you consider the last twelve months to have been wasted?ГўВҖВқ asked the
abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңForgive me!ГўВҖВқ cried Edmond, blushing deeply.

ГўВҖВңTut, tut!ГўВҖВқ answered the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңman is but man after all, and you are
about the best specimen of the genus I have ever known. Come, let me
show you my plan.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© then showed DantГғВЁs the sketch he had made for their escape. It
consisted of a plan of his own cell and that of DantГғВЁs, with the
passage which united them. In this passage he proposed to drive a level
as they do in mines; this level would bring the two prisoners
immediately beneath the gallery where the sentry kept watch; once
there, a large excavation would be made, and one of the flag-stones
with which the gallery was paved be so completely loosened that at the
desired moment it would give way beneath the feet of the soldier, who,
stunned by his fall, would be immediately bound and gagged by DantГғВЁs
before he had power to offer any resistance. The prisoners were then to
make their way through one of the gallery windows, and to let
themselves down from the outer walls by means of the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs ladder of
cords.

DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ eyes sparkled with joy, and he rubbed his hands with delight at
the idea of a plan so simple, yet apparently so certain to succeed.
That very day the miners began their labors, with a vigor and alacrity
proportionate to their long rest from fatigue and their hopes of
ultimate success. Nothing interrupted the progress of the work except
the necessity that each was under of returning to his cell in
anticipation of the turnkeyГўВҖВҷs visits. They had learned to distinguish
the almost imperceptible sound of his footsteps as he descended towards
their dungeons, and happily, never failed of being prepared for his
coming. The fresh earth excavated during their present work, and which
would have entirely blocked up the old passage, was thrown, by degrees
and with the utmost precaution, out of the window in either FariaГўВҖВҷs or
DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ cell, the rubbish being first pulverized so finely that the
night wind carried it far away without permitting the smallest trace to
remain.

More than a year had been consumed in this undertaking, the only tools
for which had been a chisel, a knife, and a wooden lever; Faria still
continuing to instruct DantГғВЁs by conversing with him, sometimes in one
language, sometimes in another; at others, relating to him the history
of nations and great men who from time to time have risen to fame and
trodden the path of glory. The abbГғВ© was a man of the world, and had,
moreover, mixed in the first society of the day; he wore an air of
melancholy dignity which DantГғВЁs, thanks to the imitative powers
bestowed on him by nature, easily acquired, as well as that outward
polish and politeness he had before been wanting in, and which is
seldom possessed except by those who have been placed in constant
intercourse with persons of high birth and breeding.

At the end of fifteen months the level was finished, and the excavation
completed beneath the gallery, and the two workmen could distinctly
hear the measured tread of the sentinel as he paced to and fro over
their heads. Compelled, as they were, to await a night sufficiently
dark to favor their flight, they were obliged to defer their final
attempt till that auspicious moment should arrive; their greatest dread
now was lest the stone through which the sentry was doomed to fall
should give way before its right time, and this they had in some
measure provided against by propping it up with a small beam which they
had discovered in the walls through which they had worked their way.
DantГғВЁs was occupied in arranging this piece of wood when he heard
Faria, who had remained in EdmondГўВҖВҷs cell for the purpose of cutting a
peg to secure their rope-ladder, call to him in a tone indicative of
great suffering. DantГғВЁs hastened to his dungeon, where he found him
standing in the middle of the room, pale as death, his forehead
streaming with perspiration, and his hands clenched tightly together.

ГўВҖВңGracious heavens!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңwhat is the matter? what has
happened?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңQuick! quick!ГўВҖВқ returned the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңlisten to what I have to say.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs looked in fear and wonder at the livid countenance of Faria,
whose eyes, already dull and sunken, were surrounded by purple circles,
while his lips were white as those of a corpse, and his very hair
seemed to stand on end.

ГўВҖВңTell me, I beseech you, what ails you?ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs, letting his
chisel fall to the floor.

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ faltered out the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңall is over with me. I am seized with a
terrible, perhaps mortal illness; I can feel that the paroxysm is fast
approaching. I had a similar attack the year previous to my
imprisonment. This malady admits but of one remedy; I will tell you
what that is. Go into my cell as quickly as you can; draw out one of
the feet that support the bed; you will find it has been hollowed out
for the purpose of containing a small phial you will see there
half-filled with a red-looking fluid. Bring it to meГўВҖВ”or ratherГўВҖВ”no,
no!ГўВҖВ”I may be found here, therefore help me back to my room while I have
the strength to drag myself along. Who knows what may happen, or how
long the attack may last?ГўВҖВқ

In spite of the magnitude of the misfortune which thus suddenly
frustrated his hopes, DantГғВЁs did not lose his presence of mind, but
descended into the passage, dragging his unfortunate companion with
him; then, half-carrying, half-supporting him, he managed to reach the
abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs chamber, when he immediately laid the sufferer on his bed.

ГўВҖВңThanks,ГўВҖВқ said the poor abbГғВ©, shivering as though his veins were filled
with ice. ГўВҖВңI am about to be seized with a fit of catalepsy; when it
comes to its height I shall probably lie still and motionless as though
dead, uttering neither sigh nor groan. On the other hand, the symptoms
may be much more violent, and cause me to fall into fearful
convulsions, foam at the mouth, and cry out loudly. Take care my cries
are not heard, for if they are it is more than probable I should be
removed to another part of the prison, and we be separated forever.
When I become quite motionless, cold, and rigid as a corpse, then, and
not before,ГўВҖВ”be careful about this,ГўВҖВ”force open my teeth with the knife,
pour from eight to ten drops of the liquor contained in the phial down
my throat, and I may perhaps revive.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerhaps!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed DantГғВЁs in grief-stricken tones.

ГўВҖВңHelp! help!ГўВҖВқ cried the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВ”IГўВҖВ”dieГўВҖВ”IГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

0229m



So sudden and violent was the fit that the unfortunate prisoner was
unable to complete the sentence; a violent convulsion shook his whole
frame, his eyes started from their sockets, his mouth was drawn on one
side, his cheeks became purple, he struggled, foamed, dashed himself
about, and uttered the most dreadful cries, which, however, DantГғВЁs
prevented from being heard by covering his head with the blanket. The
fit lasted two hours; then, more helpless than an infant, and colder
and paler than marble, more crushed and broken than a reed trampled
under foot, he fell back, doubled up in one last convulsion, and became
as rigid as a corpse.

Edmond waited till life seemed extinct in the body of his friend, then,
taking up the knife, he with difficulty forced open the closely fixed
jaws, carefully administered the appointed number of drops, and
anxiously awaited the result. An hour passed away and the old man gave
no sign of returning animation. DantГғВЁs began to fear he had delayed too
long ere he administered the remedy, and, thrusting his hands into his
hair, continued gazing on the lifeless features of his friend. At
length a slight color tinged the livid cheeks, consciousness returned
to the dull, open eyeballs, a faint sigh issued from the lips, and the
sufferer made a feeble effort to move.

ГўВҖВңHe is saved! he is saved!ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs in a paroxysm of delight.

The sick man was not yet able to speak, but he pointed with evident
anxiety towards the door. DantГғВЁs listened, and plainly distinguished
the approaching steps of the jailer. It was therefore near seven
oГўВҖВҷclock; but EdmondГўВҖВҷs anxiety had put all thoughts of time out of his
head.

The young man sprang to the entrance, darted through it, carefully
drawing the stone over the opening, and hurried to his cell. He had
scarcely done so before the door opened, and the jailer saw the
prisoner seated as usual on the side of his bed. Almost before the key
had turned in the lock, and before the departing steps of the jailer
had died away in the long corridor he had to traverse, DantГғВЁs, whose
restless anxiety concerning his friend left him no desire to touch the
food brought him, hurried back to the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs chamber, and raising the
stone by pressing his head against it, was soon beside the sick manГўВҖВҷs
couch. Faria had now fully regained his consciousness, but he still lay
helpless and exhausted on his miserable bed.

ГўВҖВңI did not expect to see you again,ГўВҖВқ said he feebly, to DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңAnd why not?ГўВҖВқ asked the young man. ГўВҖВңDid you fancy yourself dying?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, I had no such idea; but, knowing that all was ready for flight, I
thought you might have made your escape.ГўВҖВқ

The deep glow of indignation suffused the cheeks of DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңWithout you? Did you really think me capable of that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt least,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңI now see how wrong such an opinion would
have been. Alas, alas! I am fearfully exhausted and debilitated by this
attack.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBe of good cheer,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңyour strength will return.ГўВҖВқ And as
he spoke he seated himself near the bed beside Faria, and took his
hands. The abbГғВ© shook his head.

ГўВҖВңThe last attack I had,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңlasted but half an hour, and after
it I was hungry, and got up without help; now I can move neither my
right arm nor leg, and my head seems uncomfortable, which shows that
there has been a suffusion of blood on the brain. The third attack will
either carry me off, or leave me paralyzed for life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ cried DantГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңyou are mistakenГўВҖВ”you will not die! And your
third attack (if, indeed, you should have another) will find you at
liberty. We shall save you another time, as we have done this, only
with a better chance of success, because we shall be able to command
every requisite assistance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy good Edmond,ГўВҖВқ answered the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңbe not deceived. The attack which
has just passed away, condemns me forever to the walls of a prison.
None can fly from a dungeon who cannot walk.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, we will wait,ГўВҖВ”a week, a month, two months, if need be,ГўВҖВ”and
meanwhile your strength will return. Everything is in readiness for our
flight, and we can select any time we choose. As soon as you feel able
to swim we will go.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI shall never swim again,ГўВҖВқ replied Faria. ГўВҖВңThis arm is paralyzed; not
for a time, but forever. Lift it, and judge if I am mistaken.ГўВҖВқ

The young man raised the arm, which fell back by its own weight,
perfectly inanimate and helpless. A sigh escaped him.

ГўВҖВңYou are convinced now, Edmond, are you not?ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©. ГўВҖВңDepend
upon it, I know what I say. Since the first attack I experienced of
this malady, I have continually reflected on it. Indeed, I expected it,
for it is a family inheritance; both my father and grandfather died of
it in a third attack. The physician who prepared for me the remedy I
have twice successfully taken, was no other than the celebrated
Cabanis, and he predicted a similar end for me.ГўВҖВқ

0233m



ГўВҖВңThe physician may be mistaken!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңAnd as for your
poor arm, what difference will that make? I can take you on my
shoulders, and swim for both of us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy son,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңyou, who are a sailor and a swimmer, must
know as well as I do that a man so loaded would sink before he had done
fifty strokes. Cease, then, to allow yourself to be duped by vain
hopes, that even your own excellent heart refuses to believe in. Here I
shall remain till the hour of my deliverance arrives, and that, in all
human probability, will be the hour of my death. As for you, who are
young and active, delay not on my account, but flyГўВҖВ”goГўВҖВ”I give you back
your promise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is well,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңThen I shall also remain.ГўВҖВқ Then, rising and
extending his hand with an air of solemnity over the old manГўВҖВҷs head, he
slowly added, ГўВҖВңBy the blood of Christ I swear never to leave you while
you live.ГўВҖВқ

Faria gazed fondly on his noble-minded, single-hearted, high-principled
young friend, and read in his countenance ample confirmation of the
sincerity of his devotion and the loyalty of his purpose.

ГўВҖВңThanks,ГўВҖВқ murmured the invalid, extending one hand. ГўВҖВңI accept. You may
one of these days reap the reward of your disinterested devotion. But
as I cannot, and you will not, quit this place, it becomes necessary to
fill up the excavation beneath the soldierГўВҖВҷs gallery; he might, by
chance, hear the hollow sound of his footsteps, and call the attention
of his officer to the circumstance. That would bring about a discovery
which would inevitably lead to our being separated. Go, then, and set
about this work, in which, unhappily, I can offer you no assistance;
keep at it all night, if necessary, and do not return here tomorrow
till after the jailer has visited me. I shall have something of the
greatest importance to communicate to you.ГўВҖВқ

DantГғВЁs took the hand of the abbГғВ© in his, and affectionately pressed it.
Faria smiled encouragingly on him, and the young man retired to his
task, in the spirit of obedience and respect which he had sworn to show
towards his aged friend.



 Chapter 18. The Treasure

When DantГғВЁs returned next morning to the chamber of his companion in
captivity, he found Faria seated and looking composed. In the ray of
light which entered by the narrow window of his cell, he held open in
his left hand, of which alone, it will be recollected, he retained the
use, a sheet of paper, which, from being constantly rolled into a small
compass, had the form of a cylinder, and was not easily kept open. He
did not speak, but showed the paper to DantГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңWhat is that?ГўВҖВқ he inquired.

ГўВҖВңLook at it,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ© with a smile.

ГўВҖВңI have looked at it with all possible attention,ГўВҖВқ said DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңand I
only see a half-burnt paper, on which are traces of Gothic characters
inscribed with a peculiar kind of ink.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis paper, my friend,ГўВҖВқ said Faria, ГўВҖВңI may now avow to you, since I
have the proof of your fidelityГўВҖВ”this paper is my treasure, of which,
from this day forth, one-half belongs to you.ГўВҖВқ

The sweat started forth on DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ brow. Until this day and for how
long a time!ГўВҖВ”he had refrained from talking of the treasure, which had
brought upon the abbГғВ© the accusation of madness. With his instinctive
delicacy Edmond had preferred avoiding any touch on this painful chord,
and Faria had been equally silent. He had taken the silence of the old
man for a return to reason; and now these few words uttered by Faria,
after so painful a crisis, seemed to indicate a serious relapse into
mental alienation.

ГўВҖВңYour treasure?ГўВҖВқ stammered DantГғВЁs. Faria smiled.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said he. ГўВҖВңYou have, indeed, a noble nature, Edmond, and I see by
your paleness and agitation what is passing in your heart at this
moment. No, be assured, I am not mad. This treasure exists, DantГғВЁs, and
if I have not been allowed to possess it, you will. YesГўВҖВ”you. No one
would listen or believe me, because everyone thought me mad; but you,
who must know that I am not, listen to me, and believe me so afterwards
if you will.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ murmured Edmond to himself, ГўВҖВңthis is a terrible relapse! There
was only this blow wanting.ГўВҖВқ Then he said aloud, ГўВҖВңMy dear friend, your
attack has, perhaps, fatigued you; had you not better repose awhile?
Tomorrow, if you will, I will hear your narrative; but today I wish to
nurse you carefully. Besides,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңa treasure is not a thing we
need hurry about.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary, it is a matter of the utmost importance, Edmond!ГўВҖВқ
replied the old man. ГўВҖВңWho knows if tomorrow, or the next day after, the
third attack may not come on? and then must not all be over? Yes,
indeed, I have often thought with a bitter joy that these riches, which
would make the wealth of a dozen families, will be forever lost to
those men who persecute me. This idea was one of vengeance to me, and I
tasted it slowly in the night of my dungeon and the despair of my
captivity. But now I have forgiven the world for the love of you; now
that I see you, young and with a promising future,ГўВҖВ”now that I think of
all that may result to you in the good fortune of such a disclosure, I
shudder at any delay, and tremble lest I should not assure to one as
worthy as yourself the possession of so vast an amount of hidden
wealth.ГўВҖВқ

Edmond turned away his head with a sigh.

ГўВҖВңYou persist in your incredulity, Edmond,ГўВҖВқ continued Faria. ГўВҖВңMy words
have not convinced you. I see you require proofs. Well, then, read this
paper, which I have never shown to anyone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTomorrow, my dear friend,ГўВҖВқ said Edmond, desirous of not yielding to
the old manГўВҖВҷs madness. ГўВҖВңI thought it was understood that we should not
talk of that until tomorrow.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen we will not talk of it until tomorrow; but read this paper
today.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will not irritate him,ГўВҖВқ thought Edmond, and taking the paper, of
which half was wanting,ГўВҖВ”having been burnt, no doubt, by some
accident,ГўВҖВ”he read:

ГўВҖВңthis treasure, which may amount to two...

of Roman crowns in the most distant a...

of the second opening wh...

declare to belong to him alo...

heir.

                    ГўВҖВң25th April, 149ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ


ГўВҖВңWell!ГўВҖВқ said Faria, when the young man had finished reading it.

ГўВҖВңWhy,ГўВҖВқ replied DantГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңI see nothing but broken lines and unconnected
words, which are rendered illegible by fire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, to you, my friend, who read them for the first time; but not for
me, who have grown pale over them by many nightsГўВҖВҷ study, and have
reconstructed every phrase, completed every thought.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd do you believe you have discovered the hidden meaning?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am sure I have, and you shall judge for yourself; but first listen
to the history of this paper.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSilence!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed DantГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңSteps approachГўВҖВ”I goГўВҖВ”adieu!ГўВҖВқ

And DantГғВЁs, happy to escape the history and explanation which would be
sure to confirm his belief in his friendГўВҖВҷs mental instability, glided
like a snake along the narrow passage; while Faria, restored by his
alarm to a certain amount of activity, pushed the stone into place with
his foot, and covered it with a mat in order the more effectually to
avoid discovery.

It was the governor, who, hearing of FariaГўВҖВҷs illness from the jailer,
had come in person to see him.

Faria sat up to receive him, avoiding all gestures in order that he
might conceal from the governor the paralysis that had already half
stricken him with death. His fear was lest the governor, touched with
pity, might order him to be removed to better quarters, and thus
separate him from his young companion. But fortunately this was not the
case, and the governor left him, convinced that the poor madman, for
whom in his heart he felt a kind of affection, was only troubled with a
slight indisposition.

During this time, Edmond, seated on his bed with his head in his hands,
tried to collect his scattered thoughts. Faria, since their first
acquaintance, had been on all points so rational and logical, so
wonderfully sagacious, in fact, that he could not understand how so
much wisdom on all points could be allied with madness. Was Faria
deceived as to his treasure, or was all the world deceived as to Faria?

DantГғВЁs remained in his cell all day, not daring to return to his
friend, thinking thus to defer the moment when he should be convinced,
once for all, that the abbГғВ© was madГўВҖВ”such a conviction would be so
terrible!

But, towards the evening after the hour for the customary visit had
gone by, Faria, not seeing the young man appear, tried to move and get
over the distance which separated them. Edmond shuddered when he heard
the painful efforts which the old man made to drag himself along; his
leg was inert, and he could no longer make use of one arm. Edmond was
obliged to assist him, for otherwise he would not have been able to
enter by the small aperture which led to DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ chamber.

ГўВҖВңHere I am, pursuing you remorselessly,ГўВҖВқ he said with a benignant
smile. ГўВҖВңYou thought to escape my munificence, but it is in vain. Listen
to me.ГўВҖВқ

Edmond saw there was no escape, and placing the old man on his bed, he
seated himself on the stool beside him.

ГўВҖВңYou know,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңthat I was the secretary and intimate
friend of Cardinal Spada, the last of the princes of that name. I owe
to this worthy lord all the happiness I ever knew. He was not rich,
although the wealth of his family had passed into a proverb, and I
heard the phrase very often, ГўВҖВҳAs rich as a Spada.ГўВҖВҷ But he, like public
rumor, lived on this reputation for wealth; his palace was my paradise.
I was tutor to his nephews, who are dead; and when he was alone in the
world, I tried by absolute devotion to his will, to make up to him all
he had done for me during ten years of unremitting kindness. The
cardinalГўВҖВҷs house had no secrets for me. I had often seen my noble
patron annotating ancient volumes, and eagerly searching amongst dusty
family manuscripts. One day when I was reproaching him for his
unavailing searches, and deploring the prostration of mind that
followed them, he looked at me, and, smiling bitterly, opened a volume
relating to the History of the City of Rome. There, in the twentieth
chapter of the Life of Pope Alexander VI., were the following lines,
which I can never forget:ГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳThe great wars of Romagna had ended; CГғВҰsar Borgia, who had completed
his conquest, had need of money to purchase all Italy. The pope had
also need of money to bring matters to an end with Louis XII. King of
France, who was formidable still in spite of his recent reverses; and
it was necessary, therefore, to have recourse to some profitable
scheme, which was a matter of great difficulty in the impoverished
condition of exhausted Italy. His holiness had an idea. He determined
to make two cardinals.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңBy choosing two of the greatest personages of Rome, especially rich
menГўВҖВ”_this_ was the return the Holy Father looked for. In the first
place, he could sell the great appointments and splendid offices which
the cardinals already held; and then he had the two hats to sell
besides. There was a third point in view, which will appear hereafter.

ГўВҖВңThe pope and CГғВҰsar Borgia first found the two future cardinals; they
were Giovanni Rospigliosi, who held four of the highest dignities of
the Holy See, and CГғВҰsar Spada, one of the noblest and richest of the
Roman nobility; both felt the high honor of such a favor from the pope.
They were ambitious, and CГғВҰsar Borgia soon found purchasers for their
appointments. The result was, that Rospigliosi and Spada paid for being
cardinals, and eight other persons paid for the offices the cardinals
held before their elevation, and thus eight hundred thousand crowns
entered into the coffers of the speculators.

0239m



ГўВҖВңIt is time now to proceed to the last part of the speculation. The
pope heaped attentions upon Rospigliosi and Spada, conferred upon them
the insignia of the cardinalate, and induced them to arrange their
affairs and take up their residence at Rome. Then the pope and CГғВҰsar
Borgia invited the two cardinals to dinner. This was a matter of
dispute between the Holy Father and his son. CГғВҰsar thought they could
make use of one of the means which he always had ready for his friends,
that is to say, in the first place, the famous key which was given to
certain persons with the request that they go and open a designated
cupboard. This key was furnished with a small iron point,ГўВҖВ”a negligence
on the part of the locksmith. When this was pressed to effect the
opening of the cupboard, of which the lock was difficult, the person
was pricked by this small point, and died next day. Then there was the
ring with the lionГўВҖВҷs head, which CГғВҰsar wore when he wanted to greet his
friends with a clasp of the hand. The lion bit the hand thus favored,
and at the end of twenty-four hours, the bite was mortal.

ГўВҖВңCГғВҰsar proposed to his father, that they should either ask the
cardinals to open the cupboard, or shake hands with them; but Alexander
VI. replied: ГўВҖВҳNow as to the worthy cardinals, Spada and Rospigliosi,
let us ask both of them to dinner, something tells me that we shall get
that money back. Besides, you forget, CГғВҰsar, an indigestion declares
itself immediately, while a prick or a bite occasions a delay of a day
or two.ГўВҖВҷ CГғВҰsar gave way before such cogent reasoning, and the cardinals
were consequently invited to dinner.

ГўВҖВңThe table was laid in a vineyard belonging to the pope, near San
Pierdarena, a charming retreat which the cardinals knew very well by
report. Rospigliosi, quite set up with his new dignities, went with a
good appetite and his most ingratiating manner. Spada, a prudent man,
and greatly attached to his only nephew, a young captain of the highest
promise, took paper and pen, and made his will. He then sent word to
his nephew to wait for him near the vineyard; but it appeared the
servant did not find him.

ГўВҖВңSpada knew what these invitations meant; since Christianity, so
eminently civilizing, had made progress in Rome, it was no longer a
centurion who came from the tyrant with a message, ГўВҖВҳCГғВҰsar wills that
you die.ГўВҖВҷ but it was a legate _ГғВ  latere_, who came with a smile on his
lips to say from the pope, ГўВҖВҳHis holiness requests you to dine with
him.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңSpada set out about two oГўВҖВҷclock to San Pierdarena. The pope awaited
him. The first sight that attracted the eyes of Spada was that of his
nephew, in full costume, and CГғВҰsar Borgia paying him most marked
attentions. Spada turned pale, as CГғВҰsar looked at him with an ironical
air, which proved that he had anticipated all, and that the snare was
well spread.

ГўВҖВңThey began dinner and Spada was only able to inquire of his nephew if
he had received his message. The nephew replied no; perfectly
comprehending the meaning of the question. It was too late, for he had
already drunk a glass of excellent wine, placed for him expressly by
the popeГўВҖВҷs butler. Spada at the same moment saw another bottle approach
him, which he was pressed to taste. An hour afterwards a physician
declared they were both poisoned through eating mushrooms. Spada died
