who might only have taken it and then betrayed him to the governor,
DantГғВЁs carefully preserved it, that in the event of his getting out of
prison he might have wherewithal to live, for the sale of such a
diamond would have quite sufficed to make his fortune.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, I suppose,ГўВҖВқ asked Caderousse, with eager, glowing looks, ГўВҖВңthat
it was a stone of immense value?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, everything is relative,ГўВҖВқ answered the abbГғВ©. ГўВҖВңTo one in EdmondГўВҖВҷs
position the diamond certainly was of great value. It was estimated at
fifty thousand francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBless me!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Caderousse, ГўВҖВңfifty thousand francs! Surely the
diamond was as large as a nut to be worth all that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңit was not of such a size as that; but you
shall judge for yourself. I have it with me.ГўВҖВқ

The sharp gaze of Caderousse was instantly directed towards the
priestГўВҖВҷs garments, as though hoping to discover the location of the
treasure. Calmly drawing forth from his pocket a small box covered with
black shagreen, the abbГғВ© opened it, and displayed to the dazzled eyes
of Caderousse the sparkling jewel it contained, set in a ring of
admirable workmanship.

ГўВҖВңAnd that diamond,ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse, almost breathless with eager
admiration, ГўВҖВңyou say, is worth fifty thousand francs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is, without the setting, which is also valuable,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©,
as he closed the box, and returned it to his pocket, while its
brilliant hues seemed still to dance before the eyes of the fascinated
innkeeper.

ГўВҖВңBut how comes the diamond in your possession, sir? Did Edmond make you
his heir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, merely his testamentary executor. ГўВҖВҳI once possessed four dear and
faithful friends, besides the maiden to whom I was betrothedГўВҖВҷ he said;
ГўВҖВҳand I feel convinced they have all unfeignedly grieved over my loss.
The name of one of the four friends is Caderousse.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ The innkeeper
shivered.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳAnother of the number,ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ continued the abbГғВ©, without seeming to
notice the emotion of Caderousse, ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳis called Danglars; and the third,
in spite of being my rival, entertained a very sincere affection for
me.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

A fiendish smile played over the features of Caderousse, who was about
to break in upon the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs speech, when the latter, waving his hand,
said, ГўВҖВңAllow me to finish first, and then if you have any observations
to make, you can do so afterwards. ГўВҖВҳThe third of my friends, although
my rival, was much attached to me,ГўВҖВ”his name was Fernand; that of my
betrothed wasГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”Stay, stay,ГўВҖВқ continued the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңI have forgotten what
he called her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse eagerly.

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, with a stifled sigh, ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs it was.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo on,ГўВҖВқ urged Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңBring me a _carafe_ of water,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©.

Caderousse quickly performed the strangerГўВҖВҷs bidding; and after pouring
some into a glass, and slowly swallowing its contents, the abbГғВ©,
resuming his usual placidity of manner, said, as he placed his empty
glass on the table:

ГўВҖВңWhere did we leave off?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe name of EdmondГўВҖВҷs betrothed was MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo be sure. ГўВҖВҳYou will go to Marseilles,ГўВҖВҷ said DantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВ”for you
understand, I repeat his words just as he uttered them. Do you
understand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerfectly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳYou will sell this diamond; you will divide the money into five equal
parts, and give an equal portion to these good friends, the only
persons who have loved me upon earth.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut why into five parts?ГўВҖВқ asked Caderousse; ГўВҖВңyou only mentioned four
persons.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause the fifth is dead, as I hear. The fifth sharer in EdmondГўВҖВҷs
bequest, was his own father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңToo true, too true!ГўВҖВқ ejaculated Caderousse, almost suffocated by the
contending passions which assailed him, ГўВҖВңthe poor old man did die.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI learned so much at Marseilles,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©, making a strong
effort to appear indifferent; ГўВҖВңbut from the length of time that has
elapsed since the death of the elder DantГғВЁs, I was unable to obtain any
particulars of his end. Can you enlighten me on that point?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not know who could if I could not,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВңWhy, I
lived almost on the same floor with the poor old man. Ah, yes, about a
year after the disappearance of his son the poor old man died.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf what did he die?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, the doctors called his complaint gastro-enteritis, I believe; his
acquaintances say he died of grief; but I, who saw him in his dying
moments, I say he died ofГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse paused.

ГўВҖВңOf what?ГўВҖВқ asked the priest, anxiously and eagerly.

ГўВҖВңWhy, of downright starvation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStarvation!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the abbГғВ©, springing from his seat. ГўВҖВңWhy, the
vilest animals are not suffered to die by such a death as that. The
very dogs that wander houseless and homeless in the streets find some
pitying hand to cast them a mouthful of bread; and that a man, a
Christian, should be allowed to perish of hunger in the midst of other
men who call themselves Christians, is too horrible for belief. Oh, it
is impossible!ГўВҖВ”utterly impossible!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat I have said, I have said,ГўВҖВқ answered Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңAnd you are a fool for having said anything about it,ГўВҖВқ said a voice
from the top of the stairs. ГўВҖВңWhy should you meddle with what does not
concern you?ГўВҖВқ

The two men turned quickly, and saw the sickly countenance of La
Carconte peering between the baluster rails; attracted by the sound of
voices, she had feebly dragged herself down the stairs, and, seated on
the lower step, head on knees, she had listened to the foregoing
conversation.

ГўВҖВңMind your own business, wife,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse sharply. ГўВҖВңThis
gentleman asks me for information, which common politeness will not
permit me to refuse.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoliteness, you simpleton!ГўВҖВқ retorted La Carconte. ГўВҖВңWhat have you to do
with politeness, I should like to know? Better study a little common
prudence. How do you know the motives that person may have for trying
to extract all he can from you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI pledge you my word, madam,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңthat my intentions are
good; and that your husband can incur no risk, provided he answers me
candidly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, thatГўВҖВҷs all very fine,ГўВҖВқ retorted the woman. ГўВҖВңNothing is easier than
to begin with fair promises and assurances of nothing to fear; but when
poor, silly folks, like my husband there, have been persuaded to tell
all they know, the promises and assurances of safety are quickly
forgotten; and at some moment when nobody is expecting it, behold
trouble and misery, and all sorts of persecutions, are heaped on the
unfortunate wretches, who cannot even see whence all their afflictions
come.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNay, nay, my good woman, make yourself perfectly easy, I beg of you.
Whatever evils may befall you, they will not be occasioned by my
instrumentality, that I solemnly promise you.ГўВҖВқ

La Carconte muttered a few inarticulate words, then let her head again
drop upon her knees, and went into a fit of ague, leaving the two
speakers to resume the conversation, but remaining so as to be able to
hear every word they uttered. Again the abbГғВ© had been obliged to
swallow a draught of water to calm the emotions that threatened to
overpower him.

When he had sufficiently recovered himself, he said, ГўВҖВңIt appears, then,
that the miserable old man you were telling me of was forsaken by
everyone. Surely, had not such been the case, he would not have
perished by so dreadful a death.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, he was not altogether forsaken,ГўВҖВқ continued Caderousse, ГўВҖВңfor
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs the Catalan and Monsieur Morrel were very kind to him; but
somehow the poor old man had contracted a profound hatred for
FernandГўВҖВ”the very person,ГўВҖВқ added Caderousse with a bitter smile, ГўВҖВңthat
you named just now as being one of DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ faithful and attached
friends.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd was he not so?ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңGaspard, Gaspard!ГўВҖВқ murmured the woman, from her seat on the stairs,
ГўВҖВңmind what you are saying!ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse made no reply to these words, though evidently irritated and
annoyed by the interruption, but, addressing the abbГғВ©, said, ГўВҖВңCan a man
be faithful to another whose wife he covets and desires for himself?
But DantГғВЁs was so honorable and true in his own nature, that he
believed everybodyГўВҖВҷs professions of friendship. Poor Edmond, he was
cruelly deceived; but it was fortunate that he never knew, or he might
have found it more difficult, when on his deathbed, to pardon his
enemies. And, whatever people may say,ГўВҖВқ continued Caderousse, in his
native language, which was not altogether devoid of rude poetry, ГўВҖВңI
cannot help being more frightened at the idea of the malediction of the
dead than the hatred of the living.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңImbecile!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed La Carconte.

ГўВҖВңDo you, then, know in what manner Fernand injured DantГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ inquired
the abbГғВ© of Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңDo I? No one better.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSpeak out then, say what it was!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGaspard!ГўВҖВқ cried La Carconte, ГўВҖВңdo as you will; you are masterГўВҖВ”but if
you take my advice youГўВҖВҷll hold your tongue.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, wife,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt know but what youГўВҖВҷre right!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo you will say nothing?ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңWhy, what good would it do?ГўВҖВқ asked Caderousse. ГўВҖВңIf the poor lad were
living, and came to me and begged that I would candidly tell which were
his true and which his false friends, why, perhaps, I should not
hesitate. But you tell me he is no more, and therefore can have nothing
to do with hatred or revenge, so let all such feeling be buried with
him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou prefer, then,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңthat I should bestow on men you say
are false and treacherous, the reward intended for faithful
friendship?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is true enough,ГўВҖВқ returned Caderousse. ГўВҖВңYou say truly, the gift of
poor Edmond was not meant for such traitors as Fernand and Danglars;
besides, what would it be to them? no more than a drop of water in the
ocean.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRemember,ГўВҖВқ chimed in La Carconte, ГўВҖВңthose two could crush you at a
single blow!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ inquired the abbГғВ©. ГўВҖВңAre these persons, then, so rich and
powerful?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you not know their history?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not. Pray relate it to me!ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse seemed to reflect for a few moments, then said, ГўВҖВңNo, truly,
it would take up too much time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, my good friend,ГўВҖВқ returned the abbГғВ©, in a tone that indicated
utter indifference on his part, ГўВҖВңyou are at liberty, either to speak or
be silent, just as you please; for my own part, I respect your scruples
and admire your sentiments; so let the matter end. I shall do my duty
as conscientiously as I can, and fulfil my promise to the dying man. My
first business will be to dispose of this diamond.ГўВҖВқ

So saying, the abbГғВ© again drew the small box from his pocket, opened
it, and contrived to hold it in such a light, that a bright flash of
brilliant hues passed before the dazzled gaze of Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңWife, wife!ГўВҖВқ cried he in a hoarse voice, ГўВҖВңcome here!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDiamond!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed La Carconte, rising and descending to the chamber
with a tolerably firm step; ГўВҖВңwhat diamond are you talking about?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, did you not hear all we said?ГўВҖВқ inquired Caderousse. ГўВҖВңIt is a
beautiful diamond left by poor Edmond DantГғВЁs, to be sold, and the money
divided between his father, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, his betrothed bride, Fernand,
Danglars, and myself. The jewel is worth at least fifty thousand
francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, what a magnificent jewel!ГўВҖВқ cried the astonished woman.

ГўВҖВңThe fifth part of the profits from this stone belongs to us then, does
it not?ГўВҖВқ asked Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңIt does,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңwith the addition of an equal division of
that part intended for the elder DantГғВЁs, which I believe myself at
liberty to divide equally with the four survivors.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd why among us four?ГўВҖВқ inquired Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңAs being the friends Edmond esteemed most faithful and devoted to
him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt call those friends who betray and ruin you,ГўВҖВқ murmured the wife
in her turn, in a low, muttering voice.

ГўВҖВңOf course not!ГўВҖВқ rejoined Caderousse quickly; ГўВҖВңno more do I, and that
was what I was observing to this gentleman just now. I said I looked
upon it as a sacrilegious profanation to reward treachery, perhaps
crime.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRemember,ГўВҖВқ answered the abbГғВ© calmly, as he replaced the jewel and its
case in the pocket of his cassock, ГўВҖВңit is your fault, not mine, that I
do so. You will have the goodness to furnish me with the address of
both Fernand and Danglars, in order that I may execute EdmondГўВҖВҷs last
wishes.ГўВҖВқ

The agitation of Caderousse became extreme, and large drops of
perspiration rolled from his heated brow. As he saw the abbГғВ© rise from
his seat and go towards the door, as though to ascertain if his horse
were sufficiently refreshed to continue his journey, Caderousse and his
wife exchanged looks of deep meaning.

0335m



ГўВҖВңThere, you see, wife,ГўВҖВқ said the former, ГўВҖВңthis splendid diamond might
all be ours, if we chose!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you believe it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, surely a man of his holy profession would not deceive us!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ replied La Carconte, ГўВҖВңdo as you like. For my part, I wash my
hands of the affair.ГўВҖВқ

So saying, she once more climbed the staircase leading to her chamber,
her body convulsed with chills, and her teeth rattling in her head, in
spite of the intense heat of the weather. Arrived at the top stair, she
turned round, and called out, in a warning tone, to her husband,
ГўВҖВңGaspard, consider well what you are about to do!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have both reflected and decided,ГўВҖВқ answered he.

La Carconte then entered her chamber, the flooring of which creaked
beneath her heavy, uncertain tread, as she proceeded towards her
armchair, into which she fell as though exhausted.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©, as he returned to the apartment below, ГўВҖВңwhat
have you made up your mind to do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo tell you all I know,ГўВҖВқ was the reply.

ГўВҖВңI certainly think you act wisely in so doing,ГўВҖВқ said the priest. ГўВҖВңNot
because I have the least desire to learn anything you may please to
conceal from me, but simply that if, through your assistance, I could
distribute the legacy according to the wishes of the testator, why, so
much the better, that is all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hope it may be so,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, his face flushed with
cupidity.

ГўВҖВңI am all attention,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңStop a minute,ГўВҖВқ answered Caderousse; ГўВҖВңwe might be interrupted in the
most interesting part of my story, which would be a pity; and it is as
well that your visit hither should be made known only to ourselves.ГўВҖВқ

With these words he went stealthily to the door, which he closed, and,
by way of still greater precaution, bolted and barred it, as he was
accustomed to do at night.

During this time the abbГғВ© had chosen his place for listening at his
ease. He removed his seat into a corner of the room, where he himself
would be in deep shadow, while the light would be fully thrown on the
narrator; then, with head bent down and hands clasped, or rather
clenched together, he prepared to give his whole attention to
Caderousse, who seated himself on the little stool, exactly opposite to
him.

ГўВҖВңRemember, this is no affair of mine,ГўВҖВқ said the trembling voice of La
Carconte, as though through the flooring of her chamber she viewed the
scene that was enacting below.

ГўВҖВңEnough, enough!ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse; ГўВҖВңsay no more about it; I will
take all the consequences upon myself.ГўВҖВқ

And he began his story.



 Chapter 27. The Story

First, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңyou must make me a promise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is that?ГўВҖВқ inquired the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңWhy, if you ever make use of the details I am about to give you, that
you will never let anyone know that it was I who supplied them; for the
persons of whom I am about to talk are rich and powerful, and if they
only laid the tips of their fingers on me, I should break to pieces
like glass.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMake yourself easy, my friend,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©. ГўВҖВңI am a priest, and
confessions die in my breast. Recollect, our only desire is to carry
out, in a fitting manner, the last wishes of our friend. Speak, then,
without reserve, as without hatred; tell the truth, the whole truth; I
do not know, never may know, the persons of whom you are about to
speak; besides, I am an Italian, and not a Frenchman, and belong to
God, and not to man, and I shall shortly retire to my convent, which I
have only quitted to fulfil the last wishes of a dying man.ГўВҖВқ

This positive assurance seemed to give Caderousse a little courage.

ГўВҖВңWell, then, under these circumstances,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңI will, I
even believe I ought to undeceive you as to the friendship which poor
Edmond thought so sincere and unquestionable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBegin with his father, if you please.ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңEdmond talked
to me a great deal about the old man for whom he had the deepest love.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe history is a sad one, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, shaking his head;
ГўВҖВңperhaps you know all the earlier part of it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ answered the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңEdmond related to me everything until the
moment when he was arrested in a small cabaret close to Marseilles.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt La RГғВ©serve! Oh, yes; I can see it all before me this moment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas it not his betrothal feast?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was and the feast that began so gayly had a very sorrowful ending;
a police commissary, followed by four soldiers, entered, and DantГғВЁs was
arrested.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, and up to this point I know all,ГўВҖВқ said the priest. ГўВҖВңDantГғВЁs
himself only knew that which personally concerned him, for he never
beheld again the five persons I have named to you, or heard mention of
anyone of them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, when DantГғВЁs was arrested, Monsieur Morrel hastened to obtain the
particulars, and they were very sad. The old man returned alone to his
home, folded up his wedding suit with tears in his eyes, and paced up
and down his chamber the whole day, and would not go to bed at all, for
I was underneath him and heard him walking the whole night; and for
myself, I assure you I could not sleep either, for the grief of the
poor father gave me great uneasiness, and every step he took went to my
heart as really as if his foot had pressed against my breast.

ГўВҖВңThe next day MercГғВ©dГғВЁs came to implore the protection of M. de
Villefort; she did not obtain it, however, and went to visit the old
man; when she saw him so miserable and heart-broken, having passed a
sleepless night, and not touched food since the previous day, she
wished him to go with her that she might take care of him; but the old
man would not consent. ГўВҖВҳNo,ГўВҖВҷ was the old manГўВҖВҷs reply, ГўВҖВҳI will not leave
this house, for my poor dear boy loves me better than anything in the
world; and if he gets out of prison he will come and see me the first
thing, and what would he think if I did not wait here for him?ГўВҖВҷ I heard
all this from the window, for I was anxious that MercГғВ©dГғВЁs should
persuade the old man to accompany her, for his footsteps over my head
night and day did not leave me a momentГўВҖВҷs repose.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut did you not go upstairs and try to console the poor old man?ГўВҖВқ
asked the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңAh, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, ГўВҖВңwe cannot console those who will not be
consoled, and he was one of these; besides, I know not why, but he
seemed to dislike seeing me. One night, however, I heard his sobs, and
I could not resist my desire to go up to him, but when I reached his
door he was no longer weeping but praying. I cannot now repeat to you,
sir, all the eloquent words and imploring language he made use of; it
was more than piety, it was more than grief, and I, who am no canter,
and hate the Jesuits, said then to myself, ГўВҖВҳIt is really well, and I am
very glad that I have not any children; for if I were a father and felt
such excessive grief as the old man does, and did not find in my memory
or heart all he is now saying, I should throw myself into the sea at
once, for I could not bear it.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoor father!ГўВҖВқ murmured the priest.

ГўВҖВңFrom day to day he lived on alone, and more and more solitary. M.
Morrel and MercГғВ©dГғВЁs came to see him, but his door was closed; and,
although I was certain he was at home, he would not make any answer.
One day, when, contrary to his custom, he had admitted MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, and
the poor girl, in spite of her own grief and despair, endeavored to
console him, he said to her,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳBe assured, my dear daughter, he is dead;
and instead of expecting him, it is he who is awaiting us; I am quite
happy, for I am the oldest, and of course shall see him first.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңHowever well disposed a person may be, why, you see we leave off after
a time seeing persons who are in sorrow, they make one melancholy; and
so at last old DantГғВЁs was left all to himself, and I only saw from time
to time strangers go up to him and come down again with some bundle
they tried to hide; but I guessed what these bundles were, and that he
sold by degrees what he had to pay for his subsistence. At length the
poor old fellow reached the end of all he had; he owed three quartersГўВҖВҷ
rent, and they threatened to turn him out; he begged for another week,
which was granted to him. I know this, because the landlord came into
my apartment when he left his.

ГўВҖВңFor the first three days I heard him walking about as usual, but, on
the fourth I heard nothing. I then resolved to go up to him at all
risks. The door was closed, but I looked through the keyhole, and saw
him so pale and haggard, that believing him very ill, I went and told
M. Morrel and then ran on to MercГғВ©dГғВЁs. They both came immediately, M.
Morrel bringing a doctor, and the doctor said it was inflammation of
the bowels, and ordered him a limited diet. I was there, too, and I
never shall forget the old manГўВҖВҷs smile at this prescription.

ГўВҖВңFrom that time he received all who came; he had an excuse for not
eating any more; the doctor had put him on a diet.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© uttered a kind of groan.

ГўВҖВңThe story interests you, does it not, sir?ГўВҖВқ inquired Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңit is very affecting.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs came again, and she found him so altered that she was even
more anxious than before to have him taken to her own home. This was M.
MorrelГўВҖВҷs wish also, who would fain have conveyed the old man against
his consent; but the old man resisted, and cried so that they were
actually frightened. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs remained, therefore, by his bedside, and
M. Morrel went away, making a sign to the Catalan that he had left his
purse on the chimney-piece; but, availing himself of the doctorГўВҖВҷs
order, the old man would not take any sustenance; at length (after nine
days of despair and fasting), the old man died, cursing those who had
caused his misery, and saying to MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, ГўВҖВҳIf you ever see my Edmond
again, tell him I die blessing him.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© rose from his chair, made two turns round the chamber, and
pressed his trembling hand against his parched throat.

ГўВҖВңAnd you believe he diedГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf hunger, sir, of hunger,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВңI am as certain of it as
that we two are Christians.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ©, with a shaking hand, seized a glass of water that was
standing by him half-full, swallowed it at one gulp, and then resumed
his seat, with red eyes and pale cheeks.

ГўВҖВңThis was, indeed, a horrid event,ГўВҖВқ said he in a hoarse voice.

ГўВҖВңThe more so, sir, as it was menГўВҖВҷs and not GodГўВҖВҷs doing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell me of those men,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңand remember too,ГўВҖВқ he added in
an almost menacing tone, ГўВҖВңyou have promised to tell me everything. Tell
me, therefore, who are these men who killed the son with despair, and
the father with famine?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTwo men jealous of him, sir; one from love, and the other from
ambition,ГўВҖВ”Fernand and Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow was this jealousy manifested? Speak on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey denounced Edmond as a Bonapartist agent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich of the two denounced him? Which was the real delinquent?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBoth, sir; one with a letter, and the other put it in the post.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd where was this letter written?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt La RГғВ©serve, the day before the betrothal feast.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҷTwas so, thenГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҷtwas so, then,ГўВҖВқ murmured the abbГғВ©. ГўВҖВңOh, Faria, Faria,
how well did you judge men and things!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat did you please to say, sir?ГўВҖВқ asked Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңNothing, nothing,ГўВҖВқ replied the priest; ГўВҖВңgo on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was Danglars who wrote the denunciation with his left hand, that
his writing might not be recognized, and Fernand who put it in the
post.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the abbГғВ© suddenly, ГўВҖВңyou were there yourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, astonished; ГўВҖВңwho told you I was there?ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© saw he had overshot the mark, and he added quickly,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңNo one;
but in order to have known everything so well, you must have been an

ГўВҖВңTrue, true!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse in a choking voice, ГўВҖВңI was there.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd did you not remonstrate against such infamy?ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңif

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, ГўВҖВңthey had made me drink to such an excess
that I nearly lost all perception. I had only an indistinct
understanding of what was passing around me. I said all that a man in
such a state could say; but they both assured me that it was a jest
they were carrying on, and perfectly harmless.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNext dayГўВҖВ”next day, sir, you must have seen plain enough what they had
been doing, yet you said nothing, though you were present when DantГғВЁs
was arrested.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir, I was there, and very anxious to speak; but Danglars
restrained me. ГўВҖВҳIf he should really be guilty,ГўВҖВҷ said he, ГўВҖВҳand did
really put in to the Island of Elba; if he is really charged with a
letter for the Bonapartist committee at Paris, and if they find this
letter upon him, those who have supported him will pass for his
accomplices.ГўВҖВҷ I confess I had my fears, in the state in which politics
then were, and I held my tongue. It was cowardly, I confess, but it was
not criminal.ГўВҖВқ

0341m



ГўВҖВңI understandГўВҖВ”you allowed matters to take their course, that was all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir,ГўВҖВқ answered Caderousse; ГўВҖВңand remorse preys on me night and
day. I often ask pardon of God, I swear to you, because this action,
the only one with which I have seriously to reproach myself in all my
life, is no doubt the cause of my abject condition. I am expiating a
moment of selfishness, and so I always say to La Carconte, when she
complains, ГўВҖВҳHold your tongue, woman; it is the will of God.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ And
Caderousse bowed his head with every sign of real repentance.

ГўВҖВңWell, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңyou have spoken unreservedly; and thus to
accuse yourself is to deserve pardon.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUnfortunately, Edmond is dead, and has not pardoned me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe did not know,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңBut he knows it all now,ГўВҖВқ interrupted Caderousse; ГўВҖВңthey say the dead
know everything.ГўВҖВқ

There was a brief silence; the abbГғВ© rose and paced up and down
pensively, and then resumed his seat.

ГўВҖВңYou have two or three times mentioned a M. Morrel,ГўВҖВқ he said; ГўВҖВңwho was
he?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe owner of the _Pharaon_ and patron of DantГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what part did he play in this sad drama?ГўВҖВқ inquired the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңThe part of an honest man, full of courage and real regard. Twenty
times he interceded for Edmond. When the emperor returned, he wrote,
implored, threatened, and so energetically, that on the second
restoration he was persecuted as a Bonapartist. Ten times, as I told
you, he came to see DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ father, and offered to receive him in his
own house; and the night or two before his death, as I have already
said, he left his purse on the mantelpiece, with which they paid the
old manГўВҖВҷs debts, and buried him decently; and so EdmondГўВҖВҷs father died,
as he had lived, without doing harm to anyone. I have the purse still
by meГўВҖВ”a large one, made of red silk.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd,ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңis M. Morrel still alive?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңIn that case,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңhe should be a man blessed of God,
rich, happy.ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse smiled bitterly. ГўВҖВңYes, happy as myself,ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңWhat! M. Morrel unhappy?ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңHe is reduced almost to the last extremityГўВҖВ”nay, he is almost at the
point of dishonor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ continued Caderousse, ГўВҖВңso it is; after five-and-twenty years of
labor, after having acquired a most honorable name in the trade of
Marseilles, M. Morrel is utterly ruined; he has lost five ships in two
years, has suffered by the bankruptcy of three large houses, and his
only hope now is in that very _Pharaon_ which poor DantГғВЁs commanded,
and which is expected from the Indies with a cargo of cochineal and
indigo. If this ship founders, like the others, he is a ruined man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd has the unfortunate man wife or children?ГўВҖВқ inquired the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңYes, he has a wife, who through everything has behaved like an angel;
he has a daughter, who was about to marry the man she loved, but whose
family now will not allow him to wed the daughter of a ruined man; he
has, besides, a son, a lieutenant in the army; and, as you may suppose,
all this, instead of lessening, only augments his sorrows. If he were
alone in the world he would blow out his brains, and there would be an
end.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHorrible!ГўВҖВқ ejaculated the priest.

ГўВҖВңAnd it is thus heaven recompenses virtue, sir,ГўВҖВқ added Caderousse. ГўВҖВңYou
see, I, who never did a bad action but that I have told you ofГўВҖВ”am in
destitution, with my poor wife dying of fever before my very eyes, and
I unable to do anything in the world for her; I shall die of hunger, as
old DantГғВЁs did, while Fernand and Danglars are rolling in wealth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow is that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause their deeds have brought them good fortune, while honest men
have been reduced to misery.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat has become of Danglars, the instigator, and therefore the most
guilty?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat has become of him? Why, he left Marseilles, and was taken, on the
recommendation of M. Morrel, who did not know his crime, as cashier
into a Spanish bank. During the war with Spain he was employed in the
commissariat of the French army, and made a fortune; then with that
money he speculated in the funds, and trebled or quadrupled his
capital; and, having first married his bankerГўВҖВҷs daughter, who left him
a widower, he has married a second time, a widow, a Madame de Nargonne,
daughter of M. de Servieux, the kingГўВҖВҷs chamberlain, who is in high
favor at court. He is a millionaire, and they have made him a baron,
and now he is the Baron Danglars, with a fine residence in the Rue du
Mont-Blanc, with ten horses in his stables, six footmen in his
antechamber, and I know not how many millions in his strongbox.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh!ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, in a peculiar tone, ГўВҖВңhe is happy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHappy? Who can answer for that? Happiness or unhappiness is the secret
known but to oneГўВҖВҷs self and the wallsГўВҖВ”walls have ears but no tongue;
but if a large fortune produces happiness, Danglars is happy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd Fernand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFernand? Why, much the same story.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut how could a poor Catalan fisher-boy, without education or
resources, make a fortune? I confess this staggers me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd it has staggered everybody. There must have been in his life some
strange secret that no one knows.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, then, by what visible steps has he attained this high fortune or
high position?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBoth, sirГўВҖВ”he has both fortune and positionГўВҖВ”both.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis must be impossible!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt would seem so; but listen, and you will understand. Some days
before the return of the emperor, Fernand was drafted. The Bourbons
left him quietly enough at the Catalans, but Napoleon returned, a
special levy was made, and Fernand was compelled to join. I went too;
but as I was older than Fernand, and had just married my poor wife, I
was only sent to the coast. Fernand was enrolled in the active army,
went to the frontier with his regiment, and was at the battle of Ligny.
The night after that battle he was sentry at the door of a general who
carried on a secret correspondence with the enemy. That same night the
general was to go over to the English. He proposed to Fernand to
accompany him; Fernand agreed to do so, deserted his post, and followed
the general.

ГўВҖВңFernand would have been court-martialed if Napoleon had remained on
the throne, but his action was rewarded by the Bourbons. He returned to
France with the epaulet of sub-lieutenant, and as the protection of the
general, who is in the highest favor, was accorded to him, he was a
captain in 1823, during the Spanish warГўВҖВ”that is to say, at the time
when Danglars made his early speculations. Fernand was a Spaniard, and
being sent to Spain to ascertain the feeling of his fellow-countrymen,
found Danglars there, got on very intimate terms with him, won over the
support of the royalists at the capital and in the provinces, received
promises and made pledges on his own part, guided his regiment by paths
known to himself alone through the mountain gorges which were held by
the royalists, and, in fact, rendered such services in this brief
campaign that, after the taking of Trocadero, he was made colonel, and
received the title of count and the cross of an officer of the Legion
of Honor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDestiny! destiny!ГўВҖВқ murmured the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңYes, but listen: this was not all. The war with Spain being ended,
FernandГўВҖВҷs career was checked by the long peace which seemed likely to
endure throughout Europe. Greece only had risen against Turkey, and had
begun her war of independence; all eyes were turned towards AthensГўВҖВ”it
was the fashion to pity and support the Greeks. The French government,
without protecting them openly, as you know, gave countenance to
volunteer assistance. Fernand sought and obtained leave to go and serve
in Greece, still having his name kept on the army roll.

0345m



Some time after, it was stated that the Comte de Morcerf (this was the
name he bore) had entered the service of Ali Pasha with the rank of
instructor-general. Ali Pasha was killed, as you know, but before he
died he recompensed the services of Fernand by leaving him a
considerable sum, with which he returned to France, when he was
gazetted lieutenant-general.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that nowГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”?ГўВҖВқ inquired the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңSo that now,ГўВҖВқ continued Caderousse, ГўВҖВңhe owns a magnificent houseГўВҖВ”No.
27, Rue du Helder, Paris.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© opened his mouth, hesitated for a moment, then, making an
effort at self-control, he said, ГўВҖВңAnd MercГғВ©dГғВЁsГўВҖВ”they tell me that she
has disappeared?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDisappeared,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңyes, as the sun disappears, to rise
the next day with still more splendor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHas she made a fortune also?ГўВҖВқ inquired the abbГғВ©, with an ironical
smile.

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs is at this moment one of the greatest ladies in Paris,ГўВҖВқ
replied Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңGo on,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңit seems as if I were listening to the story
of a dream. But I have seen things so extraordinary, that what you tell
me seems less astonishing than it otherwise might.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs was at first in the deepest despair at the blow which
deprived her of Edmond. I have told you of her attempts to propitiate
M. de Villefort, her devotion to the elder DantГғВЁs. In the midst of her
despair, a new affliction overtook her. This was the departure of
FernandГўВҖВ”of Fernand, whose crime she did not know, and whom she regarded
as her brother. Fernand went, and MercГғВ©dГғВЁs remained alone.

ГўВҖВңThree months passed and still she weptГўВҖВ”no news of Edmond, no news of
Fernand, no companionship save that of an old man who was dying with
despair. One evening, after a day of accustomed vigil at the angle of
two roads leading to Marseilles from the Catalans, she returned to her
home more depressed than ever. Suddenly she heard a step she knew,
turned anxiously around, the door opened, and Fernand, dressed in the
uniform of a sub-lieutenant, stood before her.

ГўВҖВңIt was not the one she wished for most, but it seemed as if a part of
her past life had returned to her.

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs seized FernandГўВҖВҷs hands with a transport which he took for
love, but which was only joy at being no longer alone in the world, and
seeing at last a friend, after long hours of solitary sorrow. And then,
it must be confessed, Fernand had never been hatedГўВҖВ”he was only not
precisely loved. Another possessed all MercГғВ©dГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ heart; that other was
absent, had disappeared, perhaps was dead. At this last thought
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs burst into a flood of tears, and wrung her hands in agony; but
the thought, which she had always repelled before when it was suggested
to her by another, came now in full force upon her mind; and then, too,
old DantГғВЁs incessantly said to her, ГўВҖВҳOur Edmond is dead; if he were
not, he would return to us.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңThe old man died, as I have told you; had he lived, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs,
perchance, had not become the wife of another, for he would have been
there to reproach her infidelity. Fernand saw this, and when he learned
of the old manГўВҖВҷs death he returned. He was now a lieutenant. At his
first coming he had not said a word of love to MercГғВ©dГғВЁs; at the second
he reminded her that he loved her.

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs begged for six months more in which to await and mourn for
Edmond.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, with a bitter smile, ГўВҖВңthat makes eighteen
months in all. What more could the most devoted lover desire?ГўВҖВқ Then he
murmured the words of the English poet, ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳFrailty, thy name is woman.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSix months afterwards,ГўВҖВқ continued Caderousse, ГўВҖВңthe marriage took place
in the church of Accoules.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe very church in which she was to have married Edmond,ГўВҖВқ murmured the
priest; ГўВҖВңthere was only a change of bridegrooms.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs was married,ГўВҖВқ proceeded Caderousse; ГўВҖВңbut although in
the eyes of the world she appeared calm, she nearly fainted as she
passed La RГғВ©serve, where, eighteen months before, the betrothal had
been celebrated with him whom she might have known she still loved, had
she looked to the bottom of her heart. Fernand, more happy, but not
more at his easeГўВҖВ”for I saw at this time he was in constant dread of
EdmondГўВҖВҷs returnГўВҖВ”Fernand was very anxious to get his wife away, and to
depart himself. There were too many unpleasant possibilities associated
with the Catalans, and eight days after the wedding they left
Marseilles.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you ever see MercГғВ©dГғВЁs again?ГўВҖВқ inquired the priest.

ГўВҖВңYes, during the Spanish war, at Perpignan, where Fernand had left her;
she was attending to the education of her son.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© started. ГўВҖВңHer son?ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, ГўВҖВңlittle Albert.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, then, to be able to instruct her child,ГўВҖВқ continued the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңshe
must have received an education herself. I understood from Edmond that
she was the daughter of a simple fisherman, beautiful but uneducated.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, ГўВҖВңdid he know so little of his lovely
betrothed? MercГғВ©dГғВЁs might have been a queen, sir, if the crown were to
be placed on the heads of the loveliest and most intelligent. FernandГўВҖВҷs
fortune was already waxing great, and she developed with his growing
fortune. She learned drawing, musicГўВҖВ”everything. Besides, I believe,
between ourselves, she did this in order to distract her mind, that she
might forget; and she only filled her head in order to alleviate the
weight on her heart. But now her position in life is assured,ГўВҖВқ
continued Caderousse; ГўВҖВңno doubt fortune and honors have comforted her;
she is rich, a countess, and yetГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse paused.

ГўВҖВңAnd yet what?ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңYet, I am sure, she is not happy,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңWhat makes you believe this?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, when I found myself utterly destitute, I thought my old friends
would, perhaps, assist me. So I went to Danglars, who would not even
receive me. I called on Fernand, who sent me a hundred francs by his
valet-de-chambre.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you did not see either of them?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, but Madame de Morcerf saw me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow was that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs I went away a purse fell at my feetГўВҖВ”it contained five-and-twenty
louis; I raised my head quickly, and saw MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, who at once shut the
blind.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd M. de Villefort?ГўВҖВқ asked the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңOh, he never was a friend of mine, I did not know him, and I had
nothing to ask of him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you not know what became of him, and the share he had in EdmondГўВҖВҷs
misfortunes?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; I only know that some time after EdmondГўВҖВҷs arrest, he married
Mademoiselle de Saint-MГғВ©ran, and soon after left Marseilles; no doubt
he has been as lucky as the rest; no doubt he is as rich as Danglars,
as high in station as Fernand. I only, as you see, have remained poor,
wretched, and forgotten.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are mistaken, my friend,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңGod may seem
sometimes to forget for a time, while his justice reposes, but there
always comes a moment when he remembersГўВҖВ”and beholdГўВҖВ”a proof!ГўВҖВқ

As he spoke, the abbГғВ© took the diamond from his pocket, and giving it
to Caderousse, said, ГўВҖВңHere, my friend, take this diamond, it is yours.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat, for me only?ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse, ГўВҖВңah, sir, do not jest with me!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis diamond was to have been shared among his friends. Edmond had one
friend only, and thus it cannot be divided. Take the diamond, then, and
sell it; it is worth fifty thousand francs, and I repeat my wish that
this sum may suffice to release you from your wretchedness.ГўВҖВқ

0351m



ГўВҖВңOh, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, putting out one hand timidly, and with the
other wiping away the perspiration which bedewed his brow,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңOh, sir, do
not make a jest of the happiness or despair of a man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know what happiness and what despair are, and I never make a jest of
such feelings. Take it, then, but in exchangeГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse, who touched the diamond, withdrew his hand.

The abbГғВ© smiled.

ГўВҖВңIn exchange,ГўВҖВқ he continued, ГўВҖВңgive me the red silk purse that M. Morrel
left on old DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ chimney-piece, and which you tell me is still in
your hands.ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse, more and more astonished, went toward a large oaken
cupboard, opened it, and gave the abbГғВ© a long purse of faded red silk,
round which were two copper runners that had once been gilt. The abbГғВ©
took it, and in return gave Caderousse the diamond.

ГўВҖВңOh, you are a man of God, sir,ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse; ГўВҖВңfor no one knew
that Edmond had given you this diamond, and you might have kept it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ© to himself, ГўВҖВңyou would have done.ГўВҖВқ The abbГғВ©
rose, took his hat and gloves. ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңall you have told me
is perfectly true, then, and I may believe it in every particular.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSee, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied Caderousse, ГўВҖВңin this corner is a crucifix in holy
woodГўВҖВ”here on this shelf is my wifeГўВҖВҷs testament; open this book, and I
will swear upon it with my hand on the crucifix. I will swear to you by
my soulГўВҖВҷs salvation, my faith as a Christian, I have told everything to
you as it occurred, and as the recording angel will tell it to the ear
of God at the day of the last judgment!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҷTis well,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, convinced by his manner and tone that
Caderousse spoke the truth. ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҷTis well, and may this money profit you!
Adieu; I go far from men who thus so bitterly injure each other.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© with difficulty got away from the enthusiastic thanks of
Caderousse, opened the door himself, got out and mounted his horse,
once more saluted the innkeeper, who kept uttering his loud farewells,
and then returned by the road he had travelled in coming.

When Caderousse turned around, he saw behind him La Carconte, paler and
trembling more than ever.

ГўВҖВңIs, then, all that I have heard really true?ГўВҖВқ she inquired.

ГўВҖВңWhat? That he has given the diamond to us only?ГўВҖВқ inquired Caderousse,
half bewildered with joy; ГўВҖВңyes, nothing more true! See, here it is.ГўВҖВқ

The woman gazed at it a moment, and then said, in a gloomy voice,
ГўВҖВңSuppose itГўВҖВҷs false?ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse started and turned pale.

ГўВҖВңFalse!ГўВҖВқ he muttered. ГўВҖВңFalse! Why should that man give me a false
diamond?ГўВҖВқ

0349m



ГўВҖВңTo get your secret without paying for it, you blockhead!ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse remained for a moment aghast under the weight of such an
idea.

ГўВҖВңOh!ГўВҖВқ he said, taking up his hat, which he placed on the red
handkerchief tied round his head, ГўВҖВңwe will soon find out.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn what way?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, the fair is on at Beaucaire, there are always jewellers from
Paris there, and I will show it to them. Look after the house, wife,
and I shall be back in two hours,ГўВҖВқ and Caderousse left the house in
haste, and ran rapidly in the direction opposite to that which the
priest had taken.

ГўВҖВңFifty thousand francs!ГўВҖВқ muttered La Carconte when left alone; ГўВҖВңit is a
large sum of money, but it is not a fortune.ГўВҖВқ

20019m




 Chapter 28. The Prison Register

The day after that in which the scene we have just described had taken
place on the road between Bellegarde and Beaucaire, a man of about
thirty or two-and-thirty, dressed in a bright blue frock coat, nankeen
trousers, and a white waistcoat, having the appearance and accent of an
Englishman, presented himself before the mayor of Marseilles.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI am chief clerk of the house of Thomson & French, of
Rome. We are, and have been these ten years, connected with the house
of Morrel & Son, of Marseilles. We have a hundred thousand francs or
thereabouts loaned on their securities, and we are a little uneasy at
reports that have reached us that the firm is on the brink of ruin. I
have come, therefore, express from Rome, to ask you for information.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ replied the mayor. ГўВҖВңI know very well that during the last four
or five years misfortune has seemed to pursue M. Morrel. He has lost
four or five vessels, and suffered by three or four bankruptcies; but
it is not for me, although I am a creditor myself to the amount of ten
thousand francs, to give any information as to the state of his
finances. Ask of me, as mayor, what is my opinion of M. Morrel, and I
shall say that he is a man honorable to the last degree, and who has up
to this time fulfilled every engagement with scrupulous punctuality.
This is all I can say, sir; if you wish to learn more, address yourself
to M. de Boville, the inspector of prisons, No. 15, Rue de Nouailles;
he has, I believe, two hundred thousand francs in MorrelГўВҖВҷs hands, and
if there be any grounds for apprehension, as this is a greater amount
than mine, you will most probably find him better informed than
myself.ГўВҖВқ

The Englishman seemed to appreciate this extreme delicacy, made his bow
and went away, proceeding with a characteristic British stride towards
the street mentioned.

M. de Boville was in his private room, and the Englishman, on
perceiving him, made a gesture of surprise, which seemed to indicate
that it was not the first time he had been in his presence. As to M. de
Boville, he was in such a state of despair, that it was evident all the
faculties of his mind, absorbed in the thought which occupied him at
the moment, did not allow either his memory or his imagination to stray
to the past.

The Englishman, with the coolness of his nation, addressed him in terms
nearly similar to those with which he had accosted the mayor of
Marseilles.

ГўВҖВңOh, sir,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed M. de Boville, ГўВҖВңyour fears are unfortunately but
too well founded, and you see before you a man in despair. I had two
hundred thousand francs placed in the hands of Morrel & Son; these two
hundred thousand francs were the dowry of my daughter, who was to be
married in a fortnight, and these two hundred thousand francs were
payable, half on the 15th of this month, and the other half on the 15th
of next month. I had informed M. Morrel of my desire to have these
payments punctually, and he has been here within the last half-hour to
tell me that if his ship, the _Pharaon_, did not come into port on the
15th, he would be wholly unable to make this payment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman, ГўВҖВңthis looks very much like a suspension of
payment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt looks more like bankruptcy!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed M. de Boville despairingly.

The Englishman appeared to reflect a moment, and then said, ГўВҖВңFrom which
it would appear, sir, that this credit inspires you with considerable
apprehension?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo tell you the truth, I consider it lost.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, I will buy it of you!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut at a tremendous discount, of course?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, for two hundred thousand francs. Our house,ГўВҖВқ added the Englishman
with a laugh, ГўВҖВңdoes not do things in that way.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you will payГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReady money.ГўВҖВқ

20023m



And the Englishman drew from his pocket a bundle of bank-notes, which
might have been twice the sum M. de Boville feared to lose. A ray of
joy passed across M. de BovilleГўВҖВҷs countenance, yet he made an effort at
self-control, and said:

ГўВҖВңSir, I ought to tell you that, in all probability, you will not
realize six per cent of this sum.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs no affair of mine,ГўВҖВқ replied the Englishman, ГўВҖВңthat is the affair
of the house of Thomson & French, in whose name I act. They have,
perhaps, some motive to serve in hastening the ruin of a rival firm.
But all I know, sir, is, that I am ready to hand you over this sum in
exchange for your assignment of the debt. I only ask a brokerage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf course, that is perfectly just,ГўВҖВқ cried M. de Boville. ГўВҖВңThe
commission is usually one and a half; will you have twoГўВҖВ”threeГўВҖВ”five per
cent, or even more? Whatever you say.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ replied the Englishman, laughing, ГўВҖВңI am like my house, and do
not do such thingsГўВҖВ”no, the commission I ask is quite different.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңName it, sir, I beg.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are the inspector of prisons?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have been so these fourteen years.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou keep the registers of entries and departures?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo these registers there are added notes relative to the prisoners?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere are special reports on every prisoner.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sir, I was educated at Rome by a poor devil of an abbГғВ©, who
disappeared suddenly. I have since learned that he was confined in the
ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf, and I should like to learn some particulars of his
death.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat was his name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe AbbГғВ© Faria.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I recollect him perfectly,ГўВҖВқ cried M. de Boville; ГўВҖВңhe was crazy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo they said.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, he was, decidedly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery possibly; but what sort of madness was it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe pretended to know of an immense treasure, and offered vast sums to
the government if they would liberate him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoor devil!ГўВҖВ”and he is dead?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir, five or six months ago, last February.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have a good memory, sir, to recollect dates so well.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI recollect this, because the poor devilГўВҖВҷs death was accompanied by a
singular incident.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMay I ask what that was?ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman with an expression of
curiosity, which a close observer would have been astonished at
discovering in his phlegmatic countenance.

ГўВҖВңOh dear, yes, sir; the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs dungeon was forty or fifty feet distant
from that of one of BonaparteГўВҖВҷs emissaries,ГўВҖВ”one of those who had
contributed the most to the return of the usurper in 1815, a very
resolute and very dangerous man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed!ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied M. de Boville; ГўВҖВңI myself had occasion to see this man in
1816 or 1817, and we could only go into his dungeon with a file of
soldiers. That man made a deep impression on me; I shall never forget
his countenance!ГўВҖВқ

20025m



The Englishman smiled imperceptibly.

ГўВҖВңAnd you say, sir,ГўВҖВқ he interposed, ГўВҖВңthat the two dungeonsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWere separated by a distance of fifty feet; but it appears that this
Edmond DantГғВЁsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis dangerous manГўВҖВҷs name wasГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEdmond DantГғВЁs. It appears, sir, that this Edmond DantГғВЁs had procured
tools, or made them, for they found a tunnel through which the
prisoners held communication with one another.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis tunnel was dug, no doubt, with an intention of escape?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo doubt; but unfortunately for the prisoners, the AbbГғВ© Faria had an
attack of catalepsy, and died.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat must have cut short the projects of escape.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor the dead man, yes,ГўВҖВқ replied M. de Boville, ГўВҖВңbut not for the
survivor; on the contrary, this DantГғВЁs saw a means of accelerating his
escape. He, no doubt, thought that prisoners who died in the ChГғВўteau
dГўВҖВҷIf were interred in an ordinary burial-ground, and he conveyed the
dead man into his own cell, took his place in the sack in which they
had sewed up the corpse, and awaited the moment of interment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was a bold step, and one that showed some courage,ГўВҖВқ remarked the
Englishman.

ГўВҖВңAs I have already told you, sir, he was a very dangerous man; and,
fortunately, by his own act disembarrassed the government of the fears
it had on his account.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow was that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow? Do you not comprehend?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf has no cemetery, and they simply throw the dead into
the sea, after fastening a thirty-six-pound cannon-ball to their feet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ observed the Englishman as if he were slow of comprehension.

ГўВҖВңWell, they fastened a thirty-six-pound ball to his feet, and threw him
into the sea.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the Englishman.

ГўВҖВңYes, sir,ГўВҖВқ continued the inspector of prisons. ГўВҖВңYou may imagine the
amazement of the fugitive when he found himself flung headlong over the
rocks! I should like to have seen his face at that moment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat would have been difficult.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo matter,ГўВҖВқ replied De Boville, in supreme good-humor at the certainty
of recovering his two hundred thousand francs,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңno matter, I can fancy
it.ГўВҖВқ And he shouted with laughter.

ГўВҖВңSo can I,ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman, and he laughed too; but he laughed as
the English do, ГўВҖВңat the end of his teeth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd so,ГўВҖВқ continued the Englishman who first gained his composure, ГўВҖВңhe
was drowned?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUnquestionably.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that the governor got rid of the dangerous and the crazy prisoner
at the same time?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely.ГўВҖВқ

20027m



ГўВҖВңBut some official document was drawn up as to this affair, I suppose?ГўВҖВқ
inquired the Englishman.

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, the mortuary deposition. You understand, DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ relations,
if he had any, might have some interest in knowing if he were dead or
alive.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that now, if there were anything to inherit from him, they may do
so with easy conscience. He is dead, and no mistake about it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes; and they may have the fact attested whenever they please.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo be it,ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman. ГўВҖВңBut to return to these registers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue, this story has diverted our attention from them. Excuse me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExcuse you for what? For the story? By no means; it really seems to me
very curious.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, indeed. So, sir, you wish to see all relating to the poor abbГғВ©,
who really was gentleness itself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, you will much oblige me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo into my study here, and I will show it to you.ГўВҖВқ

And they both entered M. de BovilleГўВҖВҷs study. Everything was here
arranged in perfect order; each register had its number, each file of
papers its place. The inspector begged the Englishman to seat himself
in an armchair, and placed before him the register and documents
relative to the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf, giving him all the time he desired for
the examination, while De Boville seated himself in a corner, and began
to read his newspaper. The Englishman easily found the entries relative
to the AbbГғВ© Faria; but it seemed that the history which the inspector
had related interested him greatly, for after having perused the first
documents he turned over the leaves until he reached the deposition
respecting Edmond DantГғВЁs. There he found everything arranged in due
order,ГўВҖВ”the accusation, examination, MorrelГўВҖВҷs petition, M. de
VillefortГўВҖВҷs marginal notes. He folded up the accusation quietly, and
put it as quietly in his pocket; read the examination, and saw that the
name of Noirtier was not mentioned in it; perused, too, the application
dated 10th April, 1815, in which Morrel, by the deputy procureurГўВҖВҷs
advice, exaggerated with the best intentions (for Napoleon was then on
the throne) the services DantГғВЁs had rendered to the imperial
causeГўВҖВ”services which VillefortГўВҖВҷs certificates rendered indisputable.
Then he saw through the whole thing. This petition to Napoleon, kept
back by Villefort, had become, under the second restoration, a terrible
weapon against him in the hands of the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney. He was no
longer astonished when he searched on to find in the register this
note, placed in a bracket against his name:

Edmond DantГғВЁs.

An inveterate Bonapartist; took an active part in the return from the
Island of Elba.

To be kept in strict solitary confinement, and to be closely watched
and guarded.

Beneath these lines was written in another hand: ГўВҖВңSee note
aboveГўВҖВ”nothing can be done.ГўВҖВқ

He compared the writing in the bracket with the writing of the
certificate placed beneath MorrelГўВҖВҷs petition, and discovered that the
note in the bracket was the same writing as the certificateГўВҖВ”that is to
say, was in VillefortГўВҖВҷs handwriting.

20029m



As to the note which accompanied this, the Englishman understood that
it might have been added by some inspector who had taken a momentary
interest in DantГғВЁsГўВҖВҷ situation, but who had, from the remarks we have
quoted, found it impossible to give any effect to the interest he had
felt.

As we have said, the inspector, from discretion, and that he might not
disturb the AbbГғВ© FariaГўВҖВҷs pupil in his researches, had seated himself in
a corner, and was reading _Le Drapeau Blanc_. He did not see the
Englishman fold up and place in his pocket the accusation written by
Danglars under the arbor of La RГғВ©serve, and which had the postmark,
ГўВҖВңMarseilles, 27th February, delivery 6 oГўВҖВҷclock, P.M.ГўВҖВқ

But it must be said that if he had seen it, he attached so little
importance to this scrap of paper, and so much importance to his two
hundred thousand francs, that he would not have opposed whatever the
Englishman might do, however irregular it might be.

ГўВҖВңThanks,ГўВҖВқ said the latter, closing the register with a slam, ГўВҖВңI have
all I want; now it is for me to perform my promise. Give me a simple
assignment of your debt; acknowledge therein the receipt of the cash,
and I will hand you over the money.ГўВҖВқ

He rose, gave his seat to M. de Boville, who took it without ceremony,
and quickly drew up the required assignment, while the Englishman
counted out the bank-notes on the other side of the desk.



 Chapter 29. The House of Morrel & Son

Anyone who had quitted Marseilles a few years previously, well
acquainted with the interior of MorrelГўВҖВҷs warehouse, and had returned at
this date, would have found a great change. Instead of that air of
life, of comfort, and of happiness that permeates a flourishing and
prosperous business establishmentГўВҖВ”instead of merry faces at the
windows, busy clerks hurrying to and fro in the long corridorsГўВҖВ”instead
of the court filled with bales of goods, re-echoing with the cries and
the jokes of porters, one would have immediately perceived all aspect
of sadness and gloom. Out of all the numerous clerks that used to fill
the deserted corridor and the empty office, but two remained. One was a
young man of three or four-and-twenty, who was in love with M. MorrelГўВҖВҷs
daughter, and had remained with him in spite of the efforts of his
friends to induce him to withdraw; the other was an old one-eyed
cashier, called ГўВҖВңCocles,ГўВҖВқ or ГўВҖВңCock-eye,ГўВҖВқ a nickname given him by the
young men who used to throng this vast now almost deserted bee-hive,
and which had so completely replaced his real name that he would not,
in all probability, have replied to anyone who addressed him by it.

Cocles remained in M. MorrelГўВҖВҷs service, and a most singular change had
taken place in his position; he had at the same time risen to the rank
of cashier, and sunk to the rank of a servant. He was, however, the
same Cocles, good, patient, devoted, but inflexible on the subject of
arithmetic, the only point on which he would have stood firm against
the world, even against M. Morrel; and strong in the
multiplication-table, which he had at his fingersГўВҖВҷ ends, no matter what
scheme or what trap was laid to catch him.

In the midst of the disasters that befell the house, Cocles was the
only one unmoved. But this did not arise from a want of affection; on
the contrary, from a firm conviction. Like the rats that one by one
forsake the doomed ship even before the vessel weighs anchor, so all
the numerous clerks had by degrees deserted the office and the
warehouse. Cocles had seen them go without thinking of inquiring the
cause of their departure. Everything was as we have said, a question of
arithmetic to Cocles, and during twenty years he had always seen all
payments made with such exactitude, that it seemed as impossible to him
that the house should stop payment, as it would to a miller that the
river that had so long turned his mill should cease to flow.

Nothing had as yet occurred to shake CoclesГўВҖВҷ belief; the last monthГўВҖВҷs
payment had been made with the most scrupulous exactitude; Cocles had
detected an overbalance of fourteen sous in his cash, and the same
evening he had brought them to M. Morrel, who, with a melancholy smile,
threw them into an almost empty drawer, saying:

ГўВҖВңThanks, Cocles; you are the pearl of cashiers.ГўВҖВқ

Cocles went away perfectly happy, for this eulogium of M. Morrel,
himself the pearl of the honest men of Marseilles, flattered him more
than a present of fifty crowns. But since the end of the month M.
Morrel had passed many an anxious hour.

In order to meet the payments then due; he had collected all his
resources, and, fearing lest the report of his distress should get
bruited abroad at Marseilles when he was known to be reduced to such an
extremity, he went to the Beaucaire fair to sell his wifeГўВҖВҷs and
daughterГўВҖВҷs jewels and a portion of his plate. By this means the end of
the month was passed, but his resources were now exhausted. Credit,
owing to the reports afloat, was no longer to be had; and to meet the
one hundred thousand francs due on the 15th of the present month, and
the one hundred thousand francs due on the 15th of the next month to M.
de Boville, M. Morrel had, in reality, no hope but the return of the
_Pharaon_, of whose departure he had learnt from a vessel which had
weighed anchor at the same time, and which had already arrived in
harbor.

But this vessel which, like the _Pharaon_, came from Calcutta, had been
in for a fortnight, while no intelligence had been received of the
_Pharaon_.

20033m



Such was the state of affairs when, the day after his interview with M.
de Boville, the confidential clerk of the house of Thomson & French of
Rome, presented himself at M. MorrelГўВҖВҷs.

Emmanuel received him; this young man was alarmed by the appearance of
every new face, for every new face might be that of a new creditor,
come in anxiety to question the head of the house. The young man,
wishing to spare his employer the pain of this interview, questioned
the new-comer; but the stranger declared that he had nothing to say to
M. Emmanuel, and that his business was with M. Morrel in person.

Emmanuel sighed, and summoned Cocles. Cocles appeared, and the young
man bade him conduct the stranger to M. MorrelГўВҖВҷs apartment. Cocles went
first, and the stranger followed him. On the staircase they met a
beautiful girl of sixteen or seventeen, who looked with anxiety at the
stranger.

ГўВҖВңM. Morrel is in his room, is he not, Mademoiselle Julie?ГўВҖВқ said the
cashier.

ГўВҖВңYes; I think so, at least,ГўВҖВқ said the young girl hesitatingly. ГўВҖВңGo and
see, Cocles, and if my father is there, announce this gentleman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt will be useless to announce me, mademoiselle,ГўВҖВқ returned the
Englishman. ГўВҖВңM. Morrel does not know my name; this worthy gentleman has
only to announce the confidential clerk of the house of Thomson &
French of Rome, with whom your father does business.ГўВҖВқ

The young girl turned pale and continued to descend, while the stranger
and Cocles continued to mount the staircase. She entered the office
where Emmanuel was, while Cocles, by the aid of a key he possessed,
opened a door in the corner of a landing-place on the second staircase,
conducted the stranger into an antechamber, opened a second door, which
he closed behind him, and after having left the clerk of the house of
Thomson & French alone, returned and signed to him that he could enter.

The Englishman entered, and found Morrel seated at a table, turning
over the formidable columns of his ledger, which contained the list of
his liabilities. At the sight of the stranger, M. Morrel closed the
ledger, arose, and offered a seat to the stranger; and when he had seen
him seated, resumed his own chair. Fourteen years had changed the
worthy merchant, who, in his thirty-sixth year at the opening of this
history, was now in his fiftieth; his hair had turned white, time and
sorrow had ploughed deep furrows on his brow, and his look, once so
firm and penetrating, was now irresolute and wandering, as if he feared
being forced to fix his attention on some particular thought or person.

The Englishman looked at him with an air of curiosity, evidently
mingled with interest. ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, whose uneasiness was
increased by this examination, ГўВҖВңyou wish to speak to me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, monsieur; you are aware from whom I come?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe house of Thomson & French; at least, so my cashier tells me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe has told you rightly. The house of Thomson & French had 300,000 or
400,000 francs to pay this month in France; and, knowing your strict
punctuality, have collected all the bills bearing your signature, and
charged me as they became due to present them, and to employ the money
otherwise.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel sighed deeply, and passed his hand over his forehead, which was
covered with perspiration.

ГўВҖВңSo then, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңyou hold bills of mine?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, and for a considerable sum.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is the amount?ГўВҖВқ asked Morrel with a voice he strove to render
firm.

20035m



ГўВҖВңHere is,ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman, taking a quantity of papers from his
pocket, ГўВҖВңan assignment of 200,000 francs to our house by M. de Boville,
the inspector of prisons, to whom they are due. You acknowledge, of
course, that you owe this sum to him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; he placed the money in my hands at four and a half per cent
nearly five years ago.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen are you to pay?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHalf the 15th of this month, half the 15th of next.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJust so; and now here are 32,500 francs payable shortly; they are all
signed by you, and assigned to our house by the holders.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI recognize them,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, whose face was suffused, as he thought
that, for the first time in his life, he would be unable to honor his
own signature. ГўВҖВңIs this all?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, I have for the end of the month these bills which have been
assigned to us by the house of Pascal, and the house of Wild & Turner
of Marseilles, amounting to nearly 55,000 francs; in all, 287,500
francs.ГўВҖВқ

It is impossible to describe what Morrel suffered during this
enumeration. ГўВҖВңTwo hundred and eighty-seven thousand five hundred
francs,ГўВҖВқ repeated he.

ГўВҖВңYes, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the Englishman. ГўВҖВңI will not,ГўВҖВқ continued he, after a
momentГўВҖВҷs silence, ГўВҖВңconceal from you, that while your probity and
exactitude up to this moment are universally acknowledged, yet the
report is current in Marseilles that you are not able to meet your
liabilities.ГўВҖВқ

At this almost brutal speech Morrel turned deathly pale.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңup to this timeГўВҖВ”and it is now more than
four-and-twenty years since I received the direction of this house from
my father, who had himself conducted it for five-and-thirty yearsГўВҖВ”never
has anything bearing the signature of Morrel & Son been dishonored.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know that,ГўВҖВқ replied the Englishman. ГўВҖВңBut as a man of honor should
answer another, tell me fairly, shall you pay these with the same
punctuality?ГўВҖВқ

Morrel shuddered, and looked at the man, who spoke with more assurance
than he had hitherto shown.

ГўВҖВңTo questions frankly put,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңa straightforward answer should
be given. Yes, I shall pay, if, as I hope, my vessel arrives safely;
for its arrival will again procure me the credit which the numerous
accidents, of which I have been the victim, have deprived me; but if
the _Pharaon_ should be lost, and this last resource be goneГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

The poor manГўВҖВҷs eyes filled with tears.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said the other, ГўВҖВңif this last resource fail you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ returned Morrel, ГўВҖВңit is a cruel thing to be forced to say, but,
already used to misfortune, I must habituate myself to shame. I fear I
shall be forced to suspend payment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you no friends who could assist you?ГўВҖВқ

Morrel smiled mournfully.

ГўВҖВңIn business, sir,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңone has no friends, only correspondents.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is true,ГўВҖВқ murmured the Englishman; ГўВҖВңthen you have but one hope.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe last?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe last.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that if this failГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am ruined,ГўВҖВ”completely ruined!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs I was on my way here, a vessel was coming into port.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it, sir; a young man, who still adheres to my fallen fortunes,
passes a part of his time in a belvedere at the top of the house, in
hopes of being the first to announce good news to me; he has informed
me of the arrival of this ship.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd it is not yours?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, she is a Bordeaux vessel, _La Gironde_; she comes from India also;
but she is not mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerhaps she has spoken to the _Pharaon_, and brings you some tidings
of her?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall I tell you plainly one thing, sir? I dread almost as much to
receive any tidings of my vessel as to remain in doubt. Uncertainty is
still hope.ГўВҖВқ Then in a low voice Morrel added,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңThis delay is not
natural. The _Pharaon_ left Calcutta the 5th of February; she ought to
have been here a month ago.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is that?ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman. ГўВҖВңWhat is the meaning of that
noise?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, my God!ГўВҖВқ cried Morrel, turning pale, ГўВҖВңwhat is it?ГўВҖВқ

A loud noise was heard on the stairs of people moving hastily, and
half-stifled sobs. Morrel rose and advanced to the door; but his
strength failed him and he sank into a chair. The two men remained
opposite one another, Morrel trembling in every limb, the stranger
gazing at him with an air of profound pity. The noise had ceased; but
it seemed that Morrel expected somethingГўВҖВ”something had occasioned the
noise, and something must follow. The stranger fancied he heard
footsteps on the stairs; and that the footsteps, which were those of
several persons, stopped at the door. A key was inserted in the lock of
the first door, and the creaking of hinges was audible.

ГўВҖВңThere are only two persons who have the key to that door,ГўВҖВқ murmured
Morrel, ГўВҖВңCocles and Julie.ГўВҖВқ

At this instant the second door opened, and the young girl, her eyes
bathed with tears, appeared. Morrel rose tremblingly, supporting
himself by the arm of the chair. He would have spoken, but his voice
failed him.

ГўВҖВңOh, father!ГўВҖВқ said she, clasping her hands, ГўВҖВңforgive your child for
being the bearer of evil tidings.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel again changed color. Julie threw herself into his arms.

ГўВҖВңOh, father, father!ГўВҖВқ murmured she, ГўВҖВңcourage!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe _Pharaon_ has gone down, then?ГўВҖВқ said Morrel in a hoarse voice. The
young girl did not speak; but she made an affirmative sign with her
head as she lay on her fatherГўВҖВҷs breast.

ГўВҖВңAnd the crew?ГўВҖВқ asked Morrel.

ГўВҖВңSaved,ГўВҖВқ said the girl; ГўВҖВңsaved by the crew of the vessel that has just
entered the harbor.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel raised his two hands to heaven with an expression of resignation
and sublime gratitude.

ГўВҖВңThanks, my God,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңat least thou strikest but me alone.ГўВҖВқ

A tear moistened the eye of the phlegmatic Englishman.

ГўВҖВңCome in, come in,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңfor I presume you are all at the
door.ГўВҖВқ

Scarcely had he uttered those words when Madame Morrel entered weeping
bitterly. Emmanuel followed her, and in the antechamber were visible
the rough faces of seven or eight half-naked sailors. At the sight of
these men the Englishman started and advanced a step; then restrained
himself, and retired into the farthest and most obscure corner of the
apartment. Madame Morrel sat down by her husband and took one of his
