which formed the antechamber, was, on the right, AlbertГўВҖВҷs
breakfast-room, looking into the court, and on the left the salon,
looking into the garden. Shrubs and creeping plants covered the
windows, and hid from the garden and court these two apartments, the
only rooms into which, as they were on the ground floor, the prying
eyes of the curious could penetrate.

On the floor above were similar rooms, with the addition of a third,
formed out of the antechamber; these three rooms were a salon, a
boudoir, and a bedroom. The salon downstairs was only an Algerian
divan, for the use of smokers. The boudoir upstairs communicated with
the bedchamber by an invisible door on the staircase; it was evident
that every precaution had been taken. Above this floor was a large
_atelier_, which had been increased in size by pulling down the
partitionsГўВҖВ”a pandemonium, in which the artist and the dandy strove for
pre-eminence.

There were collected and piled up all AlbertГўВҖВҷs successive caprices,
hunting-horns, bass-viols, flutesГўВҖВ”a whole orchestra, for Albert had had
not a taste but a fancy for music; easels, palettes, brushes,
pencilsГўВҖВ”for music had been succeeded by painting; foils, boxing-gloves,
broadswords, and single-sticksГўВҖВ”for, following the example of the
fashionable young men of the time, Albert de Morcerf cultivated, with
far more perseverance than music and drawing, the three arts that
complete a dandyГўВҖВҷs education, i.e., fencing, boxing, and single-stick;
and it was here that he received Grisier, Cooks, and Charles Leboucher.

The rest of the furniture of this privileged apartment consisted of old
cabinets, filled with Chinese porcelain and Japanese vases, Lucca della
Robbia _faГғВҜences_, and Palissy platters; of old armchairs, in which
perhaps had sat Henry IV. or Sully, Louis XIII. or RichelieuГўВҖВ”for two of
these armchairs, adorned with a carved shield, on which were engraved
the fleur-de-lis of France on an azure field, evidently came from the
Louvre, or, at least, some royal residence.

Over these dark and sombre chairs were thrown splendid stuffs, dyed
beneath PersiaГўВҖВҷs sun, or woven by the fingers of the women of Calcutta
or of Chandernagor. What these stuffs did there, it was impossible to
say; they awaited, while gratifying the eyes, a destination unknown to
their owner himself; in the meantime they filled the place with their
golden and silky reflections.

In the centre of the room was a Roller and Blanchet ГўВҖВңbaby grandГўВҖВқ piano
in rosewood, but holding the potentialities of an orchestra in its
narrow and sonorous cavity, and groaning beneath the weight of the
_chefs-dГўВҖВҷГ…В“uvre_ of Beethoven, Weber, Mozart, Haydn, GrГғВ©try, and
Porpora.

On the walls, over the doors, on the ceiling, were swords, daggers,
Malay creeses, maces, battle-axes; gilded, damasked, and inlaid suits
of armor; dried plants, minerals, and stuffed birds, their
flame-colored wings outspread in motionless flight, and their beaks
forever open. This was AlbertГўВҖВҷs favorite lounging place.

However, the morning of the appointment, the young man had established
himself in the small salon downstairs. There, on a table, surrounded at
some distance by a large and luxurious divan, every species of tobacco
known,ГўВҖВ”from the yellow tobacco of Petersburg to the black of Sinai, and
so on along the scale from Maryland and Porto Rico, to Latakia,ГўВҖВ”was
exposed in pots of crackled earthenware of which the Dutch are so fond;
beside them, in boxes of fragrant wood, were ranged, according to their
size and quality, puros, regalias, havanas, and manillas; and, in an
open cabinet, a collection of German pipes, of chibouques, with their
amber mouth-pieces ornamented with coral, and of narghiles, with their
long tubes of morocco, awaiting the caprice or the sympathy of the
smokers.

Albert had himself presided at the arrangement, or, rather, the
symmetrical derangement, which, after coffee, the guests at a breakfast
of modern days love to contemplate through the vapor that escapes from
their mouths, and ascends in long and fanciful wreaths to the ceiling.

At a quarter to ten, a valet entered; he composed, with a little groom
named John, and who only spoke English, all AlbertГўВҖВҷs establishment,
although the cook of the hotel was always at his service, and on great
occasions the countГўВҖВҷs _chasseur_ also. This valet, whose name was
Germain, and who enjoyed the entire confidence of his young master,
held in one hand a number of papers, and in the other a packet of
letters, which he gave to Albert. Albert glanced carelessly at the
different missives, selected two written in a small and delicate hand,
and enclosed in scented envelopes, opened them and perused their
contents with some attention.

ГўВҖВңHow did these letters come?ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңOne by the post, Madame DanglarsГўВҖВҷ footman left the other.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLet Madame Danglars know that I accept the place she offers me in her
box. Wait; then, during the day, tell Rosa that when I leave the Opera
I will sup with her as she wishes. Take her six bottles of different
wineГўВҖВ”Cyprus, sherry, and Malaga, and a barrel of Ostend oysters; get
them at BorelГўВҖВҷs, and be sure you say they are for me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt what oГўВҖВҷclock, sir, do you breakfast?ГўВҖВқ

20227m



ГўВҖВңWhat time is it now?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA quarter to ten.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well, at half past ten. Debray will, perhaps, be obliged to go to
the ministerГўВҖВ”and besidesГўВҖВқ (Albert looked at his tablets), ГўВҖВңit is the
hour I told the count, 21st May, at half past ten; and though I do not
much rely upon his promise, I wish to be punctual. Is the countess up
yet?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf you wish, I will inquire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, ask her for one of her _liqueur_ cellarets, mine is incomplete;
and tell her I shall have the honor of seeing her about three oГўВҖВҷclock,
and that I request permission to introduce someone to her.ГўВҖВқ

The valet left the room. Albert threw himself on the divan, tore off
the cover of two or three of the papers, looked at the theatre
announcements, made a face seeing they gave an opera, and not a ballet;
hunted vainly amongst the advertisements for a new tooth-powder of
which he had heard, and threw down, one after the other, the three
leading papers of Paris, muttering,

ГўВҖВңThese papers become more and more stupid every day.ГўВҖВқ

A moment after, a carriage stopped before the door, and the servant
announced M. Lucien Debray. A tall young man, with light hair, clear
gray eyes, and thin and compressed lips, dressed in a blue coat with
beautifully carved gold buttons, a white neckcloth, and a tortoiseshell
eye-glass suspended by a silken thread, and which, by an effort of the
superciliary and zygomatic muscles, he fixed in his eye, entered, with
a half-official air, without smiling or speaking.

ГўВҖВңGood-morning, Lucien, good-morning,ГўВҖВқ said Albert; ГўВҖВңyour punctuality
really alarms me. What do I say? punctuality! You, whom I expected
last, you arrive at five minutes to ten, when the time fixed was
half-past! Has the ministry resigned?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, my dear fellow,ГўВҖВқ returned the young man, seating himself on the
divan; ГўВҖВңreassure yourself; we are tottering always, but we never fall,
and I begin to believe that we shall pass into a state of immobility,
and then the affairs of the Peninsula will completely consolidate us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, true; you drive Don Carlos out of Spain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no, my dear fellow, do not confound our plans. We take him to the
other side of the French frontier, and offer him hospitality at
Bourges.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt Bourges?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, he has not much to complain of; Bourges is the capital of Charles
VII. Do you not know that all Paris knew it yesterday, and the day
before it had already transpired on the Bourse, and M. Danglars (I do
not know by what means that man contrives to obtain intelligence as
soon as we do) made a million!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you another order, for I see you have a blue ribbon at your
button-hole.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; they sent me the order of Charles III.,ГўВҖВқ returned Debray
carelessly.

ГўВҖВңCome, do not affect indifference, but confess you were pleased to have
it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, it is very well as a finish to the toilet. It looks very neat on a
black coat buttoned up.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd makes you resemble the Prince of Wales or the Duke of Reichstadt.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is for that reason you see me so early.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause you have the order of Charles III., and you wish to announce
the good news to me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, because I passed the night writing letters,ГўВҖВ”five-and-twenty
despatches. I returned home at daybreak, and strove to sleep; but my
head ached and I got up to have a ride for an hour. At the Bois de
Boulogne, _ennui_ and hunger attacked me at once,ГўВҖВ”two enemies who
rarely accompany each other, and who are yet leagued against me, a sort
of Carlo-republican alliance. I then recollected you gave a breakfast
this morning, and here I am. I am hungry, feed me; I am bored, amuse
me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is my duty as your host,ГўВҖВқ returned Albert, ringing the bell, while
Lucien turned over, with his gold-mounted cane, the papers that lay on
the table. ГўВҖВңGermain, a glass of sherry and a biscuit. In the meantime,
my dear Lucien, here are cigarsГўВҖВ”contraband, of courseГўВҖВ”try them, and
persuade the minister to sell us such instead of poisoning us with
cabbage leaves.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Peste!_ I will do nothing of the kind; the moment they come from
government you would find them execrable. Besides, that does not
concern the home but the financial department. Address yourself to M.
Humann, section of the indirect contributions, corridor A., No. 26.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn my word,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңyou astonish me by the extent of your
knowledge. Take a cigar.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally, my dear Albert,ГўВҖВқ replied Lucien, lighting a manilla at a
rose-colored taper that burnt in a beautifully enamelled standГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңhow
happy you are to have nothing to do. You do not know your own good
fortune!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what would you do, my dear diplomatist,ГўВҖВқ replied Morcerf, with a
slight degree of irony in his voice, ГўВҖВңif you did nothing? What? private
secretary to a minister, plunged at once into European cabals and
Parisian intrigues; having kings, and, better still, queens, to
protect, parties to unite, elections to direct; making more use of your
cabinet with your pen and your telegraph than Napoleon did of his
battle-fields with his sword and his victories; possessing
five-and-twenty thousand francs a year, besides your place; a horse,
for which ChГғВўteau-Renaud offered you four hundred louis, and which you
would not part with; a tailor who never disappoints you; with the
opera, the jockey-club, and other diversions, can you not amuse
yourself? Well, I will amuse you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy introducing to you a new acquaintance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA man or a woman?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know so many men already.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you do not know this man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere does he come fromГўВҖВ”the end of the world?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFarther still, perhaps.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe deuce! I hope he does not bring our breakfast with him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no; our breakfast comes from my fatherГўВҖВҷs kitchen. Are you hungry?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHumiliating as such a confession is, I am. But I dined at M. de
VillefortГўВҖВҷs, and lawyers always give you very bad dinners. You would
think they felt some remorse; did you ever remark that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, depreciate other personsГўВҖВҷ dinners; you ministers give such
splendid ones.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but we do not invite people of fashion. If we were not forced to
entertain a parcel of country boobies because they think and vote with
us, we should never dream of dining at home, I assure you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, take another glass of sherry and another biscuit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWillingly. Your Spanish wine is excellent. You see we were quite right
to pacify that country.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but Don Carlos?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, Don Carlos will drink Bordeaux, and in ten years we will marry
his son to the little queen.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will then obtain the Golden Fleece, if you are still in the
ministry.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think, Albert, you have adopted the system of feeding me on smoke
this morning.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, you must allow it is the best thing for the stomach; but I hear
Beauchamp in the next room; you can dispute together, and that will
pass away the time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAbout what?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAbout the papers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear friend,ГўВҖВқ said Lucien with an air of sovereign contempt, ГўВҖВңdo I
ever read the papers?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you will dispute the more.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. Beauchamp,ГўВҖВқ announced the servant. ГўВҖВңCome in, come in,ГўВҖВқ said Albert,
rising and advancing to meet the young man. ГўВҖВңHere is Debray, who
detests you without reading you, so he says.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is quite right,ГўВҖВқ returned Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңfor I criticise him without
knowing what he does. Good-day, commander!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, you know that already,ГўВҖВқ said the private secretary, smiling and
shaking hands with him.

ГўВҖВң_Pardieu!_ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what do they say of it in the world?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn which world? we have so many worlds in the year of grace 1838.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn the entire political world, of which you are one of the leaders.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey say that it is quite fair, and that sowing so much red, you ought
to reap a little blue.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, come, that is not bad!ГўВҖВқ said Lucien. ГўВҖВңWhy do you not join our
party, my dear Beauchamp? With your talents you would make your fortune
in three or four years.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI only await one thing before following your advice; that is, a
minister who will hold office for six months. My dear Albert, one word,
for I must give poor Lucien a respite. Do we breakfast or dine? I must
go to the Chamber, for our life is not an idle one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou only breakfast; I await two persons, and the instant they arrive
we shall sit down to table.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 40. The Breakfast

And what sort of persons do you expect to breakfast?ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңA gentleman, and a diplomatist.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen we shall have to wait two hours for the gentleman, and three for
the diplomatist. I shall come back to dessert; keep me some
strawberries, coffee, and cigars. I shall take a cutlet on my way to
the Chamber.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not do anything of the sort; for were the gentleman a Montmorency,
and the diplomatist a Metternich, we will breakfast at eleven; in the
meantime, follow DebrayГўВҖВҷs example, and take a glass of sherry and a
biscuit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBe it so; I will stay; I must do something to distract my thoughts.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are like Debray, and yet it seems to me that when the minister is
out of spirits, the opposition ought to be joyous.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, you do not know with what I am threatened. I shall hear this
morning that M. Danglars make a speech at the Chamber of Deputies, and
at his wifeГўВҖВҷs this evening I shall hear the tragedy of a peer of
France. The devil take the constitutional government, and since we had
our choice, as they say, at least, how could we choose that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI understand; you must lay in a stock of hilarity.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not run down M. DanglarsГўВҖВҷ speeches,ГўВҖВқ said Debray; ГўВҖВңhe votes for
you, for he belongs to the opposition.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Pardieu_, that is exactly the worst of all. I am waiting until you
send him to speak at the Luxembourg, to laugh at my ease.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear friend,ГўВҖВқ said Albert to Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңit is plain that the
affairs of Spain are settled, for you are most desperately out of humor
this morning. Recollect that Parisian gossip has spoken of a marriage
between myself and Mlle. EugГғВ©nie Danglars; I cannot in conscience,
therefore, let you run down the speeches of a man who will one day say
to me, ГўВҖВҳVicomte, you know I give my daughter two millions.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, this marriage will never take place,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp. ГўВҖВңThe king
has made him a baron, and can make him a peer, but he cannot make him a
gentleman, and the Count of Morcerf is too aristocratic to consent, for
the paltry sum of two million francs, to a _mГғВ©salliance_. The Viscount
of Morcerf can only wed a marchioness.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut two million francs make a nice little sum,ГўВҖВқ replied Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңIt is the social capital of a theatre on the boulevard, or a railroad
from the Jardin des Plantes to La RГғВўpГғВ©e.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mind what he says, Morcerf,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңdo you marry her. You
marry a money-bag label, it is true; well, but what does that matter?
It is better to have a blazon less and a figure more on it. You have
seven martlets on your arms; give three to your wife, and you will
still have four; that is one more than M. de Guise had, who so nearly
became King of France, and whose cousin was Emperor of Germany.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn my word, I think you are right, Lucien,ГўВҖВқ said Albert absently.

ГўВҖВңTo be sure; besides, every millionaire is as noble as a bastardГўВҖВ”that
is, he can be.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not say that, Debray,ГўВҖВқ returned Beauchamp, laughing, ГўВҖВңfor here is
ChГғВўteau-Renaud, who, to cure you of your mania for paradoxes, will pass
the sword of Renaud de Montauban, his ancestor, through your body.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe will sully it then,ГўВҖВқ returned Lucien; ГўВҖВңfor I am lowГўВҖВ”very low.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, heavens,ГўВҖВқ cried Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңthe minister quotes BГғВ©ranger, what
shall we come to next?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de ChГғВўteau-RenaudГўВҖВ”M. Maximilian Morrel,ГўВҖВқ said the servant,
announcing two fresh guests.

ГўВҖВңNow, then, to breakfast,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңfor, if I remember, you
told me you only expected two persons, Albert.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMorrel,ГўВҖВқ muttered AlbertГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңMorrelГўВҖВ”who is he?ГўВҖВқ

But before he had finished, M. de ChГғВўteau-Renaud, a handsome young man
of thirty, gentleman all over,ГўВҖВ”that is, with the figure of a Guiche and
the wit of a Mortemart,ГўВҖВ”took AlbertГўВҖВҷs hand.

ГўВҖВңMy dear Albert,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңlet me introduce to you M. Maximilian
Morrel, captain of Spahis, my friend; and what is moreГўВҖВ”however the man
speaks for himselfГўВҖВ”my preserver. Salute my hero, viscount.ГўВҖВқ

And he stepped on one side to give place to a young man of refined and
dignified bearing, with large and open brow, piercing eyes, and black
moustache, whom our readers have already seen at Marseilles, under
circumstances sufficiently dramatic not to be forgotten. A rich
uniform, half French, half Oriental, set off his graceful and stalwart
figure, and his broad chest was decorated with the order of the Legion
of Honor. The young officer bowed with easy and elegant politeness.

ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ said Albert with affectionate courtesy, ГўВҖВңthe count of
ChГғВўteau-Renaud knew how much pleasure this introduction would give me;
you are his friend, be ours also.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell said,ГўВҖВқ interrupted ChГғВўteau-Renaud; ГўВҖВңand pray that, if you should
ever be in a similar predicament, he may do as much for you as he did
for me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat has he done?ГўВҖВқ asked Albert.

ГўВҖВңOh, nothing worth speaking of,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel; ГўВҖВңM. de ChГғВўteau-Renaud
exaggerates.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot worth speaking of?ГўВҖВқ cried ChГғВўteau-Renaud; ГўВҖВңlife is not worth
speaking of!ГўВҖВ”that is rather too philosophical, on my word, Morrel. It
is very well for you, who risk your life every day, but for me, who
only did so onceГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe gather from all this, baron, that Captain Morrel saved your life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn what occasion?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңBeauchamp, my good fellow, you know I am starving,ГўВҖВқ said Debray: ГўВҖВңdo
not set him off on some long story.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I do not prevent your sitting down to table,ГўВҖВқ replied Beauchamp,
ГўВҖВңChГғВўteau-Renaud can tell us while we eat our breakfast.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңit is only a quarter past ten, and I expect
someone else.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, true, a diplomatist!ГўВҖВқ observed Debray.

ГўВҖВңDiplomat or not, I donГўВҖВҷt know; I only know that he charged himself on
my account with a mission, which he terminated so entirely to my
satisfaction, that had I been king, I should have instantly created him
knight of all my orders, even had I been able to offer him the Golden
Fleece and the Garter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, since we are not to sit down to table,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңtake a
glass of sherry, and tell us all about it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou all know that I had the fancy of going to Africa.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is a road your ancestors have traced for you,ГўВҖВқ said Albert
gallantly.

ГўВҖВңYes? but I doubt that your object was like theirsГўВҖВ”to rescue the Holy
Sepulchre.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are quite right, Beauchamp,ГўВҖВқ observed the young aristocrat. ГўВҖВңIt
was only to fight as an amateur. I cannot bear duelling ever since two
seconds, whom I had chosen to arrange an affair, forced me to break the
arm of one of my best friends, one whom you all knowГўВҖВ”poor Franz
dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, true,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңyou did fight some time ago; about what?ГўВҖВқ

20235m



ГўВҖВңThe devil take me, if I remember,ГўВҖВқ returned ChГғВўteau-Renaud. ГўВҖВңBut I
recollect perfectly one thing, that, being unwilling to let such
talents as mine sleep, I wished to try upon the Arabs the new pistols
that had been given to me. In consequence I embarked for Oran, and went
from thence to Constantine, where I arrived just in time to witness the
raising of the siege. I retreated with the rest, for eight-and-forty
hours. I endured the rain during the day, and the cold during the night
tolerably well, but the third morning my horse died of cold. Poor
bruteГўВҖВ”accustomed to be covered up and to have a stove in the stable,
the Arabian finds himself unable to bear ten degrees of cold in
Arabia.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs why you want to purchase my English horse,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңyou
think he will bear the cold better.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are mistaken, for I have made a vow never to return to Africa.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou were very much frightened, then?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңWell, yes, and I had good reason to be so,ГўВҖВқ replied ChГғВўteau-Renaud. ГўВҖВңI
was retreating on foot, for my horse was dead. Six Arabs came up, full
gallop, to cut off my head. I shot two with my double-barrelled gun,
and two more with my pistols, but I was then disarmed, and two were
still left; one seized me by the hair (that is why I now wear it so
short, for no one knows what may happen), the other swung a yataghan,
and I already felt the cold steel on my neck, when this gentleman whom
you see here charged them, shot the one who held me by the hair, and
cleft the skull of the other with his sabre. He had assigned himself
the task of saving a manГўВҖВҷs life that day; chance caused that man to be
myself. When I am rich I will order a statue of Chance from Klagmann or
Marochetti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, smiling, ГўВҖВңit was the 5th of September, the
anniversary of the day on which my father was miraculously preserved;
therefore, as far as it lies in my power, I endeavor to celebrate it by
someГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

20237m



ГўВҖВңHeroic action,ГўВҖВқ interrupted ChГғВўteau-Renaud. ГўВҖВңI was chosen. But that is
not allГўВҖВ”after rescuing me from the sword, he rescued me from the cold,
not by sharing his cloak with me, like St. Martin, but by giving me the
whole; then from hunger by sharing with meГўВҖВ”guess what?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA Strasbourg pie?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңNo, his horse; of which we each of us ate a slice with a hearty
appetite. It was very hard.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe horse?ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, laughing.

ГўВҖВңNo, the sacrifice,ГўВҖВқ returned ChГғВўteau-Renaud; ГўВҖВңask Debray if he would
sacrifice his English steed for a stranger?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot for a stranger,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңbut for a friend I might, perhaps.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI divined that you would become mine, count,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel;
ГўВҖВңbesides, as I had the honor to tell you, heroism or not, sacrifice or
not, that day I owed an offering to bad fortune in recompense for the
favors good fortune had on other days granted to us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe history to which M. Morrel alludes,ГўВҖВқ continued ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңis
an admirable one, which he will tell you some day when you are better
acquainted with him; today let us fill our stomachs, and not our
memories. What time do you breakfast, Albert?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt half-past ten.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely?ГўВҖВқ asked Debray, taking out his watch.

ГўВҖВңOh, you will give me five minutesГўВҖВҷ grace,ГўВҖВқ replied Morcerf, ГўВҖВңfor I
also expect a preserver.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf myself,ГўВҖВқ cried Morcerf; ГўВҖВң_parbleu!_ do you think I cannot be saved
as well as anyone else, and that there are only Arabs who cut off
heads? Our breakfast is a philanthropic one, and we shall have at
tableГўВҖВ”at least, I hope soГўВҖВ”two benefactors of humanity.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat shall we do?ГўВҖВқ said Debray; ГўВҖВңwe have only one Monthyon prize.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, it will be given to someone who has done nothing to deserve it,ГўВҖВқ
said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңthat is the way the Academy mostly escapes from the
dilemma.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd where does he come from?ГўВҖВқ asked Debray. ГўВҖВңYou have already answered
the question once, but so vaguely that I venture to put it a second
time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңI do not know; when I invited him three months
ago, he was then at Rome, but since that time who knows where he may
have gone?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you think him capable of being exact?ГўВҖВқ demanded Debray.

ГўВҖВңI think him capable of everything.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, with the five minutesГўВҖВҷ grace, we have only ten left.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will profit by them to tell you something about my guest.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI beg pardon,ГўВҖВқ interrupted Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңare there any materials for an
article in what you are going to tell us?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, and for a most curious one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo on, then, for I see I shall not get to the Chamber this morning,
and I must make up for it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was at Rome during the last Carnival.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe know that,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңYes, but what you do not know is that I was carried off by bandits.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere are no bandits,ГўВҖВқ cried Debray.

ГўВҖВңYes there are, and most hideous, or rather most admirable ones, for I
found them ugly enough to frighten me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, my dear Albert,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңconfess that your cook is
behindhand, that the oysters have not arrived from Ostend or Marennes,
and that, like Madame de Maintenon, you are going to replace the dish
by a story. Say so at once; we are sufficiently well-bred to excuse
you, and to listen to your history, fabulous as it promises to be.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I say to you, fabulous as it may seem, I tell it as a true one
from beginning to end. The brigands had carried me off, and conducted
me to a gloomy spot, called the Catacombs of Saint Sebastian.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud; ГўВҖВңI narrowly escaped catching a fever
there.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I did more than that,ГўВҖВқ replied Morcerf, ГўВҖВңfor I caught one. I was
informed that I was prisoner until I paid the sum of 4,000 Roman
crownsГўВҖВ”about 24,000 francs. Unfortunately, I had not above 1,500. I was
at the end of my journey and of my credit. I wrote to FranzГўВҖВ”and were he
here he would confirm every wordГўВҖВ”I wrote then to Franz that if he did
not come with the four thousand crowns before six, at ten minutes past
I should have gone to join the blessed saints and glorious martyrs in
whose company I had the honor of being; and Signor Luigi Vampa, such
was the name of the chief of these bandits, would have scrupulously
kept his word.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut Franz did come with the four thousand crowns,ГўВҖВқ said
ChГғВўteau-Renaud. ГўВҖВңA man whose name is Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay or Albert de
Morcerf has not much difficulty in procuring them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, he arrived accompanied simply by the guest I am going to present
to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, this gentleman is a Hercules killing Cacus, a Perseus freeing
Andromeda.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, he is a man about my own size.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңArmed to the teeth?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe had not even a knitting-needle.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut he paid your ransom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe said two words to the chief and I was free.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd they apologized to him for having carried you off?ГўВҖВқ said

ГўВҖВңJust so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, he is a second Ariosto.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, his name is the Count of Monte Cristo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere is no Count of Monte CristoГўВҖВқ said Debray.

ГўВҖВңI do not think so,ГўВҖВқ added ChГғВўteau-Renaud, with the air of a man who
knows the whole of the European nobility perfectly.

ГўВҖВңDoes anyone know anything of a Count of Monte Cristo?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe comes possibly from the Holy Land, and one of his ancestors
possessed Calvary, as the Mortemarts did the Dead Sea.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think I can assist your researches,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian. ГўВҖВңMonte Cristo
is a little island I have often heard spoken of by the old sailors my
father employedГўВҖВ”a grain of sand in the centre of the Mediterranean, an
atom in the infinite.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely!ГўВҖВқ cried Albert. ГўВҖВңWell, he of whom I speak is the lord and
master of this grain of sand, of this atom; he has purchased the title
of count somewhere in Tuscany.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is rich, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI believe so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut that ought to be visible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is what deceives you, Debray.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not understand you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you read the _Arabian Nights_?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat a question!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, do you know if the persons you see there are rich or poor, if
their sacks of wheat are not rubies or diamonds? They seem like poor
fishermen, and suddenly they open some mysterious cavern filled with
the wealth of the Indies.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich means?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich means that my Count of Monte Cristo is one of those fishermen.
He has even a name taken from the book, since he calls himself Sinbad
the Sailor, and has a cave filled with gold.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you have seen this cavern, Morcerf?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңNo, but Franz has; for heavenГўВҖВҷs sake, not a word of this before him.
Franz went in with his eyes blindfolded, and was waited on by mutes and
by women to whom Cleopatra was a painted strumpet. Only he is not quite
sure about the women, for they did not come in until after he had taken
hashish, so that what he took for women might have been simply a row of

The two young men looked at Morcerf as if to say,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңAre you mad, or are
you laughing at us?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I also,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel thoughtfully, ГўВҖВңhave heard something like this

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ cried Albert, ГўВҖВңit is very lucky that M. Morrel comes to aid me;
you are vexed, are you not, that he thus gives a clew to the

ГўВҖВңMy dear Albert,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңwhat you tell us is so extraordinary.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, because your ambassadors and your consuls do not tell you of
themГўВҖВ”they have no time. They are too much taken up with interfering in
the affairs of their countrymen who travel.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow you get angry, and attack our poor agents. How will you have them
protect you? The Chamber cuts down their salaries every day, so that
now they have scarcely any. Will you be ambassador, Albert? I will send
you to Constantinople.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, lest on the first demonstration I make in favor of Mehemet Ali,
the Sultan send me the bowstring, and make my secretaries strangle me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou say very true,ГўВҖВқ responded Debray.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңbut this has nothing to do with the existence of
the Count of Monte Cristo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Pardieu!_ everyone exists.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless, but not in the same way; everyone has not black slaves, a
princely retinue, an arsenal of weapons that would do credit to an

Greek mistresses.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you seen the Greek mistress?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have both seen and heard her. I saw her at the theatre, and heard
her one morning when I breakfasted with the count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe eats, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but so little, it can hardly be called eating.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe must be a vampire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLaugh, if you will; the Countess GГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”, who knew Lord Ruthven, declared
that the count was a vampire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, capital,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp. ГўВҖВңFor a man not connected with
newspapers, here is the pendant to the famous sea-serpent of the
_Constitutionnel_.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWild eyes, the iris of which contracts or dilates at pleasure,ГўВҖВқ said
Debray; ГўВҖВңfacial angle strongly developed, magnificent forehead, livid
complexion, black beard, sharp and white teeth, politeness

ГўВҖВңJust so, Lucien,ГўВҖВқ returned Morcerf; ГўВҖВңyou have described him feature
for feature. Yes, keen and cutting politeness. This man has often made
me shudder; and one day when we were viewing an execution, I thought I
should faint, more from hearing the cold and calm manner in which he
spoke of every description of torture, than from the sight of the
executioner and the culprit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid he not conduct you to the ruins of the Colosseum and suck your

ГўВҖВңOr, having delivered you, make you sign a flaming parchment,
surrendering your soul to him as Esau did his birth-right?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRail on, rail on at your ease, gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, somewhat
piqued. ГўВҖВңWhen I look at you Parisians, idlers on the Boulevard de Gand

not of the same race.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am highly flattered,ГўВҖВқ returned Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңAt the same time,ГўВҖВқ added ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңyour Count of Monte Cristo
is a very fine fellow, always excepting his little arrangements with
the Italian banditti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere are no Italian banditti,ГўВҖВқ said Debray.

ГўВҖВңNo vampire,ГўВҖВқ cried Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңNo Count of Monte CristoГўВҖВқ added Debray. ГўВҖВңThere is half-past ten
striking, Albert.ГўВҖВқ

20243m



ГўВҖВңConfess you have dreamed this, and let us sit down to breakfast,ГўВҖВқ
continued Beauchamp.

But the sound of the clock had not died away when Germain announced,
ГўВҖВңHis excellency the Count of Monte Cristo.ГўВҖВқ The involuntary start
everyone gave proved how much MorcerfГўВҖВҷs narrative had impressed them,
and Albert himself could not wholly refrain from manifesting sudden
emotion. He had not heard a carriage stop in the street, or steps in
the antechamber; the door had itself opened noiselessly. The count
appeared, dressed with the greatest simplicity, but the most fastidious
dandy could have found nothing to cavil at in his toilet. Every article
of dressГўВҖВ”hat, coat, gloves, and bootsГўВҖВ”was from the first makers. He
seemed scarcely five-and-thirty. But what struck everybody was his
extreme resemblance to the portrait Debray had drawn. The count
advanced, smiling, into the centre of the room, and approached Albert,
who hastened towards him holding out his hand in a ceremonial manner.

ГўВҖВңPunctuality,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңis the politeness of kings,
according to one of your sovereigns, I think; but it is not the same
with travellers. However, I hope you will excuse the two or three
seconds I am behindhand; five hundred leagues are not to be
accomplished without some trouble, and especially in France, where, it
seems, it is forbidden to beat the postilions.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear count,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert, ГўВҖВңI was announcing your visit to some
of my friends, whom I had invited in consequence of the promise you did
me the honor to make, and whom I now present to you. They are the Count
of ChГғВўteau-Renaud, whose nobility goes back to the twelve peers, and
whose ancestors had a place at the Round Table; M. Lucien Debray,
private secretary to the minister of the interior; M. Beauchamp, an
editor of a paper, and the terror of the French government, but of
whom, in spite of his national celebrity, you perhaps have not heard in
Italy, since his paper is prohibited there; and M. Maximilian Morrel,
captain of Spahis.ГўВҖВқ

At this name the count, who had hitherto saluted everyone with
courtesy, but at the same time with coldness and formality, stepped a
pace forward, and a slight tinge of red colored his pale cheeks.

ГўВҖВңYou wear the uniform of the new French conquerors, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said he;
ГўВҖВңit is a handsome uniform.ГўВҖВқ

No one could have said what caused the countГўВҖВҷs voice to vibrate so
deeply, and what made his eye flash, which was in general so clear,
lustrous, and limpid when he pleased.

ГўВҖВңYou have never seen our Africans, count?ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңNever,ГўВҖВқ replied the count, who was by this time perfectly master of
himself again.

ГўВҖВңWell, beneath this uniform beats one of the bravest and noblest hearts
in the whole army.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, M. de Morcerf,ГўВҖВқ interrupted Morrel.

ГўВҖВңLet me go on, captain. And we have just heard,ГўВҖВқ continued Albert, ГўВҖВңof
a new deed of his, and so heroic a one, that, although I have seen him
today for the first time, I request you to allow me to introduce him as
my friend.ГўВҖВқ

At these words it was still possible to observe in Monte Cristo the
concentrated look, changing color, and slight trembling of the eyelid
that show emotion.

ГўВҖВңAh, you have a noble heart,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңso much the better.ГўВҖВқ

This exclamation, which corresponded to the countГўВҖВҷs own thought rather
than to what Albert was saying, surprised everybody, and especially
Morrel, who looked at Monte Cristo with wonder. But, at the same time,
the intonation was so soft that, however strange the speech might seem,
it was impossible to be offended at it.

20245m



ГўВҖВңWhy should he doubt it?ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp to ChГғВўteau-Renaud.

ГўВҖВңIn reality,ГўВҖВқ replied the latter, who, with his aristocratic glance and
his knowledge of the world, had penetrated at once all that was
penetrable in Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңAlbert has not deceived us, for the count
is a most singular being. What say you, Morrel!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi_, he has an open look about him that pleases me, in spite of
the singular remark he has made about me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңGermain informs me that breakfast is ready.
My dear count, allow me to show you the way.ГўВҖВқ They passed silently into
the breakfast-room, and everyone took his place.

ГўВҖВңGentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said the count, seating himself, ГўВҖВңpermit me to make a
confession which must form my excuse for any improprieties I may
commit. I am a stranger, and a stranger to such a degree, that this is
the first time I have ever been at Paris. The French way of living is
utterly unknown to me, and up to the present time I have followed the
Eastern customs, which are entirely in contrast to the Parisian. I beg
you, therefore, to excuse if you find anything in me too Turkish, too
Italian, or too Arabian. Now, then, let us breakfast.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith what an air he says all this,ГўВҖВқ muttered Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңdecidedly he
is a great man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA great man in his own country,ГўВҖВқ added Debray.

ГўВҖВңA great man in every country, M. Debray,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud.

The count was, it may be remembered, a most temperate guest. Albert
remarked this, expressing his fears lest, at the outset, the Parisian
mode of life should displease the traveller in the most essential
point.

ГўВҖВңMy dear count,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI fear one thing, and that is, that the fare
of the Rue du Helder is not so much to your taste as that of the Piazza
di Spagna. I ought to have consulted you on the point, and have had
some dishes prepared expressly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you know me better,ГўВҖВқ returned the count, smiling, ГўВҖВңyou would not
give one thought of such a thing for a traveller like myself, who has
successively lived on macaroni at Naples, polenta at Milan, olla
podrida at Valencia, pilau at Constantinople, curry in India, and
swallowsГўВҖВҷ nests in China. I eat everywhere, and of everything, only I
eat but little; and today, that you reproach me with my want of
appetite, is my day of appetite, for I have not eaten since yesterday
morning.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat,ГўВҖВқ cried all the guests, ГўВҖВңyou have not eaten for four-and-twenty
hours?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied the count; ГўВҖВңI was forced to go out of my road to obtain
some information near NГғВ®mes, so that I was somewhat late, and therefore
I did not choose to stop.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you ate in your carriage?ГўВҖВқ asked Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңNo, I slept, as I generally do when I am weary without having the
courage to amuse myself, or when I am hungry without feeling inclined
to eat.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you can sleep when you please, monsieur?ГўВҖВқ said Morrel.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have a recipe for it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn infallible one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat would be invaluable to us in Africa, who have not always any food
to eat, and rarely anything to drink.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңbut, unfortunately, a recipe excellent for a
man like myself would be very dangerous applied to an army, which might
not awake when it was needed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMay we inquire what is this recipe?ГўВҖВқ asked Debray.

ГўВҖВңOh, yes,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI make no secret of it. It is a
mixture of excellent opium, which I fetched myself from Canton in order
to have it pure, and the best hashish which grows in the EastГўВҖВ”that is,
between the Tigris and the Euphrates. These two ingredients are mixed
in equal proportions, and formed into pills. Ten minutes after one is
taken, the effect is produced. Ask Baron Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay; I think he
tasted them one day.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Morcerf, ГўВҖВңhe said something about it to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, who, as became a journalist, was very
incredulous, ГўВҖВңyou always carry this drug about you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlways.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWould it be an indiscretion to ask to see those precious pills?ГўВҖВқ
continued Beauchamp, hoping to take him at a disadvantage.

ГўВҖВңNo, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ returned the count; and he drew from his pocket a
marvellous casket, formed out of a single emerald and closed by a
golden lid which unscrewed and gave passage to a small greenish colored
pellet about the size of a pea. This ball had an acrid and penetrating
odor. There were four or five more in the emerald, which would contain
about a dozen. The casket passed around the table, but it was more to
examine the admirable emerald than to see the pills that it passed from
hand to hand.

ГўВҖВңAnd is it your cook who prepares these pills?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңOh, no, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI do not thus betray my
enjoyments to the vulgar. I am a tolerable chemist, and prepare my
pills myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is a magnificent emerald, and the largest I have ever seen,ГўВҖВқ said
ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңalthough my mother has some remarkable family jewels.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI had three similar ones,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңI gave one to the
Sultan, who mounted it in his sabre; another to our holy father the
Pope, who had it set in his tiara, opposite to one nearly as large,
though not so fine, given by the Emperor Napoleon to his predecessor,
Pius VII. I kept the third for myself, and I had it hollowed out, which
reduced its value, but rendered it more commodious for the purpose I
intended.ГўВҖВқ

Everyone looked at Monte Cristo with astonishment; he spoke with so
much simplicity that it was evident he spoke the truth, or that he was
mad. However, the sight of the emerald made them naturally incline to
the former belief.

ГўВҖВңAnd what did these two sovereigns give you in exchange for these
magnificent presents?ГўВҖВқ asked Debray.

ГўВҖВңThe Sultan, the liberty of a woman,ГўВҖВқ replied the Count; ГўВҖВңthe Pope, the
life of a man; so that once in my life I have been as powerful as if
heaven had brought me into the world on the steps of a throne.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd it was Peppino you saved, was it not?ГўВҖВқ cried Morcerf; ГўВҖВңit was for
him that you obtained pardon?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerhaps,ГўВҖВқ returned the count, smiling.

ГўВҖВңMy dear count, you have no idea what pleasure it gives me to hear you
speak thus,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf. ГўВҖВңI had announced you beforehand to my
friends as an enchanter of the _Arabian Nights_, a wizard of the Middle
Ages; but the Parisians are so subtle in paradoxes that they mistake
for caprices of the imagination the most incontestable truths, when
these truths do not form a part of their daily existence. For example,
here is Debray who reads, and Beauchamp who prints, every day, ГўВҖВҳA
member of the Jockey Club has been stopped and robbed on the
Boulevard;ГўВҖВҷ ГўВҖВҳfour persons have been assassinated in the Rue St. DenisГўВҖВҷ
or ГўВҖВҳthe Faubourg St. Germain;ГўВҖВҷ ГўВҖВҳten, fifteen, or twenty thieves, have
been arrested in a _cafГғВ©_ on the Boulevard du Temple, or in the Thermes
de Julien,ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”and yet these same men deny the existence of the bandits in
the Maremma, the Campagna di Romana, or the Pontine Marshes. Tell them
yourself that I was taken by bandits, and that without your generous
intercession I should now have been sleeping in the Catacombs of St.
Sebastian, instead of receiving them in my humble abode in the Rue du
Helder.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңyou promised me never to mention that
circumstance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was not I who made that promise,ГўВҖВқ cried Morcerf; ГўВҖВңit must have been
someone else whom you have rescued in the same manner, and whom you
have forgotten. Pray speak of it, for I shall not only, I trust, relate
the little I do know, but also a great deal I do not know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt seems to me,ГўВҖВқ returned the count, smiling, ГўВҖВңthat you played a
sufficiently important part to know as well as myself what happened.ГўВҖВқ

20249m



ГўВҖВңWell, you promise me, if I tell all I know, to relate, in your turn,
all that I do not know?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is but fair,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңfor three days I believed myself the object of
the attentions of a masque, whom I took for a descendant of Tullia or
PoppГғВҰa, while I was simply the object of the attentions of a
_contadina_, and I say _contadina_ to avoid saying peasant girl. What I
know is, that, like a fool, a greater fool than he of whom I spoke just
now, I mistook for this peasant girl a young bandit of fifteen or
sixteen, with a beardless chin and slim waist, and who, just as I was
about to imprint a chaste salute on his lips, placed a pistol to my
head, and, aided by seven or eight others, led, or rather dragged me,
to the Catacombs of St. Sebastian, where I found a highly educated
brigand chief perusing CГғВҰsarГўВҖВҷs _Commentaries_, and who deigned to leave
off reading to inform me, that unless the next morning, before six
oГўВҖВҷclock, four thousand piastres were paid into his account at his
bankerГўВҖВҷs, at a quarter past six I should have ceased to exist. The
letter is still to be seen, for it is in Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinayГўВҖВҷs possession,
signed by me, and with a postscript of M. Luigi Vampa. This is all I
know, but I know not, count, how you contrived to inspire so much
respect in the bandits of Rome who ordinarily have so little respect
for anything. I assure you, Franz and I were lost in admiration.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNothing more simple,ГўВҖВқ returned the count. ГўВҖВңI had known the famous
Vampa for more than ten years. When he was quite a child, and only a
shepherd, I gave him a few gold pieces for showing me my way, and he,
in order to repay me, gave me a poniard, the hilt of which he had
carved with his own hand, and which you may have seen in my collection
of arms. In after years, whether he had forgotten this interchange of
presents, which ought to have cemented our friendship, or whether he
did not recollect me, he sought to take me, but, on the contrary, it
was I who captured him and a dozen of his band. I might have handed him
over to Roman justice, which is somewhat expeditious, and which would
have been particularly so with him; but I did nothing of the sortГўВҖВ”I
suffered him and his band to depart.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith the condition that they should sin no more,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp,
laughing. ГўВҖВңI see they kept their promise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңupon the simple condition that
they should respect myself and my friends. Perhaps what I am about to
say may seem strange to you, who are socialists, and vaunt humanity and
your duty to your neighbor, but I never seek to protect a society which
does not protect me, and which I will even say, generally occupies
itself about me only to injure me; and thus by giving them a low place
in my esteem, and preserving a neutrality towards them, it is society
and my neighbor who are indebted to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBravo,ГўВҖВқ cried ChГғВўteau-Renaud; ГўВҖВңyou are the first man I ever met
sufficiently courageous to preach egotism. Bravo, count, bravo!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is frank, at least,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel. ГўВҖВңBut I am sure that the count
does not regret having once deviated from the principles he has so
boldly avowed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow have I deviated from those principles, monsieur?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte
Cristo, who could not help looking at Morrel with so much intensity,
that two or three times the young man had been unable to sustain that
clear and piercing glance.

ГўВҖВңWhy, it seems to me,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel, ГўВҖВңthat in delivering M. de
Morcerf, whom you did not know, you did good to your neighbor and to

ГўВҖВңOf which he is the brightest ornament,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, drinking off a

ГўВҖВңMy dear count,ГўВҖВқ cried Morcerf, ГўВҖВңyou are at faultГўВҖВ”you, one of the most
formidable logicians I knowГўВҖВ”and you must see it clearly proved that
instead of being an egotist, you are a philanthropist. Ah, you call
yourself Oriental, a Levantine, Maltese, Indian, Chinese; your family
name is Monte Cristo; Sinbad the Sailor is your baptismal appellation,
and yet the first day you set foot in Paris you instinctively display
the greatest virtue, or rather the chief defect, of us eccentric
Parisians,ГўВҖВ”that is, you assume the vices you have not, and conceal the
virtues you possess.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear vicomte,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI do not see, in all I have
done, anything that merits, either from you or these gentlemen, the
pretended eulogies I have received. You were no stranger to me, for I
knew you from the time I gave up two rooms to you, invited you to
breakfast with me, lent you one of my carriages, witnessed the Carnival
in your company, and saw with you from a window in the Piazza del
Popolo the execution that affected you so much that you nearly fainted.
I will appeal to any of these gentlemen, could I leave my guest in the
hands of a hideous bandit, as you term him? Besides, you know, I had
the idea that you could introduce me into some of the Paris salons when
I came to France. You might some time ago have looked upon this
resolution as a vague project, but today you see it was a reality, and
you must submit to it under penalty of breaking your word.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will keep it,ГўВҖВқ returned Morcerf; ГўВҖВңbut I fear that you will be much
disappointed, accustomed as you are to picturesque events and fantastic
horizons. Amongst us you will not meet with any of those episodes with
which your adventurous existence has so familiarized you; our
Chimborazo is Mortmartre, our Himalaya is Mount ValГғВ©rien, our Great
Desert is the plain of Grenelle, where they are now boring an artesian
well to water the caravans. We have plenty of thieves, though not so
many as is said; but these thieves stand in far more dread of a
policeman than a lord. France is so prosaic, and Paris so civilized a
city, that you will not find in its eighty-five departmentsГўВҖВ”I say
eighty-five, because I do not include CorsicaГўВҖВ”you will not find, then,
in these eighty-five departments a single hill on which there is not a
telegraph, or a grotto in which the commissary of police has not put up
a gaslamp. There is but one service I can render you, and for that I
place myself entirely at your orders, that is, to present, or make my
friends present, you everywhere; besides, you have no need of anyone to
introduce youГўВҖВ”with your name, and your fortune, and your talentГўВҖВқ (Monte

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңthat is a most conjugal reservation; I recollect

ГўВҖВңThe affair is still in projection.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd he who says in ГўВҖВҳprojection,ГўВҖВҷ means already decided,ГўВҖВқ said Debray.

ГўВҖВңEnormous ones,ГўВҖВқ answered Beauchamp. ГўВҖВңAlthough in reality a Liberal, he

ГўВҖВңI do not know him,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңbut I shall probably soon

ГўВҖВңThey are my bankers in the capital of the Christian world,ГўВҖВқ returned

ГўВҖВңTake care,ГўВҖВқ returned Albert; ГўВҖВңno one has been able to accomplish
that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, we have three millions for our police; it is true they are almost
always spent beforehand, but, no matter, we shall still have fifty
thousand francs to spend for this purpose.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd when you know, will you tell me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI promise you. _Au revoir_, Albert. Gentlemen, good morning.ГўВҖВқ

As he left the room, Debray called out loudly, ГўВҖВңMy carriage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBravo,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp to Albert; ГўВҖВңI shall not go to the Chamber, but
I have something better to offer my readers than a speech of M.
Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor heavenГўВҖВҷs sake, Beauchamp,ГўВҖВқ returned Morcerf, ГўВҖВңdo not deprive me of
the merit of introducing him everywhere. Is he not peculiar?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is more than that,ГўВҖВқ replied ChГғВўteau-Renaud; ГўВҖВңhe is one of the most
extraordinary men I ever saw in my life. Are you coming, Morrel?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDirectly I have given my card to the count, who has promised to pay us
a visit at Rue Meslay, No. 14.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBe sure I shall not fail to do so,ГўВҖВқ returned the count, bowing.

And Maximilian Morrel left the room with the Baron de ChГғВўteau-Renaud,
leaving Monte Cristo alone with Morcerf.



 Chapter 41. The Presentation

When Albert found himself alone with Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңMy dear count,ГўВҖВқ
said he, ГўВҖВңallow me to commence my services as _cicerone_ by showing you
a specimen of a bachelorГўВҖВҷs apartment. You, who are accustomed to the
palaces of Italy, can amuse yourself by calculating in how many square
feet a young man who is not the worst lodged in Paris can live. As we
pass from one room to another, I will open the windows to let you
breathe.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo had already seen the breakfast-room and the salon on the
ground floor. Albert led him first to his _atelier_, which was, as we
have said, his favorite apartment. Monte Cristo quickly appreciated all
that Albert had collected hereГўВҖВ”old cabinets, Japanese porcelain,
Oriental stuffs, Venetian glass, arms from all parts of the
worldГўВҖВ”everything was familiar to him; and at the first glance he
recognized their date, their country, and their origin.

Morcerf had expected he should be the guide; on the contrary, it was he
who, under the countГўВҖВҷs guidance, followed a course of archГғВҰology,
mineralogy, and natural history.

They descended to the first floor; Albert led his guest into the salon.
The salon was filled with the works of modern artists; there were
landscapes by DuprГғВ©, with their long reeds and tall trees, their lowing
oxen and marvellous skies; DelacroixГўВҖВҷs Arabian cavaliers, with their
long white burnouses, their shining belts, their damasked arms, their
horses, who tore each other with their teeth while their riders
contended fiercely with their maces; _aquarelles_ of Boulanger,
representing Notre Dame de Paris with that vigor that makes the artist
the rival of the poet; there were paintings by Diaz, who makes his
flowers more beautiful than flowers, his suns more brilliant than the
sun; designs by Decamp, as vividly colored as those of Salvator Rosa,
but more poetic; _pastels_ by Giraud and MГғВјller, representing children
like angels and women with the features of a virgin; sketches torn from
the album of DauzatsГўВҖВҷ ГўВҖВңTravels in the East,ГўВҖВқ that had been made in a
few seconds on the saddle of a camel, or beneath the dome of a
mosqueГўВҖВ”in a word, all that modern art can give in exchange and as
recompense for the art lost and gone with ages long since past.

Albert expected to have something new this time to show to the
traveller, but, to his great surprise, the latter, without seeking for
the signatures, many of which, indeed, were only initials, named
instantly the author of every picture in such a manner that it was easy
to see that each name was not only known to him, but that each style
associated with it had been appreciated and studied by him. From the
salon they passed into the bedchamber; it was a model of taste and
simple elegance. A single portrait, signed by LГғВ©opold Robert, shone in
its carved and gilded frame. This portrait attracted the Count of Monte
CristoГўВҖВҷs attention, for he made three rapid steps in the chamber, and
stopped suddenly before it.

It was the portrait of a young woman of five or six-and-twenty, with a
dark complexion, and light and lustrous eyes, veiled beneath long
lashes. She wore the picturesque costume of the Catalan fisherwomen, a
red and black bodice, and golden pins in her hair. She was looking at
the sea, and her form was outlined on the blue ocean and sky. The light
was so faint in the room that Albert did not perceive the pallor that
spread itself over the countГўВҖВҷs visage, or the nervous heaving of his
chest and shoulders. Silence prevailed for an instant, during which
Monte Cristo gazed intently on the picture.

ГўВҖВңYou have there a most charming mistress, viscount,ГўВҖВқ said the count in
a perfectly calm tone; ГўВҖВңand this costumeГўВҖВ”a ball costume,
doubtlessГўВҖВ”becomes her admirably.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ returned Albert, ГўВҖВңI would never forgive you this
mistake if you had seen another picture beside this. You do not know my
mother; she it is whom you see here. She had her portrait painted thus
six or eight years ago. This costume is a fancy one, it appears, and
the resemblance is so great that I think I still see my mother the same
as she was in 1830. The countess had this portrait painted during the
countГўВҖВҷs absence. She doubtless intended giving him an agreeable
surprise; but, strange to say, this portrait seemed to displease my
father, and the value of the picture, which is, as you see, one of the
best works of LГғВ©opold Robert, could not overcome his dislike to it. It
is true, between ourselves, that M. de Morcerf is one of the most
assiduous peers at the Luxembourg, a general renowned for theory, but a
most mediocre amateur of art. It is different with my mother, who
paints exceedingly well, and who, unwilling to part with so valuable a
picture, gave it to me to put here, where it would be less likely to
displease M. de Morcerf, whose portrait, by Gros, I will also show you.
Excuse my talking of family matters, but as I shall have the honor of
introducing you to the count, I tell you this to prevent you making any
allusions to this picture. The picture seems to have a malign
influence, for my mother rarely comes here without looking at it, and
still more rarely does she look at it without weeping. This
disagreement is the only one that has ever taken place between the
count and countess, who are still as much united, although married more
than twenty years, as on the first day of their wedding.ГўВҖВқ

20261m



Monte Cristo glanced rapidly at Albert, as if to seek a hidden meaning
in his words, but it was evident the young man uttered them in the
simplicity of his heart.

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңthat you have seen all my treasures, allow me to
offer them to you, unworthy as they are. Consider yourself as in your
own house, and to put yourself still more at your ease, pray accompany
me to the apartments of M. de Morcerf, he whom I wrote from Rome an
account of the services you rendered me, and to whom I announced your
promised visit, and I may say that both the count and countess
anxiously desire to thank you in person. You are somewhat _blasГғВ©_ I
know, and family scenes have not much effect on Sinbad the Sailor, who
has seen so many others. However, accept what I propose to you as an
initiation into Parisian lifeГўВҖВ”a life of politeness, visiting, and
introductions.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo bowed without making any answer; he accepted the offer
without enthusiasm and without regret, as one of those conventions of
society which every gentleman looks upon as a duty. Albert summoned his
servant, and ordered him to acquaint M. and Madame de Morcerf of the
arrival of the Count of Monte Cristo. Albert followed him with the
count. When they arrived at the antechamber, above the door was visible
a shield, which, by its rich ornaments and its harmony with the rest of
the furniture, indicated the importance the owner attached to this
blazon. Monte Cristo stopped and examined it attentively.

ГўВҖВңAzure seven merlets, or, placed bender,ГўВҖВқ said he. ГўВҖВңThese are,
doubtless, your family arms? Except the knowledge of blazons, that
enables me to decipher them, I am very ignorant of heraldryГўВҖВ”I, a count
of a fresh creation, fabricated in Tuscany by the aid of a commandery
of St. Stephen, and who would not have taken the trouble had I not been
told that when you travel much it is necessary. Besides, you must have
something on the panels of your carriage, to escape being searched by
the custom-house officers. Excuse my putting such a question to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is not indiscreet,ГўВҖВқ returned Morcerf, with the simplicity of
conviction. ГўВҖВңYou have guessed rightly. These are our arms, that is,
those of my father, but they are, as you see, joined to another shield,
which has gules, a silver tower, which are my motherГўВҖВҷs. By her side I
am Spanish, but the family of Morcerf is French, and, I have heard, one
of the oldest of the south of France.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңthese blazons prove that. Almost all the
armed pilgrims that went to the Holy Land took for their arms either a
cross, in honor of their mission, or birds of passage, in sign of the
long voyage they were about to undertake, and which they hoped to
accomplish on the wings of faith. One of your ancestors had joined the
Crusades, and supposing it to be only that of St. Louis, that makes you
mount to the thirteenth century, which is tolerably ancient.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is possible,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf; ГўВҖВңmy father has in his study a
genealogical tree which will tell you all that, and on which I made
commentaries that would have greatly edified dГўВҖВҷHozier and Jaucourt. At
present I no longer think of it, and yet I must tell you that we are
beginning to occupy ourselves greatly with these things under our
popular government.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, your government would do well to choose from the past
something better than the things that I have noticed on your monuments,
and which have no heraldic meaning whatever. As for you, viscount,ГўВҖВқ
continued Monte Cristo to Morcerf, ГўВҖВңyou are more fortunate than the
government, for your arms are really beautiful, and speak to the
imagination. Yes, you are at once from Provence and Spain; that
explains, if the portrait you showed me be like, the dark hue I so much
admired on the visage of the noble Catalan.ГўВҖВқ

It would have required the penetration of Г…В’dipus or the Sphinx to have
divined the irony the count concealed beneath these words, apparently
uttered with the greatest politeness. Morcerf thanked him with a smile,
and pushed open the door above which were his arms, and which, as we
have said, opened into the salon. In the most conspicuous part of the
salon was another portrait. It was that of a man, from five to
eight-and-thirty, in the uniform of a general officer, wearing the
double epaulet of heavy bullion, that indicates superior rank, the
ribbon of the Legion of Honor around his neck, which showed he was a
commander, and on the right breast, the star of a grand officer of the
order of the Saviour, and on the left that of the grand cross of
Charles III., which proved that the person represented by the picture
had served in the wars of Greece and Spain, or, what was just the same
thing as regarded decorations, had fulfilled some diplomatic mission in
the two countries.

Monte Cristo was engaged in examining this portrait with no less care
than he had bestowed upon the other, when another door opened, and he
found himself opposite to the Count of Morcerf in person.

He was a man of forty to forty-five years, but he seemed at least
fifty, and his black moustache and eyebrows contrasted strangely with
his almost white hair, which was cut short, in the military fashion. He
was dressed in plain clothes, and wore at his button-hole the ribbons
of the different orders to which he belonged.

He entered with a tolerably dignified step, and some little haste.
Monte Cristo saw him advance towards him without making a single step.
It seemed as if his feet were rooted to the ground, and his eyes on the
Count of Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңFather,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңI have the honor of presenting to you
the Count of Monte Cristo, the generous friend whom I had the good
fortune to meet in the critical situation of which I have told you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are most welcome, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said the Count of Morcerf, saluting
Monte Cristo with a smile, ГўВҖВңand monsieur has rendered our house, in
preserving its only heir, a service which insures him our eternal
gratitude.ГўВҖВқ

As he said these words, the count of Morcerf pointed to a chair, while
he seated himself in another opposite the window.

Monte Cristo, in taking the seat Morcerf offered him, placed himself in
such a manner as to remain concealed in the shadow of the large velvet
curtains, and read on the careworn and livid features of the count a
whole history of secret griefs written in each wrinkle time had planted
there.

ГўВҖВңThe countess,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңwas at her toilet when she was informed
of the visit she was about to receive. She will, however, be in the
salon in ten minutes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is a great honor to me,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңto be thus, on the
first day of my arrival in Paris, brought in contact with a man whose
merit equals his reputation, and to whom fortune has for once been
equitable, but has she not still on the plains of Mitidja, or in the
mountains of Atlas, a marshalГўВҖВҷs staff to offer you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ replied Morcerf, reddening slightly, ГўВҖВңI have left the service,
monsieur. Made a peer at the Restoration, I served through the first
campaign under the orders of Marshal Bourmont. I could, therefore,
expect a higher rank, and who knows what might have happened had the
elder branch remained on the throne? But the Revolution of July was, it
seems, sufficiently glorious to allow itself to be ungrateful, and it
was so for all services that did not date from the imperial period. I
tendered my resignation, for when you have gained your epaulets on the
battle-field, you do not know how to manГ…В“uvre on the slippery grounds
of the salons. I have hung up my sword, and cast myself into politics.
I have devoted myself to industry; I study the useful arts. During the
twenty years I served, I often wished to do so, but I had not the
time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThese are the ideas that render your nation superior to any other,ГўВҖВқ
returned Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңA gentleman of high birth, possessor of an
ample fortune, you have consented to gain your promotion as an obscure
soldier, step by stepГўВҖВ”this is uncommon; then become general, peer of
France, commander of the Legion of Honor, you consent to again commence
a second apprenticeship, without any other hope or any other desire
than that of one day becoming useful to your fellow-creatures; this,
indeed, is praiseworthy,ГўВҖВ”nay, more, it is sublime.ГўВҖВқ

20265m



Albert looked on and listened with astonishment; he was not used to see
Monte Cristo give vent to such bursts of enthusiasm.

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ continued the stranger, doubtless to dispel the slight cloud
that covered MorcerfГўВҖВҷs brow, ГўВҖВңwe do not act thus in Italy; we grow
according to our race and our species, and we pursue the same lines,
and often the same uselessness, all our lives.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said the Count of Morcerf, ГўВҖВңfor a man of your merit,
Italy is not a country, and France opens her arms to receive you;
respond to her call. France will not, perhaps, be always ungrateful.
She treats her children ill, but she always welcomes strangers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, father,ГўВҖВқ said Albert with a smile, ГўВҖВңit is evident you do not know
the Count of Monte Cristo; he despises all honors, and contents himself
with those written on his passport.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is the most just remark,ГўВҖВқ replied the stranger, ГўВҖВңI ever heard
made concerning myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have been free to choose your career,ГўВҖВқ observed the Count of
Morcerf, with a sigh; ГўВҖВңand you have chosen the path strewed with
flowers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo with one of those smiles
that a painter could never represent or a physiologist analyze.

ГўВҖВңIf I did not fear to fatigue you,ГўВҖВқ said the general, evidently charmed
with the countГўВҖВҷs manners, ГўВҖВңI would have taken you to the Chamber; there
is a debate very curious to those who are strangers to our modern
senators.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI shall be most grateful, monsieur, if you will, at some future time,
renew your offer, but I have been flattered with the hope of being
introduced to the countess, and I will therefore wait.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, here is my mother,ГўВҖВқ cried the viscount.

Monte Cristo, turned round hastily, and saw Madame de Morcerf at the
entrance of the salon, at the door opposite to that by which her
husband had entered, pale and motionless; when Monte Cristo turned
round, she let fall her arm, which for some unknown reason had been
resting on the gilded door-post. She had been there some moments, and
had heard the last words of the visitor. The latter rose and bowed to
the countess, who inclined herself without speaking.

ГўВҖВңAh! good heavens, madame,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңare you ill, or is it the
heat of the room that affects you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you ill, mother?ГўВҖВқ cried the viscount, springing towards her.

She thanked them both with a smile.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ returned she, ГўВҖВңbut I feel some emotion on seeing, for the first
time, the man without whose intervention we should have been in tears
and desolation. Monsieur,ГўВҖВқ continued the countess, advancing with the
majesty of a queen, ГўВҖВңI owe to you the life of my son, and for this I
bless you. Now, I thank you for the pleasure you give me in thus
affording me the opportunity of thanking you as I have blessed you,
from the bottom of my heart.ГўВҖВқ

The count bowed again, but lower than before; he was even paler than
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңthe count and yourself recompense too generously a
simple action. To save a man, to spare a fatherГўВҖВҷs feelings, or a
motherГўВҖВҷs sensibility, is not to do a good action, but a simple deed of
humanity.ГўВҖВқ

At these words, uttered with the most exquisite sweetness and
politeness, Madame de Morcerf replied:

ГўВҖВңIt is very fortunate for my son, monsieur, that he found such a
friend, and I thank God that things are thus.ГўВҖВқ

And MercГғВ©dГғВЁs raised her fine eyes to heaven with so fervent an
expression of gratitude, that the count fancied he saw tears in them.
M. de Morcerf approached her.

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ said he. ГўВҖВңI have already made my excuses to the count for
quitting him, and I pray you to do so also. The sitting commences at
two; it is now three, and I am to speak.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo, then, and monsieur and I will strive our best to forget your
absence,ГўВҖВқ replied the countess, with the same tone of deep feeling.
ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ continued she, turning to Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңwill you do us the
honor of passing the rest of the day with us?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBelieve me, madame, I feel most grateful for your kindness, but I got
out of my travelling carriage at your door this morning, and I am
ignorant how I am installed in Paris, which I scarcely know; this is
but a trifling inquietude, I know, but one that may be appreciated.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe shall have the pleasure another time,ГўВҖВқ said the countess; ГўВҖВңyou
promise that?ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo inclined himself without answering, but the gesture might
pass for assent.

ГўВҖВңI will not detain you, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ continued the countess; ГўВҖВңI would not
have our gratitude become indiscreet or importunate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear Count,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңI will endeavor to return your
politeness at Rome, and place my coupГғВ© at your disposal until your own
be ready.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA thousand thanks for your kindness, viscount,ГўВҖВқ returned the Count of
Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңbut I suppose that M. Bertuccio has suitably employed the
four hours and a half I have given him, and that I shall find a
carriage of some sort ready at the door.ГўВҖВқ

Albert was used to the countГўВҖВҷs manner of proceeding; he knew that, like
Nero, he was in search of the impossible, and nothing astonished him,
but wishing to judge with his own eyes how far the countГўВҖВҷs orders had
been executed, he accompanied him to the door of the house. Monte
Cristo was not deceived. As soon as he appeared in the Count of
MorcerfГўВҖВҷs antechamber, a footman, the same who at Rome had brought the
countГўВҖВҷs card to the two young men, and announced his visit, sprang into
the vestibule, and when he arrived at the door the illustrious
traveller found his carriage awaiting him. It was a _coupГғВ©_ of KollerГўВҖВҷs
building, and with horses and harness for which Drake had, to the
knowledge of all the lions of Paris, refused on the previous day seven
hundred guineas.

ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ said the count to Albert, ГўВҖВңI do not ask you to accompany me
to my house, as I can only show you a habitation fitted up in a hurry,
and I have, as you know, a reputation to keep up as regards not being
taken by surprise. Give me, therefore, one more day before I invite
you; I shall then be certain not to fail in my hospitality.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf you ask me for a day, count, I know what to anticipate; it will not
be a house I shall see, but a palace. You have decidedly some genius at
your control.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi_, spread that idea,ГўВҖВқ replied the Count of Monte Cristo,
putting his foot on the velvet-lined steps of his splendid carriage,
ГўВҖВңand that will be worth something to me among the ladies.ГўВҖВқ

As he spoke, he sprang into the vehicle, the door was closed, but not
so rapidly that Monte Cristo failed to perceive the almost
imperceptible movement which stirred the curtains of the apartment in
which he had left Madame de Morcerf.

When Albert returned to his mother, he found her in the boudoir
reclining in a large velvet armchair, the whole room so obscure that
only the shining spangle, fastened here and there to the drapery, and
the angles of the gilded frames of the pictures, showed with some
degree of brightness in the gloom. Albert could not see the face of the
countess, as it was covered with a thin veil she had put on her head,
and which fell over her features in misty folds, but it seemed to him
as though her voice had altered. He could distinguish amid the perfumes
of the roses and heliotropes in the flower-stands, the sharp and
fragrant odor of volatile salts, and he noticed in one of the chased
cups on the mantle-piece the countessГўВҖВҷs smelling-bottle, taken from its
shagreen case, and exclaimed in a tone of uneasiness, as he entered:
