ГўВҖВңMy dear mother, have you been ill during my absence?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no, Albert, but you know these roses, tuberoses, and
orange-flowers throw out at first, before one is used to them, such
violent perfumes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, my dear mother,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, putting his hand to the bell,
ГўВҖВңthey must be taken into the antechamber. You are really ill, and just
now were so pale as you came into the roomГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas I pale, Albert?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; a pallor that suits you admirably, mother, but which did not the
less alarm my father and myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid your father speak of it?ГўВҖВқ inquired MercГғВ©dГғВЁs eagerly.

ГўВҖВңNo, madame; but do you not remember that he spoke of the fact to you?ГўВҖВқ

20269m



ГўВҖВңYes, I do remember,ГўВҖВқ replied the countess.

A servant entered, summoned by AlbertГўВҖВҷs ring of the bell.

ГўВҖВңTake these flowers into the anteroom or dressing-room,ГўВҖВқ said the
viscount; ГўВҖВңthey make the countess ill.ГўВҖВқ

The footman obeyed his orders. A long pause ensued, which lasted until
all the flowers were removed.

ГўВҖВңWhat is this name of Monte Cristo?ГўВҖВқ inquired the countess, when the
servant had taken away the last vase of flowers, ГўВҖВңis it a family name,
or the name of the estate, or a simple title?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI believe, mother, it is merely a title. The count purchased an island
in the Tuscan archipelago, and, as he told you today, has founded a
commandery. You know the same thing was done for Saint Stephen of
Florence, Saint George Constantinian of Parma, and even for the Order
of Malta. Except this, he has no pretension to nobility, and calls
himself a chance count, although the general opinion at Rome is that
the count is a man of very high distinction.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHis manners are admirable,ГўВҖВқ said the countess, ГўВҖВңat least, as far as I
could judge in the few minutes he remained here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey are perfect mother, so perfect, that they surpass by far all I
have known in the leading aristocracy of the three proudest nobilities
of EuropeГўВҖВ”the English, the Spanish, and the German.ГўВҖВқ

The countess paused a moment; then, after a slight hesitation, she
resumed.

ГўВҖВңYou have seen, my dear AlbertГўВҖВ”I ask the question as a motherГўВҖВ”you have
seen M. de Monte Cristo in his house, you are quicksighted, have much
knowledge of the world, more tact than is usual at your age, do you
think the count is really what he appears to be?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat does he appear to be?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, you have just said,ГўВҖВ”a man of high distinction.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI told you, my dear mother, he was esteemed such.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what is your own opinion, Albert?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI must tell you that I have not come to any decided opinion respecting
him, but I think him a Maltese.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not ask you of his origin but what he is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh! what he is; that is quite another thing. I have seen so many
remarkable things in him, that if you would have me really say what I
think, I shall reply that I really do look upon him as one of ByronГўВҖВҷs
heroes, whom misery has marked with a fatal brand; some Manfred, some
Lara, some Werner, one of those wrecks, as it were, of some ancient
family, who, disinherited of their patrimony, have achieved one by the
force of their adventurous genius, which has placed them above the laws
of society.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou sayГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI say that Monte Cristo is an island in the midst of the
Mediterranean, without inhabitants or garrison, the resort of smugglers
of all nations, and pirates of every flag. Who knows whether or not
these industrious worthies do not pay to their feudal lord some dues
for his protection?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is possible,ГўВҖВқ said the countess, reflecting.

ГўВҖВңNever mind,ГўВҖВқ continued the young man, ГўВҖВңsmuggler or not, you must
agree, mother dear, as you have seen him, that the Count of Monte
Cristo is a remarkable man, who will have the greatest success in the
salons of Paris. Why, this very morning, in my rooms, he made his
_entrГғВ©e_ amongst us by striking every man of us with amazement, not
even excepting ChГғВўteau-Renaud.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what do you suppose is the countГўВҖВҷs age?ГўВҖВқ inquired MercГғВ©dГғВЁs,
evidently attaching great importance to this question.

ГўВҖВңThirty-five or thirty-six, mother.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo young,ГўВҖВ”it is impossible,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, replying at the same time
to what Albert said as well as to her own private reflection.

ГўВҖВңIt is the truth, however. Three or four times he has said to me, and
certainly without the slightest premeditation, ГўВҖВҳat such a period I was
five years old, at another ten years old, at another twelve,ГўВҖВҷ and I,
induced by curiosity, which kept me alive to these details, have
compared the dates, and never found him inaccurate. The age of this
singular man, who is of no age, is then, I am certain, thirty-five.
Besides, mother, remark how vivid his eye, how raven-black his hair,
and his brow, though so pale, is free from wrinkles,ГўВҖВ”he is not only
vigorous, but also young.ГўВҖВқ

The countess bent her head, as if beneath a heavy wave of bitter
thoughts.

ГўВҖВңAnd has this man displayed a friendship for you, Albert?ГўВҖВқ she asked
with a nervous shudder.

ГўВҖВңI am inclined to think so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAndГўВҖВ”doГўВҖВ”youГўВҖВ”likeГўВҖВ”him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, he pleases me in spite of Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, who tries to convince
me that he is a being returned from the other world.ГўВҖВқ

The countess shuddered.

ГўВҖВңAlbert,ГўВҖВқ she said, in a voice which was altered by emotion, ГўВҖВңI have
always put you on your guard against new acquaintances. Now you are a
man, and are able to give me advice; yet I repeat to you, Albert, be
prudent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, my dear mother, it is necessary, in order to make your advice
turn to account, that I should know beforehand what I have to distrust.
The count never plays, he only drinks pure water tinged with a little
sherry, and is so rich that he cannot, without intending to laugh at
me, try to borrow money. What, then, have I to fear from him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right,ГўВҖВқ said the countess, ГўВҖВңand my fears are weakness,
especially when directed against a man who has saved your life. How did
your father receive him, Albert? It is necessary that we should be more
than complaisant to the count. M. de Morcerf is sometimes occupied, his
business makes him reflective, and he might, without intending itГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNothing could be in better taste than my fatherГўВҖВҷs demeanor, madame,ГўВҖВқ
said Albert; ГўВҖВңnay, more, he seemed greatly flattered at two or three
compliments which the count very skilfully and agreeably paid him with
as much ease as if he had known him these thirty years. Each of these
little tickling arrows must have pleased my father,ГўВҖВқ added Albert with
a laugh. ГўВҖВңAnd thus they parted the best possible friends, and M. de
Morcerf even wished to take him to the Chamber to hear the speakers.ГўВҖВқ

The countess made no reply. She fell into so deep a reverie that her
eyes gradually closed. The young man, standing up before her, gazed
upon her with that filial affection which is so tender and endearing
with children whose mothers are still young and handsome. Then, after
seeing her eyes closed, and hearing her breathe gently, he believed she
had dropped asleep, and left the apartment on tiptoe, closing the door
after him with the utmost precaution.

ГўВҖВңThis devil of a fellow,ГўВҖВқ he muttered, shaking his head; ГўВҖВңI said at the
time he would create a sensation here, and I measure his effect by an
infallible thermometer. My mother has noticed him, and he must
therefore, perforce, be remarkable.ГўВҖВқ

He went down to the stables, not without some slight annoyance, when he
remembered that the Count of Monte Cristo had laid his hands on a
ГўВҖВңturnoutГўВҖВқ which sent his bays down to second place in the opinion of
connoisseurs.

ГўВҖВңMost decidedly,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңmen are not equal, and I must beg my father
to develop this theorem in the Chamber of Peers.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 42. Monsieur Bertuccio

Meanwhile the count had arrived at his house; it had taken him six
minutes to perform the distance, but these six minutes were sufficient
to induce twenty young men who knew the price of the equipage they had
been unable to purchase themselves, to put their horses in a gallop in
order to see the rich foreigner who could afford to give 20,000 francs
apiece for his horses.

The house Ali had chosen, and which was to serve as a town residence to
Monte Cristo, was situated on the right hand as you ascend the
Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es. A thick clump of trees and shrubs rose in the centre,
and masked a portion of the front; around this shrubbery two alleys,
like two arms, extended right and left, and formed a carriage-drive
from the iron gates to a double portico, on every step of which stood a
porcelain vase, filled with flowers. This house, isolated from the
rest, had, besides the main entrance, another in the Rue de Ponthieu.
Even before the coachman had hailed the _concierge_, the massy gates
rolled on their hingesГўВҖВ”they had seen the Count coming, and at Paris, as
everywhere else, he was served with the rapidity of lightning. The
coachman entered and traversed the half-circle without slackening his
speed, and the gates were closed ere the wheels had ceased to sound on
the gravel. The carriage stopped at the left side of the portico, two
men presented themselves at the carriage-window; the one was Ali, who,
smiling with an expression of the most sincere joy, seemed amply repaid
by a mere look from Monte Cristo. The other bowed respectfully, and
offered his arm to assist the count in descending.

ГўВҖВңThanks, M. Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count, springing lightly up the three
steps of the portico; ГўВҖВңand the notary?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is in the small salon, excellency,ГўВҖВқ returned Bertuccio.

ГўВҖВңAnd the cards I ordered to be engraved as soon as you knew the number
of the house?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour excellency, it is done already. I have been myself to the best
engraver of the Palais Royal, who did the plate in my presence. The
first card struck off was taken, according to your orders, to the Baron
Danglars, Rue de la ChaussГғВ©e dГўВҖВҷAntin, No. 7; the others are on the
mantle-piece of your excellencyГўВҖВҷs bedroom.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGood; what oГўВҖВҷclock is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFour oГўВҖВҷclock.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo gave his hat, cane, and gloves to the same French footman
who had called his carriage at the Count of MorcerfГўВҖВҷs, and then he
passed into the small salon, preceded by Bertuccio, who showed him the
way.

ГўВҖВңThese are but indifferent marbles in this antechamber,ГўВҖВқ said Monte
Cristo. ГўВҖВңI trust all this will soon be taken away.ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio bowed. As the steward had said, the notary awaited him in the
small salon. He was a simple-looking lawyerГўВҖВҷs clerk, elevated to the
extraordinary dignity of a provincial scrivener.

ГўВҖВңYou are the notary empowered to sell the country house that I wish to
purchase, monsieur?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңYes, count,ГўВҖВқ returned the notary.

ГўВҖВңIs the deed of sale ready?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you brought it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHere it is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well; and where is this house that I purchase?ГўВҖВқ asked the count
carelessly, addressing himself half to Bertuccio, half to the notary.
The steward made a gesture that signified, ГўВҖВңI do not know.ГўВҖВқ The notary
looked at the count with astonishment.

ГўВҖВңWhat!ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңdoes not the count know where the house he purchases
is situated?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ returned the count.

ГўВҖВңThe count does not know?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow should I know? I have arrived from Cadiz this morning. I have
never before been at Paris, and it is the first time I have ever even
set my foot in France.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, that is different; the house you purchase is at Auteuil.ГўВҖВқ

At these words Bertuccio turned pale.

ГўВҖВңAnd where is Auteuil?ГўВҖВқ asked the count.

ГўВҖВңClose by here, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ replied the notaryГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңa little beyond Passy; a
charming situation, in the heart of the Bois de Boulogne.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo near as that?ГўВҖВқ said the Count; ГўВҖВңbut that is not in the country.
What made you choose a house at the gates of Paris, M. Bertuccio?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI,ГўВҖВқ cried the steward with a strange expression. ГўВҖВңHis excellency did
not charge me to purchase this house. If his excellency will
recollectГўВҖВ”if he will thinkГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, true,ГўВҖВқ observed Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI recollect now. I read the
advertisement in one of the papers, and was tempted by the false title,
ГўВҖВҳa country house.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is not yet too late,ГўВҖВқ cried Bertuccio, eagerly; ГўВҖВңand if your
excellency will intrust me with the commission, I will find you a
better at Enghien, at Fontenay-aux-Roses, or at Bellevue.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo negligently; ГўВҖВңsince I have this, I will
keep it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you are quite right,ГўВҖВқ said the notary, who feared to lose his fee.
ГўВҖВңIt is a charming place, well supplied with spring-water and fine
trees; a comfortable habitation, although abandoned for a long time,
without reckoning the furniture, which, although old, is yet valuable,
now that old things are so much sought after. I suppose the count has
the tastes of the day?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo be sure,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңit is very convenient, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is moreГўВҖВ”it is magnificent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Peste!_ let us not lose such an opportunity,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo.
ГўВҖВңThe deed, if you please, Mr. Notary.ГўВҖВқ

And he signed it rapidly, after having first run his eye over that part
of the deed in which were specified the situation of the house and the
names of the proprietors.

ГўВҖВңBertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңgive fifty-five thousand francs to monsieur.ГўВҖВқ

The steward left the room with a faltering step, and returned with a
bundle of bank-notes, which the notary counted like a man who never
gives a receipt for money until after he is sure it is all there.

ГўВҖВңAnd now,ГўВҖВқ demanded the count, ГўВҖВңare all the forms complied with?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you the keys?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey are in the hands of the concierge, who takes care of the house,
but here is the order I have given him to install the count in his new
possessions.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well;ГўВҖВқ and Monte Cristo made a sign with his hand to the notary,
which said, ГўВҖВңI have no further need of you; you may go.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ observed the honest notary, ГўВҖВңthe count is, I think, mistaken; it
is only fifty thousand francs, everything included.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd your fee?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs included in this sum.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut have you not come from Auteuil here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, certainly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, it is but fair that you should be paid for your loss of
time and trouble,ГўВҖВқ said the count; and he made a gesture of polite
dismissal.

The notary left the room backwards, and bowing down to the ground; it
was the first time he had ever met a similar client.

ГўВҖВңSee this gentleman out,ГўВҖВқ said the count to Bertuccio. And the steward
followed the notary out of the room.

Scarcely was the count alone, when he drew from his pocket a book
closed with a lock, and opened it with a key which he wore round his
neck, and which never left him. After having sought for a few minutes,
he stopped at a leaf which had several notes, and compared them with
the deed of sale, which lay on the table, and recalling his
_souvenirs_ГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳAuteuil, Rue de la Fontaine, No. 28;ГўВҖВҷ it is indeed the same,ГўВҖВқ said
he; ГўВҖВңand now, am I to rely upon an avowal extorted by religious or
physical terror? However, in an hour I shall know all. Bertuccio!ГўВҖВқ
cried he, striking a light hammer with a pliant handle on a small gong.
ГўВҖВңBertuccio!ГўВҖВқ

The steward appeared at the door.

ГўВҖВңMonsieur Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңdid you never tell me that you
had travelled in France?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn some parts of FranceГўВҖВ”yes, excellency.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou know the environs of Paris, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, excellency, no,ГўВҖВқ returned the steward, with a sort of nervous
trembling, which Monte Cristo, a connoisseur in all emotions, rightly
attributed to great disquietude.

ГўВҖВңIt is unfortunate,ГўВҖВқ returned he, ГўВҖВңthat you have never visited the
environs, for I wish to see my new property this evening, and had you
gone with me, you could have given me some useful information.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo Auteuil!ГўВҖВқ cried Bertuccio, whose copper complexion became lividГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңI
go to Auteuil?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, what is there surprising in that? When I live at Auteuil, you
must come there, as you belong to my service.ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio hung down his head before the imperious look of his master,
and remained motionless, without making any answer.

ГўВҖВңWhy, what has happened to you?ГўВҖВ”are you going to make me ring a second
time for the carriage?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo, in the same tone that Louis
XIV. pronounced the famous, ГўВҖВңI have been almost obliged to wait.ГўВҖВқ
Bertuccio made but one bound to the antechamber, and cried in a hoarse
voice:

ГўВҖВңHis excellencyГўВҖВҷs horses!ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo wrote two or three notes, and, as he sealed the last, the
steward appeared.

ГўВҖВңYour excellencyГўВҖВҷs carriage is at the door,ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңWell, take your hat and gloves,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңAm I to accompany you, your excellency?ГўВҖВқ cried Bertuccio.

ГўВҖВңCertainly, you must give the orders, for I intend residing at the
house.ГўВҖВқ

20277m



It was unexampled for a servant of the countГўВҖВҷs to dare to dispute an
order of his, so the steward, without saying a word, followed his
master, who got into the carriage, and signed to him to follow, which
he did, taking his place respectfully on the front seat.



 Chapter 43. The House at Auteuil

Monte Cristo noticed, as they descended the staircase, that Bertuccio
signed himself in the Corsican manner; that is, had formed the sign of
the cross in the air with his thumb, and as he seated himself in the
carriage, muttered a short prayer. Anyone but a man of exhaustless
thirst for knowledge would have had pity on seeing the stewardГўВҖВҷs
extraordinary repugnance for the countГўВҖВҷs projected drive without the
walls; but the count was too curious to let Bertuccio off from this
little journey. In twenty minutes they were at Auteuil; the stewardГўВҖВҷs
emotion had continued to augment as they entered the village.
Bertuccio, crouched in the corner of the carriage, began to examine
with a feverish anxiety every house they passed.

ГўВҖВңTell them to stop at Rue de la Fontaine, No. 28,ГўВҖВқ said the count,
fixing his eyes on the steward, to whom he gave this order.

BertuccioГўВҖВҷs forehead was covered with perspiration; however, he obeyed,
and, leaning out of the window, he cried to the coachman,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңRue de la
Fontaine, No. 28.ГўВҖВқ No. 28 was situated at the extremity of the village;
during the drive night had set in, and darkness gave the surroundings
the artificial appearance of a scene on the stage. The carriage
stopped, the footman sprang off the box and opened the door.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңyou do not get out, M. BertuccioГўВҖВ”you are going
to stay in the carriage, then? What are you thinking of this evening?ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio sprang out, and offered his shoulder to the count, who, this
time, leaned upon it as he descended the three steps of the carriage.

ГўВҖВңKnock,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңand announce me.ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio knocked, the door opened, and the concierge appeared.

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ asked he.

ГўВҖВңIt is your new master, my good fellow,ГўВҖВқ said the footman. And he held
out to the concierge the notaryГўВҖВҷs order.

ГўВҖВңThe house is sold, then?ГўВҖВқ demanded the concierge; ГўВҖВңand this gentleman
is coming to live here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, my friend,ГўВҖВқ returned the count; ГўВҖВңand I will endeavor to give you
no cause to regret your old master.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said the concierge, ГўВҖВңI shall not have much cause to
regret him, for he came here but seldom; it is five years since he was
here last, and he did well to sell the house, for it did not bring him
in anything at all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat was the name of your old master?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңThe Marquis of Saint-MГғВ©ran. Ah, I am sure he has not sold the house
for what he gave for it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe Marquis of Saint-MГғВ©ran!ГўВҖВқ returned the count. ГўВҖВңThe name is not
unknown to me; the Marquis of Saint-MГғВ©ran!ГўВҖВқ and he appeared to
meditate.

ГўВҖВңAn old gentleman,ГўВҖВқ continued the concierge, ГўВҖВңa staunch follower of the
Bourbons; he had an only daughter, who married M. de Villefort, who had
been the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney at NГғВ®mes, and afterwards at Versailles.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo glanced at Bertuccio, who became whiter than the wall
against which he leaned to prevent himself from falling.

ГўВҖВңAnd is not this daughter dead?ГўВҖВқ demanded Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI fancy I have
heard so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, monsieur, one-and-twenty years ago; and since then we have not
seen the poor marquis three times.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThanks, thanks,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, judging from the stewardГўВҖВҷs utter
prostration that he could not stretch the cord further without danger
of breaking it. ГўВҖВңGive me a light.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall I accompany you, monsieur?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, it is unnecessary; Bertuccio will show me a light.ГўВҖВқ

And Monte Cristo accompanied these words by the gift of two gold
pieces, which produced a torrent of thanks and blessings from the
concierge.

ГўВҖВңAh, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said he, after having vainly searched on the
mantle-piece and the shelves, ГўВҖВңI have not got any candles.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTake one of the carriage-lamps, Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңand show
me the apartments.ГўВҖВқ

The steward obeyed in silence, but it was easy to see, from the manner
in which the hand that held the light trembled, how much it cost him to
obey. They went over a tolerably large ground floor; a first floor
consisted of a salon, a bathroom, and two bedrooms; near one of the
bedrooms they came to a winding staircase that led down to the garden.

ГўВҖВңAh, here is a private staircase,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңthat is convenient.
Light me, M. Bertuccio, and go first; we will see where it leads to.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ replied Bertuccio, ГўВҖВңit leads to the garden.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd, pray, how do you know that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt ought to do so, at least.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, let us be sure of that.ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio sighed, and went on first; the stairs did, indeed, lead to
the garden. At the outer door the steward paused.

ГўВҖВңGo on, Monsieur Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

But he who was addressed stood there, stupefied, bewildered, stunned;
his haggard eyes glanced around, as if in search of the traces of some
terrible event, and with his clenched hands he seemed striving to shut
out horrible recollections.

ГўВҖВңWell!ГўВҖВқ insisted the Count.

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ cried Bertuccio, setting down the lantern at the angle of the
interior wall. ГўВҖВңNo, monsieur, it is impossible; I can go no farther.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat does this mean?ГўВҖВқ demanded the irresistible voice of Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңWhy, you must see, your excellency,ГўВҖВқ cried the steward, ГўВҖВңthat this is
not natural; that, having a house to purchase, you purchase it exactly
at Auteuil, and that, purchasing it at Auteuil, this house should be
No. 28, Rue de la Fontaine. Oh, why did I not tell you all? I am sure
you would not have forced me to come. I hoped your house would have
been some other one than this; as if there was not another house at
Auteuil than that of the assassination!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat, what!ГўВҖВқ cried Monte Cristo, stopping suddenly, ГўВҖВңwhat words do you
utter? Devil of a man, Corsican that you areГўВҖВ”always mysteries or
superstitions. Come, take the lantern, and let us visit the garden; you
are not afraid of ghosts with me, I hope?ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio raised the lantern, and obeyed. The door, as it opened,
disclosed a gloomy sky, in which the moon strove vainly to struggle
through a sea of clouds that covered her with billows of vapor which
she illumined for an instant, only to sink into obscurity. The steward
wished to turn to the left.

ГўВҖВңNo, no, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңWhat is the use of following
the alleys? Here is a beautiful lawn; let us go on straight forwards.ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio wiped the perspiration from his brow, but obeyed; however, he
continued to take the left hand. Monte Cristo, on the contrary, took
the right hand; arrived near a clump of trees, he stopped. The steward
could not restrain himself.

ГўВҖВңMove, monsieurГўВҖВ”move away, I entreat you; you are exactly in the spot!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat spot?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere he fell.ГўВҖВқ

20281m



ГўВҖВңMy dear Monsieur Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, laughing, ГўВҖВңcontrol
yourself; we are not at SartГғВЁne or at Corte. This is not a Corsican
_maquis_ but an English garden; badly kept, I own, but still you must
not calumniate it for that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonsieur, I implore you do not stay there!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think you are going mad, Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count coldly. ГўВҖВңIf that
is the case, I warn you, I shall have you put in a lunatic asylum.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas! excellency,ГўВҖВқ returned Bertuccio, joining his hands, and shaking
his head in a manner that would have excited the countГўВҖВҷs laughter, had
not thoughts of a superior interest occupied him, and rendered him
attentive to the least revelation of this timorous conscience. ГўВҖВңAlas!
excellency, the evil has arrived!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңI am very glad to tell you, that while
you gesticulate, you wring your hands and roll your eyes like a man
possessed by a devil who will not leave him; and I have always
observed, that the devil most obstinate to be expelled is a secret. I
knew you were a Corsican. I knew you were gloomy, and always brooding
over some old history of the vendetta; and I overlooked that in Italy,
because in Italy those things are thought nothing of. But in France
they are considered in very bad taste; there are gendarmes who occupy
themselves with such affairs, judges who condemn, and scaffolds which
avenge.ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio clasped his hands, and as, in all these evolutions, he did
not let fall the lantern, the light showed his pale and altered
countenance. Monte Cristo examined him with the same look that, at
Rome, he had bent upon the execution of Andrea, and then, in a tone
that made a shudder pass through the veins of the poor stewardГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңThe AbbГғВ© Busoni, then told me an untruth,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңwhen, after his
journey in France, in 1829, he sent you to me, with a letter of
recommendation, in which he enumerated all your valuable qualities.
Well, I shall write to the abbГғВ©; I shall hold him responsible for his
_protГғВ©gГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs_ misconduct, and I shall soon know all about this
assassination. Only I warn you, that when I reside in a country, I
conform to all its code, and I have no wish to put myself within the
compass of the French laws for your sake.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, do not do that, excellency; I have always served you faithfully,ГўВҖВқ
cried Bertuccio, in despair. ГўВҖВңI have always been an honest man, and, as
far as lay in my power, I have done good.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not deny it,ГўВҖВқ returned the count; ГўВҖВңbut why are you thus agitated.
It is a bad sign; a quiet conscience does not occasion such paleness in
the cheeks, and such fever in the hands of a man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, your excellency,ГўВҖВқ replied Bertuccio hesitatingly, ГўВҖВңdid not the
AbbГғВ© Busoni, who heard my confession in the prison at NГғВ®mes, tell you
that I had a heavy burden upon my conscience?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but as he said you would make an excellent steward, I concluded
you had stolenГўВҖВ”that was all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, your excellency!ГўВҖВқ returned Bertuccio in deep contempt.

ГўВҖВңOr, as you are a Corsican, that you had been unable to resist the
desire of making a ГўВҖВҳstiff,ГўВҖВҷ as you call it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, my good master,ГўВҖВқ cried Bertuccio, casting himself at the countГўВҖВҷs
feet, ГўВҖВңit was simply vengeanceГўВҖВ”nothing else.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI understand that, but I do not understand what it is that galvanizes
you in this manner.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, monsieur, it is very natural,ГўВҖВқ returned Bertuccio, ГўВҖВңsince it was
in this house that my vengeance was accomplished.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat! my house?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, your excellency, it was not yours, then.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhose, then? The Marquis de Saint-MГғВ©ran, I think, the concierge said.
What had you to revenge on the Marquis de Saint-MГғВ©ran?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, it was not on him, monsieur; it was on another.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is strange,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo, seeming to yield to his
reflections, ГўВҖВңthat you should find yourself without any preparation in
a house where the event happened that causes you so much remorse.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonsieur,ГўВҖВқ said the steward, ГўВҖВңit is fatality, I am sure. First, you
purchase a house at AuteuilГўВҖВ”this house is the one where I have
committed an assassination; you descend to the garden by the same
staircase by which he descended; you stop at the spot where he received
the blow; and two paces farther is the grave in which he had just
buried his child. This is not chance, for chance, in this case, is too
much like Providence.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, amiable Corsican, let us suppose it is Providence. I always
suppose anything people please, and, besides, you must concede
something to diseased minds. Come, collect yourself, and tell me all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have related it but once, and that was to the AbbГғВ© Busoni. Such
things,ГўВҖВқ continued Bertuccio, shaking his head, ГўВҖВңare only related under
the seal of confession.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңI refer you to your confessor. Turn Chartreux
or Trappist, and relate your secrets, but, as for me, I do not like
anyone who is alarmed by such phantasms, and I do not choose that my
servants should be afraid to walk in the garden of an evening. I
confess I am not very desirous of a visit from the commissary of
police, for, in Italy, justice is only paid when silentГўВҖВ”in France she
is paid only when she speaks. _Peste!_ I thought you somewhat Corsican,
a great deal smuggler, and an excellent steward; but I see you have
other strings to your bow. You are no longer in my service, Monsieur
Bertuccio.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, your excellency, your excellency!ГўВҖВқ cried the steward, struck with
terror at this threat, ГўВҖВңif that is the only reason I cannot remain in
your service, I will tell all, for if I quit you, it will only be to go
to the scaffold.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is different,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңbut if you intend to tell
an untruth, reflect it were better not to speak at all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, monsieur, I swear to you, by my hopes of salvation, I will tell
you all, for the AbbГғВ© Busoni himself only knew a part of my secret;
but, I pray you, go away from that plane-tree. The moon is just
bursting through the clouds, and there, standing where you do, and
wrapped in that cloak that conceals your figure, you remind me of M. de
Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat!ГўВҖВқ cried Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңit was M. de Villefort?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour excellency knows him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe former royal attorney at NГғВ®mes?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho married the Marquis of Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs daughter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho enjoyed the reputation of being the most severe, the most upright,
the most rigid magistrate on the bench?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said Bertuccio, ГўВҖВңthis man with this spotless
reputationГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas a villain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBah,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңimpossible!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is as I tell you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, really,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңHave you proof of this?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI had it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you have lost it; how stupid!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but by careful search it might be recovered.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally,ГўВҖВқ returned the count, ГўВҖВңrelate it to me, for it begins to
interest me.ГўВҖВқ

And the count, humming an air from _Lucia_, went to sit down on a
bench, while Bertuccio followed him, collecting his thoughts. Bertuccio
remained standing before him.

20285m




 Chapter 44. The Vendetta

At what point shall I begin my story, your excellency?ГўВҖВқ asked
Bertuccio.

ГўВҖВңWhere you please,ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңsince I know nothing at all
of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI thought the AbbГғВ© Busoni had told your excellency.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSome particulars, doubtless, but that is seven or eight years ago, and
I have forgotten them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I can speak without fear of tiring your excellency.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo on, M. Bertuccio; you will supply the want of the evening papers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe story begins in 1815.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВң1815 is not yesterday.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, monsieur, and yet I recollect all things as clearly as if they had
happened but then. I had a brother, an elder brother, who was in the
service of the emperor; he had become lieutenant in a regiment composed
entirely of Corsicans. This brother was my only friend; we became
orphansГўВҖВ”I at five, he at eighteen. He brought me up as if I had been
his son, and in 1814 he married. When the emperor returned from the
Island of Elba, my brother instantly joined the army, was slightly
wounded at Waterloo, and retired with the army beyond the Loire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut that is the history of the Hundred Days, M. Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the
count; ГўВҖВңunless I am mistaken, it has been already written.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExcuse me, excellency, but these details are necessary, and you
promised to be patient.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo on; I will keep my word.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne day we received a letter. I should tell you that we lived in the
little village of Rogliano, at the extremity of Cap Corse. This letter
was from my brother. He told us that the army was disbanded, and that
he should return by ChГғВўteauroux, Clermont-Ferrand, Le Puy, and NГғВ®mes;
and, if I had any money, he prayed me to leave it for him at NГғВ®mes,
with an innkeeper with whom I had dealings.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn the smuggling line?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңEh, your excellency? Everyone must live.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly; go on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI loved my brother tenderly, as I told your excellency, and I resolved
not to send the money, but to take it to him myself. I possessed a
thousand francs. I left five hundred with Assunta, my sister-in-law,
and with the other five hundred I set off for NГғВ®mes. It was easy to do
so, and as I had my boat and a lading to take in at sea, everything
favored my project. But, after we had taken in our cargo, the wind
became contrary, so that we were four or five days without being able
to enter the RhГғВҙne. At last, however, we succeeded, and worked up to
Arles. I left the boat between Bellegarde and Beaucaire, and took the
road to NГғВ®mes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe are getting to the story now?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, your excellency; excuse me, but, as you will see, I only tell you
what is absolutely necessary. Just at this time the famous massacres
took place in the south of France. Three brigands, called Trestaillon,
Truphemy, and Graffan, publicly assassinated everybody whom they
suspected of Bonapartism. You have doubtless heard of these massacres,
your excellency?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVaguely; I was far from France at that period. Go on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs I entered NГғВ®mes, I literally waded in blood; at every step you
encountered dead bodies and bands of murderers, who killed, plundered,
and burned. At the sight of this slaughter and devastation I became
terrified, not for myselfГўВҖВ”for I, a simple Corsican fisherman, had
nothing to fear; on the contrary, that time was most favorable for us
smugglersГўВҖВ”but for my brother, a soldier of the empire, returning from
the army of the Loire, with his uniform and his epaulets, there was
everything to apprehend. I hastened to the innkeeper. My misgivings had
been but too true. My brother had arrived the previous evening at
NГғВ®mes, and, at the very door of the house where he was about to demand
hospitality, he had been assassinated. I did all in my power to
discover the murderers, but no one durst tell me their names, so much
were they dreaded. I then thought of that French justice of which I had
heard so much, and which feared nothing, and I went to the kingГўВҖВҷs
attorney.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd this kingГўВҖВҷs attorney was named Villefort?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo
carelessly.

ГўВҖВңYes, your excellency; he came from Marseilles, where he had been
deputy procureur. His zeal had procured him advancement, and he was
said to be one of the first who had informed the government of the
departure from the Island of Elba.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңyou went to him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳMonsieur,ГўВҖВҷ I said, ГўВҖВҳmy brother was assassinated yesterday in the
streets of NГғВ®mes, I know not by whom, but it is your duty to find out.
You are the representative of justice here, and it is for justice to
avenge those she has been unable to protect.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWho was your brother?ГўВҖВҷ asked he.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳA lieutenant in the Corsican battalion.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳA soldier of the usurper, then?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳA soldier of the French army.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWell,ГўВҖВҷ replied he, ГўВҖВҳhe has smitten with the sword, and he has
perished by the sword.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳYou are mistaken, monsieur,ГўВҖВҷ I replied; ГўВҖВҳhe has perished by the
poniard.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWhat do you want me to do?ГўВҖВҷ asked the magistrate.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI have already told youГўВҖВ”avenge him.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳOn whom?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳOn his murderers.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳHow should I know who they are?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳOrder them to be sought for.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWhy, your brother has been involved in a quarrel, and killed in a
duel. All these old soldiers commit excesses which were tolerated in
the time of the emperor, but which are not suffered now, for the people
here do not like soldiers of such disorderly conduct.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳMonsieur,ГўВҖВҷ I replied, ГўВҖВҳit is not for myself that I entreat your
interferenceГўВҖВ”I should grieve for him or avenge him, but my poor brother
had a wife, and were anything to happen to me, the poor creature would
perish from want, for my brotherГўВҖВҷs pay alone kept her. Pray, try and
obtain a small government pension for her.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳEvery revolution has its catastrophes,ГўВҖВҷ returned M. de Villefort;
ГўВҖВҳyour brother has been the victim of this. It is a misfortune, and
government owes nothing to his family. If we are to judge by all the
vengeance that the followers of the usurper exercised on the partisans
of the king, when, in their turn, they were in power, your brother
would be today, in all probability, condemned to death. What has
happened is quite natural, and in conformity with the law of
reprisals.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWhat,ГўВҖВҷ cried I, ГўВҖВҳdo you, a magistrate, speak thus to me?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳAll these Corsicans are mad, on my honor,ГўВҖВҷ replied M. de Villefort;
ГўВҖВҳthey fancy that their countryman is still emperor. You have mistaken
the time, you should have told me this two months ago, it is too late
now. Go now, at once, or I shall have you put out.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңI looked at him an instant to see if there was anything to hope from
further entreaty. But he was a man of stone. I approached him, and said
in a low voice, ГўВҖВҳWell, since you know the Corsicans so well, you know
that they always keep their word. You think that it was a good deed to
kill my brother, who was a Bonapartist, because you are a royalist.
Well, I, who am a Bonapartist also, declare one thing to you, which is,
that I will kill you. From this moment I declare the vendetta against
you, so protect yourself as well as you can, for the next time we meet
your last hour has come.ГўВҖВҷ And before he had recovered from his
surprise, I opened the door and left the room.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, well,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңsuch an innocent looking person as
you are to do those things, M. Bertuccio, and to a kingГўВҖВҷs attorney at
that! But did he know what was meant by the terrible word ГўВҖВҳvendettaГўВҖВҷ?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe knew so well, that from that moment he shut himself in his house,
and never went out unattended, seeking me high and low. Fortunately, I
was so well concealed that he could not find me. Then he became
alarmed, and dared not stay any longer at NГғВ®mes, so he solicited a
change of residence, and, as he was in reality very influential, he was
nominated to Versailles. But, as you know, a Corsican who has sworn to
avenge himself cares not for distance, so his carriage, fast as it
went, was never above half a dayГўВҖВҷs journey before me, who followed him
on foot. The most important thing was, not to kill him onlyГўВҖВ”for I had
an opportunity of doing so a hundred timesГўВҖВ”but to kill him without
being discoveredГўВҖВ”at least, without being arrested. I no longer belonged
to myself, for I had my sister-in-law to protect and provide for.

ГўВҖВңFor three months I watched M. de Villefort, for three months he took
not a step out-of-doors without my following him. At length I
discovered that he went mysteriously to Auteuil. I followed him
thither, and I saw him enter the house where we now are, only, instead
of entering by the great door that looks into the street, he came on
horseback, or in his carriage, left the one or the other at the little
inn, and entered by the gate you see there.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo made a sign with his head to show that he could discern in
the darkness the door to which Bertuccio alluded.

ГўВҖВңAs I had nothing more to do at Versailles, I went to Auteuil, and
gained all the information I could. If I wished to surprise him, it was
evident this was the spot to lie in wait for him. The house belonged,
as the concierge informed your excellency, to M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran,
VillefortГўВҖВҷs father-in-law. M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran lived at Marseilles, so
that this country house was useless to him, and it was reported to be
let to a young widow, known only by the name of ГўВҖВҳthe Baroness.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңOne evening, as I was looking over the wall, I saw a young and
handsome woman who was walking alone in that garden, which was not
overlooked by any windows, and I guessed that she was awaiting M. de
Villefort. When she was sufficiently near for me to distinguish her
features, I saw she was from eighteen to nineteen, tall and very fair.
As she had a loose muslin dress on and as nothing concealed her figure,
I saw she would ere long become a mother. A few moments after, the
little door was opened and a man entered. The young woman hastened to
meet him. They threw themselves into each otherГўВҖВҷs arms, embraced
tenderly, and returned together to the house. The man was M. de
Villefort; I fully believed that when he went out in the night he would
be forced to traverse the whole of the garden alone.ГўВҖВқ

20291m



ГўВҖВңAnd,ГўВҖВқ asked the count, ГўВҖВңdid you ever know the name of this woman?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, excellency,ГўВҖВқ returned Bertuccio; ГўВҖВңyou will see that I had no time
to learn it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat evening,ГўВҖВқ continued Bertuccio, ГўВҖВңI could have killed the
procureur, but as I was not sufficiently acquainted with the
neighborhood, I was fearful of not killing him on the spot, and that if
his cries were overheard I might be taken; so I put it off until the
next occasion, and in order that nothing should escape me, I took a
chamber looking into the street bordered by the wall of the garden.
Three days after, about seven oГўВҖВҷclock in the evening, I saw a servant
on horseback leave the house at full gallop, and take the road to
SГғВЁvres. I concluded that he was going to Versailles, and I was not
deceived. Three hours later, the man returned covered with dust, his
errand was performed, and two minutes after, another man on foot,
muffled in a mantle, opened the little door of the garden, which he
closed after him. I descended rapidly; although I had not seen
VillefortГўВҖВҷs face, I recognized him by the beating of my heart. I
crossed the street, and stopped at a post placed at the angle of the
wall, and by means of which I had once before looked into the garden.

ГўВҖВңThis time I did not content myself with looking, but I took my knife
out of my pocket, felt that the point was sharp, and sprang over the
wall. My first care was to run to the door; he had left the key in it,
taking the simple precaution of turning it twice in the lock. Nothing,
then, preventing my escape by this means, I examined the grounds. The
garden was long and narrow; a stretch of smooth turf extended down the
middle, and at the corners were clumps of trees with thick and massy
foliage, that made a background for the shrubs and flowers. In order to
go from the door to the house, or from the house to the door, M. de
Villefort would be obliged to pass by one of these clumps of trees.

20293m



ГўВҖВңIt was the end of September; the wind blew violently. The faint
glimpses of the pale moon, hidden momentarily by masses of dark clouds
that were sweeping across the sky, whitened the gravel walks that led
to the house, but were unable to pierce the obscurity of the thick
shrubberies, in which a man could conceal himself without any fear of
discovery. I hid myself in the one nearest to the path Villefort must
take, and scarcely was I there when, amidst the gusts of wind, I
fancied I heard groans; but you know, or rather you do not know, your
excellency, that he who is about to commit an assassination fancies
that he hears low cries perpetually ringing in his ears. Two hours
passed thus, during which I imagined I heard moans repeatedly. Midnight
struck. As the last stroke died away, I saw a faint light shine through
the windows of the private staircase by which we have just descended.
The door opened, and the man in the mantle reappeared.

ГўВҖВңThe terrible moment had come, but I had so long been prepared for it
that my heart did not fail in the least. I drew my knife from my pocket
again, opened it, and made ready to strike. The man in the mantle
advanced towards me, but as he drew near I saw that he had a weapon in
his hand. I was afraid, not of a struggle, but of a failure. When he
was only a few paces from me, I saw that what I had taken for a weapon
was only a spade. I was still unable to divine for what reason M. de
Villefort had this spade in his hands, when he stopped close to the
thicket where I was, glanced round, and began to dig a hole in the
earth. I then perceived that he was hiding something under his mantle,
which he laid on the grass in order to dig more freely. Then, I
confess, curiosity mingled with hatred; I wished to see what Villefort
was going to do there, and I remained motionless, holding my breath.
Then an idea crossed my mind, which was confirmed when I saw the
procureur lift from under his mantle a box, two feet long, and six or
eight inches deep. I let him place the box in the hole he had made,
then, while he stamped with his feet to remove all traces of his
occupation, I rushed on him and plunged my knife into his breast,
exclaiming:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI am Giovanni Bertuccio; thy death for my brotherГўВҖВҷs; thy treasure for
his widow; thou seest that my vengeance is more complete than I had
hoped.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңI know not if he heard these words; I think he did not, for he fell
without a cry. I felt his blood gush over my face, but I was
intoxicated, I was delirious, and the blood refreshed, instead of
burning me. In a second I had disinterred the box; then, that it might
not be known I had done so, I filled up the hole, threw the spade over
the wall, and rushed through the door, which I double-locked, carrying
off the key.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңit seems to me this was nothing but murder and
robbery.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, your excellency,ГўВҖВқ returned Bertuccio; ГўВҖВңit was a vendetta followed
by restitution.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd was the sum a large one?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was not money.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, I recollect,ГўВҖВқ replied the count; ГўВҖВңdid you not say something of an
infant?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, excellency; I hastened to the river, sat down on the bank, and
with my knife forced open the lock of the box. In a fine linen cloth
was wrapped a new-born child. Its purple visage, and its violet-colored
hands showed that it had perished from suffocation, but as it was not
yet cold, I hesitated to throw it into the water that ran at my feet.
After a moment I fancied that I felt a slight pulsation of the heart,
and as I had been assistant at the hospital at Bastia, I did what a
doctor would have doneГўВҖВ”I inflated the lungs by blowing air into them,
and at the expiration of a quarter of an hour, it began to breathe, and
cried feebly. In my turn I uttered a cry, but a cry of joy.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳGod has not cursed me then,ГўВҖВҷ I cried, ГўВҖВҳsince he permits me to save
the life of a human creature, in exchange for the life I have taken
away.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

20295m



ГўВҖВңAnd what did you do with the child?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңIt was an
embarrassing load for a man seeking to escape.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI had not for a moment the idea of keeping it, but I knew that at
Paris there was an asylum where they receive such creatures. As I
passed the city gates I declared that I had found the child on the
road, and I inquired where the asylum was; the box confirmed my
statement, the linen proved that the infant belonged to wealthy
parents, the blood with which I was covered might have proceeded from
the child as well as from anyone else. No objection was raised, but
they pointed out the asylum, which was situated at the upper end of the
Rue dГўВҖВҷEnfer, and after having taken the precaution of cutting the linen
in two pieces, so that one of the two letters which marked it was on
the piece wrapped around the child, while the other remained in my
possession, I rang the bell, and fled with all speed. A fortnight after
I was at Rogliano, and I said to Assunta:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳConsole thyself, sister; Israel is dead, but he is avenged.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңShe demanded what I meant, and when I had told her all,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳGiovanni,ГўВҖВҷ
said she, ГўВҖВҳyou should have brought this child with you; we would have
replaced the parents it has lost, have called it Benedetto, and then,
in consequence of this good action, God would have blessed us.ГўВҖВҷ In
reply I gave her the half of the linen I had kept in order to reclaim
him if we became rich.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat letters were marked on the linen?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңAn H and an N, surmounted by a baronГўВҖВҷs coronet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy heaven, M. Bertuccio, you make use of heraldic terms; where did you
study heraldry?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn your service, excellency, where everything is learned.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo on, I am curious to know two things.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat are they, your excellency?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat became of this little boy? for I think you told me it was a boy,
M. Bertuccio.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo excellency, I do not recollect telling you that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI thought you did; I must have been mistaken.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, you were not, for it was in reality a little boy. But your
excellency wished to know two things; what was the second?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe second was the crime of which you were accused when you asked for
a confessor, and the AbbГғВ© Busoni came to visit you at your request in
the prison at NГғВ®mes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe story will be very long, excellency.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat matter? you know I take but little sleep, and I do not suppose
you are very much inclined for it either.ГўВҖВқ Bertuccio bowed, and resumed
his story.

ГўВҖВңPartly to drown the recollections of the past that haunted me, partly
to supply the wants of the poor widow, I eagerly returned to my trade
of smuggler, which had become more easy since that relaxation of the
laws which always follows a revolution. The southern districts were
ill-watched in particular, in consequence of the disturbances that were
perpetually breaking out in Avignon, NГғВ®mes, or UzГғВЁs. We profited by
this respite on the part of the government to make friends everywhere.
Since my brotherГўВҖВҷs assassination in the streets of NГғВ®mes, I had never
entered the town; the result was that the innkeeper with whom we were
connected, seeing that we would no longer come to him, was forced to
come to us, and had established a branch to his inn, on the road from
Bellegarde to Beaucaire, at the sign of the Pont du Gard. We had thus,
at Aigues-Mortes, Martigues, or Bouc, a dozen places where we left our
goods, and where, in case of necessity, we concealed ourselves from the
gendarmes and custom-house officers. Smuggling is a profitable trade,
when a certain degree of vigor and intelligence is employed; as for
myself, brought up in the mountains, I had a double motive for fearing
the gendarmes and custom-house officers, as my appearance before the
judges would cause an inquiry, and an inquiry always looks back into
the past. And in my past life they might find something far more grave
than the selling of smuggled cigars, or barrels of brandy without a
permit. So, preferring death to capture, I accomplished the most
astonishing deeds, and which, more than once, showed me that the too
great care we take of our bodies is the only obstacle to the success of
those projects which require rapid decision, and vigorous and
determined execution. In reality, when you have once devoted your life
to your enterprises, you are no longer the equal of other men, or,
rather, other men are no longer your equals, and whosoever has taken
this resolution, feels his strength and resources doubled.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPhilosophy, M. Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ interrupted the count; ГўВҖВңyou have done a
little of everything in your life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, excellency!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no; but philosophy at half-past ten at night is somewhat late; yet
I have no other observation to make, for what you say is correct, which
is more than can be said for all philosophy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy journeys became more and more extensive and more productive.
Assunta took care of all, and our little fortune increased. One day as
I was setting off on an expedition, ГўВҖВҳGo,ГўВҖВҷ said she; ГўВҖВҳat your return I
will give you a surprise.ГўВҖВҷ I questioned her, but in vain; she would
tell me nothing, and I departed. Our expedition lasted nearly six
weeks; we had been to Lucca to take in oil, to Leghorn for English
cottons, and we ran our cargo without opposition, and returned home
full of joy. When I entered the house, the first thing I beheld in the
middle of AssuntaГўВҖВҷs chamber was a cradle that might be called sumptuous
compared with the rest of the furniture, and in it a baby seven or
eight months old. I uttered a cry of joy; the only moments of sadness I
had known since the assassination of the procureur were caused by the
recollection that I had abandoned this child. For the assassination
itself I had never felt any remorse. Poor Assunta had guessed all. She
had profited by my absence, and furnished with the half of the linen,
and having written down the day and hour at which I had deposited the
child at the asylum, had set off for Paris, and had reclaimed it. No
objection was raised, and the infant was given up to her. Ah, I
confess, your excellency, when I saw this poor creature sleeping
peacefully in its cradle, I felt my eyes filled with tears. ГўВҖВҳAh,
Assunta,ГўВҖВҷ cried I, ГўВҖВҳyou are an excellent woman, and Heaven will bless
you.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңis less correct than your philosophy,ГўВҖВ”it is
only faith.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas, your excellency is right,ГўВҖВқ replied Bertuccio, ГўВҖВңand God made this
infant the instrument of our punishment. Never did a perverse nature
declare itself more prematurely, and yet it was not owing to any fault
in his bringing up. He was a most lovely child, with large blue eyes,
of that deep color that harmonizes so well with the blond complexion;
only his hair, which was too light, gave his face a most singular
expression, and added to the vivacity of his look, and the malice of
his smile.

ГўВҖВңUnfortunately, there is a proverb which says that ГўВҖВҳred is either
altogether good or altogether bad.ГўВҖВҷ The proverb was but too correct as
regarded Benedetto, and even in his infancy he manifested the worst
disposition. It is true that the indulgence of his foster-mother
encouraged him. This child, for whom my poor sister would go to the
town, five or six leagues off, to purchase the earliest fruits and the
most tempting sweetmeats, preferred to Palma grapes or Genoese
preserves, the chestnuts stolen from a neighborГўВҖВҷs orchard, or the dried
apples in his loft, when he could eat as well of the nuts and apples
that grew in my garden.

ГўВҖВңOne day, when Benedetto was about five or six, our neighbor Wasilio,
who, according to the custom of the country, never locked up his purse
or his valuablesГўВҖВ”for, as your excellency knows, there are no thieves in
CorsicaГўВҖВ”complained that he had lost a louis out of his purse; we
thought he must have made a mistake in counting his money, but he
persisted in the accuracy of his statement. One day, Benedetto, who had
been gone from the house since morning, to our great anxiety, did not
return until late in the evening, dragging a monkey after him, which he
said he had found chained to the foot of a tree. For more than a month
past, the mischievous child, who knew not what to wish for, had taken
it into his head to have a monkey. A boatman, who had passed by
Rogliano, and who had several of these animals, whose tricks had
greatly diverted him, had, doubtless, suggested this idea to him.
ГўВҖВҳMonkeys are not found in our woods chained to trees,ГўВҖВҷ said I; ГўВҖВҳconfess
how you obtained this animal.ГўВҖВҷ Benedetto maintained the truth of what
he had said, and accompanied it with details that did more honor to his
imagination than to his veracity. I became angry; he began to laugh, I
threatened to strike him, and he made two steps backwards. ГўВҖВҳYou cannot
beat me,ГўВҖВҷ said he; ГўВҖВҳyou have no right, for you are not my father.ГўВҖВҷ

20299m



ГўВҖВңWe never knew who had revealed this fatal secret, which we had so
carefully concealed from him; however, it was this answer, in which the
childГўВҖВҷs whole character revealed itself, that almost terrified me, and
my arm fell without touching him.

ГўВҖВңThe boy triumphed, and this victory rendered him so audacious, that
all the money of Assunta, whose affection for him seemed to increase as
he became more unworthy of it, was spent in caprices she knew not how
to contend against, and follies she had not the courage to prevent.
When I was at Rogliano everything went on properly, but no sooner was
my back turned than Benedetto became master, and everything went ill.
When he was only eleven, he chose his companions from among the young
men of eighteen or twenty, the worst characters in Bastia, or, indeed,
in Corsica, and they had already, for some mischievous pranks, been
several times threatened with a prosecution. I became alarmed, as any
prosecution might be attended with serious consequences. I was
compelled, at this period, to leave Corsica on an important expedition;
I reflected for a long time, and with the hope of averting some
impending misfortune, I resolved that Benedetto should accompany me.

ГўВҖВңI hoped that the active and laborious life of a smuggler, with the
severe discipline on board, would have a salutary effect on his
character, which was now well-nigh, if not quite, corrupt. I spoke to
Benedetto alone, and proposed to him to accompany me, endeavoring to
tempt him by all the promises most likely to dazzle the imagination of
a child of twelve. He heard me patiently, and when I had finished,
burst out laughing.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳAre you mad, uncle?ГўВҖВҷ (he called me by this name when he was in good
humor); ГўВҖВҳdo you think I am going to change the life I lead for your
mode of existenceГўВҖВ”my agreeable indolence for the hard and precarious
toil you impose on yourself, exposed to the bitter frost at night, and
the scorching heat by day, compelled to conceal yourself, and when you
are perceived, receive a volley of bullets, all to earn a paltry sum?
Why, I have as much money as I want; mother Assunta always furnishes me
when I ask for it! You see that I should be a fool to accept your
offer.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңThe arguments, and his audacity, perfectly stupefied me. Benedetto
rejoined his associates, and I saw him from a distance point me out to
them as a fool.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSweet child,ГўВҖВқ murmured Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңOh, had he been my own son,ГўВҖВқ replied Bertuccio, ГўВҖВңor even my nephew, I
would have brought him back to the right road, for the knowledge that
you are doing your duty gives you strength, but the idea that I was
striking a child whose father I had killed, made it impossible for me
to punish him. I gave my sister, who constantly defended the
unfortunate boy, good advice, and as she confessed that she had several
times missed money to a considerable amount, I showed her a safe place
in which to conceal our little treasure for the future. My mind was
already made up. Benedetto could read, write, and cipher perfectly, for
when the fit seized him, he learned more in a day than others in a
week. My intention was to enter him as a clerk in some ship, and
without letting him know anything of my plan, to convey him some
morning on board; by this means his future treatment would depend upon
his own conduct. I set off for France, after having fixed upon the
plan. Our cargo was to be landed in the Gulf of Lyons, and this was a
difficult thing to do because it was then the year 1829. The most
perfect tranquillity was restored, and the vigilance of the
custom-house officers was redoubled, and their strictness was increased
at this time, in consequence of the fair at Beaucaire.

20301m



ГўВҖВңOur expedition made a favorable beginning. We anchored our
vesselГўВҖВ”which had a double hold, where our goods were concealedГўВҖВ”amidst a
number of other vessels that bordered the banks of the RhГғВҙne from
Beaucaire to Arles. On our arrival we began to discharge our cargo in
the night, and to convey it into the town, by the help of the innkeeper
with whom we were connected.

ГўВҖВңWhether success rendered us imprudent, or whether we were betrayed, I
know not; but one evening, about five oГўВҖВҷclock, our little cabin-boy
came breathlessly, to inform us that he had seen a detachment of
custom-house officers advancing in our direction. It was not their
proximity that alarmed us, for detachments were constantly patrolling
along the banks of the RhГғВҙne, but the care, according to the boyГўВҖВҷs
account, that they took to avoid being seen. In an instant we were on
the alert, but it was too late; our vessel was surrounded, and amongst
the custom-house officers I observed several gendarmes, and, as
terrified at the sight of their uniforms as I was brave at the sight of
any other, I sprang into the hold, opened a port, and dropped into the
river, dived, and only rose at intervals to breathe, until I reached a
ditch that had recently been made from the RhГғВҙne to the canal that runs
from Beaucaire to Aigues-Mortes. I was now safe, for I could swim along
the ditch without being seen, and I reached the canal in safety. I had
designedly taken this direction. I have already told your excellency of
an innkeeper from NГғВ®mes who had set up a little tavern on the road from
Bellegarde to Beaucaire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңI perfectly recollect him; I think he was
your colleague.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely,ГўВҖВқ answered Bertuccio; ГўВҖВңbut he had, seven or eight years
before this period, sold his establishment to a tailor at Marseilles,
who, having almost ruined himself in his old trade, wished to make his
fortune in another. Of course, we made the same arrangements with the
new landlord that we had with the old; and it was of this man that I
intended to ask shelter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat was his name?ГўВҖВқ inquired the count, who seemed to become somewhat
interested in BertuccioГўВҖВҷs story.

ГўВҖВңGaspard Caderousse; he had married a woman from the village of
Carconte, and whom we did not know by any other name than that of her
village. She was suffering from malarial fever, and seemed dying by

five-and-forty, who had more than once, in time of danger, given ample
proof of his presence of mind and courage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you say,ГўВҖВқ interrupted Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңthat this took place towards
the yearГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң1829, your excellency.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn what month?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJune.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe beginning or the end?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe evening of the 3rd.ГўВҖВқ

20303m



ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңthe evening of the 3rd of June, 1829. Go on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was from Caderousse that I intended demanding shelter, and, as we
never entered by the door that opened onto the road, I resolved not to
break through the rule, so climbing over the garden-hedge, I crept
amongst the olive and wild fig trees, and fearing that Caderousse might
have some guest, I entered a kind of shed in which I had often passed
the night, and which was only separated from the inn by a partition, in
which holes had been made in order to enable us to watch an opportunity
of announcing our presence.

ГўВҖВңMy intention was, if Caderousse was alone, to acquaint him with my
presence, finish the meal the custom-house officers had interrupted,
and profit by the threatened storm to return to the RhГғВҙne, and
ascertain the state of our vessel and its crew. I stepped into the
shed, and it was fortunate I did so, for at that moment Caderousse
entered with a stranger.

ГўВҖВңI waited patiently, not to overhear what they said, but because I
could do nothing else; besides, the same thing had occurred often
before. The man who was with Caderousse was evidently a stranger to the
South of France; he was one of those merchants who come to sell
jewellery at the Beaucaire fair, and who during the month the fair
lasts, and during which there is so great an influx of merchants and
customers from all parts of Europe, often have dealings to the amount
of 100,000 to 150,000 francs. Caderousse entered hastily. Then, seeing
that the room was, as usual, empty, and only guarded by the dog, he
called to his wife, ГўВҖВҳHello, Carconte,ГўВҖВҷ said he, ГўВҖВҳthe worthy priest has
not deceived us; the diamond is real.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңAn exclamation of joy was heard, and the staircase creaked beneath a
feeble step. ГўВҖВҳWhat do you say?ГўВҖВҷ asked his wife, pale as death.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI say that the diamond is real, and that this gentleman, one of the
first jewellers of Paris, will give us 50,000 francs for it. Only, in
order to satisfy himself that it really belongs to us, he wishes you to
relate to him, as I have done already, the miraculous manner in which

down, monsieur, and I will fetch you some refreshment.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңThe jeweller examined attentively the interior of the inn and the
apparent poverty of the persons who were about to sell him a diamond
that seemed to have come from the casket of a prince.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳRelate your story, madame,ГўВҖВҷ said he, wishing, no doubt, to profit by

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳOh,ГўВҖВҷ returned she, ГўВҖВҳit was a gift of heaven. My husband was a great
friend, in 1814 or 1815, of a sailor named Edmond DantГғВЁs. This poor

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳBut how did he obtain it?ГўВҖВҷ asked the jeweller; ГўВҖВҳhad he it before he

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳNo, monsieur; but it appears that in prison he made the acquaintance

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳThe same story,ГўВҖВҷ muttered the jeweller; ГўВҖВҳand improbable as it seemed

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳHow not agreed about?ГўВҖВҷ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВҳI thought we agreed for the

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳThat is,ГўВҖВҷ replied the jeweller, ГўВҖВҳI offered 40,000 francs.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВҳForty thousand,ГўВҖВҷ cried La Carconte; ГўВҖВҳwe will not part with it for that

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWhat was the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs name?ГўВҖВҷ asked the indefatigable questioner.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳThe AbbГғВ© Busoni,ГўВҖВҷ said La Carconte.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳHe was a foreigner?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңYes, your excellency. I did not look on Caderousse as a bad man, and I

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳThere,ГўВҖВҷ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВҳand now, although you have wronged us of
