fifteen years. Stories of gypsies, who steal children, are not at all
in vogue in this part of the world, and would not be believed. You sent
him for his education to a college in one of the provinces, and now you
wish him to complete his education in the Parisian world. That is the
reason which has induced you to leave Via Reggio, where you have lived
since the death of your wife. That will be sufficient.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou think so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well, then.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf they should hear of the separationГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, yes; what could I say?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat an unfaithful tutor, bought over by the enemies of your familyГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy the Corsinari?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely. Had stolen away this child, in order that your name might
become extinct.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is reasonable, since he is an only son.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, now that all is arranged, do not let these newly awakened
remembrances be forgotten. You have, doubtless, already guessed that I
was preparing a surprise for you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn agreeable one?ГўВҖВқ asked the Italian.

ГўВҖВңAh, I see the eye of a father is no more to be deceived than his
heart.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHum!ГўВҖВқ said the major.

ГўВҖВңSomeone has told you the secret; or, perhaps, you guessed that he was
here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat who was here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour childГўВҖВ”your sonГўВҖВ”your Andrea!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI did guess it,ГўВҖВқ replied the major with the greatest possible
coolness. ГўВҖВңThen he is here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңwhen the valet de chambre came in just
now, he told me of his arrival.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, very well, very well,ГўВҖВқ said the major, clutching the buttons of
his coat at each exclamation.

ГўВҖВңMy dear sir,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI understand your emotion; you must
have time to recover yourself. I will, in the meantime, go and prepare
the young man for this much-desired interview, for I presume that he is
not less impatient for it than yourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI should quite imagine that to be the case,ГўВҖВқ said Cavalcanti.

ГўВҖВңWell, in a quarter of an hour he shall be with you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will bring him, then? You carry your goodness so far as even to
present him to me yourself?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; I do not wish to come between a father and son. Your interview
will be private. But do not be uneasy; even if the powerful voice of
nature should be silent, you cannot well mistake him; he will enter by
this door. He is a fine young man, of fair complexionГўВҖВ”a little too
fair, perhapsГўВҖВ”pleasing in manners; but you will see and judge for
yourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy the way,ГўВҖВқ said the major, ГўВҖВңyou know I have only the 2,000 francs
which the AbbГғВ© Busoni sent me; this sum I have expended upon travelling
expenses, andГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you want money; that is a matter of course, my dear M. Cavalcanti.
Well, here are 8,000 francs on account.ГўВҖВқ

The majorГўВҖВҷs eyes sparkled brilliantly.

ГўВҖВңIt is 40,000 francs which I now owe you,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңDoes your excellency wish for a receipt?ГўВҖВқ said the major, at the same
time slipping the money into the inner pocket of his coat.

ГўВҖВңFor what?ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңI thought you might want it to show the AbbГғВ© Busoni.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, when you receive the remaining 40,000, you shall give me a
receipt in full. Between honest men such excessive precaution is, I
think, quite unnecessary.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, so it is, between perfectly upright people.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne word more,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңSay on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will permit me to make one remark?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly; pray do so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I should advise you to leave off wearing that style of dress.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed,ГўВҖВқ said the major, regarding himself with an air of complete
satisfaction.

ГўВҖВңYes. It may be worn at Via Reggio; but that costume, however elegant
in itself, has long been out of fashion in Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs unfortunate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, if you really are attached to your old mode of dress; you can
easily resume it when you leave Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what shall I wear?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat you find in your trunks.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn my trunks? I have but one portmanteau.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI dare say you have nothing else with you. What is the use of boring
oneГўВҖВҷs self with so many things? Besides an old soldier always likes to
march with as little baggage as possible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is just the caseГўВҖВ”precisely so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you are a man of foresight and prudence, therefore you sent your
luggage on before you. It has arrived at the HГғВҙtel des Princes, Rue de
Richelieu. It is there you are to take up your quarters.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, in these trunksГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI presume you have given orders to your valet de chambre to put in all
you are likely to need,ГўВҖВ”your plain clothes and your uniform. On grand
occasions you must wear your uniform; that will look very well. Do not
forget your crosses. They still laugh at them in France, and yet always
wear them, for all that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well, very well,ГўВҖВқ said the major, who was in ecstasy at the
attention paid him by the count.

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthat you have fortified yourself against all
painful excitement, prepare yourself, my dear M. Cavalcanti, to meet
your lost Andrea.ГўВҖВқ

Saying which Monte Cristo bowed, and disappeared behind the tapestry,
leaving the major fascinated beyond expression with the delightful
reception which he had received at the hands of the count.



 Chapter 56. Andrea Cavalcanti

The Count of Monte Cristo entered the adjoining room, which Baptistin
had designated as the drawing-room, and found there a young man, of
graceful demeanor and elegant appearance, who had arrived in a cab
about half an hour previously. Baptistin had not found any difficulty
in recognizing the person who presented himself at the door for
admittance. He was certainly the tall young man with light hair, red
beard, black eyes, and brilliant complexion, whom his master had so
particularly described to him. When the count entered the room the
young man was carelessly stretched on a sofa, tapping his boot with the
gold-headed cane which he held in his hand. On perceiving the count he
rose quickly.

ГўВҖВңThe Count of Monte Cristo, I believe?ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңYes, sir, and I think I have the honor of addressing Count Andrea
Cavalcanti?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCount Andrea Cavalcanti,ГўВҖВқ repeated the young man, accompanying his
words with a bow.

ГўВҖВңYou are charged with a letter of introduction addressed to me, are you
not?ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңI did not mention that, because the signature seemed to me so
strange.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe letter signed ГўВҖВҳSinbad the Sailor,ГўВҖВҷ is it not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly so. Now, as I have never known any Sinbad, with the exception
of the one celebrated in the _Thousand and One Nights_ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, it is one of his descendants, and a great friend of mine; he is
a very rich Englishman, eccentric almost to insanity, and his real name
is Lord Wilmore.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed? Then that explains everything that is extraordinary,ГўВҖВқ said
Andrea. ГўВҖВңHe is, then, the same Englishman whom I metГўВҖВ”atГўВҖВ”ahГўВҖВ”yes, indeed.
Well, monsieur, I am at your service.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf what you say be true,ГўВҖВқ replied the count, smiling, ГўВҖВңperhaps you
will be kind enough to give me some account of yourself and your
family?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly, I will do so,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, with a quickness which
gave proof of his ready invention. ГўВҖВңI am (as you have said) the Count
Andrea Cavalcanti, son of Major Bartolomeo Cavalcanti, a descendant of
the Cavalcanti whose names are inscribed in the golden book at
Florence. Our family, although still rich (for my fatherГўВҖВҷs income
amounts to half a million), has experienced many misfortunes, and I
myself was, at the age of five years, taken away by the treachery of my
tutor, so that for fifteen years I have not seen the author of my
existence. Since I have arrived at years of discretion and become my
own master, I have been constantly seeking him, but all in vain. At
length I received this letter from your friend, which states that my
father is in Paris, and authorizes me to address myself to you for
information respecting him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally, all you have related to me is exceedingly interesting,ГўВҖВқ said
Monte Cristo, observing the young man with a gloomy satisfaction; ГўВҖВңand
you have done well to conform in everything to the wishes of my friend
Sinbad; for your father is indeed here, and is seeking you.ГўВҖВқ

The count from the moment of first entering the drawing-room, had not
once lost sight of the expression of the young manГўВҖВҷs countenance; he
had admired the assurance of his look and the firmness of his voice;
but at these words, so natural in themselves, ГўВҖВңYour father is indeed
here, and is seeking you,ГўВҖВқ young Andrea started, and exclaimed, ГўВҖВңMy
father? Is my father here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMost undoubtedly,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңyour father, Major
Bartolomeo Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ The expression of terror which, for the moment,
had overspread the features of the young man, had now disappeared.

ГўВҖВңAh, yes, that is the name, certainly. Major Bartolomeo Cavalcanti. And
you really mean to say; monsieur, that my dear father is here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir; and I can even add that I have only just left his company.
The history which he related to me of his lost son touched me to the
quick; indeed, his griefs, hopes, and fears on that subject might
furnish material for a most touching and pathetic poem. At length, he
one day received a letter, stating that the abductors of his son now
offered to restore him, or at least to give notice where he might be
found, on condition of receiving a large sum of money, by way of
ransom. Your father did not hesitate an instant, and the sum was sent
to the frontier of Piedmont, with a passport signed for Italy. You were
in the south of France, I think?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Andrea, with an embarrassed air, ГўВҖВңI was in the south of
France.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA carriage was to await you at Nice?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely so; and it conveyed me from Nice to Genoa, from Genoa to
Turin, from Turin to ChambГғВ©ry, from ChambГғВ©ry to Pont-de-Beauvoisin, and
from Pont-de-Beauvoisin to Paris.ГўВҖВқ

30133m



ГўВҖВңIndeed? Then your father ought to have met with you on the road, for
it is exactly the same route which he himself took, and that is how we
have been able to trace your journey to this place.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңif my father had met me, I doubt if he would have
recognized me; I must be somewhat altered since he last saw me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, the voice of nature,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ interrupted the young man, ГўВҖВңI had not looked upon it in that
light.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңthere is only one source of uneasiness
left in your fatherГўВҖВҷs mind, which is thisГўВҖВ”he is anxious to know how you
have been employed during your long absence from him, how you have been
treated by your persecutors, and if they have conducted themselves
towards you with all the deference due to your rank. Finally, he is
anxious to see if you have been fortunate enough to escape the bad
moral influence to which you have been exposed, and which is infinitely
more to be dreaded than any physical suffering; he wishes to discover
if the fine abilities with which nature had endowed you have been
weakened by want of culture; and, in short, whether you consider
yourself capable of resuming and retaining in the world the high
position to which your rank entitles you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the young man, quite astounded, ГўВҖВңI hope no false
reportГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs for myself, I first heard you spoken of by my friend Wilmore, the
philanthropist. I believe he found you in some unpleasant position, but
do not know of what nature, for I did not ask, not being inquisitive.
Your misfortunes engaged his sympathies, so you see you must have been
interesting. He told me that he was anxious to restore you to the
position which you had lost, and that he would seek your father until
he found him. He did seek, and has found him, apparently, since he is
here now; and, finally, my friend apprised me of your coming, and gave
me a few other instructions relative to your future fortune. I am quite
aware that my friend Wilmore is peculiar, but he is sincere, and as
rich as a gold mine, consequently, he may indulge his eccentricities
without any fear of their ruining him, and I have promised to adhere to
his instructions. Now, sir, pray do not be offended at the question I
am about to put to you, as it comes in the way of my duty as your
patron. I would wish to know if the misfortunes which have happened to
youГўВҖВ”misfortunes entirely beyond your control, and which in no degree
diminish my regard for youГўВҖВ”I would wish to know if they have not, in
some measure, contributed to render you a stranger to the world in
which your fortune and your name entitle you to make a conspicuous
figure?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ returned the young man, with a reassurance of manner, ГўВҖВңmake your
mind easy on this score. Those who took me from my father, and who
always intended, sooner or later, to sell me again to my original
proprietor, as they have now done, calculated that, in order to make
the most of their bargain, it would be politic to leave me in
possession of all my personal and hereditary worth, and even to
increase the value, if possible. I have, therefore, received a very
good education, and have been treated by these kidnappers very much as
the slaves were treated in Asia Minor, whose masters made them
grammarians, doctors, and philosophers, in order that they might fetch
a higher price in the Roman market.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo smiled with satisfaction; it appeared as if he had not
expected so much from M. Andrea Cavalcanti.

ГўВҖВңBesides,ГўВҖВқ continued the young man, ГўВҖВңif there did appear some defect in
education, or offence against the established forms of etiquette, I
suppose it would be excused, in consideration of the misfortunes which
accompanied my birth, and followed me through my youth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo in an indifferent tone, ГўВҖВңyou will do as you
please, count, for you are the master of your own actions, and are the
person most concerned in the matter, but if I were you, I would not
divulge a word of these adventures. Your history is quite a romance,
and the world, which delights in romances in yellow covers, strangely
mistrusts those which are bound in living parchment, even though they
be gilded like yourself. This is the kind of difficulty which I wished
to represent to you, my dear count. You would hardly have recited your
touching history before it would go forth to the world, and be deemed
unlikely and unnatural. You would be no longer a lost child found, but
you would be looked upon as an upstart, who had sprung up like a
mushroom in the night. You might excite a little curiosity, but it is
not everyone who likes to be made the centre of observation and the
subject of unpleasant remark.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI agree with you, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, turning pale, and, in
spite of himself, trembling beneath the scrutinizing look of his
companion, ГўВҖВңsuch consequences would be extremely unpleasant.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNevertheless, you must not exaggerate the evil,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo,
ГўВҖВңfor by endeavoring to avoid one fault you will fall into another. You
must resolve upon one simple and single line of conduct, and for a man
of your intelligence, this plan is as easy as it is necessary; you must
form honorable friendships, and by that means counteract the prejudice
which may attach to the obscurity of your former life.ГўВҖВқ

Andrea visibly changed countenance.

ГўВҖВңI would offer myself as your surety and friendly adviser,ГўВҖВқ said Monte
Cristo, ГўВҖВңdid I not possess a moral distrust of my best friends, and a
sort of inclination to lead others to doubt them too; therefore, in
departing from this rule, I should (as the actors say) be playing a
part quite out of my line, and should, therefore, run the risk of being
hissed, which would be an act of folly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHowever, your excellency,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңin consideration of Lord
Wilmore, by whom I was recommended to youГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, certainly,ГўВҖВқ interrupted Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңbut Lord Wilmore did not
omit to inform me, my dear M. Andrea, that the season of your youth was
rather a stormy one. Ah,ГўВҖВқ said the count, watching AndreaГўВҖВҷs
countenance, ГўВҖВңI do not demand any confession from you; it is precisely
to avoid that necessity that your father was sent for from Lucca. You
shall soon see him. He is a little stiff and pompous in his manner, and
he is disfigured by his uniform; but when it becomes known that he has
been for eighteen years in the Austrian service, all that will be
pardoned. We are not generally very severe with the Austrians. In
short, you will find your father a very presentable person, I assure
you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, sir, you have given me confidence; it is so long since we were
separated, that I have not the least remembrance of him, and, besides,
you know that in the eyes of the world a large fortune covers all
defects.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is a millionaireГўВҖВ”his income is 500,000 francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, with anxiety, ГўВҖВңI shall be sure to be placed
in an agreeable position.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne of the most agreeable possible, my dear sir; he will allow you an
income of 50,000 livres per annum during the whole time of your stay in
Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen in that case I shall always choose to remain there.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou cannot control circumstances, my dear sir; ГўВҖВҳman proposes, and God
disposes.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ Andrea sighed.

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңso long as I do remain in Paris, and nothing forces me
to quit it, do you mean to tell me that I may rely on receiving the sum
you just now mentioned to me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou may.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall I receive it from my father?ГўВҖВқ asked Andrea, with some
uneasiness.

ГўВҖВңYes, you will receive it from your father personally, but Lord Wilmore
will be the security for the money. He has, at the request of your
father, opened an account of 5,000 francs a month at M. DanglarsГўВҖВҷ,
which is one of the safest banks in Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd does my father mean to remain long in Paris?ГўВҖВқ asked Andrea.

ГўВҖВңOnly a few days,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңHis service does not allow
him to absent himself more than two or three weeks together.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, my dear father!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Andrea, evidently charmed with the idea
of his speedy departure.

ГўВҖВңTherefore,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo feigning to mistake his
meaningГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңtherefore I will not, for another instant, retard the pleasure
of your meeting. Are you prepared to embrace your worthy father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hope you do not doubt it.ГўВҖВқ

30137m



ГўВҖВңGo, then, into the drawing-room, my young friend, where you will find
your father awaiting you.ГўВҖВқ

Andrea made a low bow to the count, and entered the adjoining room.
Monte Cristo watched him till he disappeared, and then touched a spring
in a panel made to look like a picture, which, in sliding partly from
the frame, discovered to view a small opening, so cleverly contrived
that it revealed all that was passing in the drawing-room now occupied
by Cavalcanti and Andrea. The young man closed the door behind him, and
advanced towards the major, who had risen when he heard steps
approaching him.

ГўВҖВңAh, my dear father!ГўВҖВқ said Andrea in a loud voice, in order that the
count might hear him in the next room, ГўВҖВңis it really you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow do you do, my dear son?ГўВҖВқ said the major gravely.

ГўВҖВңAfter so many years of painful separation,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, in the same
tone of voice, and glancing towards the door, ГўВҖВңwhat a happiness it is
to meet again!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed it is, after so long a separation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWill you not embrace me, sir?ГўВҖВқ said Andrea.

30139m



ГўВҖВңIf you wish it, my son,ГўВҖВқ said the major; and the two men embraced each
other after the fashion of actors on the stage; that is to say, each
rested his head on the otherГўВҖВҷs shoulder.

ГўВҖВңThen we are once more reunited?ГўВҖВқ said Andrea.

ГўВҖВңOnce more,ГўВҖВқ replied the major.

ГўВҖВңNever more to be separated?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, as to thatГўВҖВ”I think, my dear son, you must be by this time so
accustomed to France as to look upon it almost as a second country.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe fact is,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңthat I should be exceedingly
grieved to leave it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs for me, you must know I cannot possibly live out of Lucca;
therefore I shall return to Italy as soon as I can.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut before you leave France, my dear father, I hope you will put me in
possession of the documents which will be necessary to prove my
descent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly; I am come expressly on that account; it has cost me much
trouble to find you, but I had resolved on giving them into your hands,
and if I had to recommence my search, it would occupy all the few
remaining years of my life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere are these papers, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHere they are.ГўВҖВқ

Andrea seized the certificate of his fatherГўВҖВҷs marriage and his own
baptismal register, and after having opened them with all the eagerness
which might be expected under the circumstances, he read them with a
facility which proved that he was accustomed to similar documents, and
with an expression which plainly denoted an unusual interest in the
contents. When he had perused the documents, an indefinable expression
of pleasure lighted up his countenance, and looking at the major with a
most peculiar smile, he said, in very excellent Tuscan:

ГўВҖВңThen there is no longer any such thing, in Italy as being condemned to
the galleys?ГўВҖВқ

The major drew himself up to his full height.

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВ”what do you mean by that question?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI mean that if there were, it would be impossible to draw up with
impunity two such deeds as these. In France, my dear sir, half such a
piece of effrontery as that would cause you to be quickly despatched to
Toulon for five years, for change of air.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWill you be good enough to explain your meaning?ГўВҖВқ said the major,
endeavoring as much as possible to assume an air of the greatest
majesty.

ГўВҖВңMy dear M. Cavalcanti,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, taking the major by the arm in a
confidential manner, ГўВҖВңhow much are you paid for being my father?ГўВҖВқ

The major was about to speak, when Andrea continued, in a low voice:

ГўВҖВңNonsense, I am going to set you an example of confidence, they give me
50,000 francs a year to be your son; consequently, you can understand
that it is not at all likely I shall ever deny my parent.ГўВҖВқ

The major looked anxiously around him.

ГўВҖВңMake yourself easy, we are quite alone,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea; ГўВҖВңbesides, we are
conversing in Italian.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then,ГўВҖВқ replied the major, ГўВҖВңthey paid me 50,000 francs down.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonsieur Cavalcanti,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңdo you believe in fairy tales?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI used not to do so, but I really feel now almost obliged to have
faith in them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have, then, been induced to alter your opinion; you have had some
proofs of their truth?ГўВҖВқ The major drew from his pocket a handful of
gold.

ГўВҖВңMost palpable proofs,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңas you may perceive.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou think, then, that I may rely on the countГўВҖВҷs promises?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly I do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are sure he will keep his word with me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo the letter, but at the same time, remember, we must continue to
play our respective parts. I, as a tender fatherГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I as a dutiful son, as they choose that I shall be descended from

ГўВҖВңWhom do you mean by they?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi_, I can hardly tell, but I was alluding to those who wrote the
letter; you received one, did you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom a certain AbbГғВ© Busoni.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you any knowledge of him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, I have never seen him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat did he say in the letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will promise not to betray me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRest assured of that; you well know that our interests are the same.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen read for yourself;ГўВҖВқ and the major gave a letter into the young
manГўВҖВҷs hand. Andrea read in a low voice:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳYou are poor; a miserable old age awaits you. Would you like to
become rich, or at least independent? Set out immediately for Paris,
and demand of the Count of Monte Cristo, Avenue des Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es, No.
30, the son whom you had by the Marchesa Corsinari, and who was taken
from you at five years of age. This son is named Andrea Cavalcanti. In
order that you may not doubt the kind intention of the writer of this
letter, you will find enclosed an order for 2,400 francs, payable in
Florence, at Signor GozziГўВҖВҷs; also a letter of introduction to the Count
of Monte Cristo, on whom I give you a draft of 48,000 francs. Remember
to go to the count on the 26th May at seven oГўВҖВҷclock in the evening.


ГўВҖВң(Signed) ГўВҖВҳAbbГғВ© Busoni.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ


ГўВҖВңIt is the same.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean?ГўВҖВқ said the major.

ГўВҖВңI was going to say that I received a letter almost to the same
effect.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom the AbbГғВ© Busoni?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom whom, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom an Englishman, called Lord Wilmore, who takes the name of Sinbad
the Sailor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd of whom you have no more knowledge than I of the AbbГғВ© Busoni?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are mistaken; there I am ahead of you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have seen him, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, once.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, that is just what I cannot tell you; if I did, I should make you
as wise as myself, which it is not my intention to do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what did the letter contain?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRead it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳYou are poor, and your future prospects are dark and gloomy. Do you
wish for a name? should you like to be rich, and your own master?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ


ГўВҖВң_Parbleu!_ГўВҖВқ said the young man; ГўВҖВңwas it possible there could be two
answers to such a question?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳTake the post-chaise which you will find waiting at the Porte de
GГғВӘnes, as you enter Nice; pass through Turin, ChambГғВ©ry, and
Pont-de-Beauvoisin. Go to the Count of Monte Cristo, Avenue des

and demand of him your father. You are the son of the Marchese

some papers which will certify this fact, and authorize you to appear
under that name in the Parisian world. As to your rank, an annual
income of 50,000 livres will enable you to support it admirably. I
enclose a draft for 5,000 livres, payable on M. Ferrea, banker at Nice,
and also a letter of introduction to the Count of Monte Cristo, whom I
have directed to supply all your wants.


ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳSinbad the Sailor.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ


ГўВҖВңHumph,ГўВҖВқ said the major; ГўВҖВңvery good. You have seen the count, you say?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have only just left him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd has he conformed to all that the letter specified?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe has.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you understand it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot in the least.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere is a dupe somewhere.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt all events, it is neither you nor I.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly not.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, thenГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, it does not much concern us, do you think it does?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; I agree with you there. We must play the game to the end, and
consent to be blindfolded.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, you shall see; I promise you I will sustain my part to

ГўВҖВңWell, marquis,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңyou appear to be in no way

ГўВҖВңAh, your excellency, I am overwhelmed with delight.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what are your feelings?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, turning to the young

ГўВҖВңAs for me, my heart is overflowing with happiness.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHappy father, happy son!ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңThere is only one thing which grieves me,ГўВҖВқ observed the major, ГўВҖВңand

ГўВҖВңAh, my dear M. Cavalcanti, I trust you will not leave before I have
had the honor of presenting you to some of my friends.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am at your service, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the major.

ГўВҖВңNow, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, addressing Andrea, ГўВҖВңmake your
confession.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell M. Cavalcanti something of the state of your finances.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi!_ monsieur, you have touched upon a tender chord.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you hear what he says, major?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly I do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut do you understand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour son says he requires money.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, what would you have me do?ГўВҖВқ said the major.

ГўВҖВңYou should furnish him with some of course,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, you,ГўВҖВқ said the count, at the same time advancing towards Andrea,
and slipping a packet of bank-notes into the young manГўВҖВҷs hand.

ГўВҖВңWhat is this?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is from your father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom my father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; did you not tell him just now that you wanted money? Well, then,
he deputes me to give you this.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAm I to consider this as part of my income on account?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, it is for the first expenses of your settling in Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, how good my dear father is!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSilence,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңhe does not wish you to know that it
comes from him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI fully appreciate his delicacy,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, cramming the notes
hastily into his pocket.

ГўВҖВңAnd now, gentlemen, I wish you good-morning,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңAnd when shall we have the honor of seeing you again, your
excellency?ГўВҖВқ asked Cavalcanti.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңwhen may we hope for that pleasure?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn Saturday, if you willГўВҖВ”Yes.ГўВҖВ”Let me seeГўВҖВ”SaturdayГўВҖВ”I am to dine at my
country house, at Auteuil, on that day, Rue de la Fontaine, No. 28.
Several persons are invited, and among others, M. Danglars, your
banker. I will introduce you to him, for it will be necessary he should
know you, as he is to pay your money.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFull dress?ГўВҖВқ said the major, half aloud.

ГўВҖВңOh, yes, certainly,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңuniform, cross, knee-breeches.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd how shall I be dressed?ГўВҖВқ demanded Andrea.

30145m



ГўВҖВңOh, very simply; black trousers, patent leather boots, white
waistcoat, either a black or blue coat, and a long cravat. Go to Blin
or VГғВ©ronique for your clothes. Baptistin will tell you where, if you do
not know their address. The less pretension there is in your attire,
the better will be the effect, as you are a rich man. If you mean to
buy any horses, get them of Devedeux, and if you purchase a phaeton, go
to Baptiste for it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt what hour shall we come?ГўВҖВқ asked the young man.

ГўВҖВңAbout half-past six.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe will be with you at that time,ГўВҖВқ said the major. The two Cavalcanti
bowed to the count, and left the house. Monte Cristo went to the
window, and saw them crossing the street, arm in arm.

ГўВҖВңThere go two miscreants;ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңit is a pity they are not really
related!ГўВҖВқ Then, after an instant of gloomy reflection, ГўВҖВңCome, I will go
to see the Morrels,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңI think that disgust is even more
sickening than hatred.ГўВҖВқ

30147m




 Chapter 57. In the Lucern Patch

Our readers must now allow us to transport them again to the enclosure
surrounding M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs house, and, behind the gate, half
screened from view by the large chestnut-trees, which on all sides
spread their luxuriant branches, we shall find some people of our
acquaintance. This time Maximilian was the first to arrive. He was
intently watching for a shadow to appear among the trees, and awaiting
with anxiety the sound of a light step on the gravel walk.

At length, the long-desired sound was heard, and instead of one figure,
as he had expected, he perceived that two were approaching him. The
delay had been occasioned by a visit from Madame Danglars and EugГғВ©nie,
which had been prolonged beyond the time at which Valentine was
expected. That she might not appear to fail in her promise to
Maximilian, she proposed to Mademoiselle Danglars that they should take
a walk in the garden, being anxious to show that the delay, which was
doubtless a cause of vexation to him, was not occasioned by any neglect
on her part. The young man, with the intuitive perception of a lover,
quickly understood the circumstances in which she was involuntarily
placed, and he was comforted. Besides, although she avoided coming
within speaking distance, Valentine arranged so that Maximilian could
see her pass and repass, and each time she went by, she managed,
unperceived by her companion, to cast an expressive look at the young
man, which seemed to say, ГўВҖВңHave patience! You see it is not my fault.ГўВҖВқ

And Maximilian was patient, and employed himself in mentally
contrasting the two girls,ГўВҖВ”one fair, with soft languishing eyes, a
figure gracefully bending like a weeping willow; the other a brunette,
with a fierce and haughty expression, and as straight as a poplar. It
is unnecessary to state that, in the eyes of the young man, Valentine
did not suffer by the contrast. In about half an hour the girls went
away, and Maximilian understood that Mademoiselle DanglarsГўВҖВҷ visit had
at last come to an end. In a few minutes Valentine re-entered the
garden alone. For fear that anyone should be observing her return, she
walked slowly; and instead of immediately directing her steps towards
the gate, she seated herself on a bench, and, carefully casting her
eyes around, to convince herself that she was not watched, she
presently arose, and proceeded quickly to join Maximilian.

ГўВҖВңGood-evening, Valentine,ГўВҖВқ said a well-known voice.

ГўВҖВңGood-evening, Maximilian; I know I have kept you waiting, but you saw
the cause of my delay.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I recognized Mademoiselle Danglars. I was not aware that you were
so intimate with her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho told you we were intimate, Maximilian?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo one, but you appeared to be so. From the manner in which you walked
and talked together, one would have thought you were two school-girls
telling your secrets to each other.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe were having a confidential conversation,ГўВҖВқ returned Valentine; ГўВҖВңshe
was owning to me her repugnance to the marriage with M. de Morcerf; and
I, on the other hand, was confessing to her how wretched it made me to
think of marrying M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDear Valentine!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat will account to you for the unreserved manner which you observed
between me and EugГғВ©nie, as in speaking of the man whom I could not
love, my thoughts involuntarily reverted to him on whom my affections
were fixed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, how good you are to say so, Valentine! You possess a quality which
can never belong to Mademoiselle Danglars. It is that indefinable charm
which is to a woman what perfume is to the flower and flavor to the
fruit, for the beauty of either is not the only quality we seek.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is your love which makes you look upon everything in that light.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, Valentine, I assure you such is not the case. I was observing you
both when you were walking in the garden, and, on my honor, without at
all wishing to depreciate the beauty of Mademoiselle Danglars, I cannot
understand how any man can really love her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe fact is, Maximilian, that I was there, and my presence had the

ГўВҖВңI own that your question embarrasses me, Valentine, for I cannot say

ГўВҖВңMy dear Maximilian, you are really too fanciful; you will not love

every-day sort of attachment such as ours. But they are calling me. Do
you hear?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, Valentine,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian, ГўВҖВңgive me but one finger through this
opening in the grating, one finger, the littlest finger of all, that I
may have the happiness of kissing it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMaximilian, we said we would be to each other as two voices, two
shadows.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs you will, Valentine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall you be happy if I do what you wish?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes!ГўВҖВқ

Valentine mounted on a bench, and passed not only her finger but her
whole hand through the opening. Maximilian uttered a cry of delight,
and, springing forwards, seized the hand extended towards him, and
imprinted on it a fervent and impassioned kiss. The little hand was
then immediately withdrawn, and the young man saw Valentine hurrying
towards the house, as though she were almost terrified at her own
sensations.



 Chapter 58. M. Noirtier de Villefort

We will now relate what was passing in the house of the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney
after the departure of Madame Danglars and her daughter, and during the
time of the conversation between Maximilian and Valentine, which we
have just detailed.

M. de Villefort entered his fatherГўВҖВҷs room, followed by Madame de
Villefort. Both of the visitors, after saluting the old man and
speaking to Barrois, a faithful servant, who had been twenty-five years
in his service, took their places on either side of the paralytic.

M. Noirtier was sitting in an armchair, which moved upon casters, in
which he was wheeled into the room in the morning, and in the same way
drawn out again at night. He was placed before a large glass, which
reflected the whole apartment, and so, without any attempt to move,
which would have been impossible, he could see all who entered the room
and everything which was going on around him. M. Noirtier, although
almost as immovable as a corpse, looked at the new-comers with a quick
and intelligent expression, perceiving at once, by their ceremonious
courtesy, that they were come on business of an unexpected and official
character.

Sight and hearing were the only senses remaining, and they, like two
solitary sparks, remained to animate the miserable body which seemed
fit for nothing but the grave; it was only, however, by means of one of
these senses that he could reveal the thoughts and feelings that still
occupied his mind, and the look by which he gave expression to his
inner life was like the distant gleam of a candle which a traveller
sees by night across some desert place, and knows that a living being
dwells beyond the silence and obscurity.

NoirtierГўВҖВҷs hair was long and white, and flowed over his shoulders;
while in his eyes, shaded by thick black lashes, was concentrated, as
it often happens with an organ which is used to the exclusion of the
others, all the activity, address, force, and intelligence which were
formerly diffused over his whole body; and so although the movement of
the arm, the sound of the voice, and the agility of the body, were
wanting, the speaking eye sufficed for all. He commanded with it; it
was the medium through which his thanks were conveyed. In short, his
whole appearance produced on the mind the impression of a corpse with
living eyes, and nothing could be more startling than to observe the
expression of anger or joy suddenly lighting up these organs, while the
rest of the rigid and marble-like features were utterly deprived of the
power of participation. Three persons only could understand this
language of the poor paralytic; these were Villefort, Valentine, and
the old servant of whom we have already spoken. But as Villefort saw
his father but seldom, and then only when absolutely obliged, and as he
never took any pains to please or gratify him when he was there, all
the old manГўВҖВҷs happiness was centred in his granddaughter. Valentine, by
means of her love, her patience, and her devotion, had learned to read
in NoirtierГўВҖВҷs look all the varied feelings which were passing in his
mind. To this dumb language, which was so unintelligible to others, she
answered by throwing her whole soul into the expression of her
countenance, and in this manner were the conversations sustained
between the blooming girl and the helpless invalid, whose body could
scarcely be called a living one, but who, nevertheless, possessed a
fund of knowledge and penetration, united with a will as powerful as
ever although clogged by a body rendered utterly incapable of obeying
its impulses.

Valentine had solved the problem, and was able easily to understand his
thoughts, and to convey her own in return, and, through her untiring
and devoted assiduity, it was seldom that, in the ordinary transactions
of every-day life, she failed to anticipate the wishes of the living,
thinking mind, or the wants of the almost inanimate body.

As to the servant, he had, as we have said, been with his master for
five-and-twenty years, therefore he knew all his habits, and it was
seldom that Noirtier found it necessary to ask for anything, so prompt
was he in administering to all the necessities of the invalid.

Villefort did not need the help of either Valentine or the domestic in
order to carry on with his father the strange conversation which he was
about to begin. As we have said, he perfectly understood the old manГўВҖВҷs
vocabulary, and if he did not use it more often, it was only
indifference and _ennui_ which prevented him from so doing. He
therefore allowed Valentine to go into the garden, sent away Barrois,
and after having seated himself at his fatherГўВҖВҷs right hand, while
Madame de Villefort placed herself on the left, he addressed him thus:

ГўВҖВңI trust you will not be displeased, sir, that Valentine has not come
with us, or that I dismissed Barrois, for our conference will be one
which could not with propriety be carried on in the presence of either.
Madame de Villefort and I have a communication to make to you.ГўВҖВқ

NoirtierГўВҖВҷs face remained perfectly passive during this long preamble,
while, on the contrary, VillefortГўВҖВҷs eye was endeavoring to penetrate
into the inmost recesses of the old manГўВҖВҷs heart.

ГўВҖВңThis communication,ГўВҖВқ continued the procureur, in that cold and
decisive tone which seemed at once to preclude all discussion, ГўВҖВңwill,
we are sure, meet with your approbation.ГўВҖВқ

The eye of the invalid still retained that vacancy of expression which
prevented his son from obtaining any knowledge of the feelings which
were passing in his mind; he listened, nothing more.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ resumed Villefort, ГўВҖВңwe are thinking of marrying Valentine.ГўВҖВқ Had
the old manГўВҖВҷs face been moulded in wax it could not have shown less
emotion at this news than was now to be traced there. ГўВҖВңThe marriage
will take place in less than three months,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eye still retained its inanimate expression.

Madame de Villefort now took her part in the conversation and added:

ГўВҖВңWe thought this news would possess an interest for you, sir, who have
always entertained a great affection for Valentine; it therefore only
now remains for us to tell you the name of the young man for whom she
is destined. It is one of the most desirable connections which could
possibly be formed; he possesses fortune, a high rank in society, and
every personal qualification likely to render Valentine supremely
happy,ГўВҖВ”his name, moreover, cannot be wholly unknown to you. It is M.
Franz de Quesnel, Baron dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay.ГўВҖВқ

While his wife was speaking, Villefort had narrowly watched the old
manГўВҖВҷs countenance. When Madame de Villefort pronounced the name of
Franz, the pupil of M. NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eye began to dilate, and his eyelids
trembled with the same movement that may be perceived on the lips of an
individual about to speak, and he darted a lightning glance at Madame
de Villefort and his son. The procureur, who knew the political hatred
which had formerly existed between M. Noirtier and the elder dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay,
well understood the agitation and anger which the announcement had
produced; but, feigning not to perceive either, he immediately resumed
the narrative begun by his wife.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңyou are aware that Valentine is about to enter her
nineteenth year, which renders it important that she should lose no
time in forming a suitable alliance. Nevertheless, you have not been
forgotten in our plans, and we have fully ascertained beforehand that
ValentineГўВҖВҷs future husband will consent, not to live in this house, for
that might not be pleasant for the young people, but that you should
live with them; so that you and Valentine, who are so attached to each
other, would not be separated, and you would be able to pursue exactly
the same course of life which you have hitherto done, and thus, instead
of losing, you will be a gainer by the change, as it will secure to you
two children instead of one, to watch over and comfort you.ГўВҖВқ

30165m



NoirtierГўВҖВҷs look was furious; it was very evident that something
desperate was passing in the old manГўВҖВҷs mind, for a cry of anger and
grief rose in his throat, and not being able to find vent in utterance,
appeared almost to choke him, for his face and lips turned quite purple
with the struggle. Villefort quietly opened a window, saying, ГўВҖВңIt is
very warm, and the heat affects M. Noirtier.ГўВҖВқ He then returned to his
place, but did not sit down.

ГўВҖВңThis marriage,ГўВҖВқ added Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңis quite agreeable to the
wishes of M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay and his family; besides, he had no relations
nearer than an uncle and aunt, his mother having died at his birth, and
his father having been assassinated in 1815, that is to say, when he
was but two years old; it naturally followed that the child was
permitted to choose his own pursuits, and he has, therefore, seldom
acknowledged any other authority but that of his own will.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat assassination was a mysterious affair,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңand the
perpetrators have hitherto escaped detection, although suspicion has
fallen on the head of more than one person.ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier made such an effort that his lips expanded into a smile.

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ continued Villefort, ГўВҖВңthose to whom the guilt really belongs, by
whom the crime was committed, on whose heads the justice of man may
probably descend here, and the certain judgment of God hereafter, would
rejoice in the opportunity thus afforded of bestowing such a
peace-offering as Valentine on the son of him whose life they so
ruthlessly destroyed.ГўВҖВқ Noirtier had succeeded in mastering his emotion
more than could have been deemed possible with such an enfeebled and
shattered frame.

ГўВҖВңYes, I understand,ГўВҖВқ was the reply contained in his look; and this look
expressed a feeling of strong indignation, mixed with profound
contempt. Villefort fully understood his fatherГўВҖВҷs meaning, and answered
by a slight shrug of his shoulders. He then motioned to his wife to
take leave.

ГўВҖВңNow sir,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңI must bid you farewell. Would
you like me to send Edward to you for a short time?ГўВҖВқ

It had been agreed that the old man should express his approbation by
closing his eyes, his refusal by winking them several times, and if he
had some desire or feeling to express, he raised them to heaven. If he
wanted Valentine, he closed his right eye only, and if Barrois, the
left. At Madame de VillefortГўВҖВҷs proposition he instantly winked his
eyes.

Provoked by a complete refusal, she bit her lip and said, ГўВҖВңThen shall I
send Valentine to you?ГўВҖВқ The old man closed his eyes eagerly, thereby
intimating that such was his wish.

M. and Madame de Villefort bowed and left the room, giving orders that
Valentine should be summoned to her grandfatherГўВҖВҷs presence, and feeling
sure that she would have much to do to restore calmness to the
perturbed spirit of the invalid. Valentine, with a color still
heightened by emotion, entered the room just after her parents had
quitted it. One look was sufficient to tell her that her grandfather
was suffering, and that there was much on his mind which he was wishing
to communicate to her.

ГўВҖВңDear grandpapa,ГўВҖВқ cried she, ГўВҖВңwhat has happened? They have vexed you,
and you are angry?ГўВҖВқ

The paralytic closed his eyes in token of assent.

ГўВҖВңWho has displeased you? Is it my father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMadame de Villefort?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMe?ГўВҖВқ The former sign was repeated.

ГўВҖВңAre you displeased with me?ГўВҖВқ cried Valentine in astonishment. M.
Noirtier again closed his eyes.

ГўВҖВңAnd what have I done, dear grandpapa, that you should be angry with
me?ГўВҖВқ cried Valentine.

There was no answer, and she continued:

ГўВҖВңI have not seen you all day. Has anyone been speaking to you against
me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said the old manГўВҖВҷs look, with eagerness.

ГўВҖВңLet me think a moment. I do assure you, grandpapaГўВҖВ”AhГўВҖВ”M. and Madame de
Villefort have just left this room, have they not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd it was they who told you something which made you angry? What was
it then? May I go and ask them, that I may have the opportunity of
making my peace with you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ said NoirtierГўВҖВҷs look.

ГўВҖВңAh, you frighten me. What can they have said?ГўВҖВқ and she again tried to
think what it could be.

ГўВҖВңAh, I know,ГўВҖВқ said she, lowering her voice and going close to the old
man. ГўВҖВңThey have been speaking of my marriage,ГўВҖВ”have they not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied the angry look.

ГўВҖВңI understand; you are displeased at the silence I have preserved on
the subject. The reason of it was, that they had insisted on my keeping
the matter a secret, and begged me not to tell you anything of it. They
did not even acquaint me with their intentions, and I only discovered
them by chance, that is why I have been so reserved with you, dear
grandpapa. Pray forgive me.ГўВҖВқ

But there was no look calculated to reassure her; all it seemed to say
was, ГўВҖВңIt is not only your reserve which afflicts me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it, then?ГўВҖВқ asked the young girl. ГўВҖВңPerhaps you think I shall
abandon you, dear grandpapa, and that I shall forget you when I am
married?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey told you, then, that M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay consented to our all living
together?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen why are you still vexed and grieved?ГўВҖВқ The old manГўВҖВҷs eyes beamed
with an expression of gentle affection.

ГўВҖВңYes, I understand,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine; ГўВҖВңit is because you love me.ГўВҖВқ The
old man assented.

ГўВҖВңAnd you are afraid I shall be unhappy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do not like M. Franz?ГўВҖВқ The eyes repeated several times, ГўВҖВңNo, no,
no.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you are vexed with the engagement?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, listen,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, throwing herself on her knees, and
putting her arm round her grandfatherГўВҖВҷs neck, ГўВҖВңI am vexed, too, for I
do not love M. Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay.ГўВҖВқ

An expression of intense joy illumined the old manГўВҖВҷs eyes.

ГўВҖВңWhen I wished to retire into a convent, you remember how angry you
were with me?ГўВҖВқ A tear trembled in the eye of the invalid. ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ
continued Valentine, ГўВҖВңthe reason of my proposing it was that I might
escape this hateful marriage, which drives me to despair.ГўВҖВқ NoirtierГўВҖВҷs
breathing came thick and short.

ГўВҖВңThen the idea of this marriage really grieves you too? Ah, if you
could but help meГўВҖВ”if we could both together defeat their plan! But you
are unable to oppose them,ГўВҖВ”you, whose mind is so quick, and whose will
is so firm are nevertheless, as weak and unequal to the contest as I am
myself. Alas, you, who would have been such a powerful protector to me
in the days of your health and strength, can now only sympathize in my
joys and sorrows, without being able to take any active part in them.
However, this is much, and calls for gratitude and Heaven has not taken
away all my blessings when it leaves me your sympathy and kindness.ГўВҖВқ

At these words there appeared in NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eye an expression of such
deep meaning that the young girl thought she could read these words
there: ГўВҖВңYou are mistaken; I can still do much for you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think you can help me, dear grandpapa?ГўВҖВқ said Valentine.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ Noirtier raised his eyes, it was the sign agreed on between him
and Valentine when he wanted anything.

ГўВҖВңWhat is it you want, dear grandpapa?ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, and she
endeavored to recall to mind all the things which he would be likely to
need; and as the ideas presented themselves to her mind, she repeated
them aloud, then,ГўВҖВ”finding that all her efforts elicited nothing but a
constant _ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ_ГўВҖВ”she said, ГўВҖВңCome, since this plan does not answer, I
will have recourse to another.ГўВҖВқ

She then recited all the letters of the alphabet from A down to N. When
she arrived at that letter the paralytic made her understand that she
had spoken the initial letter of the thing he wanted.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңthe thing you desire begins with the letter N;
it is with N that we have to do, then. Well, let me see, what can you
want that begins with N? NaГўВҖВ”NeГўВҖВ”NiГўВҖВ”NoГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, yes,ГўВҖВқ said the old manГўВҖВҷs eye.

ГўВҖВңAh, it is No, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine fetched a dictionary, which she placed on a desk before
Noirtier; she opened it, and, seeing that the old manГўВҖВҷs eye was
thoroughly fixed on its pages, she ran her finger quickly up and down
the columns. During the six years which had passed since Noirtier first
fell into this sad state, ValentineГўВҖВҷs powers of invention had been too
often put to the test not to render her expert in devising expedients
for gaining a knowledge of his wishes, and the constant practice had so
perfected her in the art that she guessed the old manГўВҖВҷs meaning as
quickly as if he himself had been able to seek for what he wanted. At
the word _Notary_, Noirtier made a sign to her to stop.

30169m



ГўВҖВңNotary,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңdo you want a notary, dear grandpapa?ГўВҖВқ The old man
again signified that it was a notary he desired.

ГўВҖВңYou would wish a notary to be sent for then?ГўВҖВқ said Valentine.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall my father be informed of your wish?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you wish the notary to be sent for immediately?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen they shall go for him directly, dear grandpapa. Is that all you
want?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ Valentine rang the bell, and ordered the servant to tell
Monsieur or Madame de Villefort that they were requested to come to M.
NoirtierГўВҖВҷs room.

ГўВҖВңAre you satisfied now?ГўВҖВқ inquired Valentine.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am sure you are; it is not very difficult to discover that.ГўВҖВқ And the
young girl smiled on her grandfather, as if he had been a child. M. de
Villefort entered, followed by Barrois.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you want me for, sir?ГўВҖВқ demanded he of the paralytic.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңmy grandfather wishes for a notary.ГўВҖВқ At this
strange and unexpected demand M. de Villefort and his father exchanged
looks.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ motioned the latter, with a firmness which seemed to declare
that with the help of Valentine and his old servant, who both knew what
his wishes were, he was quite prepared to maintain the contest.

ГўВҖВңDo you wish for a notary?ГўВҖВқ asked Villefort.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat to do?ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier made no answer.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you want with a notary?ГўВҖВқ again repeated Villefort. The
invalidГўВҖВҷs eye remained fixed, by which expression he intended to
intimate that his resolution was unalterable.

ГўВҖВңIs it to do us some ill turn? Do you think it is worth while?ГўВҖВқ said
Villefort.

ГўВҖВңStill,ГўВҖВқ said Barrois, with the freedom and fidelity of an old servant,
ГўВҖВңif M. Noirtier asks for a notary, I suppose he really wishes for a
notary; therefore I shall go at once and fetch one.ГўВҖВқ Barrois
acknowledged no master but Noirtier, and never allowed his desires in
any way to be contradicted.

30171m



ГўВҖВңYes, I do want a notary,ГўВҖВқ motioned the old man, shutting his eyes with
a look of defiance, which seemed to say, ГўВҖВңand I should like to see the
person who dares to refuse my request.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou shall have a notary, as you absolutely wish for one, sir,ГўВҖВқ said
Villefort; ГўВҖВңbut I shall explain to him your state of health, and make
excuses for you, for the scene cannot fail of being a most ridiculous
one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mind that,ГўВҖВқ said Barrois; ГўВҖВңI shall go and fetch a notary,
nevertheless.ГўВҖВқ And the old servant departed triumphantly on his
mission.



 Chapter 59. The Will

As soon as Barrois had left the room, Noirtier looked at Valentine with
a malicious expression that said many things. The young girl perfectly
understood the look, and so did Villefort, for his countenance became
clouded, and he knitted his eyebrows angrily. He took a seat, and
quietly awaited the arrival of the notary. Noirtier saw him seat
himself with an appearance of perfect indifference, at the same time
giving a side look at Valentine, which made her understand that she
also was to remain in the room. Three-quarters of an hour after,
Barrois returned, bringing the notary with him.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, after the first salutations were over, ГўВҖВңyou were
sent for by M. Noirtier, whom you see here. All his limbs have become
completely paralysed, he has lost his voice also, and we ourselves find
much trouble in endeavoring to catch some fragments of his meaning.ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier cast an appealing look on Valentine, which look was at once so
earnest and imperative, that she answered immediately.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңI perfectly understand my grandfatherГўВҖВҷs meaning at
all times.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is quite true,ГўВҖВқ said Barrois; ГўВҖВңand that is what I told the
gentleman as we walked along.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPermit me,ГўВҖВқ said the notary, turning first to Villefort and then to
ValentineГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңpermit me to state that the case in question is just one of
those in which a public officer like myself cannot proceed to act
without thereby incurring a dangerous responsibility. The first thing
necessary to render an act valid is, that the notary should be
thoroughly convinced that he has faithfully interpreted the will and
wishes of the person dictating the act. Now I cannot be sure of the
approbation or disapprobation of a client who cannot speak, and as the
object of his desire or his repugnance cannot be clearly proved to me,
on account of his want of speech, my services here would be quite
useless, and cannot be legally exercised.ГўВҖВқ

The notary then prepared to retire. An imperceptible smile of triumph
was expressed on the lips of the procureur. Noirtier looked at
Valentine with an expression so full of grief, that she arrested the
departure of the notary.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңthe language which I speak with my grandfather may be
easily learnt, and I can teach you in a few minutes, to understand it
almost as well as I can myself. Will you tell me what you require, in
order to set your conscience quite at ease on the subject?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn order to render an act valid, I must be certain of the approbation
or disapprobation of my client. Illness of body would not affect the
validity of the deed, but sanity of mind is absolutely requisite.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sir, by the help of two signs, with which I will acquaint you
presently, you may ascertain with perfect certainty that my grandfather
is still in the full possession of all his mental faculties. M.
Noirtier, being deprived of voice and motion, is accustomed to convey
his meaning by closing his eyes when he wishes to signify ГўВҖВҳyes,ГўВҖВҷ and to
wink when he means ГўВҖВҳno.ГўВҖВҷ You now know quite enough to enable you to
converse with M. Noirtier;ГўВҖВ”try.ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier gave Valentine such a look of tenderness and gratitude that it
was comprehended even by the notary himself.

ГўВҖВңYou have heard and understood what your granddaughter has been saying,
sir, have you?ГўВҖВқ asked the notary. Noirtier closed his eyes.

ГўВҖВңAnd you approve of what she saidГўВҖВ”that is to say, you declare that the
signs which she mentioned are really those by means of which you are
accustomed to convey your thoughts?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was you who sent for me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo make your will?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you do not wish me to go away without fulfilling your original
intentions?ГўВҖВқ The old man winked violently.

ГўВҖВңWell, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the young girl, ГўВҖВңdo you understand now, and is your
conscience perfectly at rest on the subject?ГўВҖВқ

But before the notary could answer, Villefort had drawn him aside.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңdo you suppose for a moment that a man can sustain a
physical shock, such as M. Noirtier has received, without any detriment
to his mental faculties?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is not exactly that, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the notary, ГўВҖВңwhich makes me uneasy,
but the difficulty will be in wording his thoughts and intentions, so
as to be able to get his answers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou must see that to be an utter impossibility,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.
Valentine and the old man heard this conversation, and Noirtier fixed
his eye so earnestly on Valentine that she felt bound to answer to the
look.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңthat need not make you uneasy, however difficult it
may at first sight appear to be. I can discover and explain to you my
grandfatherГўВҖВҷs thoughts, so as to put an end to all your doubts and
fears on the subject. I have now been six years with M. Noirtier, and
let him tell you if ever once, during that time, he has entertained a
thought which he was unable to make me understand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ signed the old man.

ГўВҖВңLet us try what we can do, then,ГўВҖВқ said the notary. ГўВҖВңYou accept this
young lady as your interpreter, M. Noirtier?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sir, what do you require of me, and what document is it that you
wish to be drawn up?ГўВҖВқ

Valentine named all the letters of the alphabet until she came to W. At
this letter the eloquent eye of Noirtier gave her notice that she was
to stop.

ГўВҖВңIt is very evident that it is the letter W which M. Noirtier wants,ГўВҖВқ
said the notary.

ГўВҖВңWait,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine; and, turning to her grandfather, she repeated,
ГўВҖВңWaГўВҖВ”WeГўВҖВ”WiГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ The old man stopped her at the last syllable. Valentine
then took the dictionary, and the notary watched her while she turned
over the pages.

She passed her finger slowly down the columns, and when she came to the
word ГўВҖВңWill,ГўВҖВқ M. NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eye bade her stop.

ГўВҖВңWill,ГўВҖВқ said the notary; ГўВҖВңit is very evident that M. Noirtier is
desirous of making his will.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, yes,ГўВҖВқ motioned the invalid.

ГўВҖВңReally, sir, you must allow that this is most extraordinary,ГўВҖВқ said the
astonished notary, turning to M. de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said the procureur, ГўВҖВңand I think the will promises to be yet
more extraordinary, for I cannot see how it is to be drawn up without
the intervention of Valentine, and she may, perhaps, be considered as
too much interested in its contents to allow of her being a suitable
interpreter of the obscure and ill-defined wishes of her grandfather.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no, no,ГўВҖВқ replied the eye of the paralytic.

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңdo you mean to say that Valentine is not
interested in your will?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said the notary, whose interest had been greatly excited, and
who had resolved on publishing far and wide the account of this
extraordinary and picturesque scene, ГўВҖВңwhat appeared so impossible to me
an hour ago, has now become quite easy and practicable, and this may be
a perfectly valid will, provided it be read in the presence of seven
witnesses, approved by the testator, and sealed by the notary in the
presence of the witnesses. As to the time, it will not require very
much more than the generality of wills. There are certain forms
necessary to be gone through, and which are always the same. As to the
details, the greater part will be furnished afterwards by the state in
which we find the affairs of the testator, and by yourself, who, having
had the management of them, can doubtless give full information on the
subject. But besides all this, in order that the instrument may not be
contested, I am anxious to give it the greatest possible authenticity,
therefore, one of my colleagues will help me, and, contrary to custom,
will assist in the dictation of the testament. Are you satisfied, sir?ГўВҖВқ
continued the notary, addressing the old man.

30175m



ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ looked the invalid, his eye beaming with delight at the ready
interpretation of his meaning.

ГўВҖВңWhat is he going to do?ГўВҖВқ thought Villefort, whose position demanded
much reserve, but who was longing to know what his fatherГўВҖВҷs intentions
were. He left the room to give orders for another notary to be sent,
but Barrois, who had heard all that passed, had guessed his masterГўВҖВҷs
wishes, and had already gone to fetch one. The procureur then told his
wife to come up. In the course of a quarter of an hour everyone had
assembled in the chamber of the paralytic; the second notary had also
arrived.

A few words sufficed for a mutual understanding between the two
officers of the law. They read to Noirtier the formal copy of a will,
in order to give him an idea of the terms in which such documents are
generally couched; then, in order to test the capacity of the testator,
the first notary said, turning towards him:

ГўВҖВңWhen an individual makes his will, it is generally in favor or in
prejudice of some person.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you an exact idea of the amount of your fortune?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will name to you several sums which will increase by gradation; you
will stop me when I reach the one representing the amount of your own
possessions?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

There was a kind of solemnity in this interrogation. Never had the
struggle between mind and matter been more apparent than now, and if it
was not a sublime, it was, at least, a curious spectacle. They had
formed a circle round the invalid; the second notary was sitting at a
table, prepared for writing, and his colleague was standing before the
testator in the act of interrogating him on the subject to which we
have alluded.

ГўВҖВңYour fortune exceeds 300,000 francs, does it not?ГўВҖВқ asked he. Noirtier
made a sign that it did.

ГўВҖВңDo you possess 400,000 francs?ГўВҖВқ inquired the notary. NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eye
remained immovable.

ГўВҖВң500,000?ГўВҖВқ The same expression continued.

ГўВҖВң600,000ГўВҖВ”700,000ГўВҖВ”800,000ГўВҖВ”900,000?ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier stopped him at the last-named sum.

ГўВҖВңYou are then in possession of 900,000 francs?ГўВҖВқ asked the notary.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn landed property?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn stock?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe stock is in your own hands?ГўВҖВқ

The look which M. Noirtier cast on Barrois showed that there was
something wanting which he knew where to find. The old servant left the
room, and presently returned, bringing with him a small casket.

ГўВҖВңDo you permit us to open this casket?ГўВҖВқ asked the notary. Noirtier gave
his assent.

They opened it, and found 900,000 francs in bank scrip. The first
notary handed over each note, as he examined it, to his colleague.

The total amount was found to be as M. Noirtier had stated.

ГўВҖВңIt is all as he has said; it is very evident that the mind still
retains its full force and vigor.ГўВҖВқ Then, turning towards the paralytic,
he said, ГўВҖВңYou possess, then, 900,000 francs of capital, which,
according to the manner in which you have invested it, ought to bring
in an income of about 40,000 livres?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo whom do you desire to leave this fortune?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh!ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңthere is not much doubt on that
subject. M. Noirtier tenderly loves his granddaughter, Mademoiselle de
Villefort; it is she who has nursed and tended him for six years, and
has, by her devoted attention, fully secured the affection, I had
almost said the gratitude, of her grandfather, and it is but just that
she should reap the fruit of her devotion.ГўВҖВқ

The eye of Noirtier clearly showed by its expression that he was not
deceived by the false assent given by Madame de VillefortГўВҖВҷs words and
manner to the motives which she supposed him to entertain.

ГўВҖВңIs it, then, to Mademoiselle Valentine de Villefort that you leave
these 900,000 francs?ГўВҖВқ demanded the notary, thinking he had only to
insert this clause, but waiting first for the assent of Noirtier, which
it was necessary should be given before all the witnesses of this
singular scene.

Valentine, when her name was made the subject of discussion, had
stepped back, to escape unpleasant observation; her eyes were cast
down, and she was crying. The old man looked at her for an instant with
an expression of the deepest tenderness, then, turning towards the
notary, he significantly winked his eye in token of dissent.

ГўВҖВңWhat,ГўВҖВқ said the notary, ГўВҖВңdo you not intend making Mademoiselle
Valentine de Villefort your residuary legatee?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are not making any mistake, are you?ГўВҖВқ said the notary; ГўВҖВңyou really
mean to declare that such is not your intention?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ repeated Noirtier; ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine raised her head, struck dumb with astonishment. It was not so
much the conviction that she was disinherited that caused her grief,
but her total inability to account for the feelings which had provoked
her grandfather to such an act. But Noirtier looked at her with so much
affectionate tenderness that she exclaimed:

ГўВҖВңOh, grandpapa, I see now that it is only your fortune of which you
deprive me; you still leave me the love which I have always enjoyed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, yes, most assuredly,ГўВҖВқ said the eyes of the paralytic, for he
closed them with an expression which Valentine could not mistake.

ГўВҖВңThank you, thank you,ГўВҖВқ murmured she. The old manГўВҖВҷs declaration that
Valentine was not the destined inheritor of his fortune had excited the
hopes of Madame de Villefort; she gradually approached the invalid, and
said:

ГўВҖВңThen, doubtless, dear M. Noirtier, you intend leaving your fortune to
your grandson, Edward de Villefort?ГўВҖВқ

The winking of the eyes which answered this speech was most decided and
terrible, and expressed a feeling almost amounting to hatred.

ГўВҖВңNo?ГўВҖВқ said the notary; ГўВҖВңthen, perhaps, it is to your son, M. de
Villefort?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ The two notaries looked at each other in mute astonishment and
inquiry as to what were the real intentions of the testator. Villefort
and his wife both grew red, one from shame, the other from anger.

ГўВҖВңWhat have we all done, then, dear grandpapa?ГўВҖВқ said Valentine; ГўВҖВңyou no
longer seem to love any of us?ГўВҖВқ

The old manГўВҖВҷs eyes passed rapidly from Villefort and his wife, and
rested on Valentine with a look of unutterable fondness.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said she; ГўВҖВңif you love me, grandpapa, try and bring that love
to bear upon your actions at this present moment. You know me well
enough to be quite sure that I have never thought of your fortune;
besides, they say I am already rich in right of my motherГўВҖВ”too rich,
even. Explain yourself, then.ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier fixed his intelligent eyes on ValentineГўВҖВҷs hand.

ГўВҖВңMy hand?ГўВҖВқ said she.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHer hand!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed everyone.

ГўВҖВңOh, gentlemen, you see it is all useless, and that my fatherГўВҖВҷs mind is
really impaired,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ cried Valentine suddenly, ГўВҖВңI understand. It is my marriage you
mean, is it not, dear grandpapa?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, yes,ГўВҖВқ signed the paralytic, casting on Valentine a look of
joyful gratitude for having guessed his meaning.

30179m



ГўВҖВңYou are angry with us all on account of this marriage, are you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally, this is too absurd,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңExcuse me, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the notary; ГўВҖВңon the contrary, the meaning of
M. Noirtier is quite evident to me, and I can quite easily connect the
train of ideas passing in his mind.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do not wish me to marry M. Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay?ГўВҖВқ observed Valentine.

ГўВҖВңI do not wish it,ГўВҖВқ said the eye of her grandfather.

ГўВҖВңAnd you disinherit your granddaughter,ГўВҖВқ continued the notary, ГўВҖВңbecause
she has contracted an engagement contrary to your wishes?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that, but for this marriage, she would have been your heir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

There was a profound silence. The two notaries were holding a
consultation as to the best means of proceeding with the affair.
Valentine was looking at her grandfather with a smile of intense
gratitude, and Villefort was biting his lips with vexation, while
Madame de Villefort could not succeed in repressing an inward feeling
of joy, which, in spite of herself, appeared in her whole countenance.

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, who was the first to break the silence, ГўВҖВңI
consider that I am the best judge of the propriety of the marriage in
question. I am the only person possessing the right to dispose of my
daughterГўВҖВҷs hand. It is my wish that she should marry M. Franz
dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinayГўВҖВ”and she shall marry him.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine sank weeping into a chair.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said the notary, ГўВҖВңhow do you intend disposing of your fortune in
case Mademoiselle de Villefort still determines on marrying M. Franz?ГўВҖВқ
The old man gave no answer.

ГўВҖВңYou will, of course, dispose of it in some way or other?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn favor of some member of your family?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you intend devoting it to charitable purposes, then?ГўВҖВқ pursued the
notary.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said the notary, ГўВҖВңyou are aware that the law does not allow a
son to be entirely deprived of his patrimony?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou only intend, then, to dispose of that part of your fortune which
the law allows you to subtract from the inheritance of your son?ГўВҖВқ
Noirtier made no answer.

ГўВҖВңDo you still wish to dispose of all?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut they will contest the will after your death?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy father knows me,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort; ГўВҖВңhe is quite sure that his
wishes will be held sacred by me; besides, he understands that in my
position I cannot plead against the poor.ГўВҖВқ The eye of Noirtier beamed
with triumph.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you decide on, sir?ГўВҖВқ asked the notary of Villefort.

ГўВҖВңNothing, sir; it is a resolution which my father has taken and I know
he never alters his mind. I am quite resigned. These 900,000 francs
will go out of the family in order to enrich some hospital; but it is
ridiculous thus to yield to the caprices of an old man, and I shall,
therefore, act according to my conscience.ГўВҖВқ

Having said this, Villefort quitted the room with his wife, leaving his
father at liberty to do as he pleased. The same day the will was made,
the witnesses were brought, it was approved by the old man, sealed in
the presence of all and given in charge to M. Deschamps, the family
notary.
