 Chapter 60. The Telegraph

M. and Madame de Villefort found on their return that the Count of
Monte Cristo, who had come to visit them in their absence, had been
ushered into the drawing-room, and was still awaiting them there.
Madame de Villefort, who had not yet sufficiently recovered from her
late emotion to allow of her entertaining visitors so immediately,
retired to her bedroom, while the procureur, who could better depend
upon himself, proceeded at once to the salon.

Although M. de Villefort flattered himself that, to all outward view,
he had completely masked the feelings which were passing in his mind,
he did not know that the cloud was still lowering on his brow, so much
so that the count, whose smile was radiant, immediately noticed his
sombre and thoughtful air.

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi!_ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, after the first compliments were over,
ГўВҖВңwhat is the matter with you, M. de Villefort? Have I arrived at the
moment when you were drawing up an indictment for a capital crime?ГўВҖВқ

Villefort tried to smile.

ГўВҖВңNo, count,ГўВҖВқ he replied, ГўВҖВңI am the only victim in this case. It is I
who lose my cause, and it is ill-luck, obstinacy, and folly which have
caused it to be decided against me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo what do you refer?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with well-feigned interest.
ГўВҖВңHave you really met with some great misfortune?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort with a bitter smile; ГўВҖВңit is only a
loss of money which I have sustainedГўВҖВ”nothing worth mentioning, I assure
you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthe loss of a sum of money becomes almost
immaterial with a fortune such as you possess, and to one of your
philosophic spirit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is not so much the loss of the money that vexes me,ГўВҖВқ said
Villefort, ГўВҖВңthough, after all, 900,000 francs are worth regretting; but
I am the more annoyed with this fate, chance, or whatever you please to
call the power which has destroyed my hopes and my fortune, and may
blast the prospects of my child also, as it is all occasioned by an old
man relapsed into second childhood.ГўВҖВқ

30183m



ГўВҖВңWhat do you say?ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВң900,000 francs? It is indeed a sum
which might be regretted even by a philosopher. And who is the cause of
all this annoyance?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy father, as I told you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. Noirtier? But I thought you told me he had become entirely
paralyzed, and that all his faculties were completely destroyed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, his bodily faculties, for he can neither move nor speak,
nevertheless he thinks, acts, and wills in the manner I have described.
I left him about five minutes ago, and he is now occupied in dictating
his will to two notaries.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut to do this he must have spoken?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe has done better than thatГўВҖВ”he has made himself understood.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow was such a thing possible?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy the help of his eyes, which are still full of life, and, as you
perceive, possess the power of inflicting mortal injury.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, who had just entered the room,
ГўВҖВңperhaps you exaggerate the evil.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGood-morning, madame,ГўВҖВқ said the count, bowing.



Madame de Villefort acknowledged the salutation with one of her most
gracious smiles.

ГўВҖВңWhat is this that M. de Villefort has been telling me?ГўВҖВқ demanded Monte
Cristo ГўВҖВңand what incomprehensible misfortuneГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIncomprehensible is the word!ГўВҖВқ interrupted the procureur, shrugging
his shoulders. ГўВҖВңIt is an old manГўВҖВҷs caprice!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd is there no means of making him revoke his decision?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort; ГўВҖВңand it is still entirely in the power
of my husband to cause the will, which is now in prejudice of
Valentine, to be altered in her favor.ГўВҖВқ

The count, who perceived that M. and Madame de Villefort were beginning
to speak in parables, appeared to pay no attention to the conversation,
and feigned to be busily engaged in watching Edward, who was
mischievously pouring some ink into the birdГўВҖВҷs water-glass.

ГўВҖВңMy dear,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, in answer to his wife, ГўВҖВңyou know I have
never been accustomed to play the patriarch in my family, nor have I
ever considered that the fate of a universe was to be decided by my
nod. Nevertheless, it is necessary that my will should be respected in
my family, and that the folly of an old man and the caprice of a child
should not be allowed to overturn a project which I have entertained
for so many years. The Baron dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay was my friend, as you know, and
an alliance with his son is the most suitable thing that could possibly
be arranged.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңthat Valentine is in league
with him? She has always been opposed to this marriage, and I should
not be at all surprised if what we have just seen and heard is nothing
but the execution of a plan concerted between them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңbelieve me, a fortune of 900,000 francs is
not so easily renounced.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShe could, nevertheless, make up her mind to renounce the world, sir,
since it is only about a year ago that she herself proposed entering a
convent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mind,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort; ГўВҖВңI say that this marriage _shall_ be
consummated.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNotwithstanding your fatherГўВҖВҷs wishes to the contrary?ГўВҖВқ said Madame de
Villefort, selecting a new point of attack. ГўВҖВңThat is a serious thing.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo, who pretended not to be listening, heard however, every
word that was said.

30185m



ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort ГўВҖВңI can truly say that I have always
entertained a high respect for my father, because, to the natural
feeling of relationship was added the consciousness of his moral
superiority. The name of father is sacred in two senses; he should be
reverenced as the author of our being and as a master whom we ought to
obey. But, under the present circumstances, I am justified in doubting
the wisdom of an old man who, because he hated the father, vents his
anger on the son. It would be ridiculous in me to regulate my conduct
by such caprices. I shall still continue to preserve the same respect
toward M. Noirtier; I will suffer, without complaint, the pecuniary
deprivation to which he has subjected me; but I shall remain firm in my
determination, and the world shall see which party has reason on his
side. Consequently I shall marry my daughter to the Baron Franz
dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, because I consider it would be a proper and eligible match
for her to make, and, in short, because I choose to bestow my
daughterГўВҖВҷs hand on whomever I please.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ said the count, the approbation of whose eye Villefort had
frequently solicited during this speech. ГўВҖВңWhat? Do you say that M.
Noirtier disinherits Mademoiselle de Villefort because she is going to
marry M. le Baron Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir, that is the reason,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, shrugging his
shoulders.

ГўВҖВңThe apparent reason, at least,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңThe _real_ reason, madame, I can assure you; I know my father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut I want to know in what way M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay can have displeased your
father more than any other person?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI believe I know M. Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңis he not the
son of General de Quesnel, who was created Baron dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay by Charles
X.?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe same,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңWell, but he is a charming young man, according to my ideas.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is, which makes me believe that it is only an excuse of M. Noirtier
to prevent his granddaughter marrying; old men are always so selfish in
their affection,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңdo you not know any cause for this hatred?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, _ma foi!_ who is to know?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerhaps it is some political difference?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy father and the Baron dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay lived in the stormy times of which I
only saw the ending,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңWas not your father a Bonapartist?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI think I
remember that you told me something of that kind.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy father has been a Jacobin more than anything else,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort,
carried by his emotion beyond the bounds of prudence; ГўВҖВңand the
senatorГўВҖВҷs robe, which Napoleon cast on his shoulders, only served to
disguise the old man without in any degree changing him. When my father
conspired, it was not for the emperor, it was against the Bourbons; for
M. Noirtier possessed this peculiarity, he never projected any Utopian
schemes which could never be realized, but strove for possibilities,
and he applied to the realization of these possibilities the terrible
theories of The Mountain,ГўВҖВ”theories that never shrank from any means
that were deemed necessary to bring about the desired result.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңit is just as I thought; it was politics
which brought Noirtier and M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay into personal contact. Although
General dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay served under Napoleon, did he not still retain
royalist sentiments? And was he not the person who was assassinated one
evening on leaving a Bonapartist meeting to which he had been invited
on the supposition that he favored the cause of the emperor?ГўВҖВқ

Villefort looked at the count almost with terror.

ГўВҖВңAm I mistaken, then?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңNo, sir, the facts were precisely what you have stated,ГўВҖВқ said Madame
de Villefort; ГўВҖВңand it was to prevent the renewal of old feuds that M.
de Villefort formed the idea of uniting in the bonds of affection the
two children of these inveterate enemies.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was a sublime and charitable thought,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңand the
whole world should applaud it. It would be noble to see Mademoiselle
Noirtier de Villefort assuming the title of Madame Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort shuddered and looked at Monte Cristo as if he wished to read
in his countenance the real feelings which had dictated the words he
had just uttered. But the count completely baffled the procureur, and
prevented him from discovering anything beneath the never-varying smile
he was so constantly in the habit of assuming.

ГўВҖВңAlthough,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңit will be a serious thing for Valentine
to lose her grandfatherГўВҖВҷs fortune, I do not think that M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay will
be frightened at this pecuniary loss. He will, perhaps, hold me in
greater esteem than the money itself, seeing that I sacrifice
everything in order to keep my word with him. Besides, he knows that
Valentine is rich in right of her mother, and that she will, in all
probability, inherit the fortune of M. and Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran, her
motherГўВҖВҷs parents, who both love her tenderly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd who are fully as well worth loving and tending as M. Noirtier,ГўВҖВқ
said Madame de Villefort; ГўВҖВңbesides, they are to come to Paris in about
a month, and Valentine, after the affront she has received, need not
consider it necessary to continue to bury herself alive by being shut
up with M. Noirtier.ГўВҖВқ

The count listened with satisfaction to this tale of wounded self-love
and defeated ambition.

ГўВҖВңBut it seems to me,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңand I must begin by asking
your pardon for what I am about to say, that if M. Noirtier disinherits
Mademoiselle de Villefort because she is going to marry a man whose
father he detested, he cannot have the same cause of complaint against
this dear Edward.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, with an intonation of voice which it
is impossible to describe; ГўВҖВңis it not unjustГўВҖВ”shamefully unjust? Poor
Edward is as much M. NoirtierГўВҖВҷs grandchild as Valentine, and yet, if
she had not been going to marry M. Franz, M. Noirtier would have left
her all his money; and supposing Valentine to be disinherited by her
grandfather, she will still be three times richer than he.ГўВҖВқ

The count listened and said no more.

ГўВҖВңCount,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңwe will not entertain you any longer with our
family misfortunes. It is true that my patrimony will go to endow
charitable institutions, and my father will have deprived me of my
lawful inheritance without any reason for doing so, but I shall have
the satisfaction of knowing that I have acted like a man of sense and
feeling. M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, to whom I had promised the interest of this sum,
shall receive it, even if I endure the most cruel privations.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHowever,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, returning to the one idea which
incessantly occupied her mind, ГўВҖВңperhaps it would be better to explain
this unlucky affair to M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, in order to give him the
opportunity of himself renouncing his claim to the hand of Mademoiselle
de Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, that would be a great pity,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңA great pity,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңUndoubtedly,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, moderating the tones of his voice, ГўВҖВңa
marriage once concerted and then broken off, throws a sort of discredit
on a young lady; then again, the old reports, which I was so anxious to
put an end to, will instantly gain ground. No, it will all go well; M.
dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, if he is an honorable man, will consider himself more than
ever pledged to Mademoiselle de Villefort, unless he were actuated by a
decided feeling of avarice, but that is impossible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI agree with M. de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, fixing his eyes on
Madame de Villefort; ГўВҖВңand if I were sufficiently intimate with him to
allow of giving my advice, I would persuade him, since I have been told
M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay is coming back, to settle this affair at once beyond all
possibility of revocation. I will answer for the success of a project
which will reflect so much honor on M. de Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

The procureur arose, delighted with the proposition, but his wife
slightly changed color.

ГўВҖВңWell, that is all that I wanted, and I will be guided by a counsellor
such as you are,ГўВҖВқ said he, extending his hand to Monte Cristo.
ГўВҖВңTherefore let everyone here look upon what has passed today as if it
had not happened, and as though we had never thought of such a thing as
a change in our original plans.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңthe world, unjust as it is, will be pleased
with your resolution; your friends will be proud of you, and M.
dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, even if he took Mademoiselle de Villefort without any dowry,
which he will not do, would be delighted with the idea of entering a
family which could make such sacrifices in order to keep a promise and
fulfil a duty.ГўВҖВқ

At the conclusion of these words, the count rose to depart.

ГўВҖВңAre you going to leave us, count?ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңI am sorry to say I must do so, madame, I only came to remind you of
your promise for Saturday.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you fear that we should forget it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are very good, madame, but M. de Villefort has so many important
and urgent occupations.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy husband has given me his word, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort; ГўВҖВңyou
have just seen him resolve to keep it when he has everything to lose,
and surely there is more reason for his doing so where he has
everything to gain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңis it at your house in the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es that
you receive your visitors?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңwhich is precisely the reason which renders
your kindness more meritorious,ГўВҖВ”it is in the country.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn the country?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere is it, then? Near Paris, is it not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery near, only half a league from the Barriers,ГўВҖВ”it is at Auteuil.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt Auteuil?ГўВҖВқ said Villefort; ГўВҖВңtrue, Madame de Villefort told me you
lived at Auteuil, since it was to your house that she was taken. And in
what part of Auteuil do you reside?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRue de la Fontaine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRue de la Fontaine!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Villefort in an agitated tone; ГўВҖВңat what
number?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo. 28.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort, ГўВҖВңwas it you who bought M. de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs
house!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid it belong to M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran?ГўВҖВқ demanded Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Madame de Villefort; ГўВҖВңand, would you believe it,
countГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBelieve what?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou think this house pretty, do you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think it charming.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, my husband would never live in it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ returned Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthat is a prejudice on your part, M.
de Villefort, for which I am quite at a loss to account.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not like Auteuil, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the procureur, making an evident
effort to appear calm.

ГўВҖВңBut I hope you will not carry your antipathy so far as to deprive me
of the pleasure of your company, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңNo, count,ГўВҖВ”I hopeГўВҖВ”I assure you I shall do my best,ГўВҖВқ stammered
Villefort.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI allow of no excuse. On Saturday, at six
oГўВҖВҷclock. I shall be expecting you, and if you fail to come, I shall
thinkГўВҖВ”for how do I know to the contrary?ГўВҖВ”that this house, which has
remained uninhabited for twenty years, must have some gloomy tradition
or dreadful legend connected with it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will come, count,ГўВҖВ”I will be sure to come,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort eagerly.

ГўВҖВңThank you,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңnow you must permit me to take my
leave of you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou said before that you were obliged to leave us, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said
Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңand you were about to tell us why when your
attention was called to some other subject.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed madame,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo: ГўВҖВңI scarcely know if I dare tell you
where I am going.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNonsense; say on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, it is to see a thing on which I have sometimes mused for
hours together.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA telegraph. So now I have told my secret.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA telegraph?ГўВҖВқ repeated Madame de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңYes, a telegraph. I had often seen one placed at the end of a road on
a hillock, and in the light of the sun its black arms, bending in every
direction, always reminded me of the claws of an immense beetle, and I
assure you it was never without emotion that I gazed on it, for I could
not help thinking how wonderful it was that these various signs should
be made to cleave the air with such precision as to convey to the
distance of three hundred leagues the ideas and wishes of a man sitting
at a table at one end of the line to another man similarly placed at
the opposite extremity, and all this effected by a simple act of
volition on the part of the sender of the message. I began to think of
genii, sylphs, gnomes, in short, of all the ministers of the occult
sciences, until I laughed aloud at the freaks of my own imagination.
Now, it never occurred to me to wish for a nearer inspection of these
large insects, with their long black claws, for I always feared to find
under their stone wings some little human genius fagged to death with
cabals, factions, and government intrigues. But one fine day I learned
that the mover of this telegraph was only a poor wretch, hired for
twelve hundred francs a year, and employed all day, not in studying the
heavens like an astronomer, or in gazing on the water like an angler,
or even in enjoying the privilege of observing the country around him,
but all his monotonous life was passed in watching his white-bellied,
black-clawed fellow insect, four or five leagues distant from him. At
length I felt a desire to study this living chrysalis more closely, and
to endeavor to understand the secret part played by these insect-actors
when they occupy themselves simply with pulling different pieces of
string.ГўВҖВқ

30191m



ГўВҖВңAnd are you going there?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat telegraph do you intend visiting? that of the home department, or
of the observatory?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no; I should find there people who would force me to understand
things of which I would prefer to remain ignorant, and who would try to
explain to me, in spite of myself, a mystery which even they do not
understand. _Ma foi!_ I should wish to keep my illusions concerning
insects unimpaired; it is quite enough to have those dissipated which I
had formed of my fellow-creatures. I shall, therefore, not visit either
of these telegraphs, but one in the open country where I shall find a
good-natured simpleton, who knows no more than the machine he is
employed to work.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are a singular man,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңWhat line would you advise me to study?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe one that is most in use just at this time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe Spanish one, you mean, I suppose?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; should you like a letter to the minister that they might explain
to youГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңsince, as I told you before, I do not wish to
comprehend it. The moment I understand it there will no longer exist a
telegraph for me; it will be nothing more than a sign from M. DuchГғВўtel,
or from M. Montalivet, transmitted to the prefect of Bayonne, mystified
by two Greek words, _tГғВӘle_, _graphein_. It is the insect with black
claws, and the awful word which I wish to retain in my imagination in
all its purity and all its importance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo then; for in the course of two hours it will be dark, and you will
not be able to see anything.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi!_ you frighten me. Which is the nearest way? Bayonne?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; the road to Bayonne.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd afterwards the road to ChГғВўtillon?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy the tower of MontlhГғВ©ry, you mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you. Good-bye. On Saturday I will tell you my impressions
concerning the telegraph.ГўВҖВқ

At the door the count was met by the two notaries, who had just
completed the act which was to disinherit Valentine, and who were
leaving under the conviction of having done a thing which could not
fail of redounding considerably to their credit.



 Chapter 61. How a Gardener May Get Rid of the Dormice that Eat His
 Peaches

Not on the same night as he had stated, but the next morning, the Count
of Monte Cristo went out by the BarriГғВЁre dГўВҖВҷEnfer, taking the road to
OrlГғВ©ans. Leaving the village of Linas, without stopping at the
telegraph, which flourished its great bony arms as he passed, the count
reached the tower of MontlhГғВ©ry, situated, as everyone knows, upon the
highest point of the plain of that name. At the foot of the hill the
count dismounted and began to ascend by a little winding path, about
eighteen inches wide; when he reached the summit he found himself
stopped by a hedge, upon which green fruit had succeeded to red and
white flowers.

Monte Cristo looked for the entrance to the enclosure, and was not long
in finding a little wooden gate, working on willow hinges, and fastened
with a nail and string. The count soon mastered the mechanism, the gate
opened, and he then found himself in a little garden, about twenty feet
long by twelve wide, bounded on one side by part of the hedge, which
contained the ingenious contrivance we have called a gate, and on the
other by the old tower, covered with ivy and studded with wall-flowers.

No one would have thought in looking at this old, weather-beaten,
floral-decked tower (which might be likened to an elderly dame dressed
up to receive her grandchildren at a birthday feast) that it would have
been capable of telling strange things, if,ГўВҖВ”in addition to the menacing
ears which the proverb says all walls are provided with,ГўВҖВ”it had also a
voice.

The garden was crossed by a path of red gravel, edged by a border of
thick box, of many yearsГўВҖВҷ growth, and of a tone and color that would
have delighted the heart of Delacroix, our modern Rubens. This path was
formed in the shape of the figure of 8, thus, in its windings, making a
walk of sixty feet in a garden of only twenty.

Never had Flora, the fresh and smiling goddess of gardeners, been
honored with a purer or more scrupulous worship than that which was
paid to her in this little enclosure. In fact, of the twenty rose-trees
which formed the _parterre_, not one bore the mark of the slug, nor
were there evidences anywhere of the clustering aphis which is so
destructive to plants growing in a damp soil. And yet it was not
because the damp had been excluded from the garden; the earth, black as
soot, the thick foliage of the trees betrayed its presence; besides,
had natural humidity been wanting, it could have been immediately
supplied by artificial means, thanks to a tank of water, sunk in one of
the corners of the garden, and upon which were stationed a frog and a
toad, who, from antipathy, no doubt, always remained on the two
opposite sides of the basin. There was not a blade of grass to be seen
in the paths, or a weed in the flower-beds; no fine lady ever trained
and watered her geraniums, her cacti, and her rhododendrons, in her
porcelain _jardiniГғВЁre_ with more pains than this hitherto unseen
gardener bestowed upon his little enclosure.

Monte Cristo stopped after having closed the gate and fastened the
string to the nail, and cast a look around.

ГўВҖВңThe man at the telegraph,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңmust either engage a gardener or
devote himself passionately to agriculture.ГўВҖВқ

Suddenly he struck against something crouching behind a wheelbarrow
filled with leaves; the something rose, uttering an exclamation of
astonishment, and Monte Cristo found himself facing a man about fifty
years old, who was plucking strawberries, which he was placing upon
grape leaves. He had twelve leaves and about as many strawberries,
which, on rising suddenly, he let fall from his hand.

ГўВҖВңYou are gathering your crop, sir?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, smiling.

30193m



ГўВҖВңExcuse me, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the man, raising his hand to his cap; ГўВҖВңI am
not up there, I know, but I have only just come down.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not let me interfere with you in anything, my friend,ГўВҖВқ said the
count; ГўВҖВңgather your strawberries, if, indeed, there are any left.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have ten left,ГўВҖВқ said the man, ГўВҖВңfor here are eleven, and I had
twenty-one, five more than last year. But I am not surprised; the
spring has been warm this year, and strawberries require heat, sir.
This is the reason that, instead of the sixteen I had last year, I have
this year, you see, eleven, already pluckedГўВҖВ”twelve, thirteen, fourteen,
fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen. Ah, I miss three, they were here
last night, sirГўВҖВ”I am sure they were hereГўВҖВ”I counted them. It must be the
son of MГғВЁre Simon who has stolen them; I saw him strolling about here
this morning. Ah, the young rascalГўВҖВ”stealing in a gardenГўВҖВ”he does not
know where that may lead him to.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly, it is wrong,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңbut you should take into
consideration the youth and greediness of the delinquent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf course,ГўВҖВқ said the gardener, ГўВҖВңbut that does not make it the less
unpleasant. But, sir, once more I beg pardon; perhaps you are an
officer that I am detaining here.ГўВҖВқ And he glanced timidly at the
countГўВҖВҷs blue coat.

30197m



ГўВҖВңCalm yourself, my friend,ГўВҖВқ said the count, with the smile which he
made at will either terrible or benevolent, and which now expressed
only the kindliest feeling; ГўВҖВңI am not an inspector, but a traveller,
brought here by a curiosity he half repents of, since he causes you to
lose your time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, my time is not valuable,ГўВҖВқ replied the man with a melancholy smile.
ГўВҖВңStill it belongs to government, and I ought not to waste it; but,
having received the signal that I might rest for an hourГўВҖВқ (here he
glanced at the sun-dial, for there was everything in the enclosure of
MontlhГғВ©ry, even a sun-dial), ГўВҖВңand having ten minutes before me, and my
strawberries being ripe, when a day longerГўВҖВ”by-the-by, sir, do you think
dormice eat them?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed, I should think not,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңdormice are bad
neighbors for us who do not eat them preserved, as the Romans did.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? Did the Romans eat them?ГўВҖВқ said the gardenerГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңate dormice?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have read so in Petronius,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңReally? They canГўВҖВҷt be nice, though they do say ГўВҖВҳas fat as a dormouse.ГўВҖВҷ
It is not a wonder they are fat, sleeping all day, and only waking to
eat all night. Listen. Last year I had four apricotsГўВҖВ”they stole one, I
had one nectarine, only oneГўВҖВ”well, sir, they ate half of it on the wall;
a splendid nectarineГўВҖВ”I never ate a better.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou ate it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is to say, the half that was leftГўВҖВ”you understand; it was
exquisite, sir. Ah, those gentlemen never choose the worst morsels;
like MГғВЁre SimonГўВҖВҷs son, who has not chosen the worst strawberries. But
this year,ГўВҖВқ continued the horticulturist, ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВҷll take care it shall not
happen, even if I should be forced to sit by the whole night to watch
when the strawberries are ripe.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo had seen enough. Every man has a devouring passion in his
heart, as every fruit has its worm; that of the telegraph man was
horticulture. He began gathering the grape-leaves which screened the
sun from the grapes, and won the heart of the gardener.

ГўВҖВңDid you come here, sir, to see the telegraph?ГўВҖВқ he said.

ГўВҖВңYes, if it isnГўВҖВҷt contrary to the rules.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no,ГўВҖВқ said the gardener; ГўВҖВңnot in the least, since there is no
danger that anyone can possibly understand what we are saying.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have been told,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңthat you do not always yourselves
understand the signals you repeat.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is true, sir, and that is what I like best,ГўВҖВқ said the man,
smiling.

ГўВҖВңWhy do you like that best?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause then I have no responsibility. I am a machine then, and
nothing else, and so long as I work, nothing more is required of me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs it possible,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo to himself, ГўВҖВңthat I can have met
with a man that has no ambition? That would spoil my plans.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said the gardener, glancing at the sun-dial, ГўВҖВңthe ten minutes
are almost up; I must return to my post. Will you go up with me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI follow you.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo entered the tower, which was divided into three stories.
The tower contained implements, such as spades, rakes, watering-pots,
hung against the wall; this was all the furniture. The second was the
manГўВҖВҷs conventional abode, or rather sleeping-place; it contained a few
poor articles of household furnitureГўВҖВ”a bed, a table, two chairs, a
stone pitcherГўВҖВ”and some dry herbs, hung up to the ceiling, which the
count recognized as sweet peas, and of which the good man was
preserving the seeds; he had labelled them with as much care as if he
had been master botanist in the Jardin des Plantes.

ГўВҖВңDoes it require much study to learn the art of telegraphing?ГўВҖВқ asked
Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңThe study does not take long; it was acting as a supernumerary that
was so tedious.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what is the pay?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA thousand francs, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is nothing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; but then we are lodged, as you perceive.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo looked at the room. They passed to the third story; it was
the telegraph room. Monte Cristo looked in turn at the two iron handles
by which the machine was worked. ГўВҖВңIt is very interesting,ГўВҖВқ he said,
ГўВҖВңbut it must be very tedious for a lifetime.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes. At first my neck was cramped with looking at it, but at the end
of a year I became used to it; and then we have our hours of
recreation, and our holidays.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHolidays?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen we have a fog.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, to be sure.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThose are indeed holidays to me; I go into the garden, I plant, I
prune, I trim, I kill the insects all day long.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow long have you been here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTen years, and five as a supernumerary make fifteen.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou areГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFifty-five years old.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow long must you have served to claim the pension?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, sir, twenty-five years.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd how much is the pension?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA hundred crowns.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoor humanity!ГўВҖВқ murmured Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңWhat did you say, sir?ГўВҖВқ asked the man.

ГўВҖВңI was saying it was very interesting.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat was?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll you were showing me. And you really understand none of these
signals?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNone at all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd have you never tried to understand them?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever. Why should I?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut still there are some signals only addressed to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd do you understand them?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey are always the same.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd they meanГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳ_Nothing new; You have an hour;_ГўВҖВҷ or ГўВҖВҳ_Tomorrow_.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is simple enough,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңbut look, is not your
correspondent putting itself in motion?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, yes; thank you, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what is it sayingГўВҖВ”anything you understand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; it asks if I am ready.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you reply?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy the same sign, which, at the same time, tells my right-hand
correspondent that I am ready, while it gives notice to my left-hand
correspondent to prepare in his turn.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is very ingenious,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңYou will see,ГўВҖВқ said the man proudly; ГўВҖВңin five minutes he will speak.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have, then, five minutes,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo to himself; ГўВҖВңit is more
time than I require. My dear sir, will you allow me to ask you a
question?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it, sir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are fond of gardening?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPassionately.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you would be pleased to have, instead of this terrace of twenty
feet, an enclosure of two acres?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, I should make a terrestrial paradise of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou live badly on your thousand francs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBadly enough; but yet I do live.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but you have a wretchedly small garden.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue, the garden is not large.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd, then, such as it is, it is filled with dormice, who eat
everything.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, they are my scourges.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell me, should you have the misfortune to turn your head while your
right-hand correspondent was telegraphingГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI should not see him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen what would happen?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI could not repeat the signals.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot having repeated them, through negligence, I should be fined.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow much?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA hundred francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe tenth of your incomeГўВҖВ”that would be fine work.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh!ГўВҖВқ said the man.

ГўВҖВңHas it ever happened to you?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңOnce, sir, when I was grafting a rose-tree.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, suppose you were to alter a signal, and substitute another?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, that is another case; I should be turned off, and lose my
pension.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThree hundred francs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA hundred crowns, yes, sir; so you see that I am not likely to do any
of these things.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot even for fifteen yearsГўВҖВҷ wages? Come, it is worth thinking about?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor fifteen thousand francs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, you alarm me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNonsense.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, you are tempting me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJust so; fifteen thousand francs, do you understand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, let me see my right-hand correspondent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary, do not look at him, but at this.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? Do you not know these bits of paper?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBank-notes!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly; there are fifteen of them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd whose are they?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYours, if you like.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMine?ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the man, half-suffocated.

ГўВҖВңYes; yoursГўВҖВ”your own property.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, my right-hand correspondent is signalling.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLet him signal.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, you have distracted me; I shall be fined.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat will cost you a hundred francs; you see it is your interest to
take my bank-notes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, my right-hand correspondent redoubles his signals; he is
impatient.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mindГўВҖВ”take these;ГўВҖВқ and the count placed the packet in the manГўВҖВҷs
hands. ГўВҖВңNow this is not all,ГўВҖВқ he said; ГўВҖВңyou cannot live upon your
fifteen thousand francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI shall still have my place.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, you will lose it, for you are going to alter your correspondentГўВҖВҷs
message.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, sir, what are you proposing?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA jest.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, unless you force meГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think I can effectually force you;ГўВҖВқ and Monte Cristo drew another
packet from his pocket. ГўВҖВңHere are ten thousand more francs,ГўВҖВқ he said,
ГўВҖВңwith the fifteen thousand already in your pocket, they will make
twenty-five thousand. With five thousand you can buy a pretty little
house with two acres of land; the remaining twenty thousand will bring
you in a thousand francs a year.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA garden with two acres of land!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd a thousand francs a year.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, heavens!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, take them,ГўВҖВқ and Monte Cristo forced the bank-notes into his
hand.

ГўВҖВңWhat am I to do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNothing very difficult.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo repeat these signs.ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo took a paper from his pocket,
upon which were drawn three signs, with numbers to indicate the order
in which they were to be worked.

ГўВҖВңThere, you see it will not take long.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; butГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo this, and you will have nectarines and all the rest.ГўВҖВқ

The shot told; red with fever, while the large drops fell from his
brow, the man executed, one after the other, the three signs given by
the count, in spite of the frightful contortions of the right-hand
correspondent, who, not understanding the change, began to think the
gardener had gone mad. As to the left-hand one, he conscientiously
repeated the same signals, which were finally transmitted to the
Minister of the Interior.

ГўВҖВңNow you are rich,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied the man, ГўВҖВңbut at what a price!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen, friend,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңI do not wish to cause you any
remorse; believe me, then, when I swear to you that you have wronged no
man, but on the contrary have benefited mankind.ГўВҖВқ

The man looked at the bank-notes, felt them, counted them, turned pale,
then red, then rushed into his room to drink a glass of water, but he
had no time to reach the water-jug, and fainted in the midst of his
dried herbs. Five minutes after the new telegram reached the minister,
Debray had the horses put to his carriage, and drove to DanglarsГўВҖВҷ
house.

ГўВҖВңHas your husband any Spanish bonds?ГўВҖВқ he asked of the baroness.

ГўВҖВңI think so, indeed! He has six millionsГўВҖВҷ worth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe must sell them at whatever price.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause Don Carlos has fled from Bourges, and has returned to Spain.ГўВҖВқ

30203m



ГўВҖВңHow do you know?ГўВҖВқ Debray shrugged his shoulders.

ГўВҖВңThe idea of asking how I hear the news,ГўВҖВқ he said.

The baroness did not wait for a repetition; she ran to her husband, who
immediately hastened to his agent, and ordered him to sell at any
price. When it was seen that Danglars sold, the Spanish funds fell
directly. Danglars lost five hundred thousand francs; but he rid
himself of all his Spanish shares. The same evening the following was
read in _Le Messager_:

ГўВҖВң[By telegraph.] The king, Don Carlos, has escaped the vigilance of his
guardians at Bourges, and has returned to Spain by the Catalonian
frontier. Barcelona has risen in his favor.ГўВҖВқ

All that evening nothing was spoken of but the foresight of Danglars,
who had sold his shares, and of the luck of the stock-jobber, who only
lost five hundred thousand francs by such a blow. Those who had kept
their shares, or bought those of Danglars, looked upon themselves as
ruined, and passed a very bad night. Next morning _Le Moniteur_
contained the following:

ГўВҖВңIt was without any foundation that _Le Messager_ yesterday announced
the flight of Don Carlos and the revolt of Barcelona. The king (Don
Carlos) has not left Bourges, and the peninsula is in the enjoyment of
profound peace. A telegraphic signal, improperly interpreted, owing to
the fog, was the cause of this error.ГўВҖВқ

The funds rose one per cent higher than before they had fallen. This,
reckoning his loss, and what he had missed gaining, made the difference
of a million to Danglars.

ГўВҖВңGood,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo to Morrel, who was at his house when the news
arrived of the strange reverse of fortune of which Danglars had been
the victim, ГўВҖВңI have just made a discovery for twenty-five thousand
francs, for which I would have paid a hundred thousand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat have you discovered?ГўВҖВқ asked Morrel.

ГўВҖВңI have just discovered how a gardener may get rid of the dormice that
eat his peaches.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 62. Ghosts

At first sight, the exterior of the house at Auteuil gave no
indications of splendor, nothing one would expect from the destined
residence of the magnificent Count of Monte Cristo; but this simplicity
was according to the will of its master, who positively ordered nothing
to be altered outside. The splendor was within. Indeed, almost before
the door opened, the scene changed.

M. Bertuccio had outdone himself in the taste displayed in furnishing,
and in the rapidity with which it was executed. It is told that the Duc
dГўВҖВҷAntin removed in a single night a whole avenue of trees that annoyed
Louis XIV.; in three days M. Bertuccio planted an entirely bare court
with poplars, large spreading sycamores to shade the different parts of
the house, and in the foreground, instead of the usual paving-stones,
half hidden by the grass, there extended a lawn but that morning laid
down, and upon which the water was yet glistening. For the rest, the
orders had been issued by the count; he himself had given a plan to
Bertuccio, marking the spot where each tree was to be planted, and the
shape and extent of the lawn which was to take the place of the
paving-stones.

Thus the house had become unrecognizable, and Bertuccio himself
declared that he scarcely knew it, encircled as it was by a framework
of trees. The overseer would not have objected, while he was about it,
to have made some improvements in the garden, but the count had
positively forbidden it to be touched. Bertuccio made amends, however,
by loading the antechambers, staircases, and mantle-pieces with
flowers.

What, above all, manifested the shrewdness of the steward, and the
profound science of the master, the one in carrying out the ideas of
the other, was that this house which appeared only the night before so
sad and gloomy, impregnated with that sickly smell one can almost fancy
to be the smell of time, had in a single day acquired the aspect of
life, was scented with its masterГўВҖВҷs favorite perfumes, and had the very
light regulated according to his wish. When the count arrived, he had
under his touch his books and arms, his eyes rested upon his favorite
pictures; his dogs, whose caresses he loved, welcomed him in the
antechamber; the birds, whose songs delighted him, cheered him with
their music; and the house, awakened from its long sleep, like the
sleeping beauty in the wood, lived, sang, and bloomed like the houses
we have long cherished, and in which, when we are forced to leave them,
we leave a part of our souls.

The servants passed gayly along the fine courtyard; some, belonging to
the kitchens, gliding down the stairs, restored but the previous day,
as if they had always inhabited the house; others filling the
coach-houses, where the equipages, encased and numbered, appeared to
have been installed for the last fifty years; and in the stables the
horses replied with neighs to the grooms, who spoke to them with much
more respect than many servants pay their masters.

The library was divided into two parts on either side of the wall, and
contained upwards of two thousand volumes; one division was entirely
devoted to novels, and even the volume which had been published but the
day before was to be seen in its place in all the dignity of its red
and gold binding.

On the other side of the house, to match with the library, was the
conservatory, ornamented with rare flowers, that bloomed in china jars;
and in the midst of the greenhouse, marvellous alike to sight and
smell, was a billiard-table which looked as if it had been abandoned
during the past hour by players who had left the balls on the cloth.

One chamber alone had been respected by the magnificent Bertuccio.
Before this room, to which you could ascend by the grand, and go out by
the back staircase, the servants passed with curiosity, and Bertuccio
with terror.

At five oГўВҖВҷclock precisely, the count arrived before the house at
Auteuil, followed by Ali. Bertuccio was awaiting this arrival with
impatience, mingled with uneasiness; he hoped for some compliments,
while, at the same time, he feared to have frowns. Monte Cristo
descended into the courtyard, walked all over the house, without giving
any sign of approbation or pleasure, until he entered his bedroom,
situated on the opposite side to the closed room; then he approached a
little piece of furniture, made of rosewood, which he had noticed at a
previous visit.

ГўВҖВңThat can only be to hold gloves,ГўВҖВқ he said.

ГўВҖВңWill your excellency deign to open it?ГўВҖВқ said the delighted Bertuccio,
ГўВҖВңand you will find gloves in it.ГўВҖВқ

Elsewhere the count found everything he requiredГўВҖВ”smelling-bottles,
cigars, knick-knacks.

30207m



ГўВҖВңGood,ГўВҖВқ he said; and M. Bertuccio left enraptured, so great, so
powerful, and real was the influence exercised by this man over all who
surrounded him.

At precisely six oГўВҖВҷclock the clatter of horsesГўВҖВҷ hoofs was heard at the
entrance door; it was our captain of Spahis, who had arrived on MГғВ©dГғВ©ah.
ГўВҖВңI am sure I am the first,ГўВҖВқ cried Morrel; ГўВҖВңI did it on purpose to have
you a minute to myself, before everyone came. Julie and Emmanuel have a
thousand things to tell you. Ah, really this is magnificent! But tell
me, count, will your people take care of my horse?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not alarm yourself, my dear MaximilianГўВҖВ”they understand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI mean, because he wants petting. If you had seen at what a pace he
cameГўВҖВ”like the wind!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI should think so,ГўВҖВ”a horse that cost 5,000 francs!ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo,
in the tone which a father would use towards a son.

ГўВҖВңDo you regret them?ГўВҖВқ asked Morrel, with his open laugh.

ГўВҖВңI? Certainly not,ГўВҖВқ replied the count. ГўВҖВңNo; I should only regret if the
horse had not proved good.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is so good, that I have distanced M. de ChГғВўteau-Renaud, one of the
best riders in France, and M. Debray, who both mount the ministerГўВҖВҷs
Arabians; and close on their heels are the horses of Madame Danglars,
who always go at six leagues an hour.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen they follow you?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңSee, they are here.ГўВҖВқ And at the same minute a carriage with smoking
horses, accompanied by two mounted gentlemen, arrived at the gate,
which opened before them. The carriage drove round, and stopped at the
steps, followed by the horsemen.

The instant Debray had touched the ground, he was at the carriage-door.
He offered his hand to the baroness, who, descending, took it with a
peculiarity of manner imperceptible to everyone but Monte Cristo. But
nothing escaped the countГўВҖВҷs notice, and he observed a little note,
passed with the facility that indicates frequent practice, from the
hand of Madame Danglars to that of the ministerГўВҖВҷs secretary.

After his wife the banker descended, as pale as though he had issued
from his tomb instead of his carriage.

Madame Danglars threw a rapid and inquiring glance which could only be
interpreted by Monte Cristo, around the courtyard, over the peristyle,
and across the front of the house, then, repressing a slight emotion,
which must have been seen on her countenance if she had not kept her
color, she ascended the steps, saying to Morrel:

ГўВҖВңSir, if you were a friend of mine, I should ask you if you would sell
your horse.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel smiled with an expression very like a grimace, and then turned
round to Monte Cristo, as if to ask him to extricate him from his
embarrassment. The count understood him.

ГўВҖВңAh, madame,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңwhy did you not make that request of me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith you, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the baroness, ГўВҖВңone can wish for nothing, one
is so sure to obtain it. If it were so with M. MorrelГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUnfortunately,ГўВҖВқ replied the count, ГўВҖВңI am witness that M. Morrel cannot
give up his horse, his honor being engaged in keeping it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe laid a wager he would tame MГғВ©dГғВ©ah in the space of six months. You
understand now that if he were to get rid of the animal before the time
named, he would not only lose his bet, but people would say he was
afraid; and a brave captain of Spahis cannot risk this, even to gratify
a pretty woman, which is, in my opinion, one of the most sacred
obligations in the world.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou see my position, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, bestowing a grateful smile
on Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңIt seems to me,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, in his coarse tone, ill-concealed by a
forced smile, ГўВҖВңthat you have already got horses enough.ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars seldom allowed remarks of this kind to pass unnoticed,
but, to the surprise of the young people, she pretended not to hear it,
and said nothing. Monte Cristo smiled at her unusual humility, and
showed her two immense porcelain jars, over which wound marine plants,
of a size and delicacy that nature alone could produce. The baroness
was astonished.

ГўВҖВңWhy,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңyou could plant one of the chestnut-trees in the
Tuileries inside! How can such enormous jars have been manufactured?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh! madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңyou must not ask of us, the
manufacturers of fine porcelain, such a question. It is the work of
another age, constructed by the genii of earth and water.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВ”at what period can that have been?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not know; I have only heard that an emperor of China had an oven
built expressly, and that in this oven twelve jars like this were
successively baked. Two broke, from the heat of the fire; the other ten
were sunk three hundred fathoms deep into the sea. The sea, knowing
what was required of her, threw over them her weeds, encircled them
with coral, and encrusted them with shells; the whole was cemented by
two hundred years beneath these almost impervious depths, for a
revolution carried away the emperor who wished to make the trial, and
only left the documents proving the manufacture of the jars and their
descent into the sea. At the end of two hundred years the documents
were found, and they thought of bringing up the jars. Divers descended
in machines, made expressly on the discovery, into the bay where they
were thrown; but of ten three only remained, the rest having been
broken by the waves. I am fond of these jars, upon which, perhaps,
misshapen, frightful monsters have fixed their cold, dull eyes, and in
which myriads of small fish have slept, seeking a refuge from the
pursuit of their enemies.ГўВҖВқ

Meanwhile, Danglars, who had cared little for curiosities, was
mechanically tearing off the blossoms of a splendid orange-tree, one
after another. When he had finished with the orange-tree, he began at
the cactus; but this, not being so easily plucked as the orange-tree,
pricked him dreadfully. He shuddered, and rubbed his eyes as though
awaking from a dream.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo to him, ГўВҖВңI do not recommend my pictures to
you, who possess such splendid paintings; but, nevertheless, here are
two by Hobbema, a Paul Potter, a Mieris, two by Gerard Douw, a Raphael,
a Van Dyck, a Zurbaran, and two or three by Murillo, worth looking at.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStay,ГўВҖВқ said Debray; ГўВҖВңI recognize this Hobbema.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; it was proposed for the Museum.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich, I believe, does not contain one?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңNo; and yet they refused to buy it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud.

ГўВҖВңYou pretend not to know,ГўВҖВ”because government was not rich enough.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, pardon me,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud; ГўВҖВңI have heard of these things
every day during the last eight years, and I cannot understand them
yet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will, by and by,ГўВҖВқ said Debray.

ГўВҖВңI think not,ГўВҖВқ replied ChГғВўteau-Renaud.

ГўВҖВңMajor Bartolomeo Cavalcanti and Count Andrea Cavalcanti,ГўВҖВқ announced
Baptistin.

A black satin stock, fresh from the makerГўВҖВҷs hands, gray moustaches, a
bold eye, a majorГўВҖВҷs uniform, ornamented with three medals and five
crossesГўВҖВ”in fact, the thorough bearing of an old soldierГўВҖВ”such was the
appearance of Major Bartolomeo Cavalcanti, that tender father with whom
we are already acquainted. Close to him, dressed in entirely new
clothes, advanced smilingly Count Andrea Cavalcanti, the dutiful son,
whom we also know. The three young people were talking together. On the
entrance of the new-comers, their eyes glanced from father to son, and
then, naturally enough, rested on the latter, whom they began
criticising.

ГўВҖВңCavalcanti!ГўВҖВқ said Debray.

ГўВҖВңA fine name,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңthese Italians are well named and badly
dressed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are fastidious, ChГғВўteau-Renaud,ГўВҖВқ replied Debray; ГўВҖВңthose clothes
are well cut and quite new.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is just what I find fault with. That gentleman appears to be well
dressed for the first time in his life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho are those gentlemen?ГўВҖВқ asked Danglars of Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңYou heardГўВҖВ”Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat tells me their name, and nothing else.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh! true. You do not know the Italian nobility; the Cavalcanti are all
descended from princes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave they any fortune?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn enormous one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do they do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTry to spend it all. They have some business with you, I think, from
what they told me the day before yesterday. I, indeed, invited them
here today on your account. I will introduce you to them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut they appear to speak French with a very pure accent,ГўВҖВқ said
Danglars.

ГўВҖВңThe son has been educated in a college in the south; I believe near
Marseilles. You will find him quite enthusiastic.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUpon what subject?ГўВҖВқ asked Madame Danglars.

ГўВҖВңThe French ladies, madame. He has made up his mind to take a wife from
Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA fine idea that of his,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, shrugging his shoulders.
Madame Danglars looked at her husband with an expression which, at any
other time, would have indicated a storm, but for the second time she
controlled herself.

ГўВҖВңThe baron appears thoughtful today,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo to her; ГўВҖВңare
they going to put him in the ministry?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot yet, I think. More likely he has been speculating on the Bourse,
and has lost money.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. and Madame de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ cried Baptistin.

They entered. M. de Villefort, notwithstanding his self-control, was
visibly affected, and when Monte Cristo touched his hand, he felt it
tremble.

ГўВҖВңCertainly, women alone know how to dissimulate,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo to
himself, glancing at Madame Danglars, who was smiling on the procureur,
and embracing his wife.

After a short time, the count saw Bertuccio, who, until then, had been
occupied on the other side of the house, glide into an adjoining room.
He went to him.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you want, M. Bertuccio?ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңYour excellency has not stated the number of guests.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, true.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow many covers?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCount for yourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs everyone here, your excellency?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio glanced through the door, which was ajar. The count watched
him. ГўВҖВңGood heavens!ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed.

ГўВҖВңWhat is the matter?ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңThat womanГўВҖВ”that woman!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe one with a white dress and so many diamondsГўВҖВ”the fair one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMadame Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not know her name; but it is she, sir, it is she!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhom do you mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe woman of the garden!ГўВҖВ”she that was _enceinte_ГўВҖВ”she who was walking
while she waited forГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio stood at the open door, with his eyes starting and his hair
on end.

ГўВҖВңWaiting for whom?ГўВҖВқ Bertuccio, without answering, pointed to Villefort
with something of the gesture Macbeth uses to point out Banquo.

ГўВҖВңOh, oh!ГўВҖВқ he at length muttered, ГўВҖВңdo you see?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? Who?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHim!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHim!ГўВҖВ”M. de Villefort, the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney? Certainly I see him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I did not kill him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally, I think you are going mad, good Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңThen he is not dead?ГўВҖВқ

30213m



ГўВҖВңNo; you see plainly he is not dead. Instead of striking between the
sixth and seventh left ribs, as your countrymen do, you must have
struck higher or lower, and life is very tenacious in these lawyers, or
rather there is no truth in anything you have told meГўВҖВ”it was a fright
of the imagination, a dream of your fancy. You went to sleep full of
thoughts of vengeance; they weighed heavily upon your stomach; you had
the nightmareГўВҖВ”thatГўВҖВҷs all. Come, calm yourself, and reckon them upГўВҖВ”M.
and Madame de Villefort, two; M. and Madame Danglars, four; M. de
ChГғВўteau-Renaud, M. Debray, M. Morrel, seven; Major Bartolomeo
Cavalcanti, eight.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEight!ГўВҖВқ repeated Bertuccio.

ГўВҖВңStop! You are in a shocking hurry to be offГўВҖВ”you forget one of my
guests. Lean a little to the left. Stay! look at M. Andrea Cavalcanti,
the young man in a black coat, looking at MurilloГўВҖВҷs ГўВҖВҳMadonnaГўВҖВҷ; now he
is turning.ГўВҖВқ

This time Bertuccio would have uttered an exclamation, had not a look
from Monte Cristo silenced him.

ГўВҖВңBenedetto?ГўВҖВқ he muttered; ГўВҖВңfatality!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHalf-past six oГўВҖВҷclock has just struck, M. Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count
severely; ГўВҖВңI ordered dinner at that hour, and I do not like to wait;ГўВҖВқ
and he returned to his guests, while Bertuccio, leaning against the
wall, succeeded in reaching the dining-room. Five minutes afterwards
the doors of the drawing-room were thrown open, and Bertuccio appearing
said, with a violent effort, ГўВҖВңThe dinner waits.ГўВҖВқ

The Count of Monte Cristo offered his arm to Madame de Villefort. ГўВҖВңM.
de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңwill you conduct the Baroness Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

Villefort complied, and they passed on to the dining-room.



 Chapter 63. The Dinner

It was evident that one sentiment affected all the guests on entering
the dining-room. Each one asked what strange influence had brought them
to this house, and yet astonished, even uneasy though they were, they
still felt that they would not like to be absent. The recent events,
the solitary and eccentric position of the count, his enormous, nay,
almost incredible fortune, should have made men cautious, and have
altogether prevented ladies visiting a house where there was no one of
their own sex to receive them; and yet curiosity had been enough to
lead them to overleap the bounds of prudence and decorum.

And all present, even including Cavalcanti and his son, notwithstanding
the stiffness of the one and the carelessness of the other, were
thoughtful, on finding themselves assembled at the house of this
incomprehensible man. Madame Danglars had started when Villefort, on
the countГўВҖВҷs invitation, offered his arm; and Villefort felt that his
glance was uneasy beneath his gold spectacles, when he felt the arm of
the baroness press upon his own. None of this had escaped the count,
and even by this mere contact of individuals the scene had already
acquired considerable interest for an observer.

M. de Villefort had on the right hand Madame Danglars, on his left
Morrel. The count was seated between Madame de Villefort and Danglars;
the other seats were filled by Debray, who was placed between the two
Cavalcanti, and by ChГғВўteau-Renaud, seated between Madame de Villefort
and Morrel.

The repast was magnificent; Monte Cristo had endeavored completely to
overturn the Parisian ideas, and to feed the curiosity as much as the
appetite of his guests. It was an Oriental feast that he offered to
them, but of such a kind as the Arabian fairies might be supposed to
prepare. Every delicious fruit that the four quarters of the globe
could provide was heaped in vases from China and jars from Japan. Rare
birds, retaining their most brilliant plumage, enormous fish, spread
upon massive silver dishes, together with every wine produced in the
Archipelago, Asia Minor, or the Cape, sparkling in bottles, whose
grotesque shape seemed to give an additional flavor to the draught,ГўВҖВ”all
these, like one of the displays with which Apicius of old gratified his
guests, passed in review before the eyes of the astonished Parisians,
who understood that it was possible to expend a thousand louis upon a
dinner for ten persons, but only on the condition of eating pearls,
like Cleopatra, or drinking refined gold, like Lorenzo deГўВҖВҷ Medici.

Monte Cristo noticed the general astonishment, and began laughing and
joking about it.

ГўВҖВңGentlemen,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңyou will admit that, when arrived at a certain
degree of fortune, the superfluities of life are all that can be
desired; and the ladies will allow that, after having risen to a
certain eminence of position, the ideal alone can be more exalted. Now,
to follow out this reasoning, what is the marvellous?ГўВҖВ”that which we do
not understand. What is it that we really desire?ГўВҖВ”that which we cannot
obtain. Now, to see things which I cannot understand, to procure
impossibilities, these are the study of my life. I gratify my wishes by
two meansГўВҖВ”my will and my money. I take as much interest in the pursuit
of some whim as you do, M. Danglars, in promoting a new railway line;
you, M. de Villefort, in condemning a culprit to death; you, M. Debray,
in pacifying a kingdom; you, M. de ChГғВўteau-Renaud, in pleasing a woman;
and you, Morrel, in breaking a horse that no one can ride. For example,
you see these two fish; one brought from fifty leagues beyond St.
Petersburg, the other five leagues from Naples. Is it not amusing to
see them both on the same table?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat are the two fish?ГўВҖВқ asked Danglars.

ГўВҖВңM. ChГғВўteau-Renaud, who has lived in Russia, will tell you the name of
one, and Major Cavalcanti, who is an Italian, will tell you the name of
the other.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis one is, I think, a sterlet,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud.

ГўВҖВңAnd that one, if I mistake not, a lamprey.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJust so. Now, M. Danglars, ask these gentlemen where they are caught.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSterlets,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңare only found in the Volga.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd,ГўВҖВқ said Cavalcanti, ГўВҖВңI know that Lake Fusaro alone supplies
lampreys of that size.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly; one comes from the Volga, and the other from Lake Fusaro.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңImpossible!ГўВҖВқ cried all the guests simultaneously.

ГўВҖВңWell, this is just what amuses me,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңI am like
NeroГўВҖВ”_cupitor impossibilium_; and that is what is amusing you at this
moment. This fish, which seems so exquisite to you, is very likely no
better than perch or salmon; but it seemed impossible to procure it,
and here it is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut how could you have these fish brought to France?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, nothing more easy. Each fish was brought over in a caskГўВҖВ”one filled
with river herbs and weeds, the other with rushes and lake plants; they
were placed in a wagon built on purpose, and thus the sterlet lived
twelve days, the lamprey eight, and both were alive when my cook seized
them, killing one with milk and the other with wine. You do not believe
me, M. Danglars!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI cannot help doubting,ГўВҖВқ answered Danglars with his stupid smile.

ГўВҖВңBaptistin,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңhave the other fish brought inГўВҖВ”the
sterlet and the lamprey which came in the other casks, and which are
yet alive.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars opened his bewildered eyes; the company clapped their hands.
Four servants carried in two casks covered with aquatic plants, and in
each of which was breathing a fish similar to those on the table.

ГўВҖВңBut why have two of each sort?ГўВҖВқ asked Danglars.

ГўВҖВңMerely because one might have died,ГўВҖВқ carelessly answered Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңYou are certainly an extraordinary man,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңand
philosophers may well say it is a fine thing to be rich.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd to have ideas,ГўВҖВқ added Madame Danglars.

ГўВҖВңOh, do not give me credit for this, madame; it was done by the Romans,
who much esteemed them, and Pliny relates that they sent slaves from
Ostia to Rome, who carried on their heads fish which he calls the
_mulus_, and which, from the description, must probably be the
goldfish. It was also considered a luxury to have them alive, it being
an amusing sight to see them die, for, when dying, they change color
three or four times, and like the rainbow when it disappears, pass
through all the prismatic shades, after which they were sent to the
kitchen. Their agony formed part of their meritГўВҖВ”if they were not seen
alive, they were despised when dead.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңbut then Ostia is only a few leagues from Rome.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңbut what would be the use of living
eighteen hundred years after Lucullus, if we can do no better than he
could?ГўВҖВқ

The two Cavalcanti opened their enormous eyes, but had the good sense
not to say anything.

ГўВҖВңAll this is very extraordinary,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud; ГўВҖВңstill, what I
admire the most, I confess, is the marvellous promptitude with which
your orders are executed. Is it not true that you only bought this
house five or six days ago?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly not longer.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I am sure it is quite transformed since last week. If I remember
rightly, it had another entrance, and the courtyard was paved and
empty; while today we have a splendid lawn, bordered by trees which
appear to be a hundred years old.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy not? I am fond of grass and shade,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңthe door was towards the road before,
and on the day of my miraculous escape you brought me into the house
from the road, I remember.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңbut I preferred having an entrance
which would allow me to see the Bois de Boulogne over my gate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn four days,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel; ГўВҖВңit is extraordinary!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңit seems quite miraculous to make a new
house out of an old one; for it was very old, and dull too. I recollect
coming for my mother to look at it when M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran advertised it
for sale two or three years ago.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Saint-MГғВ©ran?ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort; ГўВҖВңthen this house
belonged to M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran before you bought it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt appears so,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңIs it possible that you do not know of whom you purchased it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңQuite so; my steward transacts all this business for me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is certainly ten years since the house had been occupied,ГўВҖВқ said
ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңand it was quite melancholy to look at it, with the
blinds closed, the doors locked, and the weeds in the court. Really, if
the house had not belonged to the father-in-law of the procureur, one
might have thought it some accursed place where a horrible crime had
been committed.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort, who had hitherto not tasted the three or four glasses of
rare wine which were placed before him, here took one, and drank it
off. Monte Cristo allowed a short time to elapse, and then said:

ГўВҖВңIt is singular, baron, but the same idea came across me the first time
I came here; it looked so gloomy I should never have bought it if my
steward had not taken the matter into his own hands. Perhaps the fellow
had been bribed by the notary.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is probable,ГўВҖВқ stammered out Villefort, trying to smile; ГўВҖВңbut I can
assure you that I had nothing to do with any such proceeding. This
house is part of ValentineГўВҖВҷs marriage-portion, and M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran
wished to sell it; for if it had remained another year or two
uninhabited it would have fallen to ruin.ГўВҖВқ

It was MorrelГўВҖВҷs turn to become pale.

ГўВҖВңThere was, above all, one room,ГўВҖВқ continued Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңvery plain
in appearance, hung with red damask, which, I know not why, appeared to
me quite dramatic.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so?ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңwhy dramatic?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCan we account for instinct?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңAre there not some
places where we seem to breathe sadness?ГўВҖВ”why, we cannot tell. It is a
chain of recollectionsГўВҖВ”an idea which carries you back to other times,
to other placesГўВҖВ”which, very likely, have no connection with the present
time and place. And there is something in this room which reminds me
forcibly of the chamber of the Marquise de Ganges10 or Desdemona. Stay,
since we have finished dinner, I will show it to you, and then we will
take coffee in the garden. After dinner, the play.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo looked inquiringly at his guests. Madame de Villefort
rose, Monte Cristo did the same, and the rest followed their example.
Villefort and Madame Danglars remained for a moment, as if rooted to
their seats; they questioned each other with vague and stupid glances.

ГўВҖВңDid you hear?ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars.

ГўВҖВңWe must go,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, offering his arm.

The others, attracted by curiosity, were already scattered in different
parts of the house; for they thought the visit would not be limited to
the one room, and that, at the same time, they would obtain a view of
the rest of the building, of which Monte Cristo had created a palace.
Each one went out by the open doors. Monte Cristo waited for the two
who remained; then, when they had passed, he brought up the rear, and
on his face was a smile, which, if they could have understood it, would
have alarmed them much more than a visit to the room they were about to
enter. They began by walking through the apartments, many of which were
fitted up in the Eastern style, with cushions and divans instead of
beds, and pipes instead of furniture. The drawing-rooms were decorated
with the rarest pictures by the old masters, the boudoirs hung with
draperies from China, of fanciful colors, fantastic design, and
wonderful texture. At length they arrived at the famous room. There was
nothing particular about it, excepting that, although daylight had
disappeared, it was not lighted, and everything in it was
old-fashioned, while the rest of the rooms had been redecorated. These
two causes were enough to give it a gloomy aspect.

ГўВҖВңOh.ГўВҖВқ cried Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңit is really frightful.ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars tried to utter a few words, but was not heard. Many
observations were made, the import of which was a unanimous opinion
that there was something sinister about the room.

30221m



ГўВҖВңIs it not so?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңLook at that large clumsy bed,
hung with such gloomy, blood-colored drapery! And those two crayon
portraits, that have faded from the dampness; do they not seem to say,
with their pale lips and staring eyes, ГўВҖВҳWe have seenГўВҖВҷ?ГўВҖВқ

Villefort became livid; Madame Danglars fell into a long seat placed
near the chimney.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, smiling, ГўВҖВңare you courageous enough to
sit down upon the very seat perhaps upon which the crime was
committed?ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars rose suddenly.

ГўВҖВңAnd then,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthis is not all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is there more?ГўВҖВқ said Debray, who had not failed to notice the
agitation of Madame Danglars.

ГўВҖВңAh, what else is there?ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңfor, at present, I cannot say
that I have seen anything extraordinary. What do you say, M.
Cavalcanti?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңwe have at Pisa, UgolinoГўВҖВҷs tower; at Ferrara, TassoГўВҖВҷs
prison; at Rimini, the room of Francesca and Paolo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, but you have not this little staircase,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo,
opening a door concealed by the drapery. ГўВҖВңLook at it, and tell me what
you think of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat a wicked-looking, crooked staircase,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud with a
smile.

ГўВҖВңI do not know whether the wine of Chios produces melancholy, but
certainly everything appears to me black in this house,ГўВҖВқ said Debray.

Ever since ValentineГўВҖВҷs dowry had been mentioned, Morrel had been silent
and sad.

ГўВҖВңCan you imagine,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңsome Othello or AbbГғВ© de Ganges,
one stormy, dark night, descending these stairs step by step, carrying
a load, which he wishes to hide from the sight of man, if not from
God?ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars half fainted on the arm of Villefort, who was obliged
to support himself against the wall.

ГўВҖВңAh, madame,ГўВҖВқ cried Debray, ГўВҖВңwhat is the matter with you? how pale you
look!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is very evident what is the matter with her,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de
Villefort; ГўВҖВңM. de Monte Cristo is relating horrible stories to us,
doubtless intending to frighten us to death.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңreally, count, you frighten the ladies.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is the matter?ГўВҖВқ asked Debray, in a whisper, of Madame Danglars.

ГўВҖВңNothing,ГўВҖВқ she replied with a violent effort. ГўВҖВңI want air, that is
all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWill you come into the garden?ГўВҖВқ said Debray, advancing towards the
back staircase.

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ she answered, ГўВҖВңI would rather remain here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you really frightened, madame?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңOh, no, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars; ГўВҖВңbut you suppose scenes in a
manner which gives them the appearance of reality.ГўВҖВқ

30223m



ГўВҖВңAh, yes,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo smiling; ГўВҖВңit is all a matter of
imagination. Why should we not imagine this the apartment of an honest
mother? And this bed with red hangings, a bed visited by the goddess
Lucina? And that mysterious staircase, the passage through which, not
to disturb their sleep, the doctor and nurse pass, or even the father
carrying the sleeping child?ГўВҖВқ

Here Madame Danglars, instead of being calmed by the soft picture,
uttered a groan and fainted.

ГўВҖВңMadame Danglars is ill,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort; ГўВҖВңit would be better to take
her to her carriage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, _mon Dieu!_ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңand I have forgotten my
smelling-bottle!ГўВҖВқ
