ГўВҖВңI have mine,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort; and she passed over to Monte
Cristo a bottle full of the same kind of red liquid whose good
properties the count had tested on Edward.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, taking it from her hand.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ she said, ГўВҖВңat your advice I have made the trial.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd have you succeeded?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think so.ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars was carried into the adjoining room; Monte Cristo
dropped a very small portion of the red liquid upon her lips; she
returned to consciousness.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ she cried, ГўВҖВңwhat a frightful dream!ГўВҖВқ

Villefort pressed her hand to let her know it was not a dream. They
looked for M. Danglars, but, as he was not especially interested in
poetical ideas, he had gone into the garden, and was talking with Major
Cavalcanti on the projected railway from Leghorn to Florence. Monte
Cristo seemed in despair. He took the arm of Madame Danglars, and
conducted her into the garden, where they found Danglars taking coffee
between the Cavalcanti.

ГўВҖВңReally, madame,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңdid I alarm you much?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no, sir,ГўВҖВқ she answered; ГўВҖВңbut you know, things impress us
differently, according to the mood of our minds.ГўВҖВқ Villefort forced a
laugh.

ГўВҖВңAnd then, you know,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңan idea, a supposition, is sufficient.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңyou may believe me if you like, but it is
my opinion that a crime has been committed in this house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTake care,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңthe kingГўВҖВҷs attorney is here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңsince that is the case, I will take
advantage of his presence to make my declaration.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour declaration?ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңYes, before witnesses.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, this is very interesting,ГўВҖВқ said Debray; ГўВҖВңif there really has been
a crime, we will investigate it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere has been a crime,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңCome this way, gentlemen;
come, M. Villefort, for a declaration to be available, should be made
before the competent authorities.ГўВҖВқ

He then took VillefortГўВҖВҷs arm, and, at the same time, holding that of
Madame Danglars under his own, he dragged the procureur to the
plantain-tree, where the shade was thickest. All the other guests
followed.

ГўВҖВңStay,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңhere, in this very spotГўВҖВқ (and he stamped
upon the ground), ГўВҖВңI had the earth dug up and fresh mould put in, to
refresh these old trees; well, my man, digging, found a box, or rather,
the iron-work of a box, in the midst of which was the skeleton of a
newly born infant.ГўВҖВқ

30225m



Monte Cristo felt the arm of Madame Danglars stiffen, while that of
Villefort trembled.

ГўВҖВңA newly born infant,ГўВҖВқ repeated Debray; ГўВҖВңthis affair becomes serious!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңI was not wrong just now then, when I
said that houses had souls and faces like men, and that their exteriors
carried the impress of their characters. This house was gloomy because
it was remorseful: it was remorseful because it concealed a crime.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho said it was a crime?ГўВҖВқ asked Villefort, with a last effort.

ГўВҖВңHow? is it not a crime to bury a living child in a garden?ГўВҖВқ cried
Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңAnd pray what do you call such an action?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut who said it was buried alive?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy bury it there if it were dead? This garden has never been a
cemetery.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is done to infanticides in this country?ГўВҖВқ asked Major Cavalcanti
innocently.

ГўВҖВңOh, their heads are soon cut off,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed?ГўВҖВқ said Cavalcanti.

ГўВҖВңI think so; am I not right, M. de Villefort?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңYes, count,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, in a voice now scarcely human.

Monte Cristo, seeing that the two persons for whom he had prepared this
scene could scarcely endure it, and not wishing to carry it too far,
said:

ГўВҖВңCome, gentlemen,ГўВҖВ”some coffee, we seem to have forgotten it,ГўВҖВқ and he
conducted the guests back to the table on the lawn.

ГўВҖВңIndeed, count,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңI am ashamed to own it, but all
your frightful stories have so upset me, that I must beg you to let me
sit down;ГўВҖВқ and she fell into a chair.

Monte Cristo bowed, and went to Madame de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңI think Madame Danglars again requires your bottle,ГўВҖВқ he said. But
before Madame de Villefort could reach her friend, the procureur had
found time to whisper to Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңI must speak to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTomorrow.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn my office, or in the court, if you like,ГўВҖВ”that is the surest place.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will be there.ГўВҖВқ

At this moment Madame de Villefort approached.

ГўВҖВңThanks, my dear friend,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars, trying to smile; ГўВҖВңit is
over now, and I am much better.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 64. The Beggar

The evening passed on; Madame de Villefort expressed a desire to return
to Paris, which Madame Danglars had not dared to do, notwithstanding
the uneasiness she experienced. On his wifeГўВҖВҷs request, M. de Villefort
was the first to give the signal of departure. He offered a seat in his
landau to Madame Danglars, that she might be under the care of his
wife. As for M. Danglars, absorbed in an interesting conversation with
M. Cavalcanti, he paid no attention to anything that was passing. While
Monte Cristo had begged the smelling-bottle of Madame de Villefort, he
had noticed the approach of Villefort to Madame Danglars, and he soon
guessed all that had passed between them, though the words had been
uttered in so low a voice as hardly to be heard by Madame Danglars.
Without opposing their arrangements, he allowed Morrel, ChГғВўteau-Renaud,
and Debray to leave on horseback, and the ladies in M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs
carriage. Danglars, more and more delighted with Major Cavalcanti, had
offered him a seat in his carriage. Andrea Cavalcanti found his tilbury
waiting at the door; the groom, in every respect a caricature of the
English fashion, was standing on tiptoe to hold a large iron-gray
horse.

Andrea had spoken very little during dinner; he was an intelligent lad,
and he feared to utter some absurdity before so many grand people,
amongst whom, with dilating eyes, he saw the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney. Then he
had been seized upon by Danglars, who, with a rapid glance at the
stiff-necked old major and his modest son, and taking into
consideration the hospitality of the count, made up his mind that he
was in the society of some nabob come to Paris to finish the worldly
education of his heir. He contemplated with unspeakable delight the
large diamond which shone on the majorГўВҖВҷs little finger; for the major,
like a prudent man, in case of any accident happening to his
bank-notes, had immediately converted them into an available asset.
Then, after dinner, on the pretext of business, he questioned the
father and son upon their mode of living; and the father and son,
previously informed that it was through Danglars the one was to receive
his 48,000 francs and the other 50,000 livres annually, were so full of
affability that they would have shaken hands even with the bankerГўВҖВҷs
servants, so much did their gratitude need an object to expend itself
upon.

One thing above all the rest heightened the respect, nay almost the
veneration, of Danglars for Cavalcanti. The latter, faithful to the
principle of Horace, _nil admirari_, had contented himself with showing
his knowledge by declaring in what lake the best lampreys were caught.
Then he had eaten some without saying a word more; Danglars, therefore,
concluded that such luxuries were common at the table of the
illustrious descendant of the Cavalcanti, who most likely in Lucca fed
upon trout brought from Switzerland, and lobsters sent from England, by
the same means used by the count to bring the lampreys from Lake
Fusaro, and the sterlet from the Volga. Thus it was with much
politeness of manner that he heard Cavalcanti pronounce these words:

ГўВҖВңTomorrow, sir, I shall have the honor of waiting upon you on
business.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңshall be most happy to receive you.ГўВҖВқ

Upon which he offered to take Cavalcanti in his carriage to the HГғВҙtel
des Princes, if it would not be depriving him of the company of his
son. To this Cavalcanti replied by saying that for some time past his
son had lived independently of him, that he had his own horses and
carriages, and that not having come together, it would not be difficult
for them to leave separately. The major seated himself, therefore, by
the side of Danglars, who was more and more charmed with the ideas of
order and economy which ruled this man, and yet who, being able to
allow his son 60,000 francs a year, might be supposed to possess a
fortune of 500,000 or 600,000 livres.

As for Andrea, he began, by way of showing off, to scold his groom,
who, instead of bringing the tilbury to the steps of the house, had
taken it to the outer door, thus giving him the trouble of walking
thirty steps to reach it. The groom heard him with humility, took the
bit of the impatient animal with his left hand, and with the right held
out the reins to Andrea, who, taking them from him, rested his polished
boot lightly on the step.

At that moment a hand touched his shoulder. The young man turned round,
thinking that Danglars or Monte Cristo had forgotten something they
wished to tell him, and had returned just as they were starting. But
instead of either of these, he saw nothing but a strange face,
sunburnt, and encircled by a beard, with eyes brilliant as carbuncles,
and a smile upon the mouth which displayed a perfect set of white
teeth, pointed and sharp as the wolfГўВҖВҷs or jackalГўВҖВҷs. A red handkerchief
encircled his gray head; torn and filthy garments covered his large
bony limbs, which seemed as though, like those of a skeleton, they
would rattle as he walked; and the hand with which he leaned upon the
young manГўВҖВҷs shoulder, and which was the first thing Andrea saw, seemed
of gigantic size.

30229m



Did the young man recognize that face by the light of the lantern in
his tilbury, or was he merely struck with the horrible appearance of
his interrogator? We cannot say; but only relate the fact that he
shuddered and stepped back suddenly.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you want of me?ГўВҖВқ he asked.

ГўВҖВңPardon me, my friend, if I disturb you,ГўВҖВқ said the man with the red
handkerchief, ГўВҖВңbut I want to speak to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have no right to beg at night,ГўВҖВқ said the groom, endeavoring to rid
his master of the troublesome intruder.

ГўВҖВңI am not begging, my fine fellow,ГўВҖВқ said the unknown to the servant,
with so ironical an expression of the eye, and so frightful a smile,
that he withdrew; ГўВҖВңI only wish to say two or three words to your
master, who gave me a commission to execute about a fortnight ago.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, with sufficient nerve for his servant not to
perceive his agitation, ГўВҖВңwhat do you want? Speak quickly, friend.ГўВҖВқ

The man said, in a low voice: ГўВҖВңI wishГўВҖВ”I wish you to spare me the walk
back to Paris. I am very tired, and as I have not eaten so good a
dinner as you, I can scarcely stand.ГўВҖВқ

The young man shuddered at this strange familiarity.

ГўВҖВңTell me,ГўВҖВқ he saidГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңtell me what you want?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, I want you to take me up in your fine carriage, and carry
me back.ГўВҖВқ Andrea turned pale, but said nothing.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said the man, thrusting his hands into his pockets, and looking
impudently at the youth; ГўВҖВңI have taken the whim into my head; do you
understand, Master Benedetto?ГўВҖВқ

At this name, no doubt, the young man reflected a little, for he went
towards his groom, saying:

ГўВҖВңThis man is right; I did indeed charge him with a commission, the
result of which he must tell me; walk to the barrier, there take a cab,
that you may not be too late.ГўВҖВқ

The surprised groom retired.

ГўВҖВңLet me at least reach a shady spot,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea.

ГўВҖВңOh, as for that, IГўВҖВҷll take you to a splendid place,ГўВҖВқ said the man with
the handkerchief; and taking the horseГўВҖВҷs bit he led the tilbury where
it was certainly impossible for anyone to witness the honor that Andrea
conferred upon him.

ГўВҖВңDonГўВҖВҷt think I want the glory of riding in your fine carriage,ГўВҖВқ said
he; ГўВҖВңoh, no, itГўВҖВҷs only because I am tired, and also because I have a
little business to talk over with you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, step in,ГўВҖВқ said the young man. It was a pity this scene had not
occurred in daylight, for it was curious to see this rascal throwing
himself heavily down on the cushion beside the young and elegant driver
of the tilbury. Andrea drove past the last house in the village without
saying a word to his companion, who smiled complacently, as though
well-pleased to find himself travelling in so comfortable a vehicle.
Once out of Auteuil, Andrea looked around, in order to assure himself
that he could neither be seen nor heard, and then, stopping the horse
and crossing his arms before the man, he asked:

ГўВҖВңNow, tell me why you come to disturb my tranquillity?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLet me ask you why you deceived me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow have I deceived you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳHow,ГўВҖВҷ do you ask? When we parted at the Pont du Var, you told me you
were going to travel through Piedmont and Tuscany; but instead of that,
you come to Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow does that annoy you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt does not; on the contrary, I think it will answer my purpose.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңyou are speculating upon me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat fine words he uses!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI warn you, Master Caderousse, that you are mistaken.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, well, donГўВҖВҷt be angry, my boy; you know well enough what it is to
be unfortunate; and misfortunes make us jealous. I thought you were
earning a living in Tuscany or Piedmont by acting as _facchino_ or
_cicerone_, and I pitied you sincerely, as I would a child of my own.
You know I always did call you my child.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, come, what then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPatienceГўВҖВ”patience!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am patient, but go on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll at once I see you pass through the barrier with a groom, a
tilbury, and fine new clothes. You must have discovered a mine, or else
become a stockbroker.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that, as you confess, you are jealous?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, I am pleasedГўВҖВ”so pleased that I wished to congratulate you; but as
I am not quite properly dressed, I chose my opportunity, that I might
not compromise you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, and a fine opportunity you have chosen!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Andrea; ГўВҖВңyou
speak to me before my servant.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow can I help that, my boy? I speak to you when I can catch you. You
have a quick horse, a light tilbury, you are naturally as slippery as
an eel; if I had missed you tonight, I might not have had another
chance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou see, I do not conceal myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are lucky; I wish I could say as much, for I do conceal myself;
and then I was afraid you would not recognize me, but you did,ГўВҖВқ added
Caderousse with his unpleasant smile. ГўВҖВңIt was very polite of you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңwhat do you want?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do not speak affectionately to me, Benedetto, my old friend, that
is not rightГўВҖВ”take care, or I may become troublesome.ГўВҖВқ

This menace smothered the young manГўВҖВҷs passion. He urged the horse again
into a trot.

ГўВҖВңYou should not speak so to an old friend like me, Caderousse, as you
said just now; you are a native of Marseilles, I amГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you know then now what you are?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, but I was brought up in Corsica; you are old and obstinate, I am
young and wilful. Between people like us threats are out of place,
everything should be amicably arranged. Is it my fault if fortune,
which has frowned on you, has been kind to me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFortune has been kind to you, then? Your tilbury, your groom, your
clothes, are not then hired? Good, so much the better,ГўВҖВқ said
Caderousse, his eyes sparkling with avarice.

ГўВҖВңOh, you knew that well enough before speaking to me,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea,
becoming more and more excited. ГўВҖВңIf I had been wearing a handkerchief
like yours on my head, rags on my back, and worn-out shoes on my feet,
you would not have known me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou wrong me, my boy; now I have found you, nothing prevents my being
as well-dressed as anyone, knowing, as I do, the goodness of your
heart. If you have two coats you will give me one of them. I used to
divide my soup and beans with you when you were hungry.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea.

ГўВҖВңWhat an appetite you used to have! Is it as good now?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes,ГўВҖВқ replied Andrea, laughing.

ГўВҖВңHow did you come to be dining with that prince whose house you have
just left?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is not a prince; simply a count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA count, and a rich one too, eh?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but you had better not have anything to say to him, for he is not
a very good-tempered gentleman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, be easy! I have no design upon your count, and you shall have him
all to yourself. But,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, again smiling with the
disagreeable expression he had before assumed, ГўВҖВңyou must pay for itГўВҖВ”you
understand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, what do you want?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think that with a hundred francs a monthГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI could liveГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUpon a hundred francs!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңComeГўВҖВ”you understand me; but that withГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith a hundred and fifty francs I should be quite happy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHere are two hundred,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea; and he placed ten gold louis in
the hand of Caderousse.

30233m



ГўВҖВңGood!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңApply to the steward on the first day of every month, and you will
receive the same sum.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere now, again you degrade me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy making me apply to the servants, when I want to transact business
with you alone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, be it so, then. Take it from me then, and so long at least as I
receive my income, you shall be paid yours.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, come; I always said you were a fine fellow, and it is a blessing
when good fortune happens to such as you. But tell me all about it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy do you wish to know?ГўВҖВқ asked Cavalcanti.

ГўВҖВңWhat? do you again defy me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; the fact is, I have found my father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? a real father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, so long as he pays meГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYouГўВҖВҷll honor and believe himГўВҖВ”thatГўВҖВҷs right. What is his name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMajor Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs he pleased with you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo far I have appeared to answer his purpose.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd who found this father for you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe Count of Monte Cristo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe man whose house you have just left?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI wish you would try and find me a situation with him as grandfather,
since he holds the money-chest!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I will mention you to him. Meanwhile, what are you going to do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is very kind of you to trouble yourself about me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSince you interest yourself in my affairs, I think it is now my turn
to ask you some questions.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, true. Well; I shall rent a room in some respectable house, wear a
decent coat, shave every day, and go and read the papers in a cafГғВ©.
Then, in the evening, I shall go to the theatre; I shall look like some
retired baker. That is what I want.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, if you will only put this scheme into execution, and be steady,
nothing could be better.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think so, M. Bossuet? And youГўВҖВ”what will you become? A peer of
France?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңwho knows?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMajor Cavalcanti is already one, perhaps; but then, hereditary rank is
abolished.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo politics, Caderousse. And now that you have all you want, and that
we understand each other, jump down from the tilbury and disappear.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot at all, my good friend.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow? Not at all?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, just think for a moment; with this red handkerchief on my head,
with scarcely any shoes, no papers, and ten gold napoleons in my
pocket, without reckoning what was there beforeГўВҖВ”making in all about two
hundred francs,ГўВҖВ”why, I should certainly be arrested at the barriers.
Then, to justify myself, I should say that you gave me the money; this
would cause inquiries, it would be found that I left Toulon without
giving due notice, and I should then be escorted back to the shores of
the Mediterranean. Then I should become simply No. 106, and good-bye to
my dream of resembling the retired baker! No, no, my boy; I prefer
remaining honorably in the capital.ГўВҖВқ

Andrea scowled. Certainly, as he had himself owned, the reputed son of
Major Cavalcanti was a wilful fellow. He drew up for a minute, threw a
rapid glance around him, and then his hand fell instantly into his
pocket, where it began playing with a pistol. But, meanwhile,
Caderousse, who had never taken his eyes off his companion, passed his
hand behind his back, and opened a long Spanish knife, which he always
carried with him, to be ready in case of need. The two friends, as we
see, were worthy of and understood one another. AndreaГўВҖВҷs hand left his
pocket inoffensively, and was carried up to the red moustache, which it
played with for some time.

ГўВҖВңGood Caderousse,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңhow happy you will be.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will do my best,ГўВҖВқ said the innkeeper of the Pont du Gard, shutting
up his knife.

ГўВҖВңWell, then, we will go into Paris. But how will you pass through the
barrier without exciting suspicion? It seems to me that you are in more
danger riding than on foot.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWait,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңwe shall see.ГўВҖВқ He then took the greatcoat
with the large collar, which the groom had left behind in the tilbury,
and put it on his back; then he took off CavalcantiГўВҖВҷs hat, which he
placed upon his own head, and finally he assumed the careless attitude
of a servant whose master drives himself.

ГўВҖВңBut, tell me,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңam I to remain bareheaded?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPooh,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңit is so windy that your hat can easily
appear to have blown off.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, come; enough of this,ГўВҖВқ said Cavalcanti.

ГўВҖВңWhat are you waiting for?ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВңI hope I am not the
cause.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHush,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea. They passed the barrier without accident. At the
first cross street Andrea stopped his horse, and Caderousse leaped out.

ГўВҖВңWell!ГўВҖВқ said Andrea,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңmy servantГўВҖВҷs coat and my hat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңyou would not like me to risk taking cold?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what am I to do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou? Oh, you are young while I am beginning to get old. _Au revoir_,
Benedetto;ГўВҖВқ and running into a court, he disappeared.

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, sighing, ГўВҖВңone cannot be completely happy in this
world!ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 65. A Conjugal Scene

At the Place Louis XV. the three young people separatedГўВҖВ”that is to say,
Morrel went to the Boulevards, ChГғВўteau-Renaud to the Pont de la
RГғВ©volution, and Debray to the Quai. Most probably Morrel and
ChГғВўteau-Renaud returned to their ГўВҖВңdomestic hearths,ГўВҖВқ as they say in the
gallery of the Chamber in well-turned speeches, and in the theatre of
the Rue Richelieu in well-written pieces; but it was not the case with
Debray. When he reached the wicket of the Louvre, he turned to the
left, galloped across the Carrousel, passed through the Rue Saint-Roch,
and, issuing from the Rue de la MichodiГғВЁre, he arrived at M. DanglarsГўВҖВҷ
door just at the same time that VillefortГўВҖВҷs landau, after having
deposited him and his wife at the Faubourg Saint-HonorГғВ©, stopped to
leave the baroness at her own house.

Debray, with the air of a man familiar with the house, entered first
into the court, threw his bridle into the hands of a footman, and
returned to the door to receive Madame Danglars, to whom he offered his
arm, to conduct her to her apartments. The gate once closed, and Debray
and the baroness alone in the court, he asked:

ГўВҖВңWhat was the matter with you, Hermine? and why were you so affected at
that story, or rather fable, which the count related?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause I have been in such shocking spirits all the evening, my
friend,ГўВҖВқ said the baroness.

ГўВҖВңNo, Hermine,ГўВҖВқ replied Debray; ГўВҖВңyou cannot make me believe that; on the
contrary, you were in excellent spirits when you arrived at the
countГўВҖВҷs. M. Danglars was disagreeable, certainly, but I know how much
you care for his ill-humor. Someone has vexed you; I will allow no one
to annoy you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are deceived, Lucien, I assure you,ГўВҖВқ replied Madame Danglars; ГўВҖВңand
what I have told you is really the case, added to the ill-humor you
remarked, but which I did not think it worth while to allude to.ГўВҖВқ

It was evident that Madame Danglars was suffering from that nervous
irritability which women frequently cannot account for even to
themselves; or that, as Debray had guessed, she had experienced some
secret agitation that she would not acknowledge to anyone. Being a man
who knew that the former of these symptoms was one of the inherent
penalties of womanhood, he did not then press his inquiries, but waited
for a more appropriate opportunity when he should again interrogate
her, or receive an avowal _proprio motu_.

At the door of her apartment the baroness met Mademoiselle CornГғВ©lie,
her confidential maid.

ГўВҖВңWhat is my daughter doing?ГўВҖВқ asked Madame Danglars.

ГўВҖВңShe practiced all the evening, and then went to bed,ГўВҖВқ replied
Mademoiselle CornГғВ©lie.

ГўВҖВңYet I think I hear her piano.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is Mademoiselle Louise dГўВҖВҷArmilly, who is playing while Mademoiselle
Danglars is in bed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңcome and undress me.ГўВҖВқ

They entered the bedroom. Debray stretched himself upon a large couch,
and Madame Danglars passed into her dressing-room with Mademoiselle
CornГғВ©lie.

ГўВҖВңMy dear M. Lucien,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars through the door, ГўВҖВңyou are
always complaining that EugГғВ©nie will not address a word to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ said Lucien, playing with a little dog, who, recognizing him
as a friend of the house, expected to be caressed, ГўВҖВңI am not the only
one who makes similar complaints, I think I heard Morcerf say that he
could not extract a word from his betrothed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars; ГўВҖВңyet I think this will all pass off, and
that you will one day see her enter your study.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy study?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt least that of the minister.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo ask for an engagement at the Opera. Really, I never saw such an
infatuation for music; it is quite ridiculous for a young lady of
fashion.ГўВҖВқ

Debray smiled. ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңlet her come, with your consent and
that of the baron, and we will try and give her an engagement, though
we are very poor to pay such talent as hers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo, CornГғВ©lie,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңI do not require you any
longer.ГўВҖВқ

CornГғВ©lie obeyed, and the next minute Madame Danglars left her room in a
charming loose dress, and came and sat down close to Debray. Then she
began thoughtfully to caress the little spaniel. Lucien looked at her
for a moment in silence.

ГўВҖВңCome, Hermine,ГўВҖВқ he said, after a short time, ГўВҖВңanswer
candidly,ГўВҖВ”something vexes youГўВҖВ”is it not so?ГўВҖВқ

30239m



ГўВҖВңNothing,ГўВҖВқ answered the baroness.

And yet, as she could scarcely breathe, she rose and went towards a
looking-glass. ГўВҖВңI am frightful tonight,ГўВҖВқ she said. Debray rose,
smiling, and was about to contradict the baroness upon this latter
point, when the door opened suddenly. M. Danglars appeared; Debray
reseated himself. At the noise of the door Madame Danglars turned
round, and looked upon her husband with an astonishment she took no
trouble to conceal.

ГўВҖВңGood-evening, madame,ГўВҖВқ said the banker; ГўВҖВңgood-evening, M. Debray.ГўВҖВқ

Probably the baroness thought this unexpected visit signified a desire
to make up for the sharp words he had uttered during the day. Assuming
a dignified air, she turned round to Debray, without answering her
husband.

ГўВҖВңRead me something, M. Debray,ГўВҖВқ she said. Debray, who was slightly
disturbed at this visit, recovered himself when he saw the calmness of
the baroness, and took up a book marked by a mother-of-pearl knife
inlaid with gold.

ГўВҖВңExcuse me,ГўВҖВқ said the banker, ГўВҖВңbut you will tire yourself, baroness, by
such late hours, and M. Debray lives some distance from here.ГўВҖВқ

Debray was petrified, not only to hear Danglars speak so calmly and
politely, but because it was apparent that beneath outward politeness
there really lurked a determined spirit of opposition to anything his
wife might wish to do. The baroness was also surprised, and showed her
astonishment by a look which would doubtless have had some effect upon
her husband if he had not been intently occupied with the paper, where
he was looking to see the closing stock quotations. The result was,
that the proud look entirely failed of its purpose.

ГўВҖВңM. Lucien,ГўВҖВқ said the baroness, ГўВҖВңI assure you I have no desire to
sleep, and that I have a thousand things to tell you this evening,
which you must listen to, even though you slept while hearing me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am at your service, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Lucien coldly.

ГўВҖВңMy dear M. Debray,ГўВҖВқ said the banker, ГўВҖВңdo not kill yourself tonight
listening to the follies of Madame Danglars, for you can hear them as
well tomorrow; but I claim tonight and will devote it, if you will
allow me, to talk over some serious matters with my wife.ГўВҖВқ

This time the blow was so well aimed, and hit so directly, that Lucien
and the baroness were staggered, and they interrogated each other with
their eyes, as if to seek help against this aggression, but the
irresistible will of the master of the house prevailed, and the husband
was victorious.

ГўВҖВңDo not think I wish to turn you out, my dear Debray,ГўВҖВқ continued
Danglars; ГўВҖВңoh, no, not at all. An unexpected occurrence forces me to
ask my wife to have a little conversation with me; it is so rarely I
make such a request, I am sure you cannot grudge it to me.ГўВҖВқ

Debray muttered something, bowed and went out, knocking himself against
the edge of the door, like Nathan in _Athalie_.

ГўВҖВңIt is extraordinary,ГўВҖВқ he said, when the door was closed behind him,
ГўВҖВңhow easily these husbands, whom we ridicule, gain an advantage over
us.ГўВҖВқ

30241m



Lucien having left, Danglars took his place on the sofa, closed the
open book, and placing himself in a dreadfully dictatorial attitude, he
began playing with the dog; but the animal, not liking him as well as
Debray, and attempting to bite him, Danglars seized him by the skin of
his neck and threw him upon a couch on the other side of the room. The
animal uttered a cry during the transit, but, arrived at its
destination, it crouched behind the cushions, and stupefied at such
unusual treatment remained silent and motionless.

ГўВҖВңDo you know, sir,ГўВҖВқ asked the baroness, ГўВҖВңthat you are improving?
Generally you are only rude, but tonight you are brutal.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is because I am in a worse humor than usual,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars.
Hermine looked at the banker with supreme disdain. These glances
frequently exasperated the pride of Danglars, but this evening he took
no notice of them.

ГўВҖВңAnd what have I to do with your ill-humor?ГўВҖВқ said the baroness,
irritated at the impassibility of her husband; ГўВҖВңdo these things concern
me? Keep your ill-humor at home in your money boxes, or, since you have
clerks whom you pay, vent it upon them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot so,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars; ГўВҖВңyour advice is wrong, so I shall not
follow it. My money boxes are my Pactolus, as, I think, M. Demoustier
says, and I will not retard its course, or disturb its calm. My clerks
are honest men, who earn my fortune, whom I pay much below their
deserts, if I may value them according to what they bring in; therefore
I shall not get into a passion with them; those with whom I will be in
a passion are those who eat my dinners, mount my horses, and exhaust my
fortune.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd pray who are the persons who exhaust your fortune? Explain
yourself more clearly, I beg, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, make yourself easy!ГўВҖВ”I am not speaking riddles, and you will soon
know what I mean. The people who exhaust my fortune are those who draw
out 700,000 francs in the course of an hour.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not understand you, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the baroness, trying to disguise
the agitation of her voice and the flush of her face.

ГўВҖВңYou understand me perfectly, on the contrary,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars: ГўВҖВңbut, if
you will persist, I will tell you that I have just lost 700,000 francs
upon the Spanish loan.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd pray,ГўВҖВқ asked the baroness, ГўВҖВңam I responsible for this loss?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs it my fault you have lost 700,000 francs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly it is not mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOnce for all, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the baroness sharply, ГўВҖВңI tell you I will
not hear cash named; it is a style of language I never heard in the
house of my parents or in that of my first husband.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I can well believe that, for neither of them was worth a penny.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe better reason for my not being conversant with the slang of the
bank, which is here dinning in my ears from morning to night; that
noise of jingling crowns, which are constantly being counted and
re-counted, is odious to me. I only know one thing I dislike more,
which is the sound of your voice.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally?ГўВҖВқ said Danglars. ГўВҖВңWell, this surprises me, for I thought you
took the liveliest interest in all my affairs!ГўВҖВқ

30243m



ГўВҖВңI? What could put such an idea into your head?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh?ГўВҖВ”what next?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMost assuredly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI should like to know upon what occasion?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, _mon Dieu!_ that is very easily done. Last February you were the
first who told me of the Haitian funds. You had dreamed that a ship had
entered the harbor at Le Havre, that this ship brought news that a
payment we had looked upon as lost was going to be made. I know how
clear-sighted your dreams are; I therefore purchased immediately as
many shares as I could of the Haitian debt, and I gained 400,000 francs
by it, of which 100,000 have been honestly paid to you. You spent it as
you pleased; that was your business. In March there was a question
about a grant to a railway. Three companies presented themselves, each
offering equal securities. You told me that your instinct,ГўВҖВ”and although
you pretend to know nothing about speculations, I think on the
contrary, that your comprehension is very clear upon certain
affairs,ГўВҖВ”well, you told me that your instinct led you to believe the
grant would be given to the company called the Southern. I bought two
thirds of the shares of that company; as you had foreseen, the shares
trebled in value, and I picked up a million, from which 250,000 francs
were paid to you for pin-money. How have you spent this 250,000
francs?ГўВҖВ”it is no business of mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen are you coming to the point?ГўВҖВқ cried the baroness, shivering with
anger and impatience.

ГўВҖВңPatience, madame, I am coming to it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs fortunate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn April you went to dine at the ministerГўВҖВҷs. You heard a private
conversation respecting Spanish affairsГўВҖВ”on the expulsion of Don Carlos.
I bought some Spanish shares. The expulsion took place and I pocketed
600,000 francs the day Charles V. repassed the Bidassoa. Of these
600,000 francs you took 50,000 crowns. They were yours, you disposed of
them according to your fancy, and I asked no questions; but it is not
the less true that you have this year received 500,000 livres.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sir, and what then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, yes, it was just after this that you spoiled everything.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally, your manner of speakingГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt expresses my meaning, and that is all I want. Well, three days
after that you talked politics with M. Debray, and you fancied from his
words that Don Carlos had returned to Spain. Well, I sold my shares,
the news got out, and I no longer soldГўВҖВ”I gave them away, next day I
find the news was false, and by this false report I have lost 700,000
francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, since I gave you a fourth of my gains, I think you owe me a
fourth of my losses; the fourth of 700,000 francs is 175,000 francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat you say is absurd, and I cannot see why M. DebrayГўВҖВҷs name is mixed
up in this affair.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause if you do not possess the 175,000 francs I reclaim, you must
have lent them to your friends, and M. Debray is one of your friends.ГўВҖВқ

30245m



ГўВҖВңFor shame!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the baroness.

ГўВҖВңOh, let us have no gestures, no screams, no modern drama, or you will
oblige me to tell you that I see Debray leave here, pocketing the whole
of the 500,000 livres you have handed over to him this year, while he
smiles to himself, saying that he has found what the most skilful
players have never discoveredГўВҖВ”that is, a roulette where he wins without
playing, and is no loser when he loses.ГўВҖВқ

The baroness became enraged.

ГўВҖВңWretch!ГўВҖВқ she cried, ГўВҖВңwill you dare to tell me you did not know what
you now reproach me with?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not say that I did know it, and I do not say that I did not know
it. I merely tell you to look into my conduct during the last four
years that we have ceased to be husband and wife, and see whether it
has not always been consistent. Some time after our rupture, you wished
to study music, under the celebrated baritone who made such a
successful appearance at the ThГғВ©ГғВўtre Italien; at the same time I felt
inclined to learn dancing of the _danseuse_ who acquired such a
reputation in London. This cost me, on your account and mine, 100,000
francs. I said nothing, for we must have peace in the house; and
100,000 francs for a lady and gentleman to be properly instructed in
music and dancing are not too much. Well, you soon become tired of
singing, and you take a fancy to study diplomacy with the ministerГўВҖВҷs
secretary. You understand, it signifies nothing to me so long as you
pay for your lessons out of your own cash box. But today I find you are
drawing on mine, and that your apprenticeship may cost me 700,000
francs per month. Stop there, madame, for this cannot last. Either the
diplomatist must give his lessons gratis, and I will tolerate him, or
he must never set his foot again in my house;ГўВҖВ”do you understand,
madame?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, this is too much,ГўВҖВқ cried Hermine, choking, ГўВҖВңyou are worse than
despicable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ continued Danglars, ГўВҖВңI find you did not even pause thereГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңInsults!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right; let us leave these facts alone, and reason coolly. I
have never interfered in your affairs excepting for your good; treat me
in the same way. You say you have nothing to do with my cash box. Be it
so. Do as you like with your own, but do not fill or empty mine.
Besides, how do I know that this was not a political trick, that the
minister enraged at seeing me in the opposition, and jealous of the
popular sympathy I excite, has not concerted with M. Debray to ruin
me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA probable thing!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy not? Who ever heard of such an occurrence as this?ГўВҖВ”a false
telegraphic despatchГўВҖВ”it is almost impossible for wrong signals to be
made as they were in the last two telegrams. It was done on purpose for
meГўВҖВ”I am sure of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said the baroness humbly, ГўВҖВңare you not aware that the man
employed there was dismissed, that they talked of going to law with
him, that orders were issued to arrest him and that this order would
have been put into execution if he had not escaped by flight, which
proves that he was either mad or guilty? It was a mistake.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, which made fools laugh, which caused the minister to have a
sleepless night, which has caused the ministerГўВҖВҷs secretaries to blacken
several sheets of paper, but which has cost me 700,000 francs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Hermine suddenly, ГўВҖВңif all this is, as you say, caused
by M. Debray, why, instead of going direct to him, do you come and tell
me of it? Why, to accuse the man, do you address the woman?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo I know M. Debray?ГўВҖВ”do I wish to know him?ГўВҖВ”do I wish to know that he
gives advice?ГўВҖВ”do I wish to follow it?ГўВҖВ”do I speculate? No; you do all
this, not I.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill it seems to me, that as you profit by itГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Danglars shrugged his shoulders. ГўВҖВңFoolish creature,ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed.
ГўВҖВңWomen fancy they have talent because they have managed two or three
intrigues without being the talk of Paris! But know that if you had
even hidden your irregularities from your husband, who has but the
commencement of the artГўВҖВ”for generally husbands _will_ not seeГўВҖВ”you would
then have been but a faint imitation of most of your friends among the
women of the world. But it has not been so with me,ГўВҖВ”I see, and always
have seen, during the last sixteen years. You may, perhaps, have hidden
a thought; but not a step, not an action, not a fault, has escaped me,
while you flattered yourself upon your address, and firmly believed you
had deceived me. What has been the result?ГўВҖВ”that, thanks to my pretended
ignorance, there is none of your friends, from M. de Villefort to M.
Debray, who has not trembled before me. There is not one who has not
treated me as the master of the house,ГўВҖВ”the only title I desire with
respect to you; there is not one, in fact, who would have dared to
speak of me as I have spoken of them this day. I will allow you to make
me hateful, but I will prevent your rendering me ridiculous, and, above
all, I forbid you to ruin me.ГўВҖВқ

The baroness had been tolerably composed until the name of Villefort
had been pronounced; but then she became pale, and, rising, as if
touched by a spring, she stretched out her hands as though conjuring an
apparition; she then took two or three steps towards her husband, as
though to tear the secret from him, of which he was ignorant, or which
he withheld from some odious calculation,ГўВҖВ”odious, as all his
calculations were.

ГўВҖВңM. de Villefort!ГўВҖВ”What do you mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI mean that M. de Nargonne, your first husband, being neither a
philosopher nor a banker, or perhaps being both, and seeing there was
nothing to be got out of a kingГўВҖВҷs attorney, died of grief or anger at
finding, after an absence of nine months, that you had been _enceinte_
six. I am brutal,ГўВҖВ”I not only allow it, but boast of it; it is one of
the reasons of my success in commercial business. Why did he kill
himself instead of you? Because he had no cash to save. My life belongs
to my cash. M. Debray has made me lose 700,000 francs; let him bear his
share of the loss, and we will go on as before; if not, let him become
bankrupt for the 250,000 livres, and do as all bankrupts doГўВҖВ”disappear.
He is a charming fellow, I allow, when his news is correct; but when it
is not, there are fifty others in the world who would do better than
he.ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars was rooted to the spot; she made a violent effort to
reply to this last attack, but she fell upon a chair thinking of
Villefort, of the dinner scene, of the strange series of misfortunes
which had taken place in her house during the last few days, and
changed the usual calm of her establishment to a scene of scandalous
debate.

Danglars did not even look at her, though she did her best to faint. He
shut the bedroom door after him, without adding another word, and
returned to his apartments; and when Madame Danglars recovered from her
half-fainting condition, she could almost believe that she had had a
disagreeable dream.



 Chapter 66. Matrimonial Projects

The day following this scene, at the hour Debray usually chose to pay a
visit to Madame Danglars on his way to his office, his _coupГғВ©_ did not
appear. At this time, that is, about half-past twelve, Madame Danglars
ordered her carriage, and went out. Danglars, hidden behind a curtain,
watched the departure he had been waiting for. He gave orders that he
should be informed as soon as Madame Danglars appeared; but at two
oГўВҖВҷclock she had not returned. He then called for his horses, drove to
the Chamber, and inscribed his name to speak against the budget. From
twelve to two oГўВҖВҷclock Danglars had remained in his study, unsealing his
dispatches, and becoming more and more sad every minute, heaping figure
upon figure, and receiving, among other visits, one from Major
Cavalcanti, who, as stiff and exact as ever, presented himself
precisely at the hour named the night before, to terminate his business
with the banker.

On leaving the Chamber, Danglars, who had shown violent marks of
agitation during the sitting, and been more bitter than ever against
the ministry, re-entered his carriage, and told the coachman to drive
to the Avenue des Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es, No. 30.

Monte Cristo was at home; only he was engaged with someone and begged
Danglars to wait for a moment in the drawing-room. While the banker was
waiting in the anteroom, the door opened, and a man dressed as an abbГғВ©
and doubtless more familiar with the house than he was, came in and
instead of waiting, merely bowed, passed on to the farther apartments,
and disappeared.

A minute after the door by which the priest had entered reopened, and
Monte Cristo appeared.

ГўВҖВңPardon me,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңmy dear baron, but one of my friends, the AbbГғВ©
Busoni, whom you perhaps saw pass by, has just arrived in Paris; not
having seen him for a long time, I could not make up my mind to leave
him sooner, so I hope this will be sufficient reason for my having made
you wait.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNay,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңit is my fault; I have chosen my visit at a
wrong time, and will retire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot at all; on the contrary, be seated; but what is the matter with
you? You look careworn; really, you alarm me. Melancholy in a
capitalist, like the appearance of a comet, presages some misfortune to
the world.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have been in ill-luck for several days,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңand I have
heard nothing but bad news.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңHave you had another fall at the
Bourse?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; I am safe for a few days at least. I am only annoyed about a
bankrupt of Trieste.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally? Does it happen to be Jacopo Manfredi?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly so. Imagine a man who has transacted business with me for I
donГўВҖВҷt know how long, to the amount of 800,000 or 900,000 francs during
the year. Never a mistake or delayГўВҖВ”a fellow who paid like a prince.
Well, I was a million in advance with him, and now my fine Jacopo
Manfredi suspends payment!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is an unheard-of fatality. I draw upon him for 600,000 francs, my
bills are returned unpaid, and, more than that, I hold bills of
exchange signed by him to the value of 400,000 francs, payable at his
correspondentГўВҖВҷs in Paris at the end of this month. Today is the 30th. I
present them; but my correspondent has disappeared. This, with my
Spanish affairs, made a pretty end to the month.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you really lost by that affair in Spain?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; only 700,000 francs out of my cash boxГўВҖВ”nothing more!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, how could you make such a mistakeГўВҖВ”such an old stager?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, it is all my wifeГўВҖВҷs fault. She dreamed Don Carlos had returned to
Spain; she believes in dreams. It is magnetism, she says, and when she
dreams a thing it is sure to happen, she assures me. On this conviction
I allow her to speculate, she having her bank and her stockbroker; she
speculated and lost. It is true she speculates with her own money, not
mine; nevertheless, you can understand that when 700,000 francs leave
the wifeГўВҖВҷs pocket, the husband always finds it out. But do you mean to
say you have not heard of this? Why, the thing has made a tremendous
noise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I heard it spoken of, but I did not know the details, and then no
one can be more ignorant than I am of the affairs in the Bourse.ГўВҖВқ

30251m



ГўВҖВңThen you do not speculate?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВ”How could I speculate when I already have so much trouble in
regulating my income? I should be obliged, besides my steward, to keep
a clerk and a boy. But touching these Spanish affairs, I think that the
baroness did not dream the whole of the Don Carlos matter. The papers
said something about it, did they not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you believe the papers?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВ”not the least in the world; only I fancied that the honest
_Messager_ was an exception to the rule, and that it only announced
telegraphic despatches.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, thatГўВҖВҷs what puzzles me,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars; ГўВҖВңthe news of the
return of Don Carlos was brought by telegraph.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo that,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңyou have lost nearly 1,700,000 francs
this month.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot nearly, indeed; that is exactly my loss.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Diable!_ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo compassionately, ГўВҖВңit is a hard blow for a
third-rate fortune.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThird-rate,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, rather humble, ГўВҖВңwhat do you mean by that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly,ГўВҖВқ continued Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI make three assortments in
fortuneГўВҖВ”first-rate, second-rate, and third-rate fortunes. I call those
first-rate which are composed of treasures one possesses under oneГўВҖВҷs
hand, such as mines, lands, and funded property, in such states as
France, Austria, and England, provided these treasures and property
form a total of about a hundred millions; I call those second-rate
fortunes, that are gained by manufacturing enterprises, joint-stock
companies, viceroyalties, and principalities, not drawing more than
1,500,000 francs, the whole forming a capital of about fifty millions;
finally, I call those third-rate fortunes, which are composed of a
fluctuating capital, dependent upon the will of others, or upon chances
which a bankruptcy involves or a false telegram shakes, such as banks,
speculations of the dayГўВҖВ”in fact, all operations under the influence of
greater or less mischances, the whole bringing in a real or fictitious
capital of about fifteen millions. I think this is about your position,
is it not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңConfound it, yes!ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars.

ГўВҖВңThe result, then, of six more such months as this would be to reduce
the third-rate house to despair.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, becoming very pale, how you are running on!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLet us imagine seven such months,ГўВҖВқ continued Monte Cristo, in the same
tone. ГўВҖВңTell me, have you ever thought that seven times 1,700,000 francs
make nearly twelve millions? No, you have not;ГўВҖВ”well, you are right, for
if you indulged in such reflections, you would never risk your
principal, which is to the speculator what the skin is to civilized
man. We have our clothes, some more splendid than others,ГўВҖВ”this is our
credit; but when a man dies he has only his skin; in the same way, on
retiring from business, you have nothing but your real principal of
about five or six millions, at the most; for third-rate fortunes are
never more than a fourth of what they appear to be, like the locomotive
on a railway, the size of which is magnified by the smoke and steam
surrounding it. Well, out of the five or six millions which form your
real capital, you have just lost nearly two millions, which must, of
course, in the same degree diminish your credit and fictitious fortune;
to follow out my simile, your skin has been opened by bleeding, and
this if repeated three or four times will cause deathГўВҖВ”so pay attention
to it, my dear Monsieur Danglars. Do you want money? Do you wish me to
lend you some?ГўВҖВқ

30253m



ГўВҖВңWhat a bad calculator you are!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Danglars, calling to his
assistance all his philosophy and dissimulation. ГўВҖВңI have made money at
the same time by speculations which have succeeded. I have made up the
loss of blood by nutrition. I lost a battle in Spain, I have been
defeated in Trieste, but my naval army in India will have taken some
galleons, and my Mexican pioneers will have discovered some mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery good, very good! But the wound remains and will reopen at the
first loss.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, for I am only embarked in certainties,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars, with the
air of a mountebank sounding his own praises; ГўВҖВңto involve me, three
governments must crumble to dust.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, such things have been.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat there should be a famine!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRecollect the seven fat and the seven lean kine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOr, that the sea should become dry, as in the days of Pharaoh, and
even then my vessels would become caravans.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo much the better. I congratulate you, my dear M. Danglars,ГўВҖВқ said
Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI see I was deceived, and that you belong to the class
of second-rate fortunes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think I may aspire to that honor,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars with a smile, which
reminded Monte Cristo of the sickly moons which bad artists are so fond
of daubing into their pictures of ruins. ГўВҖВңBut, while we are speaking of
business,ГўВҖВқ Danglars added, pleased to find an opportunity of changing
the subject, ГўВҖВңtell me what I am to do for M. Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGive him money, if he is recommended to you, and the recommendation
seems good.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExcellent; he presented himself this morning with a bond of 40,000
francs, payable at sight, on you, signed by Busoni, and returned by you
to me, with your endorsementГўВҖВ”of course, I immediately counted him over
the forty bank-notes.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo nodded his head in token of assent.

ГўВҖВңBut that is not all,ГўВҖВқ continued Danglars; ГўВҖВңhe has opened an account
with my house for his son.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMay I ask how much he allows the young man?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFive thousand francs per month.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSixty thousand francs per year. I thought I was right in believing
that Cavalcanti to be a stingy fellow. How can a young man live upon
5,000 francs a month?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you understand that if the young man should want a few thousands
moreГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not advance it; the father will never repay it. You do not know
these ultramontane millionaires; they are regular misers. And by whom
were they recommended to you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, by the house of Fenzi, one of the best in Florence.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not mean to say you will lose, but, nevertheless, mind you hold
to the terms of the agreement.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWould you not trust the Cavalcanti?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI? oh, I would advance ten millions on his signature. I was only
speaking in reference to the second-rate fortunes we were mentioning
just now.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd with all this, how unassuming he is! I should never have taken him
for anything more than a mere major.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you would have flattered him, for certainly, as you say, he has no
manner. The first time I saw him he appeared to me like an old
lieutenant who had grown mouldy under his epaulets. But all the
Italians are the same; they are like old Jews when they are not
glittering in Oriental splendor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe young man is better,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВңYes; a little nervous, perhaps, but, upon the whole, he appeared
tolerable. I was uneasy about him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause you met him at my house, just after his introduction into the
world, as they told me. He has been travelling with a very severe
tutor, and had never been to Paris before.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, I believe noblemen marry amongst themselves, do they not?ГўВҖВқ asked
Danglars carelessly; ГўВҖВңthey like to unite their fortunes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is usual, certainly; but Cavalcanti is an original who does nothing
like other people. I cannot help thinking that he has brought his son
to France to choose a wife.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am sure of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you have heard his fortune mentioned?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNothing else was talked of; only some said he was worth millions, and
others that he did not possess a farthing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what is your opinion?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI ought not to influence you, because it is only my own personal
impression.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, and it is thatГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy opinion is, that all these old _podestГғВ s_, these ancient
_condottieri_,ГўВҖВ”for the Cavalcanti have commanded armies and governed
provinces,ГўВҖВ”my opinion, I say, is, that they have buried their millions
in corners, the secret of which they have transmitted only to their
eldest sons, who have done the same from generation to generation; and
the proof of this is seen in their yellow and dry appearance, like the
florins of the republic, which, from being constantly gazed upon, have
become reflected in them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңand this is further supported by the fact
of their not possessing an inch of land.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery little, at least; I know of none which Cavalcanti possesses,
excepting his palace in Lucca.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, he has a palace?ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, laughing; ГўВҖВңcome, that is
something.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; and more than that, he lets it to the Minister of Finance while
he lives in a simple house. Oh, as I told you before, I think the old
fellow is very close.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, you do not flatter him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI scarcely know him; I think I have seen him three times in my life;
all I know relating to him is through Busoni and himself. He was
telling me this morning that, tired of letting his property lie dormant
in Italy, which is a dead nation, he wished to find a method, either in
France or England, of multiplying his millions, but remember, that
though I place great confidence in Busoni, I am not responsible for
this.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever mind; accept my thanks for the client you have sent me. It is a
fine name to inscribe on my ledgers, and my cashier was quite proud of
it when I explained to him who the Cavalcanti were. By the way, this is
merely a simple question, when this sort of people marry their sons, do
they give them any fortune?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, that depends upon circumstances. I know an Italian prince, rich as
a gold mine, one of the noblest families in Tuscany, who, when his sons
married according to his wish, gave them millions; and when they
married against his consent, merely allowed them thirty crowns a month.
Should Andrea marry according to his fatherГўВҖВҷs views, he will, perhaps,
give him one, two, or three millions. For example, supposing it were
the daughter of a banker, he might take an interest in the house of the
father-in-law of his son; then again, if he disliked his choice, the
major takes the key, double-locks his coffer, and Master Andrea would
be obliged to live like the sons of a Parisian family, by shuffling
cards or rattling the dice.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, that boy will find out some Bavarian or Peruvian princess; he will
want a crown, an El Dorado, and PotosГғВӯ.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; these grand lords on the other side of the Alps frequently marry
into plain families; like Jupiter, they like to cross the race. But do
you wish to marry Andrea, my dear M. Danglars, that you are asking so
many questions?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi_,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңit would not be a bad speculation, I fancy,
and you know I am a speculator.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are not thinking of Mademoiselle Danglars, I hope; you would not
like poor Andrea to have his throat cut by Albert?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlbert,ГўВҖВқ repeated Danglars, shrugging his shoulders; ГўВҖВңah, well; he
would care very little about it, I think.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut he is betrothed to your daughter, I believe?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, M. de Morcerf and I have talked about this marriage, but Madame
de Morcerf and AlbertГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do not mean to say that it would not be a good match?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed, I imagine that Mademoiselle Danglars is as good as M. de
Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMademoiselle DanglarsГўВҖВҷ fortune will be great, no doubt, especially if
the telegraph should not make any more mistakes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I do not mean her fortune only; but tell meГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy did you not invite M. and Madame de Morcerf to your dinner?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI did so, but he excused himself on account of Madame de Morcerf being
obliged to go to Dieppe for the benefit of sea air.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, laughing, ГўВҖВңit would do her a great deal of
good.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause it is the air she always breathed in her youth.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo took no notice of this ill-natured remark.

ГўВҖВңBut still, if Albert be not so rich as Mademoiselle Danglars,ГўВҖВқ said
the count, ГўВҖВңyou must allow that he has a fine name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo he has; but I like mine as well.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly; your name is popular, and does honor to the title they have
adorned it with; but you are too intelligent not to know that according
to a prejudice, too firmly rooted to be exterminated, a nobility which
dates back five centuries is worth more than one that can only reckon
twenty years.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd for this very reason,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars with a smile, which he tried
to make sardonic, ГўВҖВңI prefer M. Andrea Cavalcanti to M. Albert de
Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill, I should not think the Morcerfs would yield to the Cavalcanti?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe Morcerfs!ГўВҖВ”Stay, my dear count,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңyou are a man of
the world, are you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you understand heraldry?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA little.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, look at my coat-of-arms, it is worth more than MorcerfГўВҖВҷs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause, though I am not a baron by birth, my real name is, at least,
Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, what then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhile his name is not Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow?ГўВҖВ”not Morcerf?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot the least in the world.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have been made a baron, so that I actually am one; he made himself a
count, so that he is not one at all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңImpossible!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen my dear count; M. de Morcerf has been my friend, or rather my
acquaintance, during the last thirty years. You know I have made the
most of my arms, though I never forgot my origin.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA proof of great humility or great pride,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңWell, when I was a clerk, Morcerf was a mere fisherman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd then he was calledГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFernand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOnly Fernand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFernand Mondego.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are sure?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Pardieu!_ I have bought enough fish of him to know his name.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, why did you think of giving your daughter to him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause Fernand and Danglars, being both parvenus, both having become
noble, both rich, are about equal in worth, excepting that there have
been certain things mentioned of him that were never said of me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, nothing!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, yes; what you tell me recalls to mind something about the name of
Fernand Mondego. I have heard that name in Greece.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn conjunction with the affairs of Ali Pasha?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is the mystery,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars. ГўВҖВңI acknowledge I would have given
anything to find it out.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt would be very easy if you much wished it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProbably you have some correspondent in Greece?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI should think so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt Yanina?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEverywhere.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, write to your correspondent in Yanina, and ask him what part was
played by a Frenchman named Fernand Mondego in the catastrophe of Ali
Tepelini.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Danglars, rising quickly, ГўВҖВңI will write
today.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will.ГўВҖВқ

30259m



ГўВҖВңAnd if you should hear of anything very scandalousГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will communicate it to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will oblige me.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars rushed out of the room, and made but one leap into his
_coupГғВ©_.



 Chapter 67. The Office of the KingГўВҖВҷs Attorney

Let us leave the banker driving his horses at their fullest speed, and
follow Madame Danglars in her morning excursion. We have said that at
half-past twelve oГўВҖВҷclock Madame Danglars had ordered her horses, and
had left home in the carriage. She directed her course towards the
Faubourg Saint Germain, went down the Rue Mazarine, and stopped at the
Passage du Pont-Neuf. She descended, and went through the passage. She
was very plainly dressed, as would be the case with a woman of taste
walking in the morning. At the Rue GuГғВ©nГғВ©gaud she called a cab, and
directed the driver to go to the Rue de Harlay. As soon as she was
seated in the vehicle, she drew from her pocket a very thick black
veil, which she tied on to her straw bonnet. She then replaced the
bonnet, and saw with pleasure, in a little pocket-mirror, that her
white complexion and brilliant eyes were alone visible. The cab crossed
the Pont-Neuf and entered the Rue de Harlay by the Place Dauphine; the
driver was paid as the door opened, and stepping lightly up the stairs
Madame Danglars soon reached the Salle des Pas-Perdus.

There was a great deal going on that morning, and many business-like
persons at the Palais; business-like persons pay very little attention
to women, and Madame Danglars crossed the hall without exciting any
more attention than any other woman calling upon her lawyer.

There was a great press of people in M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs antechamber, but
Madame Danglars had no occasion even to pronounce her name. The instant
she appeared the door-keeper rose, came to her, and asked her whether
she was not the person with whom the procureur had made an appointment;
and on her affirmative answer being given, he conducted her by a
private passage to M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs office.

The magistrate was seated in an armchair, writing, with his back
towards the door; he did not move as he heard it open, and the
door-keeper pronounce the words, ГўВҖВңWalk in, madame,ГўВҖВқ and then reclose
it; but no sooner had the manГўВҖВҷs footsteps ceased, than he started up,
drew the bolts, closed the curtains, and examined every corner of the
room. Then, when he had assured himself that he could neither be seen
nor heard, and was consequently relieved of doubts, he said:

ГўВҖВңThanks, madame,ГўВҖВ”thanks for your punctuality;ГўВҖВқ and he offered a chair
to Madame Danglars, which she accepted, for her heart beat so violently
that she felt nearly suffocated.

30261m



ГўВҖВңIt is a long time, madame,ГўВҖВқ said the procureur, describing a
half-circle with his chair, so as to place himself exactly opposite to
Madame Danglars,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңit is a long time since I had the pleasure of
speaking alone with you, and I regret that we have only now met to
enter upon a painful conversation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNevertheless, sir, you see I have answered your first appeal, although
certainly the conversation must be much more painful for me than for
you.ГўВҖВқ Villefort smiled bitterly.

ГўВҖВңIt is true, then,ГўВҖВқ he said, rather uttering his thoughts aloud than
addressing his companion,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңit is true, then, that all our actions leave
their tracesГўВҖВ”some sad, others brightГўВҖВ”on our paths; it is true that
every step in our lives is like the course of an insect on the
sands;ГўВҖВ”it leaves its track! Alas, to many the path is traced by tears.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңyou can feel for my emotion, can you not?
Spare me, then, I beseech you. When I look at this room,ГўВҖВ”whence so many
guilty creatures have departed, trembling and ashamed, when I look at
that chair before which I now sit trembling and ashamed,ГўВҖВ”oh, it
requires all my reason to convince me that I am not a very guilty woman
and you a menacing judge.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort dropped his head and sighed.

ГўВҖВңAnd I,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңI feel that my place is not in the judgeГўВҖВҷs seat, but
on the prisonerГўВҖВҷs bench.ГўВҖВқ

30263m



ГўВҖВңYou?ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars.

ГўВҖВңYes, I.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think, sir, you exaggerate your situation,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars,
whose beautiful eyes sparkled for a moment. ГўВҖВңThe paths of which you
were just speaking have been traced by all young men of ardent
imaginations. Besides the pleasure, there is always remorse from the
indulgence of our passions, and, after all, what have you men to fear
from all this? the world excuses, and notoriety ennobles you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, ГўВҖВңyou know that I am no hypocrite, or, at
least, that I never deceive without a reason. If my brow be severe, it
is because many misfortunes have clouded it; if my heart be petrified,
it is that it might sustain the blows it has received. I was not so in
my youth, I was not so on the night of the betrothal, when we were all
seated around a table in the Rue du Cours at Marseilles. But since then
everything has changed in and about me; I am accustomed to brave
difficulties, and, in the conflict to crush those who, by their own
free will, or by chance, voluntarily or involuntarily, interfere with
me in my career. It is generally the case that what we most ardently
desire is as ardently withheld from us by those who wish to obtain it,
or from whom we attempt to snatch it. Thus, the greater number of a
manГўВҖВҷs errors come before him disguised under the specious form of
necessity; then, after error has been committed in a moment of
excitement, of delirium, or of fear, we see that we might have avoided
and escaped it. The means we might have used, which we in our blindness
could not see, then seem simple and easy, and we say, ГўВҖВҳWhy did I not do
this, instead of that?ГўВҖВҷ Women, on the contrary, are rarely tormented
with remorse; for the decision does not come from you,ГўВҖВ”your misfortunes
are generally imposed upon you, and your faults the results of othersГўВҖВҷ
crimes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn any case, sir, you will allow,ГўВҖВқ replied Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңthat,
even if the fault were alone mine, I last night received a severe
punishment for it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoor thing,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, pressing her hand, ГўВҖВңit was too severe for
your strength, for you were twice overwhelmed, and yetГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I must tell you. Collect all your courage, for you have not yet
heard all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Madame Danglars, alarmed, ГўВҖВңwhat is there more to hear?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou only look back to the past, and it is, indeed, bad enough. Well,
picture to yourself a future more gloomy stillГўВҖВ”certainly frightful,
perhaps sanguinary!ГўВҖВқ

The baroness knew how calm Villefort naturally was, and his present
excitement frightened her so much that she opened her mouth to scream,
but the sound died in her throat.

ГўВҖВңHow has this terrible past been recalled?ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort; ГўВҖВңhow is it
that it has escaped from the depths of the tomb and the recesses of our
hearts, where it was buried, to visit us now, like a phantom, whitening
our cheeks and flushing our brows with shame?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ said Hermine, ГўВҖВңdoubtless it is chance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңChance?ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort; ГўВҖВңNo, no, madame, there is no such thing as
chance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes; has not a fatal chance revealed all this? Was it not by
chance the Count of Monte Cristo bought that house? Was it not by
chance he caused the earth to be dug up? Is it not by chance that the
unfortunate child was disinterred under the trees?ГўВҖВ”that poor innocent
offspring of mine, which I never even kissed, but for whom I wept many,
many tears. Ah, my heart clung to the count when he mentioned the dear
spoil found beneath the flowers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, no, madame,ГўВҖВ”this is the terrible news I have to tell you,ГўВҖВқ said
Villefort in a hollow voiceГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңno, nothing was found beneath the flowers;
there was no child disinterredГўВҖВ”no. You must not weep, no, you must not
groan, you must tremble!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat can you mean?ГўВҖВқ asked Madame Danglars, shuddering.

ГўВҖВңI mean that M. de Monte Cristo, digging underneath these trees, found
neither skeleton nor chest, because neither of them was there!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNeither of them there?ГўВҖВқ repeated Madame Danglars, her staring,
wide-open eyes expressing her alarm. ГўВҖВңNeither of them there!ГўВҖВқ she again
said, as though striving to impress herself with the meaning of the
words which escaped her.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, burying his face in his hands, ГўВҖВңno, a hundred
times no!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you did not bury the poor child there, sir? Why did you deceive
me? Where did you place it? tell meГўВҖВ”where?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere! But listen to meГўВҖВ”listenГўВҖВ”and you will pity me who has for twenty
years alone borne the heavy burden of grief I am about to reveal,
without casting the least portion upon you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, you frighten me! But speak; I will listen.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou recollect that sad night, when you were half-expiring on that bed
in the red damask room, while I, scarcely less agitated than you,
awaited your delivery. The child was born, was given to meГўВҖВ”motionless,
breathless, voiceless; we thought it dead.ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars moved rapidly, as though she would spring from her
chair, but Villefort stopped, and clasped his hands as if to implore
her attention.

ГўВҖВңWe thought it dead,ГўВҖВқ he repeated; ГўВҖВңI placed it in the chest, which was
to take the place of a coffin; I descended to the garden, I dug a hole,
and then flung it down in haste. Scarcely had I covered it with earth,
when the arm of the Corsican was stretched towards me; I saw a shadow
rise, and, at the same time, a flash of light. I felt pain; I wished to
cry out, but an icy shiver ran through my veins and stifled my voice; I
fell lifeless, and fancied myself killed. Never shall I forget your
sublime courage, when, having returned to consciousness, I dragged
myself to the foot of the stairs, and you, almost dying yourself, came
to meet me. We were obliged to keep silent upon the dreadful
catastrophe. You had the fortitude to regain the house, assisted by
your nurse. A duel was the pretext for my wound. Though we scarcely
expected it, our secret remained in our own keeping alone. I was taken
to Versailles; for three months I struggled with death; at last, as I
seemed to cling to life, I was ordered to the South. Four men carried
me from Paris to ChГғВўlons, walking six leagues a day; Madame de
Villefort followed the litter in her carriage. At ChГғВўlons I was put
upon the SaГғВҙne, thence I passed on to the RhГғВҙne, whence I descended,
merely with the current, to Arles; at Arles I was again placed on my
litter, and continued my journey to Marseilles. My recovery lasted six
months. I never heard you mentioned, and I did not dare inquire for
you. When I returned to Paris, I learned that you, the widow of M. de
Nargonne, had married M. Danglars.

ГўВҖВңWhat was the subject of my thoughts from the time consciousness
