returned to me? Always the sameГўВҖВ”always the childГўВҖВҷs corpse, coming every
night in my dreams, rising from the earth, and hovering over the grave
with menacing look and gesture. I inquired immediately on my return to
Paris; the house had not been inhabited since we left it, but it had
just been let for nine years. I found the tenant. I pretended that I
disliked the idea that a house belonging to my wifeГўВҖВҷs father and mother
should pass into the hands of strangers. I offered to pay them for
cancelling the lease; they demanded 6,000 francs. I would have given
10,000ГўВҖВ”I would have given 20,000. I had the money with me; I made the
tenant sign the deed of resilition, and when I had obtained what I so
much wanted, I galloped to Auteuil. No one had entered the house since
I had left it.

ГўВҖВңIt was five oГўВҖВҷclock in the afternoon; I ascended into the red room,
and waited for night. There all the thoughts which had disturbed me
during my year of constant agony came back with double force. The
Corsican, who had declared the vendetta against me, who had followed me
from NГғВ®mes to Paris, who had hid himself in the garden, who had struck
me, had seen me dig the grave, had seen me inter the child,ГўВҖВ”he might
become acquainted with your person,ГўВҖВ”nay, he might even then have known
it. Would he not one day make you pay for keeping this terrible secret?
Would it not be a sweet revenge for him when he found that I had not
died from the blow of his dagger? It was therefore necessary, before
everything else, and at all risks, that I should cause all traces of
the past to disappearГўВҖВ”that I should destroy every material vestige; too
much reality would always remain in my recollection. It was for this I
had annulled the leaseГўВҖВ”it was for this I had comeГўВҖВ”it was for this I was
waiting.

ГўВҖВңNight arrived; I allowed it to become quite dark. I was without a
light in that room; when the wind shook all the doors, behind which I
continually expected to see some spy concealed, I trembled. I seemed
everywhere to hear your moans behind me in the bed, and I dared not
turn around. My heart beat so violently that I feared my wound would
open. At length, one by one, all the noises in the neighborhood ceased.
I understood that I had nothing to fear, that I should neither be seen
nor heard, so I decided upon descending to the garden.

ГўВҖВңListen, Hermine; I consider myself as brave as most men, but when I
drew from my breast the little key of the staircase, which I had found
in my coatГўВҖВ”that little key we both used to cherish so much, which you
wished to have fastened to a golden ringГўВҖВ”when I opened the door, and
saw the pale moon shedding a long stream of white light on the spiral
staircase like a spectre, I leaned against the wall, and nearly
shrieked. I seemed to be going mad. At last I mastered my agitation. I
descended the staircase step by step; the only thing I could not
conquer was a strange trembling in my knees. I grasped the railings; if
I had relaxed my hold for a moment, I should have fallen. I reached the
lower door. Outside this door a spade was placed against the wall; I
took it, and advanced towards the thicket. I had provided myself with a
dark lantern. In the middle of the lawn I stopped to light it, then I
continued my path.

ГўВҖВңIt was the end of November, all the verdure of the garden had
disappeared, the trees were nothing more than skeletons with their long
bony arms, and the dead leaves sounded on the gravel under my feet. My
terror overcame me to such a degree as I approached the thicket, that I
took a pistol from my pocket and armed myself. I fancied continually
that I saw the figure of the Corsican between the branches. I examined
the thicket with my dark lantern; it was empty. I looked carefully
around; I was indeed alone,ГўВҖВ”no noise disturbed the silence but the owl,
whose piercing cry seemed to be calling up the phantoms of the night. I
tied my lantern to a forked branch I had noticed a year before at the
precise spot where I stopped to dig the hole.

ГўВҖВңThe grass had grown very thickly there during the summer, and when
autumn arrived no one had been there to mow it. Still one place where
the grass was thin attracted my attention; it evidently was there I had
turned up the ground. I went to work. The hour, then, for which I had
been waiting during the last year had at length arrived. How I worked,
how I hoped, how I struck every piece of turf, thinking to find some
resistance to my spade! But no, I found nothing, though I had made a
hole twice as large as the first. I thought I had been deceivedГўВҖВ”had
mistaken the spot. I turned around, I looked at the trees, I tried to
recall the details which had struck me at the time. A cold, sharp wind
whistled through the leafless branches, and yet the drops fell from my
forehead. I recollected that I was stabbed just as I was trampling the
ground to fill up the hole; while doing so I had leaned against a
laburnum; behind me was an artificial rockery, intended to serve as a
resting-place for persons walking in the garden; in falling, my hand,
relaxing its hold of the laburnum, felt the coldness of the stone. On
my right I saw the tree, behind me the rock. I stood in the same
attitude, and threw myself down. I rose, and again began digging and
enlarging the hole; still I found nothing, nothingГўВҖВ”the chest was no
longer there!ГўВҖВқ

30269m



ГўВҖВңThe chest no longer there?ГўВҖВқ murmured Madame Danglars, choking with
fear.

ГўВҖВңThink not I contented myself with this one effort,ГўВҖВқ continued
Villefort. ГўВҖВңNo; I searched the whole thicket. I thought the assassin,
having discovered the chest, and supposing it to be a treasure, had
intended carrying it off, but, perceiving his error, had dug another
hole, and deposited it there; but I could find nothing. Then the idea
struck me that he had not taken these precautions, and had simply
thrown it in a corner. In the last case I must wait for daylight to
renew my search. I remained in the room and waited.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, Heaven!ГўВҖВқ

30271m



When daylight dawned I went down again. My first visit was to the
thicket. I hoped to find some traces which had escaped me in the
darkness. I had turned up the earth over a surface of more than twenty
feet square, and a depth of two feet. A laborer would not have done in
a day what occupied me an hour. But I could find nothingГўВҖВ”absolutely
nothing. Then I renewed the search. Supposing it had been thrown aside,
it would probably be on the path which led to the little gate; but this
examination was as useless as the first, and with a bursting heart I
returned to the thicket, which now contained no hope for me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ cried Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңit was enough to drive you mad!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hoped for a moment that it might,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort; ГўВҖВңbut that
happiness was denied me. However, recovering my strength and my ideas,
ГўВҖВҳWhy,ГўВҖВҷ said I, ГўВҖВҳshould that man have carried away the corpse?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you said,ГўВҖВқ replied Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңhe would require it as a
proof.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, no, madame, that could not be. Dead bodies are not kept a year;
they are shown to a magistrate, and the evidence is taken. Now, nothing
of the kind has happened.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat then?ГўВҖВқ asked Hermine, trembling violently.

ГўВҖВңSomething more terrible, more fatal, more alarming for usГўВҖВ”the child
was, perhaps, alive, and the assassin may have saved it!ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars uttered a piercing cry, and, seizing VillefortГўВҖВҷs hands,
exclaimed, ГўВҖВңMy child was alive?ГўВҖВқ said she; ГўВҖВңyou buried my child alive?
You were not certain my child was dead, and you buried it? AhГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars had risen, and stood before the procureur, whose hands
she wrung in her feeble grasp.

ГўВҖВңI know not; I merely suppose so, as I might suppose anything else,ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, my child, my poor child!ГўВҖВқ cried the baroness, falling on her

ГўВҖВңYou understand, then, that if it were so,ГўВҖВқ said he, rising in his

ГўВҖВңwe are lost. This child lives, and someone knows it livesГўВҖВ”someone is

is in possession of our secret.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJust God, avenging God!ГўВҖВқ murmured Madame Danglars.

VillefortГўВҖВҷs only answer was a stifled groan.

ГўВҖВңBut the childГўВҖВ”the child, sir?ГўВҖВқ repeated the agitated mother.

ГўВҖВңHow I have searched for him,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, wringing his hands;
ГўВҖВңhow I have called him in my long sleepless nights; how I have longed

the Corsican could have done with the child. A child encumbers a
fugitive; perhaps, on perceiving it was still alive, he had thrown it

ГўВҖВңImpossible!ГўВҖВқ cried Madame Danglars: ГўВҖВңa man may murder another out of

ГўВҖВңPerhaps,ГўВҖВқ continued Villefort, ГўВҖВңhe had put it in the foundling

ГўВҖВңOh, yes, yes,ГўВҖВқ cried the baroness; ГўВҖВңmy child is there!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI ran to the hospital, and learned that the same nightГўВҖВ”the night of
the 20th of SeptemberГўВҖВ”a child had been brought there, wrapped in part
of a fine linen napkin, purposely torn in half. This portion of the

ГўВҖВңTruly, truly,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңall my linen is marked thus;
Monsieur de Nargonne was a baron, and my name is Hermine. Thank God, my

ГўВҖВңNo, it was not dead.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you can tell me so without fearing to make me die of joy? Where is
the child?ГўВҖВқ

Villefort shrugged his shoulders.

ГўВҖВңDo I know?ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңand do you believe that if I knew I would relate
to you all its trials and all its adventures as would a dramatist or a
novel writer? Alas, no, I know not. A woman, about six months after,

the requisite particulars, and it was intrusted to her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you should have inquired for the woman; you should have traced

ГўВҖВңAnd what do you think I did? I feigned a criminal process, and
employed all the most acute bloodhounds and skilful agents in search of
her. They traced her to ChГғВўlons, and there they lost her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey lost her?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, forever.ГўВҖВқ

Madame Danglars had listened to this recital with a sigh, a tear, or a

ГўВҖВңOh, no,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort; ГўВҖВңI never ceased to search and to inquire.
However, the last two or three years I had allowed myself some respite.
But now I will begin with more perseverance and fury than ever, since
fear urges me, not my conscience.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ replied Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңthe Count of Monte Cristo can know

ГўВҖВңOh, the wickedness of man is very great,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңsince it
surpasses the goodness of God. Did you observe that manГўВҖВҷs eyes while he

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut have you ever watched him carefully?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless he is capricious, but that is all; one thing alone struck
me,ГўВҖВ”of all the exquisite things he placed before us, he touched

ГўВҖВңAnd you see you would have been deceived.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, doubtless.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut believe me, that man has other projects. For that reason I wished

ГўВҖВңNever, to anyone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou understand me,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, affectionately; ГўВҖВңwhen I say
anyone,ГўВҖВ”pardon my urgency,ГўВҖВ”to anyone living I mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, I understand very well,ГўВҖВқ ejaculated the baroness; ГўВҖВңnever, I

ГўВҖВңWere you ever in the habit of writing in the evening what had

ГўВҖВңNo, my life has been passed in frivolity; I wish to forget it myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you talk in your sleep?ГўВҖВқ

30275m



ГўВҖВңI sleep soundly, like a child; do you not remember?ГўВҖВқ

The color mounted to the baronessГўВҖВҷs face, and Villefort turned awfully

Villefort pronounced these words with an accent which would have made

door. Madame Danglars returned in another cab to the passage, on the
other side of which she found her carriage, and her coachman sleeping
peacefully on his box while waiting for her.



 Chapter 68. A Summer Ball

The same day during the interview between Madame Danglars and the
procureur, a travelling-carriage entered the Rue du Helder, passed
through the gateway of No. 27, and stopped in the yard. In a moment the
door was opened, and Madame de Morcerf alighted, leaning on her sonГўВҖВҷs
arm. Albert soon left her, ordered his horses, and having arranged his
toilet, drove to the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es, to the house of Monte Cristo.

The count received him with his habitual smile. It was a strange thing
that no one ever appeared to advance a step in that manГўВҖВҷs favor. Those
who would, as it were, force a passage to his heart, found an
impassable barrier. Morcerf, who ran towards him with open arms, was
chilled as he drew near, in spite of the friendly smile, and simply
held out his hand. Monte Cristo shook it coldly, according to his
invariable practice.

ГўВҖВңHere I am, dear count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWelcome home again.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI arrived an hour since.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom Dieppe?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, from TrГғВ©port.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I have come at once to see you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is extremely kind of you,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with a tone of
perfect indifference.

ГўВҖВңAnd what is the news?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou should not ask a stranger, a foreigner, for news.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it, but in asking for news, I mean, have you done anything for
me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHad you commissioned me?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, feigning uneasiness.

ГўВҖВңCome, come,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңdo not assume so much indifference. It is
said, sympathy travels rapidly, and when at TrГғВ©port, I felt the

ГўВҖВңPossibly,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI have indeed thought of you, but the
magnetic wire I was guiding acted, indeed, without my knowledge.ГўВҖВқ

30277m



ГўВҖВңIndeed! Pray tell me how it happened.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWillingly. M. Danglars dined with me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it; to avoid meeting him, my mother and I left town.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut he met here M. Andrea Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour Italian prince?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot so fast; M. Andrea only calls himself count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCalls himself, do you say?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, calls himself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs he not a count?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat can I know of him? He calls himself so. I, of course, give him
the same title, and everyone else does likewise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat a strange man you are! What next? You say M. Danglars dined
here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, with Count Cavalcanti, the marquis his father, Madame Danglars,
M. and Madame de Villefort,ГўВҖВ”charming people,ГўВҖВ”M. Debray, Maximilian
Morrel, and M. de ChГғВўteau-Renaud.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid they speak of me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot a word.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo much the worse.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so? I thought you wished them to forget you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf they did not speak of me, I am sure they thought about me, and I am
in despair.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow will that affect you, since Mademoiselle Danglars was not among
the number here who thought of you? Truly, she might have thought of
you at home.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have no fear of that; or, if she did, it was only in the same way in
which I think of her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTouching sympathy! So you hate each other?ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ said MorcerfГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңif Mademoiselle Danglars were disposed to take
pity on my supposed martyrdom on her account, and would dispense with
all matrimonial formalities between our two families, I am ready to
agree to the arrangement. In a word, Mademoiselle Danglars would make a
charming mistressГўВҖВ”but a wifeГўВҖВ”_diable!_ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd this,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңis your opinion of your intended
spouse?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; it is rather unkind, I acknowledge, but it is true. But as this
dream cannot be realized, since Mademoiselle Danglars must become my
lawful wife, live perpetually with me, sing to me, compose verses and
music within ten paces of me, and that for my whole life, it frightens
me. One may forsake a mistress, but a wife,ГўВҖВ”good heavens! There she
must always be; and to marry Mademoiselle Danglars would be awful.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are difficult to please, viscount.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, for I often wish for what is impossible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo find such a wife as my father found.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo turned pale, and looked at Albert, while playing with some
magnificent pistols.

ГўВҖВңYour father was fortunate, then?ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңYou know my opinion of my mother, count; look at her,ГўВҖВ”still beautiful,
witty, more charming than ever. For any other son to have stayed with
his mother for four days at TrГғВ©port, it would have been a condescension
or a martyrdom, while I return, more contented, more peacefulГўВҖВ”shall I
say more poetic!ГўВҖВ”than if I had taken Queen Mab or Titania as my
companion.ГўВҖВқ

30279m



ГўВҖВңThat is an overwhelming demonstration, and you would make everyone vow
to live a single life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSuch are my reasons for not liking to marry Mademoiselle Danglars.
Have you ever noticed how much a thing is heightened in value when we
obtain possession of it? The diamond which glittered in the window at
MarlГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs or FossinГўВҖВҷs shines with more splendor when it is our own; but

still must retain the one that is inferior, do you not know what we
have to endure?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWorldling,ГўВҖВқ murmured the count.

ГўВҖВңThus I shall rejoice when Mademoiselle EugГғВ©nie perceives I am but a

ГўВҖВңThat is what I call devoted friendship, to recommend to another one

ГўВҖВңApropos,ГўВҖВқ continued he, ГўВҖВңFranz is coming soon, but it will not

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңmy dear viscount, how have you discovered that

ГўВҖВңYes; summoned by M. de Villefort, who is apparently as anxious to get

ГўВҖВңBut M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, unlike you, bears his misfortune patiently.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill more, he talks seriously about the matter, puts on a white tie,

ГўВҖВңWhich they deserve, do they not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI believe they do. M. de Villefort has always passed for a severe but

ГўВҖВңThere is, then, one,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңwhom you do not condemn like

ГўВҖВңI foppish? how do you mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; pray take a cigar, and cease to defend yourself, and to struggle

ГўВҖВңBah!ГўВҖВқ said Albert, staring.

ГўВҖВңI would give a hundred thousand francs to be able to do so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen make yourself quite easy. M. Danglars would give double that sum

ГўВҖВңBut yet, M. Danglars appearedГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSummer balls are fashionable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf they were not, the countess has only to wish it, and they would

ГўВҖВңYou are right; You know they are select affairs; those who remain in

ГўВҖВңI do not know him, viscount.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, I never saw him until a few days since, and am not responsible for

ГўВҖВңAt your ball.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy should you not be there?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut I come expressly for that purpose.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are very kind, but I may be prevented.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell me what it is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe Comtesse de Morcerf?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, starting.

ГўВҖВңAh, count,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңI assure you Madame de Morcerf speaks freely

ГўВҖВңYou have talked of me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, that is the penalty of being a living puzzle!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I am also a puzzle to your mother? I should have thought her too

ГўВҖВңA problem, my dear count, for everyoneГўВҖВ”for my mother as well as

ГўВҖВңYou will, then, come on Saturday?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, since Madame de Morcerf invites me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are very kind.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe has already been invited by my father. We shall try to persuade the
great dГўВҖВҷAguesseau,11 M. de Villefort, to come, but have not much hope

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳNever despair of anything,ГўВҖВҷ says the proverb.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you dance, count?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI dance?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, you; it would not be astonishing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is very well before one is over forty. No, I do not dance, but I
like to see others do so. Does Madame de Morcerf dance?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever; you can talk to her, she so delights in your conversation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, truly; and I assure you. You are the only man of whom I have

ГўВҖВңWell, my dear Bertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңI now advise you to go in

Bertuccio bowed, and as his wishes were in perfect harmony with the

 Chapter 69. The Inquiry

M. de Villefort kept the promise he had made to Madame Danglars, to
endeavor to find out how the Count of Monte Cristo had discovered the
history of the house at Auteuil. He wrote the same day for the required
information to M. de Boville, who, from having been an inspector of
prisons, was promoted to a high office in the police; and the latter
begged for two days time to ascertain exactly who would be most likely
to give him full particulars. At the end of the second day M. de
Villefort received the following note:

ГўВҖВңThe person called the Count of Monte Cristo is an intimate
acquaintance of Lord Wilmore, a rich foreigner, who is sometimes seen
in Paris and who is there at this moment; he is also known to the AbbГғВ©
Busoni, a Sicilian priest, of high repute in the East, where he has
done much good.ГўВҖВқ

M. de Villefort replied by ordering the strictest inquiries to be made
respecting these two persons; his orders were executed, and the
following evening he received these details:

ГўВҖВңThe abbГғВ©, who was in Paris only for a month, inhabited a small
two-storied house behind Saint-Sulpice; there were two rooms on each
floor and he was the only tenant. The two lower rooms consisted of a
dining-room, with a table, chairs, and side-board of walnut, and a
wainscoted parlor, without ornaments, carpet, or timepiece. It was
evident that the abbГғВ© limited himself to objects of strict necessity.
He preferred to use the sitting-room upstairs, which was more library
than parlor, and was furnished with theological books and parchments,
in which he delighted to bury himself for months at a time, according
to his valet de chambre. His valet looked at the visitors through a
sort of wicket; and if their faces were unknown to him or displeased
him, he replied that the abbГғВ© was not in Paris, an answer which
satisfied most persons, because the abbГғВ© was known to be a great
traveller. Besides, whether at home or not, whether in Paris or Cairo,
the abbГғВ© always left something to give away, which the valet
distributed through this wicket in his masterГўВҖВҷs name. The other room
near the library was a bedroom. A bed without curtains, four armchairs,
and a couch, covered with yellow Utrecht velvet, composed, with a
_prie-Dieu_, all its furniture.

ГўВҖВңLord Wilmore resided in Rue Fontaine-Saint-Georges. He was one of
those English tourists who consume a large fortune in travelling. He
hired the apartment in which he lived furnished, passed only a few
hours in the day there, and rarely slept there. One of his
peculiarities was never to speak a word of French, which he however
wrote with great facility.ГўВҖВқ

The day after this important information had been given to the kingГўВҖВҷs
attorney, a man alighted from a carriage at the corner of the Rue
FГғВ©rou, and rapping at an olive-green door, asked if the AbbГғВ© Busoni
were within.

ГўВҖВңNo, he went out early this morning,ГўВҖВқ replied the valet.

ГўВҖВңI might not always be content with that answer,ГўВҖВқ replied the visitor,
ГўВҖВңfor I come from one to whom everyone must be at home. But have the
kindness to give the AbbГғВ© BusoniГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI told you he was not at home,ГўВҖВқ repeated the valet.

ГўВҖВңThen on his return give him that card and this sealed paper. Will he
be at home at eight oГўВҖВҷclock this evening?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless, unless he is at work, which is the same as if he were out.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will come again at that time,ГўВҖВқ replied the visitor, who then
retired.

At the appointed hour the same man returned in the same carriage,
which, instead of stopping this time at the end of the Rue FГғВ©rou, drove
up to the green door. He knocked, and it opened immediately to admit
him. From the signs of respect the valet paid him, he saw that his note
had produced a good effect.

ГўВҖВңIs the abbГғВ© at home?ГўВҖВқ asked he.

ГўВҖВңYes; he is at work in his library, but he expects you, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied
the valet. The stranger ascended a rough staircase, and before a table,
illumined by a lamp whose light was concentrated by a large shade while
the rest of the apartment was in partial darkness, he perceived the
abbГғВ© in a monkГўВҖВҷs dress, with a cowl on his head such as was used by
learned men of the Middle Ages.

ГўВҖВңHave I the honor of addressing the AbbГғВ© Busoni?ГўВҖВқ asked the visitor.

ГўВҖВңYes, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңand you are the person whom M. de
Boville, formerly an inspector of prisons, sends to me from the prefect
of police?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne of the agents appointed to secure the safety of Paris?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sirГўВҖВқ replied the stranger with a slight hesitation, and blushing.

The abbГғВ© replaced the large spectacles, which covered not only his eyes
but his temples, and sitting down motioned to his visitor to do the
same. ГўВҖВңI am at your service, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, with a marked Italian
accent.

ГўВҖВңThe mission with which I am charged, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the visitor,
speaking with hesitation, ГўВҖВңis a confidential one on the part of him who
fulfils it, and him by whom he is employed.ГўВҖВқ The abbГғВ© bowed. ГўВҖВңYour
probity,ГўВҖВқ replied the stranger, ГўВҖВңis so well known to the prefect that
he wishes as a magistrate to ascertain from you some particulars
connected with the public safety, to ascertain which I am deputed to
see you. It is hoped that no ties of friendship or humane consideration
will induce you to conceal the truth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProvided, sir, the particulars you wish for do not interfere with my
scruples or my conscience. I am a priest, sir, and the secrets of
confession, for instance, must remain between me and God, and not
between me and human justice.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not alarm yourself, monsieur, we will duly respect your
conscience.ГўВҖВқ

At this moment the abbГғВ© pressed down his side of the shade and so
raised it on the other, throwing a bright light on the strangerГўВҖВҷs face,
while his own remained obscured.

ГўВҖВңExcuse me, abbГғВ©,ГўВҖВқ said the envoy of the prefect of the police, ГўВҖВңbut
the light tries my eyes very much.ГўВҖВқ The abbГғВ© lowered the shade.

ГўВҖВңNow, sir, I am listeningГўВҖВ”go on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will come at once to the point. Do you know the Count of Monte
Cristo?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou mean Monsieur Zaccone, I presume?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңZaccone?ГўВҖВ”is not his name Monte Cristo?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonte Cristo is the name of an estate, or, rather, of a rock, and not
a family name.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, be it soГўВҖВ”let us not dispute about words; and since M. de Monte
Cristo and M. Zaccone are the sameГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAbsolutely the same.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLet us speak of M. Zaccone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAgreed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI asked you if you knew him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExtremely well.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho is he?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe son of a rich shipbuilder in Malta.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know that is the report; but, as you are aware, the police does not
content itself with vague reports.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHowever,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©, with an affable smile, ГўВҖВңwhen that report
is in accordance with the truth, everybody must believe it, the police
as well as all the rest.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you sure of what you assert?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean by that question?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUnderstand, sir, I do not in the least suspect your veracity; I ask if
you are certain of it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI knew his father, M. Zaccone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd when a child I often played with the son in the timber-yards.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut whence does he derive the title of count?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are aware that may be bought.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn Italy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEverywhere.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd his immense riches, whence does he procure them?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey may not be so very great.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow much do you suppose he possesses?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom one hundred and fifty to two hundred thousand livres per annum.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is reasonable,ГўВҖВқ said the visitor; ГўВҖВңI have heard he had three or
four millions.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTwo hundred thousand per annum would make four millions of capital.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut I was told he had four millions per annum.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is not probable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you know this Island of Monte Cristo?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly, everyone who has come from Palermo, Naples, or Rome to
France by sea must know it, since he has passed close to it and must

ГўВҖВңI am told it is a delightful place?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is a rock.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd why has the count bought a rock?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor the sake of being a count. In Italy one must have territorial
possessions to be a count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have, doubtless, heard the adventures of M. ZacconeГўВҖВҷs youth?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe fatherГўВҖВҷs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, the sonГўВҖВҷs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know nothing certain; at that period of his life, I lost sight of my
young comrade.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas he in the wars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think he entered the service.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn what branch?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn the navy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you not his confessor?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, sir; I believe he is a Lutheran.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA Lutheran?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI say, I believe such is the case, I do not affirm it; besides,
liberty of conscience is established in France.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless, and we are not now inquiring into his creed, but his
actions; in the name of the prefect of police, I ask you what you know
