ГўВҖВңHe passes for a very charitable man. Our holy father, the pope, has
made him a knight of Jesus Christ for the services he rendered to the
Christians in the East; he has five or six rings as testimonials from
Eastern monarchs of his services.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoes he wear them?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, but he is proud of them; he is better pleased with rewards given
to the benefactors of man than to his destroyers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is a Quaker then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly, he is a Quaker, with the exception of the peculiar dress.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHas he any friends?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, everyone who knows him is his friend.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut has he any enemies?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne only.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is his name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLord Wilmore.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere is he?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is in Paris just now.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCan he give me any particulars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңImportant ones; he was in India with Zaccone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you know his abode?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңItГўВҖВҷs somewhere in the ChaussГғВ©e dГўВҖВҷAntin; but I know neither the street
nor the number.ГўВҖВқ

30289m



ГўВҖВңAre you at variance with the Englishman?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI love Zaccone, and he hates him; we are consequently not friends.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think the Count of Monte Cristo had ever been in France before
he made this visit to Paris?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo that question I can answer positively; no, sir, he had not, because
he applied to me six months ago for the particulars he required, and as
I did not know when I might again come to Paris, I recommended M.
Cavalcanti to him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAndrea?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, Bartolomeo, his father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow, sir, I have but one question more to ask, and I charge you, in
the name of honor, of humanity, and of religion, to answer me
candidly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it, sir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you know with what design M. de Monte Cristo purchased a house at
Auteuil?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly, for he told me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it, sir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo make a lunatic asylum of it, similar to that founded by the Count
of Pisani at Palermo. Do you know about that institution?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have heard of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is a magnificent charity.ГўВҖВқ Having said this, the abbГғВ© bowed to
imply he wished to pursue his studies.

The visitor either understood the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВҷs meaning, or had no more
questions to ask; he arose, and the abbГғВ© accompanied him to the door.

ГўВҖВңYou are a great almsgiver,ГўВҖВқ said the visitor, ГўВҖВңand although you are
said to be rich, I will venture to offer you something for your poor
people; will you accept my offering?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI thank you, sir; I am only jealous in one thing, and that is that the
relief I give should be entirely from my own resources.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHoweverГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy resolution, sir, is unchangeable, but you have only to search for
yourself and you will find, alas, but too many objects upon whom to
exercise your benevolence.ГўВҖВқ

The abbГғВ© once more bowed as he opened the door, the stranger bowed and
took his leave, and the carriage conveyed him straight to the house of
M. de Villefort. An hour afterwards the carriage was again ordered, and
this time it went to the Rue Fontaine-Saint-Georges, and stopped at No.
5, where Lord Wilmore lived. The stranger had written to Lord Wilmore,
requesting an interview, which the latter had fixed for ten oГўВҖВҷclock. As
the envoy of the prefect of police arrived ten minutes before ten, he
was told that Lord Wilmore, who was precision and punctuality
personified, was not yet come in, but that he would be sure to return
as the clock struck.

The visitor was introduced into the drawing-room, which was like all
other furnished drawing-rooms. A mantle-piece, with two modern SГғВЁvres
vases, a timepiece representing Cupid with his bent bow, a mirror with
an engraving on each sideГўВҖВ”one representing Homer carrying his guide,
the other, Belisarius beggingГўВҖВ”a grayish paper; red and black
tapestryГўВҖВ”such was the appearance of Lord WilmoreГўВҖВҷs drawing-room.

It was illuminated by lamps with ground-glass shades which gave only a
feeble light, as if out of consideration for the envoyГўВҖВҷs weak sight.
After ten minutesГўВҖВҷ expectation the clock struck ten; at the fifth
stroke the door opened and Lord Wilmore appeared. He was rather above
the middle height, with thin reddish whiskers, light complexion and
light hair, turning rather gray. He was dressed with all the English
peculiarity, namely, in a blue coat, with gilt buttons and high collar,
in the fashion of 1811, a white kerseymere waistcoat, and nankeen
pantaloons, three inches too short, but which were prevented by straps
from slipping up to the knee. His first remark on entering was:

ГўВҖВңYou know, sir, I do not speak French?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know you do not like to converse in our language,ГўВҖВқ replied the
envoy.

ГўВҖВңBut you may use it,ГўВҖВқ replied Lord Wilmore; ГўВҖВңI understand it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I,ГўВҖВқ replied the visitor, changing his idiom, ГўВҖВңknow enough of
English to keep up the conversation. Do not put yourself to the
slightest inconvenience.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAw?ГўВҖВқ said Lord Wilmore, with that tone which is only known to natives
of Great Britain.

The envoy presented his letter of introduction, which the latter read
with English coolness, and having finished:

ГўВҖВңI understand,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңperfectly.ГўВҖВқ

30293m



Then began the questions, which were similar to those which had been
addressed to the AbbГғВ© Busoni. But as Lord Wilmore, in the character of
the countГўВҖВҷs enemy, was less restrained in his answers, they were more
numerous; he described the youth of Monte Cristo, who he said, at ten
years of age, entered the service of one of the petty sovereigns of
India who make war on the English. It was there Wilmore had first met
him and fought against him; and in that war Zaccone had been taken
prisoner, sent to England, and consigned to the hulks, whence he had
escaped by swimming. Then began his travels, his duels, his caprices;
then the insurrection in Greece broke out, and he had served in the
Grecian ranks. While in that service he had discovered a silver mine in
the mountains of Thessaly, but he had been careful to conceal it from
everyone. After the battle of Navarino, when the Greek government was
consolidated, he asked of King Otho a mining grant for that district,
which was given him. Hence that immense fortune, which, in Lord
WilmoreГўВҖВҷs opinion, possibly amounted to one or two millions per
annum,ГўВҖВ”a precarious fortune, which might be momentarily lost by the
failure of the mine.

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ asked the visitor, ГўВҖВңdo you know why he came to France?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is speculating in railways,ГўВҖВқ said Lord Wilmore, ГўВҖВңand as he is an
expert chemist and physicist, he has invented a new system of
telegraphy, which he is seeking to bring to perfection.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow much does he spend yearly?ГўВҖВқ asked the prefect.

ГўВҖВңNot more than five or six hundred thousand francs,ГўВҖВқ said Lord Wilmore;
ГўВҖВңhe is a miser.ГўВҖВқ Hatred evidently inspired the Englishman, who, knowing
no other reproach to bring on the count, accused him of avarice.

ГўВҖВңDo you know his house at Auteuil?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you know respecting it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you wish to know why he bought it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe count is a speculator, who will certainly ruin himself in
experiments. He supposes there is in the neighborhood of the house he
has bought a mineral spring equal to those at BagnГғВЁres, Luchon, and
Cauterets. He is going to turn his house into a _Badhaus_, as the
Germans term it. He has already dug up all the garden two or three
times to find the famous spring, and, being unsuccessful, he will soon
purchase all the contiguous houses. Now, as I dislike him, and hope his
railway, his electric telegraph, or his search for baths, will ruin
him, I am watching for his discomfiture, which must soon take place.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat was the cause of your quarrel?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen he was in England he seduced the wife of one of my friends.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy do you not seek revenge?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have already fought three duels with him,ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman, ГўВҖВңthe
first with the pistol, the second with the sword, and the third with
the sabre.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what was the result of those duels?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe first time, he broke my arm; the second, he wounded me in the
breast; and the third time, made this large wound.ГўВҖВқ The Englishman
turned down his shirt-collar, and showed a scar, whose redness proved
it to be a recent one. ГўВҖВңSo that, you see, there is a deadly feud
between us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said the envoy, ГўВҖВңyou do not go about it in the right way to kill
him, if I understand you correctly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAw?ГўВҖВқ said the Englishman, ГўВҖВңI practice shooting every day, and every
other day Grisier comes to my house.ГўВҖВқ

This was all the visitor wished to ascertain, or, rather, all the
Englishman appeared to know. The agent arose, and having bowed to Lord
Wilmore, who returned his salutation with the stiff politeness of the
English, he retired. Lord Wilmore, having heard the door close after
him, returned to his bedroom, where with one hand he pulled off his
light hair, his red whiskers, his false jaw, and his wound, to resume
the black hair, dark complexion, and pearly teeth of the Count of Monte
Cristo.

It was M. de Villefort, and not the prefect, who returned to the house
of M. de Villefort. The procureur felt more at ease, although he had
learned nothing really satisfactory, and, for the first time since the
dinner-party at Auteuil, he slept soundly.



 Chapter 70. The Ball

It was in the warmest days of July, when in due course of time the
Saturday arrived upon which the ball was to take place at M. de
MorcerfГўВҖВҷs. It was ten oГўВҖВҷclock at night; the branches of the great trees
in the garden of the countГўВҖВҷs house stood out boldly against the azure
canopy of heaven, which was studded with golden stars, but where the
last fleeting clouds of a vanishing storm yet lingered.

From the apartments on the ground floor might be heard the sound of
music, with the whirl of the waltz and galop, while brilliant streams
of light shone through the openings of the Venetian blinds. At this
moment the garden was only occupied by about ten servants, who had just
received orders from their mistress to prepare the supper, the serenity
of the weather continuing to increase. Until now, it had been undecided
whether the supper should take place in the dining-room, or under a
long tent erected on the lawn, but the beautiful blue sky, studded with
stars, had settled the question in favor of the lawn.

The gardens were illuminated with colored lanterns, according to the
Italian custom, and, as is usual in countries where the luxuries of the
tableГўВҖВ”the rarest of all luxuries in their complete formГўВҖВ”are well
understood, the supper-table was loaded with wax-lights and flowers.

30297m



At the time the Countess of Morcerf returned to the rooms, after giving
her orders, many guests were arriving, more attracted by the charming
hospitality of the countess than by the distinguished position of the
count; for, owing to the good taste of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, one was sure of
finding some devices at her entertainment worthy of describing, or even
copying in case of need.

Madame Danglars, in whom the events we have related had caused deep
anxiety, had hesitated about going to Madame de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs, when during
the morning her carriage happened to meet that of Villefort. The latter
made a sign, and when the carriages had drawn close together, said:

ГўВҖВңYou are going to Madame de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs, are you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied Madame Danglars, ГўВҖВңI am too ill.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are wrong,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, significantly; ГўВҖВңit is important
that you should be seen there.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think so?ГўВҖВқ asked the baroness.

ГўВҖВңI do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn that case I will go.ГўВҖВқ

And the two carriages passed on towards their different destinations.
Madame Danglars therefore came, not only beautiful in person, but
radiant with splendor; she entered by one door at the time when
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs appeared at the door. The countess took Albert to meet Madame
Danglars. He approached, paid her some well merited compliments on her
toilet, and offered his arm to conduct her to a seat. Albert looked
around him.

ГўВҖВңYou are looking for my daughter?ГўВҖВқ said the baroness, smiling.

ГўВҖВңI confess it,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert. ГўВҖВңCould you have been so cruel as not to
bring her?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCalm yourself. She has met Mademoiselle de Villefort, and has taken
her arm; see, they are following us, both in white dresses, one with a
bouquet of camellias, the other with one of myosotis. But tell meГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, what do you wish to know?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWill not the Count of Monte Cristo be here tonight?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSeventeen!ГўВҖВқ replied Albert.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI only mean that the count seems the rage,ГўВҖВқ replied the viscount,
smiling, ГўВҖВңand that you are the seventeenth person that has asked me the
same question. The count is in fashion; I congratulate him upon it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd have you replied to everyone as you have to me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, to be sure, I have not answered you; be satisfied, we shall have
this ГўВҖВҳlionГўВҖВҷ; we are among the privileged ones.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWere you at the Opera yesterday?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe was there.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed? And did the eccentric person commit any new originality?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCan he be seen without doing so? Elssler was dancing in _Le Diable
boiteux_; the Greek princess was in ecstasies. After the cachucha he
placed a magnificent ring on the stem of a bouquet, and threw it to the
charming danseuse, who, in the third act, to do honor to the gift,
reappeared with it on her finger. And the Greek princess,ГўВҖВ”will she be
here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, you will be deprived of that pleasure; her position in the countГўВҖВҷs
establishment is not sufficiently understood.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWait; leave me here, and go and speak to Madame de Villefort, who is
trying to attract your attention.ГўВҖВқ

Albert bowed to Madame Danglars, and advanced towards Madame de
Villefort, whose lips opened as he approached.

ГўВҖВңI wager anything,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, interrupting her, ГўВҖВңthat I know what
you were about to say.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, what is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf I guess rightly, will you confess it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn your honor?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn my honor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou were going to ask me if the Count of Monte Cristo had arrived, or
was expected.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot at all. It is not of him that I am now thinking. I was going to
ask you if you had received any news of Monsieur Franz.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВ”yesterday.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat did he tell you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat he was leaving at the same time as his letter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, now then, the count?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe count will come, of that you may be satisfied.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou know that he has another name besides Monte Cristo?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, I did not know it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonte Cristo is the name of an island, and he has a family name.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI never heard it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, I am better informed than you; his name is Zaccone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is possible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is a Maltese.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is also possible.

ГўВҖВңThe son of a shipowner.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReally, you should relate all this aloud, you would have the greatest
success.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe served in India, discovered a mine in Thessaly, and comes to Paris
to establish a mineral water-cure at Auteuil.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, IГўВҖВҷm sure,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңthis is indeed news! Am I allowed to
repeat it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, but cautiously, tell one thing at a time, and do not say I told
you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause it is a secret just discovered.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe police.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen the news originatedГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt the prefectГўВҖВҷs last night. Paris, you can understand, is astonished
at the sight of such unusual splendor, and the police have made
inquiries.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, well! Nothing more is wanting than to arrest the count as a
vagabond, on the pretext of his being too rich.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed, that doubtless would have happened if his credentials had not
been so favorable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoor count! And is he aware of the danger he has been in?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think not.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen it will be but charitable to inform him. When he arrives, I will
not fail to do so.ГўВҖВқ

Just then, a handsome young man, with bright eyes, black hair, and
glossy moustache, respectfully bowed to Madame de Villefort. Albert
extended his hand.

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңallow me to present to you M. Maximilian
Morrel, captain of Spahis, one of our best, and, above all, of our
bravest officers.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have already had the pleasure of meeting this gentleman at Auteuil,
at the house of the Count of Monte Cristo,ГўВҖВқ replied Madame de
Villefort, turning away with marked coldness of manner.

This answer, and especially the tone in which it was uttered, chilled
the heart of poor Morrel. But a recompense was in store for him;
turning around, he saw near the door a beautiful fair face, whose large
blue eyes were, without any marked expression, fixed upon him, while
the bouquet of myosotis was gently raised to her lips.

The salutation was so well understood that Morrel, with the same
expression in his eyes, placed his handkerchief to his mouth; and these
two living statues, whose hearts beat so violently under their marble
aspect, separated from each other by the whole length of the room,
forgot themselves for a moment, or rather forgot the world in their
mutual contemplation. They might have remained much longer lost in one
another, without anyone noticing their abstraction. The Count of Monte
Cristo had just entered.

We have already said that there was something in the count which
attracted universal attention wherever he appeared. It was not the
coat, unexceptional in its cut, though simple and unornamented; it was
not the plain white waistcoat; it was not the trousers, that displayed
the foot so perfectly formedГўВҖВ”it was none of these things that attracted
the attention,ГўВҖВ”it was his pale complexion, his waving black hair, his
calm and serene expression, his dark and melancholy eye, his mouth,
chiselled with such marvellous delicacy, which so easily expressed such
high disdain,ГўВҖВ”these were what fixed the attention of all upon him.

Many men might have been handsomer, but certainly there could be none
whose appearance was more _significant_, if the expression may be used.
Everything about the count seemed to have its meaning, for the constant
habit of thought which he had acquired had given an ease and vigor to
the expression of his face, and even to the most trifling gesture,
scarcely to be understood. Yet the Parisian world is so strange, that
even all this might not have won attention had there not been connected
with it a mysterious story gilded by an immense fortune.

30301m



Meanwhile he advanced through the assemblage of guests under a battery
of curious glances towards Madame de Morcerf, who, standing before a
mantle-piece ornamented with flowers, had seen his entrance in a
looking-glass placed opposite the door, and was prepared to receive
him. She turned towards him with a serene smile just at the moment he
was bowing to her. No doubt she fancied the count would speak to her,
while on his side the count thought she was about to address him; but
both remained silent, and after a mere bow, Monte Cristo directed his
steps to Albert, who received him cordially.

ГўВҖВңHave you seen my mother?ГўВҖВқ asked Albert.

ГўВҖВңI have just had the pleasure,ГўВҖВқ replied the count; ГўВҖВңbut I have not seen
your father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSee, he is down there, talking politics with that little group of
great geniuses.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңand so those gentlemen down there are men
of great talent. I should not have guessed it. And for what kind of
talent are they celebrated? You know there are different sorts.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat tall, harsh-looking man is very learned, he discovered, in the
neighborhood of Rome, a kind of lizard with a vertebra more than
lizards usually have, and he immediately laid his discovery before the
Institute. The thing was discussed for a long time, but finally decided
in his favor. I can assure you the vertebra made a great noise in the
learned world, and the gentleman, who was only a knight of the Legion
of Honor, was made an officer.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthis cross seems to me to be wisely
awarded. I suppose, had he found another additional vertebra, they
would have made him a commander.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery likely,ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңAnd who can that person be who has taken it into his head to wrap
himself up in a blue coat embroidered with green?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, that coat is not his own idea; it is the RepublicГўВҖВҷs, which deputed
David12 to devise a uniform for the Academicians.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңso this gentleman is an Academician?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWithin the last week he has been made one of the learned assembly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what is his especial talent?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHis talent? I believe he thrusts pins through the heads of rabbits, he
makes fowls eat madder, and punches the spinal marrow out of dogs with
whalebone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd he is made a member of the Academy of Sciences for this?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; of the French Academy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what has the French Academy to do with all this?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was going to tell you. It seemsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat his experiments have very considerably advanced the cause of
science, doubtless?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; that his style of writing is very good.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis must be very flattering to the feelings of the rabbits into whose
heads he has thrust pins, to the fowls whose bones he has dyed red, and
to the dogs whose spinal marrow he has punched out?ГўВҖВқ

Albert laughed.

ГўВҖВңAnd the other one?ГўВҖВқ demanded the count.

ГўВҖВңThat one?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, the third.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe one in the dark blue coat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is a colleague of the count, and one of the most active opponents
to the idea of providing the Chamber of Peers with a uniform. He was
very successful upon that question. He stood badly with the Liberal
papers, but his noble opposition to the wishes of the court is now
getting him into favor with the journalists. They talk of making him an
ambassador.ГўВҖВқ

30303m



ГўВҖВңAnd what are his claims to the peerage?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe has composed two or three comic operas, written four or five
articles in the _SiГғВЁcle_, and voted five or six years on the
ministerial side.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBravo, viscount,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, smiling; ГўВҖВңyou are a delightful
_cicerone_. And now you will do me a favor, will you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not introduce me to any of these gentlemen; and should they wish
it, you will warn me.ГўВҖВқ Just then the count felt his arm pressed. He
turned round; it was Danglars.

ГўВҖВңAh! is it you, baron?ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңWhy do you call me baron?ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңyou know that I care
nothing for my title. I am not like you, viscount; you like your title,
do you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert, ГўВҖВңseeing that without my title I should be
nothing; while you, sacrificing the baron, would still remain the
millionaire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich seems to me the finest title under the royalty of July,ГўВҖВқ replied
Danglars.

ГўВҖВңUnfortunately,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңoneГўВҖВҷs title to a millionaire does
not last for life, like that of baron, peer of France, or academician;
for example, the millionaires Franck & Poulmann, of Frankfurt, who have
just become bankrupts.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, becoming pale.

ГўВҖВңYes; I received the news this evening by a courier. I had about a
million in their hands, but, warned in time, I withdrew it a month
ago.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, _mon Dieu!_ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Danglars, ГўВҖВңthey have drawn on me for
200,000 francs!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, you can throw out the draft; their signature is worth five per
cent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, but it is too late,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңI have honored their bills.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңhere are 200,000 francs gone afterГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHush, do not mention these things,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; then, approaching
Monte Cristo, he added, ГўВҖВңespecially before young M. Cavalcanti;ГўВҖВқ after
which he smiled, and turned towards the young man in question.

Albert had left the count to speak to his mother, Danglars to converse
with young Cavalcanti; Monte Cristo was for an instant alone. Meanwhile
the heat became excessive. The footmen were hastening through the rooms
with waiters loaded with ices. Monte Cristo wiped the perspiration from
his forehead, but drew back when the waiter was presented to him; he
took no refreshment. Madame de Morcerf did not lose sight of Monte
Cristo; she saw that he took nothing, and even noticed his gesture of
refusal.

ГўВҖВңAlbert,ГўВҖВқ she asked, ГўВҖВңdid you notice that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat, mother?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat the count has never been willing to partake of food under the
roof of M. de Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but then he breakfasted with meГўВҖВ”indeed, he made his first
appearance in the world on that occasion.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut your house is not M. de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs,ГўВҖВқ murmured MercГғВ©dГғВЁs; ГўВҖВңand since
he has been here I have watched him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, he has taken nothing yet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe count is very temperate.ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs smiled sadly.

ГўВҖВңApproach him,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңand when the next waiter passes, insist upon
his taking something.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut why, mother?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJust to please me, Albert,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs. Albert kissed his motherГўВҖВҷs
hand, and drew near the count. Another salver passed, loaded like the
preceding ones; she saw Albert attempt to persuade the count, but he
obstinately refused. Albert rejoined his mother; she was very pale.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңyou see he refuses?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but why need this annoy you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou know, Albert, women are singular creatures. I should like to have
seen the count take something in my house, if only an ice. Perhaps he
cannot reconcile himself to the French style of living, and might
prefer something else.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no; I have seen him eat of everything in Italy; no doubt he does
not feel inclined this evening.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd besides,ГўВҖВқ said the countess, ГўВҖВңaccustomed as he is to burning
climates, possibly he does not feel the heat as we do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not think that, for he has complained of feeling almost
suffocated, and asked why the Venetian blinds were not opened as well
as the windows.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn a word,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңit was a way of assuring me that his
abstinence was intended.ГўВҖВқ

And she left the room.

A minute afterwards the blinds were thrown open, and through the
jessamine and clematis that overhung the window one could see the
garden ornamented with lanterns, and the supper laid under the tent.
Dancers, players, talkers, all uttered an exclamation of joyГўВҖВ”everyone
inhaled with delight the breeze that floated in. At the same time
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs reappeared, paler than before, but with that imperturbable
expression of countenance which she sometimes wore. She went straight
to the group of which her husband formed the centre.

ГўВҖВңDo not detain those gentlemen here, count,ГўВҖВқ she said; ГўВҖВңthey would
prefer, I should think, to breathe in the garden rather than suffocate
here, since they are not playing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said a gallant old general, who, in 1809, had sung _Partant pour
la Syrie_,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңwe will not go alone to the garden.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңI will lead the way.ГўВҖВқ

Turning towards Monte Cristo, she added, ГўВҖВңcount, will you oblige me
with your arm?ГўВҖВқ

The count almost staggered at these simple words; then he fixed his
eyes on MercГғВ©dГғВЁs. It was only a momentary glance, but it seemed to the
countess to have lasted for a century, so much was expressed in that
one look. He offered his arm to the countess; she took it, or rather
just touched it with her little hand, and they together descended the
steps, lined with rhododendrons and camellias. Behind them, by another
outlet, a group of about twenty persons rushed into the garden with
loud exclamations of delight.



 Chapter 71. Bread and Salt

Madame de Morcerf entered an archway of trees with her companion. It
led through a grove of lindens to a conservatory.

ГўВҖВңIt was too warm in the room, was it not, count?ГўВҖВқ she asked.

ГўВҖВңYes, madame; and it was an excellent idea of yours to open the doors
and the blinds.ГўВҖВқ As he ceased speaking, the count felt the hand of
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs tremble. ГўВҖВңBut you,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңwith that light dress, and
without anything to cover you but that gauze scarf, perhaps you feel
cold?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you know where I am leading you?ГўВҖВқ said the countess, without
replying to the question.

ГўВҖВңNo, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңbut you see I make no resistance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe are going to the greenhouse that you see at the other end of the
grove.ГўВҖВқ

The count looked at MercГғВ©dГғВЁs as if to interrogate her, but she
continued to walk on in silence, and he refrained from speaking. They
reached the building, ornamented with magnificent fruits, which ripen
at the beginning of July in the artificial temperature which takes the
place of the sun, so frequently absent in our climate. The countess
left the arm of Monte Cristo, and gathered a bunch of Muscatel grapes.

ГўВҖВңSee, count,ГўВҖВқ she said, with a smile so sad in its expression that one
could almost detect the tears on her eyelidsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңsee, our French grapes
are not to be compared, I know, with yours of Sicily and Cyprus, but
you will make allowance for our northern sun.ГўВҖВқ The count bowed, but
stepped back.

ГўВҖВңDo you refuse?ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, in a tremulous voice.

ГўВҖВңPray excuse me, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңbut I never eat
Muscatel grapes.ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs let them fall, and sighed. A magnificent peach was hanging
against an adjoining wall, ripened by the same artificial heat.
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs drew near, and plucked the fruit.

ГўВҖВңTake this peach, then,ГўВҖВқ she said. The count again refused. ГўВҖВңWhat,
again?ГўВҖВқ she exclaimed, in so plaintive an accent that it seemed to
stifle a sob; ГўВҖВңreally, you pain me.ГўВҖВқ

A long silence followed; the peach, like the grapes, fell to the
ground.

ГўВҖВңCount,ГўВҖВқ added MercГғВ©dГғВЁs with a supplicating glance, ГўВҖВңthere is a
beautiful Arabian custom, which makes eternal friends of those who have
together eaten bread and salt under the same roof.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied the count; ГўВҖВңbut we are in France, and not
in Arabia, and in France eternal friendships are as rare as the custom
of dividing bread and salt with one another.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said the countess, breathlessly, with her eyes fixed on Monte
Cristo, whose arm she convulsively pressed with both hands, ГўВҖВңwe are
friends, are we not?ГўВҖВқ

The count became pale as death, the blood rushed to his heart, and then
again rising, dyed his cheeks with crimson; his eyes swam like those of
a man suddenly dazzled.

ГўВҖВңCertainly, we are friends,ГўВҖВқ he replied; ГўВҖВңwhy should we not be?ГўВҖВқ

The answer was so little like the one MercГғВ©dГғВЁs desired, that she turned
away to give vent to a sigh, which sounded more like a groan. ГўВҖВңThank
you,ГўВҖВқ she said. And they walked on again. They went the whole length of
the garden without uttering a word.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ suddenly exclaimed the countess, after their walk had continued
ten minutes in silence, ГўВҖВңis it true that you have seen so much,
travelled so far, and suffered so deeply?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have suffered deeply, madame,ГўВҖВқ answered Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңBut now you are happy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless,ГўВҖВқ replied the count, ГўВҖВңsince no one hears me complain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd your present happiness, has it softened your heart?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy present happiness equals my past misery,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңAre you not married?ГўВҖВқ asked the countess.

ГўВҖВңI, married?ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Monte Cristo, shuddering; ГўВҖВңwho could have told
you so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo one told me you were, but you have frequently been seen at the
Opera with a young and lovely woman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShe is a slave whom I bought at Constantinople, madame, the daughter
of a prince. I have adopted her as my daughter, having no one else to
love in the world.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou live alone, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have no sisterГўВҖВ”no sonГўВҖВ”no father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have no one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow can you exist thus without anyone to attach you to life?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is not my fault, madame. At Malta, I loved a young girl, was on the
point of marrying her, when war came and carried me away. I thought she
loved me well enough to wait for me, and even to remain faithful to my
memory. When I returned she was married. This is the history of most
men who have passed twenty years of age. Perhaps my heart was weaker
than the hearts of most men, and I suffered more than they would have
done in my place; that is all.ГўВҖВқ

The countess stopped for a moment, as if gasping for breath. ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ she
said, ГўВҖВңand you have still preserved this love in your heartГўВҖВ”one can
only love onceГўВҖВ”and did you ever see her again?ГўВҖВқ

30309m



ГўВҖВңNever.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI never returned to the country where she lived.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo Malta?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; Malta.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShe is, then, now at Malta?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd have you forgiven her for all she has made you suffer?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHer,ГўВҖВ”yes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut only her; do you then still hate those who separated you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hate them? Not at all; why should I?ГўВҖВқ The countess placed herself
before Monte Cristo, still holding in her hand a portion of the
perfumed grapes.

ГўВҖВңTake some,ГўВҖВқ she said.

ГўВҖВңMadame, I never eat Muscatel grapes,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, as if the
subject had not been mentioned before. The countess dashed the grapes
into the nearest thicket, with a gesture of despair.

ГўВҖВңInflexible man!ГўВҖВқ she murmured. Monte Cristo remained as unmoved as if
the reproach had not been addressed to him.

Albert at this moment ran in. ГўВҖВңOh, mother,ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed, ГўВҖВңsuch a
misfortune has happened!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? What has happened?ГўВҖВқ asked the countess, as though awakening from
a sleep to the realities of life; ГўВҖВңdid you say a misfortune? Indeed, I
should expect misfortunes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Villefort is here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe comes to fetch his wife and daughter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran is just arrived in Paris, bringing the
news of M. de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs death, which took place on the first stage
after he left Marseilles. Madame de Villefort, who was in very good
spirits, would neither believe nor think of the misfortune, but
Mademoiselle Valentine, at the first words, guessed the whole truth,
notwithstanding all the precautions of her father; the blow struck her
like a thunderbolt, and she fell senseless.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd how was M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran related to Mademoiselle de Villefort?ГўВҖВқ
said the count.

ГўВҖВңHe was her grandfather on the motherГўВҖВҷs side. He was coming here to
hasten her marriage with Franz.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo Franz must wait. Why was not M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran also grandfather to
Mademoiselle Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlbert, Albert,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Morcerf, in a tone of mild reproof,
ГўВҖВңwhat are you saying? Ah, count, he esteems you so highly, tell him
that he has spoken amiss.ГўВҖВқ

And she took two or three steps forward. Monte Cristo watched her with
an air so thoughtful, and so full of affectionate admiration, that she
turned back and grasped his hand; at the same time she seized that of
her son, and joined them together.

ГўВҖВңWe are friends; are we not?ГўВҖВқ she asked.

ГўВҖВңOh, madame, I do not presume to call myself your friend, but at all
times I am your most respectful servant.ГўВҖВқ The countess left with an
indescribable pang in her heart, and before she had taken ten steps the
count saw her raise her handkerchief to her eyes.

ГўВҖВңDo not my mother and you agree?ГўВҖВқ asked Albert, astonished.

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary,ГўВҖВқ replied the count, ГўВҖВңdid you not hear her declare
that we were friends?ГўВҖВқ

They re-entered the drawing-room, which Valentine and Madame de
Villefort had just quitted. It is perhaps needless to add that Morrel
departed almost at the same time.



 Chapter 72. Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran

A gloomy scene had indeed just passed at the house of M. de Villefort.
After the ladies had departed for the ball, whither all the entreaties
of Madame de Villefort had failed in persuading him to accompany them,
the procureur had shut himself up in his study, according to his
custom, with a heap of papers calculated to alarm anyone else, but
which generally scarcely satisfied his inordinate desires.

But this time the papers were a mere matter of form. Villefort had
secluded himself, not to study, but to reflect; and with the door
locked and orders given that he should not be disturbed excepting for
important business, he sat down in his armchair and began to ponder
over the events, the remembrance of which had during the last eight
days filled his mind with so many gloomy thoughts and bitter
recollections.

Then, instead of plunging into the mass of documents piled before him,
he opened the drawer of his desk, touched a spring, and drew out a
parcel of cherished memoranda, amongst which he had carefully arranged,
in characters only known to himself, the names of all those who, either
in his political career, in money matters, at the bar, or in his
mysterious love affairs, had become his enemies.

Their number was formidable, now that he had begun to fear, and yet
these names, powerful though they were, had often caused him to smile
with the same kind of satisfaction experienced by a traveller who from
the summit of a mountain beholds at his feet the craggy eminences, the
almost impassable paths, and the fearful chasms, through which he has
so perilously climbed. When he had run over all these names in his
memory, again read and studied them, commenting meanwhile upon his
lists, he shook his head.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ he murmured, ГўВҖВңnone of my enemies would have waited so patiently
and laboriously for so long a space of time, that they might now come
and crush me with this secret. Sometimes, as Hamlet says:

ГўВҖВҳFoul deeds will rise,

Though all the earth oГўВҖВҷerwhelm them, to menГўВҖВҷs eyes;ГўВҖВҷ


but, like a phosphoric light, they rise but to mislead. The story has
been told by the Corsican to some priest, who in his turn has repeated
it. M. de Monte Cristo may have heard it, and to enlighten himselfГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңBut why should he wish to enlighten himself upon the subject?ГўВҖВқ asked
Villefort, after a momentГўВҖВҷs reflection, ГўВҖВңwhat interest can this M. de
Monte Cristo or M. Zaccone,ГўВҖВ”son of a shipowner of Malta, discoverer of
a mine in Thessaly, now visiting Paris for the first time,ГўВҖВ”what
interest, I say, can he take in discovering a gloomy, mysterious, and
useless fact like this? However, among all the incoherent details given
to me by the AbbГғВ© Busoni and by Lord Wilmore, by that friend and that
enemy, one thing appears certain and clear in my opinionГўВҖВ”that in no
period, in no case, in no circumstance, could there have been any
contact between him and me.ГўВҖВқ

But Villefort uttered words which even he himself did not believe. He
dreaded not so much the revelation, for he could reply to or deny its
truth;ГўВҖВ”he cared little for that _mene, mene, tekel upharsin_, which
appeared suddenly in letters of blood upon the wall;ГўВҖВ”but what he was
really anxious for was to discover whose hand had traced them. While he
was endeavoring to calm his fears,ГўВҖВ”and instead of dwelling upon the
political future that had so often been the subject of his ambitious
dreams, was imagining a future limited to the enjoyments of home, in
fear of awakening the enemy that had so long slept,ГўВҖВ”the noise of a
carriage sounded in the yard, then he heard the steps of an aged person
ascending the stairs, followed by tears and lamentations, such as
servants always give vent to when they wish to appear interested in
their masterГўВҖВҷs grief.

He drew back the bolt of his door, and almost directly an old lady
entered, unannounced, carrying her shawl on her arm, and her bonnet in
her hand. The white hair was thrown back from her yellow forehead, and
her eyes, already sunken by the furrows of age, now almost disappeared
beneath the eyelids swollen with grief.

ГўВҖВңOh, sir,ГўВҖВқ she said; ГўВҖВңoh, sir, what a misfortune! I shall die of it;
oh, yes, I shall certainly die of it!ГўВҖВқ

And then, falling upon the chair nearest the door, she burst into a
paroxysm of sobs. The servants, standing in the doorway, not daring to
approach nearer, were looking at NoirtierГўВҖВҷs old servant, who had heard
the noise from his masterГўВҖВҷs room, and run there also, remaining behind
the others. Villefort rose, and ran towards his mother-in-law, for it
was she.

ГўВҖВңWhy, what can have happened?ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed, ГўВҖВңwhat has thus disturbed
you? Is M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran with you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Saint-MГғВ©ran is dead,ГўВҖВқ answered the old marchioness, without
preface and without expression; she appeared to be stupefied. Villefort
drew back, and clasping his hands together, exclaimed:

ГўВҖВңDead!ГўВҖВ”so suddenly?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA week ago,ГўВҖВқ continued Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran, ГўВҖВңwe went out together in
the carriage after dinner. M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran had been unwell for some
days; still, the idea of seeing our dear Valentine again inspired him
with courage, and notwithstanding his illness he would leave. At six
leagues from Marseilles, after having eaten some of the lozenges he is
accustomed to take, he fell into such a deep sleep, that it appeared to
me unnatural; still I hesitated to wake him, although I fancied that
his face was flushed, and that the veins of his temples throbbed more
violently than usual. However, as it became dark, and I could no longer
see, I fell asleep; I was soon aroused by a piercing shriek, as from a
person suffering in his dreams, and he suddenly threw his head back
violently. I called the valet, I stopped the postilion, I spoke to M.
de Saint-MГғВ©ran, I applied my smelling-salts; but all was over, and I
arrived at Aix by the side of a corpse.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort stood with his mouth half open, quite stupefied.

ГўВҖВңOf course you sent for a doctor?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңImmediately; but, as I have told you, it was too late.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but then he could tell of what complaint the poor marquis had
died.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes, sir, he told me; it appears to have been an apoplectic
stroke.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what did you do then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Saint-MГғВ©ran had always expressed a desire, in case his death
happened during his absence from Paris, that his body might be brought
to the family vault. I had him put into a leaden coffin, and I am
preceding him by a few days.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh! my poor mother!ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңto have such duties to perform
at your age after such a blow!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGod has supported me through all; and then, my dear marquis, he would
certainly have done everything for me that I performed for him. It is
true that since I left him, I seem to have lost my senses. I cannot
cry; at my age they say that we have no more tears,ГўВҖВ”still I think that
when one is in trouble one should have the power of weeping. Where is
Valentine, sir? It is on her account I am here; I wish to see
Valentine.ГўВҖВқ

30315m



Villefort thought it would be terrible to reply that Valentine was at a
ball; so he only said that she had gone out with her step-mother, and
that she should be fetched. ГўВҖВңThis instant, sirГўВҖВ”this instant, I beseech
you!ГўВҖВқ said the old lady. Villefort placed the arm of Madame de
Saint-MГғВ©ran within his own, and conducted her to his apartment.

ГўВҖВңRest yourself, mother,ГўВҖВқ he said.

The marchioness raised her head at this word, and beholding the man who
so forcibly reminded her of her deeply-regretted child, who still lived
for her in Valentine, she felt touched at the name of mother, and
bursting into tears, she fell on her knees before an armchair, where
she buried her venerable head. Villefort left her to the care of the
women, while old Barrois ran, half-scared, to his master; for nothing
frightens old people so much as when death relaxes its vigilance over
them for a moment in order to strike some other old person. Then, while
Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran remained on her knees, praying fervently,
Villefort sent for a cab, and went himself to fetch his wife and
daughter from Madame de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs. He was so pale when he appeared at
the door of the ball-room, that Valentine ran to him, saying:

ГўВҖВңOh, father, some misfortune has happened!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour grandmamma has just arrived, Valentine,ГўВҖВқ said M. de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңAnd grandpapa?ГўВҖВқ inquired the young girl, trembling with apprehension.
M. de Villefort only replied by offering his arm to his daughter. It
was just in time, for ValentineГўВҖВҷs head swam, and she staggered; Madame
de Villefort instantly hastened to her assistance, and aided her
husband in dragging her to the carriage, saying:

ГўВҖВңWhat a singular event! Who could have thought it? Ah, yes, it is
indeed strange!ГўВҖВқ

And the wretched family departed, leaving a cloud of sadness hanging
over the rest of the evening. At the foot of the stairs, Valentine
found Barrois awaiting her.

ГўВҖВңM. Noirtier wishes to see you tonight, he said, in an undertone.

ГўВҖВңTell him I will come when I leave my dear grandmamma,ГўВҖВқ she replied,
feeling, with true delicacy, that the person to whom she could be of
the most service just then was Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran.

Valentine found her grandmother in bed; silent caresses, heartwrung
sobs, broken sighs, burning tears, were all that passed in this sad
interview, while Madame de Villefort, leaning on her husbandГўВҖВҷs arm,
maintained all outward forms of respect, at least towards the poor
widow. She soon whispered to her husband:

ГўВҖВңI think it would be better for me to retire, with your permission, for
the sight of me appears still to afflict your mother-in-law.ГўВҖВқ Madame de
Saint-MГғВ©ran heard her.

ГўВҖВңYes, yes,ГўВҖВқ she said softly to Valentine, ГўВҖВңlet her leave; but do you
stay.ГўВҖВқ

Madame de Villefort left, and Valentine remained alone beside the bed,
for the procureur, overcome with astonishment at the unexpected death,
had followed his wife. Meanwhile, Barrois had returned for the first
time to old Noirtier, who having heard the noise in the house, had, as
we have said, sent his old servant to inquire the cause; on his return,
his quick intelligent eye interrogated the messenger.

ГўВҖВңAlas, sir,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Barrois, ГўВҖВңa great misfortune has happened.
Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran has arrived, and her husband is dead!ГўВҖВқ

M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran and Noirtier had never been on strict terms of
friendship; still, the death of one old man always considerably affects
another. Noirtier let his head fall upon his chest, apparently
overwhelmed and thoughtful; then he closed one eye, in token of
inquiry.

Barrois asked, ГўВҖВңMademoiselle Valentine?ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier nodded his head.

ГўВҖВңShe is at the ball, as you know, since she came to say good-bye to you
in full dress.ГўВҖВқ Noirtier again closed his left eye.

ГўВҖВңDo you wish to see her?ГўВҖВқ Noirtier again made an affirmative sign.

ГўВҖВңWell, they have gone to fetch her, no doubt, from Madame de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs;
I will await her return, and beg her to come up here. Is that what you
wish for?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied the invalid.

Barrois, therefore, as we have seen, watched for Valentine, and
informed her of her grandfatherГўВҖВҷs wish. Consequently, Valentine came up
to Noirtier, on leaving Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran, who in the midst of her
grief had at last yielded to fatigue and fallen into a feverish sleep.
Within reach of her hand they placed a small table upon which stood a
bottle of orangeade, her usual beverage, and a glass. Then, as we have
said, the young girl left the bedside to see M. Noirtier.

Valentine kissed the old man, who looked at her with such tenderness
that her eyes again filled with tears, whose sources he thought must be
exhausted. The old gentleman continued to dwell upon her with the same
expression.

ГўВҖВңYes, yes,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңyou mean that I have yet a kind
grandfather left, do you not.ГўВҖВқ The old man intimated that such was his
meaning. ГўВҖВңAh, yes, happily I have,ГўВҖВқ replied Valentine. ГўВҖВңWithout that,
what would become of me?ГўВҖВқ

It was one oГўВҖВҷclock in the morning. Barrois, who wished to go to bed
himself, observed that after such sad events everyone stood in need of
rest. Noirtier would not say that the only rest he needed was to see
his child, but wished her good-night, for grief and fatigue had made
her appear quite ill.

The next morning she found her grandmother in bed; the fever had not
abated, on the contrary her eyes glistened and she appeared to be
suffering from violent nervous irritability.

ГўВҖВңOh, dear grandmamma, are you worse?ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Valentine, perceiving
all these signs of agitation.

ГўВҖВңNo, my child, no,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran; ГўВҖВңbut I was impatiently
waiting for your arrival, that I might send for your father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy father?ГўВҖВқ inquired Valentine, uneasily.

ГўВҖВңYes, I wish to speak to him.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine durst not oppose her grandmotherГўВҖВҷs wish, the cause of which
she did not know, and an instant afterwards Villefort entered.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran, without using any circumlocution,
and as if fearing she had no time to lose, ГўВҖВңyou wrote to me concerning
the marriage of this child?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, ГўВҖВңit is not only projected but
arranged.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour intended son-in-law is named M. Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, madame.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs he not the son of General dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay who was on our side, and who was
assassinated some days before the usurper returned from the Island of

ГўВҖВңThe same.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoes he not dislike the idea of marrying the granddaughter of a
Jacobin?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOur civil dissensions are now happily extinguished, mother,ГўВҖВқ said
Villefort; ГўВҖВңM. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay was quite a child when his father died, he
knows very little of M. Noirtier, and will meet him, if not with
pleasure, at least with indifference.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs it a suitable match?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn every respect.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd the young man?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs regarded with universal esteem.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou approve of him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is one of the most well-bred young men I know.ГўВҖВқ

During the whole of this conversation Valentine had remained silent.

ГўВҖВңWell, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran, after a few minutesГўВҖВҷ
reflection, ГўВҖВңI must hasten the marriage, for I have but a short time to
live.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou, madame?ГўВҖВқ ГўВҖВңYou, dear mamma?ГўВҖВқ exclaimed M. de Villefort and
Valentine at the same time.

ГўВҖВңI know what I am saying,ГўВҖВқ continued the marchioness; ГўВҖВңI must hurry

poor RenГғВ©e, whom you have so soon forgotten, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңyou forget that I was obliged to give a
mother to my child.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA stepmother is never a mother, sir. But this is not to the
purpose,ГўВҖВ”our business concerns Valentine, let us leave the dead in
peace.ГўВҖВқ

All this was said with such exceeding rapidity, that there was
something in the conversation that seemed like the beginning of
delirium.

ГўВҖВңIt shall be as you wish, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort; ГўВҖВңmore especially
since your wishes coincide with mine, and as soon as M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay
arrives in ParisГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

30319m



ГўВҖВңMy dear grandmother,ГўВҖВқ interrupted Valentine, ГўВҖВңconsider decorumГўВҖВ”the
recent death. You would not have me marry under such sad auspices?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy child,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed the old lady sharply, ГўВҖВңlet us hear none of the
conventional objections that deter weak minds from preparing for the
future. I also was married at the death-bed of my mother, and certainly
I have not been less happy on that account.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill that idea of death, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңStill?ГўВҖВ”Always! I tell you I am going to dieГўВҖВ”do you understand? Well,
before dying, I wish to see my son-in-law. I wish to tell him to make

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңyou must lay aside these exalted ideas,
which almost assume the appearance of madness. The dead, once buried in
their graves, rise no more.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I tell you, sir, that you are mistaken. This night I have had a
fearful sleep. It seemed as though my soul were already hovering over
my body, my eyes, which I tried to open, closed against my will, and
what will appear impossible above all to you, sir, I saw, with my eyes
shut, in the spot where you are now standing, issuing from that corner

saw, I tell you, silently enter, a white figure.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine screamed.

ГўВҖВңIt was the fever that disturbed you, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

30321m



ГўВҖВңDoubt, if you please, but I am sure of what I say. I saw a white
figure, and as if to prevent my discrediting the testimony of only one
of my senses, I heard my glass removedГўВҖВ”the same which is there now on
the table.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, dear mother, it was a dream.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo little was it a dream, that I stretched my hand towards the bell;

ГўВҖВңBut she saw no one?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPhantoms are visible to those only who ought to see them. It was the
soul of my husband!ГўВҖВ”Well, if my husbandГўВҖВҷs soul can come to me, why
should not my soul reappear to guard my granddaughter? the tie is even
more direct, it seems to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, deeply affected, in spite of himself, ГўВҖВңdo

ГўВҖВңNever, never, never,ГўВҖВқ said the marchioness. ГўВҖВңWhen does M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay

ГўВҖВңWe expect him every moment.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is well. As soon as he arrives inform me. We must be expeditious.
And then I also wish to see a notary, that I may be assured that all
our property returns to Valentine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, grandmamma,ГўВҖВқ murmured Valentine, pressing her lips on the burning
brow, ГўВҖВңdo you wish to kill me? Oh, how feverish you are; we must not
send for a notary, but for a doctor!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA doctor?ГўВҖВқ said she, shrugging her shoulders, ГўВҖВңI am not ill; I am
thirstyГўВҖВ”that is all.ГўВҖВқ

30323m



ГўВҖВңWhat are you drinking, dear grandmamma?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe same as usual, my dear, my glass is there on the tableГўВҖВ”give it to
me, Valentine.ГўВҖВқ Valentine poured the orangeade into a glass and gave it
to her grandmother with a certain degree of dread, for it was the same
glass she fancied that had been touched by the spectre.

The marchioness drained the glass at a single draught, and then turned
on her pillow, repeating,

ГўВҖВңThe notary, the notary!ГўВҖВқ

M. de Villefort left the room, and Valentine seated herself at the
bedside of her grandmother. The poor child appeared herself to require
the doctor she had recommended to her aged relative. A bright spot
burned in either cheek, her respiration was short and difficult, and
her pulse beat with feverish excitement. She was thinking of the
despair of Maximilian, when he should be informed that Madame de
Saint-MГғВ©ran, instead of being an ally, was unconsciously acting as his
enemy.

More than once she thought of revealing all to her grandmother, and she
would not have hesitated a moment, if Maximilian Morrel had been named
Albert de Morcerf or Raoul de ChГғВўteau-Renaud; but Morrel was of
plebeian extraction, and Valentine knew how the haughty Marquise de
Saint-MГғВ©ran despised all who were not noble. Her secret had each time
been repressed when she was about to reveal it, by the sad conviction
that it would be useless to do so; for, were it once discovered by her
father and mother, all would be lost.

Two hours passed thus; Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran was in a feverish sleep,
and the notary had arrived. Though his coming was announced in a very
low tone, Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran arose from her pillow.

ГўВҖВңThe notary!ГўВҖВқ she exclaimed, ГўВҖВңlet him come in.ГўВҖВқ

The notary, who was at the door, immediately entered. ГўВҖВңGo, Valentine,ГўВҖВқ
said Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran, ГўВҖВңand leave me with this gentleman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, grandmammaГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLeave meГўВҖВ”go!ГўВҖВқ

The young girl kissed her grandmother, and left with her handkerchief
to her eyes; at the door she found the valet de chambre, who told her
that the doctor was waiting in the dining-room. Valentine instantly ran
down. The doctor was a friend of the family, and at the same time one
of the cleverest men of the day, and very fond of Valentine, whose
birth he had witnessed. He had himself a daughter about her age, but
whose life was one continued source of anxiety and fear to him from her
mother having been consumptive.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңwe have been waiting for you with such
impatience, dear M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny. But, first of all, how are Madeleine and
Antoinette?ГўВҖВқ

Madeleine was the daughter of M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, and Antoinette his niece.
M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny smiled sadly.

ГўВҖВңAntoinette is very well,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңand Madeleine tolerably so. But
you sent for me, my dear child. It is not your father or Madame de
Villefort who is ill. As for you, although we doctors cannot divest our
patients of nerves, I fancy you have no further need of me than to
recommend you not to allow your imagination to take too wide a field.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine colored. M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny carried the science of divination
almost to a miraculous extent, for he was one of the physicians who
always work upon the body through the mind.

30325m



ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ she replied, ГўВҖВңit is for my poor grandmother. You know the
calamity that has happened to us, do you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know nothing.ГўВҖВқ said M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny.

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, restraining her tears, ГўВҖВңmy grandfather is
dead.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Saint-MГғВ©ran?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSuddenly?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom an apoplectic stroke.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn apoplectic stroke?ГўВҖВқ repeated the doctor.

ГўВҖВңYes, and my poor grandmother fancies that her husband, whom she never
left, has called her, and that she must go and join him. Oh, M.
dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, I beseech you, do something for her!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhere is she?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn her room with the notary.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd M. Noirtier?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңJust as he was, his mind perfectly clear, but the same incapability of
moving or speaking.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd the same love for youГўВҖВ”eh, my dear child?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңhe was very fond of me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho does not love you?ГўВҖВқ Valentine smiled sadly. ГўВҖВңWhat are your
grandmotherГўВҖВҷs symptoms?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn extreme nervous excitement and a strangely agitated sleep; she
fancied this morning in her sleep that her soul was hovering above her
body, which she at the same time watched. It must have been delirium;
she fancies, too, that she saw a phantom enter her chamber and even
heard the noise it made on touching her glass.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is singular,ГўВҖВқ said the doctor; ГўВҖВңI was not aware that Madame de
Saint-MГғВ©ran was subject to such hallucinations.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is the first time I ever saw her in this condition,ГўВҖВқ said
Valentine; ГўВҖВңand this morning she frightened me so that I thought her
mad; and my father, who you know is a strong-minded man, himself
appeared deeply impressed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe will go and see,ГўВҖВқ said the doctor; ГўВҖВңwhat you tell me seems very
strange.ГўВҖВқ The notary here descended, and Valentine was informed that
her grandmother was alone.

ГўВҖВңGo upstairs,ГўВҖВқ she said to the doctor.

ГўВҖВңAnd you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I dare notГўВҖВ”she forbade my sending for you; and, as you say, I am
myself agitated, feverish and out of sorts. I will go and take a turn
in the garden to recover myself.ГўВҖВқ

The doctor pressed ValentineГўВҖВҷs hand, and while he visited her
grandmother, she descended the steps. We need not say which portion of
the garden was her favorite walk. After remaining for a short time in
the parterre surrounding the house, and gathering a rose to place in
her waist or hair, she turned into the dark avenue which led to the
bench; then from the bench she went to the gate. As usual, Valentine
strolled for a short time among her flowers, but without gathering
them. The mourning in her heart forbade her assuming this simple
ornament, though she had not yet had time to put on the outward
semblance of woe.

30327m



She then turned towards the avenue. As she advanced she fancied she
heard a voice speaking her name. She stopped astonished, then the voice
reached her ear more distinctly, and she recognized it to be that of
Maximilian.



 Chapter 73. The Promise

It was indeed Maximilian Morrel, who had passed a wretched existence
since the previous day. With the instinct peculiar to lovers he had
anticipated after the return of Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran and the death of
the marquis, that something would occur at M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs in
connection with his attachment for Valentine. His presentiments were
realized, as we shall see, and his uneasy forebodings had goaded him
pale and trembling to the gate under the chestnut-trees.

Valentine was ignorant of the cause of this sorrow and anxiety, and as
it was not his accustomed hour for visiting her, she had gone to the
spot simply by accident or perhaps through sympathy. Morrel called her,
and she ran to the gate.

ГўВҖВңYou here at this hour?ГўВҖВқ said she.

ГўВҖВңYes, my poor girl,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel; ГўВҖВңI come to bring and to hear bad
tidings.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is, indeed, a house of mourning,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine; ГўВҖВңspeak,
Maximilian, although the cup of sorrow seems already full.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDear Valentine,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, endeavoring to conceal his own emotion,
ГўВҖВңlisten, I entreat you; what I am about to say is very serious. When
are you to be married?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will tell you all,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine; ГўВҖВңfrom you I have nothing to
conceal. This morning the subject was introduced, and my dear
grandmother, on whom I depended as my only support, not only declared
herself favorable to it, but is so anxious for it, that they only await
the arrival of M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, and the following day the contract will be
signed.ГўВҖВқ

A deep sigh escaped the young man, who gazed long and mournfully at her
he loved.

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ replied he, ГўВҖВңit is dreadful thus to hear my condemnation from
your own lips. The sentence is passed, and, in a few hours, will be
executed; it must be so, and I will not endeavor to prevent it. But,
since you say nothing remains but for M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay to arrive that the
contract may be signed, and the following day you will be his, tomorrow
you will be engaged to M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, for he came this morning to Paris.ГўВҖВқ
Valentine uttered a cry.

ГўВҖВңI was at the house of Monte Cristo an hour since,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel; ГўВҖВңwe
were speaking, he of the sorrow your family had experienced, and I of
your grief, when a carriage rolled into the courtyard. Never, till
then, had I placed any confidence in presentiments, but now I cannot
help believing them, Valentine. At the sound of that carriage I
shuddered; soon I heard steps on the staircase, which terrified me as
much as the footsteps of the commander did Don Juan. The door at last
opened; Albert de Morcerf entered first, and I began to hope my fears
were vain, when, after him, another young man advanced, and the count
exclaimed: ГўВҖВҳAh, here is the Baron Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay!ГўВҖВҷ I summoned all my
strength and courage to my support. Perhaps I turned pale and trembled,
but certainly I smiled; and five minutes after I left, without having
heard one word that had passed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoor Maximilian!ГўВҖВқ murmured Valentine.

ГўВҖВңValentine, the time has arrived when you must answer me. And remember
my life depends on your answer. What do you intend doing?ГўВҖВқ Valentine
held down her head; she was overwhelmed.

ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel; ГўВҖВңit is not the first time you have contemplated
our present position, which is a serious and urgent one; I do not think
it is a moment to give way to useless sorrow; leave that for those who
like to suffer at their leisure and indulge their grief in secret.
There are such in the world, and God will doubtless reward them in
heaven for their resignation on earth, but those who mean to contend
must not lose one precious moment, but must return immediately the blow
which fortune strikes. Do you intend to struggle against our
ill-fortune? Tell me, Valentine for it is that I came to know.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine trembled, and looked at him with amazement. The idea of
resisting her father, her grandmother, and all the family, had never
occurred to her.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you say, Maximilian?ГўВҖВқ asked Valentine. ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean by a
struggle? Oh, it would be a sacrilege. What? I resist my fatherГўВҖВҷs
order, and my dying grandmotherГўВҖВҷs wish? Impossible!ГўВҖВқ

Morrel started.

ГўВҖВңYou are too noble not to understand me, and you understand me so well
that you already yield, dear Maximilian. No, no; I shall need all my
strength to struggle with myself and support my grief in secret, as you
say. But to grieve my fatherГўВҖВ”to disturb my grandmotherГўВҖВҷs last
momentsГўВҖВ”never!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, calmly.

ГўВҖВңIn what a tone you speak!ГўВҖВқ cried Valentine.

ГўВҖВңI speak as one who admires you, mademoiselle.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMademoiselle,ГўВҖВқ cried Valentine; ГўВҖВңmademoiselle! Oh, selfish man! he
sees me in despair, and pretends he cannot understand me!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou mistakeГўВҖВ”I understand you perfectly. You will not oppose M.
Villefort, you will not displease the marchioness, and tomorrow you
will sign the contract which will bind you to your husband.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, _mon Dieu!_ tell me, how can I do otherwise?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not appeal to me, mademoiselle; I shall be a bad judge in such a
case; my selfishness will blind me,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel, whose low voice
and clenched hands announced his growing desperation.

ГўВҖВңWhat would you have proposed, Maximilian, had you found me willing to
accede?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is not for me to say.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are wrong; you must advise me what to do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you seriously ask my advice, Valentine?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly, dear Maximilian, for if it is good, I will follow it; you
know my devotion to you.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel pronounced these words with perfect calmness; Valentine looked

ГўВҖВңI am going to have the honor of taking my leave of you, mademoiselle,

ГўВҖВңOh!ГўВҖВқ murmured Valentine.

ГўВҖВңAdieu, Valentine, adieu!ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, bowing.

ГўВҖВңI am going, that I may not bring fresh trouble into your family: and

ГўВҖВңBefore you leave me, tell me what you are going to do, Maximilian.ГўВҖВқ
The young man smiled sorrowfully.

ГўВҖВңSpeak, speak!ГўВҖВқ said Valentine; ГўВҖВңI entreat you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, fear not,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian, stopping at a short distance, ГўВҖВңI do

ГўВҖВңOn yourself, then, unhappy man; on yourself?ГўВҖВқ

Valentine trembled convulsively; she loosened her hold of the gate, her

ГўВҖВңOh, for pityГўВҖВҷs sake,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңyou will live, will you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, on my honor,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian; ГўВҖВңbut that will not affect you. You

ГўВҖВңMaximilian,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңMaximilian, my friend, my brother on earth, my

ГўВҖВңMy God,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, raising both her hands to heaven with a

Morrel, who had already gone some few steps away, again returned, and

ГўВҖВңTruly,ГўВҖВқ murmured Valentine, ГўВҖВңwho on this earth cares for me, if he

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian, ГўВҖВңyou shall not leave him. M. Noirtier has

ГўВҖВңYes, I promise you, as faithfully as you have promised me that this

ГўВҖВңI promise you by all that is most sacred to me in the world, namely,

ГўВҖВңYes, we will wait,ГўВҖВқ replied Valentine, who revived at these words;

ГўВҖВңInstead of signingГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will go to you, and we will fly; but from this moment until then,

ГўВҖВңYou are right, Valentine; but how shall I ascertain?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom the notary, M. Deschamps.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd for myselfГўВҖВ”I will write to you, depend on me. I dread this

ГўВҖВңThank you, my adored Valentine, thank you; that is enough. When once I

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңI will now acknowledge you are right,

ГўВҖВңMy adored Valentine, words cannot express one half of my

Valentine had approached, or rather, had placed her lips so near the

ГўВҖВңAdieu, then, till we meet again,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, tearing herself

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThanks, thanks, dear love, adieu!ГўВҖВқ

The sound of a kiss was heard, and Valentine fled through the avenue.
Morrel listened to catch the last sound of her dress brushing the
branches, and of her footstep on the gravel, then raised his eyes with
an ineffable smile of thankfulness to heaven for being permitted to be
thus loved, and then also disappeared.

The young man returned home and waited all the evening and all the next

ГўВҖВңTears, entreaties, prayers, have availed me nothing. Yesterday, for
two hours, I was at the church of Saint-Philippe-du-Roule, and for two

ГўВҖВңYour betrothed,

ГўВҖВңValentine de Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңP.S.ГўВҖВ”My poor grandmother gets worse and worse; yesterday her fever
