saying a word to my father, we sent it to the convent, where it was
divided amongst the prisoners.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd how old were you at that time?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was three years old,ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e.

ГўВҖВңThen you remember everything that went on about you from the time when
you were three years old?ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңEverything.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCount,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, in a low tone to Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңdo allow the
signora to tell me something of her history. You prohibited my
mentioning my fatherГўВҖВҷs name to her, but perhaps she will allude to him

Monte Cristo turned to HaydГғВ©e, and with an expression of countenance

ГўВҖВңWhat are you saying to her?ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf in an undertone.

ГўВҖВңI again reminded her that you were a friend, and that she need not
conceal anything from you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңthis pious pilgrimage in behalf of the prisoners

ГўВҖВңOh, then I remember as if it were but yesterday sitting under the
shade of some sycamore-trees, on the borders of a lake, in the waters
of which the trembling foliage was reflected as in a mirror. Under the
oldest and thickest of these trees, reclining on cushions, sat my
father; my mother was at his feet, and I, childlike, amused myself by

with the diamond-hilt of the scimitar attached to his girdle. Then from

I paid no attention, but which he always answered in the same tone of
voice, either ГўВҖВҳKill,ГўВҖВҷ or ГўВҖВҳPardon.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is very strange,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңto hear such words proceed from the
mouth of anyone but an actress on the stage, and one needs constantly
to be saying to oneГўВҖВҷs self, ГўВҖВҳThis is no fiction, it is all reality,ГўВҖВҷ in
order to believe it. And how does France appear in your eyes,
accustomed as they have been to gaze on such enchanted scenes?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think it is a fine country,ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңbut I see France as it

joyous.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo young,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, forgetting at the moment the CountГўВҖВҷs command

HaydГғВ©e turned her eyes towards Monte Cristo, who, making at the same

ГўВҖВңГҺВ•ГЎВјВ°ГҸВҖГҺВӯГўВҖВ”speak.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNothing is ever so firmly impressed on the mind as the memory of our
early childhood, and with the exception of the two scenes I have just
described to you, all my earliest reminiscences are fraught with
deepest sadness.ГўВҖВқ

40068m



ГўВҖВңSpeak, speak, signora,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңI am listening with the most
intense delight and interest to all you say.ГўВҖВқ

HaydГғВ©e answered his remark with a melancholy smile. ГўВҖВңYou wish me, then,
to relate the history of my past sorrows?ГўВҖВқ said she.

ГўВҖВңI beg you to do so,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert.

ГўВҖВңWell, I was but four years old when one night I was suddenly awakened
by my mother. We were in the palace of Yanina; she snatched me from the
cushions on which I was sleeping, and on opening my eyes I saw hers
filled with tears. She took me away without speaking. When I saw her
weeping I began to cry too. ГўВҖВҳHush, child!ГўВҖВҷ said she. At other times in
spite of maternal endearments or threats, I had with a childГўВҖВҷs caprice

silence, that I ceased crying as soon as her command was given. She
bore me rapidly away.

ГўВҖВңI saw then that we were descending a large staircase; around us were
all my motherГўВҖВҷs servants carrying trunks, bags, ornaments, jewels,
purses of gold, with which they were hurrying away in the greatest

ГўВҖВңBehind the women came a guard of twenty men armed with long guns and
pistols, and dressed in the costume which the Greeks have assumed since
they have again become a nation. You may imagine there was something
startling and ominous,ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e, shaking her head and turning pale
at the mere remembrance of the scene, ГўВҖВңin this long file of slaves and

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳQuick!ГўВҖВҷ said a voice at the end of the gallery. This voice made
everyone bow before it, resembling in its effect the wind passing over

father. He came last, clothed in his splendid robes and holding in his
hand the carbine which your emperor presented him. He was leaning on

a shepherd would his straggling flock. My father,ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e, raising
her head, ГўВҖВңwas that illustrious man known in Europe under the name of
Ali Tepelini, pasha of Yanina, and before whom Turkey trembled.ГўВҖВқ

Albert, without knowing why, started on hearing these words pronounced

there was something supernaturally gloomy and terrible in the

recalled to his mind the remembrance of the fearful death of this man,

ГўВҖВңSoon,ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңwe halted on our march, and found ourselves on
the borders of a lake. My mother pressed me to her throbbing heart, and
at the distance of a few paces I saw my father, who was glancing
anxiously around. Four marble steps led down to the waterГўВҖВҷs edge, and
below them was a boat floating on the tide.

40070m



ГўВҖВңFrom where we stood I could see in the middle of the lake a large
blank mass; it was the kiosk to which we were going. This kiosk
appeared to me to be at a considerable distance, perhaps on account of
the darkness of the night, which prevented any object from being more
than partially discerned. We stepped into the boat. I remember well
that the oars made no noise whatever in striking the water, and when I
leaned over to ascertain the cause I saw that they were muffled with
the sashes of our Palikares.14 Besides the rowers, the boat contained
only the women, my father, mother, Selim, and myself. The Palikares had
remained on the shore of the lake, ready to cover our retreat; they
were kneeling on the lowest of the marble steps, and in that manner
intended making a rampart of the three others, in case of pursuit. Our
bark flew before the wind. ГўВҖВҳWhy does the boat go so fast?ГўВҖВҷ asked I of
my mother.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳSilence, child! Hush, we are flying!ГўВҖВҷ I did not understand. Why
should my father fly?ГўВҖВ”he, the all-powerfulГўВҖВ”he, before whom others were
accustomed to flyГўВҖВ”he, who had taken for his device,

ГўВҖВҳThey hate me; then they fear me!ГўВҖВҷ


ГўВҖВңIt was, indeed, a flight which my father was trying to effect. I have

with long serviceГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Here HaydГғВ©e cast a significant glance at Monte Cristo, whose eyes had

ГўВҖВңYou were saying, signora,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, who was paying the most

ГўВҖВңHad treated with the Seraskier15 Kourchid, who had been sent by the

ГўВҖВңAnd this officer,ГўВҖВқ asked Albert, ГўВҖВңdo you remember his name, signora?ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo exchanged a rapid glance with the young girl, which was

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңI do not remember it just at this moment; but if it

Albert was on the point of pronouncing his fatherГўВҖВҷs name, when Monte
Cristo gently held up his finger in token of reproach; the young man

ГўВҖВңIt was towards this kiosk that we were rowing. A ground floor,
ornamented with arabesques, bathing its terraces in the water, and
another floor, looking on the lake, was all which was visible to the
eye. But beneath the ground floor, stretching out into the island, was
a large subterranean cavern, to which my mother, myself, and the women
were conducted. In this place were together 60,000 pouches and 200
barrels; the pouches contained 25,000,000 of money in gold, and the
barrels were filled with 30,000 pounds of gunpowder.

ГўВҖВңNear the barrels stood Selim, my fatherГўВҖВҷs favorite, whom I mentioned
to you just now. He stood watch day and night with a lance provided
with a lighted slowmatch in his hand, and he had orders to blow up
everythingГўВҖВ”kiosk, guards, women, gold, and Ali Tepelini himselfГўВҖВ”at the
first signal given by my father. I remember well that the slaves,
convinced of the precarious tenure on which they held their lives,
passed whole days and nights in praying, crying, and groaning. As for
me, I can never forget the pale complexion and black eyes of the young
soldier, and whenever the angel of death summons me to another world, I
am quite sure I shall recognize Selim. I cannot tell you how long we
remained in this state; at that period I did not even know what time
meant. Sometimes, but very rarely, my father summoned me and my mother
to the terrace of the palace; these were hours of recreation for me, as
I never saw anything in the dismal cavern but the gloomy countenances
of the slaves and SelimГўВҖВҷs fiery lance. My father was endeavoring to
pierce with his eager looks the remotest verge of the horizon,
examining attentively every black speck which appeared on the lake,
while my mother, reclining by his side, rested her head on his
shoulder, and I played at his feet, admiring everything I saw with that
unsophisticated innocence of childhood which throws a charm round
objects insignificant in themselves, but which in its eyes are invested
with the greatest importance. The heights of Pindus towered above us;
the castle of Yanina rose white and angular from the blue waters of the
lake, and the immense masses of black vegetation which, viewed in the
distance, gave the idea of lichens clinging to the rocks, were in
reality gigantic fir-trees and myrtles.

ГўВҖВңOne morning my father sent for us; my mother had been crying all the
night, and was very wretched; we found the pasha calm, but paler than
usual. ГўВҖВҳTake courage, Vasiliki,ГўВҖВҷ said he; ГўВҖВҳtoday arrives the firman of
the master, and my fate will be decided. If my pardon be complete, we
shall return triumphant to Yanina; if the news be inauspicious, we must
fly this night.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳBut supposing our enemy should not allow us to do
so?ГўВҖВҷ said my mother. ГўВҖВҳOh, make yourself easy on that head,ГўВҖВҷ said Ali,
smiling; ГўВҖВҳSelim and his flaming lance will settle that matter. They
would be glad to see me dead, but they would not like themselves to die
with me.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңMy mother only answered by sighs to consolations which she knew did
not come from my fatherГўВҖВҷs heart. She prepared the iced water which he
was in the habit of constantly drinking,ГўВҖВ”for since his sojourn at the
kiosk he had been parched by the most violent fever,ГўВҖВ”after which she
anointed his white beard with perfumed oil, and lighted his chibouque,

arose, seizing his arms and priming his pistols. ГўВҖВҳVasiliki,ГўВҖВҷ said he to

decide everything. In the space of half an hour we shall know the

you,ГўВҖВҷ said Vasiliki; ГўВҖВҳif you die, my lord, I will die with you.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳGo to
Selim!ГўВҖВҷ cried my father. ГўВҖВҳAdieu, my lord,ГўВҖВҷ murmured my mother,
determining quietly to await the approach of death. ГўВҖВҳTake away

ГўВҖВңAs for me, I had been forgotten in the general confusion; I ran toward
Ali Tepelini; he saw me hold out my arms to him, and he stooped down
and pressed my forehead with his lips. Oh, how distinctly I remember
that kiss!ГўВҖВ”it was the last he ever gave me, and I feel as if it were
still warm on my forehead. On descending, we saw through the

looked like birds skimming the surface of the waves. During this time,
in the kiosk at my fatherГўВҖВҷs feet, were seated twenty Palikares,

quitted my father after that last kiss.

ГўВҖВңMy mother and I traversed the gloomy passage leading to the cavern.
Selim was still at his post, and smiled sadly on us as we entered. We
fetched our cushions from the other end of the cavern, and sat down by

young as I was, I quite understood that some imminent danger was

Albert had often heardГўВҖВ”not from his father, for he never spoke on the
subject, but from strangersГўВҖВ”the description of the last moments of the
vizier of Yanina; he had read different accounts of his death, but the
story seemed to acquire fresh meaning from the voice and expression of
the young girl, and her sympathetic accent and the melancholy

As to HaydГғВ©e, these terrible reminiscences seemed to have overpowered

her eyes gazing on vacancy indicated that she was mentally

sombre picture which she sketched. Monte Cristo looked at her with an
indescribable expression of interest and pity.

ГўВҖВңGo on, my child,ГўВҖВқ said the count in the Romaic language.

40074m



HaydГғВ©e looked up abruptly, as if the sonorous tones of Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs

ГўВҖВңIt was about four oГўВҖВҷclock in the afternoon, and although the day was
brilliant out-of-doors, we were enveloped in the gloomy darkness of the
cavern. One single, solitary light was burning there, and it appeared

My mother was a Christian, and she prayed. Selim repeated from time to

some hope. As she was coming down, she thought she recognized the

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWhat do you fear, Vasiliki?ГўВҖВҷ said Selim, in a voice at once so gentle

ГўВҖВңMy mother experienced the same sensations, for I felt her tremble.
ГўВҖВҳMamma, mamma,ГўВҖВҷ said I, ГўВҖВҳare we really to be killed?ГўВҖВҷ And at the sound

and I am to extinguish the match and leave the magazine untouched.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳMy
friend,ГўВҖВҷ said my mother, ГўВҖВҳwhen your masterГўВҖВҷs orders arrive, if it is
the poniard which he sends, instead of despatching us by that horrible

tranquilly.

ГўВҖВңSuddenly we heard loud cries; and, listening, discerned that they were
cries of joy. The name of the French officer who had been sent to
Constantinople resounded on all sides amongst our Palikares; it was
evident that he brought the answer of the emperor, and that it was
favorable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd do you not remember the FrenchmanГўВҖВҷs name?ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, quite
ready to aid the memory of the narrator. Monte Cristo made a sign to

ГўВҖВңI do not recollect it,ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e.

ГўВҖВңThe noise increased; steps were heard approaching nearer and nearer;
they were descending the steps leading to the cavern. Selim made ready

Selim. ГўВҖВҳBut whoever you may be, I charge you not to advance another

pardon to the Vizier Ali, and not only gives him his life, but restores
to him his fortune and his possessions.ГўВҖВҷ My mother uttered a cry of
joy, and clasped me to her bosom. ГўВҖВҳStop,ГўВҖВҷ said Selim, seeing that she
was about to go out; ГўВҖВҳyou see I have not yet received the
ring,ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳTrue,ГўВҖВҷ said my mother. And she fell on her knees, at the same
time holding me up towards heaven, as if she desired, while praying to
God in my behalf, to raise me actually to his presence.ГўВҖВқ

And for the second time HaydГғВ©e stopped, overcome by such violent
emotion that the perspiration stood upon her pale brow, and her stifled
voice seemed hardly able to find utterance, so parched and dry were her
throat and lips.

40076m



Monte Cristo poured a little iced water into a glass, and presented it
to her, saying with a mildness in which was also a shade of
command,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңCourage.ГўВҖВқ

HaydГғВ©e dried her eyes, and continued:

ГўВҖВңBy this time our eyes, habituated to the darkness, had recognized the
messenger of the pasha,ГўВҖВ”it was a friend. Selim had also recognized him,
but the brave young man only acknowledged one duty, which was to obey.
ГўВҖВҳIn whose name do you come?ГўВҖВҷ said he to him. ГўВҖВҳI come in the name of our
master, Ali Tepelini.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳIf you come from Ali himself,ГўВҖВҷ said Selim, ГўВҖВҳyou
know what you were charged to remit to me?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳYes,ГўВҖВҷ said the messenger,
ГўВҖВҳand I bring you his ring.ГўВҖВҷ At these words he raised his hand above his
head, to show the token; but it was too far off, and there was not
light enough to enable Selim, where he was standing, to distinguish and
recognize the object presented to his view. ГўВҖВҳI do not see what you have
in your hand,ГўВҖВҷ said Selim. ГўВҖВҳApproach then,ГўВҖВҷ said the messenger, ГўВҖВҳor I
will come nearer to you, if you prefer it.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳI will agree to neither
one nor the other,ГўВҖВҷ replied the young soldier; ГўВҖВҳplace the object which
I desire to see in the ray of light which shines there, and retire
while I examine it.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳBe it so,ГўВҖВҷ said the envoy; and he retired, after
having first deposited the token agreed on in the place pointed out to
him by Selim.

ГўВҖВңOh, how our hearts palpitated; for it did, indeed, seem to be a ring
which was placed there. But was it my fatherГўВҖВҷs ring? that was the
question. Selim, still holding in his hand the lighted match, walked
towards the opening in the cavern, and, aided by the faint light which
streamed in through the mouth of the cave, picked up the token.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳIt is well,ГўВҖВҷ said he, kissing it; ГўВҖВҳit is my masterГўВҖВҷs ring!ГўВҖВҷ And
throwing the match on the ground, he trampled on it and extinguished
it. The messenger uttered a cry of joy and clapped his hands. At this
signal four soldiers of the Seraskier Kourchid suddenly appeared, and
Selim fell, pierced by five blows. Each man had stabbed him separately,
and, intoxicated by their crime, though still pale with fear, they
sought all over the cavern to discover if there was any fear of fire,
after which they amused themselves by rolling on the bags of gold. At
this moment my mother seized me in her arms, and hurrying noiselessly
along numerous turnings and windings known only to ourselves, she
arrived at a private staircase of the kiosk, where was a scene of
frightful tumult and confusion. The lower rooms were entirely filled
with KourchidГўВҖВҷs troops; that is to say, with our enemies. Just as my
mother was on the point of pushing open a small door, we heard the
voice of the pasha sounding in a loud and threatening tone. My mother
applied her eye to the crack between the boards; I luckily found a
small opening which afforded me a view of the apartment and what was
passing within. ГўВҖВҳWhat do you want?ГўВҖВҷ said my father to some people who
were holding a paper inscribed with characters of gold. ГўВҖВҳWhat we want,ГўВҖВҷ
replied one, ГўВҖВҳis to communicate to you the will of his highness. Do you
see this firman?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳI do,ГўВҖВҷ said my father. ГўВҖВҳWell, read it; he demands
your head.ГўВҖВҷ

40078m



ГўВҖВңMy father answered with a loud laugh, which was more frightful than
even threats would have been, and he had not ceased when two reports of
a pistol were heard; he had fired them himself, and had killed two men.
The Palikares, who were prostrated at my fatherГўВҖВҷs feet, now sprang up
and fired, and the room was filled with fire and smoke. At the same
instant the firing began on the other side, and the balls penetrated
the boards all round us. Oh, how noble did the grand vizier my father
look at that moment, in the midst of the flying bullets, his scimitar
in his hand, and his face blackened with the powder of his enemies! and
how he terrified them, even then, and made them fly before him! ГўВҖВҳSelim,
Selim!ГўВҖВҷ cried he, ГўВҖВҳguardian of the fire, do your duty!ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳSelim is
dead,ГўВҖВҷ replied a voice which seemed to come from the depths of the
earth, ГўВҖВҳand you are lost, Ali!ГўВҖВҷ At the same moment an explosion was
heard, and the flooring of the room in which my father was sitting was
suddenly torn up and shivered to atomsГўВҖВ”the troops were firing from
underneath. Three or four Palikares fell with their bodies literally
ploughed with wounds.

ГўВҖВңMy father howled aloud, plunged his fingers into the holes which the
balls had made, and tore up one of the planks entire. But immediately
through this opening twenty more shots were fired, and the flame,
rushing up like fire from the crater of a volcano, soon reached the
tapestry, which it quickly devoured. In the midst of all this frightful
tumult and these terrific cries, two reports, fearfully distinct,
followed by two shrieks more heartrending than all, froze me with
terror. These two shots had mortally wounded my father, and it was he
who had given utterance to these frightful cries. However, he remained
standing, clinging to a window. My mother tried to force the door, that
she might go and die with him, but it was fastened on the inside. All
around him were lying the Palikares, writhing in convulsive agonies,
while two or three who were only slightly wounded were trying to escape
by springing from the windows. At this crisis the whole flooring
suddenly gave way, my father fell on one knee, and at the same moment
twenty hands were thrust forth, armed with sabres, pistols, and
poniardsГўВҖВ”twenty blows were instantaneously directed against one man,
and my father disappeared in a whirlwind of fire and smoke kindled by
these demons, and which seemed like hell itself opening beneath his
feet. I felt myself fall to the ground, my mother had fainted.ГўВҖВқ

HaydГғВ©eГўВҖВҷs arms fell by her side, and she uttered a deep groan, at the
same time looking towards the count as if to ask if he were satisfied
with her obedience to his commands.

Monte Cristo arose and approached her, took her hand, and said to her
in Romaic:

ГўВҖВңCalm yourself, my dear child, and take courage in remembering that
there is a God who will punish traitors.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is a frightful story, count,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, terrified at the
paleness of HaydГғВ©eГўВҖВҷs countenance, ГўВҖВңand I reproach myself now for having
been so cruel and thoughtless in my request.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, it is nothing,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. Then, patting the young girl on
the head, he continued, ГўВҖВңHaydГғВ©e is very courageous, and she sometimes
even finds consolation in the recital of her misfortunes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause, my lord,ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e eagerly, ГўВҖВңmy miseries recall to me the
remembrance of your goodness.ГўВҖВқ

Albert looked at her with curiosity, for she had not yet related what
he most desired to know,ГўВҖВ”how she had become the slave of the count.
HaydГғВ©e saw at a glance the same expression pervading the countenances
of her two auditors; she continued:

ГўВҖВңWhen my mother recovered her senses we were before the seraskier.
ГўВҖВҳKill,ГўВҖВҷ said she, ГўВҖВҳbut spare the honor of the widow of Ali.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳIt is not
to me to whom you must address yourself,ГўВҖВҷ said Kourchid.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳTo whom, then?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳTo your new master.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWho and where is he?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳHe is here.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңAnd Kourchid pointed out one who had more than any contributed to the
death of my father,ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e, in a tone of chastened anger.

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңyou became the property of this man?ГўВҖВқ

40080m



ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied HaydГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңhe did not dare to keep us, so we were sold to
some slave-merchants who were going to Constantinople. We traversed
Greece, and arrived half dead at the imperial gates. They were
surrounded by a crowd of people, who opened a way for us to pass, when
suddenly my mother, having looked closely at an object which was
attracting their attention, uttered a piercing cry and fell to the
ground, pointing as she did so to a head which was placed over the
gates, and beneath which were inscribed these words:

ГўВҖВҳ_This is the head of Ali Tepelini, Pasha of Yanina._ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңI cried bitterly, and tried to raise my mother from the earth, but she
was dead! I was taken to the slave-market, and was purchased by a rich
Armenian. He caused me to be instructed, gave me masters, and when I
was thirteen years of age he sold me to the Sultan Mahmoud.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf whom I bought her,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңas I told you, Albert, with
the emerald which formed a match to the one I had made into a box for
the purpose of holding my hashish pills.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, you are good, you are great, my lord!ГўВҖВқ said HaydГғВ©e, kissing the
countГўВҖВҷs hand, ГўВҖВңand I am very fortunate in belonging to such a master!ГўВҖВқ

Albert remained quite bewildered with all that he had seen and heard.

ГўВҖВңCome, finish your cup of coffee,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңthe history is
ended.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 78. We hear From Yanina

If Valentine could have seen the trembling step and agitated
countenance of Franz when he quitted the chamber of M. Noirtier, even
she would have been constrained to pity him. Villefort had only just
given utterance to a few incoherent sentences, and then retired to his
study, where he received about two hours afterwards the following
letter:

ГўВҖВңAfter all the disclosures which were made this morning, M. Noirtier de
Villefort must see the utter impossibility of any alliance being formed
between his family and that of M. Franz dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay. M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay must say
that he is shocked and astonished that M. de Villefort, who appeared to
be aware of all the circumstances detailed this morning, should not
have anticipated him in this announcement.ГўВҖВқ


No one who had seen the magistrate at this moment, so thoroughly
unnerved by the recent inauspicious combination of circumstances, would
have supposed for an instant that he had anticipated the annoyance;
although it certainly never had occurred to him that his father would
carry candor, or rather rudeness, so far as to relate such a history.
And in justice to Villefort, it must be understood that M. Noirtier,
who never cared for the opinion of his son on any subject, had always
omitted to explain the affair to Villefort, so that he had all his life
entertained the belief that General de Quesnel, or the Baron dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay,
as he was alternately styled, according as the speaker wished to
identify him by his own family name, or by the title which had been
conferred on him, fell the victim of assassination, and not that he was
killed fairly in a duel. This harsh letter, coming as it did from a man
generally so polite and respectful, struck a mortal blow at the pride
of Villefort.

Hardly had he read the letter, when his wife entered. The sudden
departure of Franz, after being summoned by M. Noirtier, had so much
astonished everyone, that the position of Madame de Villefort, left
alone with the notary and the witnesses, became every moment more
embarrassing. Determined to bear it no longer, she arose and left the
room; saying she would go and make some inquiries into the cause of his
sudden disappearance.

M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs communications on the subject were very limited and
concise; he told her, in fact, that an explanation had taken place
between M. Noirtier, M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, and himself, and that the marriage of
Valentine and Franz would consequently be broken off. This was an
awkward and unpleasant thing to have to report to those who were
waiting. She therefore contented herself with saying that M. Noirtier
having at the commencement of the discussion been attacked by a sort of
apoplectic fit, the affair would necessarily be deferred for some days
longer. This news, false as it was following so singularly in the train
of the two similar misfortunes which had so recently occurred,
evidently astonished the auditors, and they retired without a word.

During this time Valentine, at once terrified and happy, after having
embraced and thanked the feeble old man for thus breaking with a single
blow the chain which she had been accustomed to consider as
irrefragable, asked leave to retire to her own room, in order to
recover her composure. Noirtier looked the permission which she
solicited. But instead of going to her own room, Valentine, having once
gained her liberty, entered the gallery, and, opening a small door at
the end of it, found herself at once in the garden.

In the midst of all the strange events which had crowded one on the
other, an indefinable sentiment of dread had taken possession of
ValentineГўВҖВҷs mind. She expected every moment that she should see Morrel
appear, pale and trembling, to forbid the signing of the contract, like
the Laird of Ravenswood in _The Bride of Lammermoor_.

It was high time for her to make her appearance at the gate, for
Maximilian had long awaited her coming. He had half guessed what was
going on when he saw Franz quit the cemetery with M. de Villefort. He
followed M. dГўВҖВҷГғВүpinay, saw him enter, afterwards go out, and then
re-enter with Albert and ChГғВўteau-Renaud. He had no longer any doubts as
to the nature of the conference; he therefore quickly went to the gate
in the clover-patch, prepared to hear the result of the proceedings,
and very certain that Valentine would hasten to him the first moment
she should be set at liberty. He was not mistaken; peering through the
crevices of the wooden partition, he soon discovered the young girl,
who cast aside all her usual precautions and walked at once to the
barrier. The first glance which Maximilian directed towards her
entirely reassured him, and the first words she spoke made his heart
bound with delight.

ГўВҖВңWe are saved!ГўВҖВқ said Valentine.

ГўВҖВңSaved?ГўВҖВқ repeated Morrel, not being able to conceive such intense
happiness; ГўВҖВңby whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy my grandfather. Oh, Morrel, pray love him for all his goodness to
us!ГўВҖВқ

Morrel swore to love him with all his soul; and at that moment he could
safely promise to do so, for he felt as though it were not enough to
love him merely as a friend or even as a father, he worshiped him as a
god.

ГўВҖВңBut tell me, Valentine, how has it all been effected? What strange
means has he used to compass this blessed end?ГўВҖВқ

Valentine was on the point of relating all that had passed, but she
suddenly remembered that in doing so she must reveal a terrible secret
which concerned others as well as her grandfather, and she said:

ГўВҖВңAt some future time I will tell you all about it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut when will that be?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen I am your wife.ГўВҖВқ

The conversation had now turned upon a topic so pleasing to Morrel,
that he was ready to accede to anything that Valentine thought fit to
propose, and he likewise felt that a piece of intelligence such as he
just heard ought to be more than sufficient to content him for one day.
However, he would not leave without the promise of seeing Valentine
again the next night. Valentine promised all that Morrel required of
her, and certainly it was less difficult now for her to believe that
she should marry Maximilian than it was an hour ago to assure herself
that she should not marry Franz.

During the time occupied by the interview we have just detailed, Madame
de Villefort had gone to visit M. Noirtier. The old man looked at her
with that stern and forbidding expression with which he was accustomed
to receive her.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңit is superfluous for me to tell you that ValentineГўВҖВҷs
marriage is broken off, since it was here that the affair was
concluded.ГўВҖВқ

NoirtierГўВҖВҷs countenance remained immovable.

ГўВҖВңBut one thing I can tell you, of which I do not think you are aware;
that is, that I have always been opposed to this marriage, and that the
contract was entered into entirely without my consent or approbation.ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier regarded his daughter-in-law with the look of a man desiring
an explanation.

ГўВҖВңNow that this marriage, which I know you so much disliked, is done
away with, I come to you on an errand which neither M. de Villefort nor
Valentine could consistently undertake.ГўВҖВқ

NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eyes demanded the nature of her mission.

ГўВҖВңI come to entreat you, sir,ГўВҖВқ continued Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңas the
only one who has the right of doing so, inasmuch as I am the only one
who will receive no personal benefit from the transaction,ГўВҖВ”I come to
entreat you to restore, not your love, for that she has always
possessed, but to restore your fortune to your granddaughter.ГўВҖВқ

There was a doubtful expression in NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eyes; he was evidently
trying to discover the motive of this proceeding, and he could not
succeed in doing so.

ГўВҖВңMay I hope, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңthat your intentions
accord with my request?ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier made a sign that they did.

ГўВҖВңIn that case, sir,ГўВҖВқ rejoined Madame de Villefort, ГўВҖВңI will leave you
overwhelmed with gratitude and happiness at your prompt acquiescence to
my wishes.ГўВҖВқ She then bowed to M. Noirtier and retired.

The next day M. Noirtier sent for the notary; the first will was torn
up and a second made, in which he left the whole of his fortune to
Valentine, on condition that she should never be separated from him. It
was then generally reported that Mademoiselle de Villefort, the heiress
of the marquis and marchioness of Saint-MГғВ©ran, had regained the good
graces of her grandfather, and that she would ultimately be in
possession of an income of 300,000 livres.

While all the proceedings relative to the dissolution of the
marriage-contract were being carried on at the house of M. de
Villefort, Monte Cristo had paid his visit to the Count of Morcerf,
who, in order to lose no time in responding to M. DanglarsГўВҖВҷ wishes, and
at the same time to pay all due deference to his position in society,
donned his uniform of lieutenant-general, which he ornamented with all
his crosses, and thus attired, ordered his finest horses and drove to
the Rue de la ChaussГғВ©e dГўВҖВҷAntin.

Danglars was balancing his monthly accounts, and it was perhaps not the
most favorable moment for finding him in his best humor. At the first
sight of his old friend, Danglars assumed his majestic air, and settled
himself in his easy-chair.

Morcerf, usually so stiff and formal, accosted the banker in an affable
and smiling manner, and, feeling sure that the overture he was about to
make would be well received, he did not consider it necessary to adopt
any manГ…В“uvres in order to gain his end, but went at once straight to
the point.

40086m



ГўВҖВңWell, baron,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңhere I am at last; some time has elapsed since
our plans were formed, and they are not yet executed.ГўВҖВқ

Morcerf paused at these words, quietly waiting till the cloud should
have dispersed which had gathered on the brow of Danglars, and which he
attributed to his silence; but, on the contrary, to his great surprise,
it grew darker and darker.

ГўВҖВңTo what do you allude, monsieur?ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; as if he were trying
in vain to guess at the possible meaning of the generalГўВҖВҷs words.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңI see you are a stickler for forms, my dear sir,
and you would remind me that the ceremonial rites should not be
omitted. _Ma foi_, I beg your pardon, but as I have but one son, and it
is the first time I have ever thought of marrying him, I am still
serving my apprenticeship, you know; come, I will reform.ГўВҖВқ

And Morcerf with a forced smile arose, and, making a low bow to M.
Danglars, said:

ГўВҖВңBaron, I have the honor of asking of you the hand of Mademoiselle
EugГғВ©nie Danglars for my son, the Vicomte Albert de Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

But Danglars, instead of receiving this address in the favorable manner
which Morcerf had expected, knit his brow, and without inviting the
count, who was still standing, to take a seat, he said:

ГўВҖВңMonsieur, it will be necessary to reflect before I give you an
answer.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo reflect?ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, more and more astonished; ГўВҖВңhave you not had
enough time for reflection during the eight years which have elapsed
since this marriage was first discussed between us?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCount,ГўВҖВқ said the banker, ГўВҖВңthings are constantly occurring in the world
to induce us to lay aside our most established opinions, or at all
events to cause us to remodel them according to the change of
circumstances, which may have placed affairs in a totally different
light to that in which we at first viewed them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not understand you, baron,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңWhat I mean to say is this, sir,ГўВҖВ”that during the last fortnight
unforeseen circumstances have occurredГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExcuse me,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңbut is it a play we are acting?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA play?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, for it is like one; pray let us come more to the point, and
endeavor thoroughly to understand each other.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is quite my desire.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have seen M. de Monte Cristo have you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI see him very often,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, drawing himself up; ГўВҖВңhe is a
particular friend of mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, in one of your late conversations with him, you said that I
appeared to be forgetful and irresolute concerning this marriage, did
you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI did say so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, here I am, proving at once that I am really neither the one nor
the other, by entreating you to keep your promise on that score.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars did not answer.

ГўВҖВңHave you so soon changed your mind,ГўВҖВқ added Morcerf, ГўВҖВңor have you only
provoked my request that you may have the pleasure of seeing me
humbled?ГўВҖВқ

Danglars, seeing that if he continued the conversation in the same tone
in which he had begun it, the whole thing might turn out to his own
disadvantage, turned to Morcerf, and said:

ГўВҖВңCount, you must doubtless be surprised at my reserve, and I assure you
it costs me much to act in such a manner towards you; but, believe me
when I say that imperative necessity has imposed the painful task upon
me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThese are all so many empty words, my dear sir,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf: ГўВҖВңthey
might satisfy a new acquaintance, but the Comte de Morcerf does not
rank in that list; and when a man like him comes to another, recalls to
him his plighted word, and this man fails to redeem the pledge, he has
at least a right to exact from him a good reason for so doing.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars was a coward, but did not wish to appear so; he was piqued at
the tone which Morcerf had just assumed.

ГўВҖВңI am not without a good reason for my conduct,ГўВҖВқ replied the banker.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean to say?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI mean to say that I have a good reason, but that it is difficult to
explain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou must be aware, at all events, that it is impossible for me to
understand motives before they are explained to me; but one thing at
least is clear, which is, that you decline allying yourself with my
family.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңI merely suspend my decision, that is all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd do you really flatter yourself that I shall yield to all your
caprices, and quietly and humbly await the time of again being received
into your good graces?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, count, if you will not wait, we must look upon these projects as
if they had never been entertained.ГўВҖВқ

The count bit his lips till the blood almost started, to prevent the
ebullition of anger which his proud and irritable temper scarcely
allowed him to restrain; understanding, however, that in the present
state of things the laugh would decidedly be against him, he turned
from the door, towards which he had been directing his steps, and again
confronted the banker. A cloud settled on his brow, evincing decided
anxiety and uneasiness, instead of the expression of offended pride
which had lately reigned there.

ГўВҖВңMy dear Danglars,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңwe have been acquainted for many
years, and consequently we ought to make some allowance for each
otherГўВҖВҷs failings. You owe me an explanation, and really it is but fair
that I should know what circumstance has occurred to deprive my son of
your favor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is from no personal ill-feeling towards the viscount, that is all I
can say, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars, who resumed his insolent manner as
soon as he perceived that Morcerf was a little softened and calmed
down.

ГўВҖВңAnd towards whom do you bear this personal ill-feeling, then?ГўВҖВқ said
Morcerf, turning pale with anger. The expression of the countГўВҖВҷs face
had not remained unperceived by the banker; he fixed on him a look of
greater assurance than before, and said:

ГўВҖВңYou may, perhaps, be better satisfied that I should not go farther
into particulars.ГўВҖВқ

A tremor of suppressed rage shook the whole frame of the count, and
making a violent effort over himself, he said: ГўВҖВңI have a right to
insist on your giving me an explanation. Is it Madame de Morcerf who
has displeased you? Is it my fortune which you find insufficient? Is it
because my opinions differ from yours?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNothing of the kind, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars: ГўВҖВңif such had been the
case, I only should have been to blame, inasmuch as I was aware of all
these things when I made the engagement. No, do not seek any longer to
discover the reason. I really am quite ashamed to have been the cause
of your undergoing such severe self-examination; let us drop the
subject, and adopt the middle course of delay, which implies neither a
rupture nor an engagement. _Ma foi_, there is no hurry. My daughter is
only seventeen years old, and your son twenty-one. While we wait, time
will be progressing, events will succeed each other; things which in
the evening look dark and obscure, appear but too clearly in the light
of morning, and sometimes the utterance of one word, or the lapse of a
single day, will reveal the most cruel calumnies.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCalumnies, did you say, sir?ГўВҖВқ cried Morcerf, turning livid with rage.
ГўВҖВңDoes anyone dare to slander me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMonsieur, I told you that I considered it best to avoid all
explanation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, sir, I am patiently to submit to your refusal?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir, although I assure you the refusal is as painful for me to
give as it is for you to receive, for I had reckoned on the honor of
your alliance, and the breaking off of a marriage contract always
injures the lady more than the gentleman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEnough, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңwe will speak no more on the subject.ГўВҖВқ

And clutching his gloves in anger, he left the apartment. Danglars
observed that during the whole conversation Morcerf had never once
dared to ask if it was on his own account that Danglars recalled his
word.

That evening he had a long conference with several friends; and M.
Cavalcanti, who had remained in the drawing-room with the ladies, was
the last to leave the bankerГўВҖВҷs house.

The next morning, as soon as he awoke, Danglars asked for the
newspapers; they were brought to him; he laid aside three or four, and
at last fixed on _lГўВҖВҷImpartial_, the paper of which Beauchamp was the
chief editor. He hastily tore off the cover, opened the journal with
nervous precipitation, passed contemptuously over the Paris jottings,
and arriving at the miscellaneous intelligence, stopped with a
malicious smile, at a paragraph headed

_We hear from Yanina._


ГўВҖВңVery good,ГўВҖВқ observed Danglars, after having read the paragraph; ГўВҖВңhere
is a little article on Colonel Fernand, which, if I am not mistaken,
would render the explanation which the Comte de Morcerf required of me
perfectly unnecessary.ГўВҖВқ

At the same moment, that is, at nine oГўВҖВҷclock in the morning, Albert de
Morcerf, dressed in a black coat buttoned up to his chin, might have
been seen walking with a quick and agitated step in the direction of
Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs house in the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es. When he presented himself
at the gate the porter informed him that the Count had gone out about
half an hour previously.

ГўВҖВңDid he take Baptistin with him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, my lord.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCall him, then; I wish to speak to him.ГўВҖВқ

The concierge went to seek the valet de chambre, and returned with him
in an instant.

ГўВҖВңMy good friend,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңI beg pardon for my intrusion, but I
was anxious to know from your own mouth if your master was really out
or not.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is really out, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied Baptistin.

ГўВҖВңOut, even to me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know how happy my master always is to receive the vicomte,ГўВҖВқ said
Baptistin; ГўВҖВңand I should therefore never think of including him in any
general order.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right; and now I wish to see him on an affair of great
importance. Do you think it will be long before he comes in?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, I think not, for he ordered his breakfast at ten oГўВҖВҷclock.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I will go and take a turn in the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es, and at ten
oГўВҖВҷclock I will return here; meanwhile, if the count should come in,
will you beg him not to go out again without seeing me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou may depend on my doing so, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Baptistin.

Albert left the cab in which he had come at the countГўВҖВҷs door, intending
to take a turn on foot. As he was passing the AllГғВ©e des Veuves, he
thought he saw the countГўВҖВҷs horses standing at GossetГўВҖВҷs
shooting-gallery; he approached, and soon recognized the coachman.

ГўВҖВңIs the count shooting in the gallery?ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңYes, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the coachman. While he was speaking, Albert had
heard the report of two or three pistol-shots. He entered, and on his
way met the waiter.

ГўВҖВңExcuse me, my lord,ГўВҖВқ said the lad; ГўВҖВңbut will you have the kindness to
wait a moment?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat for, Philip?ГўВҖВқ asked Albert, who, being a constant visitor there,
did not understand this opposition to his entrance.

ГўВҖВңBecause the person who is now in the gallery prefers being alone, and
never practices in the presence of anyone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot even before you, Philip? Then who loads his pistol?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHis servant.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA Nubian?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA negro.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is he, then.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you know this gentleman?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, and I am come to look for him; he is a friend of mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, that is quite another thing, then. I will go immediately and
inform him of your arrival.ГўВҖВқ

And Philip, urged by his own curiosity, entered the gallery; a second
afterwards, Monte Cristo appeared on the threshold.

ГўВҖВңI ask your pardon, my dear count,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңfor following you
here, and I must first tell you that it was not the fault of your
servants that I did so; I alone am to blame for the indiscretion. I
went to your house, and they told me you were out, but that they
expected you home at ten oГўВҖВҷclock to breakfast. I was walking about in
order to pass away the time till ten oГўВҖВҷclock, when I caught sight of
your carriage and horses.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat you have just said induces me to hope that you intend
breakfasting with me.ГўВҖВқ

40092m



ГўВҖВңNo, thank you, I am thinking of other things besides breakfast just
now; perhaps we may take that meal at a later hour and in worse
company.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat on earth are you talking of?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am to fight today.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor what?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor the sake of fighting!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I understand that, but what is the quarrel? People fight for all
sorts of reasons, you know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI fight in the cause of honor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, that is something serious.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo serious, that I come to beg you to render me a service.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo be my second.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is a serious matter, and we will not discuss it here; let us
speak of nothing till we get home. Ali, bring me some water.ГўВҖВқ

The count turned up his sleeves, and passed into the little vestibule
where the gentlemen were accustomed to wash their hands after shooting.

ГўВҖВңCome in, my lord,ГўВҖВқ said Philip in a low tone, ГўВҖВңand I will show you
something droll.ГўВҖВқ Morcerf entered, and in place of the usual target, he
saw some playing-cards fixed against the wall. At a distance Albert
thought it was a complete suit, for he counted from the ace to the ten.

ГўВҖВңAh, ha,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңI see you were preparing for a game of cards.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңI was making a suit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow?ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңThose are really aces and twos which you see, but my shots have turned
them into threes, fives, sevens, eights, nines, and tens.ГўВҖВқ

Albert approached. In fact, the bullets had actually pierced the cards
in the exact places which the painted signs would otherwise have
occupied, the lines and distances being as regularly kept as if they
had been ruled with pencil. In going up to the target Morcerf picked up
two or three swallows that had been rash enough to come within the
range of the countГўВҖВҷs pistol.

ГўВҖВң_Diable!_ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңWhat would you have, my dear viscount?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, wiping his
hands on the towel which Ali had brought him; ГўВҖВңI must occupy my leisure
moments in some way or other. But come, I am waiting for you.ГўВҖВқ

Both men entered Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs carriage, which in the course of a few
minutes deposited them safely at No. 30. Monte Cristo took Albert into
his study, and pointing to a seat, placed another for himself. ГўВҖВңNow let
us talk the matter over quietly,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңYou see I am perfectly composed,ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңWith whom are you going to fight?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith Beauchamp.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne of your friends!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf course; it is always with friends that one fights.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI suppose you have some cause of quarrel?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have.ГўВҖВқ

40094m



ГўВҖВңWhat has he done to you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere appeared in his journal last nightГўВҖВ”but wait, and read for
yourself.ГўВҖВқ And Albert handed over the paper to the count, who read as
follows:

ГўВҖВңA correspondent at Yanina informs us of a fact of which until now we
had remained in ignorance. The castle which formed the protection of
the town was given up to the Turks by a French officer named Fernand,
in whom the grand vizier, Ali Tepelini, had reposed the greatest
confidence.ГўВҖВқ


ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңwhat do you see in that to annoy you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do I see in it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; what does it signify to you if the castle of Yanina was given up
by a French officer?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt signifies to my father, the Count of Morcerf, whose Christian name
is Fernand!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid your father serve under Ali Pasha?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; that is to say, he fought for the independence of the Greeks, and
hence arises the calumny.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, my dear viscount, do talk reason!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not desire to do otherwise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow, just tell me who the devil should know in France that the officer
Fernand and the Count of Morcerf are one and the same person? and who
cares now about Yanina, which was taken as long ago as the year 1822 or
1823?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat just shows the meanness of this slander. They have allowed all
this time to elapse, and then all of a sudden rake up events which have
been forgotten to furnish materials for scandal, in order to tarnish
the lustre of our high position. I inherit my fatherГўВҖВҷs name, and I do
not choose that the shadow of disgrace should darken it. I am going to
Beauchamp, in whose journal this paragraph appears, and I shall insist
on his retracting the assertion before two witnesses.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBeauchamp will never retract.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen we must fight.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo you will not, for he will tell you, what is very true, that perhaps
there were fifty officers in the Greek army bearing the same name.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe will fight, nevertheless. I will efface that blot on my fatherГўВҖВҷs
character. My father, who was such a brave soldier, whose career was so
brilliantГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, well, he will add, ГўВҖВҳWe are warranted in believing that this
Fernand is not the illustrious Count of Morcerf, who also bears the
same Christian name.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am determined not to be content with anything short of an entire
retractation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you intend to make him do it in the presence of two witnesses, do
you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do wrong.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich means, I suppose, that you refuse the service which I asked of
you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou know my theory regarding duels; I told you my opinion on that
subject, if you remember, when we were at Rome.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNevertheless, my dear count, I found you this morning engaged in an
occupation but little consistent with the notions you profess to
entertain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause, my dear fellow, you understand one must never be eccentric.
If oneГўВҖВҷs lot is cast among fools, it is necessary to study folly. I
shall perhaps find myself one day called out by some harebrained scamp,
who has no more real cause of quarrel with me than you have with
Beauchamp; he may take me to task for some foolish trifle or other, he
will bring his witnesses, or will insult me in some public place, and I
am expected to kill him for all that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou admit that you would fight, then? Well, if so, why do you object
to my doing so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not say that you ought not to fight, I only say that a duel is a
serious thing, and ought not to be undertaken without due reflection.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid he reflect before he insulted my father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf he spoke hastily, and owns that he did so, you ought to be
satisfied.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, my dear count, you are far too indulgent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you are far too exacting. Supposing, for instance, and do not be
angry at what I am going to sayГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSupposing the assertion to be really true?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA son ought not to submit to such a stain on his fatherГўВҖВҷs honor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi!_ we live in times when there is much to which we must
submit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is precisely the fault of the age.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd do you undertake to reform it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, as far as I am personally concerned.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, you are indeed exacting, my dear fellow!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I own it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you quite impervious to good advice?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot when it comes from a friend.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd do you account me that title?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly I do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, before going to Beauchamp with your witnesses, seek
further information on the subject.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom HaydГғВ©e.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, what can be the use of mixing a woman up in the affair?ГўВҖВ”what can
she do in it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShe can declare to you, for example, that your father had no hand
whatever in the defeat and death of the vizier; or if by chance he had,
indeed, the misfortune toГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have told you, my dear count, that I would not for one moment admit
of such a proposition.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou reject this means of information, then?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI doГўВҖВ”most decidedly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen let me offer one more word of advice.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo so, then, but let it be the last.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do not wish to hear it, perhaps?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary, I request it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not take any witnesses with you when you go to BeauchampГўВҖВ”visit him
alone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat would be contrary to all custom.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour case is not an ordinary one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what is your reason for advising me to go alone?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause then the affair will rest between you and Beauchamp.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExplain yourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will do so. If Beauchamp be disposed to retract, you ought at least
to give him the opportunity of doing it of his own free will,ГўВҖВ”the
satisfaction to you will be the same. If, on the contrary, he refuses
to do so, it will then be quite time enough to admit two strangers into
your secret.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey will not be strangers, they will be friends.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, but the friends of today are the enemies of tomorrow; Beauchamp,
for instance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo you recommendГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI recommend you to be prudent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you advise me to go alone to Beauchamp?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do, and I will tell you why. When you wish to obtain some concession
from a manГўВҖВҷs self-love, you must avoid even the appearance of wishing
to wound it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI believe you are right.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am glad of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I will go alone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo; but you would do better still by not going at all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is impossible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo so, then; it will be a wiser plan than the first which you
proposed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut if, in spite of all my precautions, I am at last obliged to fight,
will you not be my second?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear viscount,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo gravely, ГўВҖВңyou must have seen
before today that at all times and in all places I have been at your
disposal, but the service which you have just demanded of me is one
which it is out of my power to render you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerhaps you may know at some future period, and in the mean time I
request you to excuse my declining to put you in possession of my
reasons.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I will have Franz and ChГғВўteau-Renaud; they will be the very men
for it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo so, then.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut if I do fight, you will surely not object to giving me a lesson or
two in shooting and fencing?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat, too, is impossible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat a singular being you are!ГўВҖВ”you will not interfere in anything.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are rightГўВҖВ”that is the principle on which I wish to act.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe will say no more about it, then. Good-bye, count.ГўВҖВқ

Morcerf took his hat, and left the room. He found his carriage at the
door, and doing his utmost to restrain his anger he went at once to
find Beauchamp, who was in his office. It was a gloomy, dusty-looking
apartment, such as journalistsГўВҖВҷ offices have always been from time
immemorial. The servant announced M. Albert de Morcerf. Beauchamp
repeated the name to himself, as though he could scarcely believe that
he had heard aright, and then gave orders for him to be admitted.
Albert entered.

Beauchamp uttered an exclamation of surprise on seeing his friend leap
over and trample under foot all the newspapers which were strewed about
the room.

ГўВҖВңThis way, this way, my dear Albert!ГўВҖВқ said he, holding out his hand to
the young man. ГўВҖВңAre you out of your senses, or do you come peaceably to
take breakfast with me? Try and find a seatГўВҖВ”there is one by that
geranium, which is the only thing in the room to remind me that there
are other leaves in the world besides leaves of paper.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBeauchamp,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңit is of your journal that I come to speak.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed? What do you wish to say about it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI desire that a statement contained in it should be rectified.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo what do you refer? But pray sit down.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, with a cold and formal bow.

ГўВҖВңWill you now have the kindness to explain the nature of the statement
which has displeased you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn announcement has been made which implicates the honor of a member
of my family.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, much surprised; ГўВҖВңsurely you must be
mistaken.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe story sent you from Yanina.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYanina?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; really you appear to be totally ignorant of the cause which
brings me here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSuch is really the case, I assure you, upon my honor! Baptiste, give
me yesterdayГўВҖВҷs paper,ГўВҖВқ cried Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңHere, I have brought mine with me,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert.

Beauchamp took the paper, and read the article to which Albert pointed
in an undertone.

ГўВҖВңYou see it is a serious annoyance,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, when Beauchamp had
finished the perusal of the paragraph.

ГўВҖВңIs the officer referred to a relation of yours, then?ГўВҖВқ demanded the
journalist.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, blushing.

ГўВҖВңWell, what do you wish me to do for you?ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp mildly.

ГўВҖВңMy dear Beauchamp, I wish you to contradict this statement.ГўВҖВқ Beauchamp
looked at Albert with a benevolent expression.

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңthis matter will want a good deal of talking over; a
retractation is always a serious thing, you know. Sit down, and I will
read it again.ГўВҖВқ

Albert resumed his seat, and Beauchamp read, with more attention than
at first, the lines denounced by his friend.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Albert in a determined tone, ГўВҖВңyou see that your paper has
insulted a member of my family, and I insist on a retractation being
made.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou insist?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I insist.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPermit me to remind you that you are not in the Chamber, my dear
viscount.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNor do I wish to be there,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man, rising. ГўВҖВңI repeat
that I am determined to have the announcement of yesterday
contradicted. You have known me long enough,ГўВҖВқ continued Albert, biting
his lips convulsively, for he saw that BeauchampГўВҖВҷs anger was beginning
to rise,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңyou have been my friend, and therefore sufficiently intimate
with me to be aware that I am likely to maintain my resolution on this
point.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf I have been your friend, Morcerf, your present manner of speaking
would almost lead me to forget that I ever bore that title. But wait a
moment, do not let us get angry, or at least not yet. You are irritated
and vexedГўВҖВ”tell me how this Fernand is related to you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is merely my father,ГўВҖВқ said AlbertГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңM. Fernand Mondego, Count of
Morcerf, an old soldier who has fought in twenty battles and whose
honorable scars they would denounce as badges of disgrace.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs it your father?ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңthat is quite another thing. Then
I can well understand your indignation, my dear Albert. I will look at
it again;ГўВҖВқ and he read the paragraph for the third time, laying a
stress on each word as he proceeded. ГўВҖВңBut the paper nowhere identifies
this Fernand with your father.ГўВҖВқ

40100m



ГўВҖВңNo; but the connection will be seen by others, and therefore I will
have the article contradicted.ГўВҖВқ

At the words _I will_, Beauchamp steadily raised his eyes to AlbertГўВҖВҷs
countenance, and then as gradually lowering them, he remained
thoughtful for a few moments.

ГўВҖВңYou will retract this assertion, will you not, Beauchamp?ГўВҖВқ said Albert
with increased though stifled anger.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңImmediately?ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңWhen I am convinced that the statement is false.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe thing is worth looking into, and I will take pains to investigate
the matter thoroughly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what is there to investigate, sir?ГўВҖВқ said Albert, enraged beyond
measure at BeauchampГўВҖВҷs last remark. ГўВҖВңIf you do not believe that it is
my father, say so immediately; and if, on the contrary, you believe it
to be him, state your reasons for doing so.ГўВҖВқ

Beauchamp looked at Albert with the smile which was so peculiar to him,
and which in its numerous modifications served to express every varied
emotion of his mind.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ replied he, ГўВҖВңif you came to me with the idea of demanding
satisfaction, you should have gone at once to the point, and not have
entertained me with the idle conversation to which I have been
patiently listening for the last half hour. Am I to put this
construction on your visit?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, if you will not consent to retract that infamous calumny.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWait a momentГўВҖВ”no threats, if you please, M. Fernand Mondego, Vicomte
de Morcerf; I never allow them from my enemies, and therefore shall not
put up with them from my friends. You insist on my contradicting the
article relating to General Fernand, an article with which, I assure
you on my word of honor, I had nothing whatever to do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I insist on it,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, whose mind was beginning to get
bewildered with the excitement of his feelings.

ГўВҖВңAnd if I refuse to retract, you wish to fight, do you?ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp
in a calm tone.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert, raising his voice.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңhere is my answer, my dear sir. The article
was not inserted by meГўВҖВ”I was not even aware of it; but you have, by the
step you have taken, called my attention to the paragraph in question,
and it will remain until it shall be either contradicted or confirmed
by someone who has a right to do so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, rising, ГўВҖВңI will do myself the honor of sending my
seconds to you, and you will be kind enough to arrange with them the
place of meeting and the weapons.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly, my dear sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd this evening, if you please, or tomorrow at the latest, we will
meet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no, I will be on the ground at the proper time; but in my opinion
(and I have a right to dictate the preliminaries, as it is I who have
received the provocation)ГўВҖВ”in my opinion the time ought not to be yet. I
know you to be well skilled in the management of the sword, while I am
only moderately so; I know, too, that you are a good marksmanГўВҖВ”there we
are about equal. I know that a duel between us two would be a serious
affair, because you are brave, and I am brave also. I do not therefore
wish either to kill you, or to be killed myself without a cause. Now, I
am going to put a question to you, and one very much to the purpose
too. Do you insist on this retractation so far as to kill me if I do
not make it, although I have repeated more than once, and affirmed on
my honor, that I was ignorant of the thing with which you charge me,
and although I still declare that it is impossible for anyone but you
to recognize the Count of Morcerf under the name of Fernand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI maintain my original resolution.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well, my dear sir; then I consent to cut throats with you. But I
require three weeksГўВҖВҷ preparation; at the end of that time I shall come
and say to you, ГўВҖВҳThe assertion is false, and I retract it,ГўВҖВҷ or ГўВҖВҳThe
assertion is true,ГўВҖВҷ when I shall immediately draw the sword from its
sheath, or the pistols from the case, whichever you please.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThree weeks!ГўВҖВқ cried Albert; ГўВҖВңthey will pass as slowly as three
centuries when I am all the time suffering dishonor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHad you continued to remain on amicable terms with me, I should have
said, ГўВҖВҳPatience, my friend;ГўВҖВҷ but you have constituted yourself my
enemy, therefore I say, ГўВҖВҳWhat does that signify to me, sir?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, let it be three weeks then,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf; ГўВҖВңbut remember, at the
expiration of that time no delay or subterfuge will justify you inГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. Albert de Morcerf,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, rising in his turn, ГўВҖВңI cannot
throw you out of window for three weeksГўВҖВ”that is to say, for twenty-four
days to comeГўВҖВ”nor have you any right to split my skull open till that
time has elapsed. Today is the 29th of August; the 21st of September
will, therefore, be the conclusion of the term agreed on, and till that
time arrivesГўВҖВ”and it is the advice of a gentleman which I am about to
give youГўВҖВ”till then we will refrain from growling and barking like two
dogs chained within sight of each other.ГўВҖВқ

When he had concluded his speech, Beauchamp bowed coldly to Albert,
turned his back upon him, and went to the press-room. Albert vented his
anger on a pile of newspapers, which he sent flying all over the office
by switching them violently with his stick; after which ebullition he
departedГўВҖВ”not, however, without walking several times to the door of the
press-room, as if he had half a mind to enter.

40104m



While Albert was lashing the front of his carriage in the same manner
that he had the newspapers which were the innocent agents of his
discomfiture, as he was crossing the barrier he perceived Morrel, who
was walking with a quick step and a bright eye. He was passing the
Chinese Baths, and appeared to have come from the direction of the
Porte Saint-Martin, and to be going towards the Madeleine.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, ГўВҖВңthere goes a happy man!ГўВҖВқ And it so happened Albert
was not mistaken in his opinion.



 Chapter 79. The Lemonade

Morrel was, in fact, very happy. M. Noirtier had just sent for him, and
he was in such haste to know the reason of his doing so that he had not
stopped to take a cab, placing infinitely more dependence on his own
two legs than on the four legs of a cab-horse. He had therefore set off
at a furious rate from the Rue Meslay, and was hastening with rapid
strides in the direction of the Faubourg Saint-HonorГғВ©.

Morrel advanced with a firm, manly tread, and poor Barrois followed him
as he best might. Morrel was only thirty-one, Barrois was sixty years
of age; Morrel was deeply in love, and Barrois was dying with heat and
exertion. These two men, thus opposed in age and interests, resembled
two parts of a triangle, presenting the extremes of separation, yet
nevertheless possessing their point of union. This point of union was
Noirtier, and it was he who had just sent for Morrel, with the request
that the latter would lose no time in coming to himГўВҖВ”a command which
Morrel obeyed to the letter, to the great discomfiture of Barrois. On
arriving at the house, Morrel was not even out of breath, for love
lends wings to our desires; but Barrois, who had long forgotten what it
was to love, was sorely fatigued by the expedition he had been
constrained to use.

The old servant introduced Morrel by a private entrance, closed the
door of the study, and soon the rustling of a dress announced the
arrival of Valentine. She looked marvellously beautiful in her deep
mourning dress, and Morrel experienced such intense delight in gazing
upon her that he felt as if he could almost have dispensed with the
conversation of her grandfather.

But the easy-chair of the old man was heard rolling along the floor,
and he soon made his appearance in the room. Noirtier acknowledged by a
look of extreme kindness and benevolence the thanks which Morrel
lavished on him for his timely intervention on behalf of Valentine and
himselfГўВҖВ”an intervention which had saved them from despair. Morrel then
cast on the invalid an interrogative look as to the new favor which he
designed to bestow on him. Valentine was sitting at a little distance
from them, timidly awaiting the moment when she should be obliged to
speak. Noirtier fixed his eyes on her.

ГўВҖВңAm I to say what you told me?ГўВҖВқ asked Valentine. Noirtier made a sign
that she was to do so.

ГўВҖВңMonsieur Morrel,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine to the young man, who was regarding
her with the most intense interest, ГўВҖВңmy grandfather, M. Noirtier, had a
thousand things to say, which he told me three days ago; and now, he
has sent for you, that I may repeat them to you. I will repeat them,
then; and since he has chosen me as his interpreter, I will be faithful
to the trust, and will not alter a word of his intentions.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I am listening with the greatest impatience,ГўВҖВқ replied the young
man; ГўВҖВңspeak, I beg of you.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine cast down her eyes; this was a good omen for Morrel, for he
knew that nothing but happiness could have the power of thus overcoming
Valentine.

ГўВҖВңMy grandfather intends leaving this house,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңand Barrois is
looking out for suitable apartments for him in another.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you, Mademoiselle de Villefort,ГўВҖВ”you, who are necessary to M.
NoirtierГўВҖВҷs happinessГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ interrupted Valentine; ГўВҖВңI shall not leave my grandfather,ГўВҖВ”that is
an understood thing between us. My apartment will be close to his. Now,
M. de Villefort must either give his consent to this plan or his
refusal; in the first case, I shall leave directly, and in the second,
I shall wait till I am of age, which will be in about ten months. Then
I shall be free, I shall have an independent fortune, andГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңAnd what?ГўВҖВқ demanded Morrel.

ГўВҖВңAnd with my grandfatherГўВҖВҷs consent I shall fulfil the promise which I
have made you.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine pronounced these last few words in such a low tone, that
nothing but MorrelГўВҖВҷs intense interest in what she was saying could have
enabled him to hear them.

ГўВҖВңHave I not explained your wishes, grandpapa?ГўВҖВқ said Valentine,
addressing Noirtier.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ looked the old man.

ГўВҖВңOnce under my grandfatherГўВҖВҷs roof, M. Morrel can visit me in the
presence of my good and worthy protector, if we still feel that the
union we contemplated will be likely to insure our future comfort and
happiness; in that case I shall expect M. Morrel to come and claim me
at my own hands. But, alas, I have heard it said that hearts inflamed
by obstacles to their desire grew cold in time of security; I trust we
shall never find it so in our experience!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ cried Morrel, almost tempted to throw himself on his knees before

ГўВҖВңAnd I swear to make all the sacrifices which this word imposes, sir,ГўВҖВқ

Morrel looked obedience to her commands. Noirtier regarded the lovers

Barrois took away the waiter, and hardly was he outside the door, which

ГўВҖВңIt is past noon,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңand today is Saturday; I dare say it is

Noirtier looked his conviction that she was right in her supposition.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ signed the old man.

ГўВҖВңI promised him to wait,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel; ГўВҖВңand I will wait.ГўВҖВқ

The rigors which had attacked Barrois gradually increased, the features

ГўВҖВңMy master, my good master!ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier, burning with impatience and terror, was in despair at his

the veins of his forehead and the contraction of the muscles round the
eye, trace the terrible conflict which was going on between the living
energetic mind and the inanimate and helpless body.

Barrois, his features convulsed, his eyes suffused with blood, and his
head thrown back, was lying at full length, beating the floor with his
hands, while his legs had become so stiff, that they looked as if they
would break rather than bend. A slight appearance of foam was visible
around the mouth, and he breathed painfully, and with extreme
difficulty.

Villefort seemed stupefied with astonishment, and remained gazing
intently on the scene before him without uttering a word. He had not
