glass before his lips.

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ said Villefort.

ГўВҖВңGo to the kitchen and get me some syrup of violets.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort went immediately.

ГўВҖВңDo not be alarmed, M. Noirtier,ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny; ГўВҖВңI am going to take
my patient into the next room to bleed him; this sort of attack is very
frightful to witness.ГўВҖВқ

And taking Barrois under the arms, he dragged him into an adjoining
room; but almost immediately he returned to fetch the lemonade.
Noirtier closed his right eye.

ГўВҖВңYou want Valentine, do you not? I will tell them to send her to you.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort returned, and dГўВҖВҷAvrigny met him in the passage.

ГўВҖВңWell, how is he now?ГўВҖВқ asked he.

ГўВҖВңCome in here,ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, and he took him into the chamber where
the sick man lay.

ГўВҖВңIs he still in a fit?ГўВҖВқ said the procureur.

ГўВҖВңHe is dead.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort drew back a few steps, and, clasping his hands, exclaimed,
with real amazement and sympathy, ГўВҖВңDead?ГўВҖВ”and so soon too!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, it is very soon,ГўВҖВқ said the doctor, looking at the corpse before
him; ГўВҖВңbut that ought not to astonish you; Monsieur and Madame de
Saint-MГғВ©ran died as soon. People die very suddenly in your house, M. de
Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ cried the magistrate, with an accent of horror and
consternation, ГўВҖВңare you still harping on that terrible idea?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill, sir; and I shall always do so,ГўВҖВқ replied dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, ГўВҖВңfor it has
never for one instant ceased to retain possession of my mind; and that
you may be quite sure I am not mistaken this time, listen well to what
I am going to say, M. de Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

The magistrate trembled convulsively.

ГўВҖВңThere is a poison which destroys life almost without leaving any
perceptible traces. I know it well; I have studied it in all its forms
and in the effects which it produces. I recognized the presence of this
poison in the case of poor Barrois as well as in that of Madame de
Saint-MГғВ©ran. There is a way of detecting its presence. It restores the
blue color of litmus-paper reddened by an acid, and it turns syrup of
violets green. We have no litmus-paper, but, see, here they come with
the syrup of violets.ГўВҖВқ

The doctor was right; steps were heard in the passage. M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny
opened the door, and took from the hands of the chambermaid a cup which
contained two or three spoonfuls of the syrup, he then carefully closed
the door.

ГўВҖВңLook,ГўВҖВқ said he to the procureur, whose heart beat so loudly that it
might almost be heard, ГўВҖВңhere is in this cup some syrup of violets, and
this decanter contains the remainder of the lemonade of which M.
Noirtier and Barrois partook. If the lemonade be pure and inoffensive,
the syrup will retain its color; if, on the contrary, the lemonade be
drugged with poison, the syrup will become green. Look closely!ГўВҖВқ

The doctor then slowly poured some drops of the lemonade from the
decanter into the cup, and in an instant a light cloudy sediment began
to form at the bottom of the cup; this sediment first took a blue
shade, then from the color of sapphire it passed to that of opal, and
from opal to emerald. Arrived at this last hue, it changed no more. The
result of the experiment left no doubt whatever on the mind.

ГўВҖВңThe unfortunate Barrois has been poisoned,ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, ГўВҖВңand I
will maintain this assertion before God and man.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort said nothing, but he clasped his hands, opened his haggard
eyes, and, overcome with his emotion, sank into a chair.



 Chapter 80. The Accusation

M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny soon restored the magistrate to consciousness, who had
looked like a second corpse in that chamber of death.

ГўВҖВңOh, death is in my house!ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort.

ГўВҖВңSay, rather, crime!ГўВҖВқ replied the doctor.

ГўВҖВңM. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny,ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort, ГўВҖВңI cannot tell you all I feel at this
moment,ГўВҖВ”terror, grief, madness.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, with an imposing calmness, ГўВҖВңbut I think it is
now time to act. I think it is time to stop this torrent of mortality.
I can no longer bear to be in possession of these secrets without the
hope of seeing the victims and society generally revenged.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort cast a gloomy look around him. ГўВҖВңIn my house,ГўВҖВқ murmured he,
ГўВҖВңin my house!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome, magistrate,ГўВҖВқ said M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, ГўВҖВңshow yourself a man; as an
interpreter of the law, do honor to your profession by sacrificing your
selfish interests to it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou make me shudder, doctor. Do you talk of a sacrifice?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you then suspect anyone?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI suspect no one; death raps at your doorГўВҖВ”it entersГўВҖВ”it goes, not
blindfolded, but circumspectly, from room to room. Well, I follow its
course, I track its passage; I adopt the wisdom of the ancients, and
feel my way, for my friendship for your family and my respect for you
are as a twofold bandage over my eyes; wellГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, speak, speak, doctor; I shall have courage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sir, you have in your establishment, or in your family, perhaps,
one of the frightful monstrosities of which each century produces only
one. Locusta and Agrippina, living at the same time, were an exception,
and proved the determination of Providence to effect the entire ruin of
the Roman empire, sullied by so many crimes. Brunhilda and Fredegund
were the results of the painful struggle of civilization in its
infancy, when man was learning to control mind, were it even by an
emissary from the realms of darkness. All these women had been, or
were, beautiful. The same flower of innocence had flourished, or was
still flourishing, on their brow, that is seen on the brow of the
culprit in your house.ГўВҖВқ

40120m



Villefort shrieked, clasped his hands, and looked at the doctor with a
supplicating air. But the latter went on without pity:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳSeek whom the crime will profit,ГўВҖВҷ says an axiom of jurisprudence.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoctor,ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort, ГўВҖВңalas, doctor, how often has manГўВҖВҷs justice
been deceived by those fatal words. I know not why, but I feel that
this crimeГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou acknowledge, then, the existence of the crime?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I see too plainly that it does exist. But it seems that it is
intended to affect me personally. I fear an attack myself, after all
these disasters.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, man!ГўВҖВқ murmured dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, ГўВҖВңthe most selfish of all animals, the
most personal of all creatures, who believes the earth turns, the sun
shines, and death strikes for him alone,ГўВҖВ”an ant cursing God from the
top of a blade of grass! And have those who have lost their lives lost
nothing?ГўВҖВ”M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran, Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran, M. NoirtierГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow? M. Noirtier?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; think you it was the poor servantГўВҖВҷs life was coveted? No, no;
like ShakespeareГўВҖВҷs Polonius, he died for another. It was Noirtier the
lemonade was intended forГўВҖВ”it is Noirtier, logically speaking, who drank
it. The other drank it only by accident, and, although Barrois is dead,
it was Noirtier whose death was wished for.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut why did it not kill my father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI told you one evening in the garden after Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs
deathГўВҖВ”because his system is accustomed to that very poison, and the
dose was trifling to him, which would be fatal to another; because no
one knows, not even the assassin, that, for the last twelve months, I
have given M. Noirtier brucine for his paralytic affection, while the
assassin is not ignorant, for he has proved that brucine is a violent
poison.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, have pityГўВҖВ”have pity!ГўВҖВқ murmured Villefort, wringing his hands.

ГўВҖВңFollow the culpritГўВҖВҷs steps; he first kills M. de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, doctor!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI would swear to it; what I heard of his symptoms agrees too well with
what I have seen in the other cases.ГўВҖВқ Villefort ceased to contend; he
only groaned. ГўВҖВңHe first kills M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran,ГўВҖВқ repeated the doctor,
ГўВҖВңthen Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran,ГўВҖВ”a double fortune to inherit.ГўВҖВқ Villefort
wiped the perspiration from his forehead. ГўВҖВңListen attentively.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ stammered Villefort, ГўВҖВңI do not lose a single word.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. Noirtier,ГўВҖВқ resumed M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny in the same pitiless tone,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңM.
Noirtier had once made a will against youГўВҖВ”against your familyГўВҖВ”in favor
of the poor, in fact; M. Noirtier is spared, because nothing is
expected from him. But he has no sooner destroyed his first will and
made a second, than, for fear he should make a third, he is struck
down. The will was made the day before yesterday, I believe; you see
there has been no time lost.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, mercy, M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo mercy, sir! The physician has a sacred mission on earth; and to
fulfil it he begins at the source of life, and goes down to the
mysterious darkness of the tomb. When crime has been committed, and
God, doubtless in anger, turns away his face, it is for the physician
to bring the culprit to justice.ГўВҖВқ

40122m



ГўВҖВңHave mercy on my child, sir,ГўВҖВқ murmured Villefort.

ГўВҖВңYou see it is yourself who have first named herГўВҖВ”you, her father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave pity on Valentine! Listen, it is impossible. I would as willingly
accuse myself! Valentine, whose heart is pure as a diamond or a lily!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo pity, procureur; the crime is fragrant. Mademoiselle herself packed
all the medicines which were sent to M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran; and M. de
Saint-MГғВ©ran is dead. Mademoiselle de Villefort prepared all the cooling
draughts which Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran took, and Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ran is
dead. Mademoiselle de Villefort took from the hands of Barrois, who was
sent out, the lemonade which M. Noirtier had every morning, and he has
escaped by a miracle. Mademoiselle de Villefort is the culpritГўВҖВ”she is
the poisoner! To you, as the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney, I denounce Mademoiselle
de Villefort, do your duty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoctor, I resist no longerГўВҖВ”I can no longer defend myselfГўВҖВ”I believe
you; but, for pityГўВҖВҷs sake, spare my life, my honor!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ replied the doctor, with increased vehemence, ГўВҖВңthere
are occasions when I dispense with all foolish human circumspection. If
your daughter had committed only one crime, and I saw her meditating
another, I would say ГўВҖВҳWarn her, punish her, let her pass the remainder
of her life in a convent, weeping and praying.ГўВҖВҷ If she had committed
two crimes, I would say, ГўВҖВҳHere, M. de Villefort, is a poison that the
prisoner is not acquainted with,ГўВҖВ”one that has no known antidote, quick
as thought, rapid as lightning, mortal as the thunderbolt; give her
that poison, recommending her soul to God, and save your honor and your
life, for it is yours she aims at; and I can picture her approaching
your pillow with her hypocritical smiles and her sweet exhortations.
Woe to you, M. de Villefort, if you do not strike first!ГўВҖВҷ This is what
I would say had she only killed two persons but she has seen three
deaths,ГўВҖВ”has contemplated three murdered persons,ГўВҖВ”has knelt by three
corpses! To the scaffold with the poisonerГўВҖВ”to the scaffold! Do you talk
of your honor? Do what I tell you, and immortality awaits you!ГўВҖВқ

Villefort fell on his knees.

ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңI have not the strength of mind you have, or rather
that which you would not have, if instead of my daughter Valentine your
daughter Madeleine were concerned.ГўВҖВқ The doctor turned pale. ГўВҖВңDoctor,
every son of woman is born to suffer and to die; I am content to suffer
and to await death.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBeware,ГўВҖВқ said M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, ГўВҖВңit may come slowly; you will see it
approach after having struck your father, your wife, perhaps your son.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort, suffocating, pressed the doctorГўВҖВҷs arm.

40124m



ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ cried he; ГўВҖВңpity meГўВҖВ”help me! No, my daughter is not guilty. If
you drag us both before a tribunal I will still say, ГўВҖВҳNo, my daughter
is not guilty;ГўВҖВ”there is no crime in my house. I will not acknowledge a
crime in my house; for when crime enters a dwelling, it is like
deathГўВҖВ”it does not come alone.ГўВҖВҷ Listen. What does it signify to you if I
am murdered? Are you my friend? Are you a man? Have you a heart? No,
you are a physician! Well, I tell you I will not drag my daughter
before a tribunal, and give her up to the executioner! The bare idea
would kill meГўВҖВ”would drive me like a madman to dig my heart out with my
finger-nails! And if you were mistaken, doctorГўВҖВ”if it were not my
daughterГўВҖВ”if I should come one day, pale as a spectre, and say to you,
ГўВҖВҳAssassin, you have killed my child!ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”holdГўВҖВ”if that should happen,
although I am a Christian, M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, I should kill myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said the doctor, after a momentГўВҖВҷs silence, ГўВҖВңI will wait.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort looked at him as if he had doubted his words.

ГўВҖВңOnly,ГўВҖВқ continued M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, with a slow and solemn tone, ГўВҖВңif anyone
falls ill in your house, if you feel yourself attacked, do not send for
me, for I will come no more. I will consent to share this dreadful
secret with you, but I will not allow shame and remorse to grow and
increase in my conscience, as crime and misery will in your house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you abandon me, doctor?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, for I can follow you no farther, and I only stop at the foot of
the scaffold. Some further discovery will be made, which will bring
this dreadful tragedy to a close. Adieu.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI entreat you, doctor!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll the horrors that disturb my thoughts make your house odious and
fatal. Adieu, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne wordГўВҖВ”one single word more, doctor! You go, leaving me in all the
horror of my situation, after increasing it by what you have revealed
to me. But what will be reported of the sudden death of the poor old
servant?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny; ГўВҖВңwe will return.ГўВҖВқ

The doctor went out first, followed by M. de Villefort. The terrified
servants were on the stairs and in the passage where the doctor would
pass.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny to Villefort, so loud that all might hear, ГўВҖВңpoor
Barrois has led too sedentary a life of late; accustomed formerly to
ride on horseback, or in the carriage, to the four corners of Europe,
the monotonous walk around that armchair has killed himГўВҖВ”his blood has
thickened. He was stout, had a short, thick neck; he was attacked with
apoplexy, and I was called in too late. By the way,ГўВҖВқ added he in a low
tone, ГўВҖВңtake care to throw away that cup of syrup of violets in the
ashes.ГўВҖВқ

The doctor, without shaking hands with Villefort, without adding a word
to what he had said, went out, amid the tears and lamentations of the
whole household. The same evening all VillefortГўВҖВҷs servants, who had
assembled in the kitchen, and had a long consultation, came to tell
Madame de Villefort that they wished to leave. No entreaty, no
proposition of increased wages, could induce them to remain; to every
argument they replied, ГўВҖВңWe must go, for death is in this house.ГўВҖВқ

They all left, in spite of prayers and entreaties, testifying their
regret at leaving so good a master and mistress, and especially
Mademoiselle Valentine, so good, so kind, and so gentle.

Villefort looked at Valentine as they said this. She was in tears, and,
strange as it was, in spite of the emotions he felt at the sight of
these tears, he looked also at Madame de Villefort, and it appeared to
him as if a slight gloomy smile had passed over her thin lips, like a
meteor seen passing inauspiciously between two clouds in a stormy sky.



 Chapter 81. The Room of the Retired Baker

The evening of the day on which the Count of Morcerf had left DanglarsГўВҖВҷ
house with feelings of shame and anger at the rejection of the
projected alliance, M. Andrea Cavalcanti, with curled hair, moustaches
in perfect order, and white gloves which fitted admirably, had entered
the courtyard of the bankerГўВҖВҷs house in Rue de la ChaussГғВ©e dГўВҖВҷAntin. He
had not been more than ten minutes in the drawing-room before he drew
Danglars aside into the recess of a bow-window, and, after an ingenious
preamble, related to him all his anxieties and cares since his noble
fatherГўВҖВҷs departure. He acknowledged the extreme kindness which had been
shown him by the bankerГўВҖВҷs family, in which he had been received as a
son, and where, besides, his warmest affections had found an object on
which to centre in Mademoiselle Danglars.

Danglars listened with the most profound attention; he had expected
this declaration for the last two or three days, and when at last it
came his eyes glistened as much as they had lowered on listening to
Morcerf. He would not, however, yield immediately to the young manГўВҖВҷs
request, but made a few conscientious objections.

ГўВҖВңAre you not rather young, M. Andrea, to think of marrying?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think not, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied M. Cavalcanti; ГўВҖВңin Italy the nobility
generally marry young. Life is so uncertain, that we ought to secure
happiness while it is within our reach.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңin case your proposals, which do me honor,
are accepted by my wife and daughter, by whom shall the preliminary
arrangements be settled? So important a negotiation should, I think, be
conducted by the respective fathers of the young people.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir, my father is a man of great foresight and prudence. Thinking that
I might wish to settle in France, he left me at his departure, together
with the papers establishing my identity, a letter promising, if he
approved of my choice, 150,000 livres per annum from the day I was
married. So far as I can judge, I suppose this to be a quarter of my
fatherГўВҖВҷs revenue.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңhave always intended giving my daughter 500,000
francs as her dowry; she is, besides, my sole heiress.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll would then be easily arranged if the baroness and her daughter are
willing. We should command an annuity of 175,000 livres. Supposing,
also, I should persuade the marquis to give me my capital, which is not
likely, but still is possible, we would place these two or three
millions in your hands, whose talent might make it realize ten per
cent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI never give more than four per cent, and generally only three and a
half; but to my son-in-law I would give five, and we would share the
profits.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery good, father-in-law,ГўВҖВқ said Cavalcanti, yielding to his low-born
nature, which would escape sometimes through the aristocratic gloss
with which he sought to conceal it. Correcting himself immediately, he
said, ГўВҖВңExcuse me, sir; hope alone makes me almost mad,ГўВҖВ”what will not
reality do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well, at ten oГўВҖВҷclock; you are still at the HГғВҙtel des Princes?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

40132m

He was truly crying, but it would have been difficult to say whether

ГўВҖВңOh, it is no trouble to spend that; and I am like you, I want

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary, I esteem them, but will not have them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, since you have such a good memory.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo leave behind you the diamond you have on your finger. We shall both

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ said Andrea.

ГўВҖВңHow? You put on a livery, you disguise yourself as a servant, and yet

ГўВҖВңYou guess well.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know something of diamonds; I have had some.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is a false diamond,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңYou are joking now,ГўВҖВқ replied Andrea.

ГўВҖВңDo not be angry, we can try it.ГўВҖВқ Caderousse went to the window,

ГўВҖВң_Confiteor!_ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, putting the diamond on his little

ГўВҖВңNo; you are, after all, a good companion; I will not detain you, and

ГўВҖВңNot at least till the day after tomorrow,ГўВҖВқ thought the young man.

ГўВҖВңWell, I hope you will make a handsome wedding-present the day you
marry Mademoiselle Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have already told you it is a fancy you have taken in your head.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat fortune has she?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut I tell youГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA million?ГўВҖВқ

Andrea shrugged his shoulders.

ГўВҖВңLet it be a million,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңyou can never have so much as
I wish you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you,ГўВҖВқ said the young man.

ГўВҖВңOh, I wish it you with all my heart!ГўВҖВқ added Caderousse with his hoarse
laugh. ГўВҖВңStop, let me show you the way.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is not worthwhile.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, it is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause there is a little secret, a precaution I thought it desirable
to take, one of Huret & FichetГўВҖВҷs locks, revised and improved by Gaspard
Caderousse; I will manufacture you a similar one when you are a
capitalist.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea; ГўВҖВңI will let you know a week beforehand.ГўВҖВқ

They parted. Caderousse remained on the landing until he had not only
seen Andrea go down the three stories, but also cross the court. Then
he returned hastily, shut his door carefully, and began to study, like
a clever architect, the plan Andrea had left him.

ГўВҖВңDear Benedetto,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI think he will not be sorry to inherit his
fortune, and he who hastens the day when he can touch his five hundred
thousand will not be his worst friend.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 82. The Burglary

The day following that on which the conversation we have related took
place, the Count of Monte Cristo set out for Auteuil, accompanied by
Ali and several attendants, and also taking with him some horses whose
qualities he was desirous of ascertaining. He was induced to undertake
this journey, of which the day before he had not even thought and which
had not occurred to Andrea either, by the arrival of Bertuccio from
Normandy with intelligence respecting the house and sloop. The house
was ready, and the sloop which had arrived a week before lay at anchor
in a small creek with her crew of six men, who had observed all the
requisite formalities and were ready again to put to sea.

The count praised BertuccioГўВҖВҷs zeal, and ordered him to prepare for a
speedy departure, as his stay in France would not be prolonged more
than a month.

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI may require to go in one night from Paris to
TrГғВ©port; let eight fresh horses be in readiness on the road, which will
enable me to go fifty leagues in ten hours.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour highness had already expressed that wish,ГўВҖВқ said Bertuccio, ГўВҖВңand
the horses are ready. I have bought them, and stationed them myself at
the most desirable posts, that is, in villages, where no one generally
stops.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs well,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI remain here a day or twoГўВҖВ”arrange
accordingly.ГўВҖВқ

As Bertuccio was leaving the room to give the requisite orders,
Baptistin opened the door: he held a letter on a silver waiter.

ГўВҖВңWhat are you doing here?ГўВҖВқ asked the count, seeing him covered with
dust; ГўВҖВңI did not send for you, I think?ГўВҖВқ

Baptistin, without answering, approached the count, and presented the
letter. ГўВҖВңImportant and urgent,ГўВҖВқ said he.

The count opened the letter, and read:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳM. de Monte Cristo is apprised that this night a man will enter his
house in the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es with the intention of carrying off some
papers supposed to be in the secretaire in the dressing-room. The
countГўВҖВҷs well-known courage will render unnecessary the aid of the
police, whose interference might seriously affect him who sends this
advice. The count, by any opening from the bedroom, or by concealing
himself in the dressing-room, would be able to defend his property
himself. Many attendants or apparent precautions would prevent the
villain from the attempt, and M. de Monte Cristo would lose the
opportunity of discovering an enemy whom chance has revealed to him who
now sends this warning to the count,ГўВҖВ”a warning he might not be able to
send another time, if this first attempt should fail and another be
made.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ


The countГўВҖВҷs first idea was that this was an artificeГўВҖВ”a gross deception,
to draw his attention from a minor danger in order to expose him to a
greater. He was on the point of sending the letter to the commissary of
police, notwithstanding the advice of his anonymous friend, or perhaps
because of that advice, when suddenly the idea occurred to him that it
might be some personal enemy, whom he alone should recognize and over
whom, if such were the case, he alone would gain any advantage, as
Fiesco17 had done over the Moor who would have killed him. We know the
countГўВҖВҷs vigorous and daring mind, denying anything to be impossible,
with that energy which marks the great man.

From his past life, from his resolution to shrink from nothing, the
count had acquired an inconceivable relish for the contests in which he
had engaged, sometimes against nature, that is to say, against God, and
sometimes against the world, that is, against the devil.

ГўВҖВңThey do not want my papers,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthey want to kill me;
they are no robbers, but assassins. I will not allow the prefect of
police to interfere with my private affairs. I am rich enough,
forsooth, to distribute his authority on this occasion.ГўВҖВқ

The count recalled Baptistin, who had left the room after delivering
the letter.

ГўВҖВңReturn to Paris,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңassemble the servants who remain there. I
want all my household at Auteuil.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut will no one remain in the house, my lord?ГўВҖВқ asked Baptistin.

ГўВҖВңYes, the porter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy lord will remember that the lodge is at a distance from the house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe house might be stripped without his hearing the least noise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy thieves.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are a fool, M. Baptistin. Thieves might strip the houseГўВҖВ”it would
annoy me less than to be disobeyed.ГўВҖВқ Baptistin bowed.

40150m



ГўВҖВңYou understand me?ГўВҖВқ said the count. ГўВҖВңBring your comrades here, one and
all; but let everything remain as usual, only close the shutters of the
ground floor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd those of the first floor?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou know they are never closed. Go!ГўВҖВқ

The count signified his intention of dining alone, and that no one but
Ali should attend him. Having dined with his usual tranquillity and
moderation, the count, making a signal to Ali to follow him, went out
by the side-gate and on reaching the Bois de Boulogne turned,
apparently without design towards Paris and at twilight; found himself
opposite his house in the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es. All was dark; one solitary,
feeble light was burning in the porterГўВҖВҷs lodge, about forty paces
distant from the house, as Baptistin had said.

Monte Cristo leaned against a tree, and with that scrutinizing glance
which was so rarely deceived, looked up and down the avenue, examined
the passers-by, and carefully looked down the neighboring streets, to
see that no one was concealed. Ten minutes passed thus, and he was
convinced that no one was watching him. He hastened to the side-door
with Ali, entered hurriedly, and by the servantsГўВҖВҷ staircase, of which
he had the key, gained his bedroom without opening or disarranging a
single curtain, without even the porter having the slightest suspicion
that the house, which he supposed empty, contained its chief occupant.

Arrived in his bedroom, the count motioned to Ali to stop; then he
passed into the dressing-room, which he examined. Everything appeared
as usualГўВҖВ”the precious secretaire in its place, and the key in the
secretaire. He double locked it, took the key, returned to the bedroom
door, removed the double staple of the bolt, and went in. Meanwhile Ali
had procured the arms the count requiredГўВҖВ”namely, a short carbine and a
pair of double-barrelled pistols, with which as sure an aim might be
taken as with a single-barrelled one. Thus armed, the count held the
lives of five men in his hands. It was about half-past nine.

The count and Ali ate in haste a crust of bread and drank a glass of
Spanish wine; then Monte Cristo slipped aside one of the movable
panels, which enabled him to see into the adjoining room. He had within
his reach his pistols and carbine, and Ali, standing near him, held one
of the small Arabian hatchets, whose form has not varied since the
Crusades. Through one of the windows of the bedroom, on a line with
that in the dressing-room, the count could see into the street.

Two hours passed thus. It was intensely dark; still Ali, thanks to his
wild nature, and the count, thanks doubtless to his long confinement,
could distinguish in the darkness the slightest movement of the trees.
The little light in the lodge had long been extinct. It might be
expected that the attack, if indeed an attack was projected, would be
made from the staircase of the ground floor, and not from a window; in
Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs opinion, the villains sought his life, not his money. It
would be his bedroom they would attack, and they must reach it by the
back staircase, or by the window in the dressing-room.

The clock of the Invalides struck a quarter to twelve; the west wind
bore on its moistened gusts the doleful vibration of the three strokes.

40152m



As the last stroke died away, the count thought he heard a slight noise
in the dressing-room; this first sound, or rather this first grinding,
was followed by a second, then a third; at the fourth, the count knew
what to expect. A firm and well-practised hand was engaged in cutting
the four sides of a pane of glass with a diamond. The count felt his
heart beat more rapidly.

Inured as men may be to danger, forewarned as they may be of peril,
they understand, by the fluttering of the heart and the shuddering of
the frame, the enormous difference between a dream and a reality,
between the project and the execution. However, Monte Cristo only made
a sign to apprise Ali, who, understanding that danger was approaching
from the other side, drew nearer to his master. Monte Cristo was eager
to ascertain the strength and number of his enemies.

The window whence the noise proceeded was opposite the opening by which
the count could see into the dressing-room. He fixed his eyes on that
windowГўВҖВ”he distinguished a shadow in the darkness; then one of the panes
became quite opaque, as if a sheet of paper were stuck on the outside,
then the square cracked without falling. Through the opening an arm was
passed to find the fastening, then a second; the window turned on its
hinges, and a man entered. He was alone.

ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs a daring rascal,ГўВҖВқ whispered the count.

At that moment Ali touched him slightly on the shoulder. He turned; Ali
pointed to the window of the room in which they were, facing the
street.

ГўВҖВңI see!ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңthere are two of them; one does the work while the
other stands guard.ГўВҖВқ He made a sign to Ali not to lose sight of the man
in the street, and turned to the one in the dressing-room.

The glass-cutter had entered, and was feeling his way, his arms
stretched out before him. At last he appeared to have made himself
familiar with his surroundings. There were two doors; he bolted them
both.

When he drew near to the bedroom door, Monte Cristo expected that he
was coming in, and raised one of his pistols; but he simply heard the
sound of the bolts sliding in their copper rings. It was only a
precaution. The nocturnal visitor, ignorant of the fact that the count
had removed the staples, might now think himself at home, and pursue
his purpose with full security. Alone and free to act as he wished, the
man then drew from his pocket something which the count could not
discern, placed it on a stand, then went straight to the secretaire,
felt the lock, and contrary to his expectation found that the key was
missing. But the glass-cutter was a prudent man who had provided for
all emergencies. The count soon heard the rattling of a bunch of
skeleton keys, such as the locksmith brings when called to force a
lock, and which thieves call nightingales, doubtless from the music of
their nightly song when they grind against the bolt.

ГўВҖВңAh, ha,ГўВҖВқ whispered Monte Cristo with a smile of disappointment, ГўВҖВңhe is
only a thief.ГўВҖВқ

But the man in the dark could not find the right key. He reached the
instrument he had placed on the stand, touched a spring, and
immediately a pale light, just bright enough to render objects
distinct, was reflected on his hands and countenance.

ГўВҖВңBy heavens,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Monte Cristo, starting back, ГўВҖВңit isГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Ali raised his hatchet.

ГўВҖВңDonГўВҖВҷt stir,ГўВҖВқ whispered Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңand put down your hatchet; we
shall require no arms.ГўВҖВқ

40154m



Then he added some words in a low tone, for the exclamation which
surprise had drawn from the count, faint as it had been, had startled
the man who remained in the pose of the old knife-grinder.

It was an order the count had just given, for immediately Ali went
noiselessly, and returned, bearing a black dress and a three-cornered
hat. Meanwhile Monte Cristo had rapidly taken off his greatcoat,
waistcoat, and shirt, and one might distinguish by the glimmering
through the open panel that he wore a pliant tunic of steel mail, of
which the last in France, where daggers are no longer dreaded, was worn
by King Louis XVI., who feared the dagger at his breast, and whose head
was cleft with a hatchet. The tunic soon disappeared under a long
cassock, as did his hair under a priestГўВҖВҷs wig; the three-cornered hat
over this effectually transformed the count into an abbГғВ©.

The man, hearing nothing more, stood erect, and while Monte Cristo was
completing his disguise had advanced straight to the secretaire, whose
lock was beginning to crack under his nightingale.

ГўВҖВңTry again,ГўВҖВқ whispered the count, who depended on the secret spring,
which was unknown to the picklock, clever as he might beГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңtry again,
you have a few minutesГўВҖВҷ work there.ГўВҖВқ

And he advanced to the window. The man whom he had seen seated on a
fence had got down, and was still pacing the street; but, strange as it
appeared, he cared not for those who might pass from the avenue of the
Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es or by the Faubourg Saint-HonorГғВ©; his attention was
engrossed with what was passing at the countГўВҖВҷs, and his only aim
appeared to be to discern every movement in the dressing-room.

Monte Cristo suddenly struck his finger on his forehead and a smile
passed over his lips; then drawing near to Ali, he whispered:

ГўВҖВңRemain here, concealed in the dark, and whatever noise you hear,
whatever passes, only come in or show yourself if I call you.ГўВҖВқ

Ali bowed in token of strict obedience. Monte Cristo then drew a
lighted taper from a closet, and when the thief was deeply engaged with
his lock, silently opened the door, taking care that the light should
shine directly on his face. The door opened so quietly that the thief
heard no sound; but, to his astonishment, the room was suddenly
illuminated. He turned.

ГўВҖВңAh, good-evening, my dear M. Caderousse,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңwhat are
you doing here, at such an hour?ГўВҖВқ

40156m



ГўВҖВңThe AbbГғВ© Busoni!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Caderousse; and, not knowing how this
strange apparition could have entered when he had bolted the doors, he
let fall his bunch of keys, and remained motionless and stupefied. The
count placed himself between Caderousse and the window, thus cutting
off from the thief his only chance of retreat.

ГўВҖВңThe AbbГғВ© Busoni!ГўВҖВқ repeated Caderousse, fixing his haggard gaze on the
count.

ГўВҖВңYes, undoubtedly, the AbbГғВ© Busoni himself,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңAnd
I am very glad you recognize me, dear M. Caderousse; it proves you have
a good memory, for it must be about ten years since we last met.ГўВҖВқ

This calmness of Busoni, combined with his irony and boldness,
staggered Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңThe abbГғВ©, the abbГғВ©!ГўВҖВқ murmured he, clenching his fists, and his teeth
chattering.

ГўВҖВңSo you would rob the Count of Monte Cristo?ГўВҖВқ continued the false abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңReverend sir,ГўВҖВқ murmured Caderousse, seeking to regain the window,
which the count pitilessly blockedГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңreverend sir, I donГўВҖВҷt knowГўВҖВ”believe
meГўВҖВ”I take my oathГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA pane of glass out,ГўВҖВқ continued the count, ГўВҖВңa dark lantern, a bunch of
false keys, a secretaire half forcedГўВҖВ”it is tolerably evidentГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse was choking; he looked around for some corner to hide in,
some way of escape.

ГўВҖВңCome, come,ГўВҖВқ continued the count, ГўВҖВңI see you are still the same,ГўВҖВ”an
assassin.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReverend sir, since you know everything, you know it was not IГўВҖВ”it was
La Carconte; that was proved at the trial, since I was only condemned
to the galleys.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs your time, then, expired, since I find you in a fair way to return
there?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, reverend sir; I have been liberated by someone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat someone has done society a great kindness.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңI had promisedГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you are breaking your promise!ГўВҖВқ interrupted Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңAlas, yes!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse very uneasily.

ГўВҖВңA bad relapse, that will lead you, if I mistake not, to the Place de
GrГғВЁve. So much the worse, so much the worseГўВҖВ”_diavolo!_ as they say in
my country.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReverend sir, I am impelledГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEvery criminal says the same thing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPovertyГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPshaw!ГўВҖВқ said Busoni disdainfully; ГўВҖВңpoverty may make a man beg, steal a
loaf of bread at a bakerГўВҖВҷs door, but not cause him to open a secretaire
in a house supposed to be inhabited. And when the jeweller Johannes had
just paid you 45,000 francs for the diamond I had given you, and you
killed him to get the diamond and the money both, was that also
poverty?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPardon, reverend sir,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңyou have saved my life once,
save me again!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is but poor encouragement.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you alone, reverend sir, or have you there soldiers ready to seize
me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am alone,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңand I will again have pity on you, and
will let you escape, at the risk of the fresh miseries my weakness may
lead to, if you tell me the truth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, reverend sir,ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse, clasping his hands, and drawing
nearer to Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI may indeed say you are my deliverer!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou mean to say you have been freed from confinement?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, that is true, reverend sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho was your liberator?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn Englishman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat was his name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLord Wilmore.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know him; I shall know if you lie.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, reverend sir, I tell you the simple truth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas this Englishman protecting you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, not me, but a young Corsican, my companion.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat was this young CorsicanГўВҖВҷs name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBenedetto.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs that his Christian name?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe had no other; he was a foundling.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen this young man escaped with you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe did.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn what way?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe were working at Saint-Mandrier, near Toulon. Do you know
Saint-Mandrier?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn the hour of rest, between noon and one oГўВҖВҷclockГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGalley-slaves having a nap after dinner! We may well pity the poor
fellows!ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңNay,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңone canГўВҖВҷt always workГўВҖВ”one is not a dog.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo much the better for the dogs,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңWhile the rest slept, then, we went away a short distance; we severed

ГўВҖВңAnd what is become of this Benedetto?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou ought to know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, in truth; we parted at HyГғВЁres.ГўВҖВқ And, to give more weight to his

ГўВҖВңYou lie,ГўВҖВқ said the AbbГғВ© Busoni, with a tone of irresistible authority.

ГўВҖВңReverend sir!ГўВҖВқ

40160m



ГўВҖВңOh, reverend sir!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSince you left Toulon what have you lived on? Answer me!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn what I could get.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou lie,ГўВҖВқ repeated the abbГғВ© a third time, with a still more imperative

ГўВҖВңHow can he be the son of a great lord?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA natural son.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAndrea Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy should I stand in a comradeГўВҖВҷs way?ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңYou are right; it is not you who should apprise M. Danglars, it is I.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause you would bring us to ruin.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you think that to save such villains as you I will become an

ГўВҖВңReverend sir,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, drawing still nearer.

ГўВҖВңTo whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy Heaven!ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse, drawing from his waistcoat an open

To CaderousseГўВҖВҷs great astonishment, the knife, instead of piercing the

The count then placed his foot on his head, saying, ГўВҖВңI know not what

ГўВҖВңAh, mercyГўВҖВ”mercy!ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse.

The count withdrew his foot.

40162m



ГўВҖВңRise!ГўВҖВқ said he. Caderousse rose.

ГўВҖВңWhat a wrist you have, reverend sir!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, stroking his

ГўВҖВңOh!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, groaning with pain.

ГўВҖВңTake this pen and paper, and write what I dictate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt know how to write, reverend sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou lie! Take this pen, and write!ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse, awed by the superior power of the abbГғВ©, sat down and wrote:

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВ”The man whom you are receiving at your house, and to whom you

ГўВҖВңSign it!ГўВҖВқ continued the count.

ГўВҖВңBut would you ruin me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf I sought your ruin, fool, I should drag you to the first

you will have no more to fear. Sign it, then!ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse signed it.

ГўВҖВңThe address, ГўВҖВҳTo monsieur the Baron Danglars, banker, Rue de la
ChaussГғВ©e dГўВҖВҷAntin.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

Caderousse wrote the address. The abbГғВ© took the note.

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңthat sufficesГўВҖВ”begone!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich way?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe way you came.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou wish me to get out at that window?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou got in very well.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, you have some design against me, reverend sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIdiot! what design can I have?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, then, not let me out by the door?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat would be the advantage of waking the porter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, reverend sir, tell me, do you wish me dead?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI wish what God wills.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut swear that you will not strike me as I go down.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCowardly fool!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you intend doing with me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI ask you what can I do? I have tried to make you a happy man, and you

ГўВҖВңOh, monsieur,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңmake one more attemptГўВҖВ”try me once

ГўВҖВңI will,ГўВҖВқ said the count. ГўВҖВңListenГўВҖВ”you know if I may be relied on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңIf you arrive safely at homeГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf you reach your home safely, leave Paris, leave France, and wherever

ГўВҖВңThen?ГўВҖВқ asked Caderousse, shuddering.

40164m



ГўВҖВңThen I shall believe God has forgiven you, and I will forgive you
too.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs true as I am a Christian,ГўВҖВқ stammered Caderousse, ГўВҖВңyou will make me
die of fright!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNow begone,ГўВҖВқ said the count, pointing to the window.

Caderousse, scarcely yet relying on this promise, put his legs out of
the window and stood on the ladder.

ГўВҖВңNow go down,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, folding his arms. Understanding he had
nothing more to fear from him, Caderousse began to go down. Then the
count brought the taper to the window, that it might be seen in the
Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es that a man was getting out of the window while another
held a light.

ГўВҖВңWhat are you doing, reverend sir? Suppose a watchman should pass?ГўВҖВқ And
he blew out the light. He then descended, but it was only when he felt
his foot touch the ground that he was satisfied of his safety.

Monte Cristo returned to his bedroom, and, glancing rapidly from the
garden to the street, he saw first Caderousse, who after walking to the
end of the garden, fixed his ladder against the wall at a different
part from where he came in. The count then looking over into the
street, saw the man who appeared to be waiting run in the same
direction, and place himself against the angle of the wall where
Caderousse would come over. Caderousse climbed the ladder slowly, and
looked over the coping to see if the street was quiet. No one could be
seen or heard. The clock of the Invalides struck one. Then Caderousse
sat astride the coping, and drawing up his ladder passed it over the
wall; then he began to descend, or rather to slide down by the two
stanchions, which he did with an ease which proved how accustomed he
was to the exercise. But, once started, he could not stop. In vain did
he see a man start from the shadow when he was halfway downГўВҖВ”in vain did
he see an arm raised as he touched the ground.

Before he could defend himself that arm struck him so violently in the
back that he let go the ladder, crying, ГўВҖВңHelp!ГўВҖВқ A second blow struck
him almost immediately in the side, and he fell, calling, ГўВҖВңHelp,
murder!ГўВҖВқ Then, as he rolled on the ground, his adversary seized him by
the hair, and struck him a third blow in the chest.

This time Caderousse endeavored to call again, but he could only utter
a groan, and he shuddered as the blood flowed from his three wounds.
The assassin, finding that he no longer cried out, lifted his head up
by the hair; his eyes were closed, and the mouth was distorted. The
murderer, supposing him dead, let fall his head and disappeared.

Then Caderousse, feeling that he was leaving him, raised himself on his
elbow, and with a dying voice cried with great effort:

ГўВҖВңMurder! I am dying! Help, reverend sir,ГўВҖВ”help!ГўВҖВқ

This mournful appeal pierced the darkness. The door of the
back-staircase opened, then the side-gate of the garden, and Ali and
his master were on the spot with lights.



 Chapter 83. The Hand of God

Caderousse continued to call piteously, ГўВҖВңHelp, reverend sir, help!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is the matter?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңHelp,ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse; ГўВҖВңI am murdered!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe are here;ГўВҖВ”take courage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, itГўВҖВҷs all over! You are come too lateГўВҖВ”you are come to see me die.
What blows, what blood!ГўВҖВқ

He fainted. Ali and his master conveyed the wounded man into a room.
Monte Cristo motioned to Ali to undress him, and he then examined his
dreadful wounds.

ГўВҖВңMy God!ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed, ГўВҖВңthy vengeance is sometimes delayed, but only
that it may fall the more effectually.ГўВҖВқ Ali looked at his master for
further instructions. ГўВҖВңBring here immediately the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney, M.
de Villefort, who lives in the Faubourg Saint-HonorГғВ©. As you pass the
lodge, wake the porter, and send him for a surgeon.ГўВҖВқ

Ali obeyed, leaving the abbГғВ© alone with Caderousse, who had not yet
revived.

When the wretched man again opened his eyes, the count looked at him
with a mournful expression of pity, and his lips moved as if in prayer.
ГўВҖВңA surgeon, reverend sirГўВҖВ”a surgeon!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңI have sent for one,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңI know he cannot save my life, but he may strengthen me to give my

ГўВҖВңAgainst whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAgainst my murderer.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you recognize him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; it was Benedetto.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe young Corsican?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHimself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour comrade?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes. After giving me the plan of this house, doubtless hoping I should
kill the count and he thus become his heir, or that the count would
kill me and I should be out of his way, he waylaid me, and has murdered

ГўВҖВңI have also sent for the procureur.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe will not come in time; I feel my life fast ebbing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWait a moment,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. He left the room, and returned in
five minutes with a phial. The dying manГўВҖВҷs eyes were all the time
riveted on the door, through which he hoped succor would arrive.

ГўВҖВңHasten, reverend sir, hasten! I shall faint again!ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo
approached, and dropped on his purple lips three or four drops of the
contents of the phial. Caderousse drew a deep breath. ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said he,
ГўВҖВңthat is life to me; more, more!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTwo drops more would kill you,ГўВҖВқ replied the abbГғВ©.

ГўВҖВңOh, send for someone to whom I can denounce the wretch!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall I write your deposition? You can sign it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; and his eyes glistened at the thought of
this posthumous revenge. Monte Cristo wrote:

ГўВҖВңI die, murdered by the Corsican Benedetto, my comrade in the galleys
at Toulon, No. 59.ГўВҖВқ


ГўВҖВңQuick, quick!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңor I shall be unable to sign it.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo gave the pen to Caderousse, who collected all his
strength, signed it, and fell back on his bed, saying:

ГўВҖВңYou will relate all the rest, reverend sir; you will say he calls
himself Andrea Cavalcanti. He lodges at the HГғВҙtel des Princes. Oh, I am

phial, and he again opened his eyes. His desire for revenge had not

ГўВҖВңAh, you will tell all I have said, will you not, reverend sir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, and much more.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat more will you say?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will say he had doubtless given you the plan of this house, in the
hope the count would kill you. I will say, likewise, he had apprised
the count, by a note, of your intention, and, the count being absent, I
read the note and sat up to await you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd he will be guillotined, will he not?ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse. ГўВҖВңPromise me
that, and I will die with that hope.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will say,ГўВҖВқ continued the count, ГўВҖВңthat he followed and watched you
the whole time, and when he saw you leave the house, ran to the angle
of the wall to conceal himself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you see all that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRemember my words: ГўВҖВҳIf you return home safely, I shall believe God has
forgiven you, and I will forgive you also.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you did not warn me!ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse, raising himself on his
elbows. ГўВҖВңYou knew I should be killed on leaving this house, and did not
warn me!ГўВҖВқ

40168m



ГўВҖВңNo; for I saw GodГўВҖВҷs justice placed in the hands of Benedetto, and
should have thought it sacrilege to oppose the designs of Providence.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGodГўВҖВҷs justice! Speak not of it, reverend sir. If God were just, you
know how many would be punished who now escape.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPatience,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, in a tone which made the dying man shudder;
ГўВҖВңhave patience!ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse looked at him with amazement.

ГўВҖВңBesides,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, ГўВҖВңGod is merciful to all, as he has been to
you; he is first a father, then a judge.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you then believe in God?ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse.

ГўВҖВңHad I been so unhappy as not to believe in him until now,ГўВҖВқ said Monte
Cristo, ГўВҖВңI must believe on seeing you.ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse raised his clenched hands towards heaven.

ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©, extending his hand over the wounded man, as if
to command him to believe; ГўВҖВңthis is what the God in whom, on your
death-bed, you refuse to believe, has done for youГўВҖВ”he gave you health,
strength, regular employment, even friendsГўВҖВ”a life, in fact, which a man
might enjoy with a calm conscience. Instead of improving these gifts,
rarely granted so abundantly, this has been your courseГўВҖВ”you have given
yourself up to sloth and drunkenness, and in a fit of intoxication have
ruined your best friend.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHelp!ГўВҖВқ cried Caderousse; ГўВҖВңI require a surgeon, not a priest; perhaps I
am not mortally woundedГўВҖВ”I may not die; perhaps they can yet save my
life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour wounds are so far mortal that, without the three drops I gave
you, you would now be dead. Listen, then.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ murmured Caderousse, ГўВҖВңwhat a strange priest you are; you drive
the dying to despair, instead of consoling them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ continued the abbГғВ©. ГўВҖВңWhen you had betrayed your friend, God
began not to strike, but to warn you. Poverty overtook you. You had
already passed half your life in coveting that which you might have
honorably acquired; and already you contemplated crime under the excuse
of want, when God worked a miracle in your behalf, sending you, by my
hands, a fortuneГўВҖВ”brilliant, indeed, for you, who had never possessed
any. But this unexpected, unhoped-for, unheard-of fortune sufficed you
no longer when you once possessed it; you wished to double it, and
how?ГўВҖВ”by a murder! You succeeded, and then God snatched it from you, and
brought you to justice.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was not I who wished to kill the Jew,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse; ГўВҖВңit was La
Carconte.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңand God,ГўВҖВ”I cannot say in justice, for his
justice would have slain you,ГўВҖВ”but God, in his mercy, spared your life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Pardieu!_ to transport me for life, how merciful!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou thought it a mercy then, miserable wretch! The coward who feared
death rejoiced at perpetual disgrace; for like all galley-slaves, you
said, ГўВҖВҳI may escape from prison, I cannot from the grave.ГўВҖВҷ And you said
truly; the way was opened for you unexpectedly. An Englishman visited
Toulon, who had vowed to rescue two men from infamy, and his choice
fell on you and your companion. You received a second fortune, money
and tranquillity were restored to you, and you, who had been condemned
to a felonГўВҖВҷs life, might live as other men. Then, wretched creature,
then you tempted God a third time. ГўВҖВҳI have not enough,ГўВҖВҷ you said, when
you had more than you before possessed, and you committed a third
crime, without reason, without excuse. God is wearied; he has punished
you.ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse was fast sinking. ГўВҖВңGive me drink,ГўВҖВқ said he: ГўВҖВңI thirstГўВҖВ”I
burn!ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo gave him a glass of water. ГўВҖВңAnd yet that villain,
Benedetto, will escape!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo one, I tell you, will escape; Benedetto will be punished.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, you, too, will be punished, for you did not do your duty as a
priestГўВҖВ”you should have prevented Benedetto from killing me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ said the count, with a smile which petrified the dying man, ГўВҖВңwhen
you had just broken your knife against the coat of mail which protected
my breast! Yet perhaps if I had found you humble and penitent, I might
have prevented Benedetto from killing you; but I found you proud and
blood-thirsty, and I left you in the hands of God.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not believe there is a God,ГўВҖВқ howled Caderousse; ГўВҖВңyou do not
believe it; you lieГўВҖВ”you lie!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSilence,ГўВҖВқ said the abbГғВ©; ГўВҖВңyou will force the last drop of blood from
your veins. What! you do not believe in God when he is striking you
dead? you will not believe in him, who requires but a prayer, a word, a
tear, and he will forgive? God, who might have directed the assassinГўВҖВҷs
dagger so as to end your career in a moment, has given you this quarter
of an hour for repentance. Reflect, then, wretched man, and repent.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, ГўВҖВңno; I will not repent. There is no God; there
is no ProvidenceГўВҖВ”all comes by chance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere is a Providence; there is a God,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңof whom
you are a striking proof, as you lie in utter despair, denying him,
while I stand before you, rich, happy, safe and entreating that God in
whom you endeavor not to believe, while in your heart you still believe
in him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut who are you, then?ГўВҖВқ asked Caderousse, fixing his dying eyes on the
count.

ГўВҖВңLook well at me!ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, putting the light near his face.

ГўВҖВңWell, the abbГғВ©ГўВҖВ”the AbbГғВ© Busoni.ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo took off the wig which
disfigured him, and let fall his black hair, which added so much to the
beauty of his pallid features.

ГўВҖВңOh?ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, thunderstruck, ГўВҖВңbut for that black hair, I
should say you were the Englishman, Lord Wilmore.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am neither the AbbГғВ© Busoni nor Lord Wilmore,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo;
ГўВҖВңthink again,ГўВҖВ”do you not recollect me?ГўВҖВқ

There was a magic effect in the countГўВҖВҷs words, which once more revived
the exhausted powers of the miserable man.

ГўВҖВңYes, indeed,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңI think I have seen you and known you
formerly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, Caderousse, you have seen me; you knew me once.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho, then, are you? and why, if you knew me, do you let me die?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause nothing can save you; your wounds are mortal. Had it been
possible to save you, I should have considered it another proof of
GodГўВҖВҷs mercy, and I would again have endeavored to restore you, I swear
by my fatherГўВҖВҷs tomb.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy your fatherГўВҖВҷs tomb!ГўВҖВқ said Caderousse, supported by a supernatural
power, and half-raising himself to see more distinctly the man who had
just taken the oath which all men hold sacred; ГўВҖВңwho, then, are you?ГўВҖВқ

The count had watched the approach of death. He knew this was the last
struggle. He approached the dying man, and, leaning over him with a
calm and melancholy look, he whispered, ГўВҖВңI amГўВҖВ”I amГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

And his almost closed lips uttered a name so low that the count himself
appeared afraid to hear it. Caderousse, who had raised himself on his
knees, and stretched out his arm, tried to draw back, then clasping his
hands, and raising them with a desperate effort, ГўВҖВңOh, my God, my God!ГўВҖВқ
said he, ГўВҖВңpardon me for having denied thee; thou dost exist, thou art
indeed manГўВҖВҷs father in heaven, and his judge on earth. My God, my Lord,
I have long despised thee! Pardon me, my God; receive me, Oh, my Lord!ГўВҖВқ

Caderousse sighed deeply, and fell back with a groan. The blood no
longer flowed from his wounds. He was dead.

ГўВҖВң_One!_ГўВҖВқ said the count mysteriously, his eyes fixed on the corpse,
disfigured by so awful a death.

Ten minutes afterwards the surgeon and the procureur arrived, the one
accompanied by the porter, the other by Ali, and were received by the
AbbГғВ© Busoni, who was praying by the side of the corpse.



 Chapter 84. Beauchamp

The daring attempt to rob the count was the topic of conversation
throughout Paris for the next fortnight. The dying man had signed a
deposition declaring Benedetto to be the assassin. The police had
orders to make the strictest search for the murderer. CaderousseГўВҖВҷs
knife, dark lantern, bunch of keys, and clothing, excepting the
waistcoat, which could not be found, were deposited at the registry;
the corpse was conveyed to the morgue. The count told everyone that
this adventure had happened during his absence at Auteuil, and that he
only knew what was related by the AbbГғВ© Busoni, who that evening, by
mere chance, had requested to pass the night in his house, to examine
some valuable books in his library.

Bertuccio alone turned pale whenever BenedettoГўВҖВҷs name was mentioned in
his presence, but there was no reason why anyone should notice his
doing so.

Villefort, being called on to prove the crime, was preparing his brief
with the same ardor that he was accustomed to exercise when required to
speak in criminal cases.

But three weeks had already passed, and the most diligent search had
been unsuccessful; the attempted robbery and the murder of the robber
by his comrade were almost forgotten in anticipation of the approaching
marriage of Mademoiselle Danglars to the Count Andrea Cavalcanti. It
was expected that this wedding would shortly take place, as the young
man was received at the bankerГўВҖВҷs as the betrothed.

Letters had been despatched to M. Cavalcanti, as the countГўВҖВҷs father,
who highly approved of the union, regretted his inability to leave
Parma at that time, and promised a wedding gift of a hundred and fifty
thousand livres. It was agreed that the three millions should be
intrusted to Danglars to invest; some persons had warned the young man
of the circumstances of his future father-in-law, who had of late
sustained repeated losses; but with sublime disinterestedness and
confidence the young man refused to listen, or to express a single
doubt to the baron.

The baron adored Count Andrea Cavalcanti; not so Mademoiselle EugГғВ©nie
Danglars. With an instinctive hatred of matrimony, she suffered
AndreaГўВҖВҷs attentions in order to get rid of Morcerf; but when Andrea
urged his suit, she betrayed an entire dislike to him. The baron might
possibly have perceived it, but, attributing it to a caprice, feigned
ignorance.

The delay demanded by Beauchamp had nearly expired. Morcerf appreciated
the advice of Monte Cristo to let things die away of their own accord.
No one had taken up the remark about the general, and no one had
recognized in the officer who betrayed the castle of Yanina the noble
count in the House of Peers.

Albert, however, felt no less insulted; the few lines which had
irritated him were certainly intended as an insult. Besides, the manner
in which Beauchamp had closed the conference left a bitter recollection
in his heart. He cherished the thought of the duel, hoping to conceal
its true cause even from his seconds. Beauchamp had not been seen since
the day he visited Albert, and those of whom the latter inquired always
told him he was out on a journey which would detain him some days.
Where he was no one knew.

One morning Albert was awakened by his valet de chambre, who announced
Beauchamp. Albert rubbed his eyes, ordered his servant to introduce him
into the small smoking-room on the ground floor, dressed himself
quickly, and went down.

He found Beauchamp pacing the room; on perceiving him Beauchamp
stopped.

ГўВҖВңYour arrival here, without waiting my visit at your house today, looks
well, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Albert. ГўВҖВңTell me, may I shake hands with you, saying,
ГўВҖВҳBeauchamp, acknowledge you have injured me, and retain my friendship,ГўВҖВҷ
or must I simply propose to you a choice of arms?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlbert,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, with a look of sorrow which stupefied the
young man, ГўВҖВңlet us first sit down and talk.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңRather, sir, before we sit down, I must demand your answer.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlbert,ГўВҖВқ said the journalist, ГўВҖВңthese are questions which it is
difficult to answer.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will facilitate it by repeating the question, ГўВҖВҳWill you, or will you
not, retract?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMorcerf, it is not enough to answer ГўВҖВҳyesГўВҖВҷ or ГўВҖВҳnoГўВҖВҷ to questions which
concern the honor, the social interest, and the life of such a man as
Lieutenant-gГғВ©nГғВ©ral the Count of Morcerf, peer of France.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat must then be done?ГўВҖВқ

40174m



ГўВҖВңWhat I have done, Albert. I reasoned thusГўВҖВ”money, time, and fatigue are
nothing compared with the reputation and interests of a whole family;
probabilities will not suffice, only facts will justify a deadly combat
with a friend. If I strike with the sword, or discharge the contents of
a pistol at man with whom, for three years, I have been on terms of
intimacy, I must, at least, know why I do so; I must meet him with a
heart at ease, and that quiet conscience which a man needs when his own
arm must save his life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, impatiently, ГўВҖВңwhat does all this mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt means that I have just returned from Yanina.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFrom Yanina?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңImpossible!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHere is my passport; examine the visaГўВҖВ”Geneva, Milan, Venice, Trieste,
Delvino, Yanina. Will you believe the government of a republic, a
kingdom, and an empire?ГўВҖВқ Albert cast his eyes on the passport, then
raised them in astonishment to Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңYou have been to Yanina?ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңAlbert, had you been a stranger, a foreigner, a simple lord, like that
Englishman who came to demand satisfaction three or four months since,
and whom I killed to get rid of, I should not have taken this trouble;
but I thought this mark of consideration due to you. I took a week to
go, another to return, four days of quarantine, and forty-eight hours
to stay there; that makes three weeks. I returned last night, and here
I am.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat circumlocution! How long you are before you tell me what I most
wish to know?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause, in truth, AlbertГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou hesitate?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВ”I fear.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou fear to acknowledge that your correspondent has deceived you? Oh,
no self-love, Beauchamp. Acknowledge it, Beauchamp; your courage cannot
be doubted.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot so,ГўВҖВқ murmured the journalist; ГўВҖВңon the contraryГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

Albert turned frightfully pale; he endeavored to speak, but the words
died on his lips.

ГўВҖВңMy friend,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, in the most affectionate tone, ГўВҖВңI should
gladly make an apology; but, alas!ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe paragraph was correct, my friend.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? That French officerГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFernand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe traitor who surrendered the castle of the man in whose service he
wasГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPardon me, my friend, that man was your father!ГўВҖВқ

Albert advanced furiously towards Beauchamp, but the latter restrained
him more by a mild look than by his extended hand.

ГўВҖВңMy friend,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңhere is a proof of it.ГўВҖВқ

40176m



Albert opened the paper, it was an attestation of four notable
inhabitants of Yanina, proving that Colonel Fernand Mondego, in the
service of Ali Tepelini, had surrendered the castle for two million
crowns. The signatures were perfectly legal. Albert tottered and fell
overpowered in a chair. It could no longer be doubted; the family name
was fully given. After a momentГўВҖВҷs mournful silence, his heart
overflowed, and he gave way to a flood of tears. Beauchamp, who had
watched with sincere pity the young manГўВҖВҷs paroxysm of grief, approached
him.

ГўВҖВңNow, Albert,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңyou understand meГўВҖВ”do you not? I wished to see
all, and to judge of everything for myself, hoping the explanation
would be in your fatherГўВҖВҷs favor, and that I might do him justice. But,
on the contrary, the particulars which are given prove that Fernand
Mondego, raised by Ali Pasha to the rank of governor-general, is no
other than Count Fernand of Morcerf; then, recollecting the honor you
had done me, in admitting me to your friendship, I hastened to you.ГўВҖВқ

Albert, still extended on the chair, covered his face with both hands,
as if to prevent the light from reaching him.

ГўВҖВңI hastened to you,ГўВҖВқ continued Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңto tell you, Albert, that in
this changing age, the faults of a father cannot revert upon his
children. Few have passed through this revolutionary period, in the
midst of which we were born, without some stain of infamy or blood to
soil the uniform of the soldier, or the gown of the magistrate. Now I
have these proofs, Albert, and I am in your confidence, no human power
can force me to a duel which your own conscience would reproach you
with as criminal, but I come to offer you what you can no longer demand
of me. Do you wish these proofs, these attestations, which I alone
possess, to be destroyed? Do you wish this frightful secret to remain
with us? Confided to me, it shall never escape my lips; say, Albert, my
friend, do you wish it?ГўВҖВқ

Albert threw himself on BeauchampГўВҖВҷs neck.

ГўВҖВңAh, noble fellow!ГўВҖВқ cried he.

ГўВҖВңTake these,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, presenting the papers to Albert.

Albert seized them with a convulsive hand, tore them in pieces, and
trembling lest the least vestige should escape and one day appear to
confront him, he approached the wax-light, always kept burning for
cigars, and burned every fragment.

ГўВҖВңDear, excellent friend,ГўВҖВқ murmured Albert, still burning the papers.

ГўВҖВңLet all be forgotten as a sorrowful dream,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңlet it
vanish as the last sparks from the blackened paper, and disappear as
the smoke from those silent ashes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңand may there remain only the eternal
friendship which I promised to my deliverer, which shall be transmitted
to our childrenГўВҖВҷs children, and shall always remind me that I owe my
life and the honor of my name to you,ГўВҖВ”for had this been known, oh,
Beauchamp, I should have destroyed myself; or,ГўВҖВ”no, my poor mother! I
could not have killed her by the same blow,ГўВҖВ”I should have fled from my
country.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDear Albert,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp. But this sudden and factitious joy soon
forsook the young man, and was succeeded by a still greater grief.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңwhat still oppresses you, my friend?ГўВҖВқ

40178m



ГўВҖВңI am broken-hearted,ГўВҖВқ said Albert. ГўВҖВңListen, Beauchamp! I cannot thus,
in a moment relinquish the respect, the confidence, and pride with
which a fatherГўВҖВҷs untarnished name inspires a son. Oh, Beauchamp,
Beauchamp, how shall I now approach mine? Shall I draw back my forehead
from his embrace, or withhold my hand from his? I am the most wretched
of men. Ah, my mother, my poor mother!ГўВҖВқ said Albert, gazing through his
tears at his motherГўВҖВҷs portrait; ГўВҖВңif you know this, how much must you
suffer!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, taking both his hands, ГўВҖВңtake courage, my
friend.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut how came that first note to be inserted in your journal? Some
unknown enemyГўВҖВ”an invisible foeГўВҖВ”has done this.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe more must you fortify yourself, Albert. Let no trace of emotion be
visible on your countenance, bear your grief as the cloud bears within
it ruin and deathГўВҖВ”a fatal secret, known only when the storm bursts. Go,
my friend, reserve your strength for the moment when the crash shall
come.ГўВҖВқ

40179m



ГўВҖВңYou think, then, all is not over yet?ГўВҖВқ said Albert, horror-stricken.

ГўВҖВңI think nothing, my friend; but all things are possible. By the wayГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ said Albert, seeing that Beauchamp hesitated.

ГўВҖВңAre you going to marry Mademoiselle Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy do you ask me now?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause the rupture or fulfilment of this engagement is connected with
the person of whom we were speaking.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow?ГўВҖВқ said Albert, whose brow reddened; ГўВҖВңyou think M. DanglarsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI ask you only how your engagement stands? Pray put no construction on
my words I do not mean they should convey, and give them no undue
weight.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңthe engagement is broken off.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp. Then, seeing the young man was about to relapse
into melancholy, ГўВҖВңLet us go out, Albert,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңa ride in the wood
in the phaeton, or on horseback, will refresh you; we will then return
to breakfast, and you shall attend to your affairs, and I to mine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWillingly,ГўВҖВқ said Albert; ГўВҖВңbut let us walk. I think a little exertion
would do me good.ГўВҖВқ

The two friends walked out on the fortress. When they arrived at the
Madeleine:

ГўВҖВңSince we are out,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңlet us call on M. de Monte Cristo;
he is admirably adapted to revive oneГўВҖВҷs spirits, because he never
interrogates, and in my opinion those who ask no questions are the best
comforters.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGladly,ГўВҖВқ said Albert; ГўВҖВңlet us callГўВҖВ”I love him.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 85. The Journey

Monte Cristo uttered a joyful exclamation on seeing the young men
together. ГўВҖВңAh, ha!ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI hope all is over, explained and
settled.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңthe absurd reports have died away, and should
they be renewed, I would be the first to oppose them; so let us speak
no more of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlbert will tell you,ГўВҖВқ replied the count ГўВҖВңthat I gave him the same
advice. Look,ГўВҖВқ added he. ГўВҖВңI am finishing the most execrable morningГўВҖВҷs
work.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ said Albert; ГўВҖВңarranging your papers, apparently.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy papers, thank God, no,ГўВҖВ”my papers are all in capital order, because
I have none; but M. CavalcantiГўВҖВҷs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. CavalcantiГўВҖВҷs?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңYes; do you not know that this is a young man whom the count is
introducing?ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңLet us not misunderstand each other,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI
introduce no one, and certainly not M. Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd who,ГўВҖВқ said Albert with a forced smile, ГўВҖВңis to marry Mademoiselle
Danglars instead of me, which grieves me cruelly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? Cavalcanti is going to marry Mademoiselle Danglars?ГўВҖВқ asked
Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңCertainly! do you come from the end of the world?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo;
ГўВҖВңyou, a journalist, the husband of renown? It is the talk of all
Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you, count, have made this match?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңI? Silence, purveyor of gossip, do not spread that report. I make a
match? No, you do not know me; I have done all in my power to oppose
it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, I understand,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңon our friend AlbertГўВҖВҷs account.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn my account?ГўВҖВқ said the young man; ГўВҖВңoh, no, indeed, the count will do
me the justice to assert that I have, on the contrary, always entreated
him to break off my engagement, and happily it is ended. The count
pretends I have not him to thank;ГўВҖВ”so be itГўВҖВ”I will erect an altar _Deo
ignoto_.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI have had little to do with it, for I am
at variance both with the father-in-law and the young man; there is
only Mademoiselle EugГғВ©nie, who appears but little charmed with the
thoughts of matrimony, and who, seeing how little I was disposed to
persuade her to renounce her dear liberty, retains any affection for
me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd do you say this wedding is at hand?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes, in spite of all I could say. I do not know the young man; he
is said to be of good family and rich, but I never trust to vague
assertions. I have warned M. Danglars of it till I am tired, but he is
fascinated with his Luccanese. I have even informed him of a
circumstance I consider very serious; the young man was either charmed
by his nurse, stolen by gypsies, or lost by his tutor, I scarcely know
which. But I do know his father lost sight of him for more than ten
years; what he did during these ten years, God only knows. Well, all
that was useless. They have commissioned me to write to the major to
demand papers, and here they are. I send them, but like PilateГўВҖВ”washing
my hands.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what does Mademoiselle dГўВҖВҷArmilly say to you for robbing her of her
pupil?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, well, I donГўВҖВҷt know; but I understand that she is going to Italy.
Madame Danglars asked me for letters of recommendation for the
_impresari_; I gave her a few lines for the director of the Valle
Theatre, who is under some obligation to me. But what is the matter,
Albert? you look dull; are you, after all, unconsciously in love with
Mademoiselle EugГғВ©nie?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am not aware of it,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, smiling sorrowfully. Beauchamp
turned to look at some paintings.

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ continued Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңyou are not in your usual spirits?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have a dreadful headache,ГўВҖВқ said Albert.
