ГўВҖВңWell, my dear viscount,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI have an infallible
remedy to propose to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is that?ГўВҖВқ asked the young man.

ГўВҖВңA change.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңYes; and as I am just now excessively annoyed, I shall go from home.
Shall we go together?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou annoyed, count?ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңand by what?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, you think very lightly of it; I should like to see you with a
brief preparing in your house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat brief?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe one M. de Villefort is preparing against my amiable assassinГўВҖВ”some
brigand escaped from the gallows apparently.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңI saw it in the paper. Who is this
Caderousse?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSome ProvenГғВ§al, it appears. M. de Villefort heard of him at
Marseilles, and M. Danglars recollects having seen him. Consequently,
the procureur is very active in the affair, and the prefect of police
very much interested; and, thanks to that interest, for which I am very
grateful, they send me all the robbers of Paris and the neighborhood,
under pretence of their being CaderousseГўВҖВҷs murderers, so that in three
months, if this continues, every robber and assassin in France will
have the plan of my house at his fingersГўВҖВҷ ends. I am resolved to desert
them and go to some remote corner of the earth, and shall be happy if
you will accompany me, viscount.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWillingly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen it is settled?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, but where?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have told you, where the air is pure, where every sound soothes,
where one is sure to be humbled, however proud may be his nature. I
love that humiliation, I, who am master of the universe, as was
Augustus.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut where are you really going?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo sea, viscount; you know I am a sailor. I was rocked when an infant
in the arms of old Ocean, and on the bosom of the beautiful Amphitrite;
I have sported with the green mantle of the one and the azure robe of
the other; I love the sea as a mistress, and pine if I do not often see
her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLet us go, count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo sea?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou accept my proposal?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, viscount, there will be in my courtyard this evening a good
travelling britzka, with four post-horses, in which one may rest as in
a bed. M. Beauchamp, it holds four very well, will you accompany us?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you, I have just returned from sea.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat? you have been to sea?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; I have just made a little excursion to the Borromean Islands18.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat of that? come with us,ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңNo, dear Morcerf; you know I only refuse when the thing is impossible.
Besides, it is important,ГўВҖВқ added he in a low tone, ГўВҖВңthat I should
remain in Paris just now to watch the paper.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, you are a good and an excellent friend,ГўВҖВқ said Albert; ГўВҖВңyes, you
are right; watch, watch, Beauchamp, and try to discover the enemy who
made this disclosure.ГўВҖВқ

Albert and Beauchamp parted, the last pressure of their hands
expressing what their tongues could not before a stranger.

ГўВҖВңBeauchamp is a worthy fellow,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, when the journalist
was gone; ГўВҖВңis he not, Albert?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, and a sincere friend; I love him devotedly. But now we are
alone,ГўВҖВ”although it is immaterial to me,ГўВҖВ”where are we going?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңInto Normandy, if you like.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDelightful; shall we be quite retired? have no society, no neighbors?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOur companions will be riding-horses, dogs to hunt with, and a
fishing-boat.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly what I wish for; I will apprise my mother of my intention, and
return to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut shall you be allowed to go into Normandy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI may go where I please.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I am aware you may go alone, since I once met you in ItalyГўВҖВ”but to
accompany the mysterious Monte Cristo?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou forget, count, that I have often told you of the deep interest my
mother takes in you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWoman is fickle.ГўВҖВҷ said Francis I.; ГўВҖВҳwoman is like a wave of the sea,ГўВҖВҷ
said Shakespeare; both the great king and the great poet ought to have
known womanГўВҖВҷs nature well.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWomanГўВҖВҷs, yes; my mother is not woman, but _a_ woman.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs I am only a humble foreigner, you must pardon me if I do not
understand all the subtle refinements of your language.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat I mean to say is, that my mother is not quick to give her
confidence, but when she does she never changes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, yes, indeed,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with a sigh; ГўВҖВңand do you think she
is in the least interested in me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI repeat it, you must really be a very strange and superior man, for
my mother is so absorbed by the interest you have excited, that when I
am with her she speaks of no one else.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd does she try to make you dislike me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary, she often says, ГўВҖВҳMorcerf, I believe the count has a
noble nature; try to gain his esteem.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, sighing.

ГўВҖВңYou see, then,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңthat instead of opposing, she will
encourage me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAdieu, then, until five oГўВҖВҷclock; be punctual, and we shall arrive at
twelve or one.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAt TrГғВ©port?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; or in the neighborhood.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut can we travel forty-eight leagues in eight hours?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEasily,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңYou are certainly a prodigy; you will soon not only surpass the
railway, which would not be very difficult in France, but even the
telegraph.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, viscount, since we cannot perform the journey in less than seven
or eight hours, do not keep me waiting.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not fear, I have little to prepare.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo smiled as he nodded to Albert, then remained a moment
absorbed in deep meditation. But passing his hand across his forehead
as if to dispel his reverie, he rang the bell twice and Bertuccio
entered.

ГўВҖВңBertuccio,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI intend going this evening to Normandy, instead
of tomorrow or the next day. You will have sufficient time before five
oГўВҖВҷclock; despatch a messenger to apprise the grooms at the first
station. M. de Morcerf will accompany me.ГўВҖВқ

Bertuccio obeyed and despatched a courier to Pontoise to say the
travelling-carriage would arrive at six oГўВҖВҷclock. From Pontoise another
express was sent to the next stage, and in six hours all the horses
stationed on the road were ready.

Before his departure, the count went to HaydГғВ©eГўВҖВҷs apartments, told her
his intention, and resigned everything to her care.

Albert was punctual. The journey soon became interesting from its
rapidity, of which Morcerf had formed no previous idea.

ГўВҖВңTruly,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңwith your post-horses going at the rate of
two leagues an hour, and that absurd law that one traveller shall not
pass another without permission, so that an invalid or ill-tempered
traveller may detain those who are well and active, it is impossible to
move; I escape this annoyance by travelling with my own postilion and
horses; do I not, Ali?ГўВҖВқ

The count put his head out of the window and whistled, and the horses
appeared to fly. The carriage rolled with a thundering noise over the
pavement, and everyone turned to notice the dazzling meteor. Ali,
smiling, repeated the sound, grasped the reins with a firm hand, and
spurred his horses, whose beautiful manes floated in the breeze. This
child of the desert was in his element, and with his black face and
sparkling eyes appeared, in the cloud of dust he raised, like the
genius of the simoom and the god of the hurricane.

ГўВҖВңI never knew till now the delight of speed,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, and the
last cloud disappeared from his brow; ГўВҖВңbut where the devil do you get
such horses? Are they made to order?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңsix years since I bought a horse in
Hungary remarkable for its swiftness. The thirty-two that we shall use
tonight are its progeny; they are all entirely black, with the
exception of a star upon the forehead.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is perfectly admirable; but what do you do, count, with all these
horses?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou see, I travel with them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you are not always travelling.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen I no longer require them, Bertuccio will sell them, and he
expects to realize thirty or forty thousand francs by the sale.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut no monarch in Europe will be wealthy enough to purchase them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen he will sell them to some Eastern vizier, who will empty his
coffers to purchase them, and refill them by applying the bastinado to
his subjects.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCount, may I suggest one idea to you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is that, next to you, Bertuccio must be the richest gentleman in
Europe.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are mistaken, viscount; I believe he has not a franc in his
possession.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen he must be a wonder. My dear count, if you tell me many more
marvellous things, I warn you I shall not believe them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI countenance nothing that is marvellous, M. Albert. Tell me, why does
a steward rob his master?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause, I suppose, it is his nature to do so, for the love of
robbing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are mistaken; it is because he has a wife and family, and
ambitious desires for himself and them. Also because he is not sure of
always retaining his situation, and wishes to provide for the future.
Now, M. Bertuccio is alone in the world; he uses my property without
accounting for the use he makes of it; he is sure never to leave my
service.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause I should never get a better.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProbabilities are deceptive.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut I deal in certainties; he is the best servant over whom one has
the power of life and death.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you possess that right over Bertuccio?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

There are words which close a conversation with an iron door; such was
the countГўВҖВҷs ГўВҖВңyes.ГўВҖВқ

The whole journey was performed with equal rapidity; the thirty-two
horses, dispersed over seven stages, brought them to their destination
in eight hours. At midnight they arrived at the gate of a beautiful
park. The porter was in attendance; he had been apprised by the groom
of the last stage of the countГўВҖВҷs approach. At half past two in the
morning Morcerf was conducted to his apartments, where a bath and
supper were prepared. The servant who had travelled at the back of the
carriage waited on him; Baptistin, who rode in front, attended the
count.

Albert bathed, took his supper, and went to bed. All night he was
lulled by the melancholy noise of the surf. On rising, he went to his
window, which opened on a terrace, having the sea in front, and at the
back a pretty park bounded by a small forest.

In a creek lay a little sloop, with a narrow keel and high masts,
bearing on its flag the Monte Cristo arms which were a mountain _or_,
on a sea _azure_, with a cross _gules_ in chief which might be an
allusion to his name that recalled Calvary, the mount rendered by our
LordГўВҖВҷs passion more precious than gold, and to the degrading cross
which his blood had rendered holy; or it might be some personal
remembrance of suffering and regeneration buried in the night of this
mysterious personageГўВҖВҷs past life.

Around the schooner lay a number of small fishing-boats belonging to
the fishermen of the neighboring village, like humble subjects awaiting
orders from their queen. There, as in every spot where Monte Cristo
stopped, if but for two days, luxury abounded and life went on with the
utmost ease.

Albert found in his anteroom two guns, with all the accoutrements for
hunting; a lofty room on the ground floor containing all the ingenious
instruments the EnglishГўВҖВ”eminent in piscatory pursuits, since they are
patient and sluggishГўВҖВ”have invented for fishing. The day passed in
pursuing those exercises in which Monte Cristo excelled. They killed a
dozen pheasants in the park, as many trout in the stream, dined in a
summer-house overlooking the ocean, and took tea in the library.

Towards the evening of the third day. Albert, completely exhausted with
the exercise which invigorated Monte Cristo, was sleeping in an
armchair near the window, while the count was designing with his
architect the plan of a conservatory in his house, when the sound of a
horse at full speed on the high road made Albert look up. He was
disagreeably surprised to see his own valet de chambre, whom he had not
brought, that he might not inconvenience Monte Cristo.

40188m



ГўВҖВңFlorentin here!ГўВҖВқ cried he, starting up; ГўВҖВңis my mother ill?ГўВҖВқ And he
hastened to the door. Monte Cristo watched and saw him approach the
valet, who drew a small sealed parcel from his pocket, containing a
newspaper and a letter.

ГўВҖВңFrom whom is this?ГўВҖВқ said he eagerly.

ГўВҖВңFrom M. Beauchamp,ГўВҖВқ replied Florentin.

ГўВҖВңDid he send you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir; he sent for me to his house, gave me money for my journey,
procured a horse, and made me promise not to stop till I had reached
you, I have come in fifteen hours.ГўВҖВқ

Albert opened the letter with fear, uttered a shriek on reading the
first line, and seized the paper. His sight was dimmed, his legs sank
under him, and he would have fallen had not Florentin supported him.

ГўВҖВңPoor young man,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo in a low voice; ГўВҖВңit is then true
that the sin of the father shall fall on the children to the third and
fourth generation.ГўВҖВқ

Meanwhile Albert had revived, and, continuing to read, he threw back
his head, saying:

ГўВҖВңFlorentin, is your horse fit to return immediately?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is a poor, lame post-horse.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn what state was the house when you left?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll was quiet, but on returning from M. BeauchampГўВҖВҷs, I found madame in
tears; she had sent for me to know when you would return. I told her my
orders from M. Beauchamp; she first extended her arms to prevent me,
but after a momentГўВҖВҷs reflection, ГўВҖВҳYes, go, Florentin,ГўВҖВҷ said she, ГўВҖВҳand
may he come quickly.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, my mother,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңI will return, and woe to the infamous
wretch! But first of all I must get there.ГўВҖВқ

He went back to the room where he had left Monte Cristo. Five minutes
had sufficed to make a complete transformation in his appearance. His
voice had become rough and hoarse; his face was furrowed with wrinkles;
his eyes burned under the blue-veined lids, and he tottered like a
drunken man.

ГўВҖВңCount,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI thank you for your hospitality, which I would
gladly have enjoyed longer; but I must return to Paris.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat has happened?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA great misfortune, more important to me than life. DonГўВҖВҷt question me,
I beg of you, but lend me a horse.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy stables are at your command, viscount; but you will kill yourself
by riding on horseback. Take a post-chaise or a carriage.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, it would delay me, and I need the fatigue you warn me of; it will
do me good.ГўВҖВқ

Albert reeled as if he had been shot, and fell on a chair near the
door. Monte Cristo did not see this second manifestation of physical
exhaustion; he was at the window, calling:

ГўВҖВңAli, a horse for M. de MorcerfГўВҖВ”quick! he is in a hurry!ГўВҖВқ

40190m



These words restored Albert; he darted from the room, followed by the
count.

ГўВҖВңThank you!ГўВҖВқ cried he, throwing himself on his horse.

ГўВҖВңReturn as soon as you can, Florentin. Must I use any password to
procure a horse?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOnly dismount; another will be immediately saddled.ГўВҖВқ

Albert hesitated a moment. ГўВҖВңYou may think my departure strange and
foolish,ГўВҖВқ said the young man; ГўВҖВңyou do not know how a paragraph in a
newspaper may exasperate one. Read that,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңwhen I am gone,
that you may not be witness of my anger.ГўВҖВқ

While the count picked up the paper he put spurs to his horse, which
leaped in astonishment at such an unusual stimulus, and shot away with
the rapidity of an arrow. The count watched him with a feeling of
compassion, and when he had completely disappeared, read as follows:

ГўВҖВңThe French officer in the service of Ali Pasha of Yanina alluded to
three weeks since in _lГўВҖВҷImpartial_, who not only surrendered the castle
of Yanina, but sold his benefactor to the Turks, styled himself truly
at that time Fernand, as our esteemed contemporary states; but he has
since added to his Christian name a title of nobility and a family
name. He now calls himself the Count of Morcerf, and ranks among the
peers.ГўВҖВқ

Thus the terrible secret, which Beauchamp had so generously destroyed,
appeared again like an armed phantom; and another paper, deriving its
information from some malicious source, had published two days after
AlbertГўВҖВҷs departure for Normandy the few lines which had rendered the
unfortunate young man almost crazy.



 Chapter 86. The Trial

At eight oГўВҖВҷclock in the morning Albert had arrived at BeauchampГўВҖВҷs door.
The valet de chambre had received orders to usher him in at once.
Beauchamp was in his bath.

ГўВҖВңHere I am,ГўВҖВқ Albert said.

ГўВҖВңWell, my poor friend,ГўВҖВқ replied Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңI expected you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI need not say I think you are too faithful and too kind to have
spoken of that painful circumstance. Your having sent for me is another
proof of your affection. So, without losing time, tell me, have you the
slightest idea whence this terrible blow proceeds?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think I have some clew.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut first tell me all the particulars of this shameful plot.ГўВҖВқ

Beauchamp proceeded to relate to the young man, who was overwhelmed
with shame and grief, the following facts. Two days previously, the
article had appeared in another paper besides _ lГўВҖВҷImpartial_, and, what
was more serious, one that was well known as a government paper.
Beauchamp was breakfasting when he read the paragraph. He sent
immediately for a cabriolet, and hastened to the publisherГўВҖВҷs office.
Although professing diametrically opposite principles from those of the
editor of the other paper, BeauchampГўВҖВ”as it sometimes, we may say often,
happensГўВҖВ”was his intimate friend. The editor was reading, with apparent
delight, a leading article in the same paper on beet-sugar, probably a
composition of his own.

ГўВҖВңAh, _pardieu!_ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңwith the paper in your hand, my
friend, I need not tell you the cause of my visit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you interested in the sugar question?ГўВҖВқ asked the editor of the
ministerial paper.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңI have not considered the question; a totally
different subject interests me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe article relative to Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed? Is it not a curious affair?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo curious, that I think you are running a great risk of a prosecution
for defamation of character.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot at all; we have received with the information all the requisite
proofs, and we are quite sure M. de Morcerf will not raise his voice
against us; besides, it is rendering a service to oneГўВҖВҷs country to
denounce these wretched criminals who are unworthy of the honor
bestowed on them.ГўВҖВқ

Beauchamp was thunderstruck.

ГўВҖВңWho, then, has so correctly informed you?ГўВҖВқ asked he; ГўВҖВңfor my paper,
which gave the first information on the subject, has been obliged to
stop for want of proof; and yet we are more interested than you in
exposing M. de Morcerf, as he is a peer of France, and we are of the
opposition.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, that is very simple; we have not sought to scandalize. This news
was brought to us. A man arrived yesterday from Yanina, bringing a
formidable array of documents; and when we hesitated to publish the
accusatory article, he told us it should be inserted in some other
paper.ГўВҖВқ

Beauchamp understood that nothing remained but to submit, and left the
office to despatch a courier to Morcerf. But he had been unable to send
to Albert the following particulars, as the events had transpired after
the messengerГўВҖВҷs departure; namely, that the same day a great agitation
was manifest in the House of Peers among the usually calm members of
that dignified assembly. Everyone had arrived almost before the usual
hour, and was conversing on the melancholy event which was to attract
the attention of the public towards one of their most illustrious
colleagues. Some were perusing the article, others making comments and
recalling circumstances which substantiated the charges still more.

The Count of Morcerf was no favorite with his colleagues. Like all
upstarts, he had had recourse to a great deal of haughtiness to
maintain his position. The true nobility laughed at him, the talented
repelled him, and the honorable instinctively despised him. He was, in
fact, in the unhappy position of the victim marked for sacrifice; the
finger of God once pointed at him, everyone was prepared to raise the
hue and cry.

The Count of Morcerf alone was ignorant of the news. He did not take in
the paper containing the defamatory article, and had passed the morning
in writing letters and in trying a horse. He arrived at his usual hour,
with a proud look and insolent demeanor; he alighted, passed through
the corridors, and entered the house without observing the hesitation
of the door-keepers or the coolness of his colleagues.

40194m



Business had already been going on for half an hour when he entered.
Everyone held the accusing paper, but, as usual, no one liked to take
upon himself the responsibility of the attack. At length an honorable
peer, MorcerfГўВҖВҷs acknowledged enemy, ascended the tribune with that
solemnity which announced that the expected moment had arrived. There
was an impressive silence; Morcerf alone knew not why such profound
attention was given to an orator who was not always listened to with so
much complacency.

The count did not notice the introduction, in which the speaker
announced that his communication would be of that vital importance that
it demanded the undivided attention of the House; but at the mention of
Yanina and Colonel Fernand, he turned so frightfully pale that every
member shuddered and fixed his eyes upon him. Moral wounds have this
peculiarity,ГўВҖВ”they may be hidden, but they never close; always painful,
always ready to bleed when touched, they remain fresh and open in the
heart.

The article having been read during the painful hush that followed, a
universal shudder pervaded the assembly, and immediately the closest
attention was given to the orator as he resumed his remarks. He stated
his scruples and the difficulties of the case; it was the honor of M.
de Morcerf, and that of the whole House, he proposed to defend, by
provoking a debate on personal questions, which are always such painful
themes of discussion. He concluded by calling for an investigation,
which might dispose of the calumnious report before it had time to
spread, and restore M. de Morcerf to the position he had long held in
public opinion.

Morcerf was so completely overwhelmed by this great and unexpected
calamity that he could scarcely stammer a few words as he looked around
on the assembly. This timidity, which might proceed from the
astonishment of innocence as well as the shame of guilt, conciliated
some in his favor; for men who are truly generous are always ready to
compassionate when the misfortune of their enemy surpasses the limits
of their hatred.

The president put it to the vote, and it was decided that the
investigation should take place. The count was asked what time he
required to prepare his defence. MorcerfГўВҖВҷs courage had revived when he
found himself alive after this horrible blow.

ГўВҖВңMy lords,ГўВҖВқ answered he, ГўВҖВңit is not by time I could repel the attack
made on me by enemies unknown to me, and, doubtless, hidden in
obscurity; it is immediately, and by a thunderbolt, that I must repel
the flash of lightning which, for a moment, startled me. Oh, that I
could, instead of taking up this defence, shed my last drop of blood to
prove to my noble colleagues that I am their equal in worth.ГўВҖВқ

These words made a favorable impression on behalf of the accused.

ГўВҖВңI demand, then, that the examination shall take place as soon as
possible, and I will furnish the house with all necessary information.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat day do you fix?ГўВҖВқ asked the president.

ГўВҖВңToday I am at your service,ГўВҖВқ replied the count.

The president rang the bell. ГўВҖВңDoes the House approve that the
examination should take place today?ГўВҖВқ

40196m



ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ was the unanimous answer.

A committee of twelve members was chosen to examine the proofs brought
forward by Morcerf. The investigation would begin at eight oГўВҖВҷclock that
evening in the committee-room, and if postponement were necessary, the
proceedings would be resumed each evening at the same hour. Morcerf
asked leave to retire; he had to collect the documents he had long been
preparing against this storm, which his sagacity had foreseen.

Beauchamp related to the young man all the facts we have just narrated;
his story, however, had over ours all the advantage of the animation of
living things over the coldness of dead things.

Albert listened, trembling now with hope, then with anger, and then
again with shame, for from BeauchampГўВҖВҷs confidence he knew his father
was guilty, and he asked himself how, since he was guilty, he could
prove his innocence. Beauchamp hesitated to continue his narrative.

ГўВҖВңWhat next?ГўВҖВқ asked Albert.

ГўВҖВңWhat next? My friend, you impose a painful task on me. Must you know
all?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAbsolutely; and rather from your lips than anotherГўВҖВҷs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMuster up all your courage, then, for never have you required it
more.ГўВҖВқ

Albert passed his hand over his forehead, as if to try his strength, as
a man who is preparing to defend his life proves his shield and bends
his sword. He thought himself strong enough, for he mistook fever for
energy. ГўВҖВңGo on,ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңThe evening arrived; all Paris was in expectation. Many said your
father had only to show himself to crush the charge against him; many
others said he would not appear; while some asserted that they had seen
him start for Brussels; and others went to the police-office to inquire
if he had taken out a passport. I used all my influence with one of the
committee, a young peer of my acquaintance, to get admission to one of
the galleries. He called for me at seven oГўВҖВҷclock, and, before anyone
had arrived, asked one of the door-keepers to place me in a box. I was
concealed by a column, and might witness the whole of the terrible
scene which was about to take place. At eight oГўВҖВҷclock all were in their
places, and M. de Morcerf entered at the last stroke. He held some
papers in his hand; his countenance was calm, and his step firm, and he
was dressed with great care in his military uniform, which was buttoned
completely up to the chin. His presence produced a good effect. The
committee was made up of Liberals, several of whom came forward to
shake hands with him.ГўВҖВқ

Albert felt his heart bursting at these particulars, but gratitude
mingled with his sorrow: he would gladly have embraced those who had
given his father this proof of esteem at a moment when his honor was so
powerfully attacked.

ГўВҖВңAt this moment one of the door-keepers brought in a letter for the
president. ГўВҖВҳYou are at liberty to speak, M. de Morcerf,ГўВҖВҷ said the
president, as he unsealed the letter; and the count began his defence,
I assure you, Albert, in a most eloquent and skilful manner. He
produced documents proving that the Vizier of Yanina had up to the last
moment honored him with his entire confidence, since he had interested
him with a negotiation of life and death with the emperor. He produced
the ring, his mark of authority, with which Ali Pasha generally sealed
his letters, and which the latter had given him, that he might, on his
return at any hour of the day or night, gain access to the presence,
even in the harem. Unfortunately, the negotiation failed, and when he
returned to defend his benefactor, he was dead. ГўВҖВҳBut,ГўВҖВҷ said the count,
ГўВҖВҳso great was Ali PashaГўВҖВҷs confidence, that on his death-bed he resigned
his favorite mistress and her daughter to my care.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

Albert started on hearing these words; the history of HaydГғВ©e recurred
to him, and he remembered what she had said of that message and the
ring, and the manner in which she had been sold and made a slave.

ГўВҖВңAnd what effect did this discourse produce?ГўВҖВқ anxiously inquired
Albert.

ГўВҖВңI acknowledge it affected me, and, indeed, all the committee also,ГўВҖВқ
said Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңMeanwhile, the president carelessly opened the letter which had been
brought to him; but the first lines aroused his attention; he read them
again and again, and fixing his eyes on M. de Morcerf, ГўВҖВҳCount,ГўВҖВҷ said
he, ГўВҖВҳyou have said that the Vizier of Yanina confided his wife and
daughter to your care?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳYes, sir,ГўВҖВҷ replied Morcerf; ГўВҖВҳbut in that, like
all the rest, misfortune pursued me. On my return, Vasiliki and her
daughter HaydГғВ©e had disappeared.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳDid you know them?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳMy intimacy
with the pasha and his unlimited confidence had gained me an
introduction to them, and I had seen them above twenty times.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳHave you any idea what became of them?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳYes, sir; I heard they had
fallen victims to their sorrow, and, perhaps, to their poverty. I was
not rich; my life was in constant danger; I could not seek them, to my
great regret.ГўВҖВҷ The president frowned imperceptibly. ГўВҖВҳGentlemen,ГўВҖВҷ said
he, ГўВҖВҳyou have heard the Comte de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs defence. Can you, sir,
produce any witnesses to the truth of what you have asserted?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳAlas,
no, monsieur,ГўВҖВҷ replied the count; ГўВҖВҳall those who surrounded the vizier,
or who knew me at his court, are either dead or gone away, I know not
where. I believe that I alone, of all my countrymen, survived that
dreadful war. I have only the letters of Ali Tepelini, which I have
placed before you; the ring, a token of his good-will, which is here;
and, lastly, the most convincing proof I can offer, after an anonymous
attack, and that is the absence of any witness against my veracity and
the purity of my military life.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңA murmur of approbation ran through the assembly; and at this moment,
Albert, had nothing more transpired, your fatherГўВҖВҷs cause had been
gained. It only remained to put it to the vote, when the president
resumed: ГўВҖВҳGentlemen and you, monsieur,ГўВҖВ”you will not be displeased, I
presume, to listen to one who calls himself a very important witness,
and who has just presented himself. He is, doubtless, come to prove the
perfect innocence of our colleague. Here is a letter I have just
received on the subject; shall it be read, or shall it be passed over?
and shall we take no notice of this incident?ГўВҖВҷ M. de Morcerf turned
pale, and clenched his hands on the papers he held. The committee
decided to hear the letter; the count was thoughtful and silent. The
president read:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳMr. President,ГўВҖВ”I can furnish the committee of inquiry into the
conduct of the Lieutenant-General the Count of Morcerf in Epirus and in
Macedonia with important particulars.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңThe president paused, and the count turned pale. The president looked
at his auditors. ГўВҖВҳProceed,ГўВҖВҷ was heard on all sides. The president
resumed:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI was on the spot at the death of Ali Pasha. I was present during his
last moments. I know what is become of Vasiliki and HaydГғВ©e. I am at the
command of the committee, and even claim the honor of being heard. I
shall be in the lobby when this note is delivered to you.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳAnd who is this witness, or rather this enemy?ГўВҖВҷ asked the count, in a
tone in which there was a visible alteration. ГўВҖВҳWe shall know, sir,ГўВҖВҷ
replied the president. ГўВҖВҳIs the committee willing to hear this
witness?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳYes, yes,ГўВҖВҷ they all said at once. The door-keeper was
called. ГўВҖВҳIs there anyone in the lobby?ГўВҖВҷ said the president.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳYes, sir.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳWho is it?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳA woman, accompanied by a servant.ГўВҖВҷ Everyone
looked at his neighbor. ГўВҖВҳBring her in,ГўВҖВҷ said the president. Five
minutes after the door-keeper again appeared; all eyes were fixed on
the door, and I,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңshared the general expectation and
anxiety. Behind the door-keeper walked a woman enveloped in a large
veil, which completely concealed her. It was evident, from her figure
and the perfumes she had about her, that she was young and fastidious
in her tastes, but that was all. The president requested her to throw
aside her veil, and it was then seen that she was dressed in the
Grecian costume, and was remarkably beautiful.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңit was she.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHaydГғВ©e.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho told you that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas, I guess it. But go on, Beauchamp. You see I am calm and strong.
And yet we must be drawing near the disclosure.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. de Morcerf,ГўВҖВқ continued Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңlooked at this woman with
surprise and terror. Her lips were about to pass his sentence of life
or death. To the committee the adventure was so extraordinary and
curious, that the interest they had felt for the countГўВҖВҷs safety became
now quite a secondary matter. The president himself advanced to place a
seat for the young lady; but she declined availing herself of it. As
for the count, he had fallen on his chair; it was evident that his legs
refused to support him.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳMadame,ГўВҖВҷ said the president, ГўВҖВҳyou have engaged to furnish the
committee with some important particulars respecting the affair at
Yanina, and you have stated that you were an eyewitness of the
event.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳI was, indeed,ГўВҖВҷ said the stranger, with a tone of sweet
melancholy, and with the sonorous voice peculiar to the East.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳBut allow me to say that you must have been very young then.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳI was
four years old; but as those events deeply concerned me, not a single
detail has escaped my memory.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳIn what manner could these events
concern you? and who are you, that they should have made so deep an
impression on you?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳOn them depended my fatherГўВҖВҷs life,ГўВҖВҷ replied she.
ГўВҖВҳI am HaydГғВ©e, the daughter of Ali Tepelini, pasha of Yanina, and of
Vasiliki, his beloved wife.ГўВҖВҷ

40202m



ГўВҖВңThe blush of mingled pride and modesty which suddenly suffused the
cheeks of the young woman, the brilliancy of her eye, and her highly
important communication, produced an indescribable effect on the
assembly. As for the count, he could not have been more overwhelmed if
a thunderbolt had fallen at his feet and opened an immense gulf before
him.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳMadame,ГўВҖВҷ replied the president, bowing with profound respect, ГўВҖВҳallow
me to ask one question; it shall be the last: Can you prove the
authenticity of what you have now stated?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI can, sir,ГўВҖВҷ said HaydГғВ©e, drawing from under her veil a satin satchel
highly perfumed; ГўВҖВҳfor here is the register of my birth, signed by my
father and his principal officers, and that of my baptism, my father
having consented to my being brought up in my motherГўВҖВҷs faith,ГўВҖВ”this
latter has been sealed by the grand primate of Macedonia and Epirus;
and lastly (and perhaps the most important), the record of the sale of
my person and that of my mother to the Armenian merchant El-Kobbir, by
the French officer, who, in his infamous bargain with the Porte, had
reserved as his part of the booty the wife and daughter of his
benefactor, whom he sold for the sum of four hundred thousand francs.ГўВҖВҷ
A greenish pallor spread over the countГўВҖВҷs cheeks, and his eyes became
bloodshot at these terrible imputations, which were listened to by the
assembly with ominous silence.

ГўВҖВңHaydГғВ©e, still calm, but with a calmness more dreadful than the anger
of another would have been, handed to the president the record of her
sale, written in Arabic. It had been supposed some of the papers might
be in the Arabian, Romaic, or Turkish language, and the interpreter of
the House was in attendance. One of the noble peers, who was familiar
with the Arabic language, having studied it during the famous Egyptian
campaign, followed with his eye as the translator read aloud:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI, El-Kobbir, a slave-merchant, and purveyor of the harem of his
highness, acknowledge having received for transmission to the sublime
emperor, from the French lord, the Count of Monte Cristo, an emerald
valued at eight hundred thousand francs; as the ransom of a young
Christian slave of eleven years of age, named HaydГғВ©e, the acknowledged
daughter of the late lord Ali Tepelini, pasha of Yanina, and of
Vasiliki, his favorite; she having been sold to me seven years
previously, with her mother, who had died on arriving at
Constantinople, by a French colonel in the service of the Vizier Ali
Tepelini, named Fernand Mondego. The above-mentioned purchase was made
on his highnessГўВҖВҷs account, whose mandate I had, for the sum of four
hundred thousand francs.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳGiven at Constantinople, by authority of his highness, in the year
1247 of the Hegira.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳSigned, El-Kobbir.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳThat this record should have all due authority, it shall bear the
imperial seal, which the vendor is bound to have affixed to it.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңNear the merchantГўВҖВҷs signature there was, indeed, the seal of the
sublime emperor. A dreadful silence followed the reading of this
document; the count could only stare, and his gaze, fixed as if
unconsciously on HaydГғВ©e, seemed one of fire and blood. ГўВҖВҳMadame,ГўВҖВҷ said
the president, ГўВҖВҳmay reference be made to the Count of Monte Cristo, who
is now, I believe, in Paris?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳSir,ГўВҖВҷ replied HaydГғВ©e, ГўВҖВҳthe Count of Monte Cristo, my foster-father,
has been in Normandy the last three days.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWho, then, has counselled you to take this step, one for which the
court is deeply indebted to you, and which is perfectly natural,
considering your birth and your misfortunes?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳSir,ГўВҖВҷ replied HaydГғВ©e, ГўВҖВҳI
have been led to take this step from a feeling of respect and grief.
Although a Christian, may God forgive me, I have always sought to
revenge my illustrious father. Since I set my foot in France, and knew
the traitor lived in Paris, I have watched carefully. I live retired in
the house of my noble protector, but I do it from choice. I love
retirement and silence, because I can live with my thoughts and
recollections of past days. But the Count of Monte Cristo surrounds me
with every paternal care, and I am ignorant of nothing which passes in
the world. I learn all in the silence of my apartments,ГўВҖВ”for instance, I
see all the newspapers, every periodical, as well as every new piece of
music; and by thus watching the course of the life of others, I learned
what had transpired this morning in the House of Peers, and what was to
take place this evening; then I wrote.ГўВҖВҷ

40204m



ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳThen,ГўВҖВҷ remarked the president, ГўВҖВҳthe Count of Monte Cristo knows
nothing of your present proceedings?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳHe is quite unaware of them, and
I have but one fear, which is that he should disapprove of what I have
done. But it is a glorious day for me,ГўВҖВҷ continued the young girl,
raising her ardent gaze to heaven, ГўВҖВҳthat on which I find at last an
opportunity of avenging my father!ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңThe count had not uttered one word the whole of this time. His
colleagues looked at him, and doubtless pitied his prospects, blighted
under the perfumed breath of a woman. His misery was depicted in
sinister lines on his countenance. ГўВҖВҳM. de Morcerf,ГўВҖВҷ said the president,
ГўВҖВҳdo you recognize this lady as the daughter of Ali Tepelini, pasha of
Yanina?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳNo,ГўВҖВҷ said Morcerf, attempting to rise, ГўВҖВҳit is a base plot,
contrived by my enemies.ГўВҖВҷ HaydГғВ©e, whose eyes had been fixed on the
door, as if expecting someone, turned hastily, and, seeing the count
standing, shrieked, ГўВҖВҳYou do not know me?ГўВҖВҷ said she. ГўВҖВҳWell, I
fortunately recognize you! You are Fernand Mondego, the French officer
who led the troops of my noble father! It is you who surrendered the
castle of Yanina! It is you who, sent by him to Constantinople, to
treat with the emperor for the life or death of your benefactor,
brought back a false mandate granting full pardon! It is you who, with
that mandate, obtained the pashaГўВҖВҷs ring, which gave you authority over
Selim, the fire-keeper! It is you who stabbed Selim. It is you who sold
us, my mother and me, to the merchant, El-Kobbir! Assassin, assassin,
assassin, you have still on your brow your masterГўВҖВҷs blood! Look,
gentlemen, all!ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңThese words had been pronounced with such enthusiasm and evident
truth, that every eye was fixed on the countГўВҖВҷs forehead, and he himself
passed his hand across it, as if he felt AliГўВҖВҷs blood still lingering
there. ГўВҖВҳYou positively recognize M. de Morcerf as the officer, Fernand
Mondego?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳIndeed I do!ГўВҖВҷ cried HaydГғВ©e. ГўВҖВҳOh, my mother, it was you who
said, ГўВҖВңYou were free, you had a beloved father, you were destined to be
almost a queen. Look well at that man; it is he who raised your
fatherГўВҖВҷs head on the point of a spear; it is he who sold us; it is he
who forsook us! Look well at his right hand, on which he has a large
wound; if you forgot his features, you would know him by that hand,
into which fell, one by one, the gold pieces of the merchant
El-Kobbir!ГўВҖВқ I know him! Ah, let him say now if he does not recognize
me!ГўВҖВҷ Each word fell like a dagger on Morcerf, and deprived him of a
portion of his energy; as she uttered the last, he hid his mutilated
hand hastily in his bosom, and fell back on his seat, overwhelmed by
wretchedness and despair. This scene completely changed the opinion of
the assembly respecting the accused count.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳCount of Morcerf,ГўВҖВҷ said the president, ГўВҖВҳdo not allow yourself to be
cast down; answer. The justice of the court is supreme and impartial as
that of God; it will not suffer you to be trampled on by your enemies
without giving you an opportunity of defending yourself. Shall further
inquiries be made? Shall two members of the House be sent to Yanina?
Speak!ГўВҖВҷ Morcerf did not reply. Then all the members looked at each
other with terror. They knew the countГўВҖВҷs energetic and violent temper;
it must be, indeed, a dreadful blow which would deprive him of courage
to defend himself. They expected that his stupefied silence would be
followed by a fiery outburst. ГўВҖВҳWell,ГўВҖВҷ asked the president, ГўВҖВҳwhat is
your decision?ГўВҖВҷ

40206m



ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳI have no reply to make,ГўВҖВҷ said the count in a low tone.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳHas the daughter of Ali Tepelini spoken the truth?ГўВҖВҷ said the
president. ГўВҖВҳIs she, then, the terrible witness to whose charge you dare
not plead ГўВҖВңNot guiltyГўВҖВқ? Have you really committed the crimes of which
you are accused?ГўВҖВҷ The count looked around him with an expression which
might have softened tigers, but which could not disarm his judges. Then
he raised his eyes towards the ceiling, but withdrew then, immediately,
as if he feared the roof would open and reveal to his distressed view
that second tribunal called heaven, and that other judge named God.
Then, with a hasty movement, he tore open his coat, which seemed to
stifle him, and flew from the room like a madman; his footstep was
heard one moment in the corridor, then the rattling of his
carriage-wheels as he was driven rapidly away. ГўВҖВҳGentlemen,ГўВҖВҷ said the
president, when silence was restored, ГўВҖВҳis the Count of Morcerf
convicted of felony, treason, and conduct unbecoming a member of this
House?ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳYes,ГўВҖВҷ replied all the members of the committee of inquiry with
a unanimous voice.

ГўВҖВңHaydГғВ©e had remained until the close of the meeting. She heard the
countГўВҖВҷs sentence pronounced without betraying an expression of joy or
pity; then drawing her veil over her face she bowed majestically to the
councillors, and left with that dignified step which Virgil attributes
to his goddesses.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 87. The Challenge

Then,ГўВҖВқ continued Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңI took advantage of the silence and the
darkness to leave the house without being seen. The usher who had
introduced me was waiting for me at the door, and he conducted me
through the corridors to a private entrance opening into the Rue de
Vaugirard. I left with mingled feelings of sorrow and delight. Excuse
me, Albert,ГўВҖВ”sorrow on your account, and delight with that noble girl,
thus pursuing paternal vengeance. Yes, Albert, from whatever source the
blow may have proceededГўВҖВ”it may be from an enemy, but that enemy is only
the agent of Providence.ГўВҖВқ

Albert held his head between his hands; he raised his face, red with
shame and bathed in tears, and seizing BeauchampГўВҖВҷs arm:

ГўВҖВңMy friend,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңmy life is ended. I cannot calmly say with you,
ГўВҖВҳProvidence has struck the blow;ГўВҖВҷ but I must discover who pursues me
with this hatred, and when I have found him I shall kill him, or he
will kill me. I rely on your friendship to assist me, Beauchamp, if
contempt has not banished it from your heart.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңContempt, my friend? How does this misfortune affect you? No, happily
that unjust prejudice is forgotten which made the son responsible for
the fatherГўВҖВҷs actions. Review your life, Albert; although it is only
just beginning, did a lovely summerГўВҖВҷs day ever dawn with greater purity
than has marked the commencement of your career? No, Albert, take my
advice. You are young and richГўВҖВ”leave ParisГўВҖВ”all is soon forgotten in
this great Babylon of excitement and changing tastes. You will return
after three or four years with a Russian princess for a bride, and no
one will think more of what occurred yesterday than if it had happened
sixteen years ago.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you, my dear Beauchamp, thank you for the excellent feeling
which prompts your advice; but it cannot be. I have told you my wish,
or rather my determination. You understand that, interested as I am in
this affair, I cannot see it in the same light as you do. What appears
to you to emanate from a celestial source, seems to me to proceed from
one far less pure. Providence appears to me to have no share in this
affair; and happily so, for instead of the invisible, impalpable agent
of celestial rewards and punishments, I shall find one both palpable
and visible, on whom I shall revenge myself, I assure you, for all I
have suffered during the last month. Now, I repeat, Beauchamp, I wish
to return to human and material existence, and if you are still the
friend you profess to be, help me to discover the hand that struck the
blow.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBe it so,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңif you must have me descend to earth, I
submit; and if you will seek your enemy, I will assist you, and I will
engage to find him, my honor being almost as deeply interested as
yours.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, then, you understand, Beauchamp, that we begin our search
immediately. Each momentГўВҖВҷs delay is an eternity for me. The calumniator
is not yet punished, and he may hope that he will not be; but, on my
honor, if he thinks so, he deceives himself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, listen, Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, Beauchamp, I see you know something already; you will restore me
to life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not say there is any truth in what I am going to tell you, but it
is, at least, a ray of light in a dark night; by following it we may,
perhaps, discover something more certain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell me; satisfy my impatience.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I will tell you what I did not like to mention on my return from
Yanina.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSay on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI went, of course, to the chief banker of the town to make inquiries.
At the first word, before I had even mentioned your fatherГўВҖВҷs nameГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳAh,ГўВҖВҷ said he. ГўВҖВҳI guess what brings you here.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳHow, and why?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳBecause a fortnight since I was questioned on the same subject.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳBy whom?ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳBy a banker of Paris, my correspondent.ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWhose name isГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҷ

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳDanglars.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe!ГўВҖВқ cried Albert; ГўВҖВңyes, it is indeed he who has so long pursued my
father with jealous hatred. He, the man who would be popular, cannot
forgive the Count of Morcerf for being created a peer; and this
marriage broken off without a reason being assignedГўВҖВ”yes, it is all from
the same cause.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMake inquiries, Albert, but do not be angry without reason; make
inquiries, and if it be trueГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes, if it be true,ГўВҖВқ cried the young man, ГўВҖВңhe shall pay me all I
have suffered.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBeware, Morcerf, he is already an old man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will respect his age as he has respected the honor of my family; if
my father had offended him, why did he not attack him personally? Oh,
no, he was afraid to encounter him face to face.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not condemn you, Albert; I only restrain you. Act prudently.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, do not fear; besides, you will accompany me. Beauchamp, solemn
transactions should be sanctioned by a witness. Before this day closes,
if M. Danglars is guilty, he shall cease to live, or I shall die.
_Pardieu_, Beauchamp, mine shall be a splendid funeral!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen such resolutions are made, Albert, they should be promptly
executed. Do you wish to go to M. Danglars? Let us go immediately.ГўВҖВқ

They sent for a cabriolet. On entering the bankerГўВҖВҷs mansion, they
perceived the phaeton and servant of M. Andrea Cavalcanti.

ГўВҖВңAh! _parbleu!_ thatГўВҖВҷs good,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, with a gloomy tone. ГўВҖВңIf M.
Danglars will not fight with me, I will kill his son-in-law; Cavalcanti
will certainly fight.ГўВҖВқ

The servant announced the young man; but the banker, recollecting what
had transpired the day before, did not wish him admitted. It was,
however, too late; Albert had followed the footman, and, hearing the
order given, forced the door open, and followed by Beauchamp found
himself in the bankerГўВҖВҷs study.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ cried the latter, ГўВҖВңam I no longer at liberty to receive whom I
choose in my house? You appear to forget yourself sadly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, coldly; ГўВҖВңthere are circumstances in which one
cannot, except through cowardice,ГўВҖВ”I offer you that refuge,ГўВҖВ”refuse to
admit certain persons at least.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is your errand, then, with me, sir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI mean,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, drawing near, and without apparently noticing
Cavalcanti, who stood with his back towards the fireplaceГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңI mean to
propose a meeting in some retired corner where no one will interrupt us
for ten minutes; that will be sufficientГўВҖВ”where two men having met, one
of them will remain on the ground.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars turned pale; Cavalcanti moved a step forward, and Albert
turned towards him.

ГўВҖВңAnd you, too,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңcome, if you like, monsieur; you have a
claim, being almost one of the family, and I will give as many
rendezvous of that kind as I can find persons willing to accept them.ГўВҖВқ

Cavalcanti looked at Danglars with a stupefied air, and the latter,
making an effort, arose and stepped between the two young men. AlbertГўВҖВҷs
attack on Andrea had placed him on a different footing, and he hoped
this visit had another cause than that he had at first supposed.

ГўВҖВңIndeed, sir,ГўВҖВқ said he to Albert, ГўВҖВңif you are come to quarrel with this
gentleman because I have preferred him to you, I shall resign the case
to the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou mistake, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf with a gloomy smile; ГўВҖВңI am not
referring in the least to matrimony, and I only addressed myself to M.
Cavalcanti because he appeared disposed to interfere between us. In one
respect you are right, for I am ready to quarrel with everyone today;
but you have the first claim, M. Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

40212m



ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ replied Danglars, pale with anger and fear, ГўВҖВңI warn you, when I
have the misfortune to meet with a mad dog, I kill it; and far from
thinking myself guilty of a crime, I believe I do society a kindness.
Now, if you are mad and try to bite me, I will kill you without pity.
Is it my fault that your father has dishonored himself?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, miserable wretch!ГўВҖВқ cried Morcerf, ГўВҖВңit is your fault.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars retreated a few steps. ГўВҖВңMy fault?ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңyou must be mad!
What do I know of the Grecian affair? Have I travelled in that country?
Did I advise your father to sell the castle of YaninaГўВҖВ”to betrayГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSilence!ГўВҖВқ said Albert, with a thundering voice. ГўВҖВңNo; it is not you who
have directly made this exposure and brought this sorrow on us, but you
hypocritically provoked it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; you! How came it known?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI suppose you read it in the paper in the account from Yanina?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho wrote to Yanina?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo Yanina?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes. Who wrote for particulars concerning my father?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI imagine anyone may write to Yanina.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut one person only wrote!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne only?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; and that was you!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI, doubtless, wrote. It appears to me that when about to marry your
daughter to a young man, it is right to make some inquiries respecting
his family; it is not only a right, but a duty.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou wrote, sir, knowing what answer you would receive.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI, indeed? I assure you,ГўВҖВқ cried Danglars, with a confidence and
security proceeding less from fear than from the interest he really
felt for the young man, ГўВҖВңI solemnly declare to you, that I should never
have thought of writing to Yanina, did I know anything of Ali PashaГўВҖВҷs
misfortunes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho, then, urged you to write? Tell me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Pardieu!_ it was the most simple thing in the world. I was speaking
of your fatherГўВҖВҷs past history. I said the origin of his fortune
remained obscure. The person to whom I addressed my scruples asked me
where your father had acquired his property? I answered, ГўВҖВҳIn
Greece.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВҳThen,ГўВҖВҷ said he, ГўВҖВҳwrite to Yanina.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd who thus advised you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo other than your friend, Monte Cristo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe Count of Monte Cristo told you to write to Yanina?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; and I wrote, and will show you my correspondence, if you like.ГўВҖВқ

Albert and Beauchamp looked at each other.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, who had not yet spoken, ГўВҖВңyou appear to accuse
the count, who is absent from Paris at this moment, and cannot justify
himself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI accuse no one, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңI relate, and I will repeat
before the count what I have said to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoes the count know what answer you received?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; I showed it to him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid he know my fatherГўВҖВҷs Christian name was Fernand, and his family
name Mondego?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I had told him that long since, and I did only what any other
would have done in my circumstances, and perhaps less. When, the day
after the arrival of this answer, your father came by the advice of
Monte Cristo to ask my daughterГўВҖВҷs hand for you, I decidedly refused
him, but without any explanation or exposure. In short, why should I
have any more to do with the affair? How did the honor or disgrace of
M. de Morcerf affect me? It neither increased nor decreased my income.ГўВҖВқ

Albert felt the blood mounting to his brow; there was no doubt upon the
subject. Danglars defended himself with the baseness, but at the same
time with the assurance, of a man who speaks the truth, at least in
part, if not whollyГўВҖВ”not for conscienceГўВҖВҷ sake, but through fear.
Besides, what was Morcerf seeking? It was not whether Danglars or Monte
Cristo was more or less guilty; it was a man who would answer for the
offence, whether trifling or serious; it was a man who would fight, and
it was evident Danglars would not fight.

In addition to this, everything forgotten or unperceived before
presented itself now to his recollection. Monte Cristo knew everything,
as he had bought the daughter of Ali Pasha; and, knowing everything, he
had advised Danglars to write to Yanina. The answer known, he had
yielded to AlbertГўВҖВҷs wish to be introduced to HaydГғВ©e, and allowed the
conversation to turn on the death of Ali, and had not opposed HaydГғВ©eГўВҖВҷs
recital (but having, doubtless, warned the young girl, in the few
Romaic words he spoke to her, not to implicate MorcerfГўВҖВҷs father).
Besides, had he not begged of Morcerf not to mention his fatherГўВҖВҷs name
before HaydГғВ©e? Lastly, he had taken Albert to Normandy when he knew the
final blow was near. There could be no doubt that all had been
calculated and previously arranged; Monte Cristo then was in league
with his fatherГўВҖВҷs enemies. Albert took Beauchamp aside, and
communicated these ideas to him.

ГўВҖВңYou are right,ГўВҖВқ said the latter; ГўВҖВңM. Danglars has only been a
secondary agent in this sad affair, and it is of M. de Monte Cristo
that you must demand an explanation.ГўВҖВқ

Albert turned.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he to Danglars, ГўВҖВңunderstand that I do not take a final
leave of you; I must ascertain if your insinuations are just, and am
going now to inquire of the Count of Monte Cristo.ГўВҖВқ

He bowed to the banker, and went out with Beauchamp, without appearing
to notice Cavalcanti. Danglars accompanied him to the door, where he
again assured Albert that no motive of personal hatred had influenced
him against the Count of Morcerf.



 Chapter 88. The Insult

At the bankerГўВҖВҷs door Beauchamp stopped Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңListen,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңjust now I told you it was of M. de Monte Cristo
you must demand an explanation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; and we are going to his house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңReflect, Morcerf, one moment before you go.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn what shall I reflect?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn the importance of the step you are taking.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs it more serious than going to M. Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; M. Danglars is a money-lover, and those who love money, you know,
think too much of what they risk to be easily induced to fight a duel.
The other is, on the contrary, to all appearance a true nobleman; but
do you not fear to find him a bully?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI only fear one thing; namely, to find a man who will not fight.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not be alarmed,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңhe will meet you. My only fear is
that he will be too strong for you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy friend,ГўВҖВқ said Morcerf, with a sweet smile, ГўВҖВңthat is what I wish.
The happiest thing that could occur to me, would be to die in my
fatherГўВҖВҷs stead; that would save us all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour mother would die of grief.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy poor mother!ГўВҖВқ said Albert, passing his hand across his eyes, ГўВҖВңI
know she would; but better so than die of shame.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you quite decided, Albert?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; let us go.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut do you think we shall find the count at home?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe intended returning some hours after me, and doubtless he is now at
home.ГўВҖВқ

They ordered the driver to take them to No. 30 Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es.
Beauchamp wished to go in alone, but Albert observed that as this was
an unusual circumstance he might be allowed to deviate from the usual
etiquette of duels. The cause which the young man espoused was one so
sacred that Beauchamp had only to comply with all his wishes; he
yielded and contented himself with following Morcerf. Albert sprang
from the porterГўВҖВҷs lodge to the steps. He was received by Baptistin. The
count had, indeed, just arrived, but he was in his bath, and had
forbidden that anyone should be admitted.

ГўВҖВңBut after his bath?ГўВҖВқ asked Morcerf.

ГўВҖВңMy master will go to dinner.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd after dinner?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe will sleep an hour.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is going to the Opera.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you sure of it?ГўВҖВқ asked Albert.

ГўВҖВңQuite, sir; my master has ordered his horses at eight oГўВҖВҷclock
precisely.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery good,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert; ГўВҖВңthat is all I wished to know.ГўВҖВқ

Then, turning towards Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңIf you have anything to attend to,
Beauchamp, do it directly; if you have any appointment for this
evening, defer it till tomorrow. I depend on you to accompany me to the
Opera; and if you can, bring ChГғВўteau-Renaud with you.ГўВҖВқ

Beauchamp availed himself of AlbertГўВҖВҷs permission, and left him,
promising to call for him at a quarter before eight. On his return
home, Albert expressed his wish to Franz Debray, and Morrel, to see
them at the Opera that evening. Then he went to see his mother, who
since the events of the day before had refused to see anyone, and had
kept her room. He found her in bed, overwhelmed with grief at this
public humiliation.

The sight of Albert produced the effect which might naturally be
expected on MercГғВ©dГғВЁs; she pressed her sonГўВҖВҷs hand and sobbed aloud, but
her tears relieved her. Albert stood one moment speechless by the side
of his motherГўВҖВҷs bed. It was evident from his pale face and knit brows
that his resolution to revenge himself was growing weaker.

ГўВҖВңMy dear mother,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңdo you know if M. de Morcerf has any
enemy?ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs started; she noticed that the young man did not say ГўВҖВңmy
father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy son,ГўВҖВқ she said, ГўВҖВңpersons in the countГўВҖВҷs situation have many secret
enemies. Those who are known are not the most dangerous.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it, and appeal to your penetration. You are of so superior a
mind, nothing escapes you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy do you say so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause, for instance, you noticed on the evening of the ball we gave,
that M. de Monte Cristo would eat nothing in our house.ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs raised herself on her feverish arm.

ГўВҖВңM. de Monte Cristo!ГўВҖВқ she exclaimed; ГўВҖВңand how is he connected with the
question you asked me?ГўВҖВқ

40219m



ГўВҖВңYou know, mother, M. de Monte Cristo is almost an Oriental, and it is
customary with the Orientals to secure full liberty for revenge by not
eating or drinking in the houses of their enemies.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you say M. de Monte Cristo is our enemy?ГўВҖВқ replied MercГғВ©dГғВЁs,
becoming paler than the sheet which covered her. ГўВҖВңWho told you so? Why,
you are mad, Albert! M. de Monte Cristo has only shown us kindness. M.
de Monte Cristo saved your life; you yourself presented him to us. Oh,
I entreat you, my son, if you had entertained such an idea, dispel it;
and my counsel to youГўВҖВ”nay, my prayerГўВҖВ”is to retain his friendship.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMother,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man, ГўВҖВңyou have special reasons for telling
me to conciliate that man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, blushing as rapidly as she had turned pale, and
again becoming paler than ever.

ГўВҖВңYes, doubtless; and is it not that he may never do us any harm?ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs shuddered, and, fixing on her son a scrutinizing gaze, ГўВҖВңYou
speak strangely,ГўВҖВқ said she to Albert, ГўВҖВңand you appear to have some
singular prejudices. What has the count done? Three days since you were
with him in Normandy; only three days since we looked on him as our
best friend.ГўВҖВқ

An ironical smile passed over AlbertГўВҖВҷs lips. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs saw it and with
the double instinct of woman and mother guessed all; but as she was
prudent and strong-minded she concealed both her sorrows and her fears.
Albert was silent; an instant after, the countess resumed:

ГўВҖВңYou came to inquire after my health; I will candidly acknowledge that
I am not well. You should install yourself here, and cheer my solitude.
I do not wish to be left alone.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMother,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңyou know how gladly I would obey your
wish, but an urgent and important affair obliges me to leave you for
the whole evening.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ replied MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, sighing, ГўВҖВңgo, Albert; I will not make you a
slave to your filial piety.ГўВҖВқ

Albert pretended he did not hear, bowed to his mother, and quitted her.
Scarcely had he shut her door, when MercГғВ©dГғВЁs called a confidential
servant, and ordered him to follow Albert wherever he should go that
evening, and to come and tell her immediately what he observed. Then
she rang for her ladyГўВҖВҷs maid, and, weak as she was, she dressed, in
order to be ready for whatever might happen. The footmanГўВҖВҷs mission was
an easy one. Albert went to his room, and dressed with unusual care. At
ten minutes to eight Beauchamp arrived; he had seen ChГғВўteau-Renaud, who
had promised to be in the orchestra before the curtain was raised. Both
got into AlbertГўВҖВҷs _coupГғВ©_; and, as the young man had no reason to
conceal where he was going, he called aloud, ГўВҖВңTo the Opera.ГўВҖВқ In his
impatience he arrived before the beginning of the performance.

40221m



ChГғВўteau-Renaud was at his post; apprised by Beauchamp of the
circumstances, he required no explanation from Albert. The conduct of
the son in seeking to avenge his father was so natural that
ChГғВўteau-Renaud did not seek to dissuade him, and was content with
renewing his assurances of devotion. Debray was not yet come, but
Albert knew that he seldom lost a scene at the Opera.

Albert wandered about the theatre until the curtain was drawn up. He
hoped to meet with M. de Monte Cristo either in the lobby or on the
stairs. The bell summoned him to his seat, and he entered the orchestra
with ChГғВўteau-Renaud and Beauchamp. But his eyes scarcely quitted the
box between the columns, which remained obstinately closed during the
whole of the first act. At last, as Albert was looking at his watch for
about the hundredth time, at the beginning of the second act the door
opened, and Monte Cristo entered, dressed in black, and, leaning over
the front of the box, looked around the pit. Morrel followed him, and
looked also for his sister and brother in-law; he soon discovered them
in another box, and kissed his hand to them.

The count, in his survey of the pit, encountered a pale face and
threatening eyes, which evidently sought to gain his attention. He
recognized Albert, but thought it better not to notice him, as he
looked so angry and discomposed. Without communicating his thoughts to
his companion, he sat down, drew out his opera-glass, and looked
another way. Although apparently not noticing Albert, he did not,
however, lose sight of him, and when the curtain fell at the end of the
second act, he saw him leave the orchestra with his two friends. Then
his head was seen passing at the back of the boxes, and the count knew
that the approaching storm was intended to fall on him. He was at the
moment conversing cheerfully with Morrel, but he was well prepared for
what might happen.

The door opened, and Monte Cristo, turning round, saw Albert, pale and
trembling, followed by Beauchamp and ChГғВўteau-Renaud.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ cried he, with that benevolent politeness which distinguished
his salutation from the common civilities of the world, ГўВҖВңmy cavalier
has attained his object. Good-evening, M. de Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

The countenance of this man, who possessed such extraordinary control
over his feelings, expressed the most perfect cordiality. Morrel only
then recollected the letter he had received from the viscount, in
which, without assigning any reason, he begged him to go to the Opera,
but he understood that something terrible was brooding.

ГўВҖВңWe are not come here, sir, to exchange hypocritical expressions of
politeness, or false professions of friendship,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңbut to
demand an explanation.ГўВҖВқ

The young manГўВҖВҷs trembling voice was scarcely audible.

ГўВҖВңAn explanation at the Opera?ГўВҖВқ said the count, with that calm tone and
penetrating eye which characterize the man who knows his cause is good.
ГўВҖВңLittle acquainted as I am with the habits of Parisians, I should not
have thought this the place for such a demand.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill, if people will shut themselves up,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңand cannot be
seen because they are bathing, dining, or asleep, we must avail
ourselves of the opportunity whenever they are to be seen.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am not difficult of access, sir; for yesterday, if my memory does
not deceive me, you were at my house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYesterday I was at your house, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the young man; ГўВҖВңbecause then
I knew not who you were.ГўВҖВқ

In pronouncing these words Albert had raised his voice so as to be
heard by those in the adjoining boxes and in the lobby. Thus the
attention of many was attracted by this altercation.

ГўВҖВңWhere are you come from, sir? ГўВҖВң said Monte Cristo ГўВҖВңYou do not appear
to be in the possession of your senses.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProvided I understand your perfidy, sir, and succeed in making you
understand that I will be revenged, I shall be reasonable enough,ГўВҖВқ said
Albert furiously.

ГўВҖВңI do not understand you, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңand if I did,
your tone is too high. I am at home here, and I alone have a right to
raise my voice above anotherГўВҖВҷs. Leave the box, sir!ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo pointed towards the door with the most commanding dignity.

ГўВҖВңAh, I shall know how to make you leave your home!ГўВҖВқ replied Albert,
clasping in his convulsed grasp the glove, which Monte Cristo did not
lose sight of.

ГўВҖВңWell, well,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo quietly, ГўВҖВңI see you wish to quarrel
with me; but I would give you one piece of advice, which you will do
well to keep in mind. It is in poor taste to make a display of a
challenge. Display is not becoming to everyone, M. de Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

At this name a murmur of astonishment passed around the group of
spectators of this scene. They had talked of no one but Morcerf the
whole day. Albert understood the allusion in a moment, and was about to
throw his glove at the count, when Morrel seized his hand, while
Beauchamp and ChГғВўteau-Renaud, fearing the scene would surpass the
limits of a challenge, held him back. But Monte Cristo, without rising,
and leaning forward in his chair, merely stretched out his arm and,
taking the damp, crushed glove from the clenched hand of the young man:

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he in a solemn tone, ГўВҖВңI consider your glove thrown, and
will return it to you wrapped around a bullet. Now leave me or I will
summon my servants to throw you out at the door.ГўВҖВқ

Wild, almost unconscious, and with eyes inflamed, Albert stepped back,
and Morrel closed the door. Monte Cristo took up his glass again as if
nothing had happened; his face was like marble, and his heart was like
bronze. Morrel whispered, ГўВҖВңWhat have you done to him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI? NothingГўВҖВ”at least personally,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңBut there must be some cause for this strange scene.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe Count of MorcerfГўВҖВҷs adventure exasperates the young man.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you anything to do with it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt was through HaydГғВ©e that the Chamber was informed of his fatherГўВҖВҷs
treason.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ said Morrel. ГўВҖВңI had been told, but would not credit it, that
the Grecian slave I have seen with you here in this very box was the
daughter of Ali Pasha.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is true, nevertheless.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңI understand it all, and this scene was
premeditated.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes. Albert wrote to request me to come to the Opera, doubtless that I
might be a witness to the insult he meant to offer you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProbably,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with his imperturbable tranquillity.

ГўВҖВңBut what shall you do with him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith whom?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith Albert.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat shall I do with Albert? As certainly, Maximilian, as I now press
your hand, I shall kill him before ten oГўВҖВҷclock tomorrow morning.ГўВҖВқ
Morrel, in his turn, took Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs hand in both of his, and he
shuddered to feel how cold and steady it was.

ГўВҖВңAh, count,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңhis father loves him so much!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not speak to me of that,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, with the first
movement of anger he had betrayed; ГўВҖВңI will make him suffer.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel, amazed, let fall Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs hand. ГўВҖВңCount, count!ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңDear Maximilian,ГўВҖВқ interrupted the count, ГўВҖВңlisten how adorably Duprez
is singing that line,ГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВҳO Mathilde! idole de mon ГғВўme!ГўВҖВҷ


ГўВҖВңI was the first to discover Duprez at Naples, and the first to applaud
him. Bravo, bravo!ГўВҖВқ

Morrel saw it was useless to say more, and refrained. The curtain,
which had risen at the close of the scene with Albert, again fell, and
a rap was heard at the door.

ГўВҖВңCome in,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with a voice that betrayed not the least
emotion; and immediately Beauchamp appeared. ГўВҖВңGood-evening, M.
Beauchamp,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, as if this was the first time he had
seen the journalist that evening; ГўВҖВңbe seated.ГўВҖВқ

Beauchamp bowed, and, sitting down, ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI just now
accompanied M. de Morcerf, as you saw.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd that means,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, laughing, ГўВҖВңthat you had,
probably, just dined together. I am happy to see, M. Beauchamp, that
you are more sober than he was.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said M. Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңAlbert was wrong, I acknowledge, to betray
so much anger, and I come, on my own account, to apologize for him. And
having done so, entirely on my own account, be it understood, I would
add that I believe you too gentlemanly to refuse giving him some
explanation concerning your connection with Yanina. Then I will add two
words about the young Greek girl.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo motioned him to be silent. ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said he, laughing,
ГўВҖВңthere are all my hopes about to be destroyed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңDoubtless you wish to make me appear a very eccentric character. I am,
in your opinion, a Lara, a Manfred, a Lord Ruthven; then, just as I am
arriving at the climax, you defeat your own end, and seek to make an
ordinary man of me. You bring me down to your own level, and demand
explanations! Indeed, M. Beauchamp, it is quite laughable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYet,ГўВҖВқ replied Beauchamp haughtily, ГўВҖВңthere are occasions when probity
commandsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. Beauchamp,ГўВҖВқ interposed this strange man, ГўВҖВңthe Count of Monte Cristo
bows to none but the Count of Monte Cristo himself. Say no more, I
entreat you. I do what I please, M. Beauchamp, and it is always well
done.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man, ГўВҖВңhonest men are not to be paid with such
coin. I require honorable guaranties.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am, sir, a living guaranty,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, motionless, but
with a threatening look; ГўВҖВңwe have both blood in our veins which we wish
to shedГўВҖВ”that is our mutual guaranty. Tell the viscount so, and that
tomorrow, before ten oГўВҖВҷclock, I shall see what color his is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I have only to make arrangements for the duel,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңIt is quite immaterial to me,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңand it was very
unnecessary to disturb me at the Opera for such a trifle. In France
people fight with the sword or pistol, in the colonies with the
carbine, in Arabia with the dagger. Tell your client that, although I
am the insulted party, in order to carry out my eccentricity, I leave
