him the choice of arms, and will accept without discussion, without
dispute, anything, even combat by drawing lots, which is always stupid,
but with me different from other people, as I am sure to gain.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSure to gain!ГўВҖВқ repeated Beauchamp, looking with amazement at the
count.

ГўВҖВңCertainly,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, slightly shrugging his shoulders;
ГўВҖВңotherwise I would not fight with M. de Morcerf. I shall kill himГўВҖВ”I
cannot help it. Only by a single line this evening at my house let me
know the arms and the hour; I do not like to be kept waiting.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPistols, then, at eight oГўВҖВҷclock, in the Bois de Vincennes,ГўВҖВқ said
Beauchamp, quite disconcerted, not knowing if he was dealing with an
arrogant braggadocio or a supernatural being.

ГўВҖВңVery well, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңNow all that is settled, do let
me see the performance, and tell your friend Albert not to come any
more this evening; he will hurt himself with all his ill-chosen
barbarisms: let him go home and go to sleep.ГўВҖВқ

Beauchamp left the box, perfectly amazed.

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, turning towards Morrel, ГўВҖВңI may depend upon
you, may I not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңI am at your service, count; stillГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is desirable I should know the real cause.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is to say, you would rather not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe young man himself is acting blindfolded, and knows not the true
cause, which is known only to God and to me; but I give you my word,
Morrel, that God, who does know it, will be on our side.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEnough,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel; ГўВҖВңwho is your second witness?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know no one in Paris, Morrel, on whom I could confer that honor
besides you and your brother Emmanuel. Do you think Emmanuel would
oblige me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will answer for him, count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell? that is all I require. Tomorrow morning, at seven oГўВҖВҷclock, you
will be with me, will you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe will.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHush, the curtain is rising. Listen! I never lose a note of this opera
if I can avoid it; the music of _William Tell_ is so sweet.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 89. The Night

Monte Cristo waited, according to his usual custom, until Duprez had
sung his famous ГўВҖВң_Suivez-moi!_ГўВҖВқ then he rose and went out. Morrel took
leave of him at the door, renewing his promise to be with him the next
morning at seven oГўВҖВҷclock, and to bring Emmanuel. Then he stepped into
his _coupГғВ©_, calm and smiling, and was at home in five minutes. No one
who knew the count could mistake his expression when, on entering, he
said:

ГўВҖВңAli, bring me my pistols with the ivory cross.ГўВҖВқ

Ali brought the box to his master, who examined the weapons with a
solicitude very natural to a man who is about to intrust his life to a
little powder and shot. These were pistols of an especial pattern,
which Monte Cristo had had made for target practice in his own room. A
cap was sufficient to drive out the bullet, and from the adjoining room
no one would have suspected that the count was, as sportsmen would say,
keeping his hand in.

He was just taking one up and looking for the point to aim at on a
little iron plate which served him as a target, when his study door
opened, and Baptistin entered. Before he had spoken a word, the count
saw in the next room a veiled woman, who had followed closely after
Baptistin, and now, seeing the count with a pistol in his hand and
swords on the table, rushed in. Baptistin looked at his master, who
made a sign to him, and he went out, closing the door after him.

ГўВҖВңWho are you, madame?ГўВҖВқ said the count to the veiled woman.

40227m



The stranger cast one look around her, to be certain that they were
quite alone; then bending as if she would have knelt, and joining her
hands, she said with an accent of despair:

ГўВҖВңEdmond, you will not kill my son!ГўВҖВқ

The count retreated a step, uttered a slight exclamation, and let fall
the pistol he held.

ГўВҖВңWhat name did you pronounce then, Madame de Morcerf?ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңYours!ГўВҖВқ cried she, throwing back her veil,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңyours, which I alone,
perhaps, have not forgotten. Edmond, it is not Madame de Morcerf who is
come to you, it is MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs is dead, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI know no one now of
that name.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs lives, sir, and she remembers, for she alone recognized you
when she saw you, and even before she saw you, by your voice,
Edmond,ГўВҖВ”by the simple sound of your voice; and from that moment she has
followed your steps, watched you, feared you, and she needs not to
inquire what hand has dealt the blow which now strikes M. de Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFernand, do you mean?ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, with bitter irony; ГўВҖВңsince
we are recalling names, let us remember them all.ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo had
pronounced the name of Fernand with such an expression of hatred that
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs felt a thrill of horror run through every vein.

ГўВҖВңYou see, Edmond, I am not mistaken, and have cause to say, ГўВҖВҳSpare my
son!ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd who told you, madame, that I have any hostile intentions against
your son?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo one, in truth; but a mother has twofold sight. I guessed all; I
followed him this evening to the Opera, and, concealed in a parquet
box, have seen all.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf you have seen all, madame, you know that the son of Fernand has
publicly insulted me,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with awful calmness.

ГўВҖВңOh, for pityГўВҖВҷs sake!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have seen that he would have thrown his glove in my face if
Morrel, one of my friends, had not stopped him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen to me, my son has also guessed who you are,ГўВҖВ”he attributes his
fatherГўВҖВҷs misfortunes to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMadame, you are mistaken, they are not misfortunes,ГўВҖВ”it is a
punishment. It is not I who strike M. de Morcerf; it is Providence
which punishes him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd why do you represent Providence?ГўВҖВқ cried MercГғВ©dГғВЁs. ГўВҖВңWhy do you
remember when it forgets? What are Yanina and its vizier to you,
Edmond? What injury has Fernand Mondego done you in betraying Ali
Tepelini?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңall this is an affair between the
French captain and the daughter of Vasiliki. It does not concern me,
you are right; and if I have sworn to revenge myself, it is not on the
French captain, or the Count of Morcerf, but on the fisherman Fernand,
the husband of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs the Catalane.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, sir!ГўВҖВқ cried the countess, ГўВҖВңhow terrible a vengeance for a fault
which fatality made me commit!ГўВҖВ”for I am the only culprit, Edmond, and
if you owe revenge to anyone, it is to me, who had not fortitude to
bear your absence and my solitude.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңwhy was I absent? And why were you
alone?ГўВҖВқ

40231m



ГўВҖВңBecause you had been arrested, Edmond, and were a prisoner.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd why was I arrested? Why was I a prisoner?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not know,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.

ГўВҖВңYou do not, madame; at least, I hope not. But I will tell you. I was
arrested and became a prisoner because, under the arbor of La RГғВ©serve,
the day before I was to marry you, a man named Danglars wrote this
letter, which the fisherman Fernand himself posted.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo went to a secretaire, opened a drawer by a spring, from
which he took a paper which had lost its original color, and the ink of
which had become of a rusty hueГўВҖВ”this he placed in the hands of
MercГғВ©dГғВЁs. It was DanglarsГўВҖВҷ letter to the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney, which the
Count of Monte Cristo, disguised as a clerk from the house of Thomson &
French, had taken from the file against Edmond DantГғВЁs, on the day he
had paid the two hundred thousand francs to M. de Boville. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs
read with terror the following lines:

ГўВҖВңThe kingГўВҖВҷs attorney is informed by a friend to the throne and religion
that one Edmond DantГғВЁs, second in command on board the _Pharaon_, this
day arrived from Smyrna, after having touched at Naples and
Porto-Ferrajo, is the bearer of a letter from Murat to the usurper, and
of another letter from the usurper to the Bonapartist club in Paris.
Ample corroboration of this statement may be obtained by arresting the
above-mentioned Edmond DantГғВЁs, who either carries the letter for Paris
about with him, or has it at his fatherГўВҖВҷs abode. Should it not be found
in possession of either father or son, then it will assuredly be
discovered in the cabin belonging to the said DantГғВЁs on board the
_Pharaon_.ГўВҖВқ


ГўВҖВңHow dreadful!ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, passing her hand across her brow, moist
with perspiration; ГўВҖВңand that letterГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI bought it for two hundred thousand francs, madame,ГўВҖВқ said Monte
Cristo; ГўВҖВңbut that is a trifle, since it enables me to justify myself to
you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd the result of that letterГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou well know, madame, was my arrest; but you do not know how long
that arrest lasted. You do not know that I remained for fourteen years
within a quarter of a league of you, in a dungeon in the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf.
You do not know that every day of those fourteen years I renewed the
vow of vengeance which I had made the first day; and yet I was not
aware that you had married Fernand, my calumniator, and that my father
had died of hunger!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCan it be?ГўВҖВқ cried MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, shuddering.

ГўВҖВңThat is what I heard on leaving my prison fourteen years after I had
entered it; and that is why, on account of the living MercГғВ©dГғВЁs and my
deceased father, I have sworn to revenge myself on Fernand, andГўВҖВ”I have
revenged myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you are sure the unhappy Fernand did that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am satisfied, madame, that he did what I have told you; besides,
that is not much more odious than that a Frenchman by adoption should
pass over to the English; that a Spaniard by birth should have fought
against the Spaniards; that a stipendiary of Ali should have betrayed
and murdered Ali. Compared with such things, what is the letter you
have just read?ГўВҖВ”a loverГўВҖВҷs deception, which the woman who has married
that man ought certainly to forgive; but not so the lover who was to
have married her. Well, the French did not avenge themselves on the
traitor, the Spaniards did not shoot the traitor, Ali in his tomb left
the traitor unpunished; but I, betrayed, sacrificed, buried, have risen
from my tomb, by the grace of God, to punish that man. He sends me for
that purpose, and here I am.ГўВҖВқ

The poor womanГўВҖВҷs head and arms fell; her legs bent under her, and she
fell on her knees.

ГўВҖВңForgive, Edmond, forgive for my sake, who love you still!ГўВҖВқ

The dignity of the wife checked the fervor of the lover and the mother.
Her forehead almost touched the carpet, when the count sprang forward
and raised her. Then seated on a chair, she looked at the manly
countenance of Monte Cristo, on which grief and hatred still impressed
a threatening expression.

ГўВҖВңNot crush that accursed race?ГўВҖВқ murmured he; ГўВҖВңabandon my purpose at the
moment of its accomplishment? Impossible, madame, impossible!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEdmond,ГўВҖВқ said the poor mother, who tried every means, ГўВҖВңwhen I call you
Edmond, why do you not call me MercГғВ©dГғВЁs?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs!ГўВҖВқ repeated Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңMercГғВ©dГғВЁs! Well yes, you are right;
that name has still its charms, and this is the first time for a long
period that I have pronounced it so distinctly. Oh, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, I have
uttered your name with the sigh of melancholy, with the groan of
sorrow, with the last effort of despair; I have uttered it when frozen
with cold, crouched on the straw in my dungeon; I have uttered it,
consumed with heat, rolling on the stone floor of my prison. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs,
I must revenge myself, for I suffered fourteen years,ГўВҖВ”fourteen years I
wept, I cursed; now I tell you, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, I must revenge myself.ГўВҖВқ

The count, fearing to yield to the entreaties of her he had so ardently
loved, called his sufferings to the assistance of his hatred.

ГўВҖВңRevenge yourself, then, Edmond,ГўВҖВқ cried the poor mother; ГўВҖВңbut let your
vengeance fall on the culprits,ГўВҖВ”on him, on me, but not on my son!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is written in the good book,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthat the sins of
the fathers shall fall upon their children to the third and fourth
generation. Since God himself dictated those words to his prophet, why
should I seek to make myself better than God?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEdmond,ГўВҖВқ continued MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, with her arms extended towards the count,
ГўВҖВңsince I first knew you, I have adored your name, have respected your
memory. Edmond, my friend, do not compel me to tarnish that noble and
pure image reflected incessantly on the mirror of my heart. Edmond, if
you knew all the prayers I have addressed to God for you while I
thought you were living and since I have thought you must be dead! Yes,
dead, alas! I imagined your dead body buried at the foot of some gloomy
tower, or cast to the bottom of a pit by hateful jailers, and I wept!
What could I do for you, Edmond, besides pray and weep? Listen; for ten
years I dreamed each night the same dream. I had been told that you had
endeavored to escape; that you had taken the place of another prisoner;
that you had slipped into the winding sheet of a dead body; that you
had been thrown alive from the top of the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf, and that the
cry you uttered as you dashed upon the rocks first revealed to your
jailers that they were your murderers. Well, Edmond, I swear to you, by
the head of that son for whom I entreat your pity,ГўВҖВ”Edmond, for ten
years I saw every night every detail of that frightful tragedy, and for
ten years I heard every night the cry which awoke me, shuddering and
cold. And I, too, EdmondГўВҖВ”oh! believe meГўВҖВ”guilty as I wasГўВҖВ”oh, yes, I,
too, have suffered much!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you known what it is to have your father starve to death in your
absence?ГўВҖВқ cried Monte Cristo, thrusting his hands into his hair; ГўВҖВңhave
you seen the woman you loved giving her hand to your rival, while you
were perishing at the bottom of a dungeon?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ interrupted MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңbut I have seen him whom I loved on the
point of murdering my son.ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs uttered these words with such deep anguish, with an accent of
such intense despair, that Monte Cristo could not restrain a sob. The
lion was daunted; the avenger was conquered.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you ask of me?ГўВҖВқ said he,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңyour sonГўВҖВҷs life? Well, he shall
live!ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs uttered a cry which made the tears start from Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs
eyes; but these tears disappeared almost instantaneously, for,
doubtless, God had sent some angel to collect themГўВҖВ”far more precious
were they in his eyes than the richest pearls of Guzerat and Ophir.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said she, seizing the countГўВҖВҷs hand and raising it to her lips;
ГўВҖВңoh, thank you, thank you, Edmond! Now you are exactly what I dreamt
you were,ГўВҖВ”the man I always loved. Oh, now I may say so!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo much the better,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңas that poor Edmond will
not have long to be loved by you. Death is about to return to the tomb,
the phantom to retire in darkness.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you say, Edmond?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI say, since you command me, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, I must die.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDie? and why so? Who talks of dying? Whence have you these ideas of
death?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do not suppose that, publicly outraged in the face of a whole
theatre, in the presence of your friends and those of your
sonГўВҖВ”challenged by a boy who will glory in my forgiveness as if it were
a victoryГўВҖВ”you do not suppose that I can for one moment wish to live.
What I most loved after you, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, was myself, my dignity, and that
strength which rendered me superior to other men; that strength was my
life. With one word you have crushed it, and I die.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut the duel will not take place, Edmond, since you forgive?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt will take place,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, in a most solemn tone; ГўВҖВңbut
instead of your sonГўВҖВҷs blood to stain the ground, mine will flow.ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs shrieked, and sprang towards Monte Cristo, but, suddenly
stopping, ГўВҖВңEdmond,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңthere is a God above us, since you live
and since I have seen you again; I trust to him from my heart. While
waiting his assistance I trust to your word; you have said that my son
should live, have you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, madame, he shall live,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, surprised that without
more emotion MercГғВ©dГғВЁs had accepted the heroic sacrifice he made for
her. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs extended her hand to the count.

ГўВҖВңEdmond,ГўВҖВқ said she, and her eyes were wet with tears while looking at
him to whom she spoke, ГўВҖВңhow noble it is of you, how great the action
you have just performed, how sublime to have taken pity on a poor woman
who appealed to you with every chance against her, Alas, I am grown old
with grief more than with years, and cannot now remind my Edmond by a
smile, or by a look, of that MercГғВ©dГғВЁs whom he once spent so many hours
in contemplating. Ah, believe me, Edmond, as I told you, I too have
suffered much; I repeat, it is melancholy to pass oneГўВҖВҷs life without
having one joy to recall, without preserving a single hope; but that
proves that all is not yet over. No, it is not finished; I feel it by
what remains in my heart. Oh, I repeat it, Edmond; what you have just
done is beautifulГўВҖВ”it is grand; it is sublime.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you say so now, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs?ГўВҖВ”then what would you say if you knew the
extent of the sacrifice I make to you? Suppose that the Supreme Being,
after having created the world and fertilized chaos, had paused in the
work to spare an angel the tears that might one day flow for mortal
sins from her immortal eyes; suppose that when everything was in
readiness and the moment had come for God to look upon his work and see
that it was goodГўВҖВ”suppose he had snuffed out the sun and tossed the
world back into eternal nightГўВҖВ”thenГўВҖВ”even then, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, you could not
imagine what I lose in sacrificing my life at this moment.ГўВҖВқ

MercГғВ©dГғВЁs looked at the count in a way which expressed at the same time
her astonishment, her admiration, and her gratitude. Monte Cristo
pressed his forehead on his burning hands, as if his brain could no
longer bear alone the weight of its thoughts.

ГўВҖВңEdmond,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңI have but one word more to say to you.ГўВҖВқ

The count smiled bitterly.

ГўВҖВңEdmond,ГўВҖВқ continued she, ГўВҖВңyou will see that if my face is pale, if my
eyes are dull, if my beauty is gone; if MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, in short, no longer
resembles her former self in her features, you will see that her heart
is still the same. Adieu, then, Edmond; I have nothing more to ask of
heavenГўВҖВ”I have seen you again, and have found you as noble and as great
as formerly you were. Adieu, Edmond, adieu, and thank you.ГўВҖВқ

But the count did not answer. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs opened the door of the study and
had disappeared before he had recovered from the painful and profound
reverie into which his thwarted vengeance had plunged him.

The clock of the Invalides struck one when the carriage which conveyed
Madame de Morcerf rolled away on the pavement of the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es,
and made Monte Cristo raise his head.

ГўВҖВңWhat a fool I was,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңnot to tear my heart out on the day when
I resolved to avenge myself!ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 90. The Meeting

After MercГғВ©dГғВЁs had left Monte Cristo, he fell into profound gloom.
Around him and within him the flight of thought seemed to have stopped;
his energetic mind slumbered, as the body does after extreme fatigue.

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ said he to himself, while the lamp and the wax lights were
nearly burnt out, and the servants were waiting impatiently in the
anteroom; ГўВҖВңwhat? this edifice which I have been so long preparing,
which I have reared with so much care and toil, is to be crushed by a
single touch, a word, a breath! Yes, this self, of whom I thought so
much, of whom I was so proud, who had appeared so worthless in the
dungeons of the ChГғВўteau dГўВҖВҷIf, and whom I had succeeded in making so
great, will be but a lump of clay tomorrow. Alas, it is not the death
of the body I regret; for is not the destruction of the vital
principle, the repose to which everything is tending, to which every
unhappy being aspires,ГўВҖВ”is not this the repose of matter after which I
so long sighed, and which I was seeking to attain by the painful
process of starvation when Faria appeared in my dungeon? What is death
for me? One step farther into rest,ГўВҖВ”two, perhaps, into silence. No, it
is not existence, then, that I regret, but the ruin of projects so
slowly carried out, so laboriously framed. Providence is now opposed to
them, when I most thought it would be propitious. It is not GodГўВҖВҷs will
that they should be accomplished. This burden, almost as heavy as a
world, which I had raised, and I had thought to bear to the end, was
too great for my strength, and I was compelled to lay it down in the
middle of my career. Oh, shall I then, again become a fatalist, whom
fourteen years of despair and ten of hope had rendered a believer in
Providence?

ГўВҖВңAnd all thisГўВҖВ”all this, because my heart, which I thought dead, was
only sleeping; because it has awakened and has begun to beat again,
because I have yielded to the pain of the emotion excited in my breast
by a womanГўВҖВҷs voice.

ГўВҖВңYet,ГўВҖВқ continued the count, becoming each moment more absorbed in the
anticipation of the dreadful sacrifice for the morrow, which MercГғВ©dГғВЁs
had accepted, ГўВҖВңyet, it is impossible that so noble-minded a woman
should thus through selfishness consent to my death when I am in the
prime of life and strength; it is impossible that she can carry to such
a point maternal love, or rather delirium. There are virtues which
become crimes by exaggeration. No, she must have conceived some
pathetic scene; she will come and throw herself between us; and what
would be sublime here will there appear ridiculous.ГўВҖВқ

The blush of pride mounted to the countГўВҖВҷs forehead as this thought
passed through his mind.

ГўВҖВңRidiculous?ГўВҖВқ repeated he; ГўВҖВңand the ridicule will fall on me. I
ridiculous? No, I would rather die.ГўВҖВқ

By thus exaggerating to his own mind the anticipated ill-fortune of the
next day, to which he had condemned himself by promising MercГғВ©dГғВЁs to
spare her son, the count at last exclaimed:

ГўВҖВңFolly, folly, folly!ГўВҖВ”to carry generosity so far as to put myself up as
a mark for that young man to aim at. He will never believe that my
death was suicide; and yet it is important for the honor of my
memory,ГўВҖВ”and this surely is not vanity, but a justifiable pride,ГўВҖВ”it is
important the world should know that I have consented, by my free will,
to stop my arm, already raised to strike, and that with the arm which
has been so powerful against others I have struck myself. It must be;
it shall be.ГўВҖВқ

Seizing a pen, he drew a paper from a secret drawer in his desk, and
wrote at the bottom of the document (which was no other than his will,
made since his arrival in Paris) a sort of codicil, clearly explaining
the nature of his death.

ГўВҖВңI do this, Oh, my God,ГўВҖВқ said he, with his eyes raised to heaven, ГўВҖВңas
much for thy honor as for mine. I have during ten years considered
myself the agent of thy vengeance, and other wretches, like Morcerf,
Danglars, Villefort, even Morcerf himself, must not imagine that chance
has freed them from their enemy. Let them know, on the contrary, that
their punishment, which had been decreed by Providence, is only delayed
by my present determination, and although they escape it in this world,
it awaits them in another, and that they are only exchanging time for
eternity.ГўВҖВқ

While he was thus agitated by gloomy uncertainties,ГўВҖВ”wretched waking
dreams of grief,ГўВҖВ”the first rays of morning pierced his windows, and
shone upon the pale blue paper on which he had just inscribed his
justification of Providence.

It was just five oГўВҖВҷclock in the morning when a slight noise like a
stifled sigh reached his ear. He turned his head, looked around him,
and saw no one; but the sound was repeated distinctly enough to
convince him of its reality.

He arose, and quietly opening the door of the drawing-room, saw HaydГғВ©e,
who had fallen on a chair, with her arms hanging down and her beautiful
head thrown back. She had been standing at the door, to prevent his
going out without seeing her, until sleep, which the young cannot
resist, had overpowered her frame, wearied as she was with watching.
The noise of the door did not awaken her, and Monte Cristo gazed at her
with affectionate regret.

ГўВҖВңShe remembered that she had a son,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңand I forgot I had a
daughter.ГўВҖВқ Then, shaking his head sorrowfully, ГўВҖВңPoor HaydГғВ©e,ГўВҖВқ said he;
ГўВҖВңshe wished to see me, to speak to me; she has feared or guessed
something. Oh, I cannot go without taking leave of her; I cannot die
without confiding her to someone.ГўВҖВқ

He quietly regained his seat, and wrote under the other lines:

ГўВҖВңI bequeath to Maximilian Morrel, captain of Spahis,ГўВҖВ”and son of my
former patron, Pierre Morrel, shipowner at Marseilles,ГўВҖВ”the sum of
twenty millions, a part of which may be offered to his sister Julie and
brother-in-law Emmanuel, if he does not fear this increase of fortune
may mar their happiness. These twenty millions are concealed in my
grotto at Monte Cristo, of which Bertuccio knows the secret. If his
heart is free, and he will marry HaydГғВ©e, the daughter of Ali Pasha of
Yanina, whom I have brought up with the love of a father, and who has
shown the love and tenderness of a daughter for me, he will thus
accomplish my last wish. This will has already constituted HaydГғВ©e
heiress of the rest of my fortune, consisting of lands, funds in
England, Austria, and Holland, furniture in my different palaces and
houses, and which without the twenty millions and the legacies to my
servants, may still amount to sixty millions.ГўВҖВқ


40241m



He was finishing the last line when a cry behind him made him start,
and the pen fell from his hand.

ГўВҖВңHaydГғВ©e,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңdid you read it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, my lord,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңwhy are you writing thus at such an hour? Why
are you bequeathing all your fortune to me? Are you going to leave me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am going on a journey, dear child,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, with an
expression of infinite tenderness and melancholy; ГўВҖВңand if any
misfortune should happen to meГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

The count stopped.

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ asked the young girl, with an authoritative tone the count had
never observed before, and which startled him.

ГўВҖВңWell, if any misfortune happen to me,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI wish
my daughter to be happy.ГўВҖВқ HaydГғВ©e smiled sorrowfully, and shook her
head.

ГўВҖВңDo you think of dying, my lord?ГўВҖВқ said she.

ГўВҖВңThe wise man, my child, has said, ГўВҖВҳIt is good to think of death.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, if you die,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңbequeath your fortune to others, for if
you die I shall require nothing;ГўВҖВқ and, taking the paper, she tore it in
four pieces, and threw it into the middle of the room. Then, the effort
having exhausted her strength, she fell, not asleep this time, but
fainting on the floor.

The count leaned over her and raised her in his arms; and seeing that
sweet pale face, those lovely eyes closed, that beautiful form
motionless and to all appearance lifeless, the idea occurred to him for
the first time, that perhaps she loved him otherwise than as a daughter
loves a father.

ГўВҖВңAlas,ГўВҖВқ murmured he, with intense suffering, ГўВҖВңI might, then, have been
happy yet.ГўВҖВқ

Then he carried HaydГғВ©e to her room, resigned her to the care of her
attendants, and returning to his study, which he shut quickly this
time, he again copied the destroyed will. As he was finishing, the
sound of a cabriolet entering the yard was heard. Monte Cristo
approached the window, and saw Maximilian and Emmanuel alight. ГўВҖВңGood,ГўВҖВқ
said he; ГўВҖВңit was time,ГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”and he sealed his will with three seals.

A moment afterwards he heard a noise in the drawing-room, and went to
open the door himself. Morrel was there; he had come twenty minutes
before the time appointed.

ГўВҖВңI am perhaps come too soon, count,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңbut I frankly
acknowledge that I have not closed my eyes all night, nor has anyone in
my house. I need to see you strong in your courageous assurance, to
recover myself.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo could not resist this proof of affection; he not only
extended his hand to the young man, but flew to him with open arms.

ГўВҖВңMorrel,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңit is a happy day for me, to feel that I am beloved
by such a man as you. Good-morning, Emmanuel; you will come with me
then, Maximilian?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you doubt it?ГўВҖВқ said the young captain.

ГўВҖВңBut if I were wrongГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI watched you during the whole scene of that challenge yesterday; I
have been thinking of your firmness all night, and I said to myself
that justice must be on your side, or manГўВҖВҷs countenance is no longer to
be relied on.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, Morrel, Albert is your friend?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSimply an acquaintance, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou met on the same day you first saw me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, that is true; but I should not have recollected it if you had not
reminded me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you, Morrel.ГўВҖВқ Then ringing the bell once, ГўВҖВңLook.ГўВҖВқ said he to
Ali, who came immediately, ГўВҖВңtake that to my solicitor. It is my will,
Morrel. When I am dead, you will go and examine it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңyou dead?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; must I not be prepared for everything, dear friend? But what did
you do yesterday after you left me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI went to TortoniГўВҖВҷs, where, as I expected, I found Beauchamp and
ChГғВўteau-Renaud. I own I was seeking them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, when all was arranged?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen, count; the affair is serious and unavoidable.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you doubt it!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; the offence was public, and everyone is already talking of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I hoped to get an exchange of arms,ГўВҖВ”to substitute the sword for
the pistol; the pistol is blind.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you succeeded?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo quickly, with an imperceptible
gleam of hope.

ГўВҖВңNo; for your skill with the sword is so well known.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh?ГўВҖВ”who has betrayed me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe skilful swordsman whom you have conquered.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you failed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey positively refused.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMorrel,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңhave you ever seen me fire a pistol?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNever.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, we have time; look.ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo took the pistols he held in
his hand when MercГғВ©dГғВЁs entered, and fixing an ace of clubs against the
iron plate, with four shots he successively shot off the four sides of
the club. At each shot Morrel turned pale. He examined the bullets with
which Monte Cristo performed this dexterous feat, and saw that they
were no larger than buckshot.

ГўВҖВңIt is astonishing,ГўВҖВқ said he. ГўВҖВңLook, Emmanuel.ГўВҖВқ Then turning towards
Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңCount,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңin the name of all that is dear to
you, I entreat you not to kill Albert!ГўВҖВ”the unhappy youth has a mother.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңand I have none.ГўВҖВқ These words were
uttered in a tone which made Morrel shudder.

ГўВҖВңYou are the offended party, count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless; what does that imply?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat you will fire first.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI fire first?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I obtained, or rather claimed that; we had conceded enough for
them to yield us that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd at what distance?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTwenty paces.ГўВҖВқ A smile of terrible import passed over the countГўВҖВҷs
lips.

ГўВҖВңMorrel,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңdo not forget what you have just seen.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe only chance for AlbertГўВҖВҷs safety, then, will arise from your
emotion.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI suffer from emotion?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңOr from your generosity, my friend; to so good a marksman as you are,
I may say what would appear absurd to another.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBreak his armГўВҖВ”wound himГўВҖВ”but do not kill him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will tell you, Morrel,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңthat I do not need
entreating to spare the life of M. de Morcerf; he shall be so well
spared, that he will return quietly with his two friends, while IГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat will be another thing; I shall be brought home.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, no,ГўВҖВқ cried Maximilian, quite unable to restrain his feelings.

ГўВҖВңAs I told you, my dear Morrel, M. de Morcerf will kill me.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel looked at him in utter amazement. ГўВҖВңBut what has happened, then,
since last evening, count?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe same thing that happened to Brutus the night before the battle of
Philippi; I have seen a ghost.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd that ghostГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTold me, Morrel, that I had lived long enough.ГўВҖВқ

Maximilian and Emmanuel looked at each other. Monte Cristo drew out his
watch. ГўВҖВңLet us go,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңit is five minutes past seven, and the
appointment was for eight oГўВҖВҷclock.ГўВҖВқ

A carriage was in readiness at the door. Monte Cristo stepped into it
with his two friends. He had stopped a moment in the passage to listen
at a door, and Maximilian and Emmanuel, who had considerately passed
forward a few steps, thought they heard him answer by a sigh to a sob
from within. As the clock struck eight they drove up to the place of
meeting.

ГўВҖВңWe are first,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, looking out of the window.

ГўВҖВңExcuse me, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Baptistin, who had followed his master with
indescribable terror, ГўВҖВңbut I think I see a carriage down there under
the trees.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo sprang lightly from the carriage, and offered his hand to
assist Emmanuel and Maximilian. The latter retained the countГўВҖВҷs hand
between his.

ГўВҖВңI like,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңto feel a hand like this, when its owner relies on
the goodness of his cause.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt seems to me,ГўВҖВқ said Emmanuel, ГўВҖВңthat I see two young men down there,
who are evidently, waiting.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo drew Morrel a step or two behind his brother-in-law.

ГўВҖВңMaximilian,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңare your affections disengaged?ГўВҖВқ Morrel looked
at Monte Cristo with astonishment. ГўВҖВңI do not seek your confidence, my
dear friend. I only ask you a simple question; answer it;ГўВҖВ”that is all I
require.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI love a young girl, count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you love her much?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMore than my life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnother hope defeated!ГўВҖВқ said the count. Then, with a sigh, ГўВҖВңPoor
HaydГғВ©e!ГўВҖВқ murmured he.

ГўВҖВңTo tell the truth, count, if I knew less of you, I should think that
you were less brave than you are.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause I sigh when thinking of someone I am leaving? Come, Morrel, it
is not like a soldier to be so bad a judge of courage. Do I regret
life? What is it to me, who have passed twenty years between life and
death? Moreover, do not alarm yourself, Morrel; this weakness, if it is
such, is betrayed to you alone. I know the world is a drawing-room,
from which we must retire politely and honestly; that is, with a bow,
and our debts of honor paid.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is to the purpose. Have you brought your arms?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВ”what for? I hope these gentlemen have theirs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will inquire,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel.

ГўВҖВңDo; but make no treatyГўВҖВ”you understand me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou need not fear.ГўВҖВқ Morrel advanced towards Beauchamp and
ChГғВўteau-Renaud, who, seeing his intention, came to meet him. The three
young men bowed to each other courteously, if not affably.

ГўВҖВңExcuse me, gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңbut I do not see M. de Morcerf.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe sent us word this morning,ГўВҖВқ replied ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңthat he would
meet us on the ground.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel. Beauchamp pulled out his watch.

ГўВҖВңIt is only five minutes past eight,ГўВҖВқ said he to Morrel; ГўВҖВңthere is not
much time lost yet.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I made no allusion of that kind,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel.

ГўВҖВңThere is a carriage coming,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud. It advanced rapidly
along one of the avenues leading towards the open space where they were
assembled.

ГўВҖВңYou are doubtless provided with pistols, gentlemen? M. de Monte Cristo
yields his right of using his.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe had anticipated this kindness on the part of the count,ГўВҖВқ said
Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңand I have brought some weapons which I bought eight or ten
days since, thinking to want them on a similar occasion. They are quite
new, and have not yet been used. Will you examine them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, M. Beauchamp, if you assure me that M. de Morcerf does not know
these pistols, you may readily believe that your word will be quite
sufficient.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңit is not Morcerf coming in that
carriage;ГўВҖВ”faith, it is Franz and Debray!ГўВҖВқ

The two young men he announced were indeed approaching. ГўВҖВңWhat chance
brings you here, gentlemen?ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, shaking hands with
each of them.

ГўВҖВңBecause,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңAlbert sent this morning to request us to
come.ГўВҖВқ Beauchamp and ChГғВўteau-Renaud exchanged looks of astonishment. ГўВҖВңI
think I understand his reason,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel.

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYesterday afternoon I received a letter from M. de Morcerf, begging me
to attend the Opera.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I,ГўВҖВқ said Debray.

ГўВҖВңAnd I also,ГўВҖВқ said Franz.

ГўВҖВңAnd we, too,ГўВҖВқ added Beauchamp and ChГғВўteau-Renaud.

ГўВҖВңHaving wished you all to witness the challenge, he now wishes you to
be present at the combat.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExactly so,ГўВҖВқ said the young men; ГўВҖВңyou have probably guessed right.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut, after all these arrangements, he does not come himself,ГўВҖВқ said
ChГғВўteau-Renaud. ГўВҖВңAlbert is ten minutes after time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThere he comes,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңon horseback, at full gallop,
followed by a servant.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow imprudent,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңto come on horseback to fight a
duel with pistols, after all the instructions I had given him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd besides,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңwith a collar above his cravat, an open
coat and white waistcoat! Why has he not painted a spot upon his
heart?ГўВҖВ”it would have been more simple.ГўВҖВқ

Meanwhile Albert had arrived within ten paces of the group formed by
the five young men. He jumped from his horse, threw the bridle on his
servantГўВҖВҷs arms, and joined them. He was pale, and his eyes were red and
swollen; it was evident that he had not slept. A shade of melancholy
gravity overspread his countenance, which was not natural to him.

ГўВҖВңI thank you, gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңfor having complied with my
request; I feel extremely grateful for this mark of friendship.ГўВҖВқ Morrel
had stepped back as Morcerf approached, and remained at a short
distance. ГўВҖВңAnd to you also, M. Morrel, my thanks are due. Come, there
cannot be too many.ГўВҖВқ

40248m



ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian, ГўВҖВңyou are not perhaps aware that I am M. de
Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs friend?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was not sure, but I thought it might be so. So much the better; the
more honorable men there are here the better I shall be satisfied.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңM. Morrel,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңwill you apprise the Count of Monte
Cristo that M. de Morcerf is arrived, and we are at his disposal?ГўВҖВқ

Morrel was preparing to fulfil his commission. Beauchamp had meanwhile
drawn the box of pistols from the carriage.

ГўВҖВңStop, gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said Albert; ГўВҖВңI have two words to say to the Count
of Monte Cristo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn private?ГўВҖВқ asked Morrel.

ГўВҖВңNo, sir; before all who are here.ГўВҖВқ

AlbertГўВҖВҷs witnesses looked at each other. Franz and Debray exchanged
some words in a whisper, and Morrel, rejoiced at this unexpected
incident, went to fetch the count, who was walking in a retired path
with Emmanuel.

ГўВҖВңWhat does he want with me?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңI do not know, but he wishes to speak to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI trust he is not going to tempt me by some
fresh insult!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not think that such is his intention,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel.

The count advanced, accompanied by Maximilian and Emmanuel. His calm
and serene look formed a singular contrast to AlbertГўВҖВҷs grief-stricken
face, who approached also, followed by the other four young men.

When at three paces distant from each other, Albert and the count
stopped.

ГўВҖВңApproach, gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said Albert; ГўВҖВңI wish you not to lose one word of
what I am about to have the honor of saying to the Count of Monte
Cristo, for it must be repeated by you to all who will listen to it,
strange as it may appear to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProceed, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, at first with a tremulous voice, but which
gradually became firmer, ГўВҖВңI reproached you with exposing the conduct of
M. de Morcerf in Epirus, for guilty as I knew he was, I thought you had
no right to punish him; but I have since learned that you had that
right. It is not Fernand MondegoГўВҖВҷs treachery towards Ali Pasha which
induces me so readily to excuse you, but the treachery of the fisherman
Fernand towards you, and the almost unheard-of miseries which were its
consequences; and I say, and proclaim it publicly, that you were
justified in revenging yourself on my father, and I, his son, thank you
for not using greater severity.ГўВҖВқ

Had a thunderbolt fallen in the midst of the spectators of this
unexpected scene, it would not have surprised them more than did
AlbertГўВҖВҷs declaration. As for Monte Cristo, his eyes slowly rose towards
heaven with an expression of infinite gratitude. He could not
understand how AlbertГўВҖВҷs fiery nature, of which he had seen so much
among the Roman bandits, had suddenly stooped to this humiliation. He
recognized the influence of MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, and saw why her noble heart had
not opposed the sacrifice she knew beforehand would be useless.

ГўВҖВңNow, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Albert, ГўВҖВңif you think my apology sufficient, pray give
me your hand. Next to the merit of infallibility which you appear to
possess, I rank that of candidly acknowledging a fault. But this
confession concerns me only. I acted well as a man, but you have acted
better than man. An angel alone could have saved one of us from
deathГўВҖВ”that angel came from heaven, if not to make us friends (which,
alas, fatality renders impossible), at least to make us esteem each
other.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo, with moistened eye, heaving breast, and lips half open,
extended to Albert a hand which the latter pressed with a sentiment
resembling respectful fear.

ГўВҖВңGentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңM. de Monte Cristo receives my apology. I had
acted hastily towards him. Hasty actions are generally bad ones. Now my
fault is repaired. I hope the world will not call me cowardly for
acting as my conscience dictated. But if anyone should entertain a
false opinion of me,ГўВҖВқ added he, drawing himself up as if he would
challenge both friends and enemies, ГўВҖВңI shall endeavor to correct his
mistake.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat happened during the night?ГўВҖВқ asked Beauchamp of ChГғВўteau-Renaud;
ГўВҖВңwe appear to make a very sorry figure here.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn truth, what Albert has just done is either very despicable or very
noble,ГўВҖВқ replied the baron.

ГўВҖВңWhat can it mean?ГўВҖВқ said Debray to Franz.

ГўВҖВңThe Count of Monte Cristo acts dishonorably to M. de Morcerf, and is
justified by his son! Had I ten Yaninas in my family, I should only
consider myself the more bound to fight ten times.ГўВҖВқ

As for Monte Cristo, his head was bent down, his arms were powerless.
Bowing under the weight of twenty-four yearsГўВҖВҷ reminiscences, he thought
not of Albert, of Beauchamp, of ChГғВўteau-Renaud, or of any of that
group; but he thought of that courageous woman who had come to plead
for her sonГўВҖВҷs life, to whom he had offered his, and who had now saved
it by the revelation of a dreadful family secret, capable of destroying
forever in that young manГўВҖВҷs heart every feeling of filial piety.

ГўВҖВңProvidence still,ГўВҖВқ murmured he; ГўВҖВңnow only am I fully convinced of
being the emissary of God!ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 91. Mother and Son

The Count of Monte Cristo bowed to the five young men with a melancholy
and dignified smile, and got into his carriage with Maximilian and
Emmanuel. Albert, Beauchamp, and ChГғВўteau-Renaud remained alone. Albert
looked at his two friends, not timidly, but in a way that appeared to
ask their opinion of what he had just done.

ГўВҖВңIndeed, my dear friend,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp first, who had either the most
feeling or the least dissimulation, ГўВҖВңallow me to congratulate you; this
is a very unhoped-for conclusion of a very disagreeable affair.ГўВҖВқ

Albert remained silent and wrapped in thought. ChГғВўteau-Renaud contented
himself with tapping his boot with his flexible cane.

ГўВҖВңAre we not going?ГўВҖВқ said he, after this embarrassing silence.

ГўВҖВңWhen you please,ГўВҖВқ replied Beauchamp; ГўВҖВңallow me only to compliment M.
de Morcerf, who has given proof today of rare chivalric generosity.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud.

ГўВҖВңIt is magnificent,ГўВҖВқ continued Beauchamp, ГўВҖВңto be able to exercise so
much self-control!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAssuredly; as for me, I should have been incapable of it,ГўВҖВқ said
ChГғВўteau-Renaud, with most significant coolness.

ГўВҖВңGentlemen,ГўВҖВқ interrupted Albert, ГўВҖВңI think you did not understand that
something very serious had passed between M. de Monte Cristo and
myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPossibly, possibly,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp immediately; ГўВҖВңbut every simpleton
would not be able to understand your heroism, and sooner or later you
will find yourself compelled to explain it to them more energetically
than would be convenient to your bodily health and the duration of your
life. May I give you a friendly counsel? Set out for Naples, the Hague,
or St. PetersburgГўВҖВ”calm countries, where the point of honor is better
understood than among our hot-headed Parisians. Seek quietude and
oblivion, so that you may return peaceably to France after a few years.
Am I not right, M. de ChГғВўteau-Renaud?ГўВҖВқ

40252m



ГўВҖВңThat is quite my opinion,ГўВҖВқ said the gentleman; ГўВҖВңnothing induces
serious duels so much as a duel forsworn.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you, gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert, with a smile of indifference;
ГўВҖВңI shall follow your adviceГўВҖВ”not because you give it, but because I had
before intended to quit France. I thank you equally for the service you
have rendered me in being my seconds. It is deeply engraved on my
heart, and, after what you have just said, I remember that only.ГўВҖВқ

ChГғВўteau-Renaud and Beauchamp looked at each other; the impression was
the same on both of them, and the tone in which Morcerf had just
expressed his thanks was so determined that the position would have
become embarrassing for all if the conversation had continued.

ГўВҖВңGood-bye, Albert,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp suddenly, carelessly extending his
hand to the young man. The latter did not appear to arouse from his
lethargy; in fact, he did not notice the offered hand.

ГўВҖВңGood-bye,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud in his turn, keeping his little cane in
his left hand, and saluting with his right.

AlbertГўВҖВҷs lips scarcely whispered ГўВҖВңGood-bye,ГўВҖВқ but his look was more
explicit; it expressed a whole poem of restrained anger, proud disdain,
and generous indignation. He preserved his melancholy and motionless
position for some time after his two friends had regained their
carriage; then suddenly unfastening his horse from the little tree to
which his servant had tied it, he mounted and galloped off in the
direction of Paris.

In a quarter of an hour he was entering the house in the Rue du Helder.
As he alighted, he thought he saw his fatherГўВҖВҷs pale face behind the
curtain of the countГўВҖВҷs bedroom. Albert turned away his head with a
sigh, and went to his own apartments. He cast one lingering look on all
the luxuries which had rendered life so easy and so happy since his
infancy; he looked at the pictures, whose faces seemed to smile, and
the landscapes, which appeared painted in brighter colors. Then he took
away his motherГўВҖВҷs portrait, with its oaken frame, leaving the gilt
frame from which he took it black and empty. Then he arranged all his
beautiful Turkish arms, his fine English guns, his Japanese china, his
cups mounted in silver, his artistic bronzes by FeuchГғВЁres or Barye;
examined the cupboards, and placed the key in each; threw into a drawer
of his secretaire, which he left open, all the pocket-money he had
about him, and with it the thousand fancy jewels from his vases and his
jewel-boxes; then he made an exact inventory of everything, and placed
it in the most conspicuous part of the table, after putting aside the
books and papers which had collected there.

At the beginning of this work, his servant, notwithstanding orders to
the contrary, came to his room.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you want?ГўВҖВқ asked he, with a more sorrowful than angry tone.

ГўВҖВңPardon me, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied the valet; ГўВҖВңyou had forbidden me to disturb
you, but the Count of Morcerf has called me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell!ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңI did not like to go to him without first seeing you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause the count is doubtless aware that I accompanied you to the
meeting this morning.ГўВҖВқ

40254m



ГўВҖВңIt is probable,ГўВҖВқ said Albert.

ГўВҖВңAnd since he has sent for me, it is doubtless to question me on what
happened there. What must I answer?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe truth.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I shall say the duel did not take place?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will say I apologized to the Count of Monte Cristo. Go.ГўВҖВқ

The valet bowed and retired, and Albert returned to his inventory. As
he was finishing this work, the sound of horses prancing in the yard,
and the wheels of a carriage shaking his window, attracted his
attention. He approached the window, and saw his father get into it,
and drive away. The door was scarcely closed when Albert bent his steps
to his motherГўВҖВҷs room; and, no one being there to announce him, he
advanced to her bedchamber, and distressed by what he saw and guessed,
stopped for one moment at the door.

As if the same idea had animated these two beings, MercГғВ©dГғВЁs was doing
the same in her apartments that he had just done in his. Everything was
in order,ГўВҖВ”laces, dresses, jewels, linen, money, all were arranged in
the drawers, and the countess was carefully collecting the keys. Albert
saw all these preparations and understood them, and exclaiming, ГўВҖВңMy
mother!ГўВҖВқ he threw his arms around her neck.

The artist who could have depicted the expression of these two
countenances would certainly have made of them a beautiful picture. All
these proofs of an energetic resolution, which Albert did not fear on
his own account, alarmed him for his mother. ГўВҖВңWhat are you doing?ГўВҖВқ
asked he.

ГўВҖВңWhat were you doing?ГўВҖВқ replied she.

ГўВҖВңOh, my mother!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Albert, so overcome he could scarcely speak;
ГўВҖВңit is not the same with you and meГўВҖВ”you cannot have made the same
resolution I have, for I have come to warn you that I bid adieu to your
house, andГўВҖВ”and to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI also,ГўВҖВқ replied MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңam going, and I acknowledge I had depended
on your accompanying me; have I deceived myself?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMother,ГўВҖВқ said Albert with firmness. ГўВҖВңI cannot make you share the fate
I have planned for myself. I must live henceforth without rank and
fortune, and to begin this hard apprenticeship I must borrow from a
friend the loaf I shall eat until I have earned one. So, my dear
mother, I am going at once to ask Franz to lend me the small sum I
shall require to supply my present wants.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou, my poor child, suffer poverty and hunger? Oh, do not say so; it
will break my resolutions.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut not mine, mother,ГўВҖВқ replied Albert. ГўВҖВңI am young and strong; I
believe I am courageous, and since yesterday I have learned the power
of will. Alas, my dear mother, some have suffered so much, and yet
live, and have raised a new fortune on the ruin of all the promises of
happiness which heaven had made themГўВҖВ”on the fragments of all the hope
which God had given them! I have seen that, mother; I know that from
the gulf in which their enemies have plunged them they have risen with
so much vigor and glory that in their turn they have ruled their former
conquerors, and have punished them. No, mother; from this moment I have
done with the past, and accept nothing from itГўВҖВ”not even a name, because
you can understand that your son cannot bear the name of a man who
ought to blush for it before another.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlbert, my child,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, ГўВҖВңif I had a stronger heart, that is
the counsel I would have given you; your conscience has spoken when my
voice became too weak; listen to its dictates. You had friends, Albert;
break off their acquaintance. But do not despair; you have life before
you, my dear Albert, for you are yet scarcely twenty-two years old; and
as a pure heart like yours wants a spotless name, take my fatherГўВҖВҷsГўВҖВ”it
was Herrera. I am sure, my dear Albert, whatever may be your career,
you will soon render that name illustrious. Then, my son, return to the
world still more brilliant because of your former sorrows; and if I am
wrong, still let me cherish these hopes, for I have no future to look
forward to. For me the grave opens when I pass the threshold of this
house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will fulfil all your wishes, my dear mother,ГўВҖВқ said the young man.
ГўВҖВңYes, I share your hopes; the anger of Heaven will not pursue us, since
you are pure and I am innocent. But, since our resolution is formed,
let us act promptly. M. de Morcerf went out about half an hour ago; the
opportunity is favorable to avoid an explanation.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am ready, my son,ГўВҖВқ said MercГғВ©dГғВЁs.

Albert ran to fetch a carriage. He recollected that there was a small
furnished house to let in the Rue des Saints-PГғВЁres, where his mother
would find a humble but decent lodging, and thither he intended
conducting the countess. As the carriage stopped at the door, and
Albert was alighting, a man approached and gave him a letter.

Albert recognized the bearer. ГўВҖВңFrom the count,ГўВҖВқ said Bertuccio. Albert
took the letter, opened, and read it, then looked round for Bertuccio,
but he was gone.

He returned to MercГғВ©dГғВЁs with tears in his eyes and heaving breast, and
without uttering a word he gave her the letter. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs read:

ГўВҖВңAlbert,ГўВҖВ”While showing you that I have discovered your plans, I hope
also to convince you of my delicacy. You are free, you leave the
countГўВҖВҷs house, and you take your mother to your home; but reflect,
Albert, you owe her more than your poor noble heart can pay her. Keep
the struggle for yourself, bear all the suffering, but spare her the
trial of poverty which must accompany your first efforts; for she
deserves not even the shadow of the misfortune which has this day
fallen on her, and Providence is not willing that the innocent should
suffer for the guilty. I know you are going to leave the Rue du Helder
without taking anything with you. Do not seek to know how I discovered
it; I know itГўВҖВ”that is sufficient.

ГўВҖВңNow, listen, Albert. Twenty-four years ago I returned, proud and
joyful, to my country. I had a betrothed, Albert, a lovely girl whom I
adored, and I was bringing to my betrothed a hundred and fifty louis,
painfully amassed by ceaseless toil. This money was for her; I destined
it for her, and, knowing the treachery of the sea I buried our treasure
in the little garden of the house my father lived in at Marseilles, on
the AllГғВ©es de Meilhan. Your mother, Albert, knows that poor house well.
A short time since I passed through Marseilles, and went to see the old
place, which revived so many painful recollections; and in the evening
I took a spade and dug in the corner of the garden where I had
concealed my treasure. The iron box was thereГўВҖВ”no one had touched
itГўВҖВ”under a beautiful fig-tree my father had planted the day I was born,
which overshadowed the spot. Well, Albert, this money, which was
formerly designed to promote the comfort and tranquillity of the woman
I adored, may now, through strange and painful circumstances, be
devoted to the same purpose.

ГўВҖВңOh, feel for me, who could offer millions to that poor woman, but who
return her only the piece of black bread forgotten under my poor roof
since the day I was torn from her I loved. You are a generous man,
Albert, but perhaps you may be blinded by pride or resentment; if you
refuse me, if you ask another for what I have a right to offer you, I
will say it is ungenerous of you to refuse the life of your mother at
the hands of a man whose father was allowed by your father to die in
all the horrors of poverty and despair.ГўВҖВқ

Albert stood pale and motionless to hear what his mother would decide
after she had finished reading this letter. MercГғВ©dГғВЁs turned her eyes
with an ineffable look towards heaven.

ГўВҖВңI accept it,ГўВҖВқ said she; ГўВҖВңhe has a right to pay the dowry, which I
shall take with me to some convent!ГўВҖВқ

Putting the letter in her bosom, she took her sonГўВҖВҷs arm, and with a
firmer step than she even herself expected she went downstairs.



 Chapter 92. The Suicide

Meanwhile Monte Cristo had also returned to town with Emmanuel and
Maximilian. Their return was cheerful. Emmanuel did not conceal his joy
at the peaceful termination of the affair, and was loud in his
expressions of delight. Morrel, in a corner of the carriage, allowed
his brother-in-lawГўВҖВҷs gayety to expend itself in words, while he felt
equal inward joy, which, however, betrayed itself only in his
countenance.

At the BarriГғВЁre du TrГғВҙne they met Bertuccio, who was waiting there,
motionless as a sentinel at his post. Monte Cristo put his head out of
the window, exchanged a few words with him in a low tone, and the
steward disappeared.

ГўВҖВңCount,ГўВҖВқ said Emmanuel, when they were at the end of the Place Royale,
ГўВҖВңput me down at my door, that my wife may not have a single moment of
needless anxiety on my account or yours.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIf it were not ridiculous to make a display of our triumph, said
Morrel, I would invite the count to our house; besides that, he
doubtless has some trembling heart to comfort. So we will take leave of
our friend, and let him hasten home.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStop a moment,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңdo not let me lose both my
companions. Return, Emmanuel, to your charming wife, and present my
best compliments to her; and do you, Morrel, accompany me to the
Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWillingly,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian; ГўВҖВңparticularly as I have business in that
quarter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall we wait breakfast for you?ГўВҖВқ asked Emmanuel.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied the young man. The door was closed, and the carriage
proceeded. ГўВҖВңSee what good fortune I brought you!ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, when he
was alone with the count. ГўВҖВңHave you not thought so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңfor that reason I wished to keep you near
me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is miraculous!ГўВҖВқ continued Morrel, answering his own thoughts.

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңWhat has just happened.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said the Count, ГўВҖВңyou are rightГўВҖВ”it is miraculous.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFor Albert is brave,ГўВҖВқ resumed Morrel.

ГўВҖВңVery brave,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI have seen him sleep with a sword
suspended over his head.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd I know he has fought two duels,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel. ГўВҖВңHow can you
reconcile that with his conduct this morning?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAll owing to your influence,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, smiling.

ГўВҖВңIt is well for Albert he is not in the army,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel.

ГўВҖВңWhy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAn apology on the ground!ГўВҖВқ said the young captain, shaking his head.

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said the count mildly, ГўВҖВңdo not entertain the prejudices of
ordinary men, Morrel! Acknowledge, that if Albert is brave, he cannot
be a coward; he must then have had some reason for acting as he did
this morning, and confess that his conduct is more heroic than
otherwise.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless, doubtless,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel; ГўВҖВңbut I shall say, like the
Spaniard, ГўВҖВҳHe has not been so brave today as he was yesterday.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou will breakfast with me, will you not, Morrel?ГўВҖВқ said the count, to
turn the conversation.

ГўВҖВңNo; I must leave you at ten oГўВҖВҷclock.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour engagement was for breakfast, then?ГўВҖВқ said the count.

Morrel smiled, and shook his head.

ГўВҖВңStill you must breakfast somewhere.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut if I am not hungry?ГўВҖВқ said the young man.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңI only know two things which destroy the
appetite,ГўВҖВ”griefГўВҖВ”and as I am happy to see you very cheerful, it is not
thatГўВҖВ”and love. Now after what you told me this morning of your heart, I
may believeГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, count,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel gayly, ГўВҖВңI will not dispute it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you will not make me your confidant, Maximilian?ГўВҖВқ said the count,
in a tone which showed how gladly he would have been admitted to the
secret.

ГўВҖВңI showed you this morning that I had a heart, did I not, count?ГўВҖВқ Monte
Cristo only answered by extending his hand to the young man. ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ
continued the latter, ГўВҖВңsince that heart is no longer with you in the
Bois de Vincennes, it is elsewhere, and I must go and find it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo,ГўВҖВқ said the count deliberately; ГўВҖВңgo, dear friend, but promise me if
you meet with any obstacle to remember that I have some power in this
world, that I am happy to use that power in the behalf of those I love,
and that I love you, Morrel.ГўВҖВқ

40260m



ГўВҖВңI will remember it,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңas selfish children
recollect their parents when they want their aid. When I need your
assistance, and the moment arrives, I will come to you, count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I rely upon your promise. Good-bye, then.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGood-bye, till we meet again.ГўВҖВқ

They had arrived in the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es. Monte Cristo opened the
carriage-door, Morrel sprang out on the pavement, Bertuccio was waiting
on the steps. Morrel disappeared down the Avenue de Marigny, and Monte
Cristo hastened to join Bertuccio.

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ asked he.

ГўВҖВңShe is going to leave her house,ГўВҖВқ said the steward.

ГўВҖВңAnd her son?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңFlorentin, his valet, thinks he is going to do the same.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome this way.ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo took Bertuccio into his study, wrote the
letter we have seen, and gave it to the steward. ГўВҖВңGo,ГўВҖВқ said he quickly.
ГўВҖВңBut first, let HaydГғВ©e be informed that I have returned.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHere I am,ГўВҖВқ said the young girl, who at the sound of the carriage had
run downstairs and whose face was radiant with joy at seeing the count
return safely. Bertuccio left. Every transport of a daughter finding a
father, all the delight of a mistress seeing an adored lover, were felt
by HaydГғВ©e during the first moments of this meeting, which she had so
eagerly expected. Doubtless, although less evident, Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs joy
was not less intense. Joy to hearts which have suffered long is like
the dew on the ground after a long drought; both the heart and the
ground absorb that beneficent moisture falling on them, and nothing is
outwardly apparent.

Monte Cristo was beginning to think, what he had not for a long time
dared to believe, that there were two MercГғВ©dГғВЁs in the world, and he
might yet be happy. His eye, elate with happiness, was reading eagerly
the tearful gaze of HaydГғВ©e, when suddenly the door opened. The count
knit his brow.

ГўВҖВңM. de Morcerf!ГўВҖВқ said Baptistin, as if that name sufficed for his
excuse. In fact, the countГўВҖВҷs face brightened.

ГўВҖВңWhich,ГўВҖВқ asked he, ГўВҖВңthe viscount or the count?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed HaydГғВ©e, ГўВҖВңis it not yet over?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know not if it is finished, my beloved child,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo,
taking the young girlГўВҖВҷs hands; ГўВҖВңbut I do know you have nothing more to
fear.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut it is the wretchedГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat man cannot injure me, HaydГғВ©e,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңit was his son
alone that there was cause to fear.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what I have suffered,ГўВҖВқ said the young girl, ГўВҖВңyou shall never know,
my lord.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo smiled. ГўВҖВңBy my fatherГўВҖВҷs tomb,ГўВҖВқ said he, extending his hand
over the head of the young girl, ГўВҖВңI swear to you, HaydГғВ©e, that if any
misfortune happens, it will not be to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI believe you, my lord, as implicitly as if God had spoken to me,ГўВҖВқ
said the young girl, presenting her forehead to him. Monte Cristo
pressed on that pure beautiful forehead a kiss which made two hearts
throb at once, the one violently, the other secretly.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ murmured the count, ГўВҖВңshall I then be permitted to love again? Ask
M. de Morcerf into the drawing-room,ГўВҖВқ said he to Baptistin, while he
led the beautiful Greek girl to a private staircase.

We must explain this visit, which although expected by Monte Cristo, is
unexpected to our readers. While MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, as we have said, was making
a similar inventory of her property to AlbertГўВҖВҷs, while she was
arranging her jewels, shutting her drawers, collecting her keys, to
leave everything in perfect order, she did not perceive a pale and
sinister face at a glass door which threw light into the passage, from
which everything could be both seen and heard. He who was thus looking,
without being heard or seen, probably heard and saw all that passed in
Madame de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs apartments. From that glass door the pale-faced man
went to the countГўВҖВҷs bedroom and raised with a constricted hand the
curtain of a window overlooking the courtyard. He remained there ten
minutes, motionless and dumb, listening to the beating of his own
heart. For him those ten minutes were very long. It was then Albert,
returning from his meeting with the count, perceived his father
watching for his arrival behind a curtain, and turned aside. The
countГўВҖВҷs eye expanded; he knew Albert had insulted the count dreadfully,
and that in every country in the world such an insult would lead to a
deadly duel. Albert returned safelyГўВҖВ”then the count was revenged.

An indescribable ray of joy illumined that wretched countenance like
the last ray of the sun before it disappears behind the clouds which
bear the aspect, not of a downy couch, but of a tomb. But as we have
said, he waited in vain for his son to come to his apartment with the
account of his triumph. He easily understood why his son did not come
to see him before he went to avenge his fatherГўВҖВҷs honor; but when that
was done, why did not his son come and throw himself into his arms?

It was then, when the count could not see Albert, that he sent for his
servant, who he knew was authorized not to conceal anything from him.
Ten minutes afterwards, General Morcerf was seen on the steps in a
black coat with a military collar, black pantaloons, and black gloves.
He had apparently given previous orders, for as he reached the bottom
step his carriage came from the coach-house ready for him. The valet
threw into the carriage his military cloak, in which two swords were
wrapped, and, shutting the door, he took his seat by the side of the
coachman. The coachman stooped down for his orders.

ГўВҖВңTo the Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es,ГўВҖВқ said the general; ГўВҖВңthe Count of Monte
CristoГўВҖВҷs. Hurry!ГўВҖВқ

The horses bounded beneath the whip; and in five minutes they stopped
before the countГўВҖВҷs door. M. de Morcerf opened the door himself, and as
the carriage rolled away he passed up the walk, rang, and entered the
open door with his servant.

A moment afterwards, Baptistin announced the Count of Morcerf to Monte
Cristo, and the latter, leading HaydГғВ©e aside, ordered that Morcerf be
asked into the drawing-room. The general was pacing the room the third
time when, in turning, he perceived Monte Cristo at the door.

ГўВҖВңAh, it is M. de Morcerf,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo quietly; ГўВҖВңI thought I had
not heard aright.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, it is I,ГўВҖВқ said the count, whom a frightful contraction of the
lips prevented from articulating freely.

ГўВҖВңMay I know the cause which procures me the pleasure of seeing M. de
Morcerf so early?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHad you not a meeting with my son this morning?ГўВҖВқ asked the general.

ГўВҖВңI had,ГўВҖВқ replied the count.

ГўВҖВңAnd I know my son had good reasons to wish to fight with you, and to
endeavor to kill you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir, he had very good ones; but you see that in spite of them he
has not killed me, and did not even fight.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYet he considered you the cause of his fatherГўВҖВҷs dishonor, the cause of
the fearful ruin which has fallen on my house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is true, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with his dreadful calmness; ГўВҖВңa
secondary cause, but not the principal.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDoubtless you made, then, some apology or explanation?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI explained nothing, and it is he who apologized to me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut to what do you attribute this conduct?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo the conviction, probably, that there was one more guilty than I.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd who was that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHis father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat may be,ГўВҖВқ said the count, turning pale; ГўВҖВңbut you know the guilty
do not like to find themselves convicted.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it, and I expected this result.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou expected my son would be a coward?ГўВҖВқ cried the count.

ГўВҖВңM. Albert de Morcerf is no coward!ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңA man who holds a sword in his hand, and sees a mortal enemy within
reach of that sword, and does not fight, is a coward! Why is he not
here that I may tell him so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo coldly, ГўВҖВңI did not expect that you had come
here to relate to me your little family affairs. Go and tell M. Albert
that, and he may know what to answer you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no, no,ГўВҖВқ said the general, smiling faintly, ГўВҖВңI did not come for
that purpose; you are right. I came to tell you that I also look upon
you as my enemy. I came to tell you that I hate you instinctively; that
it seems as if I had always known you, and always hated you; and, in
short, since the young people of the present day will not fight, it
remains for us to do so. Do you think so, sir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly. And when I told you I had foreseen the result, it is the
honor of your visit I alluded to.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo much the better. Are you prepared?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou know that we shall fight till one of us is dead,ГўВҖВқ said the
general, whose teeth were clenched with rage.

40262m



ГўВҖВңUntil one of us dies,ГўВҖВқ repeated Monte Cristo, moving his head slightly
up and down.

ГўВҖВңLet us start, then; we need no witnesses.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery true,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңit is unnecessary, we know each other
so well!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңwe know so little of each other.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, with the same indomitable coolness; ГўВҖВңlet
us see. Are you not the soldier Fernand who deserted on the eve of the
battle of Waterloo? Are you not the Lieutenant Fernand who served as
guide and spy to the French army in Spain? Are you not the Captain
Fernand who betrayed, sold, and murdered his benefactor, Ali? And have
not all these Fernands, united, made Lieutenant-General, the Count of
Morcerf, peer of France?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ cried the general, as if branded with a hot iron, ГўВҖВңwretch,ГўВҖВ”to
reproach me with my shame when about, perhaps, to kill me! No, I did
not say I was a stranger to you. I know well, demon, that you have
penetrated into the darkness of the past, and that you have read, by
the light of what torch I know not, every page of my life; but perhaps
I may be more honorable in my shame than you under your pompous
coverings. NoГўВҖВ”no, I am aware you know me; but I know you only as an
adventurer sewn up in gold and jewellery. You call yourself, in Paris,
the Count of Monte Cristo; in Italy, Sinbad the Sailor; in Malta, I
forget what. But it is your real name I want to know, in the midst of
your hundred names, that I may pronounce it when we meet to fight, at
the moment when I plunge my sword through your heart.ГўВҖВқ

The Count of Monte Cristo turned dreadfully pale; his eye seemed to
burn with a devouring fire. He leaped towards a dressing-room near his
bedroom, and in less than a moment, tearing off his cravat, his coat
and waistcoat, he put on a sailorГўВҖВҷs jacket and hat, from beneath which
rolled his long black hair. He returned thus, formidable and
implacable, advancing with his arms crossed on his breast, towards the
general, who could not understand why he had disappeared, but who on
seeing him again, and feeling his teeth chatter and his legs sink under
him, drew back, and only stopped when he found a table to support his
clenched hand.

ГўВҖВңFernand,ГўВҖВқ cried he, ГўВҖВңof my hundred names I need only tell you one, to
overwhelm you! But you guess it now, do you not?ГўВҖВ”or, rather, you
remember it? For, notwithstanding all my sorrows and my tortures, I
show you today a face which the happiness of revenge makes young
againГўВҖВ”a face you must often have seen in your dreams since your
marriage with MercГғВ©dГғВЁs, my betrothed!ГўВҖВқ

40268m



The general, with his head thrown back, hands extended, gaze fixed,
looked silently at this dreadful apparition; then seeking the wall to
support him, he glided along close to it until he reached the door,
through which he went out backwards, uttering this single mournful,
lamentable, distressing cry:

ГўВҖВңEdmond DantГғВЁs!ГўВҖВқ

Then, with sighs which were unlike any human sound, he dragged himself
to the door, reeled across the courtyard, and falling into the arms of
his valet, he said in a voice scarcely intelligible,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңHome, home.ГўВҖВқ

The fresh air and the shame he felt at having exposed himself before
his servants, partly recalled his senses, but the ride was short, and
as he drew near his house all his wretchedness revived. He stopped at a
short distance from the house and alighted. The door was wide open, a
hackney-coach was standing in the middle of the yardГўВҖВ”a strange sight
before so noble a mansion; the count looked at it with terror, but
without daring to inquire its meaning, he rushed towards his apartment.

Two persons were coming down the stairs; he had only time to creep into
an alcove to avoid them. It was MercГғВ©dГғВЁs leaning on her sonГўВҖВҷs arm and
leaving the house. They passed close by the unhappy being, who,
concealed behind the damask curtain, almost felt MercГғВ©dГғВЁs dress brush
past him, and his sonГўВҖВҷs warm breath, pronouncing these words:

ГўВҖВңCourage, mother! Come, this is no longer our home!ГўВҖВқ
