The words died away, the steps were lost in the distance. The general
drew himself up, clinging to the curtain; he uttered the most dreadful
sob which ever escaped from the bosom of a father abandoned at the same
time by his wife and son. He soon heard the clatter of the iron step of
the hackney-coach, then the coachmanГўВҖВҷs voice, and then the rolling of
the heavy vehicle shook the windows. He darted to his bedroom to see
once more all he had loved in the world; but the hackney-coach drove on
and the head of neither MercГғВ©dГғВЁs nor her son appeared at the window to
take a last look at the house or the deserted father and husband.

And at the very moment when the wheels of that coach crossed the
gateway a report was heard, and a thick smoke escaped through one of
the panes of the window, which was broken by the explosion.

40270m




 Chapter 93. Valentine

We may easily conceive where MorrelГўВҖВҷs appointment was. On leaving Monte
Cristo he walked slowly towards VillefortГўВҖВҷs; we say slowly, for Morrel
had more than half an hour to spare to go five hundred steps, but he
had hastened to take leave of Monte Cristo because he wished to be
alone with his thoughts. He knew his time wellГўВҖВ”the hour when Valentine
was giving Noirtier his breakfast, and was sure not to be disturbed in
the performance of this pious duty. Noirtier and Valentine had given
him leave to go twice a week, and he was now availing himself of that
permission.

He arrived; Valentine was expecting him. Uneasy and almost crazed, she
seized his hand and led him to her grandfather. This uneasiness,
amounting almost to frenzy, arose from the report MorcerfГўВҖВҷs adventure
had made in the world, for the affair at the Opera was generally known.
No one at VillefortГўВҖВҷs doubted that a duel would ensue from it.
Valentine, with her womanГўВҖВҷs instinct, guessed that Morrel would be
Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs second, and from the young manГўВҖВҷs well-known courage and

she knew that the termination of this affair was as happy as it was
unexpected.

ГўВҖВңNow,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, motioning to Morrel to sit down near her

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian, ГўВҖВңI recollect the project, of which I highly

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңyou may approve again, for grandpapa is again

ГўВҖВңBravo,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian.

40272m

ГўВҖВңOh, it must not be called suffering; I feel a general uneasiness, that

Maximilian, in his devotedness, gazed silently at her. She was very
beautiful, but her usual pallor had increased; her eyes were more

40274m

Barrois, ran in at the same moment. Valentine was so pale, so cold, so

seized with the fear which pervaded that house, and they flew into the
passage crying for help. Madame Danglars and EugГғВ©nie were going out at
that moment; they heard the cause of the disturbance.

ГўВҖВңI told you so!ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Madame de Villefort. ГўВҖВңPoor child!ГўВҖВқ

40278m




 Chapter 94. MaximilianГўВҖВҷs Avowal

At the same moment M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs voice was heard calling from his
study, ГўВҖВңWhat is the matter?ГўВҖВқ

Morrel looked at Noirtier who had recovered his self-command, and with
a glance indicated the closet where once before under somewhat similar
circumstances, he had taken refuge. He had only time to get his hat and
throw himself breathless into the closet when the procureurГўВҖВҷs footstep
was heard in the passage.

Villefort sprang into the room, ran to Valentine, and took her in his
arms.

ГўВҖВңA physician, a physician,ГўВҖВ”M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny!ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort; ГўВҖВңor rather I
will go for him myself.ГўВҖВқ

He flew from the apartment, and Morrel at the same moment darted out at
the other door. He had been struck to the heart by a frightful
recollectionГўВҖВ”the conversation he had heard between the doctor and
Villefort the night of Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs death, recurred to him;
these symptoms, to a less alarming extent, were the same which had
preceded the death of Barrois. At the same time Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs voice
seemed to resound in his ear with the words he had heard only two hours
before, ГўВҖВңWhatever you want, Morrel, come to me; I have great power.ГўВҖВқ

More rapidly than thought, he darted down the Rue Matignon, and thence
to the Avenue des Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es.

Meanwhile M. de Villefort arrived in a hired cabriolet at M.
dГўВҖВҷAvrignyГўВҖВҷs door. He rang so violently that the porter was alarmed.
Villefort ran upstairs without saying a word. The porter knew him, and
let him pass, only calling to him:

ГўВҖВңIn his study, Monsieur ProcureurГўВҖВ”in his study!ГўВҖВқ Villefort pushed, or
rather forced, the door open.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said the doctor, ГўВҖВңis it you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, closing the door after him, ГўВҖВңit is I, who am
come in my turn to ask you if we are quite alone. Doctor, my house is
accursed!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВқ said the latter with apparent coolness, but with deep emotion,
ГўВҖВңhave you another invalid?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, doctor,ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort, clutching his hair, ГўВҖВңyes!ГўВҖВқ

DГўВҖВҷAvrignyГўВҖВҷs look implied, ГўВҖВңI told you it would be so.ГўВҖВқ Then he slowly
uttered these words, ГўВҖВңWho is now dying in your house? What new victim
is going to accuse you of weakness before God?ГўВҖВқ

A mournful sob burst from VillefortГўВҖВҷs heart; he approached the doctor,
and seizing his arm,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңValentine,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңit is ValentineГўВҖВҷs turn!ГўВҖВқ

40284m



ГўВҖВңYour daughter!ГўВҖВқ cried dГўВҖВҷAvrigny with grief and surprise.

ГўВҖВңYou see you were deceived,ГўВҖВқ murmured the magistrate; ГўВҖВңcome and see

ГўВҖВңEach time you have applied to me,ГўВҖВқ said the doctor, ГўВҖВңit has been too
late; still I will go. But let us make haste, sir; with the enemies you

ГўВҖВңOh, this time, doctor, you shall not have to reproach me with

ГўВҖВңLet us try first to save the victim before we think of revenging her,ГўВҖВқ
said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny. ГўВҖВңCome.ГўВҖВқ

The same cabriolet which had brought Villefort took them back at full
speed, and at this moment Morrel rapped at Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs door.

The count was in his study and was reading with an angry look something

had left him only two hours before, the count raised his head, arose,

ГўВҖВңWhat is the matter, Maximilian?ГўВҖВқ asked he; ГўВҖВңyou are pale, and the

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңI came quickly; I wanted to speak to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre all your family well?ГўВҖВқ asked the count, with an affectionate
benevolence, whose sincerity no one could for a moment doubt.

ГўВҖВңThank you, countГўВҖВ”thank you,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, evidently embarrassed
how to begin the conversation; ГўВҖВңyes, everyone in my family is well.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo much the better; yet you have something to tell me?ГўВҖВқ replied the

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңit is true; I have but now left a house where

ГўВҖВңAre you then come from M. de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel; ГўВҖВңis someone dead in his house?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe general has just blown his brains out,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo with

ГўВҖВңOh, what a dreadful event!ГўВҖВқ cried Maximilian.

ГўВҖВңNot for the countess, or for Albert,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңa dead
father or husband is better than a dishonored one,ГўВҖВ”blood washes out

ГўВҖВңPoor countess,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian, ГўВҖВңI pity her very much; she is so

ГўВҖВңPity Albert also, Maximilian; for believe me he is the worthy son of

40286m



ГўВҖВңYes, I need your help: that is I thought like a madman that you could
lend me your assistance in a case where God alone can succor me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell me what it is,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңI know not, indeed, if I may reveal this secret to

Morrel hesitated.

ГўВҖВңDo you think I love you?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, taking the young manГўВҖВҷs

ГўВҖВңOh, you encourage me, and something tells me there,ГўВҖВқ placing his hand
on his heart, ГўВҖВңthat I ought to have no secret from you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right, Morrel; God is speaking to your heart, and your heart
speaks to you. Tell me what it says.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCount, will you allow me to send Baptistin to inquire after someone

ГўВҖВңI am at your service, and still more my servants.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I cannot live if she is not better.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall I ring for Baptistin?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, I will go and speak to him myself.ГўВҖВқ Morrel went out, called
Baptistin, and whispered a few words to him. The valet ran directly.

ГўВҖВңWell, have you sent?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo, seeing Morrel return.

ГўВҖВңYes, and now I shall be more calm.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou know I am waiting,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, smiling.

ГўВҖВңYes, and I will tell you. One evening I was in a garden; a clump of
trees concealed me; no one suspected I was there. Two persons passed
near meГўВҖВ”allow me to conceal their names for the present; they were
speaking in an undertone, and yet I was so interested in what they said
that I did not lose a single word.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is a gloomy introduction, if I may judge from your pallor and
shuddering, Morrel.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes, very gloomy, my friend. Someone had just died in the house to
which that garden belonged. One of the persons whose conversation I
overheard was the master of the house; the other, the physician. The
former was confiding to the latter his grief and fear, for it was the
second time within a month that death had suddenly and unexpectedly
entered that house which was apparently destined to destruction by some
exterminating angel, as an object of GodГўВҖВҷs anger.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, looking earnestly at the young man,
and by an imperceptible movement turning his chair, so that he remained

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ continued Morrel, ГўВҖВңdeath had entered that house twice within one
month.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what did the doctor answer?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңHe repliedГўВҖВ”he replied, that the death was not a natural one, and must
be attributedГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңTo what?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo poison.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed!ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with a slight cough which in moments of
extreme emotion helped him to disguise a blush, or his pallor, or the
intense interest with which he listened; ГўВҖВңindeed, Maximilian, did you
hear that?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, my dear count, I heard it; and the doctor added that if another
death occurred in a similar way he must appeal to justice.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo listened, or appeared to do so, with the greatest
calmness.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Maximilian, ГўВҖВңdeath came a third time, and neither the
master of the house nor the doctor said a word. Death is now, perhaps,
striking a fourth blow. Count, what am I bound to do, being in
possession of this secret?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy dear friend,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңyou appear to be relating an
adventure which we all know by heart. I know the house where you heard
it, or one very similar to it; a house with a garden, a master, a
physician, and where there have been three unexpected and sudden
deaths. Well, I have not intercepted your confidence, and yet I know
all that as well as you, and I have no conscientious scruples. No, it
does not concern me. You say an exterminating angel appears to have
devoted that house to GodГўВҖВҷs angerГўВҖВ”well, who says your supposition is
not reality? Do not notice things which those whose interest it is to
see them pass over. If it is GodГўВҖВҷs justice, instead of his anger, which
is walking through that house, Maximilian, turn away your face and let
his justice accomplish its purpose.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel shuddered. There was something mournful, solemn, and terrible in
the countГўВҖВҷs manner.

ГўВҖВңBesides,ГўВҖВқ continued he, in so changed a tone that no one would have
supposed it was the same person speakingГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңbesides, who says that it
will begin again?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt has returned, count,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Morrel; ГўВҖВңthat is why I hastened to
you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, what do you wish me to do? Do you wish me, for instance, to give
information to the procureur?ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo uttered the last words with
so much meaning that Morrel, starting up, cried out:

ГўВҖВңYou know of whom I speak, count, do you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerfectly well, my good friend; and I will prove it to you by putting
the dots to the _i_, or rather by naming the persons. You were walking
one evening in M. de VillefortГўВҖВҷs garden; from what you relate, I
suppose it to have been the evening of Madame de Saint-MГғВ©ranГўВҖВҷs death.
You heard M. de Villefort talking to M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny about the death of M.
de Saint-MГғВ©ran, and that no less surprising, of the countess. M.
dГўВҖВҷAvrigny said he believed they both proceeded from poison; and you,
honest man, have ever since been asking your heart and sounding your
conscience to know if you ought to expose or conceal this secret. We
are no longer in the Middle Ages; there is no longer a Vehmgericht, or
Free Tribunals; what do you want to ask these people? ГўВҖВҳConscience, what
hast thou to do with me?ГўВҖВҷ as Sterne said. My dear fellow, let them
sleep on, if they are asleep; let them grow pale in their drowsiness,
if they are disposed to do so, and pray do you remain in peace, who
have no remorse to disturb you.ГўВҖВқ

Deep grief was depicted on MorrelГўВҖВҷs features; he seized Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs
hand. ГўВҖВңBut it is beginning again, I say!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said the Count, astonished at his perseverance, which he could
not understand, and looking still more earnestly at Maximilian, ГўВҖВңlet it
begin again,ГўВҖВ”it is like the house of the Atreidae;19 God has condemned
them, and they must submit to their punishment. They will all
disappear, like the fabrics children build with cards, and which fall,
one by one, under the breath of their builder, even if there are two
hundred of them. Three months since it was M. de Saint-MГғВ©ran; Madame de
Saint-MГғВ©ran two months since; the other day it was Barrois; today, the
old Noirtier, or young Valentine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou knew it?ГўВҖВқ cried Morrel, in such a paroxysm of terror that Monte
Cristo started,ГўВҖВ”he whom the falling heavens would have found unmoved;
ГўВҖВңyou knew it, and said nothing?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what is it to me?ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, shrugging his shoulders;
ГўВҖВңdo I know those people? and must I lose the one to save the other?
Faith, no, for between the culprit and the victim I have no choice.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut I,ГўВҖВқ cried Morrel, groaning with sorrow, ГўВҖВңI love her!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou love?ГўВҖВ”whom?ГўВҖВқ cried Monte Cristo, starting to his feet, and seizing
the two hands which Morrel was raising towards heaven.

ГўВҖВңI love most fondlyГўВҖВ”I love madlyГўВҖВ”I love as a man who would give his
life-blood to spare her a tearГўВҖВ”I love Valentine de Villefort, who is
being murdered at this moment! Do you understand me? I love her; and I
ask God and you how I can save her?ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo uttered a cry which those only can conceive who have heard
the roar of a wounded lion. ГўВҖВңUnhappy man,ГўВҖВқ cried he, wringing his hands
in his turn; ГўВҖВңyou love Valentine,ГўВҖВ”that daughter of an accursed race!ГўВҖВқ

Never had Morrel witnessed such an expressionГўВҖВ”never had so terrible an
eye flashed before his faceГўВҖВ”never had the genius of terror he had so
often seen, either on the battle-field or in the murderous nights of
Algeria, shaken around him more dreadful fire. He drew back terrified.

As for Monte Cristo, after this ebullition he closed his eyes as if
dazzled by internal light. In a moment he restrained himself so
powerfully that the tempestuous heaving of his breast subsided, as
turbulent and foaming waves yield to the sunГўВҖВҷs genial influence when
the cloud has passed. This silence, self-control, and struggle lasted
about twenty seconds, then the count raised his pallid face.

ГўВҖВңSee,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңmy dear friend, how God punishes the most thoughtless
and unfeeling men for their indifference, by presenting dreadful scenes
to their view. I, who was looking on, an eager and curious
spectator,ГўВҖВ”I, who was watching the working of this mournful tragedy,ГўВҖВ”I,
who like a wicked angel was laughing at the evil men committed
protected by secrecy (a secret is easily kept by the rich and
powerful), I am in my turn bitten by the serpent whose tortuous course
I was watching, and bitten to the heart!ГўВҖВқ

Morrel groaned.

ГўВҖВңCome, come,ГўВҖВқ continued the count, ГўВҖВңcomplaints are unavailing, be a
man, be strong, be full of hope, for I am here and will watch over

Morrel shook his head sorrowfully.

ГўВҖВңI tell you to hope. Do you understand me?ГўВҖВқ cried Monte Cristo.
ГўВҖВңRemember that I never uttered a falsehood and am never deceived. It is
twelve oГўВҖВҷclock, Maximilian; thank heaven that you came at noon rather
than in the evening, or tomorrow morning. Listen, MorrelГўВҖВ”it is noon; if
Valentine is not now dead, she will not die.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ cried Morrel, ГўВҖВңwhen I left her dying?ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo pressed his hands to his forehead. What was passing in
that brain, so loaded with dreadful secrets? What does the angel of
light or the angel of darkness say to that mind, at once implacable and
generous? God only knows.

Monte Cristo raised his head once more, and this time he was calm as a
child awaking from its sleep.

ГўВҖВңMaximilian,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңreturn home. I command you not to stirГўВҖВ”attempt
nothing, not to let your countenance betray a thought, and I will send
you tidings. Go.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, count, you overwhelm me with that coolness. Have you, then, power
against death? Are you superhuman? Are you an angel?ГўВҖВқ And the young
man, who had never shrunk from danger, shrank before Monte Cristo with
indescribable terror. But Monte Cristo looked at him with so melancholy
and sweet a smile, that Maximilian felt the tears filling his eyes.

ГўВҖВңI can do much for you, my friend,ГўВҖВқ replied the count. ГўВҖВңGo; I must be
alone.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel, subdued by the extraordinary ascendancy Monte Cristo exercised
over everything around him, did not endeavor to resist it. He pressed
the countГўВҖВҷs hand and left. He stopped one moment at the door for
Baptistin, whom he saw in the Rue Matignon, and who was running.

Meanwhile, Villefort and dГўВҖВҷAvrigny had made all possible haste,
Valentine had not revived from her fainting fit on their arrival, and
the doctor examined the invalid with all the care the circumstances
demanded, and with an interest which the knowledge of the secret
intensified twofold. Villefort, closely watching his countenance and
his lips, awaited the result of the examination. Noirtier, paler than
even the young girl, more eager than Villefort for the decision, was
watching also intently and affectionately.

At last dГўВҖВҷAvrigny slowly uttered these words: ГўВҖВңShe is still alive!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill?ГўВҖВқ cried Villefort; ГўВҖВңoh, doctor, what a dreadful word is that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said the physician, ГўВҖВңI repeat it; she is still alive, and I am
astonished at it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut is she safe?ГўВҖВқ asked the father.

ГўВҖВңYes, since she lives.ГўВҖВқ

At that moment dГўВҖВҷAvrignyГўВҖВҷs glance met NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eye. It glistened with
such extraordinary joy, so rich and full of thought, that the physician
was struck. He placed the young girl again on the chair,ГўВҖВ”her lips were
scarcely discernible, they were so pale and white, as well as her whole
face,ГўВҖВ”and remained motionless, looking at Noirtier, who appeared to
anticipate and commend all he did.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny to Villefort, ГўВҖВңcall Mademoiselle ValentineГўВҖВҷs
maid, if you please.ГўВҖВқ

Villefort went himself to find her; and dГўВҖВҷAvrigny approached Noirtier.

ГўВҖВңHave you something to tell me?ГўВҖВқ asked he. The old man winked his eyes
expressively, which we may remember was his only way of expressing his
approval.

ГўВҖВңPrivately?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, I will remain with you.ГўВҖВқ At this moment Villefort returned,
followed by the ladyГўВҖВҷs maid; and after her came Madame de Villefort.

ГўВҖВңWhat is the matter, then, with this dear child? she has just left me,
and she complained of being indisposed, but I did not think seriously
of it.ГўВҖВқ

The young woman with tears in her eyes and every mark of affection of a
true mother, approached Valentine and took her hand. DГўВҖВҷAvrigny
continued to look at Noirtier; he saw the eyes of the old man dilate
and become round, his cheeks turn pale and tremble; the perspiration
stood in drops upon his forehead.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said he, involuntarily following NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eyes, which were
fixed on Madame de Villefort, who repeated:

ГўВҖВңThis poor child would be better in bed. Come, Fanny, we will put her
to bed.ГўВҖВқ

M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, who saw that would be a means of his remaining alone with
Noirtier, expressed his opinion that it was the best thing that could
be done; but he forbade that anything should be given to her except
what he ordered.

They carried Valentine away; she had revived, but could scarcely move
or speak, so shaken was her frame by the attack. She had, however, just
power to give one parting look to her grandfather, who in losing her
seemed to be resigning his very soul. DГўВҖВҷAvrigny followed the invalid,
wrote a prescription, ordered Villefort to take a cabriolet, go in
person to a chemistГўВҖВҷs to get the prescribed medicine, bring it himself,
and wait for him in his daughterГўВҖВҷs room. Then, having renewed his
injunction not to give Valentine anything, he went down again to
Noirtier, shut the doors carefully, and after convincing himself that
no one was listening:

ГўВҖВңDo you,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңknow anything of this young ladyГўВҖВҷs illness?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said the old man.

ГўВҖВңWe have no time to lose; I will question, and do you answer me.ГўВҖВқ
Noirtier made a sign that he was ready to answer. ГўВҖВңDid you anticipate
the accident which has happened to your granddaughter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ DГўВҖВҷAvrigny reflected a moment; then approaching Noirtier:

ГўВҖВңPardon what I am going to say,ГўВҖВқ added he, ГўВҖВңbut no indication should be
neglected in this terrible situation. Did you see poor Barrois die?ГўВҖВқ
Noirtier raised his eyes to heaven.

ГўВҖВңDo you know of what he died!ГўВҖВқ asked dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, placing his hand on
NoirtierГўВҖВҷs shoulder.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied the old man.

ГўВҖВңDo you think he died a natural death?ГўВҖВқ A sort of smile was discernible
on the motionless lips of Noirtier.

ГўВҖВңThen you have thought that Barrois was poisoned?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think the poison he fell a victim to was intended for him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think the same hand which unintentionally struck Barrois has
now attacked Valentine?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen will she die too?ГўВҖВқ asked dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, fixing his penetrating gaze
on Noirtier. He watched the effect of this question on the old man.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ replied he with an air of triumph which would have puzzled the
most clever diviner.

ГўВҖВңThen you hope?ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, with surprise.

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you hope?ГўВҖВқ The old man made him understand with his eyes that
he could not answer.

ГўВҖВңAh, yes, it is true,ГўВҖВқ murmured dГўВҖВҷAvrigny. Then, turning to
Noirtier,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңDo you hope the assassin will be tried?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you hope the poison will take no effect on Valentine?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is no news to you,ГўВҖВқ added dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, ГўВҖВңto tell you that an attempt
has been made to poison her?ГўВҖВқ The old man made a sign that he
entertained no doubt upon the subject. ГўВҖВңThen how do you hope Valentine
will escape?ГўВҖВқ

Noirtier kept his eyes steadfastly fixed on the same spot. DГўВҖВҷAvrigny
followed the direction and saw that they were fixed on a bottle
containing the mixture which he took every morning. ГўВҖВңAh, indeed?ГўВҖВқ said
dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, struck with a sudden thought, ГўВҖВңhas it occurred to
youГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”Noirtier did not let him finish.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңTo prepare her system to resist poison?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy accustoming her by degreesГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, yes,ГўВҖВқ said Noirtier, delighted to be understood.

ГўВҖВңOf course. I had told you that there was brucine in the mixture I give
you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd by accustoming her to that poison, you have endeavored to
neutralize the effect of a similar poison?ГўВҖВқ NoirtierГўВҖВҷs joy continued.
ГўВҖВңAnd you have succeeded,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed dГўВҖВҷAvrigny. ГўВҖВңWithout that precaution
Valentine would have died before assistance could have been procured.
The dose has been excessive, but she has only been shaken by it; and
this time, at any rate, Valentine will not die.ГўВҖВқ

A superhuman joy expanded the old manГўВҖВҷs eyes, which were raised towards
heaven with an expression of infinite gratitude. At this moment
Villefort returned.

ГўВҖВңHere, doctor,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңis what you sent me for.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas this prepared in your presence?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied the procureur.

ГўВҖВңHave you not let it go out of your hands?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

DГўВҖВҷAvrigny took the bottle, poured some drops of the mixture it
contained in the hollow of his hand, and swallowed them.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңlet us go to Valentine; I will give instructions to
everyone, and you, M. de Villefort, will yourself see that no one
deviates from them.ГўВҖВқ

40294m



At the moment when dГўВҖВҷAvrigny was returning to ValentineГўВҖВҷs room,
accompanied by Villefort, an Italian priest, of serious demeanor and
calm and firm tone, hired for his use the house adjoining the hotel of
M. de Villefort. No one knew how the three former tenants of that house
left it. About two hours afterwards its foundation was reported to be
unsafe; but the report did not prevent the new occupant establishing
himself there with his modest furniture the same day at five oГўВҖВҷclock.
The lease was drawn up for three, six, or nine years by the new tenant,
who, according to the rule of the proprietor, paid six months in
advance.

This new tenant, who, as we have said, was an Italian, was called Il
Signor Giacomo Busoni. Workmen were immediately called in, and that
same night the passengers at the end of the faubourg saw with surprise
that carpenters and masons were occupied in repairing the lower part of
the tottering house.



 Chapter 95. Father and Daughter

We saw in a preceding chapter how Madame Danglars went formally to
announce to Madame de Villefort the approaching marriage of EugГғВ©nie
Danglars and M. Andrea Cavalcanti. This formal announcement, which
implied or appeared to imply, the approval of all the persons concerned
in this momentous affair, had been preceded by a scene to which our
readers must be admitted. We beg them to take one step backward, and to
transport themselves, the morning of that day of great catastrophes,
into the showy, gilded salon we have before shown them, and which was
the pride of its owner, Baron Danglars.

In this room, at about ten oГўВҖВҷclock in the morning, the banker himself
had been walking to and fro for some minutes thoughtfully and in
evident uneasiness, watching both doors, and listening to every sound.
When his patience was exhausted, he called his valet.

ГўВҖВңГғВүtienne,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңsee why Mademoiselle EugГғВ©nie has asked me to meet
her in the drawing-room, and why she makes me wait so long.ГўВҖВқ

Having given this vent to his ill-humor, the baron became more calm;
Mademoiselle Danglars had that morning requested an interview with her
father, and had fixed on the gilded drawing-room as the spot. The
singularity of this step, and above all its formality, had not a little
surprised the banker, who had immediately obeyed his daughter by
repairing first to the drawing-room. ГғВүtienne soon returned from his
errand.

ГўВҖВңMademoiselleГўВҖВҷs ladyГўВҖВҷs maid says, sir, that mademoiselle is finishing
her toilette, and will be here shortly.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars nodded, to signify that he was satisfied. To the world and to
his servants Danglars assumed the character of the good-natured man and
the indulgent father. This was one of his parts in the popular comedy
he was performing,ГўВҖВ”a make-up he had adopted and which suited him about
as well as the masks worn on the classic stage by paternal actors, who
seen from one side, were the image of geniality, and from the other
showed lips drawn down in chronic ill-temper. Let us hasten to say that
in private the genial side descended to the level of the other, so that
generally the indulgent man disappeared to give place to the brutal
husband and domineering father.

ГўВҖВңWhy the devil does that foolish girl, who pretends to wish to speak to
me, not come into my study? and why on earth does she want to speak to
me at all?ГўВҖВқ

He was turning this thought over in his brain for the twentieth time,
when the door opened and EugГғВ©nie appeared, attired in a figured black
satin dress, her hair dressed and gloves on, as if she were going to
the Italian Opera.

ГўВҖВңWell, EugГғВ©nie, what is it you want with me? and why in this solemn
drawing-room when the study is so comfortable?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI quite understand why you ask, sir,ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie, making a sign that
her father might be seated, ГўВҖВңand in fact your two questions suggest
fully the theme of our conversation. I will answer them both, and
contrary to the usual method, the last first, because it is the least
difficult. I have chosen the drawing-room, sir, as our place of
meeting, in order to avoid the disagreeable impressions and influences
of a bankerГўВҖВҷs study. Those gilded cashbooks, drawers locked like gates
of fortresses, heaps of bank-bills, come from I know not where, and the
quantities of letters from England, Holland, Spain, India, China, and
Peru, have generally a strange influence on a fatherГўВҖВҷs mind, and make
him forget that there is in the world an interest greater and more
sacred than the good opinion of his correspondents. I have, therefore,
chosen this drawing-room, where you see, smiling and happy in their
magnificent frames, your portrait, mine, my motherГўВҖВҷs, and all sorts of
rural landscapes and touching pastorals. I rely much on external
impressions; perhaps, with regard to you, they are immaterial, but I
should be no artist if I had not some fancies.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well,ГўВҖВқ replied M. Danglars, who had listened to all this preamble
with imperturbable coolness, but without understanding a word, since
like every man burdened with thoughts of the past, he was occupied with
seeking the thread of his own ideas in those of the speaker.

ГўВҖВңThere is, then, the second point cleared up, or nearly so,ГўВҖВқ said
EugГғВ©nie, without the least confusion, and with that masculine
pointedness which distinguished her gesture and her language; ГўВҖВңand you
appear satisfied with the explanation. Now, let us return to the first.
You ask me why I have requested this interview; I will tell you in two
words, sir; I will not marry count Andrea Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

Danglars leaped from his chair and raised his eyes and arms towards
heaven.

40298m



ГўВҖВңYes, indeed, sir,ГўВҖВқ continued EugГғВ©nie, still quite calm; ГўВҖВңyou are
astonished, I see; for since this little affair began, I have not
manifested the slightest opposition, and yet I am always sure, when the
opportunity arrives, to oppose a determined and absolute will to people
who have not consulted me, and things which displease me. However, this
time, my tranquillity, or passiveness as philosophers say, proceeded
from another source; it proceeded from a wish, like a submissive and
devoted daughterГўВҖВқ (a slight smile was observable on the purple lips of
the young girl), ГўВҖВңto practice obedience.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ asked Danglars.

ГўВҖВңWell, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied EugГғВ©nie, ГўВҖВңI have tried to the very last and now
that the moment has come, I feel in spite of all my efforts that it is
impossible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, whose weak mind was at first quite overwhelmed
with the weight of this pitiless logic, marking evident premeditation
and force of will, ГўВҖВңwhat is your reason for this refusal, EugГғВ©nie? what
reason do you assign?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy reason?ГўВҖВқ replied the young girl. ГўВҖВңWell, it is not that the man is
more ugly, more foolish, or more disagreeable than any other; no, M.
Andrea Cavalcanti may appear to those who look at menГўВҖВҷs faces and
figures as a very good specimen of his kind. It is not, either, that my
heart is less touched by him than any other; that would be a
schoolgirlГўВҖВҷs reason, which I consider quite beneath me. I actually love
no one, sir; you know it, do you not? I do not then see why, without
real necessity, I should encumber my life with a perpetual companion.
Has not some sage said, ГўВҖВҳNothing too muchГўВҖВҷ? and another, ГўВҖВҳI carry all
my effects with meГўВҖВҷ? I have been taught these two aphorisms in Latin
and in Greek; one is, I believe, from PhГғВҰdrus, and the other from Bias.
Well, my dear father, in the shipwreck of lifeГўВҖВ”for life is an eternal
shipwreck of our hopesГўВҖВ”I cast into the sea my useless encumbrance, that
is all, and I remain with my own will, disposed to live perfectly
alone, and consequently perfectly free.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUnhappy girl, unhappy girl!ГўВҖВқ murmured Danglars, turning pale, for he
knew from long experience the solidity of the obstacle he had so
suddenly encountered.

ГўВҖВңUnhappy girl,ГўВҖВқ replied EugГғВ©nie, ГўВҖВңunhappy girl, do you say, sir? No,
indeed; the exclamation appears quite theatrical and affected. Happy,
on the contrary, for what am I in want of? The world calls me
beautiful. It is something to be well received. I like a favorable
reception; it expands the countenance, and those around me do not then
appear so ugly. I possess a share of wit, and a certain relative
sensibility, which enables me to draw from life in general, for the
support of mine, all I meet with that is good, like the monkey who
cracks the nut to get at its contents. I am rich, for you have one of
the first fortunes in France. I am your only daughter, and you are not
so exacting as the fathers of the Porte Saint-Martin and GaГғВ®tГғВ©, who
disinherit their daughters for not giving them grandchildren. Besides,
the provident law has deprived you of the power to disinherit me, at
least entirely, as it has also of the power to compel me to marry
Monsieur This or Monsieur That. And soГўВҖВ”being, beautiful, witty,
somewhat talented, as the comic operas say, and richГўВҖВ”and that is
happiness, sirГўВҖВ”why do you call me unhappy?ГўВҖВқ

Danglars, seeing his daughter smiling, and proud even to insolence,
could not entirely repress his brutal feelings, but they betrayed
themselves only by an exclamation. Under the fixed and inquiring gaze
levelled at him from under those beautiful black eyebrows, he prudently
turned away, and calmed himself immediately, daunted by the power of a
resolute mind.

ГўВҖВңTruly, my daughter,ГўВҖВқ replied he with a smile, ГўВҖВңyou are all you boast
of being, excepting one thing; I will not too hastily tell you which,
but would rather leave you to guess it.ГўВҖВқ

EugГғВ©nie looked at Danglars, much surprised that one flower of her crown
of pride, with which she had so superbly decked herself, should be
disputed.

ГўВҖВңMy daughter,ГўВҖВқ continued the banker, ГўВҖВңyou have perfectly explained to
me the sentiments which influence a girl like you, who is determined
she will not marry; now it remains for me to tell you the motives of a
father like me, who has decided that his daughter shall marry.ГўВҖВқ

EugГғВ©nie bowed, not as a submissive daughter, but as an adversary
prepared for a discussion.

ГўВҖВңMy daughter,ГўВҖВқ continued Danglars, ГўВҖВңwhen a father asks his daughter to
choose a husband, he has always some reason for wishing her to marry.
Some are affected with the mania of which you spoke just now, that of
living again in their grandchildren. This is not my weakness, I tell
you at once; family joys have no charm for me. I may acknowledge this
to a daughter whom I know to be philosophical enough to understand my
indifference, and not to impute it to me as a crime.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is not to the purpose,ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie; ГўВҖВңlet us speak candidly,
sir; I admire candor.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңI can, when circumstances render it desirable,
adopt your system, although it may not be my general practice. I will
therefore proceed. I have proposed to you to marry, not for your sake,
for indeed I did not think of you in the least at the moment (you
admire candor, and will now be satisfied, I hope); but because it
suited me to marry you as soon as possible, on account of certain
commercial speculations I am desirous of entering into.ГўВҖВқ EugГғВ©nie became
uneasy.

40302m



ГўВҖВңIt is just as I tell you, I assure you, and you must not be angry with
me, for you have sought this disclosure. I do not willingly enter into
arithmetical explanations with an artist like you, who fears to enter
my study lest she should imbibe disagreeable or anti-poetic impressions
and sensations. But in that same bankerГўВҖВҷs study, where you very
willingly presented yourself yesterday to ask for the thousand francs I
give you monthly for pocket-money, you must know, my dear young lady,
that many things may be learned, useful even to a girl who will not
marry. There one may learn, for instance, what, out of regard to your
nervous susceptibility, I will inform you of in the drawing-room,
namely, that the credit of a banker is his physical and moral life;
that credit sustains him as breath animates the body; and M. de Monte
Cristo once gave me a lecture on that subject, which I have never
forgotten. There we may learn that as credit sinks, the body becomes a
corpse, and this is what must happen very soon to the banker who is
proud to own so good a logician as you for his daughter.ГўВҖВқ

But EugГғВ©nie, instead of stooping, drew herself up under the blow.
ГўВҖВңRuined?ГўВҖВқ said she.

ГўВҖВңExactly, my daughter; that is precisely what I mean,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars,
almost digging his nails into his breast, while he preserved on his
harsh features the smile of the heartless though clever man;
ГўВҖВңruinedГўВҖВ”yes, that is it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh!ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie.

ГўВҖВңYes, ruined! Now it is revealed, this secret so full of horror, as the
tragic poet says. Now, my daughter, learn from my lips how you may
alleviate this misfortune, so far as it will affect you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ cried EugГғВ©nie, ГўВҖВңyou are a bad physiognomist, if you imagine I
deplore on my own account the catastrophe of which you warn me. I
ruined? and what will that signify to me? Have I not my talent left?
Can I not, like Pasta, Malibran, Grisi, acquire for myself what you
would never have given me, whatever might have been your fortune, a
hundred or a hundred and fifty thousand livres per annum, for which I
shall be indebted to no one but myself; and which, instead of being
given as you gave me those poor twelve thousand francs, with sour looks
and reproaches for my prodigality, will be accompanied with
acclamations, with bravos, and with flowers? And if I do not possess
that talent, which your smiles prove to me you doubt, should I not
still have that ardent love of independence, which will be a substitute
for wealth, and which in my mind supersedes even the instinct of
self-preservation? No, I grieve not on my own account, I shall always
find a resource; my books, my pencils, my piano, all the things which
cost but little, and which I shall be able to procure, will remain my
own.

ГўВҖВңDo you think that I sorrow for Madame Danglars? Undeceive yourself
again; either I am greatly mistaken, or she has provided against the
catastrophe which threatens you, and, which will pass over without
affecting her. She has taken care for herself,ГўВҖВ”at least I hope so,ГўВҖВ”for
her attention has not been diverted from her projects by watching over
me. She has fostered my independence by professedly indulging my love
for liberty. Oh, no, sir; from my childhood I have seen too much, and
understood too much, of what has passed around me, for misfortune to
have an undue power over me. From my earliest recollections, I have
been beloved by no oneГўВҖВ”so much the worse; that has naturally led me to
love no oneГўВҖВ”so much the betterГўВҖВ”now you have my profession of faith.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, pale with anger, which was not at all due to
offended paternal love,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңthen, mademoiselle, you persist in your
determination to accelerate my ruin?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour ruin? I accelerate your ruin? What do you mean? I do not
understand you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo much the better, I have a ray of hope left; listen.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am all attention,ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie, looking so earnestly at her father
that it was an effort for the latter to endure her unrelenting gaze.

ГўВҖВңM. Cavalcanti,ГўВҖВқ continued Danglars, ГўВҖВңis about to marry you, and will
place in my hands his fortune, amounting to three million livres.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is admirable!ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie with sovereign contempt, smoothing
her gloves out one upon the other.

ГўВҖВңYou think I shall deprive you of those three millions,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars;
ГўВҖВңbut do not fear it. They are destined to produce at least ten. I and a
brother banker have obtained a grant of a railway, the only industrial
enterprise which in these days promises to make good the fabulous
prospects that Law once held out to the eternally deluded Parisians, in
the fantastic Mississippi scheme. As I look at it, a millionth part of
a railway is worth fully as much as an acre of waste land on the banks
of the Ohio. We make in our case a deposit, on a mortgage, which is an
advance, as you see, since we gain at least ten, fifteen, twenty, or a
hundred livresГўВҖВҷ worth of iron in exchange for our money. Well, within a
week I am to deposit four millions for my share; the four millions, I
promise you, will produce ten or twelve.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut during my visit to you the day before yesterday, sir, which you
appear to recollect so well,ГўВҖВқ replied EugГғВ©nie, ГўВҖВңI saw you arranging a
depositГўВҖВ”is not that the term?ГўВҖВ”of five millions and a half; you even
pointed it out to me in two drafts on the treasury, and you were
astonished that so valuable a paper did not dazzle my eyes like
lightning.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, but those five millions and a half are not mine, and are only a
proof of the great confidence placed in me; my title of popular banker
has gained me the confidence of charitable institutions, and the five
millions and a half belong to them; at any other time I should not have
hesitated to make use of them, but the great losses I have recently
sustained are well known, and, as I told you, my credit is rather
shaken. That deposit may be at any moment withdrawn, and if I had
employed it for another purpose, I should bring on me a disgraceful
bankruptcy. I do not despise bankruptcies, believe me, but they must be
those which enrich, not those which ruin. Now, if you marry M.
Cavalcanti, and I get the three millions, or even if it is thought I am
going to get them, my credit will be restored, and my fortune, which
for the last month or two has been swallowed up in gulfs which have
been opened in my path by an inconceivable fatality, will revive. Do
you understand me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPerfectly; you pledge me for three millions, do you not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe greater the amount, the more flattering it is to you; it gives you
an idea of your value.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you. One word more, sir; do you promise me to make what use you
can of the report of the fortune M. Cavalcanti will bring without
touching the money? This is no act of selfishness, but of delicacy. I
am willing to help rebuild your fortune, but I will not be an
accomplice in the ruin of others.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut since I tell you,ГўВҖВқ cried Danglars, ГўВҖВңthat with these three
millionГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you expect to recover your position, sir, without touching those
three million?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI hope so, if the marriage should take place and confirm my credit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall you be able to pay M. Cavalcanti the five hundred thousand
francs you promise for my dowry?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe shall receive them on returning from the mayorГўВҖВҷs20.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery well!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat next? what more do you want?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI wish to know if, in demanding my signature, you leave me entirely
free in my person?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAbsolutely.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, as I said before, sir,ГўВҖВ”very well; I am ready to marry M.
Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what are you up to?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, that is my affair. What advantage should I have over you, if
knowing your secret I were to tell you mine?ГўВҖВқ

Danglars bit his lips. ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңyou are ready to pay the
official visits, which are absolutely indispensable?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied EugГғВ©nie.

ГўВҖВңAnd to sign the contract in three days?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, in my turn, I also say, very well!ГўВҖВқ

Danglars pressed his daughterГўВҖВҷs hand in his. But, extraordinary to
relate, the father did not say, ГўВҖВңThank you, my child,ГўВҖВқ nor did the
daughter smile at her father.

ГўВҖВңIs the conference ended?ГўВҖВқ asked EugГғВ©nie, rising.

Danglars motioned that he had nothing more to say. Five minutes
afterwards the piano resounded to the touch of Mademoiselle dГўВҖВҷArmillyГўВҖВҷs
fingers, and Mademoiselle Danglars was singing BrabantioГўВҖВҷs malediction
on Desdemona. At the end of the piece ГғВүtienne entered, and announced to
EugГғВ©nie that the horses were to the carriage, and that the baroness was
waiting for her to pay her visits. We have seen them at VillefortГўВҖВҷs;
they proceeded then on their course.

 Chapter 96. The Contract

Three days after the scene we have just described, namely towards five
oГўВҖВҷclock in the afternoon of the day fixed for the signature of the
contract between Mademoiselle EugГғВ©nie Danglars and Andrea Cavalcanti,
whom the banker persisted in calling prince, a fresh breeze was
stirring the leaves in the little garden in front of the Count of Monte
CristoГўВҖВҷs house, and the count was preparing to go out. While his horses
were impatiently pawing the ground, held in by the coachman, who had
been seated a quarter of an hour on his box, the elegant phaeton with
which we are familiar rapidly turned the angle of the entrance-gate,
and cast out on the doorsteps M. Andrea Cavalcanti, as decked up and
gay as if he were going to marry a princess.

He inquired after the count with his usual familiarity, and ascending
lightly to the first story met him at the top of the stairs.

The count stopped on seeing the young man. As for Andrea, he was
launched, and when he was once launched nothing stopped him.

ГўВҖВңAh, good morning, my dear count,ГўВҖВқ said he.

ГўВҖВңAh, M. Andrea,ГўВҖВқ said the latter, with his half-jesting tone; ГўВҖВңhow do
you do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCharmingly, as you see. I am come to talk to you about a thousand
things; but, first tell me, were you going out or just returned?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was going out, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen, in order not to hinder you, I will get up with you if you please
in your carriage, and Tom shall follow with my phaeton in tow.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said the count, with an imperceptible smile of contempt, for he
had no wish to be seen in the young manГўВҖВҷs society,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңno; I prefer
listening to you here, my dear M. Andrea; we can chat better in-doors,
and there is no coachman to overhear our conversation.ГўВҖВқ

The count returned to a small drawing-room on the first floor, sat
down, and crossing his legs motioned to the young man to take a seat
also. Andrea assumed his gayest manner.

ГўВҖВңYou know, my dear count,ГўВҖВқ said he, ГўВҖВңthe ceremony is to take place this
evening. At nine oГўВҖВҷclock the contract is to be signed at my
father-in-lawГўВҖВҷs.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, indeed?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңWhat; is it news to you? Has not M. Danglars informed you of the
ceremony?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, yes,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңI received a letter from him yesterday, but
I do not think the hour was mentioned.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPossibly my father-in-law trusted to its general notoriety.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңyou are fortunate, M. Cavalcanti; it is a
most suitable alliance you are contracting, and Mademoiselle Danglars
is a handsome girl.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, indeed she is,ГўВҖВқ replied Cavalcanti, in a very modest tone.

ГўВҖВңAbove all, she is very rich,ГўВҖВ”at least, I believe so,ГўВҖВқ said Monte
Cristo.

ГўВҖВңVery rich, do you think?ГўВҖВқ replied the young man.

ГўВҖВңDoubtless; it is said M. Danglars conceals at least half of his
fortune.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd he acknowledges fifteen or twenty millions,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea with a
look sparkling with joy.

ГўВҖВңWithout reckoning,ГўВҖВқ added Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthat he is on the eve of
entering into a sort of speculation already in vogue in the United
States and in England, but quite novel in France.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, I know what you mean,ГўВҖВ”the railway, of which he has obtained
the grant, is it not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrecisely; it is generally believed he will gain ten millions by that
affair.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTen millions! Do you think so? It is magnificent!ГўВҖВқ said Cavalcanti,
who was quite confounded at the metallic sound of these golden words.

ГўВҖВңWithout reckoning,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңthat all his fortune will
come to you, and justly too, since Mademoiselle Danglars is an only
daughter. Besides, your own fortune, as your father assured me, is
almost equal to that of your betrothed. But enough of money matters. Do
you know, M. Andrea, I think you have managed this affair rather
skilfully?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot badly, by any means,ГўВҖВқ said the young man; ГўВҖВңI was born for a
diplomatist.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, you must become a diplomatist; diplomacy, you know, is something
that is not to be acquired; it is instinctive. Have you lost your
heart?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed, I fear it,ГўВҖВқ replied Andrea, in the tone in which he had heard
Dorante or ValГғВЁre reply to Alceste21 at the ThГғВ©ГғВўtre FranГғВ§ais.

ГўВҖВңIs your love returned?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI suppose so,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea with a triumphant smile, ГўВҖВңsince I am
accepted. But I must not forget one grand point.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhich?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat I have been singularly assisted.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNonsense.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have, indeed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy circumstances?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; by you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBy me? Not at all, prince,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo laying a marked stress
on the title, ГўВҖВңwhat have I done for you? Are not your name, your social
position, and your merit sufficient?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңno; it is useless for you to say so, count. I
maintain that the position of a man like you has done more than my
name, my social position, and my merit.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are completely mistaken, sir,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo coldly, who felt
the perfidious manГ…В“uvre of the young man, and understood the bearing of
his words; ГўВҖВңyou only acquired my protection after the influence and
fortune of your father had been ascertained; for, after all, who
procured for me, who had never seen either you or your illustrious
father, the pleasure of your acquaintance?ГўВҖВ”two of my good friends, Lord
Wilmore and the AbbГғВ© Busoni. What encouraged me not to become your
surety, but to patronize you?ГўВҖВ”your fatherГўВҖВҷs name, so well known in
Italy and so highly honored. Personally, I do not know you.ГўВҖВқ

This calm tone and perfect ease made Andrea feel that he was, for the
moment, restrained by a more muscular hand than his own, and that the
restraint could not be easily broken through.

ГўВҖВңOh, then my father has really a very large fortune, count?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt appears so, sir,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңDo you know if the marriage settlement he promised me has come?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have been advised of it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut the three millions?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe three millions are probably on the road.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I shall really have them?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, well,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңI do not think you have yet known the want
of money.ГўВҖВқ

Andrea was so surprised that he pondered the matter for a moment. Then,
arousing from his reverie:

ГўВҖВңNow, sir, I have one request to make to you, which you will
understand, even if it should be disagreeable to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңProceed,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңI have formed an acquaintance, thanks to my good fortune, with many
noted persons, and have, at least for the moment, a crowd of friends.
But marrying, as I am about to do, before all Paris, I ought to be
supported by an illustrious name, and in the absence of the paternal
hand some powerful one ought to lead me to the altar; now, my father is
not coming to Paris, is he?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is old, covered with wounds, and suffers dreadfully, he says, in
travelling.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI understand; well, I am come to ask a favor of you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, of you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd pray what may it be?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, to take his part.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, my dear sir! What?ГўВҖВ”after the varied relations I have had the
happiness to sustain towards you, can it be that you know me so little
as to ask such a thing? Ask me to lend you half a million and, although
such a loan is somewhat rare, on my honor, you would annoy me less!
Know, then, what I thought I had already told you, that in
participation in this worldГўВҖВҷs affairs, more especially in their moral
aspects, the Count of Monte Cristo has never ceased to entertain the
scruples and even the superstitions of the East. I, who have a seraglio
at Cairo, one at Smyrna, and one at Constantinople, preside at a
wedding?ГўВҖВ”never!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen you refuse me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDecidedly; and were you my son or my brother I would refuse you in the
same way.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut what must be done?ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, disappointed.

ГўВҖВңYou said just now that you had a hundred friends.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңVery true, but you introduced me at M. DanglarsГўВҖВҷ.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNot at all! Let us recall the exact facts. You met him at a dinner
party at my house, and you introduced yourself at his house; that is a
totally different affair.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, but, by my marriage, you have forwarded that.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВ”not in the least, I beg you to believe. Recollect what I told you
when you asked me to propose you. ГўВҖВҳOh, I never make matches, my dear
prince, it is my settled principle.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ Andrea bit his lips.

50025m



ГўВҖВңBut, at least, you will be there?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWill all Paris be there?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, certainly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, like all Paris, I shall be there too,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңAnd will you sign the contract?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI see no objection to that; my scruples do not go thus far.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, since you will grant me no more, I must be content with what you
give me. But one word more, count.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAdvice.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBe careful; advice is worse than a service.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, you can give me this without compromising yourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTell me what it is.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIs my wifeГўВҖВҷs fortune five hundred thousand livres?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat is the sum M. Danglars himself announced.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMust I receive it, or leave it in the hands of the notary?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is the way such affairs are generally arranged when it is wished
to do them stylishly: Your two solicitors appoint a meeting, when the
contract is signed, for the next or the following day; then they
exchange the two portions, for which they each give a receipt; then,
when the marriage is celebrated, they place the amount at your disposal
as the chief member of the alliance.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, with a certain ill-concealed uneasiness, ГўВҖВңI
thought I heard my father-in-law say that he intended embarking our
property in that famous railway affair of which you spoke just now.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңit will be the way, everybody says, of
trebling your fortune in twelve months. Baron Danglars is a good
father, and knows how to calculate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn that case,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңeverything is all right, excepting your
refusal, which quite grieves me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou must attribute it only to natural scruples under similar
circumstances.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Andrea, ГўВҖВңlet it be as you wish. This evening, then, at
nine oГўВҖВҷclock.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAdieu till then.ГўВҖВқ

Notwithstanding a slight resistance on the part of Monte Cristo, whose
lips turned pale, but who preserved his ceremonious smile, Andrea
seized the countГўВҖВҷs hand, pressed it, jumped into his phaeton, and
disappeared.

The four or five remaining hours before nine oГўВҖВҷclock arrived, Andrea
employed in riding, paying visits,ГўВҖВ”designed to induce those of whom he
had spoken to appear at the bankerГўВҖВҷs in their gayest
equipages,ГўВҖВ”dazzling them by promises of shares in schemes which have
since turned every brain, and in which Danglars was just taking the
initiative.

In fact, at half-past eight in the evening the grand salon, the gallery
adjoining, and the three other drawing-rooms on the same floor, were
filled with a perfumed crowd, who sympathized but little in the event,
but who all participated in that love of being present wherever there
is anything fresh to be seen. An Academician would say that the
entertainments of the fashionable world are collections of flowers
which attract inconstant butterflies, famished bees, and buzzing
drones.

50025m



No one could deny that the rooms were splendidly illuminated; the light
streamed forth on the gilt mouldings and the silk hangings; and all the
bad taste of decorations, which had only their richness to boast of,
shone in its splendor. Mademoiselle EugГғВ©nie was dressed with elegant
simplicity in a figured white silk dress, and a white rose half
concealed in her jet black hair was her only ornament, unaccompanied by
a single jewel. Her eyes, however, betrayed that perfect confidence
which contradicted the girlish simplicity of this modest attire.

Madame Danglars was chatting at a short distance with Debray,
Beauchamp, and ChГғВўteau-Renaud. Debray was admitted to the house for
this grand ceremony, but on the same plane with everyone else, and
without any particular privilege. M. Danglars, surrounded by deputies
and men connected with the revenue, was explaining a new theory of
taxation which he intended to adopt when the course of events had
compelled the government to call him into the ministry. Andrea, on
whose arm hung one of the most consummate dandies of the Opera, was
explaining to him rather cleverly, since he was obliged to be bold to
appear at ease, his future projects, and the new luxuries he meant to
introduce to Parisian fashions with his hundred and seventy-five
thousand livres per annum.

The crowd moved to and fro in the rooms like an ebb and flow of
turquoises, rubies, emeralds, opals, and diamonds. As usual, the oldest
women were the most decorated, and the ugliest the most conspicuous. If
there was a beautiful lily, or a sweet rose, you had to search for it,
concealed in some corner behind a mother with a turban, or an aunt with
a bird-of-paradise.

At each moment, in the midst of the crowd, the buzzing, and the
laughter, the door-keeperГўВҖВҷs voice was heard announcing some name well
known in the financial department, respected in the army, or
illustrious in the literary world, and which was acknowledged by a
slight movement in the different groups. But for one whose privilege it
was to agitate that ocean of human waves, how many were received with a
look of indifference or a sneer of disdain!

At the moment when the hand of the massive time-piece, representing
Endymion asleep, pointed to nine on its golden face, and the hammer,
the faithful type of mechanical thought, struck nine times, the name of
the Count of Monte Cristo resounded in its turn, and as if by an
electric shock all the assembly turned towards the door. The count was
dressed in black and with his habitual simplicity; his white waistcoat
displayed his expansive noble chest and his black stock was singularly
noticeable because of its contrast with the deadly paleness of his
face. His only jewellery was a chain, so fine that the slender gold
thread was scarcely perceptible on his white waistcoat.

A circle was immediately formed around the door. The count perceived at
one glance Madame Danglars at one end of the drawing-room, M. Danglars
at the other, and EugГғВ©nie in front of him. He first advanced towards
the baroness, who was chatting with Madame de Villefort, who had come
alone, Valentine being still an invalid; and without turning aside, so
clear was the road left for him, he passed from the baroness to
EugГғВ©nie, whom he complimented in such rapid and measured terms, that
the proud artist was quite struck. Near her was Mademoiselle Louise
dГўВҖВҷArmilly, who thanked the count for the letters of introduction he had
so kindly given her for Italy, which she intended immediately to make
use of. On leaving these ladies he found himself with Danglars, who had
advanced to meet him.

Having accomplished these three social duties, Monte Cristo stopped,
looking around him with that expression peculiar to a certain class,
which seems to say, ГўВҖВңI have done my duty, now let others do theirs.ГўВҖВқ

Andrea, who was in an adjoining room, had shared in the sensation
caused by the arrival of Monte Cristo, and now came forward to pay his
respects to the count. He found him completely surrounded; all were
eager to speak to him, as is always the case with those whose words are
few and weighty. The solicitors arrived at this moment and arranged
their scrawled papers on the velvet cloth embroidered with gold which
covered the table prepared for the signature; it was a gilt table
supported on lionsГўВҖВҷ claws. One of the notaries sat down, the other
remained standing. They were about to proceed to the reading of the
contract, which half Paris assembled was to sign. All took their
places, or rather the ladies formed a circle, while the gentlemen (more
indifferent to the restraints of what Boileau calls the _style
ГғВ©nergique_) commented on the feverish agitation of Andrea, on M.
DanglarsГўВҖВҷ riveted attention, EugГғВ©nieГўВҖВҷs composure, and the light and
sprightly manner in which the baroness treated this important affair.

The contract was read during a profound silence. But as soon as it was
finished, the buzz was redoubled through all the drawing-rooms; the
brilliant sums, the rolling millions which were to be at the command of
the two young people, and which crowned the display of the wedding
presents and the young ladyГўВҖВҷs diamonds, which had been made in a room
entirely appropriated for that purpose, had exercised to the full their
delusions over the envious assembly.

Mademoiselle DanglarsГўВҖВҷ charms were heightened in the opinion of the
young men, and for the moment seemed to outvie the sun in splendor. As
for the ladies, it is needless to say that while they coveted the
millions, they thought they did not need them for themselves, as they
were beautiful enough without them. Andrea, surrounded by his friends,
complimented, flattered, beginning to believe in the reality of his
dream, was almost bewildered. The notary solemnly took the pen,
flourished it above his head, and said:

ГўВҖВңGentlemen, we are about to sign the contract.ГўВҖВқ

The baron was to sign first, then the representative of M. Cavalcanti,
senior, then the baroness, afterwards the ГўВҖВңfuture couple,ГўВҖВқ as they are
styled in the abominable phraseology of legal documents.

The baron took the pen and signed, then the representative. The
baroness approached, leaning on Madame de VillefortГўВҖВҷs arm.

ГўВҖВңMy dear,ГўВҖВқ said she, as she took the pen, ГўВҖВңis it not vexatious? An
unexpected incident, in the affair of murder and theft at the Count of
Monte CristoГўВҖВҷs, in which he nearly fell a victim, deprives us of the
pleasure of seeing M. de Villefort.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed?ГўВҖВқ said M. Danglars, in the same tone in which he would have
said, ГўВҖВңOh, well, what do I care?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAs a matter of fact,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, approaching, ГўВҖВңI am much
afraid that I am the involuntary cause of his absence.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat, you, count?ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars, signing; ГўВҖВңif you are, take
care, for I shall never forgive you.ГўВҖВқ

Andrea pricked up his ears.

ГўВҖВңBut it is not my fault, as I shall endeavor to prove.ГўВҖВқ

Everyone listened eagerly; Monte Cristo who so rarely opened his lips,
was about to speak.

ГўВҖВңYou remember,ГўВҖВқ said the count, during the most profound silence, ГўВҖВңthat
the unhappy wretch who came to rob me died at my house; the supposition
is that he was stabbed by his accomplice, on attempting to leave it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВңIn order that his wounds might be examined he was undressed, and his
clothes were thrown into a corner, where the police picked them up,
with the exception of the waistcoat, which they overlooked.ГўВҖВқ

Andrea turned pale, and drew towards the door; he saw a cloud rising in
the horizon, which appeared to forebode a coming storm.

ГўВҖВңWell, this waistcoat was discovered today, covered with blood, and
with a hole over the heart.ГўВҖВқ The ladies screamed, and two or three
prepared to faint. ГўВҖВңIt was brought to me. No one could guess what the
dirty rag could be; I alone suspected that it was the waistcoat of the
murdered man. My valet, in examining this mournful relic, felt a paper
in the pocket and drew it out; it was a letter addressed to you,
baron.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo me?ГўВҖВқ cried Danglars.

ГўВҖВңYes, indeed, to you; I succeeded in deciphering your name under the
blood with which the letter was stained,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, amid
the general outburst of amazement.

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ asked Madame Danglars, looking at her husband with uneasiness,
ГўВҖВңhow could that prevent M. de VillefortГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn this simple way, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңthe waistcoat and
the letter were both what is termed circumstantial evidence; I
therefore sent them to the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney. You understand, my dear
baron, that legal methods are the safest in criminal cases; it was,
perhaps, some plot against you.ГўВҖВқ Andrea looked steadily at Monte Cristo
and disappeared in the second drawing-room.

ГўВҖВңPossibly,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңwas not this murdered man an old
galley-slave?ГўВҖВқ

50025m



ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied the count; ГўВҖВңa felon named Caderousse.ГўВҖВқ Danglars turned
slightly pale; Andrea reached the anteroom beyond the little
drawing-room.

ГўВҖВңBut go on signing,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI perceive that my story has
caused a general emotion, and I beg to apologize to you, baroness, and
to Mademoiselle Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

The baroness, who had signed, returned the pen to the notary.

ГўВҖВңPrince Cavalcanti,ГўВҖВқ said the latter; ГўВҖВңPrince Cavalcanti, where are
you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAndrea, Andrea,ГўВҖВқ repeated several young people, who were already on
sufficiently intimate terms with him to call him by his Christian name.

ГўВҖВңCall the prince; inform him that it is his turn to sign,ГўВҖВқ cried
Danglars to one of the floorkeepers.

But at the same instant the crowd of guests rushed in alarm into the
principal salon as if some frightful monster had entered the
apartments, _quГғВҰrens quem devoret_. There was, indeed, reason to
retreat, to be alarmed, and to scream. An officer was placing two
soldiers at the door of each drawing-room, and was advancing towards
Danglars, preceded by a commissary of police, girded with his scarf.
Madame Danglars uttered a scream and fainted. Danglars, who thought
himself threatened (certain consciences are never calm),ГўВҖВ”Danglars even
before his guests showed a countenance of abject terror.

ГўВҖВңWhat is the matter, sir?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo, advancing to meet the
commissioner.

ГўВҖВңWhich of you gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ asked the magistrate, without replying to the
count, ГўВҖВңanswers to the name of Andrea Cavalcanti?ГўВҖВқ

A cry of astonishment was heard from all parts of the room. They
searched; they questioned.

ГўВҖВңBut who then is Andrea Cavalcanti?ГўВҖВқ asked Danglars in amazement.

ГўВҖВңA galley-slave, escaped from confinement at Toulon.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what crime has he committed?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHe is accused,ГўВҖВқ said the commissary with his inflexible voice, ГўВҖВңof
having assassinated the man named Caderousse, his former companion in
prison, at the moment he was making his escape from the house of the
Count of Monte Cristo.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo cast a rapid glance around him. Andrea was gone.



 Chapter 97. The Departure for Belgium

A few minutes after the scene of confusion produced in the salons of M.
Danglars by the unexpected appearance of the brigade of soldiers, and
by the disclosure which had followed, the mansion was deserted with as
much rapidity as if a case of plague or of cholera morbus had broken
out among the guests.

In a few minutes, through all the doors, down all the staircases, by
every exit, everyone hastened to retire, or rather to fly; for it was a
situation where the ordinary condolences,ГўВҖВ”which even the best friends
are so eager to offer in great catastrophes,ГўВҖВ”were seen to be utterly
futile. There remained in the bankerГўВҖВҷs house only Danglars, closeted in
his study, and making his statement to the officer of gendarmes; Madame
Danglars, terrified, in the boudoir with which we are acquainted; and
EugГғВ©nie, who with haughty air and disdainful lip had retired to her
room with her inseparable companion, Mademoiselle Louise dГўВҖВҷArmilly.

As for the numerous servants (more numerous that evening than usual,
for their number was augmented by cooks and butlers from the CafГғВ© de
Paris), venting on their employers their anger at what they termed the
insult to which they had been subjected, they collected in groups in
the hall, in the kitchens, or in their rooms, thinking very little of
their duty, which was thus naturally interrupted. Of all this
household, only two persons deserve our notice; these are Mademoiselle
EugГғВ©nie Danglars and Mademoiselle Louise dГўВҖВҷArmilly.

The betrothed had retired, as we said, with haughty air, disdainful
lip, and the demeanor of an outraged queen, followed by her companion,
who was paler and more disturbed than herself. On reaching her room
EugГғВ©nie locked her door, while Louise fell on a chair.

ГўВҖВңAh, what a dreadful thing,ГўВҖВқ said the young musician; ГўВҖВңwho would have
suspected it? M. Andrea Cavalcanti a murdererГўВҖВ”a galley-slave escapedГўВҖВ”a
convict!ГўВҖВқ

An ironical smile curled the lip of EugГғВ©nie. ГўВҖВңIn truth, I was fated,ГўВҖВқ
said she. ГўВҖВңI escaped the Morcerf only to fall into the Cavalcanti.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, do not confound the two, EugГғВ©nie.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHold your tongue! The men are all infamous, and I am happy to be able
now to do more than detest themГўВҖВ”I despise them.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat shall we do?ГўВҖВқ asked Louise.

ГўВҖВңWhat shall we do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy, the same we had intended doing three days sinceГўВҖВ”set off.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВ”although you are not now going to be married, you intend
stillГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen, Louise. I hate this life of the fashionable world, always
ordered, measured, ruled, like our music-paper. What I have always
wished for, desired, and coveted, is the life of an artist, free and
independent, relying only on my own resources, and accountable only to
myself. Remain here? What for?ГўВҖВ”that they may try, a month hence, to
marry me again; and to whom?ГўВҖВ”M. Debray, perhaps, as it was once
proposed. No, Louise, no! This eveningГўВҖВҷs adventure will serve for my
excuse. I did not seek one, I did not ask for one. God sends me this,
and I hail it joyfully!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow strong and courageous you are!ГўВҖВқ said the fair, frail girl to her
brunette companion.

ГўВҖВңDid you not yet know me? Come, Louise, let us talk of our affairs. The
post-chaiseГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWas happily bought three days since.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave you had it sent where we are to go for it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOur passport?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHere it is.ГўВҖВқ

And EugГғВ©nie, with her usual precision, opened a printed paper, and
read:

ГўВҖВңM. LГғВ©on dГўВҖВҷArmilly, twenty years of age; profession, artist; hair
black, eyes black; travelling with his sister.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCapital! How did you get this passport?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhen I went to ask M. de Monte Cristo for letters to the directors of
the theatres at Rome and Naples, I expressed my fears of travelling as
a woman; he perfectly understood them, and undertook to procure for me
a manГўВҖВҷs passport, and two days after I received this, to which I have
added with my own hand, ГўВҖВҳtravelling with his sister.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

50035m



ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie cheerfully, ГўВҖВңwe have then only to pack up our
trunks; we shall start the evening of the signing of the contract,

ГўВҖВңBut consider the matter seriously, EugГғВ©nie!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I am done with considering! I am tired of hearing only of market

understand?ГўВҖВ”air, liberty, melody of birds, plains of Lombardy, Venetian

The young girl to whom this question was addressed drew from an inlaid

ГўВҖВңTwenty-three thousand francs,ГўВҖВқ said she.

ГўВҖВңAnd as much, at least, in pearls, diamonds, and jewels,ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie.
ГўВҖВңWe are rich. With forty-five thousand francs we can live like

so that if one of us had the misfortune to lose her treasure, the other
would still have hers left. Now, the portmanteauГўВҖВ”let us make hasteГўВҖВ”the

ГўВҖВңStop!ГўВҖВқ said Louise, going to listen at Madame DanglarsГўВҖВҷ door.

ГўВҖВңWhat do you fear?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThat we may be discovered.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe door is locked.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey may tell us to open it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey may if they like, but we will not.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are a perfect Amazon, EugГғВ©nie!ГўВҖВқ And the two young girls began to
heap into a trunk all the things they thought they should require.

ГўВҖВңThere now,ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie, ГўВҖВңwhile I change my costume do you lock the
portmanteau.ГўВҖВқ Louise pressed with all the strength of her little hands
on the top of the portmanteau.

ГўВҖВңBut I cannot,ГўВҖВқ said she; ГўВҖВңI am not strong enough; do you shut it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, you do well to ask,ГўВҖВқ said EugГғВ©nie, laughing; ГўВҖВңI forgot that I was

And the young girl, kneeling on the top, pressed the two parts of the

ГўВҖВңHere,ГўВҖВқ said she, ГўВҖВңyou see I have thought of everything; with this

ГўВҖВңBut you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I am never cold, you know! Besides, with these menГўВҖВҷs clothesГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWill you dress here?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShall you have time?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo not be uneasy, you little coward! All our servants are busy,
discussing the grand affair. Besides, what is there astonishing, when
you think of the grief I ought to be in, that I shut myself up?ГўВҖВ”tell

ГўВҖВңNo, trulyГўВҖВ”you comfort me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCome and help me.ГўВҖВқ

From the same drawer she took a manГўВҖВҷs complete costume, from the boots
to the coat, and a provision of linen, where there was nothing
superfluous, but every requisite. Then, with a promptitude which
indicated that this was not the first time she had amused herself by
adopting the garb of the opposite sex, EugГғВ©nie drew on the boots and
pantaloons, tied her cravat, buttoned her waistcoat up to the throat,
and put on a coat which admirably fitted her beautiful figure.
