ГўВҖВңAlas, were it only for my own sake, I should prefer to die!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou must not confide in anyoneГўВҖВ”not even in your father.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy father is not engaged in this fearful plot, is he, sir?ГўВҖВқ asked
Valentine, clasping her hands.

ГўВҖВңNo; and yet your father, a man accustomed to judicial accusations,
ought to have known that all these deaths have not happened naturally;
it is he who should have watched over youГўВҖВ”he should have occupied my
placeГўВҖВ”he should have emptied that glassГўВҖВ”he should have risen against
the assassin. Spectre against spectre!ГўВҖВқ he murmured in a low voice, as
he concluded his sentence.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңI will do all I can to live, for there are two
beings who love me and will die if I dieГўВҖВ”my grandfather and
Maximilian.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will watch over them as I have over you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, sir, do as you will with me;ГўВҖВқ and then she added, in a low
voice, ГўВҖВңoh, heavens, what will befall me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhatever may happen, Valentine, do not be alarmed; though you suffer;
though you lose sight, hearing, consciousness, fear nothing; though you
should awake and be ignorant where you are, still do not fear; even
though you should find yourself in a sepulchral vault or coffin.
Reassure yourself, then, and say to yourself: ГўВҖВҳAt this moment, a
friend, a father, who lives for my happiness and that of Maximilian,
watches over me!ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAlas, alas, what a fearful extremity!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңValentine, would you rather denounce your stepmother?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI would rather die a hundred timesГўВҖВ”oh, yes, die!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, you will not die; but will you promise me, whatever happens, that
you will not complain, but hope?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will think of Maximilian!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are my own darling child, Valentine! I alone can save you, and I
will.ГўВҖВқ

Valentine in the extremity of her terror joined her hands,ГўВҖВ”for she felt
that the moment had arrived to ask for courage,ГўВҖВ”and began to pray, and
while uttering little more than incoherent words, she forgot that her
white shoulders had no other covering than her long hair, and that the
pulsations of her heart could be seen through the lace of her
nightdress. Monte Cristo gently laid his hand on the young girlГўВҖВҷs arm,
drew the velvet coverlet close to her throat, and said with a paternal
smile:

ГўВҖВңMy child, believe in my devotion to you as you believe in the goodness
of Providence and the love of Maximilian.ГўВҖВқ Valentine gave him a look
full of gratitude, and remained as docile as a child.

Then he drew from his waistcoat-pocket the little emerald box, raised
the golden lid, and took from it a pastille about the size of a pea,
which he placed in her hand. She took it, and looked attentively on the
count; there was an expression on the face of her intrepid protector
which commanded her veneration. She evidently interrogated him by her
look.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said he.

Valentine carried the pastille to her mouth, and swallowed it.

ГўВҖВңAnd now, my dear child, adieu for the present. I will try and gain a
little sleep, for you are saved.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo,ГўВҖВқ said Valentine, ГўВҖВңwhatever happens, I promise you not to fear.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo for some time kept his eyes fixed on the young girl, who
gradually fell asleep, yielding to the effects of the narcotic the
count had given her. Then he took the glass, emptied three parts of the
contents in the fireplace, that it might be supposed Valentine had
taken it, and replaced it on the table; then he disappeared, after
throwing a farewell glance on Valentine, who slept with the confidence
and innocence of an angel at the feet of the Lord.



 Chapter 102. Valentine

The night-light continued to burn on the chimney-piece, exhausting the
last drops of oil which floated on the surface of the water. The globe
of the lamp appeared of a reddish hue, and the flame, brightening
before it expired, threw out the last flickerings which in an inanimate
object have been so often compared with the convulsions of a human
creature in its final agonies. A dull and dismal light was shed over
the bedclothes and curtains surrounding the young girl. All noise in
the streets had ceased, and the silence was frightful.

It was then that the door of EdwardГўВҖВҷs room opened, and a head we have
before noticed appeared in the glass opposite; it was Madame de
Villefort, who came to witness the effects of the drink she had
prepared. She stopped in the doorway, listened for a moment to the
flickering of the lamp, the only sound in that deserted room, and then
advanced to the table to see if ValentineГўВҖВҷs glass were empty. It was
still about a quarter full, as we before stated. Madame de Villefort
emptied the contents into the ashes, which she disturbed that they
might the more readily absorb the liquid; then she carefully rinsed the
glass, and wiping it with her handkerchief replaced it on the table.

If anyone could have looked into the room just then he would have
noticed the hesitation with which Madame de Villefort approached the
bed and looked fixedly on Valentine. The dim light, the profound
silence, and the gloomy thoughts inspired by the hour, and still more
by her own conscience, all combined to produce a sensation of fear; the
poisoner was terrified at the contemplation of her own work.

At length she rallied, drew aside the curtain, and leaning over the
pillow gazed intently on Valentine. The young girl no longer breathed,
no breath issued through the half-closed teeth; the white lips no
longer quiveredГўВҖВ”the eyes were suffused with a bluish vapor, and the
long black lashes rested on a cheek white as wax. Madame de Villefort
gazed upon the face so expressive even in its stillness; then she
ventured to raise the coverlet and press her hand upon the young girlГўВҖВҷs
heart. It was cold and motionless. She only felt the pulsation in her
own fingers, and withdrew her hand with a shudder. One arm was hanging
out of the bed; from shoulder to elbow it was moulded after the arms of
Germain PillonГўВҖВҷs ГўВҖВңGraces,ГўВҖВқ23 but the fore-arm seemed to be slightly
distorted by convulsion, and the hand, so delicately formed, was
resting with stiff outstretched fingers on the framework of the bed.
The nails, too, were turning blue.

Madame de Villefort had no longer any doubt; all was overГўВҖВ”she had
consummated the last terrible work she had to accomplish. There was no
more to do in the room, so the poisoner retired stealthily, as though
fearing to hear the sound of her own footsteps; but as she withdrew she
still held aside the curtain, absorbed in the irresistible attraction
always exerted by the picture of death, so long as it is merely
mysterious and does not excite disgust.

The minutes passed; Madame de Villefort could not drop the curtain
which she held like a funeral pall over the head of Valentine. She was
lost in reverie, and the reverie of crime is remorse.

Just then the lamp again flickered; the noise startled Madame de
Villefort, who shuddered and dropped the curtain. Immediately
afterwards the light expired, and the room was plunged in frightful
obscurity, while the clock at that minute struck half-past four.

Overpowered with agitation, the poisoner succeeded in groping her way
to the door, and reached her room in an agony of fear. The darkness
lasted two hours longer; then by degrees a cold light crept through the
Venetian blinds, until at length it revealed the objects in the room.

About this time the nurseГўВҖВҷs cough was heard on the stairs and the woman
entered the room with a cup in her hand. To the tender eye of a father
or a lover, the first glance would have sufficed to reveal ValentineГўВҖВҷs
condition; but to this hireling, Valentine only appeared to sleep.

ГўВҖВңGood,ГўВҖВқ she exclaimed, approaching the table, ГўВҖВңshe has taken part of
her draught; the glass is three-quarters empty.ГўВҖВқ

Then she went to the fireplace and lit the fire, and although she had
just left her bed, she could not resist the temptation offered by
ValentineГўВҖВҷs sleep, so she threw herself into an armchair to snatch a
little more rest. The clock striking eight awoke her. Astonished at the
prolonged slumber of the patient, and frightened to see that the arm
was still hanging out of the bed, she advanced towards Valentine, and
for the first time noticed the white lips. She tried to replace the
arm, but it moved with a frightful rigidity which could not deceive a
sick-nurse. She screamed aloud; then running to the door exclaimed:

ГўВҖВңHelp, help!ГўВҖВқ

50083m



ГўВҖВңWhat is the matter?ГўВҖВқ asked M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, at the foot of the stairs, it
being the hour he usually visited her.

ГўВҖВңWhat is it?ГўВҖВқ asked Villefort, rushing from his room. ГўВҖВңDoctor, do you
hear them call for help?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, yes; let us hasten up; it was in ValentineГўВҖВҷs room.ГўВҖВқ

But before the doctor and the father could reach the room, the servants
who were on the same floor had entered, and seeing Valentine pale and
motionless on her bed, they lifted up their hands towards heaven and
stood transfixed, as though struck by lightening.

ГўВҖВңCall Madame de Villefort!ГўВҖВ”Wake Madame de Villefort!ГўВҖВқ cried the
procureur from the door of his chamber, which apparently he scarcely
dared to leave. But instead of obeying him, the servants stood watching
M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, who ran to Valentine, and raised her in his arms.

ГўВҖВңWhat?ГўВҖВ”this one, too?ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed. ГўВҖВңOh, where will be the end?ГўВҖВқ

Villefort rushed into the room.

ГўВҖВңWhat are you saying, doctor?ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed, raising his hands to
heaven.

ГўВҖВңI say that Valentine is dead!ГўВҖВқ replied dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, in a voice terrible
in its solemn calmness.

50085m



M. de Villefort staggered and buried his head in the bed. On the
exclamation of the doctor and the cry of the father, the servants all
fled with muttered imprecations; they were heard running down the
stairs and through the long passages, then there was a rush in the
court, afterwards all was still; they had, one and all, deserted the
accursed house.

Just then, Madame de Villefort, in the act of slipping on her
dressing-gown, threw aside the drapery and for a moment stood
motionless, as though interrogating the occupants of the room, while
she endeavored to call up some rebellious tears. On a sudden she
stepped, or rather bounded, with outstretched arms, towards the table.
She saw dГўВҖВҷAvrigny curiously examining the glass, which she felt certain
of having emptied during the night. It was now a third full, just as it
was when she threw the contents into the ashes. The spectre of
Valentine rising before the poisoner would have alarmed her less. It
was, indeed, the same color as the draught she had poured into the
glass, and which Valentine had drunk; it was indeed the poison, which
could not deceive M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, which he now examined so closely; it
was doubtless a miracle from heaven, that, notwithstanding her
precautions, there should be some trace, some proof remaining to reveal
the crime.

While Madame de Villefort remained rooted to the spot like a statue of
terror, and Villefort, with his head hidden in the bedclothes, saw
nothing around him, dГўВҖВҷAvrigny approached the window, that he might the
better examine the contents of the glass, and dipping the tip of his
finger in, tasted it.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ he exclaimed, ГўВҖВңit is no longer brucine that is used; let me see
what it is!ГўВҖВқ

Then he ran to one of the cupboards in ValentineГўВҖВҷs room, which had been
transformed into a medicine closet, and taking from its silver case a
small bottle of nitric acid, dropped a little of it into the liquor,
which immediately changed to a blood-red color.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, in a voice in which the horror of a judge
unveiling the truth was mingled with the delight of a student making a
discovery.

Madame de Villefort was overpowered; her eyes first flashed and then
swam, she staggered towards the door and disappeared. Directly
afterwards the distant sound of a heavy weight falling on the ground
was heard, but no one paid any attention to it; the nurse was engaged
in watching the chemical analysis, and Villefort was still absorbed in

ГўВҖВңGo to the assistance of Madame de Villefort,ГўВҖВқ he said to the nurse.
ГўВҖВңMadame de Villefort is ill.ГўВҖВқ

50087m



ГўВҖВңBut Mademoiselle de VillefortГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ stammered the nurse.

ГўВҖВңMademoiselle de Villefort no longer requires help,ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny,

ГўВҖВңDead,ГўВҖВ”dead!ГўВҖВқ groaned forth Villefort, in a paroxysm of grief, which

ГўВҖВңDead!ГўВҖВқ repeated a third voice. ГўВҖВңWho said Valentine was dead?ГўВҖВқ

The two men turned round, and saw Morrel standing at the door, pale and
terror-stricken. This is what had happened. At the usual time, Morrel
had presented himself at the little door leading to NoirtierГўВҖВҷs room.
Contrary to custom, the door was open, and having no occasion to ring
he entered. He waited for a moment in the hall and called for a servant
to conduct him to M. Noirtier; but no one answered, the servants
having, as we know, deserted the house. Morrel had no particular reason
for uneasiness; Monte Cristo had promised him that Valentine should
live, and so far he had always fulfilled his word. Every night the
count had given him news, which was the next morning confirmed by

ГўВҖВңHow are you, sir?ГўВҖВқ asked Morrel, with a sickness of heart.

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ answered the old man, by closing his eyes; but his appearance
manifested increasing uneasiness.

ГўВҖВңYou are thoughtful, sir,ГўВҖВқ continued Morrel; ГўВҖВңyou want something; shall
I call one of the servants?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ replied Noirtier.

Morrel pulled the bell, but though he nearly broke the cord no one

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Morrel, ГўВҖВңwhy do they not come? Is anyone ill in the

ГўВҖВңYes, yes,ГўВҖВқ signed Noirtier.

Maximilian tried to speak, but he could articulate nothing; he

ГўВҖВңYes, yes, yes!ГўВҖВқ continued the old man.

50089m



Maximilian rushed up the little staircase, while NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eyes seemed
to say,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңQuicker, quicker!ГўВҖВқ

In a minute the young man darted through several rooms, till at length

There was no occasion to push the door, it was wide open. A sob was the

ГўВҖВңDead,ГўВҖВ”dead!ГўВҖВқ

50090m




 Chapter 103. Maximilian

Villefort rose, half-ashamed of being surprised in such a paroxysm of
grief. The terrible office he had held for twenty-five years had
succeeded in making him more or less than man. His glance, at first
wandering, fixed itself upon Morrel. ГўВҖВңWho are you, sir,ГўВҖВқ he asked,
ГўВҖВңthat forget that this is not the manner to enter a house stricken with
death? Go, sir, go!ГўВҖВқ

But Morrel remained motionless; he could not detach his eyes from that
disordered bed, and the pale corpse of the young girl who was lying on

ГўВҖВңGo!ГўВҖВ”do you hear?ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, while dГўВҖВҷAvrigny advanced to lead

fearful spectacle was Noirtier being pushed towards the bed, his face
expressing all his meaning, and his eyes supplying the want of every

with his father, something terrible had happened.

ГўВҖВңSee what they have done!ГўВҖВқ cried Morrel, with one hand leaning on the

Villefort drew back and looked with astonishment on the young man, who,

DГўВҖВҷAvrigny rushed towards the old man and made him inhale a powerful

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ cried Morrel, seizing the moist hand of the paralytic, ГўВҖВңthey ask

As for the old man, his chest heaved with his panting respiration. One

At length, happier than the young man, who sobbed without weeping,

ГўВҖВңTell them,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel in a hoarse voice, ГўВҖВңtell them that I am her

The young man overwhelmed by the weight of his anguish, fell heavily on

But Morrel saw nothing; he had grasped the hand of Valentine, and

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ said he to Maximilian, ГўВҖВңyou say you loved Valentine, that you

50093m

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean, sir?ГўВҖВқ asked Villefort, trembling at the new idea

ГўВҖВңGentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, reading all that passed through the minds of

50095m



ГўВҖВңI rave?ГўВҖВқ said Morrel; ГўВҖВңwell, then, I appeal to M. dГўВҖВҷAvrigny himself.

We have before stated that all the servants had fled. M. de Villefort

50099m



ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ answered the doctor laconically, dropping the sheet he had

ГўВҖВңThe nearest,ГўВҖВқ said the district doctor, ГўВҖВңis a good Italian abbГғВ©, who

ГўВҖВңDГўВҖВҷAvrigny,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, ГўВҖВңbe so kind, I beseech you, as to
accompany this gentleman. Here is the key of the door, so that you can
go in and out as you please; you will bring the priest with you, and
will oblige me by introducing him into my childГўВҖВҷs room.ГўВҖВқ

50101m



ГўВҖВңDo you wish to see him?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI only wish to be alone. You will excuse me, will you not? A priest
can understand a fatherГўВҖВҷs grief.ГўВҖВқ

And M. de Villefort, giving the key to dГўВҖВҷAvrigny, again bade farewell
to the strange doctor, and retired to his study, where he began to
work. For some temperaments work is a remedy for all afflictions.

As the doctors entered the street, they saw a man in a cassock standing
on the threshold of the next door.

ГўВҖВңThis is the abbГғВ© of whom I spoke,ГўВҖВқ said the doctor to dГўВҖВҷAvrigny.
DГўВҖВҷAvrigny accosted the priest.

ГўВҖВңSir,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңare you disposed to confer a great obligation on an
unhappy father who has just lost his daughter? I mean M. de Villefort,
the kingГўВҖВҷs attorney.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said the priest, in a marked Italian accent; ГўВҖВңyes, I have heard
that death is in that house.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I need not tell you what kind of service he requires of you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was about to offer myself, sir,ГўВҖВқ said the priest; ГўВҖВңit is our mission
to forestall our duties.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is a young girl.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI know it, sir; the servants who fled from the house informed me. I
also know that her name is Valentine, and I have already prayed for
her.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThank you, sir,ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny; ГўВҖВңsince you have commenced your sacred
office, deign to continue it. Come and watch by the dead, and all the
wretched family will be grateful to you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI am going, sir; and I do not hesitate to say that no prayers will be
more fervent than mine.ГўВҖВқ

DГўВҖВҷAvrigny took the priestГўВҖВҷs hand, and without meeting Villefort, who
was engaged in his study, they reached ValentineГўВҖВҷs room, which on the
following night was to be occupied by the undertakers. On entering the
room, NoirtierГўВҖВҷs eyes met those of the abbГғВ©, and no doubt he read some
particular expression in them, for he remained in the room. DГўВҖВҷAvrigny
recommended the attention of the priest to the living as well as to the
dead, and the abbГғВ© promised to devote his prayers to Valentine and his
attentions to Noirtier.

In order, doubtless, that he might not be disturbed while fulfilling
his sacred mission, the priest rose as soon as dГўВҖВҷAvrigny departed, and
not only bolted the door through which the doctor had just left, but
also that leading to Madame de VillefortГўВҖВҷs room.



 Chapter 104. DanglarsГўВҖВҷ Signature

The next morning dawned dull and cloudy. During the night the
undertakers had executed their melancholy office, and wrapped the
corpse in the winding-sheet, which, whatever may be said about the
equality of death, is at least a last proof of the luxury so pleasing
in life. This winding-sheet was nothing more than a beautiful piece of
cambric, which the young girl had bought a fortnight before.

During the evening two men, engaged for the purpose, had carried
Noirtier from ValentineГўВҖВҷs room into his own, and contrary to all
expectation there was no difficulty in withdrawing him from his child.
The AbbГғВ© Busoni had watched till daylight, and then left without
calling anyone. DГўВҖВҷAvrigny returned about eight oГўВҖВҷclock in the morning;
he met Villefort on his way to NoirtierГўВҖВҷs room, and accompanied him to
see how the old man had slept. They found him in the large armchair,
which served him for a bed, enjoying a calm, nay, almost a smiling
sleep. They both stood in amazement at the door.

ГўВҖВңSee,ГўВҖВқ said dГўВҖВҷAvrigny to Villefort, ГўВҖВңnature knows how to alleviate the
deepest sorrow. No one can say that M. Noirtier did not love his child,
and yet he sleeps.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, you are right,ГўВҖВқ replied Villefort, surprised; ГўВҖВңhe sleeps, indeed!
And this is the more strange, since the least contradiction keeps him
awake all night.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGrief has stunned him,ГўВҖВқ replied dГўВҖВҷAvrigny; and they both returned
thoughtfully to the procureurГўВҖВҷs study.

ГўВҖВңSee, I have not slept,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort, showing his undisturbed bed;
ГўВҖВңgrief does not stun me. I have not been in bed for two nights; but
then look at my desk; see what I have written during these two days and
nights. I have filled those papers, and have made out the accusation
against the assassin Benedetto. Oh, work, work,ГўВҖВ”my passion, my joy, my
delight,ГўВҖВ”it is for thee to alleviate my sorrows!ГўВҖВқ and he convulsively
grasped the hand of dГўВҖВҷAvrigny.

ГўВҖВңDo you require my services now?ГўВҖВқ asked dГўВҖВҷAvrigny.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Villefort; ГўВҖВңonly return again at eleven oГўВҖВҷclock; at twelve
theГўВҖВ”theГўВҖВ”oh, Heavens, my poor, poor child!ГўВҖВқ and the procureur again
becoming a man, lifted up his eyes and groaned.

ГўВҖВңShall you be present in the reception-room?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; I have a cousin who has undertaken this sad office. I shall work,
doctorГўВҖВ”when I work I forget everything.ГўВҖВқ

And, indeed, no sooner had the doctor left the room, than he was again
absorbed in work. On the doorsteps dГўВҖВҷAvrigny met the cousin whom
Villefort had mentioned, a personage as insignificant in our story as
in the world he occupiedГўВҖВ”one of those beings designed from their birth
to make themselves useful to others. He was punctual, dressed in black,
with crape around his hat, and presented himself at his cousinГўВҖВҷs with a
face made up for the occasion, and which he could alter as might be
required.

At eleven oГўВҖВҷclock the mourning-coaches rolled into the paved court, and
the Rue du Faubourg Saint-HonorГғВ© was filled with a crowd of idlers,
equally pleased to witness the festivities or the mourning of the rich,
and who rush with the same avidity to a funeral procession as to the
marriage of a duchess.

Gradually the reception-room filled, and some of our old friends made
their appearanceГўВҖВ”we mean Debray, ChГғВўteau-Renaud, and Beauchamp,
accompanied by all the leading men of the day at the bar, in
literature, or the army, for M. de Villefort moved in the first
Parisian circles, less owing to his social position than to his
personal merit.

The cousin standing at the door ushered in the guests, and it was
rather a relief to the indifferent to see a person as unmoved as
themselves, and who did not exact a mournful face or force tears, as
would have been the case with a father, a brother, or a lover. Those
who were acquainted soon formed into little groups. One of them was
made of Debray, ChГғВўteau-Renaud, and Beauchamp.

ГўВҖВңPoor girl,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, like the rest, paying an involuntary tribute

most three weeks ago, about to sign that contract?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed, no,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDid you know her?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI spoke to her once or twice at Madame de MorcerfГўВҖВҷs, among the rest;
she appeared to me charming, though rather melancholy. Where is her

ГўВҖВңShe is spending the day with the wife of the worthy gentleman who is

50105m



ГўВҖВңWho is he?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhom do you mean?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe gentleman who receives us? Is he a deputy?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, no. I am condemned to witness those gentlemen every day,ГўВҖВқ said

ГўВҖВңI am seeking the Count of Monte CristoГўВҖВқ said the young man.

ГўВҖВңI met him on the boulevard, on my way here,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp. ГўВҖВңI think

ГўВҖВңI believe so,ГўВҖВқ replied the secretary with slight uneasiness. ГўВҖВңBut

ГўВҖВңMorrel? Do they know him?ГўВҖВқ asked ChГғВўteau-Renaud. ГўВҖВңI think he has only

ГўВҖВңStill, he ought to have been here,ГўВҖВқ said Debray; ГўВҖВңI wonder what will

Beauchamp told the truth when he said that on his way to the funeral he
had met Monte Cristo, who was directing his steps towards the Rue de la
ChaussГғВ©e dГўВҖВҷAntin, to M. DanglarsГўВҖВҷ. The banker saw the carriage of the

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said he, extending his hand to Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI suppose you
have come to sympathize with me, for indeed misfortune has taken

ГўВҖВңBesides what?ГўВҖВқ asked the Count.

ГўВҖВңAlas, do you not know?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat new calamity?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy daughterГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMademoiselle Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңEugГғВ©nie has left us!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGood heavens, what are you telling me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe truth, my dear count. Oh, how happy you must be in not having
either wife or children!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you think so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed I do.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd so Mademoiselle DanglarsГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShe could not endure the insult offered to us by that wretch, so she
asked permission to travel.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd is she gone?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe other night she left.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWith Madame Danglars?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo, with a relation. But still, we have quite lost our dear EugГғВ©nie;
for I doubt whether her pride will ever allow her to return to France.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStill, baron,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңfamily griefs, or indeed any other
affliction which would crush a man whose child was his only treasure,
are endurable to a millionaire. Philosophers may well say, and
practical men will always support the opinion, that money mitigates
many trials; and if you admit the efficacy of this sovereign balm, you
ought to be very easily consoledГўВҖВ”you, the king of finance, the focus of

Danglars looked at him askance, as though to ascertain whether he spoke
seriously.

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ he answered, ГўВҖВңif a fortune brings consolation, I ought to be
consoled; I am rich.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңSo rich, dear sir, that your fortune resembles the pyramids; if you
wished to demolish them you could not, and if it were possible, you
would not dare!ГўВҖВқ

Danglars smiled at the good-natured pleasantry of the count. ГўВҖВңThat
reminds me,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңthat when you entered I was on the point of
signing five little bonds; I have already signed two: will you allow me
to do the same to the others?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPray do so.ГўВҖВқ

There was a momentГўВҖВҷs silence, during which the noise of the bankerГўВҖВҷs
pen was alone heard, while Monte Cristo examined the gilt mouldings on
the ceiling.

ГўВҖВңAre they Spanish, Haitian, or Neapolitan bonds?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo.

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, smiling, ГўВҖВңthey are bonds on the bank of France,
payable to bearer. Stay, count,ГўВҖВқ he added, ГўВҖВңyou, who may be called the
emperor, if I claim the title of king of finance, have you many pieces

The count took into his hands the papers, which Danglars had so proudly
presented to him, and read:ГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳTo the Governor of the Bank. Please pay to my order, from the fund
deposited by me, the sum of a million, and charge the same to my

ГўВҖВңBaron Danglars.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOne, two, three, four, five,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңfive millionsГўВҖВ”why
what a CrГ…В“sus you are!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThis is how I transact business,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВңIt is really wonderful,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңabove all, if, as I suppose,
it is payable at sight.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is, indeed,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВңIt is a fine thing to have such credit; really, it is only in France
these things are done. Five millions on five little scraps of paper!ГўВҖВ”it
must be seen to be believed.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou do not doubt it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou say so with an accentГўВҖВ”stay, you shall be convinced; take my clerk

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo folding the five notes, ГўВҖВңmost decidedly not;
the thing is so curious, I will make the experiment myself. I am
credited on you for six millions. I have drawn nine hundred thousand
francs, you therefore still owe me five millions and a hundred thousand
francs. I will take the five scraps of paper that I now hold as bonds,

And Monte Cristo placed the bonds in his pocket with one hand, while

ГўВҖВңWhat,ГўВҖВқ he stammered, ГўВҖВңdo you mean to keep that money? Excuse me,

ГўВҖВңOh, well, then,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI am not particular about these

preparation the house of Danglars had paid me five millions without a

Suddenly he rallied, made a violent effort to restrain himself, and
then a smile gradually widened the features of his disturbed
countenance.

50109m



ГўВҖВңCertainly,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңyour receipt is money.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh dear, yes; and if you were at Rome, the house of Thomson & French
would make no more difficulty about paying the money on my receipt than

ГўВҖВңPardon me, count, pardon me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen I may keep this money?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, while the perspiration started from the roots of

Monte Cristo replaced the notes in his pocket with that indescribable

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңno, decidedly no; keep my signatures. But you

ГўВҖВңCertainly, I excuse you,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo graciously, ГўВҖВңand pocket

ГўВҖВңBut,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, ГўВҖВңthere is still a sum of one hundred thousand

ГўВҖВңOh, a mere nothing,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңThe balance would come to

ГўВҖВңI never joke with bankers,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo in a freezing manner,

ГўВҖВңM. de Boville, Receiver-General of the charities.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi_,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo; ГўВҖВңI think I arrived just in time to

Danglars again became pale, and hastened to conduct the count out.
Monte Cristo exchanged a ceremonious bow with M. de Boville, who was
standing in the waiting-room, and who was introduced into DanglarsГўВҖВҷ
room as soon as the count had left.

The countГўВҖВҷs serious face was illumined by a faint smile, as he noticed
the portfolio which the receiver-general held in his hand. At the door
he found his carriage, and was immediately driven to the bank.
Meanwhile Danglars, repressing all emotion, advanced to meet the
receiver-general. We need not say that a smile of condescension was
stamped upon his lips.

ГўВҖВңGood-morning, creditor,ГўВҖВқ said he; ГўВҖВңfor I wager anything it is the
creditor who visits me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou are right, baron,ГўВҖВқ answered M. de Boville; ГўВҖВңthe charities present

ГўВҖВңAnd yet they say orphans are to be pitied,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, wishing to
prolong the jest. ГўВҖВңPoor things!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHere I am in their name,ГўВҖВқ said M. de Boville; ГўВҖВңbut did you receive my
letter yesterday?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have brought my receipt.ГўВҖВқ

50111m



ГўВҖВңMy dear M. de Boville, your widows and orphans must oblige me by

ГўВҖВңYes; well?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell, M. de Monte Cristo has just carried off their five millions.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHow so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThe count has an unlimited credit upon me; a credit opened by Thomson

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said Boville, with a tone of entire incredulity, ГўВҖВңfive millions

ГўВҖВңPerhaps he knows you, though you do not know him; M. de Monte Cristo

ГўВҖВңFive millions!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIt is really true,ГўВҖВқ said M. de Boville.

ГўВҖВңDo you know the house of Thomson & French?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, I once had business to transact with it to the amount of 200,000

ГўВҖВңIt is one of the best houses in Europe,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars, carelessly

ГўВҖВңAnd he had five millions in your hands alone! Why, this Count of Monte

ГўВҖВңIndeed I do not know what he is; he has three unlimited creditsГўВҖВ”one on

M. de Boville manifested signs of extraordinary admiration.

ГўВҖВңOh, you may make sure of him; his charities alone amount to 20,000

ГўВҖВңIt is magnificent! I will set before him the example of Madame de

ГўВҖВңWhat example?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThey gave all their fortune to the hospitals.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat fortune?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause they would not spend money so guiltily acquired.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd what are they to live upon?ГўВҖВқ

50113m



ГўВҖВңWell, I must confess, these are scruples.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI registered their deed of gift yesterday.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, not muchГўВҖВ”from twelve to thirteen hundred thousand francs. But to

ГўВҖВңAt two oГўВҖВҷclock.ГўВҖВқ

M. de Boville said nothing, but nodded his head, and took up the

ГўВҖВңNow I think of it, you can do better,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars.

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi!_ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңI prefer waiting till tomorrow. What a

ГўВҖВңI thought, perhaps,ГўВҖВқ said Danglars with supreme impertinence, ГўВҖВңthat

ГўВҖВңAnd if that were the case it would be worth while to make some

ГўВҖВңThank you, no, sir.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen it will be tomorrow.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; but without fail.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, you are laughing at me; send tomorrow at twelve, and the bank
shall be notified.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will come myself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBetter still, since it will afford me the pleasure of seeing you.ГўВҖВқ
They shook hands.

ГўВҖВңBy the way,ГўВҖВқ said M. de Boville, ГўВҖВңare you not going to the funeral of

ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ said the banker; ГўВҖВңI have appeared rather ridiculous since that

ГўВҖВңBah, you are wrong. How were you to blame in that affair?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListenГўВҖВ”when one bears an irreproachable name, as I do, one is rather

ГўВҖВңEverybody pities you, sir; and, above all, Mademoiselle Danglars!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoor EugГғВ©nie!ГўВҖВқ said Danglars; ГўВҖВңdo you know she is going to embrace a

ГўВҖВңNo.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, it is terrible!ГўВҖВқ and M. de Boville retired with this exclamation,

ГўВҖВңFool!ГўВҖВқ

Then he double-locked his door, emptied all his drawers, collected
about fifty thousand francs in bank-notes, burned several papers, left
others exposed to view, and then commenced writing a letter which he
addressed:

ГўВҖВңTo Madame la Baronne Danglars.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI will place it on her table myself tonight,ГўВҖВқ he murmured. Then taking
a passport from his drawer he said,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңGood, it is available for two
months longer.ГўВҖВқ



 Chapter 105. The Cemetery of PГғВЁre-Lachaise

M. de Boville had indeed met the funeral procession which was taking
Valentine to her last home on earth. The weather was dull and stormy, a
cold wind shook the few remaining yellow leaves from the boughs of the
trees, and scattered them among the crowd which filled the boulevards.
M. de Villefort, a true Parisian, considered the cemetery of
PГғВЁre-Lachaise alone worthy of receiving the mortal remains of a
Parisian family; there alone the corpses belonging to him would be
surrounded by worthy associates. He had therefore purchased a vault,
which was quickly occupied by members of his family. On the front of
the monument was inscribed: ГўВҖВңThe families of Saint-MГғВ©ran and
Villefort,ГўВҖВқ for such had been the last wish expressed by poor RenГғВ©e,
ValentineГўВҖВҷs mother. The pompous procession therefore wended its way
towards PГғВЁre-Lachaise from the Faubourg Saint-HonorГғВ©. Having crossed
Paris, it passed through the Faubourg du Temple, then leaving the
exterior boulevards, it reached the cemetery. More than fifty private
carriages followed the twenty mourning-coaches, and behind them more
than five hundred persons joined in the procession on foot.

50117m



These last consisted of all the young people whom ValentineГўВҖВҷs death had
struck like a thunderbolt, and who, notwithstanding the raw chilliness
of the season, could not refrain from paying a last tribute to the
memory of the beautiful, chaste, and adorable girl, thus cut off in the
flower of her youth.

As they left Paris, an equipage with four horses, at full speed, was
seen to draw up suddenly; it contained Monte Cristo. The count left the
carriage and mingled in the crowd who followed on foot. ChГғВўteau-Renaud
perceived him and immediately alighting from his _coupГғВ©_, joined him;
Beauchamp did the same.

The count looked attentively through every opening in the crowd; he was
evidently watching for someone, but his search ended in disappointment.

50119m



ГўВҖВңWhere is Morrel?ГўВҖВқ he asked; ГўВҖВңdo either of these gentlemen know where
he is?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe have already asked that question,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, ГўВҖВңfor none
of us has seen him.ГўВҖВқ

The count was silent, but continued to gaze around him. At length they
arrived at the cemetery. The piercing eye of Monte Cristo glanced
through clusters of bushes and trees, and was soon relieved from all
anxiety, for seeing a shadow glide between the yew-trees, Monte Cristo
recognized him whom he sought.

One funeral is generally very much like another in this magnificent
metropolis. Black figures are seen scattered over the long white
avenues; the silence of earth and heaven is alone broken by the noise
made by the crackling branches of hedges planted around the monuments;
then follows the melancholy chant of the priests, mingled now and then
with a sob of anguish, escaping from some woman concealed behind a mass
of flowers.

The shadow Monte Cristo had noticed passed rapidly behind the tomb of
AbГғВ©lard and HГғВ©loГғВҜse, placed itself close to the heads of the horses
belonging to the hearse, and following the undertakerГўВҖВҷs men, arrived
with them at the spot appointed for the burial. Each personГўВҖВҷs attention
was occupied. Monte Cristo saw nothing but the shadow, which no one
else observed. Twice the count left the ranks to see whether the object
of his interest had any concealed weapon beneath his clothes. When the
procession stopped, this shadow was recognized as Morrel, who, with his
coat buttoned up to his throat, his face livid, and convulsively
crushing his hat between his fingers, leaned against a tree, situated
on an elevation commanding the mausoleum, so that none of the funeral
details could escape his observation.

Everything was conducted in the usual manner. A few men, the least
impressed of all by the scene, pronounced a discourse, some deploring
this premature death, others expatiating on the grief of the father,
and one very ingenious person quoting the fact that Valentine had
solicited pardon of her father for criminals on whom the arm of justice
was ready to fallГўВҖВ”until at length they exhausted their stores of
metaphor and mournful speeches, elaborate variations on the stanzas of
Malherbe to Du PГғВ©rier.

Monte Cristo heard and saw nothing, or rather he only saw Morrel, whose
calmness had a frightful effect on those who knew what was passing in
his heart.

ГўВҖВңSee,ГўВҖВқ said Beauchamp, pointing out Morrel to Debray. ГўВҖВңWhat is he doing
up there?ГўВҖВқ And they called ChГғВўteau-RenaudГўВҖВҷs attention to him.

ГўВҖВңHow pale he is!ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, shuddering.

ГўВҖВңHe is cold,ГўВҖВқ said Debray.

ГўВҖВңNot at all,ГўВҖВқ said ChГғВўteau-Renaud, slowly; ГўВҖВңI think he is violently
agitated. He is very susceptible.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBah,ГўВҖВқ said Debray; ГўВҖВңhe scarcely knew Mademoiselle de Villefort; you
said so yourself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTrue. Still I remember he danced three times with her at Madame de
MorcerfГўВҖВҷs. Do you recollect that ball, count, where you produced such
an effect?ГўВҖВқ

50121m



ГўВҖВңNo, I do not,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, without even knowing of what or
to whom he was speaking, so much was he occupied in watching Morrel,
who was holding his breath with emotion.

ГўВҖВңThe discourse is over; farewell, gentlemen,ГўВҖВқ said the count,
unceremoniously.

And he disappeared without anyone seeing whither he went.

The funeral being over, the guests returned to Paris. ChГғВўteau-Renaud
looked for a moment for Morrel; but while they were watching the
departure of the count, Morrel had quitted his post, and
ChГғВўteau-Renaud, failing in his search, joined Debray and Beauchamp.

Monte Cristo concealed himself behind a large tomb and awaited the
arrival of Morrel, who by degrees approached the tomb now abandoned by
spectators and workmen. Morrel threw a glance around, but before it
reached the spot occupied by Monte Cristo the latter had advanced yet
nearer, still unperceived. The young man knelt down. The count, with
outstretched neck and glaring eyes, stood in an attitude ready to
pounce upon Morrel upon the first occasion. Morrel bent his head till
it touched the stone, then clutching the grating with both hands, he
murmured:

ГўВҖВңOh, Valentine!ГўВҖВқ

The countГўВҖВҷs heart was pierced by the utterance of these two words; he
stepped forward, and touching the young manГўВҖВҷs shoulder, said:

ГўВҖВңI was looking for you, my friend.ГўВҖВқ Monte Cristo expected a burst of
passion, but he was deceived, for Morrel turning round, said calmly,ГўВҖВ”

ГўВҖВңYou see I was praying.ГўВҖВқ The scrutinizing glance of the count searched
the young man from head to foot. He then seemed more easy.

ГўВҖВңShall I drive you back to Paris?ГўВҖВқ he asked.

ГўВҖВңNo, thank you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңDo you wish anything?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLeave me to pray.ГўВҖВқ

The count withdrew without opposition, but it was only to place himself
in a situation where he could watch every movement of Morrel, who at
length arose, brushed the dust from his knees, and turned towards
Paris, without once looking back. He walked slowly down the Rue de la
Roquette. The count, dismissing his carriage, followed him about a
hundred paces behind. Maximilian crossed the canal and entered the Rue
Meslay by the boulevards.

Five minutes after the door had been closed on MorrelГўВҖВҷs entrance, it
was again opened for the count. Julie was at the entrance of the
garden, where she was attentively watching Penelon, who, entering with
zeal into his profession of gardener, was very busy grafting some
Bengal roses. ГўВҖВңAh, count,ГўВҖВқ she exclaimed, with the delight manifested
by every member of the family whenever he visited the Rue Meslay.

ГўВҖВңMaximilian has just returned, has he not, madame?ГўВҖВқ asked the count.

50123m



ГўВҖВңYes, I think I saw him pass; but pray, call Emmanuel.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңExcuse me, madame, but I must go up to MaximilianГўВҖВҷs room this
instant,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, ГўВҖВңI have something of the greatest
importance to tell him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңGo, then,ГўВҖВқ she said with a charming smile, which accompanied him until
he had disappeared.

Monte Cristo soon ran up the staircase conducting from the ground floor
to MaximilianГўВҖВҷs room; when he reached the landing he listened
attentively, but all was still. Like many old houses occupied by a
single family, the room door was panelled with glass; but it was
locked, Maximilian was shut in, and it was impossible to see what was
passing in the room, because a red curtain was drawn before the glass.
The countГўВҖВҷs anxiety was manifested by a bright color which seldom
appeared on the face of that imperturbable man.

ГўВҖВңWhat shall I do!ГўВҖВқ he uttered, and reflected for a moment; ГўВҖВңshall I
ring? No, the sound of a bell, announcing a visitor, will but
accelerate the resolution of one in MaximilianГўВҖВҷs situation, and then
the bell would be followed by a louder noise.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo trembled from head to foot and as if his determination had
been taken with the rapidity of lightning, he struck one of the panes
of glass with his elbow; the glass was shivered to atoms, then
withdrawing the curtain he saw Morrel, who had been writing at his
desk, bound from his seat at the noise of the broken window.

ГўВҖВңI beg a thousand pardons,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңthere is nothing the
matter, but I slipped down and broke one of your panes of glass with my
elbow. Since it is opened, I will take advantage of it to enter your
room; do not disturb yourselfГўВҖВ”do not disturb yourself!ГўВҖВқ

And passing his hand through the broken glass, the count opened the
door. Morrel, evidently discomposed, came to meet Monte Cristo less
with the intention of receiving him than to exclude his entry.

ГўВҖВң_Ma foi_,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, rubbing his elbow, ГўВҖВңitГўВҖВҷs all your
servantГўВҖВҷs fault; your stairs are so polished, it is like walking on
glass.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAre you hurt, sir?ГўВҖВқ coldly asked Morrel.

ГўВҖВңI believe not. But what are you about there? You were writing.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYour fingers are stained with ink.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh, true, I was writing. I do sometimes, soldier though I am.ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo advanced into the room; Maximilian was obliged to let him
pass, but he followed him.

ГўВҖВңYou were writing?ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo with a searching look.

ГўВҖВңI have already had the honor of telling you I was,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel.

The count looked around him.

ГўВҖВңYour pistols are beside your desk,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, pointing with
his finger to the pistols on the table.

ГўВҖВңI am on the point of starting on a journey,ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel
disdainfully.

ГўВҖВңMy friend,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Monte Cristo in a tone of exquisite sweetness.

ГўВҖВңSir?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy friend, my dear Maximilian, do not make a hasty resolution, I
entreat you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI make a hasty resolution?ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, shrugging his shoulders; ГўВҖВңis
there anything extraordinary in a journey?ГўВҖВқ

50125m



ГўВҖВңMaximilian,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңlet us both lay aside the mask we have
assumed. You no more deceive me with that false calmness than I impose
upon you with my frivolous solicitude. You can understand, can you not,
that to have acted as I have done, to have broken that glass, to have
intruded on the solitude of a friendГўВҖВ”you can understand that, to have
done all this, I must have been actuated by real uneasiness, or rather
by a terrible conviction. Morrel, you are going to destroy yourself!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIndeed, count,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, shuddering; ГўВҖВңwhat has put this into your
head?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI tell you that you are about to destroy yourself,ГўВҖВқ continued the
count, ГўВҖВңand here is proof of what I say;ГўВҖВқ and, approaching the desk, he
removed the sheet of paper which Morrel had placed over the letter he
had begun, and took the latter in his hands.

Morrel rushed forward to tear it from him, but Monte Cristo perceiving
his intention, seized his wrist with his iron grasp.

ГўВҖВңYou wish to destroy yourself,ГўВҖВқ said the count; ГўВҖВңyou have written it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWell,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, changing his expression of calmness for one of
violenceГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңwell, and if I do intend to turn this pistol against myself,
who shall prevent meГўВҖВ”who will dare prevent me? All my hopes are
blighted, my heart is broken, my life a burden, everything around me is
sad and mournful; earth has become distasteful to me, and human voices
distract me. It is a mercy to let me die, for if I live I shall lose my
reason and become mad. When, sir, I tell you all this with tears of
heartfelt anguish, can you reply that I am wrong, can you prevent my
putting an end to my miserable existence? Tell me, sir, could you have
the courage to do so?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes, Morrel,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, with a calmness which contrasted
strangely with the young manГўВҖВҷs excitement; ГўВҖВңyes, I would do so.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou?ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Morrel, with increasing anger and reproachГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңyou, who
have deceived me with false hopes, who have cheered and soothed me with
vain promises, when I might, if not have saved her, at least have seen
her die in my arms! You, who pretend to understand everything, even the
hidden sources of knowledge,ГўВҖВ”and who enact the part of a guardian angel
upon earth, and could not even find an antidote to a poison
administered to a young girl! Ah, sir, indeed you would inspire me with
pity, were you not hateful in my eyes.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMorrelГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; you tell me to lay aside the mask, and I will do so, be
satisfied! When you spoke to me at the cemetery, I answered youГўВҖВ”my
heart was softened; when you arrived here, I allowed you to enter. But
since you abuse my confidence, since you have devised a new torture
after I thought I had exhausted them all, then, Count of Monte Cristo
my pretended benefactorГўВҖВ”then, Count of Monte Cristo, the universal
guardian, be satisfied, you shall witness the death of your friend;ГўВҖВқ
and Morrel, with a maniacal laugh, again rushed towards the pistols.

ГўВҖВңAnd I again repeat, you shall not commit suicide.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPrevent me, then!ГўВҖВқ replied Morrel, with another struggle, which, like
the first, failed in releasing him from the countГўВҖВҷs iron grasp.

ГўВҖВңI will prevent you.ГўВҖВқ

50127m



ГўВҖВңAnd who are you, then, that arrogate to yourself this tyrannical right
over free and rational beings?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWho am I?ГўВҖВқ repeated Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңListen; I am the only man in the
world having the right to say to you, ГўВҖВҳMorrel, your fatherГўВҖВҷs son shall
not die today;ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ and Monte Cristo, with an expression of majesty and
sublimity, advanced with arms folded toward the young man, who,
involuntarily overcome by the commanding manner of this man, recoiled a
step.

ГўВҖВңWhy do you mention my father?ГўВҖВқ stammered he; ГўВҖВңwhy do you mingle a
recollection of him with the affairs of today?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBecause I am he who saved your fatherГўВҖВҷs life when he wished to destroy
himself, as you do todayГўВҖВ”because I am the man who sent the purse to
your young sister, and the _Pharaon_ to old MorrelГўВҖВ”because I am the
Edmond DantГғВЁs who nursed you, a child, on my knees.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel made another step back, staggering, breathless, crushed; then
all his strength give way, and he fell prostrate at the feet of Monte
Cristo. Then his admirable nature underwent a complete and sudden
revulsion; he arose, rushed out of the room and to the stairs,
exclaiming energetically, ГўВҖВңJulie, JulieГўВҖВ”Emmanuel, Emmanuel!ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo endeavored also to leave, but Maximilian would have died
rather than relax his hold of the handle of the door, which he closed
upon the count. Julie, Emmanuel, and some of the servants, ran up in
alarm on hearing the cries of Maximilian. Morrel seized their hands,
and opening the door exclaimed in a voice choked with sobs:

ГўВҖВңOn your kneesГўВҖВ”on your kneesГўВҖВ”he is our benefactorГўВҖВ”the saviour of our
father! He isГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

He would have added ГўВҖВңEdmond DantГғВЁs,ГўВҖВқ but the count seized his arm and
prevented him.

Julie threw herself into the arms of the count; Emmanuel embraced him
as a guardian angel; Morrel again fell on his knees, and struck the
ground with his forehead. Then the iron-hearted man felt his heart
swell in his breast; a flame seemed to rush from his throat to his
eyes, he bent his head and wept. For a while nothing was heard in the
room but a succession of sobs, while the incense from their grateful
hearts mounted to heaven. Julie had scarcely recovered from her deep
emotion when she rushed out of the room, descended to the next floor,
ran into the drawing-room with childlike joy and raised the crystal
globe which covered the purse given by the unknown of the AllГғВ©es de
Meilhan. Meanwhile, Emmanuel in a broken voice said to the count:

ГўВҖВңOh, count, how could you, hearing us so often speak of our unknown
benefactor, seeing us pay such homage of gratitude and adoration to his
memory,ГўВҖВ”how could you continue so long without discovering yourself to
us? Oh, it was cruel to us, andГўВҖВ”dare I say it?ГўВҖВ”to you also.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңListen, my friends,ГўВҖВқ said the countГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңI may call you so since we have
really been friends for the last eleven yearsГўВҖВ”the discovery of this
secret has been occasioned by a great event which you must never know.
I wished to bury it during my whole life in my own bosom, but your
brother Maximilian wrested it from me by a violence he repents of now,
I am sure.ГўВҖВқ

Then turning around, and seeing that Morrel, still on his knees, had
thrown himself into an armchair, he added in a low voice, pressing
EmmanuelГўВҖВҷs hand significantly, ГўВҖВңWatch over him.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhy so?ГўВҖВқ asked the young man, surprised.

ГўВҖВңI cannot explain myself; but watch over him.ГўВҖВқ Emmanuel looked around
the room and caught sight of the pistols; his eyes rested on the
weapons, and he pointed to them. Monte Cristo bent his head. Emmanuel
went towards the pistols.

ГўВҖВңLeave them,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo. Then walking towards Morrel, he took
his hand; the tumultuous agitation of the young man was succeeded by a
profound stupor. Julie returned, holding the silken purse in her hands,
while tears of joy rolled down her cheeks, like dewdrops on the rose.

ГўВҖВңHere is the relic,ГўВҖВқ she said; ГўВҖВңdo not think it will be less dear to us
now we are acquainted with our benefactor!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy child,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, coloring, ГўВҖВңallow me to take back that
purse? Since you now know my face, I wish to be remembered alone
through the affection I hope you will grant me.

ГўВҖВңOh,ГўВҖВқ said Julie, pressing the purse to her heart, ГўВҖВңno, no, I beseech
you do not take it, for some unhappy day you will leave us, will you
not?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYou have guessed rightly, madame,ГўВҖВқ replied Monte Cristo, smiling; ГўВҖВңin
a week I shall have left this country, where so many persons who merit
the vengeance of Heaven lived happily, while my father perished of
hunger and grief.ГўВҖВқ

While announcing his departure, the count fixed his eyes on Morrel, and
remarked that the words, ГўВҖВңI shall have left this country,ГўВҖВқ had failed
to rouse him from his lethargy. He then saw that he must make another
struggle against the grief of his friend, and taking the hands of
Emmanuel and Julie, which he pressed within his own, he said with the
mild authority of a father:

ГўВҖВңMy kind friends, leave me alone with Maximilian.ГўВҖВқ

Julie saw the means offered of carrying off her precious relic, which
Monte Cristo had forgotten. She drew her husband to the door. ГўВҖВңLet us
leave them,ГўВҖВқ she said.

The count was alone with Morrel, who remained motionless as a statue.

ГўВҖВңCome,ГўВҖВқ said Monte-Cristo, touching his shoulder with his finger, ГўВҖВңare
you a man again, Maximilian?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; for I begin to suffer again.ГўВҖВқ

The count frowned, apparently in gloomy hesitation.

ГўВҖВңMaximilian, Maximilian,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңthe ideas you yield to are unworthy
of a Christian.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, do not fear, my friend,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, raising his head, and
smiling with a sweet expression on the count; ГўВҖВңI shall no longer
attempt my life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen we are to have no more pistolsГўВҖВ”no more despair?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңNo; I have found a better remedy for my grief than either a bullet or
a knife.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңPoor fellow, what is it?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy grief will kill me of itself.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңMy friend,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, with an expression of melancholy equal
to his own, ГўВҖВңlisten to me. One day, in a moment of despair like yours,
since it led to a similar resolution, I also wished to kill myself; one
day your father, equally desperate, wished to kill himself too. If
anyone had said to your father, at the moment he raised the pistol to
his headГўВҖВ”if anyone had told me, when in my prison I pushed back the
food I had not tasted for three daysГўВҖВ”if anyone had said to either of us
then, ГўВҖВҳLiveГўВҖВ”the day will come when you will be happy, and will bless
life!ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВ”no matter whose voice had spoken, we should have heard him with
the smile of doubt, or the anguish of incredulity,ГўВҖВ”and yet how many
times has your father blessed life while embracing youГўВҖВ”how often have I
myselfГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ exclaimed Morrel, interrupting the count, ГўВҖВңyou had only lost your
liberty, my father had only lost his fortune, but I have lost
Valentine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLook at me,ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo, with that expression which sometimes
made him so eloquent and persuasiveГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңlook at me. There are no tears in
my eyes, nor is there fever in my veins, yet I see you sufferГўВҖВ”you,
Maximilian, whom I love as my own son. Well, does not this tell you
that in grief, as in life, there is always something to look forward to
beyond? Now, if I entreat, if I order you to live, Morrel, it is in the
conviction that one day you will thank me for having preserved your
life.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, heavens,ГўВҖВқ said the young man, ГўВҖВңoh, heavensГўВҖВ”what are you saying,
count? Take care. But perhaps you have never loved!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңChild!ГўВҖВқ replied the count.

ГўВҖВңI mean, as I love. You see, I have been a soldier ever since I
attained manhood. I reached the age of twenty-nine without loving, for
none of the feelings I before then experienced merit the appellation of
love. Well, at twenty-nine I saw Valentine; for two years I have loved
her, for two years I have seen written in her heart, as in a book, all
the virtues of a daughter and wife. Count, to possess Valentine would
have been a happiness too infinite, too ecstatic, too complete, too
divine for this world, since it has been denied me; but without
Valentine the earth is desolate.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have told you to hope,ГўВҖВқ said the count.

ГўВҖВңThen have a care, I repeat, for you seek to persuade me, and if you
succeed I should lose my reason, for I should hope that I could again
behold Valentine.ГўВҖВқ

The count smiled.

ГўВҖВңMy friend, my father,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel with excitement, ГўВҖВңhave a care, I
again repeat, for the power you wield over me alarms me. Weigh your
words before you speak, for my eyes have already become brighter, and
my heart beats strongly; be cautious, or you will make me believe in
supernatural agencies. I must obey you, though you bade me call forth
the dead or walk upon the water.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHope, my friend,ГўВҖВқ repeated the count.

ГўВҖВңAh,ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, falling from the height of excitement to the abyss
of despairГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңah, you are playing with me, like those good, or rather
selfish mothers who soothe their children with honeyed words, because
their screams annoy them. No, my friend, I was wrong to caution you; do
not fear, I will bury my grief so deep in my heart, I will disguise it
so, that you shall not even care to sympathize with me. Adieu, my
friend, adieu!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOn the contrary,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңafter this time you must live with
meГўВҖВ”you must not leave me, and in a week we shall have left France
behind us.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңAnd you still bid me hope?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI tell you to hope, because I have a method of curing you.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCount, you render me sadder than before, if it be possible. You think
the result of this blow has been to produce an ordinary grief, and you
would cure it by an ordinary remedyГўВҖВ”change of scene.ГўВҖВқ And Morrel
dropped his head with disdainful incredulity.

ГўВҖВңWhat can I say more?ГўВҖВқ asked Monte Cristo. ГўВҖВңI have confidence in the
remedy I propose, and only ask you to permit me to assure you of its
efficacy.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCount, you prolong my agony.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen,ГўВҖВқ said the count, ГўВҖВңyour feeble spirit will not even grant me the
trial I request? ComeГўВҖВ”do you know of what the Count of Monte Cristo is
capable? do you know that he holds terrestrial beings under his
control? nay, that he can almost work a miracle? Well, wait for the
miracle I hope to accomplish, orГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOr?ГўВҖВқ repeated Morrel.

ГўВҖВңOr, take care, Morrel, lest I call you ungrateful.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHave pity on me, count!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI feel so much pity towards you, Maximilian, thatГўВҖВ”listen to me
attentivelyГўВҖВ”if I do not cure you in a month, to the day, to the very
hour, mark my words, Morrel, I will place loaded pistols before you,
and a cup of the deadliest Italian poisonГўВҖВ”a poison more sure and prompt
than that which has killed Valentine.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWill you promise me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; for I am a man, and have suffered like yourself, and also
contemplated suicide; indeed, often since misfortune has left me I have
longed for the delights of an eternal sleep.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңBut you are sure you will promise me this?ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, intoxicated.

ГўВҖВңI not only promise, but swear it!ГўВҖВқ said Monte Cristo extending his
hand.

ГўВҖВңIn a month, then, on your honor, if I am not consoled, you will let me
take my life into my own hands, and whatever may happen you will not
call me ungrateful?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңIn a month, to the day, the very hour and the date is a sacred one,
Maximilian. I do not know whether you remember that this is the 5th of
September; it is ten years today since I saved your fatherГўВҖВҷs life, who
wished to die.ГўВҖВқ

Morrel seized the countГўВҖВҷs hand and kissed it; the count allowed him to
pay the homage he felt due to him.

ГўВҖВңIn a month you will find on the table, at which we shall be then
sitting, good pistols and a delicious draught; but, on the other hand,
you must promise me not to attempt your life before that time.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, I also swear it!ГўВҖВқ

Monte Cristo drew the young man towards him, and pressed him for some
time to his heart. ГўВҖВңAnd now,ГўВҖВқ he said, ГўВҖВңafter today, you will come and
live with me; you can occupy HaydГғВ©eГўВҖВҷs apartment, and my daughter will
at least be replaced by my son.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңHaydГғВ©e?ГўВҖВқ said Morrel, ГўВҖВңwhat has become of her?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңShe departed last night.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo leave you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo wait for me. Hold yourself ready then to join me at the
Champs-ГғВүlysГғВ©es, and lead me out of this house without anyone seeing my
departure.ГўВҖВқ

Maximilian hung his head, and obeyed with childlike reverence.



 Chapter 106. Dividing the Proceeds

The apartment on the first floor of the house in the Rue
Saint-Germain-des-PrГғВ©s, where Albert de Morcerf had selected a home for
his mother, was let to a very mysterious person. This was a man whose
face the concierge himself had never seen, for in the winter his chin
was buried in one of the large red handkerchiefs worn by gentlemenГўВҖВҷs
coachmen on a cold night, and in the summer he made a point of always
blowing his nose just as he approached the door. Contrary to custom,
this gentleman had not been watched, for as the report ran that he was
a person of high rank, and one who would allow no impertinent
interference, his _incognito_ was strictly respected.

His visits were tolerably regular, though occasionally he appeared a
little before or after his time, but generally, both in summer and
winter, he took possession of his apartment about four oГўВҖВҷclock, though
he never spent the night there. At half-past three in the winter the
fire was lighted by the discreet servant, who had the superintendence
of the little apartment, and in the summer ices were placed on the
table at the same hour. At four oГўВҖВҷclock, as we have already stated, the
mysterious personage arrived.

Twenty minutes afterwards a carriage stopped at the house, a lady
alighted in a black or dark blue dress, and always thickly veiled; she
passed like a shadow through the lodge, and ran upstairs without a
sound escaping under the touch of her light foot. No one ever asked her
where she was going. Her face, therefore, like that of the gentleman,
was perfectly unknown to the two concierges, who were perhaps
unequalled throughout the capital for discretion. We need not say she
stopped at the first floor. Then she tapped in a peculiar manner at a
door, which after being opened to admit her was again fastened, and
curiosity penetrated no farther. They used the same precautions in
leaving as in entering the house. The lady always left first, and as
soon as she had stepped into her carriage, it drove away, sometimes
towards the right hand, sometimes to the left; then about twenty
minutes afterwards the gentleman would also leave, buried in his cravat
or concealed by his handkerchief.

The day after Monte Cristo had called upon Danglars, the mysterious
lodger entered at ten oГўВҖВҷclock in the morning instead of four in the
afternoon. Almost directly afterwards, without the usual interval of
time, a cab arrived, and the veiled lady ran hastily upstairs. The door
opened, but before it could be closed, the lady exclaimed:

ГўВҖВңOh, LucienГўВҖВ”oh, my friend!ГўВҖВқ

The concierge therefore heard for the first time that the lodgerГўВҖВҷs name
was Lucien; still, as he was the very perfection of a door-keeper, he
made up his mind not to tell his wife.

ГўВҖВңWell, what is the matter, my dear?ГўВҖВқ asked the gentleman whose name the
ladyГўВҖВҷs agitation revealed; ГўВҖВңtell me what is the matter.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, Lucien, can I confide in you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOf course, you know you can do so. But what can be the matter? Your
note of this morning has completely bewildered me. This
precipitationГўВҖВ”this unusual appointment. Come, ease me of my anxiety, or
else frighten me at once.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLucien, a great event has happened!ГўВҖВқ said the lady, glancing
inquiringly at Lucien,ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВңM. Danglars left last night!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңLeft?ГўВҖВ”M. Danglars left? Where has he gone?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not know.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWhat do you mean? Has he gone intending not to return?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңUndoubtedly;ГўВҖВ”at ten oГўВҖВҷclock at night his horses took him to the
barrier of Charenton; there a post-chaise was waiting for himГўВҖВ”he
entered it with his valet de chambre, saying that he was going to
Fontainebleau.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңThen what did you meanГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВ”ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңStayГўВҖВ”he left a letter for me.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңA letter?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; read it.ГўВҖВқ

And the baroness took from her pocket a letter which she gave to
Debray. Debray paused a moment before reading, as if trying to guess
its contents, or perhaps while making up his mind how to act, whatever
it might contain. No doubt his ideas were arranged in a few minutes,
for he began reading the letter which caused so much uneasiness in the
heart of the baroness, and which ran as follows:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳMadame and most faithful wife.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

Debray mechanically stopped and looked at the baroness, whose face
became covered with blushes.

ГўВҖВңRead,ГўВҖВқ she said.

Debray continued:

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳWhen you receive this, you will no longer have a husband. Oh, you
need not be alarmed, you will only have lost him as you have lost your
daughter; I mean that I shall be travelling on one of the thirty or
forty roads leading out of France. I owe you some explanations for my
conduct, and as you are a woman that can perfectly understand me, I
will give them. Listen, then. I received this morning five millions
which I paid away; almost directly afterwards another demand for the
same sum was presented to me; I put this creditor off till tomorrow and
I intend leaving today, to escape that tomorrow, which would be rather
too unpleasant for me to endure. You understand this, do you not, my
most precious wife? I say you understand this, because you are as
conversant with my affairs as I am; indeed, I think you understand them
better, since I am ignorant of what has become of a considerable
portion of my fortune, once very tolerable, while I am sure, madame,
that you know perfectly well. For women have infallible instincts; they
can even explain the marvellous by an algebraic calculation they have
invented; but I, who only understand my own figures, know nothing more
than that one day these figures deceived me. Have you admired the
rapidity of my fall? Have you been slightly dazzled at the sudden
fusion of my ingots? I confess I have seen nothing but the fire; let us
hope you have found some gold among the ashes. With this consoling
idea, I leave you, madame, and most prudent wife, without any
conscientious reproach for abandoning you; you have friends left, and
the ashes I have already mentioned, and above all the liberty I hasten
to restore to you. And here, madame, I must add another word of
explanation. So long as I hoped you were working for the good of our
house and for the fortune of our daughter, I philosophically closed my
eyes; but as you have transformed that house into a vast ruin I will
not be the foundation of another manГўВҖВҷs fortune. You were rich when I
married you, but little respected. Excuse me for speaking so very
candidly, but as this is intended only for ourselves, I do not see why
I should weigh my words. I have augmented our fortune, and it has
continued to increase during the last fifteen years, till extraordinary
and unexpected catastrophes have suddenly overturned it,ГўВҖВ”without any
fault of mine, I can honestly declare. You, madame, have only sought to
increase your own, and I am convinced that you have succeeded. I leave
you, therefore, as I took you,ГўВҖВ”rich, but little respected. Adieu! I
also intend from this time to work on my own account. Accept my
acknowledgments for the example you have set me, and which I intend
following.

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳYour very devoted husband,

ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳBaron Danglars.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ

The baroness had watched Debray while he read this long and painful
letter, and saw him, notwithstanding his self-control, change color
once or twice. When he had ended the perusal, he folded the letter and
resumed his pensive attitude.

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ asked Madame Danglars, with an anxiety easy to be understood.

ГўВҖВңWell, madame?ГўВҖВқ unhesitatingly repeated Debray.

ГўВҖВңWith what ideas does that letter inspire you?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, it is simple enough, madame; it inspires me with the idea that M.
Danglars has left suspiciously.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңCertainly; but is this all you have to say to me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI do not understand you,ГўВҖВқ said Debray with freezing coldness.

ГўВҖВңHe is gone! Gone, never to return!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңOh, madame, do not think that!ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI tell you he will never return. I know his character; he is
inflexible in any resolutions formed for his own interests. If he could
have made any use of me, he would have taken me with him; he leaves me
in Paris, as our separation will conduce to his benefit;ГўВҖВ”therefore he
has gone, and I am free forever,ГўВҖВқ added Madame Danglars, in the same
supplicating tone.

Debray, instead of answering, allowed her to remain in an attitude of
nervous inquiry.

ГўВҖВңWell?ГўВҖВқ she said at length, ГўВҖВңdo you not answer me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI have but one question to ask you,ГўВҖВ”what do you intend to do?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI was going to ask you,ГўВҖВқ replied the baroness with a beating heart.

ГўВҖВңAh, then, you wish to ask advice of me?ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңYes; I do wish to ask your advice,ГўВҖВқ said Madame Danglars with anxious
expectation.

ГўВҖВңThen if you wish to take my advice,ГўВҖВқ said the young man coldly, ГўВҖВңI
would recommend you to travel.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңTo travel!ГўВҖВқ she murmured.

ГўВҖВңCertainly; as M. Danglars says, you are rich, and perfectly free. In
my opinion, a withdrawal from Paris is absolutely necessary after the
double catastrophe of Mademoiselle DanglarsГўВҖВҷ broken contract and M.
DanglarsГўВҖВҷ disappearance. The world will think you abandoned and poor,
for the wife of a bankrupt would never be forgiven, were she to keep up
an appearance of opulence. You have only to remain in Paris for about a
fortnight, telling the world you are abandoned, and relating the
details of this desertion to your best friends, who will soon spread
the report. Then you can quit your house, leaving your jewels and
giving up your jointure, and everyoneГўВҖВҷs mouth will be filled with
praises of your disinterestedness. They will know you are deserted, and
think you also poor, for I alone know your real financial position, and
am quite ready to give up my accounts as an honest partner.ГўВҖВқ

The dread with which the pale and motionless baroness listened to this,
was equalled by the calm indifference with which Debray had spoken.

ГўВҖВңDeserted?ГўВҖВқ she repeated; ГўВҖВңah, yes, I am, indeed, deserted! You are
right, sir, and no one can doubt my position.ГўВҖВқ

These were the only words that this proud and violently enamoured woman
could utter in response to Debray.

50137m



ГўВҖВңBut then you are rich,ГўВҖВ”very rich, indeed,ГўВҖВқ continued Debray, taking
out some papers from his pocket-book, which he spread upon the table.
Madame Danglars did not see them; she was engaged in stilling the
beatings of her heart, and restraining the tears which were ready to
gush forth. At length a sense of dignity prevailed, and if she did not
entirely master her agitation, she at least succeeded in preventing the
fall of a single tear.

ГўВҖВңMadame,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, ГўВҖВңit is nearly six months since we have been
associated. You furnished a principal of 100,000 francs. Our
partnership began in the month of April. In May we commenced
operations, and in the course of the month gained 450,000 francs. In
June the profit amounted to 900,000. In July we added 1,700,000
francs,ГўВҖВ”it was, you know, the month of the Spanish bonds. In August we
lost 300,000 francs at the beginning of the month, but on the 13th we
made up for it, and we now find that our accounts, reckoning from the
first day of partnership up to yesterday, when I closed them, showed a

madame,ГўВҖВқ said Debray, delivering up his accounts in the methodical
manner of a stockbroker, ГўВҖВңthere are still 80,000 francs, the interest
